topics.title: Mass and Molar Flux
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mass and Molar Flux

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mass Flux — The Formal KTU Definition

**Mass flux** of a species **A** in a mixture is defined as the amount (mass) of component **A** flowing per unit cross-sectional area per unit time, measured with respect to **fixed spatial coordinates** (a stationary observer).

$$
n_A \;\; \left[\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass flux is a vector quantity.** It has both magnitude AND direction. In 1-D mass transfer (which dominates KTU problems), direction is implicit along the z-axis.

**Molar flux** of a species **A** is similarly defined on a *molar* basis:

$$
N_A \;\; \left[\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}\right]
$$

The two are inter-related by the molecular weight $M_A$:

$$
n_A = M_A \cdot N_A
$$

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "The Crowd at the Concert Gate"

Imagine a stadium gate where two things happen simultaneously:

| Phenomenon | Real-World Picture | Mass-Transfer Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| People walking **randomly** in and out on their own (jostling, Brownian motion) | Random mixing at the molecular level | **Diffusive flux** $J_A$ |
| A **current of crowd** (everyone pushed in one direction by a performer leaving) | Bulk motion of the entire fluid | **Convective flux** $c_A v_m$ |
| **Total people passing** through the gate | Sum of both effects | **Total molar flux** $N_A$ |

So:

$$
\underbrace{N_A}_{\text{Total people at gate}} = \underbrace{J_A}_{\text{Random jostling}} + \underbrace{c_A \, v_m}_{\text{Whole crowd moving}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Even if there were *no* diffusion ($J_A = 0$), species A would still be transported if the bulk fluid itself is moving (e.g., forced convection in an absorber). KTU problems almost always involve BOTH terms.

## 1.3 Two Reference Frames — Why We Need Them

The KTU syllabus distinguishes **two velocities** because species in a mixture can move at *different* molecular speeds:

| Velocity Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $v$ | Mass-average velocity | Velocity of centre of mass of the fluid |
| $v_m$ | Molar-average velocity | Velocity weighted by mole fractions |
| $v_A$ | Velocity of species A | Local velocity of molecules of A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Molar flux $N_A$ is measured by a stationary observer.** **Diffusive flux $J_A$ is measured by an observer moving with the molar-average velocity $v_m$.** This subtle distinction is the most heavily tested concept in Module 1.

## 1.4 The Two Reference Frames Diagrammed

The relative velocity of A with respect to the moving observer is:

$$
u_A = v_A - v_m
$$

Therefore:

$$
J_A = c_A (v_A - v_m) = c_A \cdot u_A
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deriving the Total Flux — Logic Flow

**Step 1 — Define molar flux with respect to a stationary observer:**

$$
N_A = c_A \cdot v_A
$$

This is simply "concentration of A times the absolute velocity of A".

**Step 2 — Add and subtract $c_A v_m$ strategically (the classic trick):**

$$
N_A = c_A(v_A - v_m) + c_A \cdot v_m
$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the first term as the diffusive flux:**

$$
N_A = \underbrace{c_A(v_A - v_m)}_{J_A} + \underbrace{c_A \cdot v_m}_{\text{convective part}}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A = J_A + c_A \, v_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that the total molar flux $N = N_A + N_B$ and that the convective part equals $x_A \cdot N$:**

Since $v_m = \Sigma x_i v_i$ and $N = c \cdot v_m$ (where $c$ is total concentration):

$$
c_A \, v_m = \frac{c_A}{c}(c \, v_m) = x_A \cdot N
$$

Therefore the **master equation of Module 1** emerges:

$$
\boxed{N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz} + x_A \, N}
$$

where $D_{AB}$ is the **diffusivity (mass diffusivity)** of A in B, with units of **m²/s** (or **cm²/s** in CGS).

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Cases (Most Tested on KTU ESE)

### Case A — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)

Here $N_A = -N_B$, so $N = N_A + N_B = 0$. Plugging into the master equation:

$$
\boxed{N_A = J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz}}
$$

**Real-world example:** Distillation — for every mole of light key vaporised, one mole of heavy key condenses.

### Case B — Diffusion of A through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)

Here $N_B = 0$, so $N = N_A$. Substitution gives:

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \frac{dc_A}{dz}
$$

In terms of partial pressure (ideal gas, constant T, P):

$$
\boxed{N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{1}{1 - x_A} \frac{dP_A}{dz} = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \frac{1}{P_{BM}} \frac{dP_A}{dz}}
$$

where $P_{BM}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of B**:

$$
P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stefan diffusion** is the **single most important case** for KTU numericals. Examples: evaporation of water into air, absorption of a pure gas into a liquid, sublimation of naphthalene into still air.

### Case C — A Diffusing with Stagnant Boundary Approximation

When $x_A \ll 1$ (very dilute), $1 - x_A \approx 1$ and the equation collapses back to Fick's first law.

## 2.3 Mass Flux — Analogous Formulation

Mirroring the molar derivation, the **mass flux** of A is:

$$
\boxed{n_A = -\rho D_{AB} \frac{d\omega_A}{dz} + \omega_A \, n}
$$

where $\omega_A$ is the mass fraction of A and $n = n_A + n_B$ is the total mass flux.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\mid` or `\vert` instead of vertical bars inside table cells to preserve markdown syntax.

| # | Equation Name | Final Formula | SI Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's First Law (diffusive) | $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz}$ | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | Relative to moving frame |
| 2 | Total molar flux (master) | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz} + x_A N$ | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | Stationary observer |
| 3 | Equimolar counter-diffusion | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz}$ | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | $N = 0$ (distillation) |
| 4 | Stefan diffusion (liquid/gas) | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz}$ | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | $N_B = 0$ |
| 5 | Stefan diffusion (gas, P-form) | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT \, P_{BM}} \dfrac{dP_A}{dz}$ | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | Ideal gas Stefan problem |
| 6 | Log-mean partial pressure | $P_{BM} = \dfrac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$ | Pa or bar | Stefan numericals |
| 7 | Molar flux from mass flux | $N_A = \dfrac{n_A}{M_A}$ | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | Conversion between forms |
| 8 | Mass flux (master) | $n_A = -\rho D_{AB} \dfrac{d\omega_A}{dz} + \omega_A n$ | $\text{kg/m}^2\text{s}$ | When mass fractions given |
| 9 | Diffusive flux relation | $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz}$ | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | Always; pure diffusion |
| 10 | N = c v_m relation | $N = c \, v_m$ | $\text{kmol/m}^2\text{s}$ | Linking bulk velocity to total flux |

## 2.5 Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> Every mass-transfer operation in industry — **absorption, stripping, distillation, drying, humidification, leaching, extraction** — is solved by integrating one of the flux equations above over a control volume. The seemingly abstract flux concepts of Module 1 are the **engine that drives** $70\%$ of the entire Mass Transfer Operations 1 syllabus.

Real-world industries directly relying on these equations:
- **Petrochemical**: Diffusivity of hydrocarbons in refinery gas streams.
- **Pharmaceutical**: Drying of granules — water diffusing through stagnant air (Stefan diffusion).
- **Environmental**: Scrubbing of $SO_2$ from flue gas using water.
- **Food**: Aroma compound diffusion during coffee roasting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Fick's Law to Stefan Equation

### Starting Postulate (Fick's First Law)

Fick proposed that the diffusive flux is proportional to the concentration gradient:

$$
J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz}
$$

The constant of proportionality $D_{AB}$ is the **binary diffusivity** with units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

### Step 1 — Express Total Molar Flux

The total molar flux is the sum of the two species fluxes:

$$
N = N_A + N_B
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Master Equation for A and B

For species A:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz} + x_A \, N
$$

For species B:

$$
N_B = -D_{BA} \frac{dc_B}{dz} + x_B \, N
$$

By the **reciprocity relation** (which KTU may quote without proof):

$$
D_{AB} = D_{BA}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Constraint of the Problem

**Stefan diffusion:** $N_B = 0$, so $N = N_A$. Substituting:

$$
0 = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_B}{dz} + (1 - x_A) N_A
$$

Because $c_A + c_B = c$ (total concentration is constant for liquid, or $P/RT$ for ideal gas), we get:

$$
\frac{dc_B}{dz} = -\frac{dc_A}{dz}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Denominator

Substituting back:

$$
0 = D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz} + (1 - x_A) N_A
$$

$$
N_A(1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \frac{dc_A}{dz}}
$$

### Step 5 — Transform to Partial Pressure (for Ideal Gas Case)

For ideal gas: $c_A = \dfrac{P_A}{RT}$, so $\dfrac{dc_A}{dz} = \dfrac{1}{RT} \dfrac{dP_A}{dz}$.

Also $x_A = \dfrac{P_A}{P}$, hence $1 - x_A = \dfrac{P_B}{P} = \dfrac{P - P_A}{P}$.

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{(P - P_A)/P} \cdot \frac{1}{RT} \cdot \frac{dP_A}{dz}
$$

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, (P - P_A)} \cdot \frac{dP_A}{dz}
$$

Since $(P - P_A) = P_B$, define $P_{BM}$ as the log-mean value of $P_B$ across the diffusion path.

$$
\boxed{N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, P_{BM}} \cdot \frac{dP_A}{dz}}
$$

### Step 6 — Integrate for Steady-State 1-D Diffusion Across a Stagnant Film

Assuming $N_A$, $D_{AB}$, $P$, $T$, and $P_{BM}$ are constant over the path from $z_1$ to $z_2$:

$$
N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, P_{BM}} \int_{P_{A1}}^{P_{A2}} dP_A
$$

$$
N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, P_{BM}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})
$$

Let $\delta = z_2 - z_1$ be the film thickness:

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, \delta \, P_{BM}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **final form used in every KTU numerical**. Memorise the structure: **(D × driving force) / (resistance)**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Stefan Diffusion (Full KTU Valuation Standard)

**Problem (typical KTU style):**
Ammonia (A) is diffusing through a stagnant film of nitrogen (B) at **298 K** and **1 atm**. The film is **2 mm** thick. At the gas–liquid interface $z_1$, $P_{A1} = 0.20 \text{ atm}$. At the bulk gas edge $z_2$, $P_{A2} = 0.05 \text{ atm}$. Given $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, calculate the steady-state molar flux of ammonia.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $P_{B1}$ and $P_{B2}$:**

$$
P_{B1} = P - P_{A1} = 1.00 - 0.20 = 0.80 \text{ atm}
$$

$$
P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 1.00 - 0.05 = 0.95 \text{ atm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute log-mean partial pressure of B:**

$$
P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}
$$

$$
P_{BM} = \frac{0.95 - 0.80}{\ln(0.95/0.80)} = \frac{0.15}{\ln(1.1875)}
$$

$$
P_{BM} = \frac{0.15}{0.17185} = 0.8728 \text{ atm}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the integrated Stefan equation:**

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, \delta \, P_{BM}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})
$$

Using $R = 8.206 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 \text{ atm} / (\text{kmol K})$:

$$
RT = 8.206 \times 10^{-5} \times 298 = 0.02445 \text{ m}^3 \text{ atm}/\text{kmol}
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1.00)}{(0.02445)(2 \times 10^{-3})(0.8728)} (0.20 - 0.05)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
\text{Denominator} = 0.02445 \times 0.002 \times 0.8728 = 4.268 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerator factor:**

$$
(2.30 \times 10^{-5}) \times (0.15) = 3.45 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$
N_A = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-6}}{4.268 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0808 \text{ kmol/m}^2 \text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to engineering-friendly units:**

$$
N_A = 0.0808 \times 3600 \approx 291 \text{ kmol/m}^2 \text{h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation hint (KTU pattern):** Equation (1 mark), log-mean calculation (2 marks), substitution (2 marks), numerical answer (2 marks). Skipping the log-mean step is the #1 mark-loss reason.

## 3.3 Quick Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario Given in Problem | Boundary Condition | Simplified Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Distillation, EMCD stated | $N = 0$ | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz}$ |
| Absorption, "stagnant B" | $N_B = 0$ | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz}$ |
| Forced bulk flow given | $v_m$ specified | $N_A = J_A + c_A v_m$ |
| Mass fractions given | Use mass form | $n_A = -\rho D_{AB} \dfrac{d\omega_A}{dz} + \omega_A n$ |
| Liquid-phase diffusion | $c$ = constant | Use molar form directly |
| Gas-phase diffusion | $c = P/RT$ | Use $P_A$ form with $P_{BM}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flux Relationships

The diagram below maps the conceptual flow between different flux types and reference frames. This is the *mental map* you must hold while solving any KTU Module 1 problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Species A in mixture"]:::root --> B{"Is bulk fluid moving?"}
    B -- "No, stationary observer" --> C["Total Molar Flux NA"]
    B -- "Yes, observer moves with vm" --> D["Diffusive Flux JA"]
    A --> E{"Are species moving at same speed?"}
    E -- "Yes, EMCD NA equals NB" --> F["Fick Law simple form"]
    E -- "No, NA different from NB" --> G["Master equation needed"]
    C --> H["Master Equation NA = -DAB dcA/dz + xA N"]
    D --> H
    H --> I{"Boundary condition?"}
    I -- "NB equals 0 stagnant B" --> J["Stefan diffusion form"]
    I -- "N equals 0 EMCD" --> F
    I -- "N nonzero general" --> K["General numerical integration"]
    J --> L["Integrated form with log-mean PBM"]
    F --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["Final numerical answer kmol per m2 s"]
    classDef root fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Reference Frame Schematic

The block diagram below clarifies the **two-observer problem**, which is the conceptual core of Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBS1["Stationary Observer measures NA"]:::obs --> EQ1["NA = cA times vA"]
    EQ1 --> SPLIT{"Add and subtract cA times vm"}
    SPLIT --> TERM1["cA times vA minus vm equals JA diffusive"]
    SPLIT --> TERM2["cA times vm equals convective part"]
    TERM1 --> FINAL["NA = JA plus cA vm"]
    TERM2 --> FINAL
    OBS2["Moving Observer moving at vm measures JA"]:::obs --> TERM1
    FINAL --> APPLY{"Which case?"}
    APPLY --> CAS1["EMCD: N equals 0, NA equals JA"]
    APPLY --> CAS2["Stagnant B: NB equals 0, Stefan form"]
    APPLY --> CAS3["General: integrate master eq"]
    classDef obs fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Stefan Diffusion Physical Picture (Block Form)

Since the *physical* gas-liquid interface cannot be drawn in Mermaid, the following block schematic conveys the same engineering information:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph z1["At interface z1"]
        Z1P["PA1 high"]:::high --> Z1B["PB1 low"]
    end
    subgraph z2["In bulk gas z2"]
        Z2P["PA2 low"]:::low --> Z2B["PB2 high"]
    end
    Z1P -- "Diffuses across stagnant film" --> Z2P
    Z1B -- "Remains stagnant NB equals 0" --> Z2B
    FILM["Film thickness delta between z1 and z2"]:::film --- z1
    FILM --- z2
    classDef high fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef low fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef film fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> In this physical picture, **NB = 0 means species B is not moving across the film.** It is a *boundary condition*, not a statement that B is absent. The flux of A is enhanced (relative to Fick's law alone) by the factor $\dfrac{1}{1 - x_A}$ because A "carries" itself along with the bulk motion.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define molar flux and diffusive flux. How are they related for a binary mixture?**

**Model Answer:**

**Molar flux of A ($N_A$):** The amount of A in **kmol** flowing per unit cross-sectional area per unit time, measured with respect to **fixed stationary coordinates**. SI unit: $\text{kmol m}^{-2} \text{s}^{-1}$.

**Diffusive flux of A ($J_A$):** The molar flux of A measured with respect to an observer **moving at the molar-average velocity** $v_m$ of the mixture. It represents the motion of A *relative* to the bulk flow. SI unit: $\text{kmol m}^{-2} \text{s}^{-1}$.

**Relation:**

$$
N_A = J_A + c_A v_m = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz} + x_A N
$$

where $N = N_A + N_B$ is the total molar flux. The first term is the diffusive part, the second is the convective part arising from bulk motion.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the relation.)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**State Fick's first law of diffusion. What is the unit and physical significance of diffusivity $D_{AB}$?**

**Model Answer:**

**Fick's First Law:** The diffusive molar flux of component A in a binary mixture is proportional to the concentration gradient of A:

$$
J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz}
$$

The negative sign indicates that mass transfer occurs in the direction of **decreasing concentration** (down the gradient).

**Unit of $D_{AB}$:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (or $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ in CGS).

**Physical significance:** $D_{AB}$ represents the **ease with which molecules of A move through B**. A high $D_{AB}$ means rapid mixing; a low value means sluggish transport. Typical orders of magnitude:
- Gases: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Liquids: $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Solids: $10^{-14}$ to lower

**(3 marks: 1 law + 1 unit + 1 significance with orders.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Derive an expression for the steady-state molar flux of component A diffusing through a **stagnant** film of component B under constant temperature and pressure, starting from Fick's first law. Clearly state all assumptions.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

Hydrogen sulfide ($H_2S$, A) is absorbed from a **CO₂** mixture (B, stagnant) into a solvent at **30 °C** and **1.013 bar**. The diffusion path length is **1.5 mm**. At the gas–liquid interface, partial pressure of $H_2S$ is **0.08 bar**; at the bulk gas edge, it is **0.02 bar**. Given $D_{AB} = 1.65 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, calculate the molar flux of $H_2S$. Use $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol K)}$.

---

### Model Solution for Question B-A

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state (flux constant along z).
2. 1-D diffusion along z-axis.
3. Constant T and P (hence constant total concentration $c = P/RT$).
4. Ideal gas behaviour.
5. $N_B = 0$ (B is stagnant).

**Step 1 — Start from Fick's law in terms of partial pressure:**

For ideal gas, $c_A = P_A/RT$, so:

$$
J_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 2 — Express total flux $N$:**

$$
N_A = J_A + \frac{P_A}{P} N
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $N_B = 0$, so $N = N_A$:**

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_A}{dz} + \frac{P_A}{P} N_A
$$

**Step 4 — Group $N_A$ terms:**

$$
N_A \left(1 - \frac{P_A}{P}\right) = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_A}{dz}
$$

$$
N_A \cdot \frac{P - P_A}{P} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_A}{dz}
$$

$$
N_A \cdot \frac{P_B}{P} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dP_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for $N_A$:**

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, P_B} \frac{dP_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 6 — Recognise that $P_B$ varies with position, so use log-mean $P_{BM}$:**

$$
P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}
$$

**Step 7 — Integrate from $z_1$ to $z_2$:**

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, \delta \, P_{BM}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})
$$

This is the **final derived expression** — 7 marks (1 mark per key step).

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $P_{B1}$ and $P_{B2}$:**

$$
P_{B1} = 1.013 - 0.08 = 0.933 \text{ bar}
$$

$$
P_{B2} = 1.013 - 0.02 = 0.993 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_{BM}$:**

$$
P_{BM} = \frac{0.993 - 0.933}{\ln(0.993/0.933)} = \frac{0.0600}{\ln(1.0643)}
$$

$$
P_{BM} = \frac{0.0600}{0.06228} = 0.9634 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $RT$:**

$$
T = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}, \quad R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol K)}
$$

$$
RT = 8.314 \times 303 = 2519 \text{ J/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
N_A = \frac{(1.65 \times 10^{-5})(1.013)}{(2519)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.9634)} (0.08 - 0.02)
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$
2519 \times 0.0015 \times 0.9634 = 3.640
$$

**Step 6 — Numerator:**

$$
(1.65 \times 10^{-5}) \times 1.013 \times 0.06 = 1.0029 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 7 — Final answer:**

$$
N_A = \frac{1.0029 \times 10^{-6}}{3.640} = 2.755 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/m}^2 \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A \approx 2.76 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/m}^2 \text{s}}
$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Explain the concepts of **molar-average velocity** and **mass-average velocity** for a binary mixture. Derive the relation between molar flux and diffusive flux.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

In a binary gas mixture of **A and B** at **1 atm and 25 °C**, the diffusivity $D_{AB} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. The concentration of A at point 1 is **0.020 kmol/m³** and at point 2 is **0.008 kmol/m³**, separated by **4 mm**. If the bulk molar flow is from point 1 to point 2 with $N = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/m}^2\text{s}$, compute the molar flux of A.

---

### Model Solution for Question B-B

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Molar-average velocity ($v_m$):** Velocity of the fluid weighted by mole fractions:

$$
v_m = \Sigma x_i v_i = x_A v_A + x_B v_B
$$

**Mass-average velocity ($v$):** Velocity weighted by mass fractions:

$$
v = \Sigma \omega_i v_i = \omega_A v_A + \omega_B v_B
$$

**Derivation:**

Define $N_A = c_A v_A$ (molar flux relative to stationary observer). Add and subtract $c_A v_m$:

$$
N_A = c_A(v_A - v_m) + c_A v_m
$$

The first term $c_A(v_A - v_m) = J_A$ is the diffusive flux. The second term is convective flux. Since $c_A v_m = (c_A/c)(c v_m) = x_A N$:

$$
\boxed{N_A = J_A + x_A N = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz} + x_A N}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 marks definitions + 5 marks derivation.)**

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute concentration gradient:**

$$
\frac{dc_A}{dz} = \frac{c_{A2} - c_{A1}}{\Delta z} = \frac{0.008 - 0.020}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{-0.012}{0.004} = -3.0 \text{ kmol/m}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $x_A$ (mole fraction at the midpoint, or use average):**

Using mean $c_A = 0.014 \text{ kmol/m}^3$. Total concentration:

$$
c = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.88 \text{ mol/m}^3 = 0.04088 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

$$
x_A = \frac{0.014}{0.04088} = 0.3424
$$

**Step 3 — Compute diffusive flux $J_A$:**

$$
J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dc_A}{dz} = -(1.0 \times 10^{-5})(-3.0) = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/m}^2\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute convective part:**

$$
x_A N = 0.3424 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 1.712 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/m}^2\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Total molar flux:**

$$
N_A = J_A + x_A N = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} + 1.712 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A = 4.71 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/m}^2\text{s}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing mass flux with molar flux** — if the problem gives $n_A$ but asks for $N_A$, you must divide by $M_A$. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/(1 - x_A)$ enhancement factor** in Stefan diffusion — a full 3-mark penalty if missed.
> 3. **Using arithmetic mean instead of log-mean** for $P_{BM}$ when $P_{B1}$ and $P_{B2}$ differ by more than ~30%. KTU specifically tests this.
> 4. **Wrong sign convention** — flux is positive when species moves in the +z direction. Always define coordinate direction explicitly.
> 5. **Unit slip-ups** — $D_{AB}$ is almost always given in $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ in older textbooks. Convert to $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ by multiplying by $10^{-4}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flux is a vector**: magnitude + direction. In 1-D problems, it reduces to a signed scalar.
- **Two reference frames**: stationary (gives $N_A$) and moving with $v_m$ (gives $J_A$).
- **Master equation** (memorise verbatim): $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz} + x_A N$.
- **Fick's First Law** (memorise verbatim): $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dc_A}{dz}$.
- **Equimolar counter-diffusion** ⇒ $N = 0$ ⇒ $N_A = J_A$ (simplest case).
- **Stagnant B (Stefan diffusion)** ⇒ $N_B = 0$ ⇒ enhancement factor $1/(1-x_A)$ appears.
- **Log-mean trick** is mandatory when $P_B$ varies along the diffusion path — never use arithmetic mean for accurate solutions.
- **Diffusivity orders**: Gases $10^{-5}$ m²/s, Liquids $10^{-9}$ m²/s, Solids $10^{-14}$ m²/s.
- **Conversion**: $n_A = M_A N_A$ (mass flux = molecular weight × molar flux).
- **Final integrated form**: $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, \delta \, P_{BM}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})$ — used in 80% of KTU numericals.
- **Always define coordinate system** at the start of every solution to avoid sign errors.
- **KTU expects unit consistency**: if you mix $\text{cm}$ with $\text{m}$, expect a 0.5-mark deduction.
- **Boundary conditions to recognise instantly**: EMCD vs Stefan vs general bulk flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fick's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fick's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (Formal Definition)

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PCCHT403 – Mass Transfer Operations 1*, **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** is the fundamental constitutive equation that governs the transport of mass by molecular diffusion. It states that:

> *"The molar flux of a component A by molecular diffusion relative to an observer moving with the molar average velocity is proportional to the gradient of its molar concentration in the direction of diffusion, and the constant of proportionality is the molecular diffusivity (or diffusion coefficient)."*

Mathematically, for one-dimensional steady-state diffusion in the **z-direction**:

$$J_{A,z} = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where:
- $J_{A,z}$ = molar diffusive flux of component **A** relative to the molar average velocity, in **kmol/(m²·s)**.
- $D_{AB}$ = binary molecular diffusivity (or diffusion coefficient) of **A** in **B**, in **m²/s** (bolded as the key physical constant).
- $\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ = concentration gradient of **A** in the z-direction, in **kmol/m⁴**.
- The **negative sign** indicates that mass transfer occurs in the direction of **decreasing** concentration (i.e., down the concentration gradient).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Fick's Law is the *cornerstone equation* of mass transfer. It is the direct mass-transfer analogue of Fourier's Law of heat conduction and Newton's Law of viscous flow. Examiners frequently test the physical meaning of the negative sign, units, and the assumptions of the law.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded classroom in Kerala during the monsoon holidays. The air near the open window (low CO₂ concentration) is fresher, while the air near the students (high CO₂ concentration) is "stale." If a student opens a window, CO₂ molecules spontaneously move **from the crowded region to the fresh-air region** — not because they are pushed, but because random molecular motion tends to **even out** concentration differences. The bigger the difference in "staleness" (the concentration gradient), the faster CO₂ spreads out.

- The **diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$** behaves like the "ease of spreading" — gases spread faster than liquids, and liquids faster than solids.
- The **concentration gradient** is the "driving force" — analogous to a height difference causing water to flow downhill, or a temperature difference causing heat to flow.
- The **negative sign** is just saying *"things flow downhill,"* from high concentration to low concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two equivalent ways to write Fick's Law depending on the velocity frame:**
> 1. **Relative to molar average velocity:** $J_A = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$
> 2. **Relative to mass average velocity:** $j_A = -D_{AB} \, \rho \dfrac{d\omega_A}{dz}$ where $\omega_A$ is mass fraction and $\rho$ is mixture density.
> For KTU Module 1, focus on the **molar flux form** with the molar average velocity.

## 1.3 Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (Unsteady-State)

When concentration changes with **time** as well as position, we must use **Fick's Second Law**, derived by combining Fick's First Law with a mass balance on a differential element:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

This is a **parabolic partial differential equation (PDE)** — directly analogous to the heat equation and Laplace's equation. It is the starting point for unsteady-state diffusion problems (e.g., drying, gas absorption in transient regime, transient diffusion in catalysts).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Assumptions of Fick's First Law

For the simple 1-D molar form to be valid, the following conditions apply:

- **Isothermal, isobaric system** (no temperature or pressure gradients driving flow).
- **Binary mixture** of components **A** and **B** only.
- **No chemical reaction** in the control volume.
- **Constant diffusivity $D_{AB}$** (valid for ideal gases; for liquids it is a strong function of concentration).
- **No bulk flow** contribution — flux is purely diffusive.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Negative Sign

The gradient $\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ is **positive** when concentration increases in the **+z direction**. But physically, A diffuses toward the **lower** concentration (i.e., the **−z direction**). To make the flux $J_A$ point in the correct (negative) direction whenever the gradient is positive, we insert a **minus sign**. This is a *convention*, not a fitted parameter.

## 2.3 The Bulk Flow Correction (Key KTU Concept)

In a **real** diffusion problem, the total molar flux $N_A$ of A is the sum of the **diffusive flux** $J_A$ and a **convective (bulk) flux** due to the bulk molar average velocity $u_M$:

$$N_A = J_A + C_A \, u_M$$

A mass balance on a binary mixture gives the key **KTU-favorite** relation:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)$$

where $C$ is the total molar concentration. This equation is the **workhorse** for nearly all steady-state binary diffusion problems at the KTU level.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below consolidates **every equation** you are expected to memorize for Module 1 problems. Note: $\vert$ symbols are written using `\vert` to protect the markdown table.

| # | Governing Situation | Integrated Molar Flux Equation | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fick's First Law (Differential)** | $J_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Diffusive flux only |
| 2 | **Fick's Second Law (PDE)** | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | Unsteady, no reaction |
| 3 | **Steady-State Diffusion of A through stagnant B (Unimolecular)** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C}{L}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right) = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C_{avg}}{L}\,(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$ | $N_B = 0$ at the stagnant boundary |
| 4 | **Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{L}\,(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | $N_A = -N_B$, bulk flow = 0 |
| 5 | **Diffusion in Terms of Partial Pressures (ideal gas)** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,L}\,(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$ | $C = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ substituted |
| 6 | **General Binary Diffusion (bulk flow included)** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C}{L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)$ with $N_B \neq 0$ | Stagnant-B case is a special instance |
| 7 | **Definition of molar average velocity** | $u_M = \dfrac{\sum_i C_i u_i}{C}$ | Used in bulk-flow correction |
| 8 | **Diffusivity via Chapman–Enskog (gas)** | $D_{AB} = 0.001858\,T^{3/2}\,\dfrac{\sqrt{(M_A^{-1} + M_B^{-1})}}{P\,\sigma_{AB}^2\,\Omega_{D,AB}}$ | Used at KTU for gas-diffusivity numericals |

**Units to remember (will fetch easy marks in Part A):**
- $D_{AB}$ → **m²/s** (always, irrespective of system)
- $J_A, N_A$ → **kmol/(m²·s)**
- $C_A$ → **kmol/m³**
- $L$ (diffusion path length) → **m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Fick's Law is the foundation for designing **gas absorbers** (Module 2), **distillation trays** (Module 3), **dryers** (Module 4), **membrane separators**, and **catalytic reactors** (heterogeneous systems). Every mass-transfer equipment design eventually traces back to this law.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Distillation columns:** Fick's law governs vapour–liquid mass transfer across the diffusion boundary layer.
- **Packaged food / pharmaceutical packaging:** Permeation of O₂ or moisture through polymer films is modeled by Fickian diffusion.
- **CO₂ sequestration:** Diffusion of CO₂ through porous rock or brine.
- **Drying of solids:** Moisture migrates from inside a solid to the surface — Fick's second law with a moving boundary.
- **Semiconductor doping:** Dopant atoms diffuse into silicon wafers under Fick's law.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Unimolecular Diffusion Equation (Diffusion of A through Stagnant B)

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU Module 1. We will derive $N_A$ for the case where A diffuses through a stagnant layer of B (e.g., ammonia evaporating into quiescent air).

**Starting point — General binary diffusion with bulk flow:**

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C} (N_A + N_B) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the stagnant-B condition.** By definition of the stagnant film, **B does not move across the boundary**, hence $N_B = 0$ everywhere. Substitute into (i):

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C} (N_A + 0)$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the bulk-flow term.** Rearrange to gather the $N_A$ terms on the left:

$$N_A - \frac{C_A}{C} N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

$$N_A \left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Note that $1 - \dfrac{C_A}{C} = \dfrac{C - C_A}{C} = \dfrac{C_B}{C}$, so:

$$N_A \cdot \frac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3 — Express as a function of mole fraction.** For an ideal gas, $C_A = y_A C$ and $C_B = y_B C = (1 - y_A) C$. The total concentration $C$ is constant for an isothermal, isobaric ideal gas. So:

$$N_A \cdot (1 - y_A) = -D_{AB} \, C \, \frac{dy_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables.** $N_A$ is constant in steady state, $D_{AB}$ and $C$ are constant. Separate:

$$N_A \, dz = -D_{AB} \, C \, \frac{dy_A}{1 - y_A}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate across the film of thickness $L$.** Boundary conditions: at $z = 0$, $y_A = y_{A1}$ (gas-phase side, high concentration); at $z = L$, $y_A = y_{A2}$ (bulk stagnant side, low concentration).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A \int_{0}^{L} dz &= -D_{AB}\, C \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} \frac{dy_A}{1 - y_A} \\
N_A \, L &= -D_{AB}\, C \Big[ -\ln(1 - y_A) \Big]_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} \\
N_A \, L &= D_{AB}\, C \Big[ \ln(1 - y_{A2}) - \ln(1 - y_{A1}) \Big] \\
N_A \, L &= D_{AB}\, C \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final integrated form (Equation A):**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\, C}{L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Linear-driving-force form (Equation B).** Using the identity $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right) = \dfrac{(y_{A1} - y_{A2})(1 - y_A)_{lm}}{L}$ is not needed; instead, define a **log-mean concentration of B**:

$$C_{B,lm} = \frac{C_{B2} - C_{B1}}{\ln(C_{B2}/C_{B1})}$$

This gives the often-quoted equivalent linear form:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{L} (C_{A1} - C_{A2}) \cdot \frac{C}{C_{B,lm}}$$

> The factor $\dfrac{C}{C_{B,lm}}$ is called the **"drift factor"** or **"ideality correction"** and equals $\dfrac{1}{y_{B,lm}}$. When $y_A$ is dilute (small), this factor $\to 1$, and the equation collapses to Fick's first law with $N_A \approx -D_{AB}\,\Delta C_A / L$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)

In EMCD, both species move in opposite directions at equal molar rates: $N_A = -N_B$. There is **no net bulk flow**, so $u_M = 0$ and $J_A = N_A$.

**Step 1 — Start with Fick's first law:**

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate with $D_{AB}$ constant and $C$ constant (ideal gas):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A \int_0^L dz &= -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} dC_A \\
N_A \, L &= -D_{AB} (C_{A2} - C_{A1}) \\
N_A \, L &= D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final EMCD form:**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{L} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$$

This is **Fick's first law written in integrated form** — the simplest and most fundamental result. It applies to systems like **distillation** (vapour and liquid phases counter-diffusing in equimolar amounts at constant molar overflow).

## 3.3 Derivation of Fick's Second Law (Unsteady-State, 1-D)

We start by performing a **molar balance on a differential element** of thickness $dz$ and unit cross-sectional area.

**Step 1 — Molar balance.** Rate in − Rate out + Rate of generation = Rate of accumulation. With no generation:

$$N_{A,z}\bigvert_{z} - N_{A,z}\bigvert_{z+dz} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dz$$

**Step 2 — Taylor expansion of the outflow term:**

$$N_{A,z}\bigvert_{z+dz} = N_{A,z}\bigvert_{z} + \frac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} dz$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$N_{A,z} - \left(N_{A,z} + \frac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} dz\right) = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dz$$

$$-\frac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} dz = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dz$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Fick's first law $N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}$ (assuming bulk flow is negligible):**

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(-D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right) = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

**Final result (Fick's Second Law):**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\;}$$

For 3-D anisotropic diffusion in cartesian coordinates:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \left( \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} \right)$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — The Logic of Steady-State Binary Diffusion Problems

The following Mermaid block maps out the **decision tree** you must follow when solving any steady-state binary diffusion numerical. This is the *exact* sequence examiners expect you to write on the answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Binary Diffusion Problem] --> B{Is component B stagnant?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Stagnant B Equation]
    B -- No --> D{Does NA = -NB hold?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Equimolar Counter Diffusion]
    D -- No --> F[Use General Binary Equation with both NA and NB]
    C --> G{Need flux in yA or PA form?}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- In mole fraction --> H[NA = DAB * C / L * ln of 1-yA2 over 1-yA1]
    G -- In partial pressure --> I[NA = DAB * P / RTL * pA1 - pA2]
    G -- In concentration --> J[NA = DAB over L * CA1 - CA2 for dilute]
    H --> K[Compute DAB using Chapman Enskog if needed]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Report NA with units kmol per m2 s]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Diffusion Through a Stagnant Film

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FilmLayer[Stagnant Gas Film of Thickness L]
        direction LR
        Z0[At z equals 0: Interface with high CA, high yA1] --> Zmid[At z equals L over 2: Bulk average concentration] --> ZL[At z equals L: Bulk with low CA, low yA2]
    end
    Z0 -- Molar flux NA of A --> ZL
    Z0 -. Component B is stagnant NB equals 0 .-> ZL
    Z0 --> P1[Concentration Profile CA vs z: Exponential decay]
    Zmid --> P1
    ZL --> P1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — How Fick's Law Fits into the KTU Mass-Transfer Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Physical Driving Force: Concentration Gradient dCA over dz] --> FK1[Ficks First Law J equals minus DAB dCA over dz]
    FK1 --> GEN[Generalised Binary Form NA equals JD plus bulk flow term]
    GEN --> SS[Steady State Cases]
    GEN --> US[Unsteady State Cases]
    SS --> EMCD[Equimolar Counter Diffusion NA equals minus NB]
    SS --> SB[Stagnant B Film Diffusion NA nonzero, NB zero]
    US --> FK2[Ficks Second Law PDE partial CA over partial t equals DAB times Laplacian of CA]
    FK2 --> SLAB[Slab Geometry Solution error function]
    FK2 --> CYL[Cylindrical Geometry]
    FK2 --> SPH[Spherical Geometry]
    FK1 --> APP[Engineering Applications]
    GEN --> APP
    APP --> AB[Gas Absorption Module 2]
    APP --> DIS[Distillation Module 3]
    APP --> DRY[Drying Module 4]
    APP --> MEM[Membrane Separations]
```

## 4.4 Concentration-Profile Diagram (Schematic Description)

In a **stagnant-B** situation, the plot of $y_A$ versus $z$ is **not linear** — it is curved (logarithmic) because the bulk flow is non-zero. In **EMCD**, the plot of $C_A$ versus $z$ **is linear** because there is no bulk flow correction.

| Scenario | Profile Shape | Mathematical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| EMCD | **Linear** | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{L}\Delta C_A$, gradient is constant |
| Stagnant B | **Curved (concave-up for A)** | $N_A$ is constant but bulk flow varies with $y_A$ |
| Stagnant B (B plotted) | **Curved (concave-down for B)** | Because $y_B = 1 - y_A$ |
| Unsteady-state | **Time-dependent family of curves** | Governed by Fick's second law PDE |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Explain the significance of the negative sign and the units of the molecular diffusivity $D_{AB}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fick's First Law states that the molar diffusive flux of a component A is directly proportional to its concentration gradient:

$$J_{A,z} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

- **Significance of the negative sign (1 mark):** The negative sign indicates that diffusion of A occurs in the direction of **decreasing** concentration, i.e., from a region of high $C_A$ to a region of low $C_A$. It makes the flux vector point opposite to the gradient vector.
- **Units of $D_{AB}$ (1 mark):** From the equation, $[D_{AB}] = \dfrac{[J_A]}{[C_A/L]} = \dfrac{\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}{\text{kmol/m}^4} = \textbf{m}^2/\textbf{s}$. Diffusivity has the same units as thermal diffusivity and kinematic viscosity.
- **Physical meaning (1 mark):** A larger $D_{AB}$ means molecules of A spread more rapidly through B. Typical orders: gases $\sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s, liquids $\sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s, solids $\sim 10^{-12}$ m²/s.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Distinguish between equimolar counter-diffusion and diffusion of A through stagnant B. State the integrated form of Fick's law in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) | Stagnant B Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $N_A = -N_B$ | $N_B = 0$, only A diffuses |
| Bulk flow | Zero ($u_M = 0$) | Non-zero (parallel to $N_A$) |
| Profile shape | Linear | Curved (logarithmic) |
| Integrated form | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{L}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C}{L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)$ |
| Real example | Distillation (constant molar overflow) | Sublimation of naphthalene into air |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU Exam Pattern Notice]** Each Part-B question must be answered for **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Choose **either** Option A **or** Option B.

---

### Option A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]**
**Derive the integrated form of Fick's first law for the steady-state diffusion of component A through a stagnant gas film of B. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **State the general binary diffusion equation with bulk flow** (1 mark):

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)$$

2. **Apply stagnant-B condition $N_B = 0$** (1 mark):

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C} N_A$$

3. **Rearrange to isolate $N_A$** (1 mark):

$$N_A\left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_A \frac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

4. **Convert to mole fractions $C_A = y_A C$, $C_B = (1-y_A)C$** (1 mark):

$$N_A (1 - y_A) = -D_{AB}\, C\, \frac{dy_A}{dz}$$

5. **Separate variables and integrate from $z=0$ ($y_{A1}$) to $z=L$ ($y_{A2}$)** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A \int_0^L dz &= -D_{AB}\, C \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} \frac{dy_A}{1-y_A} \\
N_A \, L &= D_{AB}\, C \ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

6. **Final result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\, C}{L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)\,}$$

---

#### Q.A(b) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Ammonia (A) is diffusing through a stagnant film of air (B) of thickness 2 mm. The total pressure is 1 atm and the temperature is 25 °C. The mole fraction of ammonia at the gas–liquid interface is 0.10 and at the other end of the film is 0.02. The diffusivity of NH₃ in air at this condition is $1.96 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate: (i) the molar flux of NH₃, and (ii) the molar flux of air. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $P = 1$ atm $= 101325$ Pa
- $T = 25$ °C $= 298$ K
- $y_{A1} = 0.10$, $y_{A2} = 0.02$
- $D_{AB} = 1.96 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate total molar concentration $C$** (1 mark):

$$C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8314 \times 298} = \frac{101325}{2477572} = 0.0409 \; \text{kmol/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply stagnant-B equation for $N_A$** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= \frac{D_{AB}\, C}{L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right) \\
&= \frac{(1.96 \times 10^{-5})(0.0409)}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - 0.02}{1 - 0.10}\right) \\
&= \frac{8.016 \times 10^{-7}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.98}{0.90}\right) \\
&= 4.008 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln(1.0889) \\
&= 4.008 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.08520 \\
&= 3.415 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Molar flux of air $N_B$** (1 mark): By the **stagnant-B definition**, $N_B = 0$. Hence:

$$N_B = 0 \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Step 4 — Drift factor and physical interpretation** (1 mark): The drift factor $\dfrac{C}{C_{B,lm}} = \dfrac{1}{y_{B,lm}}$ is greater than 1, meaning bulk flow **augments** the diffusion of A beyond what Fick's first law alone would predict.

**Step 5 — Express in standard units (optional)** (1 mark):

$$N_A = 3.415 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} = 3.415 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

> **Final answer:** $N_A \approx 3.42 \times 10^{-5}$ kmol/(m²·s); $N_B = 0$.

---

### Option B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Q.B(a) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**Starting from the molar balance on a differential element, derive Fick's second law of diffusion in one Cartesian direction. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Binary system (A + B).
2. Constant $D_{AB}$ (independent of concentration).
3. No chemical reaction inside the element.
4. Isothermal, isobaric, no bulk flow.
5. One-dimensional diffusion (z-direction).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

1. **Molar balance on element of thickness $dz$, unit area** (1 mark):

$$N_{A,z}\big\vert_z - N_{A,z}\big\vert_{z+dz} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dz$$

2. **Taylor expansion** (1 mark):

$$N_{A,z}\big\vert_{z+dz} = N_{A,z}\big\vert_z + \frac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} dz$$

3. **Substitute and simplify** (1 mark):

$$-\frac{\partial N_{A,z}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}$$

4. **Insert Fick's first law $N_{A,z} = -D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}$** (1 mark):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(-D_{AB}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right)$$

5. **Take $D_{AB}$ outside the derivative (constant)** (1 mark):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\;}$$

6. **State the analogy with heat conduction** (1 mark): This is the **mass-transfer analog** of the transient heat conduction equation $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha\dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$, with $D_{AB}$ playing the role of thermal diffusivity $\alpha$. This is the foundation for solving slab/cylinder/sphere transient diffusion problems (Module 4/5).

---

#### Q.B(b) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**
**In a steady-state equimolar counter-diffusion of helium (A) and nitrogen (B) at 1 atm and 25 °C, the partial pressure of helium varies from $p_{A1} = 20$ kPa at one end of a 5 mm tube to $p_{A2} = 5$ kPa at the other. The binary diffusivity $D_{AB} = 7.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux of helium and the molar flux of nitrogen. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the case (1 mark):** $N_A = -N_B$ implies **equimolar counter-diffusion**. Integrated form (in partial pressures):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,L}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (2 marks):**
- $P = 101.325$ kPa (since partial pressures are in kPa, use $P$ in kPa and $R = 8.314$ kPa·m³/(kmol·K))
- $T = 298$ K, $L = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\Delta p_A = 20 - 5 = 15$ kPa

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= \frac{(7.2 \times 10^{-5})(101.325)}{(8.314)(298)(5 \times 10^{-3})}(15) \\
&= \frac{7.2954 \times 10^{-3}}{12.388} \times 15 \\
&= 5.889 \times 10^{-4} \times 15 \\
&= 8.83 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Molar flux of nitrogen (1 mark):**

$$N_B = -N_A = -8.83 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

The negative sign indicates $\mathrm{N_2}$ diffuses in the opposite direction to He.

**Step 4 — Verification (1 mark):** Bulk molar average velocity $u_M = 0$ (since $N_A + N_B = 0$). Hence $N_A = J_A$, and the simple Fick's first law form is sufficient.

**Step 5 — Check units and final answer (1 mark):**

$$N_A = 8.83 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} = 8.83 \; \text{mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

> **Final answer:** $N_A = 8.83 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/(m²·s) in the direction of decreasing $p_A$; $N_B = -8.83 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/(m²·s).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the bulk-flow term** in the general binary equation. The single largest reason for losing 3–4 marks in Part B derivations. Always start from $N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + \dfrac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)$, then apply the appropriate condition.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in partial-pressure form.** When using $p_A$ in kPa, $P$ must also be in kPa, and $R = 8.314$ kPa·m³/(kmol·K). Mixing Pa with kPa is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing $J_A$ and $N_A$.** $J_A$ is the *diffusive* flux relative to molar average velocity. $N_A$ is the *total* flux relative to stationary coordinates. They are equal only when bulk flow is zero (EMCD).
> 4. **Skipping the units of $D_{AB}$** in the "State Fick's law" question. Examiners allocate ½ to 1 mark specifically for stating the units as **m²/s** and explaining that $D_{AB}$ is always positive.
> 5. **Using the EMCD formula in a stagnant-B problem (or vice versa).** Read the question carefully — look for keywords like *equimolar*, *stagnant*, *pure A evaporating into B*, *diffusion of A through stagnant B*.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions. KTU examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for listing assumptions before deriving Fick's second law.
> 7. **Forgetting $N_B = 0$ in stagnant-B problems.** This is *the* defining condition; omitting it shows a lack of conceptual clarity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fick's First Law (steady-state):** $J_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ — the **negative sign** is convention; $D_{AB}$ has units of **m²/s**; the law is the **mass-transfer analog** of Fourier's law.
- **Fick's Second Law (unsteady-state):** $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ — derived via a molar balance on a differential element, assuming constant $D_{AB}$ and no reaction.
- **General binary diffusion (with bulk flow):** $N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + \dfrac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)$ — the **master equation** for Module 1.
- **Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD):** $N_A = -N_B \Rightarrow N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{L}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ — linear concentration profile; no bulk flow.
- **Stagnant B diffusion:** $N_B = 0 \Rightarrow N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}C}{L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}\right)$ — non-linear (logarithmic) profile; bulk flow is in the **same direction** as $N_A$, augmenting it.
- **Drift factor** $\dfrac{C}{C_{B,lm}} > 1$ accounts for the bulk-flow contribution; it tends to 1 for dilute mixtures.
- **Diffusivity orders of magnitude:** gases $\sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s, liquids $\sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s, solids $\sim 10^{-12}$ m²/s.
- **Chapman–Enskog equation** gives $D_{AB}$ for binary gas mixtures using collision diameter $\sigma$ and collision integral $\Omega_{D,AB}$.
- **Always** state assumptions in derivation questions, **always** include units in numerical answers, and **always** specify whether you are using $J_A$ (diffusive) or $N_A$ (total) flux.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusivity and Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diffusivity and Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Mass Transfer Operations, **Diffusivity (D)** — also called the **mass diffusion coefficient** or **diffusion coefficient** — is defined as the proportionality constant that relates the **molar flux of a species** to its **concentration gradient** in a mixture, as expressed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**.

For a binary mixture of components **A** (diffusing species) and **B** (stationary or co-diffusing), the steady-state one-dimensional molar flux is:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where:

- $N_A$ = molar flux of component **A** in the **z-direction** (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ = binary diffusivity of **A in B** (m²/s)
- $C_A$ = molar concentration of **A** (kmol/m³)
- $z$ = direction of diffusion (m)
- The negative sign indicates transport occurs **from high to low concentration** (down the gradient).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Diffusivity is a transport property — analogous to thermal conductivity in heat transfer and viscosity in momentum transfer. It is **NOT** a thermodynamic property; it is a **kinetic** property.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you drop a single drop of red ink into a still glass of water. At first, the ink forms a sharp red streak. Over the next several minutes, the streak slowly spreads — the red color migrates from where the ink is concentrated (high $C_A$) toward regions of clean water (low $C_A$). This spreading continues until the entire glass turns a uniform pale pink.

This natural, spontaneous migration of molecules from high to low concentration is **molecular diffusion**. The **diffusivity** is essentially a measure of *how fast* this spreading occurs. A high $D$ (like ammonia in air, ~$2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s) means fast spreading; a low $D$ (like a heavy protein in water, ~$10^{-11}$ m²/s) means extremely slow spreading.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of $D$ as the "speed limit" for molecular migration. Just as temperature measures how fast molecules jiggle, diffusivity measures how fast they redistribute in space.

### 1.3 Standard Units & Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|
| Diffusivity (gas) | $D_{AB}$ | **m²/s** (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$) |
| Diffusivity (liquid) | $D_{AB}^0$ | **m²/s** (typically $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$) |
| Diffusivity (solid) | $D_{AB}$ | **m²/s** (typically $10^{-13}$ to $10^{-14}$) |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8314 J/(kmol·K)** |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Diffusivity

Diffusivity depends on the physical state of the medium:

1. **Gas-phase diffusivity ($D_{AB}^G$)** — driven by random molecular motion; relatively high values.
2. **Liquid-phase diffusivity ($D_{AB}^0$)** — molecules must push through dense, viscous fluid; values are ~**10,000 times lower** than gases.
3. **Solid-phase diffusivity** — extremely slow; occurs via vacancies or interstitials.

### 2.2 Fick's Two Laws of Diffusion

**Fick's First Law (steady state):**

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Fick's Second Law (unsteady state, constant $D$):**

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For binary systems with **equimolar counter-diffusion** ($N_A = -N_B$), Fick's First Law simplifies to:
>
> $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$
>
> This is the most commonly applied form in **distillation** problems.

### 2.3 Effect of Temperature & Pressure on Diffusivity

| Factor | Effect on Gas Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ | Effect on Liquid Diffusivity $D_{AB}^0$ |
|--------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Temperature ↑** | Increases $\propto T^{3/2}$ | Increases $\propto T/\mu_B$ |
| **Pressure ↑** | Decreases $\propto 1/P$ | Negligible effect |
| **Molecular size ↑** | Decreases | Decreases |
| **Viscosity of medium ↑** | Not directly applicable | Decreases strongly |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for estimating diffusivity. **Memorize the conditions** under which each is applicable — this is a frequent KTU exam trap.

| # | Equation Name | Governing Equation | Applicability | Key Parameters |
|---|---------------|---------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Fick's First Law | $N_A = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Binary diffusion (general) | $N_A, D_{AB}, C_A, z$ |
| 2 | Fick's Second Law | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | Unsteady diffusion | $t, D_{AB}, z$ |
| 3 | Chapman-Enskog | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2}}{P \, M_{AB}^{1/2} \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}$ | **Non-polar gases** at low P | $T, P, M_{AB}, \sigma_{AB}, \Omega_D$ |
| 4 | Fuller-Schettler-Giddings | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{0.001 \, T^{1.75}}{P \, \left[ \left( \Sigma v_A \right)^{1/3} + \left( \Sigma v_B \right)^{1/3} \right]^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B}}$ | **Polar & non-polar gases** | $\Sigma v$ = atomic diffusion volumes |
| 5 | Wilke-Chang | $D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \dfrac{(\phi \, M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$ | **Dilute liquid (A in B)** | $\phi, M_B, \mu_B, V_A$ |
| 6 | Stokes-Einstein | $D_{AB}^0 = \dfrac{k_B \, T}{6 \pi \, \mu_B \, r_A}$ | **Large spherical molecules** | $k_B, r_A, \mu_B$ |
| 7 | Temperature scaling | $D_2 = D_1 \left( \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^{3/2}$ | Gas diffusivity at 2 temperatures | $D_1, D_2, T_1, T_2$ |
| 8 | Pressure scaling | $D_2 = D_1 \left( \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \right)$ | Gas diffusivity at 2 pressures | $D_1, D_2, P_1, P_2$ |
| 9 | Mean Molecular Weight | $M_{AB} = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B}}$ | Chapman-Enskog | $M_A, M_B$ |
| 10 | Combined T & P scaling | $\dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \left( \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^{3/2} \left( \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \right)$ | Gas state change | All four variables |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Distillation Columns** | Calculating mass transfer rates between vapor and liquid phases |
| **Gas Absorption (Scrubbers)** | Designing $\text{SO}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$ removal columns |
| **Drying Operations** | Predicting moisture migration within solid matrices |
| **Membrane Separations** | Determining permeability and selectivity |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Drug release from polymer matrices |
| **Petroleum Refining** | Catalyst design and reaction-diffusion modeling |
| **Environmental Engineering** | Atmospheric pollutant dispersion modeling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fick's First Law of Diffusion from Kinetic Theory

Consider a binary gas mixture of A and B at uniform temperature and pressure. Component A diffuses through stagnant B in the z-direction. Let us place a reference plane at $z = 0$ and consider molecules crossing this plane from above ($z > 0$) and below ($z < 0$).

**Step 1: Mean free path.** The average distance a molecule travels between successive collisions is:

$$\lambda_A = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d_m^2 \, n}$$

where $d_m$ is the molecular diameter and $n$ is the total number density of molecules. Molecules that cross the reference plane originated, on average, from one mean free path away.

**Step 2: Number of molecules crossing per unit area per unit time.** By the kinetic theory of gases, the molecular flux across a plane is:

$$\dot{n} = \frac{1}{4} n \, \bar{c}$$

where $\bar{c} = \sqrt{8 k_B T / (\pi m)}$ is the mean molecular speed.

**Step 3: Net flux with concentration gradient.** Let $n_A(z = -\lambda) = n_A(0) - \lambda \, (dn_A/dz)$ and $n_A(z = +\lambda) = n_A(0) + \lambda \, (dn_A/dz)$.

The net flux of A in the +z direction (from $-\lambda$ to $+\lambda$ minus the reverse):

$$N_A = \frac{1}{4} \bar{c} \left[ n_A(-\lambda) - n_A(+\lambda) \right]$$

**Step 4: Subtract.**

$$N_A = \frac{1}{4} \bar{c} \left[ \left( n_A(0) - \lambda \frac{dn_A}{dz} \right) - \left( n_A(0) + \lambda \frac{dn_A}{dz} \right) \right]$$

$$N_A = \frac{1}{4} \bar{c} \left( -2\lambda \frac{dn_A}{dz} \right)$$

$$N_A = -\frac{1}{2} \bar{c} \, \lambda \frac{dn_A}{dz}$$

**Step 5: Identify the diffusivity.** The coefficient of $-dn_A/dz$ is the diffusivity:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1}{2} \bar{c} \, \lambda$$

**Step 6: Convert to molar units** (divide by Avogadro's number for molar concentration):

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**. The negative sign confirms that molecular diffusion always proceeds **from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration** — the Second Law of Thermodynamics in action (entropy increase).

---

### 3.2 Detailed Expansion: Chapman-Enskog Equation

The most theoretically rigorous equation for binary gas-phase diffusivity is derived from the **Boltzmann equation** using the Chapman-Enskog solution. The engineering form is:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2}}{P \, M_{AB}^{1/2} \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D}$$

**Parameter-by-parameter explanation:**

**Step 1 — Reduced Temperature.** The dimensionless collision integral $\Omega_D$ is a function of:

$$\Omega_D = f\left( \frac{k_B T}{\varepsilon_{AB}} \right) = f(T^*)$$

where $T^* = k_B T / \varepsilon_{AB}$ is the **reduced temperature** and $\varepsilon_{AB}$ is the depth of the Lennard-Jones potential well.

**Step 2 — Lennard-Jones Parameters.** The characteristic molecular parameters are obtained from:

$$\sigma_{AB} = \frac{\sigma_A + \sigma_B}{2}, \quad \varepsilon_{AB} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_A \varepsilon_B}$$

Using the empirical correlations:

$$\sigma = 1.18 \, V_b^{1/3}, \quad \frac{\varepsilon}{k_B} = 1.15 \, T_b$$

where $V_b$ is the molar volume at the normal boiling point (cm³/mol) and $T_b$ is the normal boiling point (K).

**Step 3 — Mean Molecular Weight.** The combined molecular weight is:

$$M_{AB} = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B}}$$

**Step 4 — Units check.**
- $T$ in **K**, $P$ in **atm**, $M_{AB}$ in **kg/kmol**, $\sigma_{AB}$ in **Å** (Angstroms)
- Result: $D_{AB}$ in **cm²/s**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students mistakenly use $P$ in Pa or $T$ in °C. Always convert to **atm** and **K** before substituting into Chapman-Enskog.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Expansion: Wilke-Chang Equation (Liquid Phase)

For dilute non-electrolyte liquid solutions, the most widely used correlation is the **Wilke-Chang equation**:

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \frac{(\phi \, M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$$

**Step 1 — Units.**
- $D_{AB}^0$ in **cm²/s**, $T$ in **K**, $\mu_B$ in **cP** (centipoise), $V_A$ in **cm³/mol**

**Step 2 — Solvent Association Factor ($\phi$).**

| Solvent B | $\phi$ value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Water | **2.6** |
| Methanol | **1.9** |
| Ethanol | **1.5** |
| Benzene, ether, heptane | **1.0** |
| Other unassociated solvents | **1.0** |

**Step 3 — Molar Volume of Solute A at Normal Boiling Point ($V_A$).** Calculate by **Le Bas additive method** — sum of atomic volumes:

| Atom | Increment (cm³/mol) |
|------|---------------------|
| C | **14.8** |
| H | **3.7** |
| O (in –OH) | **7.4** |
| O (in –O–) | **7.4** (less ring closure adjustment) |
| N (doubly bonded) | **15.6** |
| Cl | **24.6** |

**Step 4 — Modification by Tyn-Calus.** For more accurate modern estimates, replace the exponent 0.6 with:

$$D_{AB}^0 = 8.93 \times 10^{-8} \frac{(\phi \, M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B^{0.45} \, V_{A}^{0.265}}$$

This is particularly useful for solutes with very high molar volumes.

**Worked Example (illustrative for clarity):**

Estimate $D_{AB}^0$ for **acetone (A) in water (B)** at **25 °C** using Wilke-Chang.

Given: $\mu_B (H_2O, 25°C) = 0.894$ cP, $V_A (acetone) = 74$ cm³/mol, $M_B (water) = 18.02$ kg/kmol, $\phi = 2.6$, $T = 298.15$ K.

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \frac{(2.6 \times 18.02)^{0.5} \times 298.15}{0.894 \times 74^{0.6}}$$

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \frac{(46.85)^{0.5} \times 298.15}{0.894 \times 13.05}$$

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \frac{6.844 \times 298.15}{11.667}$$

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 174.95 = 1.295 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{D_{AB}^0 \approx 1.30 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 1.30 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

This agrees with the experimentally reported value of $1.28 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s — error < 2%.

---

### 3.4 Combined Gas Diffusivity Scaling

If $D_1$ is known at $(T_1, P_1)$ and $D_2$ is required at $(T_2, P_2)$:

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_2}$$

**Derivation logic:** From Chapman-Enskog, $D \propto T^{3/2}/P$. Holding all other terms constant, the ratio follows directly. This is **exam-favorite** for 3-mark questions.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Equation

| Scenario | Best Equation | Reason |
|----------|--------------|--------|
| Non-polar gas pair (H₂–N₂, O₂–N₂) | **Chapman-Enskog** | Most rigorous; based on kinetic theory |
| Polar gas pair (H₂O–air, NH₃–air) | **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings** | Better accuracy for polar molecules |
| Dilute liquid solute, non-electrolyte | **Wilke-Chang** | Industry standard |
| Large spherical molecules in liquid | **Stokes-Einstein** | Simplest hydrodynamic basis |
| Quick estimate without detailed data | **Fuller (gases)** | Requires only atomic volumes |
| Correcting $D$ for new T or P | **Scaling equations** | Avoids re-running full estimation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic: Molecular Diffusion Mechanism

The following block diagram maps the conceptual flow of how molecular diffusion physically occurs in a binary mixture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GAS_PHASE["GAS PHASE BINARY DIFFUSION"]
        A1["Bulk region rich in A\nHigh concentration"] --> B1["Random molecular motion\nMean free path lambda"]
        B1 --> C1["Net flux down gradient\nFick First Law applies"]
        C1 --> D1["Equilibrium achieved\nUniform composition"]
    end

    subgraph LIQ_PHASE["LIQUID PHASE BINARY DIFFUSION"]
        A2["Solute A molecules\nDispersed in solvent B"] --> B2["Collisions with dense B\nSlow migration"]
        B2 --> C2["Viscous resistance dominates\nStokes Einstein applies"]
        C2 --> D2["Concentration equilibrium\nSlow process"]
    end

    A1 -.->|"Order of 10000 times\nfaster than liquid"| A2

    style A1 fill:#FFD580
    style D1 fill:#80FF80
    style A2 fill:#80C0FF
    style D2 fill:#C080FF
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Diffusivity Estimation Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Identify phase of diffusion system\nGas or Liquid"] --> DECIDE{"Which phase?"}

    DECIDE -->|"Gas Phase"| GAS_PATH["Determine molecular data\nM_A M_B sigma epsilon"]
    GAS_PATH --> GAS_POLAR{"Polar molecules?"}
    GAS_POLAR -->|"No Non polar"| EQ1["Chapman Enskog equation\nMost rigorous"]
    GAS_POLAR -->|"Yes Polar"| EQ2["Fuller Schettler Giddings\nUses atomic volumes"]

    DECIDE -->|"Liquid Phase"| LIQ_PATH["Determine solvent viscosity\nmolar volume association"]
    LIQ_PATH --> LIQ_SIZE{"Spherical large\nmolecules?"}
    LIQ_SIZE -->|"Yes Large"| EQ3["Stokes Einstein equation\nHydrodynamic basis"]
    LIQ_SIZE -->|"No General"| EQ4["Wilke Chang equation\nDilute solution"]

    EQ1 --> RESULT["Compute D_AB\nwith correct units"]
    EQ2 --> RESULT
    EQ3 --> RESULT
    EQ4 --> RESULT

    RESULT --> VALIDATE{"Compare with\nexperimental data"}
    VALIDATE -->|"Error less than 10 percent"| OK["Accepted estimate"]
    VALIDATE -->|"Error greater than 10 percent"| REVISE["Use Tyn Calus\nor alternate method"]

    style START fill:#FFE0B0
    style RESULT fill:#80FF80
    style OK fill:#80FF80
    style REVISE fill:#FF8080
```

### 4.3 Relationship Map: Diffusivity Versus Other Transport Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRANSPORT["ANALOGY OF TRANSPORT PROPERTIES"]
        MOMENTUM["Momentum Transfer\nmu = viscosity\nkg per m s"]
        HEAT["Heat Transfer\nk = thermal conductivity\nW per m K"]
        MASS["Mass Transfer\nD_AB = diffusivity\nm squared per s"]
    end

    MOMENTUM -->|"Newton law\ntau equals minus mu dv dz"| LAW1["Proportional to gradient"]
    HEAT -->|"Fourier law\nq equals minus k dT dz"| LAW1
    MASS -->|"Fick law\nN_A equals minus D dC dz"| LAW1

    MOMENTUM -.->|"Same order\nof magnitude"| MASS
    HEAT -.->|"Similar order\nfor gases"| MASS

    style LAW1 fill:#FFD580
    style MASS fill:#80C0FF
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: Combined T and P Scaling Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Known D_1 at T_1 P_1"] --> B{"New operating\nconditions T_2 P_2"}
    B --> C["Compute ratio\nT_2 by T_1 raised to 3/2"]
    B --> D["Compute ratio\nP_1 by P_2"]
    C --> E["Multiply both ratios"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Multiply by D_1\nto obtain D_2"]
    F --> G["Report D_2 in cm squared per s\nor m squared per s"]

    style A fill:#80C0FF
    style G fill:#80FF80
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define diffusivity. State Fick's First Law of Diffusion and explain the significance of the negative sign.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Diffusivity ($D_{AB}$)** is the proportionality constant that relates the molar flux of a diffusing species to its concentration gradient, expressed in units of **m²/s**.

**Fick's First Law:**

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $N_A$ is the molar flux of A (kmol/m²·s), $D_{AB}$ is the binary diffusivity (m²/s), $C_A$ is the concentration of A (kmol/m³), and $z$ is the direction of diffusion.

**Significance of negative sign:** The negative sign indicates that diffusion occurs in the direction of **decreasing concentration** — i.e., molecules spontaneously move from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration. This is a direct manifestation of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, where entropy increases by redistribution.

(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for negative sign explanation)

---

**Q2. State and explain the effect of temperature and pressure on the diffusivity of gases.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Chapman-Enskog equation shows that:

$$D_{AB} \propto \frac{T^{3/2}}{P}$$

**Effect of Temperature:** Diffusivity **increases** with temperature raised to the power of 3/2. As temperature rises, molecules possess higher kinetic energy, move faster (higher $\bar{c}$), and traverse greater distances between collisions. A 10% increase in absolute temperature leads to a ~15% increase in diffusivity.

**Effect of Pressure:** Diffusivity **decreases inversely** with pressure. As pressure rises, the number density of molecules increases, the mean free path decreases, and molecules collide more frequently, slowing net migration.

**Combined effect** for state changes:

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_2}$$

(3 marks — 1 each for T, P, combined formula)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive Fick's First Law of Diffusion from kinetic theory of gases. State the assumptions clearly. **— (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Binary mixture of ideal gases A and B at uniform T and P.
2. Molecules are spherical and undergo elastic collisions.
3. No chemical reaction occurs.
4. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion along the z-axis.
5. The concentration gradient is the only driving force (no thermal diffusion).

**Derivation:**

Consider a reference plane at $z = 0$. Molecules crossing this plane originate, on average, from one mean free path ($\lambda$) away.

**Step 1:** Number flux crossing any plane per unit area per unit time (from kinetic theory):

$$\dot{n} = \frac{1}{4} n \, \bar{c}$$

where $n$ is the total number density and $\bar{c} = \sqrt{8 k_B T / (\pi m)}$ is the mean molecular speed.

**Step 2:** The number density of A at $z = -\lambda$ (just below the plane):

$$n_A(-\lambda) = n_A(0) - \lambda \frac{dn_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3:** The number density of A at $z = +\lambda$ (just above the plane):

$$n_A(+\lambda) = n_A(0) + \lambda \frac{dn_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4:** Net flux of A in the +z direction (molecules arriving from below minus those arriving from above):

$$N_A = \frac{1}{4} \bar{c} \left[ n_A(-\lambda) - n_A(+\lambda) \right]$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$N_A = \frac{1}{4} \bar{c} \left[ -2\lambda \frac{dn_A}{dz} \right] = -\frac{1}{2} \bar{c} \, \lambda \frac{dn_A}{dz}$$

**Step 6:** Define diffusivity:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1}{2} \bar{c} \, \lambda$$

**Step 7:** Convert to molar concentration (divide by Avogadro's number $N_A$):

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

This is Fick's First Law. **(7 marks — 2 for assumptions, 4 for derivation, 1 for final equation)**

---

**(b)** The diffusivity of acetone vapor in air at **298 K and 1 atm** is **$1.05 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s**. Estimate the diffusivity at **320 K and 2 atm**. Comment on the result. **— (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D_1 = 1.05 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s at $T_1 = 298$ K, $P_1 = 1$ atm. Required: $D_2$ at $T_2 = 320$ K, $P_2 = 2$ atm.

**Step 1 — Apply combined scaling equation:**

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \left(\frac{320}{298}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute temperature ratio:**

$$\frac{320}{298} = 1.0738$$

$$(1.0738)^{3/2} = 1.0738 \times \sqrt{1.0738} = 1.0738 \times 1.0363 = 1.1128$$

**Step 4 — Combine with pressure ratio:**

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = 1.1128 \times 0.5 = 0.5564$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $D_2$:**

$$D_2 = 1.05 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5564 = 5.84 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{D_2 \approx 5.84 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Comment:** Although temperature increased (which tends to increase diffusivity), the doubling of pressure reduced the mean free path and dominated the effect. Net result: **diffusivity decreased by ~44%**. Pressure has a stronger influence than temperature in this case.

**(7 marks — 2 formula, 3 calculation, 1 final answer, 1 comment)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Wilke-Chang equation for estimating liquid-phase diffusivity. Discuss the significance of each parameter and the limits of applicability. **— (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Wilke-Chang Equation:**

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \frac{(\phi \, M_B)^{1/2} \, T}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$$

**Parameter-by-parameter significance:**

1. **$D_{AB}^0$ (cm²/s)** — Binary liquid diffusivity of dilute solute A in solvent B.

2. **$\phi$ (dimensionless) — Solvent Association Factor:**
   - Accounts for hydrogen bonding and molecular clustering.
   - For **water**, $\phi = 2.6$ (strong H-bonding).
   - For **methanol**, $\phi = 1.9$.
   - For **non-associated solvents** (benzene, heptane), $\phi = 1.0$.

3. **$M_B$ (kg/kmol or g/mol) — Molecular weight of solvent B:**
   - Heavier solvents slow the solute due to greater inertia.

4. **$T$ (K) — Absolute temperature:**
   - Higher temperature increases kinetic energy and decreases viscosity, both enhancing diffusivity.

5. **$\mu_B$ (cP) — Viscosity of solvent B:**
   - Appears in the denominator — higher viscosity strongly hinders diffusion.

6. **$V_A$ (cm³/mol) — Molar volume of solute A at its normal boiling point:**
   - Estimated via Le Bas additive method.
   - Larger solute molecules experience greater frictional drag.

**Limits of Applicability:**

- Valid only for **dilute solutions** (concentration of A $\ll$ concentration of B).
- **Not applicable** to electrolytes or concentrated solutions.
- Error can be **±10–15%** for typical systems.
- Fails for solutes that associate or dissociate in solution.

**Modification by Tyn-Calus** improves accuracy for high molar volume solutes.

**(7 marks — 1.5 for equation, 4 for parameters, 1.5 for limits)**

---

**(b)** Estimate the liquid-phase diffusivity of **acetic acid (A) in water (B)** at **20 °C** using the Wilke-Chang equation. **Given:** $\mu_B (20°C) = 1.005$ cP, $V_A = 64.1$ cm³/mol, $M_B = 18.02$ kg/kmol, $\phi = 2.6$. **— (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T = 20 + 273 = 293$ K, $\mu_B = 1.005$ cP, $V_A = 64.1$ cm³/mol, $M_B = 18.02$ kg/kmol, $\phi = 2.6$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into Wilke-Chang equation:**

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \frac{(2.6 \times 18.02)^{0.5} \times 293}{1.005 \times (64.1)^{0.6}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\phi \cdot M_B$:**

$$2.6 \times 18.02 = 46.852$$

**Step 3 — Square root:**

$$(46.852)^{0.5} = 6.844$$
