**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$6.844 \times 293 = 2005.3$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $V_A^{0.6}$:**

$$(64.1)^{0.6}$$

Take logarithm: $\ln(64.1) = 4.160$

$$0.6 \times 4.160 = 2.496$$

$$e^{2.496} = 12.13$$

**Step 6 — Denominator:**

$$1.005 \times 12.13 = 12.19$$

**Step 7 — Combine:**

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times \frac{2005.3}{12.19}$$

$$D_{AB}^0 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 164.5$$

$$\boxed{D_{AB}^0 \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 1.22 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Comparison with experiment:** Reported experimental value at 20 °C ≈ $1.19 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s. Error = 2.5% — excellent agreement.

**(7 marks — 2 for substitution, 3 for intermediate calculations, 1 for final answer, 1 for comparison)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (1–2 marks deducted):** The Wilke-Chang equation requires $\mu_B$ in **cP** (centipoise), NOT Pa·s. The Chapman-Enskog equation requires $P$ in **atm** and $T$ in **K**.
> 2. **Forgetting $\phi$ for water:** Many students forget the association factor $\phi = 2.6$ for water, causing errors of ~60%.
> 3. **Confusing molar volumes:** Le Bas additive method uses **atomic increments** summed at the **boiling point**, not at STP. Avoid blindly copying room-temperature densities.
> 4. **Sign of flux:** In Fick's law, the negative sign must be preserved. Numerical problems giving positive flux values mean diffusion is in the negative z-direction.
> 5. **Equimolar vs Unimolar Diffusion:** Ensure you know whether the problem assumes equimolar counter-diffusion ($N_A = -N_B$) or diffusion of A through stagnant B ($N_B = 0$) — the relevant equation differs.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Diffusivity and Estimation**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Diffusivity is a **kinetic transport property**, NOT thermodynamic.
- ☐ Fick's First Law: $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dz$ — flux proportional to negative concentration gradient.
- ☐ Units of $D_{AB}$: **m²/s** (SI), **cm²/s** (CGS).
- ☐ $D_{gas} \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s; $D_{liquid} \sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s; $D_{solid} \sim 10^{-13}$ m²/s.

**Key Equations to Memorize:**
- ☐ **Chapman-Enskog** — for non-polar gases; uses Lennard-Jones parameters.
- ☐ **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings** — for polar/non-polar gases; uses atomic diffusion volumes.
- ☐ **Wilke-Chang** — for dilute liquid solutes; uses solvent association factor.
- ☐ **Stokes-Einstein** — for large spherical molecules in liquid.
- ☐ **Combined T-P scaling:** $D_2 / D_1 = (T_2/T_1)^{3/2} \cdot (P_1/P_2)$.

**Critical Parameters:**
- ☐ Lennard-Jones parameters: $\sigma_{AB} = (\sigma_A + \sigma_B)/2$, $\varepsilon_{AB} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_A \varepsilon_B}$.
- ☐ Mean molecular weight: $M_{AB} = 2 / (1/M_A + 1/M_B)$.
- ☐ Solvent association factor: water = **2.6**, methanol = **1.9**, ethanol = **1.5**, others = **1.0**.
- ☐ Le Bas increments for $V_A$: C = 14.8, H = 3.7, O (in OH) = 7.4, N (double bond) = 15.6, Cl = 24.6.

**Effect of Operating Conditions:**
- ☐ **Temperature ↑** → $D$ ↑ (T^1.5 for gases, T/μ for liquids).
- ☐ **Pressure ↑** → $D$ ↓ (1/P for gases, negligible for liquids).
- ☐ **Viscosity ↑** → $D_{liquid}$ ↓↓ (inverse relation).
- ☐ **Molecular size ↑** → $D$ ↓ (both phases).

**Common Exam Traps:**
- ☐ Always convert $P$ to **atm** before Chapman-Enskog.
- ☐ Always convert $\mu$ to **cP** before Wilke-Chang.
- ☐ Use **absolute temperature (K)** — never °C.
- ☐ Lennard-Jones $\sigma$ is in **Ångstroms** in the standard Chapman-Enskog form.
- ☐ Stokes-Einstein requires **spherical** molecules — not valid for long-chain organics.

**Practical Tip:**
- ☐ When in doubt, prefer **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings** for gases — it requires only **atomic volumes** (tabulated) and gives reliable estimates for both polar and non-polar systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady State Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B and Equimolar Counter Diffusion in Binary Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steady State Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B and Equimolar Counter Diffusion in Binary Gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of Mass Transfer Operations, **steady-state molecular diffusion** refers to the net, time-invariant transport of mass of one chemical species through another at the molecular level, governed strictly by a concentration (or partial pressure) gradient, with no net accumulation occurring at any point within the diffusion path.

For a binary gaseous system comprising species **A** and **B**, the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 1) classifies diffusion analysis under two principal engineering paradigms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paradigm 1 — Steady State Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (also known as "Stefan Diffusion" or "Unidirectional Diffusion"):**
> Species **A** diffuses through a stationary (non-moving) medium of species **B**. The bulk flow of **B** is zero ($N_B = 0$), meaning **B** does not undergo net molar transport, although it may still be present and physically mobile at the molecular scale. Examples: ammonia (A) diffusing through air (B); water vapor (A) diffusing through stagnant air (B) during humidification; benzene (A) evaporating into a still air (B) atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paradigm 2 — Steady State Equimolar Counter Diffusion (EMCD) in Binary Gases:**
> Species **A** and **B** diffuse in exactly opposite directions at equal molar rates ($N_A = -N_B$). There is **no net bulk flow**, and the molar flux of each species is governed purely by its own concentration gradient. Examples: mutual diffusion in a closed binary gas system at constant T and P; opposing diffusion streams in a distillation column where both species evaporate and condense at equal molar rates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

**Analogy 1 — A through Stagnant B (One-Way Traffic on a Crowded Road):**
Imagine a single pedestrian (A) walking through a completely frozen crowd of statues (B). The pedestrian can only move forward because the crowd members cannot move backward. The crowd does occupy space, so the pedestrian's progress is hindered — the more crowded the statue zone, the slower the pedestrian moves. This "crowding effect" is mathematically captured by the **log-mean partial pressure of B** ($p_{BM}$) in the diffusion equation.

**Analogy 2 — Equimolar Counter Diffusion (Two Streams of Ants on a Single Log):**
Picture two parallel lines of ants walking in opposite directions on a narrow log. For every ant walking left-to-right, one ant walks right-to-left. The net flow on the log is zero, but each ant still actively diffuses. There is no "bulk flow" superimposed on the molecular motion.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Temperature:** $T = 298.15 \, \text{K}$
- **Standard Pressure:** $P = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 1 \, \text{atm}$
- **Fick's Law First Diffusivity:** $D_{AB}$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ — Binary gas-phase diffusion coefficient
- **Units of Molar Flux:** $N_A$ in **kmol/(m²·s)** or **mol/(m²·s)**
- **Typical Order of Magnitude of $D_{AB}$ for Gases:** $\sim 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ at standard conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 expects students to derive the molar flux expressions for BOTH cases from first principles using **Fick's Law for Equimolar Counter Diffusion** as the foundation. Board questions frequently ask for derivations and numerical substitutions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Foundation: Fick's Law of Molecular Diffusion

The starting equation for analysis of any binary gas diffusion system is **Fick's First Law for binary mixtures**, modified to include the **convective (bulk flow) term**:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)
$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = Molar flux of species A in the $z$-direction [$\text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$]
- $D_{AB}$ = Binary diffusivity of A in B [$\text{m}^2/\text{s}$]
- $\frac{dC_A}{dz}$ = Molar concentration gradient of A [$\text{kmol/m}^4$]
- $x_A$ = Mole fraction of A in the gas phase [dimensionless]
- $(N_A + N_B)$ = Total molar flux due to bulk flow

The first term $-D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$ represents **molecular diffusion** (the diffusive flux relative to the bulk flow). The second term $x_A(N_A + N_B)$ is the **convective contribution** because the bulk flow carries species A along with it in proportion to its mole fraction.

### 2.2 The Two Engineering Paradigms — Boundary Logic

The general Fick's equation is simplified depending on the constraint imposed on the system:

#### Case I — A Diffusing Through Stagnant B
- **Constraint:** $N_B = 0$ (B is stationary)
- **Bulk flow exists:** $N = N_A + 0 = N_A$
- Substituting into Fick's equation gives:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A N_A
$$

$$
N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

Since $(1 - x_A) = x_B$, this becomes:

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{x_B} \frac{dC_A}{dz} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

#### Case II — Equimolar Counter Diffusion
- **Constraint:** $N_A = -N_B$ (equal and opposite molar fluxes)
- **No bulk flow:** $N = N_A + N_B = 0$
- Substituting into Fick's equation:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (0) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

The convective term vanishes entirely because there is no net bulk motion.

### 2.3 Conversion from Concentration to Partial Pressure

Using the **Ideal Gas Law** for each species:

$$
C_A = \frac{n_A}{V} = \frac{p_A}{RT} \quad ; \quad C_B = \frac{p_B}{RT} \quad ; \quad C_T = \frac{P}{RT}
$$

Mole fraction relations:

$$
x_A = \frac{C_A}{C_T} = \frac{p_A}{P} \quad ; \quad x_B = 1 - x_A = \frac{p_B}{P}
$$

Differential conversion:

$$
dC_A = \frac{dp_A}{RT}
$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Case | Final Integrated Flux Equation | Variables & Meaning |
|---|------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **A through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \dfrac{(p_{A1} - p_{A2})}{p_{BM}}$ | $p_{BM}$ = log-mean partial pressure of B |
| 2 | **Equimolar Counter Diffusion** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ | $p_{BM}$ is NOT used |
| 3 | **EMCD — Concentration form** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}$ | Direct Fick's Law application |
| 4 | **Log-mean partial pressure of B** | $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ | When $p_{B1} = p_{B2}$, $p_{BM} = p_{B1}$ |
| 5 | **Fick's General (starting)** | $N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$ | Foundation for both cases |
| 6 | **Fick's Law — EMCD form** | $N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Convective term drops out |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications in Chemical Engineering:**
> - **Humidification & Cooling Towers:** Water vapor diffuses through stagnant air → Use **Case I** (Stefan Diffusion).
> - **Distillation Columns:** Vapor-liquid interface shows mutual evaporation → Use **Case II** (EMCD).
> - **Gas Absorption (e.g., NH₃ scrubbing):** Solute gas diffuses through carrier gas → **Case I** applies.
> - **Drying Operations:** Moisture leaves a solid into still air → **Case I** with air as stagnant medium.
> - **Membrane Separations & Pervaporation:** Counter-diffusion streams → **Case II** relevant.

### 2.6 Key Limiting Behavior

| Limit | Implication |
|-------|-------------|
| $x_A \ll 1$ (Dilute A in B) | $p_{BM} \approx P$ → both equations converge; bulk flow effect is negligible |
| $x_A \rightarrow 1$ (Pure A limit) | Stagnant B equation becomes singular — physically, pure A cannot diffuse through pure A |
| $N_B = -N_A$ exactly | EMCD condition; convective contribution vanishes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady State Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B

**Starting Point — Fick's Law for Binary Gases with Bulk Flow:**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)
$$

**Step 1: Apply the Stagnant B Constraint**

Since B is stationary at the macroscopic level:

$$
N_B = 0
$$

Substituting into Fick's equation:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + 0)
$$

**Step 2: Algebraic Rearrangement — Isolate $N_A$**

Move the $x_A N_A$ term to the left-hand side:

$$
N_A - x_A N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

Factor out $N_A$:

$$
N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

Recognize that $(1 - x_A) = x_B$:

$$
N_A \, x_B = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

Solve for $N_A$:

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{x_B} \cdot \frac{dC_A}{dz} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{(1 - x_A)} \cdot \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 3: Convert Concentration Gradient to Partial Pressure Gradient**

Using the Ideal Gas relation:

$$
C_A = \frac{p_A}{RT} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dC_A = \frac{dp_A}{RT}
$$

Also, since the total pressure is constant (isobaric diffusion):

$$
P = p_A + p_B = \text{constant}
$$

Differentiating:

$$
dp_A = -dp_B
$$

Substitute into the flux equation:

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT (1 - p_A/P)} \cdot \frac{dp_A}{dz}
$$

Using the identity $(1 - p_A/P) = p_B/P$:

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, p_B} \cdot \frac{dp_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 4: Separate Variables and Integrate**

Rearranging:

$$
N_A \, dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \cdot \frac{dp_A}{p_B}
$$

Integrating from $z_1$ (point 1) to $z_2$ (point 2) — and noting $N_A$ is constant at steady state:

$$
N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_A}{p_B}
$$

The left-hand side yields:

$$
N_A \, (z_2 - z_1) = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_A}{P - p_A}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the Integral**

Let $u = P - p_A$, so $du = -dp_A$. When $p_A = p_{A1}$, $u = P - p_{A1} = p_{B1}$. When $p_A = p_{A2}$, $u = P - p_{A2} = p_{B2}$.

$$
\int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_A}{P - p_A} = -\int_{p_{B1}}^{p_{B2}} \frac{du}{u} = -\ln \left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right) = \ln \left(\frac{p_{B1}}{p_{B2}}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_A \, (z_2 - z_1) = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \ln \left(\frac{p_{B1}}{p_{B2}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \ln \left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right)
$$

**Step 6: Final Algebraic Manipulation to Introduce $p_{BM}$**

Multiply and divide numerator/denominator inside the logarithm by $(p_{B2} - p_{B1})$:

$$
\ln \left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right) = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{p_{BM}}
$$

Where the **log-mean partial pressure of B** is defined as:

$$
p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
N_A \, (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \cdot \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{p_{BM}}
$$

**Step 7: Final Result — Stefan's Equation**

Since $p_{A1} - p_{A2} = (P - p_{B1}) - (P - p_{B2}) = p_{B2} - p_{B1}$:

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}}
$$

This is the **famous Stefan's Law (or Stefan's Equation)** for diffusion of A through stagnant B. The presence of $p_{BM}$ in the denominator reflects the "crowding effect" — the more B is present, the slower A diffuses.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Equimolar Counter Diffusion (EMCD)

**Starting Point — Fick's Law with EMCD Constraint:**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)
$$

**Step 1: Apply the EMCD Constraint**

By definition:

$$
N_A = -N_B
$$

Therefore the total bulk molar flux is:

$$
N = N_A + N_B = N_A + (-N_A) = 0
$$

**Step 2: Substitution Simplifies the Equation**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (0)
$$

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

This is **pure Fick's Law** — the bulk flow correction disappears entirely.

**Step 3: Steady-State Integration**

At steady state, $N_A$ is constant. Separate variables:

$$
N_A \, dz = -D_{AB} \, dC_A
$$

Integrate from state 1 ($z = z_1, C_A = C_{A1}$) to state 2 ($z = z_2, C_A = C_{A2}$):

$$
N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} dC_A
$$

$$
N_A (z_2 - z_1) = -D_{AB} (C_{A2} - C_{A1}) = D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})
$$

**Step 4: Final Result — EMCD Equation (Concentration Form)**

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to Partial Pressure Form**

Using $C_A = p_A / (RT)$:

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}
$$

Note carefully: **No $p_{BM}$ term appears** because there is no bulk flow correction needed.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Summary Table of the Two Cases

| Feature | Case I (Stagnant B) | Case II (EMCD) |
|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| Constraint | $N_B = 0$ | $N_A = -N_B$ |
| Bulk flow | Exists ($N = N_A$) | Zero ($N = 0$) |
| Convective term | Present ($x_A N_A$) | Absent |
| Final flux equation | Includes $p_{BM}$ | No $p_{BM}$ |
| Effect of diluteness | Significant when $x_A$ is high | Insensitive |
| Magnitude of $N_A$ (same $p_{A1}-p_{A2}$) | Smaller (divided by $p_{BM}$) | Larger |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** Ammonia (A) diffuses through stagnant air (B) across a stagnant film of thickness 2 mm. Partial pressure of NH₃ at one boundary is 20 kPa; at the other boundary is 5 kPa. Total pressure = 101.325 kPa. $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, T = 298 K. Calculate the molar flux $N_A$.

**Step 1: Find partial pressures of B at both boundaries.**

$$
p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 101.325 - 20 = 81.325 \, \text{kPa}
$$

$$
p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 101.325 - 5 = 96.325 \, \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute log-mean $p_{BM}$.**

$$
p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})} = \frac{96.325 - 81.325}{\ln(96.325 / 81.325)}
$$

$$
p_{BM} = \frac{15.0}{\ln(1.1844)} = \frac{15.0}{0.16935} = 88.575 \, \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Stefan's Equation.**

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101{,}325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)} \cdot \frac{20{,}000 - 5{,}000}{88{,}575}
$$

Compute the coefficient:

$$
\frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101{,}325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)} = \frac{2.3305}{4.955} = 0.4703 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

Compute the pressure ratio:

$$
\frac{15{,}000}{88{,}575} = 0.1693
$$

**Step 4: Final flux.**

$$
N_A = 0.4703 \times 0.1693 = 0.0796 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

**Step 5: For comparison, calculate EMCD flux (same conditions but $N_B = -N_A$):**

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2}) = 0.4703 \times (20{,}000 - 5{,}000) \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = 0.4703 \times 15 = 7.055 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

Wait — that calculation is incorrect in scale. Let me recompute properly with consistent units (Pa):

For EMCD:

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101{,}325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)} \cdot \frac{(20{,}000 - 5{,}000)}{101{,}325} \times 101{,}325
$$

Let me redo in pure SI units: $p_{A1} = 20{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$, $p_{A2} = 5{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$, $P = 101{,}325 \, \text{Pa}$.

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101{,}325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)} \cdot (20{,}000 - 5{,}000)
$$

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = 0.4703 \times 15{,}000 = 7054 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

But this is huge — clearly an inconsistency in unit handling. The correct Stefan's equation form when using SI Pa is:

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{(p_{A1} - p_{A2})}{p_{BM}}
$$

All pressures in same units (Pa). Let me recheck: numerator $D_{AB} P (p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ has units $\text{m}^2/\text{s} \cdot \text{Pa} \cdot \text{Pa}$, denominator $R T (z_2 - z_1) \cdot p_{BM}$ has units $\text{J/(mol·K)} \cdot \text{K} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{Pa}$.

Since $\text{Pa} = \text{J/m}^3$, the numerator units become $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{J}^2 / (\text{s} \cdot \text{m}^6)$, denominator $\text{J/m} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{J/m}^3 = \text{J}^2/\text{m}^3$. Final unit: $\text{m}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{m}^3 = \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$ (after dividing by J/mol = m³·Pa/mol).

So the result for Case I (stagnant B): $N_A = 0.0796 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$.

For Case II (EMCD), using the same logic but without $p_{BM}$:

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101{,}325)}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)} \cdot \frac{(20{,}000 - 5{,}000)}{101{,}325}
$$

Wait — looking at the standard EMCD partial-pressure equation more carefully:

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})
$$

This is the correct form, where $p$ values are partial pressures. Let me re-derive the units: numerator $\text{m}^2/\text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}$, denominator $\text{J/(mol·K)} \cdot \text{K} \cdot \text{m} = \text{Pa·m}^3/\text{mol} \cdot \text{m} = \text{Pa·m}^4/\text{mol}$.

Final units: $(\text{m}^2/\text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}) / (\text{Pa·m}^4/\text{mol}) = \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$. ✓

So:

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)} \cdot (20{,}000 - 5{,}000)
$$

$$
N_A^{EMCD} = \frac{2.30 \times 10^{-5}}{4.955} \cdot 15{,}000 = 4.642 \times 10^{-6} \times 15{,}000 = 0.0696 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

For Case I (Stagnant B), with full Stefan's form:

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(101{,}325)}{4.955} \cdot \frac{15{,}000}{88{,}575}
$$

$$
N_A = 0.4703 \times 0.1693 = 0.0796 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** For this particular numerical example, the stagnant B case yields a slightly **higher** flux than EMCD because the bulk flow enhancement (since B cannot move back, it is "swept along") adds to the molecular diffusion of A. The $P/p_{BM}$ factor in Stefan's equation can be greater than 1 when $p_{BM} < P$.

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Two Diffusion Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Binary Gas System A B] --> B{Type of Diffusion Scenario}
    B -- N sub B equals 0 --> C[Case I: A through Stagnant B]
    B -- N sub A equals negative N sub B --> D[Case II: Equimolar Counter Diffusion]
    C --> E[Convective Bulk Flow Exists]
    C --> F[Ficks Law modified with x sub A N sub A term]
    C --> G[Stefans Equation with log mean p sub BM]
    D --> H[No Bulk Flow N equals 0]
    D --> I[Ficks Law simplified without convective term]
    D --> J[Pure Diffusive Flux equation]
    F --> K[Derivation Steps 1 to 7 in SECTION 3]
    I --> L[Derivation Steps 1 to 5 in SECTION 3]
    G --> M[Final Stefan Flux N sub A expression]
    J --> N[Final EMCD Flux N sub A expression]
    M --> O[Applications: Humidification, Drying, Absorption]
    N --> P[Applications: Distillation, Membrane separation]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Diffusion Path Geometry (Concentration Profiles)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Point 1 at z equals z1] -->|Diffusion Path Delta Z| Z2[Point 2 at z equals z2]
    Z1 --> P1[Partial pressure pA1 high xA1 high]
    Z2 --> P2[Partial pressure pA2 low xA2 low]
    P1 --> PB1[Partial pressure of B: pB1 low]
    P2 --> PB2[Partial pressure of B: pB2 high]
    PB1 --> PBM1[log-mean pBM]
    PB2 --> PBM1
    PBM1 --> STEFAN[Stefan Equation for stagnant B]
    P1 --> EMCD[EMCD Equation without pBM]
    P2 --> EMCD
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic Flow for KTU Problem Solving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Given a binary gas diffusion problem] --> R{Identify the constraint}
    R -- B is stagnant --> S[Apply Stefans Equation]
    R -- Equimolar counter diffusion --> T[Apply EMCD Equation]
    R -- Neither stated explicitly --> U[Analyze context: absorption vs distillation]
    S --> V1[Step 1: Calculate pB1 and pB2]
    V1 --> V2[Step 2: Compute log mean pBM]
    V2 --> V3[Step 3: Substitute into Stefan flux equation]
    T --> W1[Step 1: Verify EMCD condition]
    W1 --> W2[Step 2: Apply pure Ficks Law integration]
    U --> X1[Case: Open system with non reacting gas B stagnant]
    U --> X2[Case: Closed system mutual diffusion EMCD]
    X1 --> S
    X2 --> T
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Case1[Case I: A through Stagnant B]
        A1[Bulk Flow Direction: same as A] --> A2[Net molar flux N equals N sub A]
        A3[B is stationary at macroscopic level] --> A4[Effective diffusivity reduced by factor pBM]
    end
    subgraph Case2[Case II: Equimolar Counter Diffusion]
        B1[N sub A flows in positive z] --> B3[Net bulk flow equals zero]

    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Equimolar Counter Diffusion" in binary gaseous systems. Under what conditions does it occur? Give two industrial examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Equimolar Counter Diffusion (EMCD) is the steady-state mass transfer process in which two gaseous species **A** and **B** diffuse in **exactly opposite directions** at **equal molar rates**, i.e., $N_A = -N_B$. The net bulk molar flux is zero, and there is no convective contribution to mass transfer.
>
> **Conditions:** EMCD occurs in **isothermal, isobaric, closed binary systems** where both species are diffusing simultaneously without any net bulk flow. It is typically observed when **equimolar evaporation and condensation** take place at the two boundaries.
>
> **Industrial Examples:**
> 1. **Distillation column** at the vapor-liquid interface — light and heavy components evaporate/condense at equal molar rates.
> 2. **Mutual diffusion** in a closed binary gas vessel (e.g., H₂ and N₂ at constant T and P).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is meant by "steady state diffusion of A through stagnant B"? Explain the significance of the log-mean partial pressure $p_{BM}$ in Stefan's equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> In steady-state diffusion of A through stagnant B, species **B** is **stationary at the macroscopic level** ($N_B = 0$) while species **A** diffuses through it under a partial pressure gradient. This is also called **Stefan diffusion** or **unidirectional diffusion**.
>
> The **log-mean partial pressure of B**, $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$, accounts for the **non-linear variation** of $p_B$ along the diffusion path. It arises because as A diffuses in one direction, the bulk flow of the gas mixture opposes (or enhances) the molecular flux, and the magnitude of this convective correction depends on the local composition. The factor $P/p_{BM}$ essentially captures the **ratio of total pressure to the average partial pressure of B**, representing the **"crowding effect"** — the more B present, the more it hinders the diffusion of A. For dilute systems where $p_{BM} \approx P$, this factor approaches 1 and the equation reduces to simple Fick's Law.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE 1 — INTERNAL CHOICE PROVISION:**
> *Answer ANY ONE full question from Q.No. X (A) OR (B). Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.*

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a) Derive the equation for the steady-state molar flux of species A diffusing through stagnant B. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State Fick's Law for binary gas mixture**

The general equation for steady-state molar diffusion in a binary gas mixture including bulk flow is:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)
$$

**Step 2: Apply the stagnant B constraint**

Since B is stagnant, $N_B = 0$. Substituting:

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A N_A
$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to isolate $N_A$**

$$
N_A (1 - x_A) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

Recognizing $1 - x_A = x_B$:

$$
N_A x_B = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{x_B} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to partial pressure form using Ideal Gas Law**

Since $C_A = p_A / (RT)$ and $dC_A = dp_A / (RT)$, with $p_B / P = x_B$:

$$
N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, p_B} \frac{dp_A}{dz}
$$

**Step 5: Separate variables**

$$
N_A \, dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \cdot \frac{dp_A}{p_B}
$$

**Step 6: Integrate from $z_1$ to $z_2$**

$$
N_A (z_2 - z_1) = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_A}{P - p_A} = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \ln \frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}
$$

**Step 7: Introduce log-mean partial pressure $p_{BM}$**

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}}
$$

where $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation (flux independent of time)
2. Isothermal and isobaric conditions
3. Ideal gas behavior
4. One-dimensional diffusion along z-direction
5. No chemical reaction in the gas phase
6. Constant diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$
7. B is stagnant at the macroscopic level ($N_B = 0$)

---

**(b) Water vapor (A) is diffusing through a stagnant film of air (B) of thickness 1.5 mm. The partial pressure of water vapor at the water-film interface is 4.246 kPa and at the outer edge of the film is 0 kPa. The total pressure is 101.325 kPa and temperature is 298 K. The diffusivity of water vapor in air is $2.6 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the steady-state molar flux of water vapor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $z_2 - z_1 = 1.5 \, \text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$
- $p_{A1} = 4.246 \, \text{kPa} = 4246 \, \text{Pa}$
- $p_{A2} = 0 \, \text{Pa}$
- $P = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 101{,}325 \, \text{Pa}$
- $T = 298 \, \text{K}$
- $D_{AB} = 2.6 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1: Compute partial pressures of B**

$$
p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 101{,}325 - 4246 = 97{,}079 \, \text{Pa}
$$

$$
p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 101{,}325 - 0 = 101{,}325 \, \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute log-mean $p_{BM}$**

$$
p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})} = \frac{101{,}325 - 97{,}079}{\ln(101{,}325 / 97{,}079)}
$$

$$
p_{BM} = \frac{4246}{\ln(1.04373)} = \frac{4246}{0.04278} = 99{,}252 \, \text{Pa} \approx 99.25 \, \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Stefan's equation**

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT(z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{(2.6 \times 10^{-5})(101{,}325)}{(8.314)(298)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})} \cdot \frac{4246 - 0}{99{,}252}
$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator term**

$$
RT(z_2 - z_1) = (8.314)(298)(1.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 3.716 \, \text{J·m/mol}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the leading coefficient**

$$
\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT(z_2 - z_1)} = \frac{(2.6 \times 10^{-5})(101{,}325)}{3.716} = \frac{2.6345}{3.716} = 0.7090 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
$$

**Step 6: Compute pressure ratio**

$$
\frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}} = \frac{4246}{99{,}252} = 0.04278
$$

**Step 7: Final flux**

$$
\boxed{N_A = 0.7090 \times 0.04278 = 0.03033 \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)} = 3.033 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023]*

**(a) Derive the expression for equimolar counter diffusion (EMCD) in a binary gas mixture. Under what physical situations is EMCD applicable? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Starting equation — Fick's Law with bulk flow**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)
$$

**Step 2: Apply EMCD constraint $N_A = -N_B$**

The total bulk molar flux:

$$
N = N_A + N_B = N_A + (-N_A) = 0
$$

**Step 3: Substitution**

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (0)
$$

$$
N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}
$$

The convective term vanishes entirely.

**Step 4: Steady-state integration**

At steady state, $N_A$ is constant:

$$
N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} dC_A
$$

$$
N_A (z_2 - z_1) = -D_{AB} (C_{A2} - C_{A1})
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}}
$$

**Step 5: In partial pressure form (using $C = P/(RT)$)**

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}
$$

**Physical Situations for EMCD:**

1. **Distillation columns** — Light and heavy key components evaporate at one interface and condense at the other at equal molar rates.
2. **Closed binary gas systems** at constant T and P (e.g., mutual diffusion of H₂ and N₂ in a sealed vessel).
3. **Membrane separation processes** where counter-permeating species have nearly equal fluxes.
4. **Equimolar liquid-liquid extraction** systems at thermodynamic equilibrium.
5. **Catalytic surfaces** where two reactant species diffuse toward and product species diffuse away in equal molar proportions (e.g., $A + B \rightarrow C + D$).

---

**(b) Two large reservoirs of a binary mixture of A and B are connected by a tube of length 0.1 m. The partial pressures are: Reservoir 1: $p_{A1} = 50 \, \text{kPa}$, $p_{B1} = 50 \, \text{kPa}$; Reservoir 2: $p_{A2} = 10 \, \text{kPa}$, $p_{B2} = 90 \, \text{kPa}$. Total pressure is 100 kPa, T = 300 K, $D_{AB} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.**

**Determine the molar flux $N_A$ for:**
- **(i) Equimolar counter diffusion (3.5 marks)**
- **(ii) A diffusing through stagnant B (3.5 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 100 \, \text{kPa} = 100{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$, $T = 300 \, \text{K}$, $z_2 - z_1 = 0.1 \, \text{m}$, $D_{AB} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Part (i) — EMCD:**

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-5})}{(8.314)(300)(0.1)} (50{,}000 - 10{,}000)
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{249.42} \times 40{,}000 = 6.013 \times 10^{-8} \times 40{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A^{EMCD} = 2.405 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}}
$$

**Part (ii) — A through Stagnant B:**

First compute $p_{BM}$:

$$
p_{B1} = 50 \, \text{kPa}, \quad p_{B2} = 90 \, \text{kPa}
$$

$$
p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})} = \frac{90 - 50}{\ln(90/50)} = \frac{40}{\ln(1.8)} = \frac{40}{0.5878} = 68.05 \, \text{kPa} = 68{,}050 \, \text{Pa}
$$

Apply Stefan's equation:

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-5})(100{,}000)}{(8.314)(300)(0.1)} \cdot \frac{40{,}000}{68{,}050}
$$

$$
N_A = \frac{1.5}{249.42} \cdot 0.5878 = 6.013 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5878
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A^{Stagnant} = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)}}
$$

**Observation:** The stagnant B case yields a flux approximately **47% larger** than EMCD for these conditions because the bulk flow enhancement (the $P/p_{BM}$ ratio > 1) augments the molecular diffusion of A.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Most Frequent Mark Loser):** Students mix kPa and Pa, or forget to convert mm to m. Always convert ALL quantities to **consistent SI units** (Pa, m, K) before substitution.
> 2. **Misapplication of Stefan's Equation to EMCD:** Do NOT include $p_{BM}$ in equimolar counter diffusion — the convective term drops out.
> 3. **Confusion Between $x_B$ and $p_B/P$:** These are dimensionally equivalent for ideal gases but must be applied correctly in the algebraic step.
> 4. **Sign Convention Error:** $N_A$ is positive when A diffuses in the +z direction (from high to low concentration). Do not introduce unnecessary negative signs in the final answer.
> 5. **Log-Mean Calculation Error:** When $p_{B1} = p_{B2}$, the log-mean equals the arithmetic value. Check for this edge case.
> 6. **Missing Assumptions:** Always list at least 4–5 assumptions in derivation problems — examiners allot 1–2 marks specifically for this in KTU valuations.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Fick's Law for Binary Mixtures** — The master equation with convective term: $N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$.
- [x] **Stefan Diffusion (Stagnant B)** — Constraint $N_B = 0$ → flux equation includes log-mean $p_{BM}$.
- [x] **EMCD** — Constraint $N_A = -N_B$ → no bulk flow, pure Fick's Law.
- [x] **Log-Mean $p_{BM}$** — Defined as $\frac{p_{B2}-p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$; physical meaning = "average crowding effect."
- [x] **Stefan Equation (final form)** — $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT(z_2-z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1}-p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$.
- [x] **EMCD Equation (final form)** — $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1}-C_{A2})}{z_2-z_1} = \frac{D_{AB}(p_{A1}-p_{A2})}{RT(z_2-z_1)}$.
- [x] **Assumptions Required for Derivations** — Steady state, isothermal, isobaric, ideal gas, 1-D diffusion, no reaction.
- [x] **Industrial Examples** — Humidification, drying, absorption → Stagnant B; Distillation, membrane separation → EMCD.
- [x] **Dilute Limit Check** — When $p_{BM} \to P$, both cases give identical fluxes (bulk flow negligible).
- [x] **Magnitude Comparison** — Stagnant B flux can be larger or smaller than EMCD depending on $P/p_{BM}$ ratio.
- [x] **Units Vigilance** — Always: $D_{AB}$ in m²/s, $P$ and $p$ in Pa, $T$ in K, length in m, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K).
- [x] **Log-Mean Edge Case** — When $p_{B1} = p_{B2}$, log-mean = arithmetic mean = $p_{B1}$.
- [x] **Physical Constants to Memorize** — $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$, STP conditions: 101.325 kPa, 273.15 K / 298.15 K.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquids and Multi-Component Gas Mixtures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liquids and Multi-Component Gas Mixtures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Liquids — Formal Definition

In the context of Mass Transfer Operations, a **liquid** is a condensed, virtually incompressible fluid phase in which molecules are held together by intermolecular cohesive forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, or dipole-dipole interactions) strong enough to give it a definite volume but weak enough to allow it to flow and conform to the shape of its container.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Properties of Liquids (Recall from Fluid Mechanics):**
> - **Density (ρ)** [kg/m³] — mass per unit volume
> - **Viscosity (μ)** [Pa·s] — resistance to flow
> - **Surface Tension (σ)** [N/m] — resistance to surface area increase
> - **Vapor Pressure (Pᵢᵃᵗ)** [Pa or mmHg] — pressure exerted by a liquid when in equilibrium with its own vapor
> - **Boiling Point** [°C or K] — temperature at which vapor pressure equals total system pressure

## 1.2 Multi-Component Gas Mixtures — Formal Definition

A **multi-component gas mixture** is a homogeneous gaseous phase composed of **two or more chemical species (components)** that do not react chemically with one another, but coexist as a single physical phase. Each component *i* contributes a **partial pressure (pᵢ)** to the total pressure **P** in accordance with its mole fraction **yᵢ**.

The fundamental governing relationships are:

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \quad \text{...(Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures)}$$

$$y_i = \frac{p_i}{P} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} \quad \text{...(Mole Fraction Definition)}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Crowded Party Room"**
>
> Imagine a sealed room (the gas mixture) filled with people of different nationalities (different gas components). Each person is jostling against the walls, and the **total pressure on the walls** is the combined push from everyone.
> - The push exerted by the *Americans alone* is the **partial pressure of component A**.
> - The fraction of all people who are Americans is the **mole fraction (yᵢ)**.
> - Even though the people are mixed, each still behaves as an **ideal gas independently** — Dalton's Law!
>
> For **liquids** — think of a *tea* solution: water (solvent) and sugar (solute) are mixed at the molecular level. The *escaping tendency* of water molecules into the vapor phase is the **vapor pressure**, which depends on how much sugar is dissolved. This is where **Raoult's Law** comes in.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Mass Transfer

| Concept | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Partial Pressure (pᵢ)** | Drives diffusion in gas-phase mass transfer (e.g., absorption, stripping) |
| **Vapor Pressure (Pᵢᵃᵗ)** | Determines volatility — critical in distillation column design |
| **Raoult's / Henry's Law** | Establishes VLE (Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium) — the *backbone* of all separation equipment design |
| **K-Value (Kᵢ = yᵢ/xᵢ)** | Foundation for McCabe-Thiele, multi-component distillation, and flash calculations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The understanding of liquids and multi-component gas mixtures forms the *prerequisite foundation* for Module 1 topics like **Diffusion in Gases & Liquids** and **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)**. Mastery here directly translates to marks in **distillation, absorption, and extraction** modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Multi-Component Gas Mixtures — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures

**Statement:** The total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases is equal to the **sum of the partial pressures** of the individual components.

**Underlying assumption:** Each gas behaves as an **ideal gas** independently, occupying the entire container volume.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Consider *n* components in volume *V* at temperature *T*.
2. Apply ideal gas law to each component *i*: $p_i V = n_i R T$
3. Sum over all components: $V \sum p_i = RT \sum n_i$
4. Recognize that $P = \frac{(n_{total})RT}{V}$ for the mixture
5. Therefore: $\sum p_i = P$

### 2.1.2 Amagat's Law (Law of Partial Volumes)

An equivalent statement: $V = \sum v_i$, where $v_i$ is the partial volume of component *i*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dalton's Law ↔ Amagat's Law:**
> Dalton's Law uses **pressure additivity** (constant V, T).
> Amagat's Law uses **volume additivity** (constant P, T).
> Both are valid for **ideal gas mixtures**.

### 2.1.3 Ideal Gas Mixture Equation

For a multi-component gas mixture:

$$PV = n_{total} R T = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i\right) R T$$

Or equivalently, the **partial pressure equation**:

$$p_i = y_i P = \frac{n_i}{V} R T$$

## 2.2 Liquid Mixtures — Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) Foundation

### 2.2.1 Raoult's Law (Ideal Solutions)

**Statement:** The partial pressure of component *i* in the vapor phase is **equal to its mole fraction in the liquid phase times its pure-component vapor pressure** at the same temperature.

$$p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$$

where:
- $p_i$ = partial pressure of component *i* in vapor
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of component *i* in liquid
- $P_i^{sat}(T)$ = pure-component vapor pressure of *i* at temperature *T*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raoult's Law applies to IDEAL solutions** — i.e., liquid mixtures where intermolecular forces between unlike molecules equal those between like molecules (e.g., benzene-toluene, hexane-heptane).

### 2.2.2 Combined Raoult's + Dalton's Law (VLE Expression)

For an ideal binary mixture at equilibrium:

$$y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$$

$$y_i = \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

### 2.2.3 Henry's Law (Dilute / Non-Ideal Solutions)

**Statement:** For a solute *i* in a dilute liquid solution, the partial pressure is **proportional to its mole fraction**:

$$p_i = H_i \cdot x_i$$

where $H_i$ is the **Henry's Law constant** [Pa or atm]. Henry's Law applies when the solute is **below its infinite dilution limit** — i.e., the solvent is the majority component and solute-solvent interactions dominate.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Insight:** Henry's Law governs:
> - Dissolved oxygen in water (environmental engineering)
> - CO₂ capture in amine absorption columns (petrochemical)
> - Anaesthesia gas solubility in blood (medical engineering)

### 2.2.4 K-Value (Equilibrium Ratio)

The **K-value** (or distribution coefficient) is the heart of multi-component VLE:

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i}$$

For ideal solutions:

$$K_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

For non-ideal systems, $K_i$ is corrected by **activity coefficients** (γᵢ) or **fugacity coefficients** (φ̂ᵢ):

$$K_i = \frac{\gamma_i P_i^{sat}(T)}{\phi_i^{vap} P}$$

## 2.3 Relative Volatility (α)

A critical concept for distillation design — measures how easily one component separates from another:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{K_i}{K_j} = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P_j^{sat}(T)} \quad \text{(for ideal systems)}$$

- If $\alpha > 1$: component *i* is **more volatile** than *j* (separates as overhead)
- If $\alpha = 1$: separation is **impossible** by simple distillation

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | Dalton's Law — total pressure | [Pa] — ideal gases |
| 2 | $y_i = p_i / P$ | Mole fraction in gas | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ | Amagat's Law — partial volumes | [m³] |
| 4 | $p_i V = n_i R T$ | Partial ideal gas equation | [Pa] |
| 5 | $p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ | Raoult's Law | [Pa] — ideal liquids |
| 6 | $p_i = H_i x_i$ | Henry's Law | [Pa] — dilute solutes |
| 7 | $y_i = x_i P_i^{sat}/P$ | Combined VLE relation | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $K_i = y_i / x_i$ | K-value / Equilibrium ratio | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P$ | K-value (ideal solution) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\alpha_{ij} = K_i/K_j$ | Relative volatility | Dimensionless, >1 for separability |
| 11 | $\sum y_i = \sum x_i = 1$ | Mole fraction constraint | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\rho_L = m/V$ | Liquid density | [kg/m³] |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Distillation Column Design:** K-values drive the McCabe-Thiele method and rigorous MESH equations.
- **Absorption Columns:** Henry's Law determines gas solubility in the liquid absorbent.
- **Flash Drums:** Combined VLE equations predict vapor-liquid split.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Henry's Law models VOC emissions from water bodies.
- **Cryogenic Air Separation:** Dalton's Law + Raoult's Law enable O₂/N₂ separation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures

**Starting Premise:** Each gas in the mixture obeys the ideal gas law independently.

**Step 1 — Apply Ideal Gas Law to Component *i*:**

$$p_i V = n_i R T$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all *n* components:**

$$V \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = RT \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i$$

**Step 3 — Recognize total moles:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i = n_{total}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$V \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = n_{total} R T$$

**Step 5 — Apply ideal gas law to total mixture:**

$$P V = n_{total} R T$$

**Step 6 — Equate Step 4 and Step 5:**

$$V \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = P V$$

**Step 7 — Cancel volume *V* (non-zero):**

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = P}$$

**Conclusion:** Dalton's Law is a direct consequence of the ideal gas law applied to each component independently.

## 3.2 Derivation: Combined VLE Relation (Raoult's + Dalton's)

**Starting Premise:** A liquid mixture is in equilibrium with its vapor at temperature *T* and total pressure *P*.

**Step 1 — Apply Raoult's Law to component *i*:**

$$p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 2 — Express partial pressure via mole fraction (Dalton's Law):**

$$p_i = y_i P$$

**Step 3 — Equate both expressions (same partial pressure):**

$$x_i P_i^{sat}(T) = y_i P$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $y_i$:**

$$y_i = \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

**Step 5 — Constraint equation (mole fractions must sum to 1):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}(T)}{P} = 1$$

**Step 6 — Solve for total pressure (bubble point pressure):**

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 7 — Or solve for temperature (bubble point temperature) — implicit equation:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i P_i^{sat}(T_{BP}) = P \quad \text{...(Bubble Point Equation)}$$

**Step 8 — Inverting gives dew point equation:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T_{DP})} = 1 \quad \text{...(Dew Point Equation)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bubble Point** = temperature at which the first bubble of vapor forms when heating a liquid.
> **Dew Point** = temperature at which the first drop of liquid forms when cooling a vapor.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Bubble Point Calculation (KTU-Style Problem)

### **Problem Statement:**

A liquid mixture contains:
- Component A: $x_A = 0.40$ (more volatile)
- Component B: $x_B = 0.60$ (less volatile)

Vapor pressures at 80°C:
- $P_A^{sat} = 1200 \text{ mmHg}$
- $P_B^{sat} = 500 \text{ mmHg}$

Find the **total pressure** and **vapor composition** at bubble point.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply bubble point equation:**

$$P = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$P = (0.40)(1200) + (0.60)(500)$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**

$$P = 480 + 300$$

**Step 4 — Final total pressure:**

$$P = 780 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate vapor mole fraction of A:**

$$y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{P} = \frac{(0.40)(1200)}{780} = \frac{480}{780}$$

$$\boxed{y_A = 0.6154}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate vapor mole fraction of B:**

$$y_B = 1 - y_A = 1 - 0.6154 = 0.3846$$

**Verification:**

$$y_A + y_B = 0.6154 + 0.3846 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Compute K-values:**

$$K_A = P_A^{sat}/P = 1200/780 = 1.538$$

$$K_B = P_B^{sat}/P = 500/780 = 0.641$$

**Step 8 — Compute relative volatility:**

$$\alpha_{AB} = K_A/K_B = 1.538/0.641 = 2.40$$

> Since $\alpha_{AB} > 1$, **Component A is more volatile** and can be separated from B by distillation.

## 3.4 Derivation of K-Value Relationship

**Starting Point:** K-value definition $K_i = y_i / x_i$

**Step 1 — Substitute from combined VLE:**

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}/P}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Conclude for ideal systems:**

$$\boxed{K_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}}$$

**Physical meaning:** $K_i > 1$ means component *i* prefers the **vapor phase**; $K_i < 1$ means it prefers the **liquid phase**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — VLE Foundations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multicomponent System at Equilibrium] --> B{Vapor Phase}
    A --> C{Liquid Phase}
    B --> D[Daltons Law: P = sum p_i]
    B --> E[Mole Fraction: y_i = p_i / P]
    C --> F[Raoults Law: p_i = x_i P_i_sat]
    C --> G[Henrys Law: p_i = H_i x_i]
    D --> H[Combined VLE: y_i P = x_i P_i_sat]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> I[Dilute Solution Limit]
    H --> J[K Value: K_i = y_i / x_i]
    J --> K[Bubble Point Equation]
    J --> L[Dew Point Equation]
    J --> M[Flash Vaporization]
    J --> N[Distillation Column Design]
    K --> O[Relative Volatility alpha_ij]
    L --> O
    O --> P[Separation Feasibility]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Flash Vaporization Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed F, z_i, T_F, P] --> B[Heater / Pressure Reducer]
    B --> C[Flash Drum / Equilibrium Stage]
    C --> D[Vapor Product V, y_i]
    C --> E[Liquid Product L, x_i]
    D --> F[Condenser or Distillate]
    E --> G[Reboiler or Bottoms]
    H[Material Balance: F = V + L] --> C
    I[Component Balance: F z_i = V y_i + L x_i] --> C
    J[Equilibrium: y_i = K_i x_i] --> C
    K[Sum constraints: sum y_i = sum x_i = 1] --> C
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Multi-Component Separation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Feed Composition z_i] --> B[Step 2: Set Operating Pressure P]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute Vapor Pressures P_i_sat T]
    C --> D{System Type?}
    D -->|Ideal| E[Apply Raoults Law: K_i = P_i_sat / P]
    D -->|Non Ideal| F[Use gamma_i or phi_i corrections]
    E --> G[Step 4: Calculate Bubble or Dew Point]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 5: Determine Vapor Liquid Split]
    H --> I[Step 6: Check Material and Energy Balances]
    I --> J[Step 7: Iterate for Final Equilibrium]
    J --> K[Step 8: Report y_i, x_i, T, P]
```

## 4.4 Phase Diagram Schematic (T-xy Diagram for Binary Ideal System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pure Component A Boiling Point T_A] --> B[Mixture Region]
    C[Pure Component B Boiling Point T_B] --> B
    B --> D[Bubble Point Curve y vs x at constant P]
    B --> E[Dew Point Curve x vs y at constant P]
    D --> F[Region: Subcooled Liquid]
    E --> G[Region: Superheated Vapor]
    F --> H[Two Phase Region between curves]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Equilibrium Tie Lines connect x and y]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the T-xy Diagram:** The upper curve is the **dew point curve** (vapor line), the lower curve is the **bubble point curve** (liquid line). A horizontal line (tie line) at a given temperature connects the equilibrium liquid composition (x) on the lower curve to the equilibrium vapor composition (y) on the upper curve.

## 4.5 Conceptual Hierarchy — From Microscopic to Macroscopic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Molecular Level: Intermolecular Forces] --> B[Macroscopic Liquid Properties]
    A --> C[Macroscopic Gas Properties]
    B --> D[Raoults Law governs p_i]
    C --> E[Daltons Law governs p_i]
    D --> F[Combined into VLE]
    E --> F
    F --> G[K Value and Relative Volatility]
    G --> H[Equipment Design: Distillation, Absorption]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures. Under what conditions is it valid?**

**Model Answer:**

Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that the **total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting ideal gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of individual components**.

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

**Validity Conditions:**
- The gases must be **non-reacting** with each other.
- The mixture must behave as an **ideal gas mixture** (negligible intermolecular forces, no molecular volume).
- Components must not **associate or dissociate**.

**Mark Allocation (3 marks):**
- Statement: 1 mark
- Equation: 1 mark
- Conditions: 1 mark

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Define K-value (equilibrium ratio) and relative volatility. State their significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**K-Value:** Defined as the ratio of mole fraction of a component in the vapor phase to that in the liquid phase at equilibrium.

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i}$$

**Relative Volatility:** Ratio of K-values of two components:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{K_i}{K_j}$$

**Significance:**
- K-value determines whether a component prefers the **vapor** ($K_i > 1$) or **liquid** ($K_i < 1$) phase.
- Relative volatility $\alpha > 1$ indicates the components can be **separated by distillation**; $\alpha = 1$ means **separation is impossible** by ordinary distillation.

**Mark Allocation (3 marks):**
- K-value definition + equation: 1 mark
- Relative volatility definition + equation: 1 mark
- Significance: 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A liquid mixture contains 60 mol% benzene (A) and 40 mol% toluene (B). At 100°C, the vapor pressures are: $P_A^{sat} = 1350 \text{ mmHg}$, $P_B^{sat} = 560 \text{ mmHg}$. Calculate:
1. Total pressure of the system at bubble point
2. Vapor phase composition
3. K-values and relative volatility **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multi-component gas mixture contains 30 mol% N₂, 50 mol% O₂, and 20 mol% CO₂ at a total pressure of 5 atm. Calculate the partial pressure of each component. State Dalton's Law and Amagat's Law. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Given:**
- $x_A = 0.60$, $x_B = 0.40$
- $P_A^{sat} = 1350 \text{ mmHg}$, $P_B^{sat} = 560 \text{ mmHg}$

**Step 1 — Total pressure (bubble point equation):**

$$P = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}$$

$$P = (0.60)(1350) + (0.40)(560)$$

$$P = 810 + 224 = 1034 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 2 — Vapor composition:**

$$y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{P} = \frac{(0.60)(1350)}{1034} = \frac{810}{1034} = 0.7834$$

$$y_B = 1 - y_A = 1 - 0.7834 = 0.2166$$

**Step 3 — K-values:**

$$K_A = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P} = \frac{1350}{1034} = 1.306$$

$$K_B = \frac{P_B^{sat}}{P} = \frac{560}{1034} = 0.5416$$

**Step 4 — Relative volatility:**

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{1.306}{0.5416} = 2.411$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\alpha_{AB} > 1$, benzene is more volatile and the mixture is **separable by distillation**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Given:**
- $y_{N_2} = 0.30$, $y_{O_2} = 0.50$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.20$
- $P = 5 \text{ atm}$

**Step 1 — Partial pressure of N₂:**

$$p_{N_2} = y_{N_2} \cdot P = 0.30 \times 5 = 1.5 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 2 — Partial pressure of O₂:**

$$p_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot P = 0.50 \times 5 = 2.5 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3 — Partial pressure of CO₂:**

$$p_{CO_2} = y_{CO_2} \cdot P = 0.20 \times 5 = 1.0 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$\sum p_i = 1.5 + 2.5 + 1.0 = 5.0 \text{ atm} = P \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Dalton's Law:** The total pressure is the sum of the partial pressures of individual gases (valid for ideal gas mixtures).

**Step 6 — Amagat's Law:** The total volume of an ideal gas mixture is the sum of the partial volumes of each component.

$$V = \sum_{i} v_i \quad \text{where} \quad v_i = \frac{n_i R T}{P}$$

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** A mixture of n-hexane (A) and n-heptane (B) is at 101.325 kPa total pressure. At 100°C: $P_A^{sat} = 244.8 \text{ kPa}$, $P_B^{sat} = 106.6 \text{ kPa}$. If the liquid contains 35 mol% A, find:
1. The temperature at which this mixture will start to boil
2. The composition of the first vapor bubble
3. K-values and relative volatility **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the assumptions, applicability, and limitations of Raoult's Law. Explain how Henry's Law is used for non-ideal dilute solutions with two real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Given:**
- $x_A = 0.35$, $x_B = 0.65$
- $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- $P_A^{sat}(100°C) = 244.8 \text{ kPa}$, $P_B^{sat}(100°C) = 106.6 \text{ kPa}$

**Step 1 — Bubble point condition:**

$$\sum y_i = \sum \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P} = 1$$

**Step 2 — At 100°C, calculate:**

$$\sum \frac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P} = \frac{(0.35)(244.8) + (0.65)(106.6)}{101.325}$$

$$= \frac{85.68 + 69.29}{101.325} = \frac{154.97}{101.325} = 1.529$$

Since this sum > 1, the mixture **boils below 100°C** (because vapor pressures are too high to maintain liquid at 100°C).

**Step 3 — Iteration to find bubble point temperature:**

Try $T = 90°C$: $P_A^{sat} = 178.7 \text{ kPa}$, $P_B^{sat} = 78.37 \text{ kPa}$

$$\sum = \frac{(0.35)(178.7) + (0.65)(78.37)}{101.325} = \frac{62.55 + 50.94}{101.325} = \frac{113.49}{101.325} = 1.120$$

Still > 1. Try $T = 85°C$: $P_A^{sat} = 152.3 \text{ kPa}$, $P_B^{sat} = 65.95 \text{ kPa}$

$$\sum = \frac{(0.35)(152.3) + (0.65)(65.95)}{101.325} = \frac{53.31 + 42.87}{101.325} = \frac{96.18}{101.325} = 0.949$$

Slightly < 1. Interpolate:

$$T_{BP} \approx 85 + 5 \times \frac{1 - 0.949}{1.120 - 0.949} = 85 + 5 \times \frac{0.051}{0.171} = 85 + 1.49 \approx 86.5°C$$

**Step 4 — Vapor composition at bubble point:**

$$y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{P} = \frac{(0.35)(152.3)}{101.325} = \frac{53.31}{101.325} = 0.526$$

$$y_B = 1 - 0.526 = 0.474$$

**Step 5 — K-values and relative volatility:**

$$K_A = P_A^{sat}/P = 152.3/101.325 = 1.503$$

$$K_B = P_B^{sat}/P = 65.95/101.325 = 0.651$$

$$\alpha_{AB} = 1.503/0.651 = 2.309$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Assumptions of Raoult's Law:**
1. Liquid phase behaves as an **ideal solution**.
2. Molecules of different components are **chemically similar**.
3. The enthalpy of mixing is **zero** ($\Delta H_{mix} = 0$).
4. The volume of mixing is **zero** ($\Delta V_{mix} = 0$).
5. Intermolecular forces: A-A = B-B = A-B.

**Applicability:**
- Systems with chemically similar components: **benzene-toluene**, **hexane-heptane**, **methanol-ethanol**.
- **Non-polar** mixtures with similar molecular sizes.
- Low-to-moderate pressures and temperatures.

**Limitations:**
- **Fails for polar + non-polar** systems (e.g., acetone-water, chloroform-acetone).
- **Fails for electrolytes** (e.g., NaCl in water).
- Cannot predict **azeotrope formation**.
- Inaccurate near **critical point** or at very **high pressures**.

**Henry's Law for Non-Ideal Dilute Solutions:**

**Statement:** $p_i = H_i \cdot x_i$, where $H_i$ is the Henry's constant.

**Example 1: Dissolved Oxygen in Water**
- O₂ is sparingly soluble in water; at 25°C, $H_{O_2} \approx 4.3 \times 10^4 \text{ atm}$.
- Used in designing **aeration tanks** in wastewater treatment plants and **biorectors**.

**Example 2: CO₂ Capture in Amine Absorption**
- CO₂ is absorbed in monoethanolamine (MEA) solutions.
- Henry's Law determines the **driving force** for mass transfer in absorption columns used in natural gas processing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mmHg and kPa in the same problem. Always convert to a single unit system (preferably **SI: kPa or Pa**).
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint equation:** $\sum y_i = 1$ and $\sum x_i = 1$ must always hold. Students often compute $y_A$ only and forget $y_B = 1 - y_A$.
> 3. **Misapplying Raoult's Law to non-ideal systems:** Raoult's Law gives **wrong answers** for polar + non-polar mixtures. Use modified Raoult's with activity coefficients.
> 4. **Confusing bubble point and dew point:** Bubble point uses $\sum x_i P_i^{sat} / P = 1$ (given $x_i$); dew point uses $\sum y_i P / P_i^{sat} = 1$ (given $y_i$).
> 5. **Not verifying the relative volatility** at the actual operating temperature — $\alpha$ varies with $T$.
> 6. **Skipping the iteration step** in bubble point problems where $T$ is unknown — KTU expects a **trial-and-error iteration** or **interpolation**.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention KTU-specific keywords** in 3-mark answers: terms like "ideal solution", "equilibrium", "partial pressure" carry marks in keyword-based valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Liquids & Multi-Component Gas Mixtures**

- [ ] **Dalton's Law:** $P = \sum p_i$ — valid for ideal gas mixtures, non-reacting gases
- [ ] **Amagat's Law:** $V = \sum v_i$ — volume additivity at constant P, T
- [ ] **Partial Pressure:** $p_i = y_i P = n_i RT / V$
- [ ] **Raoult's Law:** $p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ — ideal solutions only
- [ ] **Henry's Law:** $p_i = H_i x_i$ — dilute solute limit
- [ ] **Combined VLE:** $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ — equilibrium condition
- [ ] **K-Value:** $K_i = y_i / x_i = P_i^{sat}/P$ (ideal)
- [ ] **Bubble Point:** $\sum x_i P_i^{sat} / P = 1$ (when heating liquid, first bubble forms)
- [ ] **Dew Point:** $\sum y_i P / P_i^{sat} = 1$ (when cooling vapor, first drop forms)
- [ ] **Relative Volatility:** $\alpha_{ij} = K_i/K_j$; $\alpha > 1$ ⇒ separable
- [ ] **Mole Fraction Constraint:** $\sum x_i = \sum y_i = 1$
- [ ] **Liquid properties to recall:** density, viscosity, surface tension, vapor pressure
- [ ] **Standard units:** Pressure in **kPa** (SI) or **atm**; Temperature in **K** for gas law calculations
- [ ] **Engineering applications:** distillation columns, absorption towers, flash drums, environmental engineering, cryogenic air separation
- [ ] **Key assumption flags:** "Ideal solution" → use Raoult's; "Dilute solute" → use Henry's; "Non-ideal" → use activity coefficient models (Margules, van Laar, Wilson)
- [ ] **Master the iteration logic** for bubble point / dew point problems — these are KTU favorites
- [ ] **Always verify** your final answer by checking $\sum y_i = 1$
- [ ] **Real-world connection:** Know at least **2 industrial applications** of Henry's Law for theory questions
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Transfer Coefficients: Introduction to Mass Transfer Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1 > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Mass Transfer Coefficients: Introduction to Mass Transfer Coefficients # Module 1 — Introduction to Mass Transfer Coefficients ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mass Transfer Coefficient (k):** A proportionality constant that relates the **mass flux** (rate of mass transfer per unit area) of a species to a **driving force** (concentration difference, partial pressure difference, or mole fraction difference) existing across a phase boundary or within a phase. Mathematically, the steady-state molar flux $N_A$ of a species $A$ is expressed as: $$N_A = k_C \cdot \Delta C_A$$ where: - $N_A$ = molar flux of species A $\left[\dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}\right]$ - $k_C$ = mass transfer coefficient based on concentration driving force $\left[\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right]$ - $\Delta C_A$ = concentration driving force $\left[\dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^3}\right]$ The units of $k_C$ are simply **length/time** $\left[\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right]$, analogous to a **velocity**. This is why it is often called a "transfer velocity." ### 1.2 Why a Coefficient and Not Just Fick's Law? In an ideal, stagnant, binary system, we use **Fick's First Law**: $$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ But in real engineering equipment (packed columns, bubble columns, trays), the flow is **turbulent**, the geometry is **complex**, and we **cannot easily measure concentration gradients** at every point. Therefore, engineers lump all the complex diffusion + convection physics into a single empirical/semi-theoretical constant: the **mass transfer coefficient** $k$. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Bridge" > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a crowded footbridge connecting two markets (Market A and Market B). People want to move from the busy market to the empty one. The number of people crossing the bridge per second (the *flux*) depends on: > 1. How crowded one market is versus the other (the *driving force*, $\Delta C$). > 2. The width of the bridge, how fast people walk, and the handrails (the *ease of transfer*, $k$). > > The mass transfer coefficient $k$ is essentially the "**ease of crossing**" between the two bulk phases through an interface. ### 1.4 Types of Mass Transfer Coefficients The form of the driving force dictates the symbol and units: | Driving Force Type | Symbol | Units | |---|---|---| | Concentration difference | $k_C$ | $\text{m/s}$ | | Partial pressure difference | $k_G$ | $\text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa)}$ | | Mole fraction difference | $k_y$ or $k_x$ | $\text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$ | | Liquid concentration (mol/L) | $k_L$ | $\text{m/s}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Distinction:** The coefficient can be classified as **Individual** (resistance in a single phase, e.g., $k_G$ in gas, $k_L$ in liquid) or **Overall** (resistance across **both** phases combined, e.g., $K_G$ or $K_L$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Phase Mass Transfer Scenario In almost every mass transfer operation (absorption, stripping, extraction, humidification), a solute **A** transfers from one bulk phase to another across a phase interface. Consider gas–liquid absorption: - Bulk gas phase (concentration $y_{A,G}$) - Gas-side interface (concentration $y_{A,i}$) - Liquid-side interface (concentration $x_{A,i}$) - Bulk liquid phase (concentration $x_{A,L}$) The flux can be written **three equivalent ways**: ### Step 1: Gas-Phase Individual Coefficient $$N_A = k_G \left( p_{A,G} - p_{A,i} \right)$$ ### Step 2: Liquid-Phase Individual Coefficient $$N_A = k_L \left( C_{A,i} - C_{A,L} \right)$$ ### Step 3: Overall Coefficient (across both phases) $$N_A = K_G \left( p_{A,G} - p_{A}^{\,*} \right) = K_L \left( C_{A}^{\,*} - C_{A,L} \right)$$ where $p_{A}^{\,*}$ is the **partial pressure that would be in equilibrium with the bulk liquid concentration**, and $C_{A}^{\,*}$ is the **liquid concentration in equilibrium with the bulk gas**. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Coefficient > [!NOTE] > **Why use individual AND overall coefficients?** > - **Individual coefficients** ($k_G$, $k_L$) are easier to correlate with **hydrodynamics** (Reynolds number, Schmidt number) in each phase. > - **Overall coefficients** ($K_G$, $K_L$) are **measured experimentally** in real equipment and are useful for **design calculations**. > - The **resistance** to mass transfer is the **reciprocal** of the coefficient (just like Ohm's law: $R = 1/k$). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS** | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $N_A = k_C \, \Delta C_A$ | General flux–driving force definition | | 2 | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Overall gas-phase resistance = gas + liquid resistances (in gas units) | | 3 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{m \, k_G} + \dfrac{1}{k_L}$ | Overall liquid-phase resistance (in liquid units) | | 4 | $K_G = \dfrac{K_L}{m}$ | Conversion between overall coefficients; $m = dy/dx$ slope of equilibrium line | | 5 | $Sh = \dfrac{k_C \, L}{D_{AB}}$ | Sherwood number (dimensionless mass transfer coefficient) | | 6 | $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$ | Schmidt number (ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity) | | 7 | $Sh = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Sc^{0.33}$ | Standard Dimensional-type correlation form (e.g., for packed columns) | | 8 | $J_D = \dfrac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc^{1/3}}$ | Chilton–Colburn $J$-factor analogy | | 9 | $N_A = k_y (y_{A,G} - y_{A,i})$ | Flux in mole-fraction gas units | | 10 | $N_A = k_x (x_{A,i} - x_{A,L})$ | Flux in mole-fraction liquid units | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The slope $m$ in equations 2 and 3 refers to the **slope of the equilibrium line** ($m = dy^{\,*}/dx$), **not** the distribution coefficient at a single point. This is a classic KTU mark-loss area. ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Absorption columns** (CO₂ capture, SO₂ scrubbing): Use $K_G a$ (volumetric coefficient) for column height via HTU–NTU method. - **Distillation trays**: Bubble–cap, sieve, valve trays use $k_G$ and $k_L$ correlations. - **Membrane separation**: Overall resistance model $1/K = 1/k_1 + 1/k_m + 1/k_2$ where $k_m$ is the membrane permeability. - **Drying, humidification, leaching**: Each uses the appropriate phase coefficient. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Individual and Overall Coefficients **Problem Setup:** Solute $A$ transfers from a gas bulk to a liquid bulk across a stagnant interface. ### Given Information $$N_A = k_G \, (p_{A,G} - p_{A,i}) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$N_A = k_L \, (C_{A,i} - C_{A,L}) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ $$N_A = K_G \, (p_{A,G} - p_{A}^{\,*}) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ Assume the **equilibrium relation** at the interface is linear: $p_A^{\,*} = m \, C_A$ (Henry's Law form). ### Derivation Steps **Step 1 — Express the gas-side driving force in terms of the total driving force.** We know that at the interface, equilibrium gives $p_{A,i} = m \, C_{A,i}$. Rearranging (i): $$p_{A,G} - p_{A,i} = \frac{N_A}{k_G}$$ Rearranging (ii): $$C_{A,i} - C_{A,L} = \frac{N_A}{k_L}$$ **Step 2 — Use the equilibrium line to convert liquid driving force into gas units.** Multiply (ii) by $m$: $$m (C_{A,i} - C_{A,L}) = m \cdot \frac{N_A}{k_L}$$ Since $m \cdot C_{A,i} = p_{A,i}$ and $m \cdot C_{A,L} = p_{A}^{\,*}$: $$p_{A,i} - p_{A}^{\,*} = \frac{m \, N_A}{k_L}$$ **Step 3 — Add the two gas-unit driving forces.** The total driving force in gas units is the sum of the two segmental driving forces: $$(p_{A,G} - p_{A,i}) + (p_{A,i} - p_{A}^{\,*}) = p_{A,G} - p_{A}^{\,*}$$ Substituting from Step 1 and Step 2: $$\frac{N_A}{k_G} + \frac{m \, N_A}{k_L} = p_{A,G} - p_{A}^{\,*}$$ **Step 4 — Factor out $N_A$ and compare with equation (iii).** $$N_A \left( \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} \right) = p_{A,G} - p_{A}^{\,*}$$ But from (iii): $$\frac{N_A}{K_G} = p_{A,G} - p_{A}^{\,*}$$ **Step 5 — Equate to get the resistance-in-series model.** $$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}}$$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When Does Each Phase Control? | Scenario | Dominant Resistance | Simplified Equation | |---|---|---| | Very soluble gas (e.g., NH₃ in water) | Liquid phase | $K_G \approx k_G$ | | Slightly soluble gas (e.g., O₂ in water) | Liquid phase | $K_G \approx k_L / m$ (very small) | | Highly soluble gas, dilute | Gas phase | $K_L \approx k_L$ | | Resistances comparable | Both phases | Full equation required | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The percent resistance in the gas phase is calculated as: $$\% \text{ Resistance (Gas)} = \frac{1/k_G}{1/K_G} \times 100\%$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Two-Film Conceptual Model — Mass Transfer Pathway The classic **Whitman Two-Film Theory** visualizes mass transfer as a steady-state process through two stagnant films on either side of the interface. ```mermaid flowchart LR bulkG[("Bulk Gas Phase
pA_G or yA_G")] filmG["Gas Film
Thickness delta_G
Resistance 1/kG"] interface(("Interface
pA_i = m xA_i")) filmL["Liquid Film
Thickness delta_L
Resistance 1/kL"] bulkL[("Bulk Liquid Phase
xA_L or CA_L")] eqLine["Equilibrium Line
m = dy*/dx"] bulkG -->|Convection| filmG filmG -->|Diffusion| interface interface -->|Diffusion| filmL filmL -->|Convection| bulkL interface -.->|Governs pA*| eqLine eqLine -.->|Relates pA* to xA_L| bulkL ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Coefficient Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB MT["Mass Transfer Flux N_A"] OVR["Overall Coefficients
Used in Design"] KG["K_G (Gas-Phase Basis)
1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L"] KL["K_L (Liquid-Phase Basis)
1/K_L = 1/mk_G + 1/k_L"] IND["Individual Coefficients
Used in Hydrodynamic Correlations"] KG_ind["k_G (Gas Film)"] KL_ind["k_L (Liquid Film)"] DIM["Dimensionless Numbers
Sh = k_C L / D_AB"] RE["Re (Reynolds)"] SC["Sc (Schmidt)"] COR["Correlations
Sh = a Re^b Sc^0.33"] MEAS["Experimental Methods
Wetted-Wall Column
Buble Column"] PRED["Predictive Models
Film Theory
Penetration Theory
Surface Renewal"] MT --> OVR MT --> IND OVR --> KG OVR --> KL IND --> KG_ind IND --> KL_ind IND --> DIM DIM --> RE DIM --> SC DIM --> COR COR --> MEAS COR --> PRED ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Resistance-in-Series Electrical Analogy ```mermaid flowchart LR V1(("pA_G")) --> R1["R_G = 1/k_G
Gas Film Resistor"] R1 --> Node(("pA_i")) Node --> R2["R_L = m/k_L
Liquid Film Resistor
converted to gas units"] R2 --> V2(("pA*")) Node1(("pA*")) --> R3["R_total = 1/K_G
Overall Resistance"] V1 --> R3 R3 --> V3(("pA*")) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Builder:** The electrical analogy (Ohm's law: $I = V/R$) maps directly to mass transfer: $N_A = \Delta p / R$. Adding resistors in series adds their resistances — exactly as the **two-film model** adds gas and liquid resistances. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper] **Define the mass transfer coefficient. What are its SI units when the driving force is expressed as a concentration difference?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **mass transfer coefficient** $k_C$ is defined as the **proportionality constant** between the **molar flux** of a species and its **concentration driving force** between two locations (typically the bulk phase and the interface). $$N_A = k_C \cdot (C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})$$ **SI Units:** Since $N_A$ is in $\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ and $\Delta C$ is in $\text{kmol/m}^3$, the units of $k_C$ reduce to: $$[k_C] = \frac{\text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}{\text{kmol/m}^3} = \boxed{\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}$$ This is dimensionally equivalent to a **velocity** (hence the alternate name "mass transfer velocity"). **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] **Distinguish between individual and overall mass transfer coefficients with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Individual ($k_G$, $k_L$) | Overall ($K_G$, $K_L$) | |---|---|---| | **Phase Considered** | Single phase resistance | Combined two-phase resistance | | **Driving Force** | Bulk of one phase to interface | Bulk of one phase to equilibrium with the other bulk | | **Example** | $k_G$ = gas-film coefficient in ammonia absorption | $K_G$ = overall gas coefficient for CO₂–NaOH system | | **Use** | Correlate with hydrodynamics | Equipment design via HTU/NTU | The relationship is given by the resistance-in-series model: $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$. **(3 marks)** --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### INTERNAL CHOICE — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Aligned with 2024 Scheme]` **Q.A.** (a) **Derive the relationship** between the overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient $K_G$ and the individual coefficients $k_G$ and $k_L$ for a gas–liquid absorption system. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** (b) **In an absorption experiment**, the following data are obtained at operating conditions: - $k_G = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{kPa)}$ - $k_L = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$ - Equilibrium slope $m = 0.85$ (dimensionless, since $p_A^* = m \, x_A$ in mole fraction basis with total pressure factored) Calculate $K_G$ and identify which phase offers the **dominant resistance**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Q.A.(a) The derivation is identical to Section 3.1 of these notes. **Step-by-step presentation expected by examiner:** 1. State the three flux equations: gas-film, liquid-film, and overall. 2. Convert the liquid-side driving force into gas units using the equilibrium relation $p_{A,i} = m \, C_{A,i}$ and $p_{A}^{\,*} = m \, C_{A,L}$. 3. Recognize that total driving force = sum of segmental driving forces (continuity of flux). 4. Factor out $N_A$ and equate to the overall flux definition. 5. Arrive at the final boxed result. **Final Result:** $$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L} \quad \textbf{...(7 marks)}$$ **Assumptions** (state explicitly for full marks): - Steady-state, 1-D mass transfer - Equilibrium at interface (no interfacial resistance) - Dilute system, no coupling of fluxes - Linear equilibrium relation $p_A^* = m \, C_A$ #### Model Solution — Q.A.(b) **Step 1 — Apply the resistance formula.** $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.** $$\frac{1}{k_G} = \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 8333.3 \; \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{kPa / kmol}$$ $$\frac{m}{k_L} = \frac{0.85}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 340 \; \text{s/m}$$ > **Note:** For unit consistency, we need to convert $k_L$ to compatible gas-side units. If $k_L$ is in m/s and $C$ in kmol/m³, then $k_L$ should be converted using $p = CRT$. For a quick KTU exam, assume the conversion has been done and use: $$\frac{m}{k_L} = 340 \; \text{(compatible gas-side resistance units)}$$ **Step 3 — Add the resistances.** $$\frac{1}{K_G} = 8333.3 + 340 = 8673.3$$ **Step 4 — Invert to get $K_G$.** $$K_G = 1.153 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{kPa)}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the dominant resistance.** $$\% \text{ Gas-side resistance} = \frac{8333.3}{8673.3} \times 100\% = 96.1\%$$ $$\% \text{ Liquid-side resistance} = \frac{340}{8673.3} \times 100\% = 3.9\%$$ **Conclusion:** The **gas phase offers the dominant resistance** (96.1%). The system is said to be **gas-phase controlled**. **(7 marks)** --- ### INTERNAL CHOICE — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative **Q.B.** (a) **Explain the Whitman two-film theory** of mass transfer with a neat sketch.
