Discuss its **limitations**. **(7 marks)** (b) **For a wetted-wall absorption column**, the following correlation is given for the gas-film coefficient: $$k_G = \frac{0.023 \, D_{AB} \, (Re)^{0.83} \, (Sc)^{0.44}}{RT \, d}$$ Given: $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, gas velocity $u = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$, tube diameter $d = 0.025 \text{ m}$, gas density $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $T = 298 \text{ K}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ kPa}\cdot\text{m}^3/(\text{kmol}\cdot\text{K})$. Calculate $k_G$ in $\text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{kPa)}$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Q.B.(a) — Key Points - **Concept**: Two stagnant films (gas film + liquid film) on either side of the interface; mass transfer is by **molecular diffusion** through these films; bulk phases are perfectly mixed. - **Sketch**: Same as Section 4.1 of these notes (bulk → film → interface → film → bulk). - **Key equations**: Fick's law applied across each film; linear concentration profile assumed. - **Limitations** (any three for full marks): 1. Assumes steady state — fails for unsteady industrial processes. 2. Film thickness cannot be measured directly — $k$ is empirical. 3. Ignores turbulence at the interface. 4. For low-solubility gases, penetration theory is more accurate. 5. Equilibrium at the interface is an idealization. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Q.B.(b) **Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds number.** $$Re = \frac{\rho \, u \, d}{\mu} = \frac{1.2 \times 1.5 \times 0.025}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$Re = \frac{0.045}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 2500$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Schmidt number.** $$Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho \, D_{AB}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$Sc = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.6$$ **Step 3 — Plug into the correlation.** $$k_G = \frac{0.023 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times (2500)^{0.83} \times (0.6)^{0.44}}{8.314 \times 298 \times 0.025}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate powers.** $$(2500)^{0.83} \approx 569.4 \quad ; \quad (0.6)^{0.44} \approx 0.793$$ **Step 5 — Compute numerator.** $$\text{Numerator} = 0.023 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 569.4 \times 0.793$$ $$= 0.023 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 451.5 = 2.596 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 6 — Compute denominator.** $$\text{Denominator} = 8.314 \times 298 \times 0.025 = 61.94$$ **Step 7 — Final result.** $$k_G = \frac{2.596 \times 10^{-4}}{61.94} = 4.19 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{kPa)}$$ **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing $m$ and $K$:** $m$ is the **equilibrium slope** at the operating point; do not confuse with overall mass transfer coefficient $K_G$ or $K_L$. > 2. **Unit Mismatch:** When using $k_L$ (m/s) in a gas-units equation, you **must** convert via $C = p/RT$. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for ignoring this. > 3. **Forgetting to express $D_{AB}$ units consistently** in correlations — always convert to m²/s and check $Re$, $Sc$ are dimensionless. > 4. **Direction of driving force:** $(C_{A,i} - C_{A,L})$ for absorption INTO liquid; $(p_{A,G} - p_{A,i})$ for absorption FROM gas. Reversing either is a sign convention error and loses marks. > 5. **Two-film limitations question** — students often forget to mention that **turbulence** and **interfacial renewal** are ignored. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024** - ✅ Mass transfer coefficient $k$ is the **proportionality constant** between **flux** and **driving force**; SI unit is **m/s** when driving force is in $\text{kmol/m}^3$. - ✅ **Individual** coefficients ($k_G$, $k_L$) refer to single-phase resistance; **Overall** ($K_G$, $K_L$) refer to combined two-phase resistance. - ✅ Master equation: $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ — the **resistances add in series** (electrical analogy). - ✅ Use $K_L$ form when working in liquid concentration units: $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{m \, k_G} + \dfrac{1}{k_L}$. - ✅ $m$ = **slope of the equilibrium line** at the operating point, NOT the distribution ratio. - ✅ Dominant resistance phase is the one with $>70\text{–}80\%$ of total resistance. - ✅ Dimensionless numbers: $Sh = k_C L / D_{AB}$, $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$, $Re = \rho u L / \mu$. - ✅ Standard correlation form: $Sh = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Sc^{0.33}$ (Chilton–Colburn analogy). - ✅ Two-film theory: bulk → film → interface → film → bulk; steady-state, stagnant films, equilibrium at interface. - ✅ Penetration & surface-renewal theories improve on the two-film model for **unsteady/short-contact** processes (e.g., bubble columns). - ✅ For **highly soluble gases**: $K_G \approx k_G$ (gas controls). - ✅ For **sparingly soluble gases**: $K_L \approx k_L$ (liquid controls). - ✅ $J_D = Sh / (Re \cdot Sc^{1/3})$ is the Chilton–Colburn $J$-factor — used to **predict $k$ from heat-transfer data** via Reynolds analogy.

topics.title: Dimensionless Groups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dimensionless Groups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensionless Groups** (also called **Dimensional Numbers**, **Pi Groups**, or **Similarity Parameters**) are pure numbers obtained by multiplying a physical variable by an appropriate combination of other variables such that all fundamental units (M, L, T, etc.) cancel out, leaving a number independent of the system of units used.

In Mass Transfer Operations, dimensionless groups are used to:
- **Correlate experimental data** across different scales and operating conditions.
- **Establish analogies** between momentum, heat, and mass transfer (Reynolds, Prandtl–Schmidt, Colburn analogies).
- **Predict transport coefficients** (e.g., mass transfer coefficient $k_c$) from empirical correlations of the form $Sh = f(Re, Sc)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional Homogeneity Theorem (Buckingham Pi Theorem):** Any physically meaningful equation involving $n$ variables expressed in $k$ fundamental dimensions can be reduced to a relationship between $n-k$ independent dimensionless groups.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are cooking **dosa batter** on a tawa:
- The batter's flow is governed by how fast you pour (velocity), how thick it is (viscosity), and the size of the tawa.
- A chemist in **Kerala** will measure in cm and g, while one in the **USA** will use inches and pounds.
- A **dimensionless group** is like a *universal recipe ratio*. Instead of saying "add 200 g of flour," you say "use a **3 : 1** flour-to-water ratio" — the number works everywhere, regardless of units.

Just as the 3:1 ratio universally describes the batter, dimensionless groups like **Re = 5 × 10⁴** universally describe a flow regime (laminar/turbulent) in any unit system.

### 1.3 Fundamental Dimensions Used

| Symbol | Dimension | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Mass | kg |
| $L$ | Length | m |
| $T$ | Time | s |
| $\Theta$ | Temperature | K |
| $N$ | Amount of substance | mol |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Key Properties

The following are the **central transport properties** used to construct all mass transfer dimensionless groups:

> **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$)** — resistance of a fluid to deformation, in **kg/(m·s)** or **Pa·s**
> **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu = \mu/\rho$)** — momentum diffusivity, in **m²/s**
> **Mass Diffusivity ($D_{AB}$)** — molecular diffusion coefficient, in **m²/s**
> **Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha = k/(\rho C_p)$)** — heat diffusivity, in **m²/s**
> **Density ($\rho$)** — in **kg/m³**
> **Characteristic Length ($L_c$)** — geometry-dependent, in **m**
> **Velocity ($v$)** — in **m/s**
> **Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_c$)** — in **m/s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Dimensionless Groups? — The Four Pillars

1. **Scale-up:** Laboratory data (small equipment) can be reliably applied to industrial-scale (large) equipment if dimensionless groups are matched.
2. **Reduction of Variables:** A problem with 8 variables can often be reduced to 3–4 dimensionless groups.
3. **Physical Insight:** Each group represents a ratio of competing physical effects (e.g., inertia vs. viscosity).
4. **Analogy Building:** Reynolds analogy, Chilton–Colburn analogy, and Prandtl–Taylor analogy all rely on dimensionless ratios.

### 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Framework

**Step 1:** List all variables influencing the phenomenon.
**Step 2:** Choose repeating variables (one from each fundamental dimension).
**Step 3:** Form non-repeating variables divided by products of repeating variables raised to unknown exponents.
**Step 4:** Force dimensional homogeneity → solve for exponents.
**Step 5:** Each resulting $\Pi$ term is one dimensionless group.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Framework:** For forced convection mass transfer, the variables are $k_c, L, D_{AB}, \rho, \mu, v$. With $k = 3$ (M, L, T) and $n = 6$, we obtain $n - k = 3$ dimensionless groups, classically identified as **Sherwood, Reynolds, and Schmidt numbers**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the **Name, Symbol, Formula, Physical Meaning, and Order of Magnitude** for every group below. This is the single most-tested item in KTU Module 1.

| # | Group Name | Symbol | Formula | Ratio Represented | Typical Range (Mass Transfer) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re$ | $\dfrac{\rho v L_c}{\mu} = \dfrac{v L_c}{\nu}$ | Inertial forces / Viscous forces | $10^3$ – $10^6$ |
| 2 | Schmidt Number | $Sc$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}} = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | Momentum diffusivity / Mass diffusivity | $0.6$ (gases) – $3000$ (liquids) |
| 3 | Sherwood Number | $Sh$ | $\dfrac{k_c L_c}{D_{AB}}$ | Convective mass transfer / Diffusive mass transfer | $2$ – $10^4$ |
| 4 | Peclet Number (Mass) | $Pe_{AB}$ | $\dfrac{v L_c}{D_{AB}} = Re \cdot Sc$ | Bulk mass transport / Diffusive transport | $10^{-3}$ – $10^6$ |
| 5 | Lewis Number | $Le$ | $\dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{Sc}{Pr}$ | Thermal diffusivity / Mass diffusivity | $0.1$ – $10$ |
| 6 | Grashof Number (Mass) | $Gr_{AB}$ | $\dfrac{g L_c^3 (\rho_A - \rho)}{\nu^2}$ | Buoyancy / Viscous forces | $10^2$ – $10^{10}$ |
| 7 | Stanton Number | $St$ | $\dfrac{k_c}{v} = \dfrac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc}$ | Mass transferred / Convective capacity | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Power Number | $N_p$ | $\dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5}$ | Resistance force / Inertial force | $1$ – $10$ |
| 9 | Weber Number | $We$ | $\dfrac{\rho v^2 L_c}{\sigma}$ | Inertia / Surface tension | $10^{-2}$ – $10^4$ |
| 10 | Froude Number | $Fr$ | $\dfrac{v^2}{g L_c}$ | Inertial / Gravitational | $10^{-2}$ – $10^2$ |

> **Note on Table Notation:** Vertical absolute-value bars (e.g., $Sh = \vert Sh \vert$) are intentionally written as $\vert$ to avoid breaking KTU markdown tables.

### 2.4 Physical Significance — The "Why" Behind Each Group

> **Reynolds Number ($Re$):** Tells you **flow regime**. $Re < 2100$ = laminar; $Re > 4000$ = turbulent. Low $Re$ → diffusion dominates; high $Re$ → convection dominates.

> **Schmidt Number ($Sc$):** Compares how fast momentum spreads versus how fast mass spreads. For gases $Sc \approx 0.6$–$1.0$ (similar rates); for liquids $Sc \gg 1$ (momentum spreads much faster than mass).

> **Sherwood Number ($Sh$):** Quantifies **enhancement of mass transfer due to convection** over pure diffusion. $Sh = 2$ corresponds to pure molecular diffusion past a sphere; $Sh \gg 2$ indicates convective augmentation.

> **Peclet Number ($Pe$):** High $Pe$ → bulk flow overwhelms diffusion (typical in packed columns with high liquid velocities).

> **Lewis Number ($Le$):** When $Le = 1$, heat and mass boundary layers coincide (used in simultaneous heat–mass transfer, e.g., cooling towers).

> **Grashof Number ($Gr$):** In **natural convection** mass transfer (no forced flow), $Gr$ replaces $Re$ in correlations.

> **Stanton Number ($St$):** Fraction of the bulk mass flux that is transferred to the wall — directly the "efficiency" of the contact.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Packed Columns:** $Sh = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.83} \cdot Sc^{0.33}$ (Dobbins correlation for wet packed towers).
- **Wet-Bulb Thermometry:** Uses $Le$ to link psychrometric charts.
- **Gas Absorption:** $Sh$ correlations give $k_c$, the cornerstone of the two-film theory.
- **Drying Operations:** $Bi_m = k_c L_c / D_{AB}$ (Biot for mass) tells whether internal diffusion controls.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number (Dimensional Analysis)

**Step 1 — Identify Variables:** Pressure drop ($\Delta P$) in a pipe depends on pipe diameter ($D$), length ($L$), fluid velocity ($v$), density ($\rho$), and viscosity ($\mu$).
Variables: $\Delta P, D, L, v, \rho, \mu$ → $n = 6$
Fundamental dimensions: $M, L, T$ → $k = 3$
Expected $\Pi$ groups: $6 - 3 = 3$

**Step 2 — Choose Repeating Variables:** $D, v, \rho$ (covers M, L, T).

**Step 3 — Form the $\Pi$ Groups:**

$$\Pi_1 = \dfrac{\Delta P}{D^{a_1} v^{b_1} \rho^{c_1}}$$

**Step 4 — Force Homogeneity:**

$$\left[ M^1 L^{-1} T^{-2} \right] = \left[ L \right]^{a_1} \left[ L T^{-1} \right]^{b_1} \left[ M L^{-3} \right]^{c_1}$$

Equating exponents of M, L, T:
- $M: 1 = c_1$ → $c_1 = 1$
- $T: -2 = -b_1$ → $b_1 = 2$
- $L: -1 = a_1 + b_1 - 3c_1 = a_1 + 2 - 3 = a_1 - 1$ → $a_1 = 0$

Therefore:

$$\Pi_1 = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho v^2}$$

This is the **Euler Number** (pressure coefficient).

$$\Pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{D^{a_2} v^{b_2} \rho^{c_2}}$$

$$\left[ M L^{-1} T^{-1} \right] = \left[ L \right]^{a_2} \left[ L T^{-1} \right]^{b_2} \left[ M L^{-3} \right]^{c_2}$$

- $M: 1 = c_2$ → $c_2 = 1$
- $T: -1 = -b_2$ → $b_2 = 1$
- $L: -1 = a_2 + 1 - 3 = a_2 - 2$ → $a_2 = 1$

$$\Pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{D v \rho} = \dfrac{1}{Re}$$

$$\Pi_3 = \dfrac{L}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Final Functional Relationship:**

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho v^2} = \phi \left( Re, \dfrac{L}{D} \right)$$

This is the famous **Fanning/Darcy–Weisbach equation** in dimensionless form.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sherwood Number from First Principles

**Starting from the mass transfer flux definition:**

$$N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

**Step 1 — Non-dimensionalize concentration and distance:**

Define dimensionless concentration $C^* = \dfrac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}}$ and dimensionless distance $y^* = y/L_c$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the convective diffusion equation:**

$$v_x \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = D_{AB} \left( \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Divide by characteristic scales $v_0 (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) / L_c$ and $D_{AB} (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})/L_c^2$:**

$$\dfrac{v_x L_c}{D_{AB}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial C^*}{\partial x^*} + \dfrac{v_y L_c}{D_{AB}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial C^*}{\partial y^*} = \dfrac{\partial^2 C^*}{\partial x^{*2}} + \dfrac{\partial^2 C^*}{\partial y^{*2}}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the coefficient $v L_c / D_{AB}$ as the Peclet Number:**

$$Pe_{AB} \cdot \dfrac{\partial C^*}{\partial x^*} + \text{similar terms} = \nabla^2 C^*$$

**Step 5 — Boundary condition at the surface ($y^* = 0$):**

The flux is $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial y}\Big|_{y=0} = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$.

Non-dimensionalizing:

$$k_c = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{L_c} \cdot \left( -\dfrac{\partial C^*}{\partial y^*} \right)_{y^*=0}$$

$$\dfrac{k_c L_c}{D_{AB}} = -\left( \dfrac{\partial C^*}{\partial y^*} \right)_{y^*=0} = Sh$$

**Conclusion:** The Sherwood number is the **dimensionless concentration gradient at the wall**, and it is a function of $Re$, $Sc$, and geometry:

$$Sh = f(Re, Sc, \text{geometry})$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Relationship $Pe_{AB} = Re \cdot Sc$

Starting from the individual definitions:

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho v L_c}{\mu} = \dfrac{v L_c}{\nu}$$

$$Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$$

**Multiplying term by term:**

$$Re \cdot Sc = \dfrac{\rho v L_c}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$$

**Cancel $\mu$ and $\rho$:**

$$Re \cdot Sc = \dfrac{v L_c}{D_{AB}}$$

**Recognize the right-hand side as the mass Peclet number:**

$$\boxed{Pe_{AB} = Re \cdot Sc}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Lewis Number Relationship

$$Le = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{k / (\rho C_p)}{D_{AB}}$$

$$Pr = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha} = \dfrac{\mu / \rho}{\alpha} \Rightarrow \alpha = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \cdot Pr}$$

Substituting:

$$Le = \dfrac{\mu}{(\rho \cdot Pr) \cdot D_{AB}} = \dfrac{1}{Pr} \cdot \dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}} = \dfrac{Sc}{Pr}$$

$$\boxed{Le = \dfrac{Sc}{Pr}}$$

### 3.5 The Three Analogies (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 1 — Reynolds Analogy (for $Pr = Sc = 1$):**

$$St_m \approx \dfrac{C_f}{2}$$

where $C_f$ is the friction factor.

> **Analogy 2 — Prandtl Analogy (for $Pr \neq 1$, $Sc \neq 1$):**

$$St_m = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1 + 5 \sqrt{C_f/2} (Sc - 1)}$$

> **Analogy 3 — Chilton–Colburn Analogy (most widely used in mass transfer):**

$$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$$

$$j_H = St_H \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$$

$$j_M = j_H$$

where $j_D$ is the **mass transfer factor**. This gives the **Chilton–Colburn $j$-factor analogy**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Dimensionless Groups in Mass Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dimensionless Groups in Mass Transfer] --> B[Flow Characterization]
    A --> C[Transport Property Ratios]
    A --> D[Convective Strength]
    A --> E[Combined Groups]

    B --> B1[Reynolds Number Re]
    B --> B2[Grashof Number Gr]
    B --> B3[Froude Number Fr]

    C --> C1[Schmidt Number Sc]
    C --> C2[Prandtl Number Pr]
    C --> C3[Lewis Number Le]

    D --> D1[Sherwood Number Sh]
    D --> D2[Stanton Number St]
    D --> D3[Nusselt Number Nu]

    E --> E1[Peclet Number Pe equals Re times Sc]
    E --> E2[Chilton Colburn j factor]
    E --> E3[Biot Number Bi]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Mass Transfer Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Variables kc, v, L, DAB, rho, mu] --> P2{Dimensional Analysis via Buckingham Pi}
    P2 --> P3[Pi 1 Sh equals kc L over DAB]
    P2 --> P4[Pi 2 Re equals rho v L over mu]
    P2 --> P5[Pi 3 Sc equals mu over rho DAB]
    P3 --> P6[Empirical Correlation Sh equals a Re to b Sc to c]
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Output kc predicted for scale up]
    P6 --> P8[Output kc predicted for design]
```

### 4.3 Analogy Triangle (Heat–Mass–Momentum)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MT[MOMENTUM TRANSFER] -->|friction factor Cf over 2| AN1[Analogy Bridge]
    HT[HEAT TRANSFER] -->|jH equals St Pr to 2 over 3| AN1
    MASS[MASS TRANSFER] -->|jD equals St Sc to 2 over 3| AN1
    AN1 --> RESULT[jM equals jH equals Cf over 2]
```

### 4.4 Boundary Layer Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BLP[Bulk Phase] -->|convective transport| HBL[Hydrodynamic BL]
    HBL -->|via Sc| MBL[Mass Transfer BL]
    HBL -->|via Pr| TBL[Thermal BL]
    MBL -->|via Le| TBL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Sherwood Number and state its physical significance in mass transfer.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition:** The Sherwood number is a dimensionless group that represents the ratio of convective mass transfer to diffusive mass transfer, defined as:
> $$Sh = \dfrac{k_c L_c}{D_{AB}}$$
> **Significance:** $Sh$ quantifies the **enhancement of mass transfer due to convection** over pure molecular diffusion. A value of $Sh = 2$ corresponds to pure diffusion past a sphere. When $Sh \gg 2$, convection is dominant. It is the **mass transfer analog of the Nusselt number** in heat transfer and is used as the dependent variable in most mass transfer correlations of the form $Sh = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Sc^c$.
> *(Award: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for ratio meaning, 1 mark for physical significance.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Distinguish between Schmidt Number and Peclet Number.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Basis | Schmidt Number ($Sc$) | Peclet Number ($Pe_{AB}$) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Formula | $\mu/(\rho D_{AB})$ | $v L_c / D_{AB}$ |
> | Represents | Momentum diffusivity / Mass diffusivity | Bulk mass transport / Diffusive transport |
> | Depends on | Fluid properties only | Flow velocity and geometry |
> | Typical value | Gases: 0.6–1.0; Liquids: 100–3000 | 10⁻³ to 10⁶ |
> **Key relation:** $Pe_{AB} = Re \cdot Sc$.
> *(Award: 1 mark for each correct formula, 1 mark for distinction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** **(a)** Derive the Buckingham Pi-Theorem-based dimensional analysis for forced-convection mass transfer and obtain the functional relationship $Sh = \phi(Re, Sc)$ for a single sphere in a flowing fluid. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A sphere of diameter 25 mm is exposed to a flowing gas at 25 °C and 1 atm. The gas velocity is 5 m/s, the kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, and the diffusivity $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Using the correlation $Sh = 2 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$ (Ranz–Marshall correlation), calculate the mass transfer coefficient $k_c$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Variables influencing $k_c$ for forced convection past a sphere: $k_c, D, v, \rho, \mu, D_{AB}$ → $n = 6$.
**Step 2:** Dimensions involved: $M, L, T$ → $k = 3$.
**Step 3:** Number of $\Pi$ groups = $6 - 3 = 3$.
**Step 4:** Choose repeating variables: $D, v, \rho$.
**Step 5:** Form $\Pi_1$ with $k_c$:

$$\Pi_1 = \dfrac{k_c}{D^{a} v^{b} \rho^{c}}$$

$$\left[ L T^{-1} \right] = \left[ L \right]^{a} \left[ L T^{-1} \right]^{b} \left[ M L^{-3} \right]^{c}$$

- $M: 0 = c \Rightarrow c = 0$
- $T: -1 = -b \Rightarrow b = 1$
- $L: 1 = a + b = a + 1 \Rightarrow a = 0$

$$\Pi_1 = \dfrac{k_c}{v}$$

**Step 6:** Form $\Pi_2$ with $\mu$:

$$\Pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{D^{a'} v^{b'} \rho^{c'}}$$

Solving similarly: $a' = 1, b' = 1, c' = 1$.

$$\Pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{D v \rho} = \dfrac{1}{Re}$$

**Step 7:** Form $\Pi_3$ with $D_{AB}$:

$$\Pi_3 = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{D^{a''} v^{b''} \rho^{c''}}$$

Solving: $a'' = 1, b'' = 1, c'' = 0$.

$$\Pi_3 = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{D v}$$

**Step 8:** Combine $\Pi_2$ and $\Pi_3$ to get classical groups:

$$\dfrac{\Pi_1}{\Pi_3} = \dfrac{k_c}{v} \cdot \dfrac{D v}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{k_c D}{D_{AB}} = Sh$$

$$\Pi_2^{-1} = \dfrac{D v \rho}{\mu} = Re$$

$$\dfrac{\Pi_3}{\Pi_2} = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{D v} \cdot \dfrac{D v \rho}{\mu} = \dfrac{\rho D_{AB}}{\mu} = \dfrac{1}{Sc}$$

**Step 9:** Final functional form:

$$\boxed{Sh = \phi(Re, Sc) \text{ for a given geometry}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, isothermal flow.
2. Dilute system (constant $D_{AB}$).
3. No chemical reaction at the surface.
4. Newtonian fluid.
5. Single sphere (no interference effects).

*(Mark split: 1 mark each for variable list, choice of repeating variables, three $\Pi$ groups, recognition of classical groups, and final relationship; 2 marks for assumptions.)*

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $D = 0.025$ m, $v = 5$ m/s, $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s.

**Step 1:** Calculate Reynolds number:

$$Re = \dfrac{v D}{\nu} = \dfrac{5 \times 0.025}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{0.125}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 8333.33$$

**Step 2:** Calculate Schmidt number:

$$Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.6$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Ranz–Marshall correlation:

$$Sh = 2 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$

$$Re^{0.5} = \sqrt{8333.33} = 91.287$$

$$Sc^{1/3} = (0.6)^{1/3} = 0.8434$$

$$Sh = 2 + 0.6 \times 91.287 \times 0.8434 = 2 + 46.21 = 48.21$$

**Step 4:** Convert to $k_c$:

$$Sh = \dfrac{k_c D}{D_{AB}} \Rightarrow k_c = \dfrac{Sh \cdot D_{AB}}{D} = \dfrac{48.21 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.025}$$

$$k_c = \dfrac{1.20525 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025} = 0.04821 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $k_c \approx 4.82 \times 10^{-2}$ m/s.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for $Re$, 1 mark for $Sc$, 2 marks for $Sh$ calculation, 1 mark for $Sh$ to $k_c$ conversion, 1 mark for final answer with units.)*

---

#### ✅ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Reynolds, Prandtl, and Chilton–Colburn analogies** between momentum, heat, and mass transfer. State the Chilton–Colburn $j$-factor analogy clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a packed absorption column, the gas-phase mass transfer coefficient is given by the Chilton–Colburn analogy $j_D = 0.023 \cdot Re^{-0.2}$. For a gas with $Re = 5000$, $Sc = 0.7$, and velocity $v = 1.2$ m/s, calculate (i) the mass transfer factor $j_D$, (ii) the Stanton number $St_m$, and (iii) the mass transfer coefficient $k_c$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Reynolds Analogy (1874):**
Reynolds postulated that for a fluid with $Pr = 1$ and $Sc = 1$ (i.e., thermal and momentum diffusivities equal), the heat and momentum transfer are analogous.

$$St_H = St_m = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$$

**Limitation:** Only valid for gases where $Pr \approx Sc \approx 1$. Fails for liquids.

**2. Prandtl Analogy (1910):**
Prandtl extended Reynolds analogy to fluids with $Pr \neq 1$ by considering a laminar sub-layer near the wall.

$$St_H = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1 + 5 \sqrt{C_f/2} (Pr - 1)}$$

**Limitation:** Only considers laminar sub-layer, ignores buffer region; still approximate.

**3. von Kármán Analogy (1939):**
Added a buffer zone correction — more accurate but more complex.

**4. Chilton–Colburn Analogy (1934) — Most Widely Used:**
Empirically modified the Reynolds analogy using experimental data. Introduced the $j$-factor:

$$j_H = St_H \cdot Pr^{2/3} \quad \text{(heat transfer factor)}$$

$$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} \quad \text{(mass transfer factor)}$$

$$\boxed{j_H = j_D = \dfrac{C_f}{2}}$$

**Significance:** $j$-factor analogy is valid for **$0.6 < Sc < 3000$** and $0.6 < Pr < 100$, covering both gases and most liquids. It is the workhorse of industrial mass transfer design.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for Reynolds, 2 marks for Prandtl, 3 marks for Chilton–Colburn with formula.)*

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $j_D = 0.023 \cdot Re^{-0.2}$, $Re = 5000$, $Sc = 0.7$, $v = 1.2$ m/s.

**(i) Mass transfer factor $j_D$:**

$$j_D = 0.023 \times (5000)^{-0.2}$$

$$(5000)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln 5000} = e^{0.2 \times 8.517} = e^{1.7034} = 5.493$$

$$j_D = \dfrac{0.023}{5.493} = 4.187 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(ii) Stanton number $St_m$:**

$$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} \Rightarrow St_m = \dfrac{j_D}{Sc^{2/3}}$$

$$Sc^{2/3} = (0.7)^{2/3} = 0.788$$

$$St_m = \dfrac{4.187 \times 10^{-3}}{0.788} = 5.313 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(iii) Mass transfer coefficient $k_c$:**

$$St_m = \dfrac{k_c}{v} \Rightarrow k_c = St_m \cdot v$$

$$k_c = 5.313 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2 = 6.376 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $j_D = 4.19 \times 10^{-3}$
- (ii) $St_m = 5.31 \times 10^{-3}$
- (iii) $k_c = 6.38 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s

*(Mark split: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 1 mark for units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $k_c$:** Forgetting that $Sh$ gives $k_c$ in m/s, not kg/(m²·s). Always use $D_{AB}$ in m²/s and $L$ in m.
> 2. **Wrong $\nu$ vs. $D_{AB}$ substitution:** $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ — students often write $Sc = D_{AB} / \nu$ (inverted). Result is off by orders of magnitude.
> 3. **Forgetting the "2" in Ranz–Marshall:** $Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Sc^{1/3}$. The constant 2 represents pure diffusion contribution; omitting it gives a wrong low-Re limit.
> 4. **Confusing Pe and Peclet:** Mass Peclet $Pe_{AB} = v L_c / D_{AB}$, NOT $v L_c / \alpha$ (that is thermal Peclet).
> 5. **Chilton–Colburn exponent error:** The exponent is **2/3**, not 1/3 or 1/2.
> 6. **Dimensional group identification:** Examiner expects **Name → Symbol → Formula → Ratio Meaning**. All four elements are mandatory for full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Dimensionless Groups**

- **Definition:** Dimensionless groups are unit-free ratios expressing the relative magnitude of two competing physical effects.
- **Buckingham Pi Theorem:** $n$ variables, $k$ fundamental dimensions → $(n - k)$ independent $\Pi$ groups.
- **Master Formulas (must memorize cold):**
  - $Re = \rho v L_c / \mu$
  - $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB}) = \nu / D_{AB}$
  - $Sh = k_c L_c / D_{AB}$
  - $Pe_{AB} = v L_c / D_{AB} = Re \cdot Sc$
  - $Le = \alpha / D_{AB} = Sc / Pr$
  - $St_m = k_c / v = Sh / (Re \cdot Sc)$
  - $Gr_{AB} = g L_c^3 \Delta \rho / \rho \nu^2$
  - $j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = C_f/2$
- **Key Analogies to Remember:**
  - Reynolds: $St = C_f / 2$ (valid for $Pr = Sc = 1$).
  - Prandtl: Includes $Pr$ correction for laminar sub-layer.
  - **Chilton–Colburn: $j_H = j_D = C_f/2$** — the **most important** for KTU.
- **Key Relationships:**
  - $Pe_{AB} = Re \cdot Sc$
  - $Le = Sc / Pr$
  - $Sh = St_m \cdot Re \cdot Sc$
  - $Bi_m = k_c L_c / D_{AB} = Sh$ (Biot for mass equals Sherwood)
- **Typical Ranges:**
  - $Sc_{gases} \approx 0.6$–$1.0$
  - $Sc_{liquids} \approx 100$–$3000$
  - $Le \approx 1$ for gases (heat and mass transfer analogous); $Le \ll 1$ for liquids.
- **Standard Correlations to Know:**
  - Ranz–Marshall: $Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Sc^{1/3}$ (sphere, $1 < Re < 10^5$).
  - Dittus–Boelter analog: $Sh = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Sc^{0.33}$ (turbulent pipe flow).
  - Frössling: $Sh = 2 + 0.552 Re^{0.5} Sc^{1/3}$ (droplet evaporation).
- **Engineering Uses:** $Sh$ gives $k_c$ (used in two-film theory); $Re$ selects flow regime; $Sc$ identifies rate-limiting step (gas vs. liquid); $j_D$ enables scale-up.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive" a dimensionless group, **always** show (1) the variable list, (2) the choice of repeating variables, (3) the $\Pi$-group construction, and (4) the final identification. A derivation without these four steps earns **zero** marks on a 7-mark KTU sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analogy Between Mass, Heat and Momentum Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analogy Between Mass, Heat and Momentum Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Analogy Between Mass, Heat and Momentum Transfer** is a unified theoretical framework in transport phenomena that establishes a quantitative correspondence among three seemingly distinct molecular and turbulent transport processes. These three processes are governed by analogous differential equations and, under specific boundary conditions, exhibit mathematically equivalent flux–gradient relationships.

The three transfer mechanisms are:

1. **Momentum Transfer** — governed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**
2. **Heat Transfer** — governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**
3. **Mass Transfer** — governed by **Fick's Law of Diffusion**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The analogies of Reynolds, Prandtl, von Kármán, and Chilton–Colburn are the **core high-yield concepts** in Module 1. Expect 14-mark derivation questions and 3-mark definitional questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 The Three Governing Flux Equations (Steady, One-Dimensional)

| Process | Flux | Driving Force | Proportionality Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| Momentum | $\tau_{yx}$ | $-\dfrac{dV_x}{dy}$ (velocity gradient) | Dynamic Viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s) |
| Heat | $q_y$ | $-\dfrac{dT}{dy}$ (temperature gradient) | Thermal Conductivity $k$ (W/m·K) |
| Mass | $N_{A,y}$ | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dy}$ (concentration gradient) | Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ (m²/s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three fluxes follow the same mathematical form: **Flux = (Transport Property) × (Negative Gradient of Driving Potential)**. This structural identity is the *root cause* of the analogy.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a crowded railway station platform (the boundary layer):**
>
> - **Momentum Transfer** → Imagine a fast train (high-velocity free stream) moving past people standing at the platform edge. The train drags the people along, transferring **momentum** from the train to the platform crowd. The faster the train, the more momentum is "pushed" onto the people.
>
> - **Heat Transfer** → Now imagine the train's engine is hot (high temperature), and the platform is cold. Heat energy flows from the hot engine to the cold platform crowd, raising their body temperature.
>
> - **Mass Transfer** → Imagine the train is releasing perfume vapor (concentration of A) into still air (zero concentration). The perfume molecules diffuse from the train toward the platform crowd.
>
> **The KEY insight:** In all three cases, the transfer happens because of a **gradient** (velocity, temperature, or concentration), and the *rate* of transfer depends on a **transport property** ($\mu$, $k$, or $D_{AB}$). The mathematical similarity of the three flux equations is what allows us to **predict one transfer rate from another**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transport Equations in Turbulent Flow (Total Flux)

In turbulent flow, the instantaneous velocity, temperature, and concentration can be split into time-averaged and fluctuating components (Reynolds decomposition). The **total flux** becomes the sum of a *molecular* contribution and a *turbulent (eddy)* contribution.

**Total Momentum Flux:**

$$\tau_{yx}^{total} = (\mu + \rho \varepsilon_M)\frac{d \langle V_x \rangle}{dy}$$

**Total Heat Flux:**

$$q_y^{total} = -(k + \rho c_p \varepsilon_H)\frac{d \langle T \rangle}{dy}$$

**Total Mass Flux:**

$$N_{A,y}^{total} = -(D_{AB} + \varepsilon_D)\frac{d \langle C_A \rangle}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_M, \varepsilon_H, \varepsilon_D$ = **eddy diffusivities** (turbulent transport coefficients) for momentum, heat, and mass
- $\langle \cdot \rangle$ denotes time-averaged quantity

## 2.2 Why the Analogy Exists — The Common Foundation

- **Conservation of mass, energy, and momentum** are all derived from the same statistical-mechanical principles.
- In the **laminar sublayer** of a turbulent boundary layer, molecular transport dominates and the three processes behave analogously.
- In the **turbulent core**, eddy transport dominates, and if $\varepsilon_M = \varepsilon_H = \varepsilon_D$, the three transfer rates are numerically proportional.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds' Original Hypothesis (1874):** *In turbulent flow, the mechanisms of momentum, heat, and mass transfer are identical, so their transfer coefficients can be directly related.*

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Why the Analogy Works

1. **Start with three identical flux equations** (Newton, Fourier, Fick).
2. **Assume Pr = Sc = 1**, so the molecular diffusivities ($\nu = \alpha = D_{AB}$) are equal.
3. **Assume turbulent flow** where the *eddy* diffusivities dominate, and set $\varepsilon_M = \varepsilon_H = \varepsilon_D$.
4. **Equate the integrated form** of the flux equations across the boundary layer.
5. **Result:** A direct proportionality between the **friction factor** ($C_f$) and the **Stanton number** ($St$), which is the *Reynolds Analogy*.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master every equation in this table — it is your single source of truth for Module 1 derivations.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dV}{dy}$ | Valid for laminar flow |
| 2 | Fourier's Law | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dy}$ | Conduction form |
| 3 | Fick's Law | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dy}$ | One-directional diffusion |
| 4 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/s |
| 5 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | m²/s |
| 6 | **Prandtl Number** | $\Pr = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha} = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | Ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity |
| 7 | **Schmidt Number** | $\Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity |
| 8 | **Lewis Number** | $\Le = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{\Sc}{\Pr}$ | Ratio of thermal to mass diffusivity |
| 9 | Friction Coefficient (local) | $C_f = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2}$ | Local skin-friction coefficient |
| 10 | **Stanton Number (Heat)** | $St_H = \dfrac{h}{\rho V c_p}$ | Heat-transfer Stanton number |
| 11 | **Stanton Number (Mass)** | $St_D = \dfrac{k_c}{V}$ | Mass-transfer Stanton number |
| 12 | **Reynolds Analogy** | $St_H = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$ | Valid only for **Pr = 1** |
| 13 | **Prandtl Analogy** | $St_H = \dfrac{C_f / 2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,(\Pr - 1)}$ | Includes laminar sublayer correction |
| 14 | **Von Kármán Analogy** | $St_H = \dfrac{C_f / 2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\left[(\Pr - 1) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5\Pr + 1}{6}\right)\right]}$ | Adds buffer-layer correction |
| 15 | **Chilton–Colburn j-Factor (Heat)** | $j_H = St_H \cdot \Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$ | Empirical, valid for $\Pr \in (0.6, 60)$ |
| 16 | **Chilton–Colburn j-Factor (Mass)** | $j_D = St_D \cdot \Sc^{2/3} = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$ | Empirical, valid for $\Sc \in (0.6, 3000)$ |
| 17 | **Combined Chilton–Colburn** | $j_H = j_D$ | $\dfrac{h}{\rho V c_p}\Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{k_c}{V}\Sc^{2/3}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat Exchanger Design:** When heat-transfer data is unavailable but friction data exists (e.g., for a new gas), use Reynolds analogy to *estimate heat-transfer coefficients from pressure-drop measurements alone*.
- **Cooling Towers & Humidification:** Use the $j_H = j_D$ analogy to relate water-cooling rates (heat) to water-vapor pickup rates (mass).
- **Chemical Reactor Cooling Coils:** Use the analogies to extrapolate mass-transfer data to heat-transfer scenarios when geometries are similar.
- **Boundary Layer Theory in Aerospace:** The analogies form the foundation for predicting skin friction, heat soak, and ablation on re-entry vehicles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Examiners award **full marks** only when (i) the starting assumption is clearly stated, (ii) each algebraic step is justified, and (iii) the final result is boxed with the conditions of validity. Follow the derivation style shown below precisely.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Analogy (Most Important — Frequently Asked)

### **Starting Point: Prandtl's Mixing-Length Concept**

Prandtl proposed that the *eddy viscosity* $\varepsilon_M$ can be expressed analogously to molecular viscosity:

$$\tau^{turb} = \rho \varepsilon_M \frac{dV}{dy}$$

For momentum, heat, and mass, the turbulent fluxes are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau^{turb} &= \rho \varepsilon_M \frac{dV}{dy} \\
\frac{q^{turb}}{\rho c_p} &= \varepsilon_H \frac{dT}{dy} \\
N_A^{turb} &= \varepsilon_D \frac{dC_A}{dy}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Assume $\varepsilon_M = \varepsilon_H = \varepsilon_D = \varepsilon$** (the *Reynolds hypothesis*):

When $\Pr = \Sc = 1$, the molecular diffusivities are equal, and in the fully turbulent region the eddy diffusivities also collapse to a common value.

**Step 2 — Write the total fluxes** (molecular + turbulent). In the bulk turbulent region, molecular transport is negligible compared to eddy transport, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &\approx \rho \varepsilon \frac{dV}{dy} \\
\frac{q}{\rho c_p} &\approx \varepsilon \frac{dT}{dy} \\
N_A &\approx \varepsilon \frac{dC_A}{dy}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio of the heat and momentum flux equations:**

$$\frac{q}{\tau c_p} = \frac{\varepsilon \, (dT/dy)}{\varepsilon \, (dV/dy)} = \frac{dT}{dV}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$q = \tau c_p \frac{dT}{dV}$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate** across the boundary layer from the wall ($y = 0$, $V = 0$, $T = T_w$) to the free stream ($y = \delta$, $V = V_\infty$, $T = T_\infty$):

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} q \, dy = c_p \int_{0}^{\delta} \tau \, \frac{dT}{dV} \, dy$$

Since $q$ and $\tau$ are functions of $y$, this is awkward. Instead, use the ratio of integrated forms:

**Step 6 — Use the linear-gradient approximation.** Assume the velocity and temperature profiles in the turbulent boundary layer are linear (i.e., $V/V_\infty = (T - T_w)/(T_\infty - T_w)$). Then:

$$\frac{dT}{dV} = \frac{T_\infty - T_w}{V_\infty} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and integrate:**

$$q \cdot \delta = c_p \cdot \frac{T_\infty - T_w}{V_\infty} \int_{0}^{\delta} \tau \, dy$$

**Step 8 — Recognize that the integral $\int_0^\delta \tau \, dy$ is the total drag per unit area** (momentum thickness × $\rho V_\infty$):

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} \tau \, dy = \tau_w \cdot \delta$$

**Step 9 — Substitute back:**

$$q \cdot \delta = c_p \cdot \frac{T_\infty - T_w}{V_\infty} \cdot \tau_w \cdot \delta$$

**Step 10 — Solve for $q$:**

$$q = \tau_w \, c_p \, \frac{T_\infty - T_w}{V_\infty}$$

**Step 11 — Divide numerator and denominator by the dynamic pressure $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2$:**

$$q = \frac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2} \cdot \rho V_\infty c_p \cdot (T_\infty - T_w) = C_f \cdot \rho V_\infty c_p \cdot (T_\infty - T_w)$$

**Step 12 — Recognize the heat-transfer coefficient $h$ from Newton's law of cooling** $q = h(T_\infty - T_w)$:

$$h = C_f \cdot \frac{\rho V_\infty c_p}{2} \cdot 2 \Rightarrow h = \frac{C_f}{2} \rho V_\infty c_p$$

**Step 13 — Express in terms of Stanton number** $St_H = h/(\rho V_\infty c_p)$:

$$\boxed{St_H = \frac{C_f}{2}}$$

This is the **Reynolds Analogy**.

### Conditions of Validity:
- Turbulent flow only (laminar sublayer neglected)
- $\Pr = 1$ (so $\nu = \alpha$)
- No form drag (flat plate or smooth pipe)
- Constant physical properties
- No chemical reaction or viscous dissipation

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Prandtl Analogy (Modification of Reynolds)

### **Reason for Modification:**
Reynolds analogy neglects the **laminar sublayer** near the wall, where heat and momentum *do not* transfer at equal rates (because $\Pr \ne 1$). Prandtl (1910) introduced a correction.

**Step 1 — Divide the boundary layer into two zones:**
- **Laminar sublayer** (thickness $\delta_b$): Molecular transport dominates
- **Turbulent core** ($\delta > y > \delta_b$): Eddy transport dominates, Reynolds analogy holds

**Step 2 — Heat transfer in the laminar sublayer** (Fourier's law with linear gradient):

$$q = \frac{k}{\delta_b}(T_w - T_b)$$

**Step 3 — Heat transfer in the turbulent core** (Reynolds analogy applied between $y = \delta_b$ and $y = \delta$):

$$q = \frac{C_f}{2} \rho V_\infty c_p (T_b - T_\infty)$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate the unknown interface temperature $T_b$.** Solve the two equations for $T_w - T_b$ and $T_b - T_\infty$, then add:

$$T_w - T_\infty = (T_w - T_b) + (T_b - T_\infty) = \frac{q \delta_b}{k} + \frac{2q}{C_f \rho V_\infty c_p}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $q$:**

$$q = \frac{T_w - T_\infty}{\dfrac{\delta_b}{k} + \dfrac{2}{C_f \rho V_\infty c_p}}$$

**Step 6 — Define the overall heat-transfer coefficient:**

$$\frac{1}{h} = \frac{\delta_b}{k} + \frac{2}{C_f \rho V_\infty c_p}$$

**Step 7 — Express $\delta_b$ in terms of $C_f$** (empirically, $\delta_b \approx 5 \nu / V_\infty \sqrt{C_f/2}$):

$$\frac{\delta_b}{k} = \frac{5 \nu}{k V_\infty} \sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}} = \frac{5}{\rho c_p V_\infty} \sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}} \cdot \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{5 \Pr}{\rho c_p V_\infty} \sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute and divide by $\rho V_\infty c_p$:**

$$\frac{1}{St_H} = \frac{2}{C_f} + 5\sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}}\,(\Pr - 1)$$

**Step 9 — Invert to get the Prandtl Analogy:**

$$\boxed{St_H = \frac{C_f/2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,(\Pr - 1)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Von Kármán Analogy (Adds Buffer Layer)

Von Kármán (1939) recognized that between the laminar sublayer and the fully turbulent zone, there exists a **buffer layer** where *both* molecular and eddy transport contribute.

**Step 1 — Three-zone model:** Laminar sublayer → buffer layer → turbulent core.

**Step 2 — Heat transfer in the buffer layer** (combined molecular + eddy, with $\Pr \ne 1$):

$$q = \rho c_p (\alpha + \varepsilon) \frac{dT}{dy} = \frac{\tau}{(\nu + \varepsilon)} \cdot (\alpha + \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{dT}{dV}$$

**Step 3 — Since the velocity and temperature gradients differ by the factor $(\nu + \varepsilon)/(\alpha + \varepsilon)$:**

$$\frac{dT}{dV} = \frac{\alpha + \varepsilon}{\nu + \varepsilon} \cdot \frac{dT/dy}{dV/dy}$$

**Step 4 — The ratio of gradients integrated across the buffer layer** yields the term $\ln[(5\Pr + 1)/6]$ (the buffer-layer correction).

**Step 5 — Final form (combining laminar sublayer + buffer + turbulent core):**

$$\boxed{St_H = \frac{C_f/2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\left[(\Pr - 1) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5\Pr + 1}{6}\right)\right]}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Chilton–Colburn Analogy (Empirical j-Factor)

Chilton and Colburn (1934) made an *empirical observation* that for moderate ranges of Pr and Sc, the heat and mass transfer data could be correlated by the **j-factor**:

$$j_H = St_H \cdot \Pr^{2/3} \quad \text{and} \quad j_D = St_D \cdot \Sc^{2/3}$$

Empirically, both $j$-factors equal the friction factor:

$$\boxed{j_H = j_D = \frac{C_f}{2}}$$

**Step 1 — For heat transfer:**

$$St_H \cdot \Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{h}{\rho V c_p} \Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

$$h = \frac{C_f}{2} \rho V c_p \Pr^{-2/3}$$

**Step 2 — For mass transfer:**

$$St_D \cdot \Sc^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{k_c}{V} \Sc^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

$$k_c = \frac{C_f}{2} V \Sc^{-2/3}$$

**Step 3 — Combining the two:**

$$\frac{h}{\rho c_p \Pr^{2/3}} = k_c \cdot \Sc^{2/3}$$

This is the **most general form** of the Chilton–Colburn analogy and is the workhorse correlation for industrial mass-transfer equipment design.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Four Analogies

| Feature | Reynolds | Prandtl | Von Kármán | Chilton–Colburn |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Author** | 1874 / Reynolds | 1910 / Prandtl | 1939 / Von Kármán | 1934 / Chilton–Colburn |
| **Pr Range** | $\Pr = 1$ only | $0.6 < \Pr < 50$ | $0.6 < \Pr < 50$ | $0.6 < \Pr < 60$ |
| **Sc Range** | $\Sc = 1$ only | Not extended | Not extended | $0.6 < \Sc < 3000$ |
| **Laminar Sublayer** | Neglected | Included | Included | Implicit (empirical) |
| **Buffer Layer** | Neglected | Neglected | Included | Implicit |
| **Type** | Analytical | Analytical | Analytical | Empirical |
| **Accuracy** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest (analytical) | Highest (empirical) |
| **Most Used** | Pedagogical | Pedagogical | Reference | **Industrial design** |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Reynolds Analogy. Mention any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

Reynolds (1874) proposed that in turbulent flow, the rate of heat transfer from a surface to a fluid is directly proportional to the friction experienced by the fluid, and is given by the equation:

$$St_H = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

where $St_H = h/(\rho V c_p)$ is the heat-transfer Stanton number and $C_f$ is the local friction coefficient.

**Limitations:**
1. The analogy is valid only for **Prandtl number Pr = 1**. It fails for liquids ($\Pr \gg 1$, e.g., oils) and for liquid metals ($\Pr \ll 1$).
2. The laminar sublayer near the wall is **neglected**, which is significant for fluids with $\Pr \ne 1$.
3. The analogy does not account for **form drag** (only skin friction), so it is restricted to smooth flat plates or fully developed pipe flow.
4. The physical properties ($\mu$, $k$, $c_p$, $\rho$) are assumed to be **constant** across the boundary layer.
5. It applies only to **turbulent flow**, not laminar.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define the j-factors of Chilton and Colburn for heat and mass transfer. Write the Chilton–Colburn analogy.**

**Model Answer:**

The **j-factor** is a dimensionless transfer coefficient introduced by Chilton and Colburn (1934) to empirically correlate heat and mass transfer data.

**For Heat Transfer:**

$$j_H = St_H \cdot \Pr^{2/3} = \frac{h}{\rho V c_p} \Pr^{2/3}$$

**For Mass Transfer:**

$$j_D = St_D \cdot \Sc^{2/3} = \frac{k_c}{V} \Sc^{2/3}$$

**Chilton–Colburn Analogy:**

$$\boxed{j_H = j_D = \frac{C_f}{2}}$$

This states that the heat and mass j-factors are both equal to half the friction factor. **Valid range:** $\Pr$ between 0.6 and 60, and $\Sc$ between 0.6 and 3000.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT skip the starting assumptions.** Examiners specifically look for the words *"Assume Pr = 1"* and *"Assume εM = εH = εD"* before the Reynolds derivation. Loss of 2 marks if omitted.
> - **Units must be consistent.** Always carry $\rho$, $c_p$, and $\mu$ with their SI units in the final numerical answer.
> - **Chilton–Colburn requires the $2/3$ power** — students frequently write $St = C_f/2$ and lose 7 full marks.
> - **For Von Kármán,** the natural log term $\ln[(5\Pr + 1)/6]$ is often missed. Memorize it.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a) Derive Reynolds Analogy between heat and momentum transfer starting from the basic transport equations. State clearly the assumptions and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Start with the three flux equations.** Newton's law for momentum, Fourier's law for heat, and Fick's law for mass are written in one-dimensional form.
>
> **Step 2 — Express the total flux in turbulent flow** as a sum of molecular and turbulent (eddy) contributions. In the turbulent core, the molecular part is negligible.
>
> **Step 3 — Invoke the Reynolds hypothesis** that the eddy diffusivities are equal: $\varepsilon_M = \varepsilon_H = \varepsilon_D = \varepsilon$.
>
> **Step 4 — Take the ratio of the heat flux equation to the momentum flux equation** after multiplying the heat equation by $1/(c_p)$ to make the units consistent.
>
> **Step 5 — Obtain $dT/dV$ = (constant)** using the linearized profile in the turbulent core.
>
> **Step 6 — Integrate** across the boundary layer and substitute the wall shear stress and heat flux definitions.
>
> **Step 7 — Obtain the final result:** $\dfrac{h}{\rho V c_p} = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$, or $St_H = C_f/2$.

**Assumptions (must be listed):**
1. Steady, one-dimensional turbulent flow.
2. $\Pr = 1$ (so molecular and thermal diffusivities are equal).
3. $\varepsilon_M = \varepsilon_H = \varepsilon_D$ (equal eddy diffusivities).
4. No form drag — only skin friction.
5. Constant physical properties.
6. The linear profile approximation $V/V_\infty = (T - T_w)/(T_\infty - T_w)$.

**Limitations:**
1. Fails for fluids with $\Pr \ne 1$ (e.g., oils, liquid metals, gases above 200 °C).
2. Neglects the laminar sublayer.
3. Cannot be used for laminar flow.
4. Restricted to smooth surfaces (no roughness form drag).

**The full step-by-step derivation is given in Section 3.1 of these notes — replicate it on the answer script.**

---

**(b) Derive Prandtl's modification of Reynolds Analogy. Discuss how it overcomes the limitation of Reynolds Analogy. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Recognize the flaw in Reynolds Analogy:** Reynolds ignored the laminar sublayer where *molecular* transport dominates and where heat and momentum do not transfer at the same rate (because $\Pr \ne 1$).
>
> **Step 2 — Divide the boundary layer into two zones:** (i) Laminar sublayer of thickness $\delta_b$ at the wall, where Fourier's law gives a linear temperature profile. (ii) Turbulent core ($\delta_b$ to $\delta$), where Reynolds analogy is valid.
>
> **Step 3 — Write Fourier's law in the laminar sublayer:** $q = (k/\delta_b)(T_w - T_b)$.
>
> **Step 4 — Apply Reynolds analogy in the turbulent core** between $y = \delta_b$ and the free stream: $q = (C_f/2) \rho V_\infty c_p (T_b - T_\infty)$.
>
> **Step 5 — Eliminate the unknown interface temperature $T_b$** by adding the two heat-transfer equations.
>
> **Step 6 — Express the laminar sublayer thickness** in terms of the friction factor: $\delta_b = 5\nu / (V_\infty \sqrt{C_f/2})$.
>
> **Step 7 — Combine and simplify** to obtain the final form:

$$St_H = \frac{C_f/2}{1 + 5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,(\Pr - 1)}$$

**How it overcomes the Reynolds limitation:**
- When $\Pr = 1$, the denominator becomes 1, and the equation collapses back to the Reynolds analogy.
- For $\Pr \ne 1$, the term $5\sqrt{C_f/2}\,(\Pr - 1)$ in the denominator **reduces the predicted $St_H$** when $\Pr > 1$ (oils, where heat transfer is slower than momentum transfer) and **increases $St_H$** when $\Pr < 1$ (liquid metals, where heat transfer is faster). This matches physical reality and corrects the Reynolds over-prediction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**(a) What is Chilton–Colburn analogy? Derive the j-factor correlations for heat and mass transfer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Define j-factors.** Chilton and Colburn (1934) introduced the dimensionless groups $j_H$ and $j_D$ as empirical corrections to the Stanton number:

$$j_H = St_H \cdot \Pr^{2/3} \quad ; \quad j_D = St_D \cdot \Sc^{2/3}$$

> **Step 2 — State the analogy.** They observed experimentally that for a wide range of conditions:

$$j_H = j_D = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

> **Step 3 — Derive the heat-transfer j-factor.** Substitute the definition of $St_H$:

$$\frac{h}{\rho V c_p} \Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h = \frac{C_f}{2} \rho V c_p \Pr^{-2/3}$$

> **Step 4 — Derive the mass-transfer j-factor.** Substitute the definition of $St_D$:

$$\frac{k_c}{V} \Sc^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k_c = \frac{C_f}{2} V \Sc^{-2/3}$$

> **Step 5 — Combine** to get the working form:

$$\frac{h}{\rho c_p \Pr^{2/3}} = k_c \Sc^{2/3}$$

**Utility:** This equation lets the designer estimate the mass-transfer coefficient $k_c$ from heat-transfer measurements, or vice versa, with good accuracy (within ~10–15 %) for engineering design.

---

**(b) Hot air at 150 °C flows across a flat plate at a free-stream velocity of 10 m/s. The properties of air are: $\Pr = 0.71$, $\Sc = 0.61$, $\rho = 0.85\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1008\ \text{J/kg·K}$, $\mu = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa·s}$. The local friction coefficient at a point on the plate is $C_f = 0.0045$. Calculate (i) the local heat-transfer coefficient $h$ using the Chilton–Colburn analogy, and (ii) the local mass-transfer coefficient $k_c$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Recall the Chilton–Colburn j-factor equations:**

$$h = \frac{C_f}{2} \rho V c_p \Pr^{-2/3} \quad ; \quad k_c = \frac{C_f}{2} V \Sc^{-2/3}$$

> **Step 2 — Compute the Prandtl correction** $\Pr^{-2/3}$:

$$\Pr^{-2/3} = (0.71)^{-2/3} = (0.71)^{-0.6667} \approx 1.244$$

> **Step 3 — Compute the heat-transfer coefficient $h$:**

$$h = \frac{0.0045}{2} \times 0.85 \times 10 \times 1008 \times 1.244$$

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 0.00225 \times 0.85 \times 10 \times 1008 \times 1.244 \\
  &= 0.00225 \times 10662.5 \times 1.244 \\
  &= 0.00225 \times 13262.2 \\
  &\approx 29.84\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 29.8\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}}$$

> **Step 4 — Compute the Schmidt correction** $\Sc^{-2/3}$:

$$\Sc^{-2/3} = (0.61)^{-2/3} = (0.61)^{-0.6667} \approx 1.354$$

> **Step 5 — Compute the mass-transfer coefficient $k_c$:**

$$k_c = \frac{0.0045}{2} \times 10 \times 1.354$$

$$\begin{aligned}
k_c &= 0.00225 \times 10 \times 1.354 \\
    &= 0.00225 \times 13.54 \\
    &\approx 0.0305\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{k_c \approx 0.0305\ \text{m/s}}$$

> **Verification by Reynolds Analogy (Pr = Sc = 1 case):** If we ignored the $2/3$-power correction, $h = 23.98\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ and $k_c = 0.0225\ \text{m/s}$. The Chilton–Colburn corrections increase both by ~24 %, consistent with $\Pr < 1$ (gases transfer heat and mass *faster* than momentum, because $\Pr < 1$ means the thermal sublayer is thinner than the velocity sublayer).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- The three fundamental transport laws — **Newton, Fourier, Fick** — have *identical mathematical structure*: Flux = (Property) × (Gradient).
- The **Reynolds analogy** $St_H = C_f/2$ is the *starting point* of all subsequent analogies; it is valid **only for Pr = 1**.
- **Prandtl analogy** adds the **laminar sublayer** correction: $\Pr - 1$ in the denominator.
- **Von Kármán analogy** adds the **buffer layer** correction: $\ln[(5\Pr + 1)/6]$ in the denominator.
- **Chilton–Colburn analogy** is the *empirical* and *most widely used* in industry: $j_H = j_D = C_f/2$, with the $\Pr^{2/3}$ and $\Sc^{2/3}$ corrections.
- The **Prandtl number Pr** compares momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity: $\Pr = \nu/\alpha$.
- The **Schmidt number Sc** compares momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity: $\Sc = \nu/D_{AB}$.
- The **Lewis number Le** compares thermal to mass diffusivity: $\Le = \alpha/D_{AB} = \Sc/\Pr$.
- For **gases**, $\Pr \approx 0.7$ and $\Sc \approx 0.6$ to $2.0$ (close to but not equal to 1).
- For **liquids**, $\Pr$ can be very large (oils: $\Pr \sim 100$–$10000$); for **liquid metals**, $\Pr \ll 1$ ($\Pr \sim 0.005$–$0.03$).
- The analogies are valid only for **turbulent flow**, **constant properties**, and **no form drag**.
- The Chilton–Colburn $j$-factor is the **bridge** between heat and mass transfer — use it freely in cooling-tower, humidifier, and absorber designs.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always state the **assumptions** *before* starting the derivation, and the **validity range** *after* the final result.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts and Assumptions Involved in Theories of Mass Transfer: Penetration and Surface Renewal Theories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts and Assumptions Involved in Theories of Mass Transfer: Penetration and Surface Renewal Theories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
<!-- SECTION_1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Two-Film Problem & Why We Need Theories

Mass transfer between two immiscible fluid phases (e.g., gas-liquid absorption, liquid-liquid extraction) fundamentally occurs across an **interface**. Because directly measuring concentration gradients across a thin, dynamic interfacial layer is experimentally very difficult, engineers rely on **theoretical models** that translate bulk driving forces into a mass transfer coefficient $k$ or $k_L$ (in m/s) or $k_G$ (in mol/m²·s·Pa).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The two classical theories that dominate this topic are **Higbie's Penetration Theory (1935)** and **Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory (1951)**. Both reject the stagnant "film" assumption of the older **Whitman Two-Film Theory (1923)** and instead treat the interface as being constantly refreshed by turbulent eddies from the bulk fluid.

---

### 1.2 Higbie's Penetration Theory – Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Penetration Theory (Higbie, 1935):** Each element of fluid arriving at the phase interface is exposed to the second phase for a **brief, fixed contact time** $t_e$ (called the *exposure time*). During this short contact, **unsteady-state (transient) molecular diffusion** takes place into the element. After time $t_e$, that element is swept back into the bulk and replaced by a fresh element from the turbulent core.

**Key Equation (the high-yield result):**

$$k_L = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \, t_e}}$$

where:
- $k_L$ = liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $D_{AB}$ = molecular diffusivity of solute A in solvent B (m²/s)
- $t_e$ = exposure time (s)

#### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine dipping a **fresh, dry sponge** into a bucket of ink, holding it there for exactly **2 seconds**, then pulling it out. The ink that soaked in only penetrated a tiny bit into the surface — it did not reach the core. The **deeper the ink goes**, the **longer** the contact. Now imagine doing this with millions of sponges, each held for exactly the same 2-second "exposure time". This is what Higbie proposed: every fluid parcel at the interface behaves like an independent sponge being dipped, held for a fixed time $t_e$, then yanked away.

---

### 1.3 Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory – Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts, 1951):** A direct, more realistic extension of Higbie's theory. Instead of every element having the **same** age, fluid elements at the interface have a **statistical distribution of residence times**, governed by a random surface renewal process. The probability that an element at the surface has an age between $t$ and $t + dt$ is given by:
>
> $$\phi(t) = s \, e^{-st}$$
>
> where $s$ is the **fractional rate of surface renewal** (units: s⁻¹).

**Key Equation:**

$$k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$$

#### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Now imagine the same bucket of ink, but instead of holding every sponge for exactly 2 seconds, you **randomly yank them out** at different times — some after 1 second, some after 5 seconds, and some get yanked instantly. The probability that any given sponge is "still on the surface" decays exponentially with time. The parameter $s$ controls how fast the sponges are replaced — a high $s$ means very vigorous turbulence and rapid refreshment.

---

### 1.4 Why These Theories Matter in KTU

| Real-World Equipment | Theory Most Often Invoked |
|---|---|
| Packed absorption column (irrigated) | Penetration / Surface Renewal |
| Bubble-cap tray (gas-liquid) | Surface Renewal |
| Wetted-wall column (lab reference) | Penetration |
| Stirred-tank fermentor | Surface Renewal |
| Spray tower / droplet scrubber | Surface Renewal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Both theories predict $k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$, in contrast to the stagnant-film theory that predicts $k_L \propto D_{AB}$. This square-root dependence on diffusivity is the **single most-tested fingerprint** of these two theories in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
<!-- SECTION_2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Common Foundation – Unsteady-State Diffusion into a Stagnant Slab

Both Higbie and Danckwerts build their analyses on **Fick's Second Law** of diffusion for an isotropic medium with constant diffusivity:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

This is the same PDE solved in transient heat conduction problems (e.g., a sudden surface temperature change on a semi-infinite solid). The geometry at hand is a **semi-infinite fluid element** whose $x$-coordinate measures penetration depth *into* the element from the interface.

---

### 2.2 Penetration Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Physical Picture.** A packet of fluid is brought from the bulk (concentration $C_{A0}$) to the interface (concentration $C_{Ai}$ at $x = 0$). Diffusion is into the $x$-direction.

**Step 2 — Governing Equation & Boundary Conditions.**

| # | Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| IC | At $t = 0$: $C_A = C_{A0}$ for all $x > 0$ | The fresh fluid element is uniform. |
| BC-1 | For $t > 0$: $C_A = C_{Ai}$ at $x = 0$ | Surface concentration jumps instantly. |
| BC-2 | For $t > 0$: $C_A \to C_{A0}$ as $x \to \infty$ | Far inside the packet, concentration is unchanged. |

**Step 3 — Mathematical Solution (Higbie's result).** Solving the diffusion equation by similarity transformation gives:

$$C_A(x,t) - C_{A0} = (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\,\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t}}\right)$$

where $\operatorname{erfc}$ is the complementary error function.

**Step 4 — Instantaneous Flux at the Interface (x = 0).** Using Fick's First Law:

$$N_A\big|_{x=0}(t) = -D_{AB}\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} = (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$

**Step 5 — Average Over the Exposure Window.** Since the packet stays at the surface for a fixed time $t_e$, the *time-averaged* flux is:

$$\bar{N}_A = \frac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e} (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}\,dt$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Mass Transfer Coefficient.**

$$k_L \equiv \frac{\bar{N}_A}{C_{Ai} - C_{A0}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\, t_e}}$$

---

### 2.3 Surface Renewal Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Relax the Equal-Age Assumption.** Penetration theory assumes all elements have the same age $t_e$. Danckwerts argued this is unphysical in real turbulent flow, where eddies are replaced *randomly*.

**Step 2 — Age Distribution Function.** Define $\phi(t)\, dt$ = fraction of interface occupied by fluid elements with ages between $t$ and $t + dt$. A purely random renewal process gives:

$$\phi(t) = s\,e^{-s t} \quad \text{with} \quad \int_0^{\infty} s\,e^{-s t}\, dt = 1$$

**Step 3 — Average Flux Over All Ages.** Weight the instantaneous flux $N_A(t)$ by the age distribution:

$$\bar{N}_A = \int_0^{\infty} N_A(t)\,\phi(t)\,dt = \int_0^{\infty} (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}\cdot s\, e^{-s t}\,dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral** (using $\int_0^\infty e^{-st}/\sqrt{t}\,dt = \sqrt{\pi/s}$):

$$\bar{N}_A = (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{D_{AB}\, s}$$

**Step 5 — Identify $k_L$.**

$$k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB}\, s}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **must-memorize** formula cluster for Module 1, frequently tested as Part A (3-mark) or short Part B sub-questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's Second Law (1-D) | $\partial C_A/\partial t = D_{AB}\,\partial^{2}C_A/\partial x^{2}$ | — | Governing PDE for both theories. |
| 2 | Penetration profile | $C_A - C_{A0} = (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\operatorname{erfc}[x/(2\sqrt{D_{AB}t})]$ | mol/m³ | Similarity solution. |
| 3 | Instantaneous flux (Pen.) | $N_A(t) = (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t)}$ | mol/(m²·s) | At $x = 0$. |
| 4 | **Penetration $k_L$** | $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)}$ | m/s | Fixed exposure time. |
| 5 | Age distribution | $\phi(t) = s\,e^{-s t}$ | s⁻¹ | $s$ = renewal rate. |
| 6 | **Surface Renewal $k_L$** | $k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB}\, s}$ | m/s | Random surface age. |
| 7 | Comparison to film | $k_{film} = D_{AB}/\delta$ | m/s | Linear in $D_{AB}$. |
| 8 | Penetration/film link | $t_e = 4\delta^{2}/(\pi D_{AB})$ | s | At equal $k_L$. |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of absorption columns** for $\text{CO}_2$ capture, $\text{SO}_2$ scrubbing, ammonia absorption: $k_L$ values from these theories enter the HTU (height of a transfer unit) calculation.
- **Fermenter / bioreactor design**: oxygen transfer into microbial broth depends on $k_L a$, which scales as $\sqrt{D_{O_2}\, s}$.
- **Scrubbers and Strippers**: surface renewal rate $s$ correlates empirically with power input per unit volume and impeller Reynolds number.
- **Process intensification**: understanding $k_L \propto \sqrt{D}$ helps justify choosing low-viscosity solvents to maximize transfer rates.

---

### 2.6 Critical Comparison of the Two Theories

| Feature | Penetration (Higbie) | Surface Renewal (Danckwerts) |
|---|---|---|
| Element age at interface | Fixed, equal to $t_e$ | Exponentially distributed |
| Renewal rate | All replaced together at $t_e$ | Random, with mean $1/s$ |
| $k_L$ dependence on $D_{AB}$ | $\sqrt{D_{AB}}$ | $\sqrt{D_{AB}}$ |
| $k_L$ dependence on hydrodynamics | Through $t_e$ only | Through $s$ only |
| Predicts: effect of increased turbulence on $k_L$ | Higher $k_L$ via shorter $t_e$ | Higher $k_L$ via larger $s$ |
| Holds best for | Laminar/well-defined flow (e.g., wetted-wall column) | Highly turbulent gas-liquid interfaces |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exam-ready**: each algebraic transition is shown explicitly. KTU frequently asks students to start from Fick's second law and arrive at the $k_L$ expression for either theory.

---

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Higbie's Penetration Theory

#### 3.1.1 Start with the Governing PDE

We postulate that each fluid "packet" arriving at the interface behaves as a semi-infinite stagnant medium into which solute A diffuses in the $x$-direction only:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \;=\; D_{AB}\,\frac{\partial^{2} C_A}{\partial x^{2}} \qquad (1)$$

with the three conditions stated earlier.

#### 3.1.2 Apply the Boltzmann Similarity Transformation

Introduce the dimensionless similarity variable $\eta$:

$$\eta \;=\; \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t}}$$

Assume a solution of the form $C_A(\eta)$. By the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} \;=\; \frac{d C_A}{d\eta}\cdot\frac{\partial\eta}{\partial x} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t}}\,\frac{d C_A}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} C_A}{\partial x^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{4D_{AB}\,t}\,\frac{d^{2} C_A}{d\eta^{2}}$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \;=\; \frac{d C_A}{d\eta}\cdot\frac{\partial\eta}{\partial t} \;=\; -\frac{x}{4\,t\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t}}\,\frac{d C_A}{d\eta} \;=\; -\frac{\eta}{2t}\,\frac{d C_A}{d\eta}$$

#### 3.1.3 Reduce to an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE)

Substitute the three transformed derivatives into Equation (1):

$$-\frac{\eta}{2t}\,\frac{d C_A}{d\eta} \;=\; D_{AB}\cdot\frac{1}{4 D_{AB}\,t}\,\frac{d^{2} C_A}{d\eta^{2}}$$

Simplify (the $t$'s cancel):

$$-\frac{\eta}{2}\,\frac{d C_A}{d\eta} \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\,\frac{d^{2} C_A}{d\eta^{2}}$$

Multiply both sides by $4$:

$$\frac{d^{2} C_A}{d\eta^{2}} \;+\; 2\eta\,\frac{d C_A}{d\eta} \;=\; 0$$

#### 3.1.4 Solve the ODE

Let $p = dC_A/d\eta$. Then:

$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta\,p = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{dp}{p} = -2\eta\,d\eta$$

Integrating:

$$\ln p = -\eta^{2} + \text{const} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; p = A_1\, e^{-\eta^{2}}$$

Integrate once more:

$$C_A(\eta) = A_1\int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}}du + A_2 = A_1\cdot\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,\operatorname{erf}(\eta) + A_2$$

Equivalently, in terms of the complementary error function $\operatorname{erfc}(\eta) = 1 - \operatorname{erf}(\eta)$:

$$C_A(\eta) = A_3 + A_4\,\operatorname{erfc}(\eta)$$

#### 3.1.5 Apply Boundary Conditions

**Apply BC-2:** As $\eta \to \infty$, $\operatorname{erfc}(\eta) \to 0$, so $C_A \to A_3 = C_{A0}$.

**Apply IC:** At $t = 0$, $\eta = \infty$ for any finite $x$, so $C_A \to C_{A0}$. ✓

**Apply BC-1:** At $x = 0$, $\eta = 0$, so $\operatorname{erfc}(0) = 1$. Thus:

$$C_{Ai} = C_{A0} + A_4 \cdot 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; A_4 = C_{Ai} - C_{A0}$$

#### 3.1.6 Final Concentration Profile

$$\boxed{\,C_A(x,t) - C_{A0} \;=\; (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\,\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t}}\right)\,}$$

#### 3.1.7 Compute the Instantaneous Flux at x = 0

Differentiate the profile with respect to $x$. Let $u = x/(2\sqrt{D_{AB}t})$, so $\partial u/\partial x = 1/(2\sqrt{D_{AB}t})$.

Recall $\frac{d}{du}\operatorname{erfc}(u) = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,e^{-u^{2}}$.

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} \;=\; (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\cdot\left(-\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}e^{-u^{2}}\right)\cdot\frac{1}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t}}$$

$$=\; -(C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB}\,t}}\,e^{-x^{2}/(4D_{AB}\,t)}$$

Evaluate at $x = 0$ (so $e^{0} = 1$):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} \;=\; -(C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB}\,t}}$$

Apply Fick's First Law (negative sign convention):

$$N_A\big|_{x=0}(t) \;=\; -D_{AB}\,\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} \;=\; (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t}}$$

#### 3.1.8 Time Average Over the Exposure Window $[0, t_e]$

$$\bar{N}_A \;=\; \frac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e}(C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}\,dt$$

$$=\; \frac{(C_{Ai} - C_{A0})}{t_e}\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}}\,\int_0^{t_e}\frac{dt}{\sqrt{t}}$$

The integral evaluates to $2\sqrt{t_e}$:

$$=\; \frac{(C_{Ai} - C_{A0})}{t_e}\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}}\cdot 2\sqrt{t_e} \;=\; 2(C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_e}}$$

#### 3.1.9 Final Result for $k_L$

By definition, $k_L \equiv \bar{N}_A/(C_{Ai} - C_{A0})$:

$$\boxed{\,k_L \;=\; 2\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_e}}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Full Derivation of Danckwerts' Surface Renewal Theory

#### 3.2.1 Start with the Instantaneous Flux from Penetration Theory

For an element of *age* $t$, the flux from Higbie's analysis is still valid (the local physics doesn't change; only the age distribution does):

$$N_A(t) \;=\; (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\,\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t}}$$

#### 3.2.2 Weight by the Age Distribution

The mean flux over the entire interface is:

$$\bar{N}_A \;=\; \int_0^{\infty} N_A(t)\,\phi(t)\,dt \;=\; \int_0^{\infty}(C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}\cdot s\, e^{-st}\,dt$$

#### 3.2.3 Pull Out Constants

$$\bar{N}_A \;=\; (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}}\cdot s\,\int_0^{\infty}\frac{e^{-st}}{\sqrt{t}}\,dt$$

#### 3.2.4 Evaluate the Integral

Use the standard result (derivable by substitution $u = st$, $\Gamma(1/2)=\sqrt{\pi}$):

$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{e^{-st}}{\sqrt{t}}\,dt \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\int_0^{\infty} u^{-1/2}\,e^{-u}\,du \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\,\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{s}}$$

#### 3.2.5 Substitute Back

$$\bar{N}_A \;=\; (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}}\cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{s}}$$

$$=\; (C_{Ai} - C_{A0})\,\sqrt{D_{AB}\,s}$$

#### 3.2.6 Final Result for $k_L$

$$\boxed{\,k_L \;=\; \sqrt{D_{AB}\,s}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison at Equal $k_L$

If we equate the two expressions for $k_L$ to find what value of $s$ in Danckwerts' theory reproduces Higbie's $k_L$:

$$2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\,t_e}} \;=\; \sqrt{D_{AB}\,s} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s \;=\; \frac{4}{\pi\,t_e}$$

Thus, a Higbie-style "batch" replacement of all elements every $t_e$ seconds is equivalent to a Danckwerts random replacement with mean age $\langle t \rangle = 1/s = \pi t_e/4$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** Penetration theory predicts $k_L \propto 1/\sqrt{t_e}$ and surface renewal predicts $k_L \propto \sqrt{s}$. Both predict $k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$. The latter is the **unifying signature** distinguishing them from the stagnant-film theory.
