| 13 | $K_G = \dfrac{K_y}{P}$ | Overall coefficient in pressure basis | $\text{kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{Pa}$ |
| 14 | $K_L = \dfrac{K_x}{C}$ | Overall coefficient in molarity basis | $\text{m/s}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION (KTU Board Exam)**: Use $y_{AG}, y_{AL}$ for **bulk** gas and **equivalent** liquid compositions, and $y_{Ai}, x_{Ai}$ for **interfacial** compositions. Do NOT confuse them — examiners deduct marks for ambiguous notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Equipment Sizing**: The slope of the operating line and the position of the interface $(y_{Ai}, x_{Ai})$ are determined using overall coefficients $K_y$ or $K_x$.
- **Process Control**: Identifying the **controlling film** tells the engineer whether to change gas flow rate (gas-film control) or liquid flow rate (liquid-film control) to enhance transfer.
- **Pilot Plant Design**: Two-film theory provides the conceptual basis for **wetted-wall column** experiments, the standard method to measure $k_G$ and $k_L$ for binary systems.
- **Pollution Control**: In $\text{SO}_2$ scrubbing, the liquid film typically controls (highly soluble gas), so the engineer focuses on increasing liquid flow rate to enhance $K_G$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Additive Resistance Law (Gas-Phase Basis)

We start with the two individual flux equations and eliminate the unknown interfacial compositions $y_{Ai}$ and $x_{Ai}$.

**Step 1 — Write the individual film fluxes**

$$
N_A = k_y (y_{AG} - y_{Ai}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_{AG} - y_{Ai} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_y}
$$

$$
N_A = k_x (x_{Ai} - x_{AL}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{Ai} - x_{AL} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_x}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the interface composition through the equilibrium line**

From $y_{Ai} = m \, x_{Ai}$, we obtain $x_{Ai} = y_{Ai} / m$. Substituting in the second equation:

$$
\dfrac{y_{Ai}}{m} - x_{AL} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_{Ai} = m \, x_{AL} + \dfrac{m \, N_A}{k_x}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the gas-film equation**

The bulk gas composition satisfies the operating line. The composition $y_A^*$ is the gas composition **in equilibrium with the bulk liquid** $x_{AL}$:

$$
y_A^* = m \, x_{AL}
$$

So we can rewrite:

$$
y_{AG} - y_{Ai} = (y_{AG} - m \, x_{AL}) + (m \, x_{AL} - y_{Ai})
$$

$$
y_{AG} - y_{Ai} = (y_{AG} - y_A^*) - \dfrac{m \, N_A}{k_x}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine with the gas-film flux expression**

Equating both expressions for $y_{AG} - y_{Ai}$:

$$
\dfrac{N_A}{k_y} = (y_{AG} - y_A^*) - \dfrac{m \, N_A}{k_x}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for $N_A$**

$$
(y_{AG} - y_A^*) = \dfrac{N_A}{k_y} + \dfrac{m \, N_A}{k_x} = N_A \left( \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Define the overall gas-phase coefficient $K_y$**

$$
N_A = \dfrac{y_{AG} - y_A^*}{\dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}} \equiv K_y (y_{AG} - y_A^*)
$$

**Step 7 — The Additive Resistance Law (Final Form)**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: The reciprocal of an overall mass transfer coefficient equals the **sum of individual film resistances**. This is mathematically equivalent to **resistors in series** in an electrical circuit.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Additive Resistance Law (Liquid-Phase Basis)

**Step 1 — Start from the same two-film fluxes**

By eliminating the gas-side interfacial composition using $x_{Ai} = y_{Ai} / m$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{K_x} = \dfrac{1}{k_x} + \dfrac{1}{m \, k_y}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A = K_x (x_A^* - x_{AL})}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the consistency relation $K_y = m \, K_x$**

Multiplying the denominator of $1/K_y$ by $m$:

$$
\dfrac{m}{K_y} = \dfrac{m}{k_y} + \dfrac{m^2}{k_x} = \dfrac{1}{k_x \, \cdot m \cdot k_y / m} + \ldots
$$

By direct algebraic manipulation (invert and multiply both sides), one can show $K_y = m \, K_x$, which is required for thermodynamic consistency between the two overall driving forces.

### 3.3 Derivation of Fractional Resistances

The **fractional resistance in the gas film** is:

$$
\dfrac{R_G}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1/k_y}{1/K_y} = \dfrac{1/k_y}{1/k_y + m/k_x}
$$

The **fractional resistance in the liquid film** is:

$$
\dfrac{R_L}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{m/k_x}{1/k_y + m/k_x} = 1 - \dfrac{R_G}{R_{total}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb**:
> - If $\dfrac{1/k_y}{m/k_x} \gg 1$ → **Gas film controls** (typical: low solubility gases, e.g., $\text{O}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$ in water).
> - If $\dfrac{1/k_y}{m/k_x} \ll 1$ → **Liquid film controls** (typical: highly soluble gases, e.g., $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{HCl}$ in water).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem**: A gas mixture containing **15 mol%** of solute A in inert B is brought into contact with a liquid containing **0.04 mol fraction** of A. The gas-film coefficient is $k_y = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ and the liquid-film coefficient is $k_x = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$. The equilibrium relation is $y_A^* = 1.2 \, x_A$. Find:
1. The overall gas-phase coefficient $K_y$.
2. The molar flux $N_A$.
3. The percentage of total resistance in the gas film.

**Step 1 — Compute $K_y$**

$$
\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x} = \dfrac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} + \dfrac{1.2}{6.0 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{K_y} = 833.33 + 200.0 = 1033.33 \ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{s/kmol}
$$

$$
K_y = 9.677 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $y_A^*$ (gas in equilibrium with $x_{AL} = 0.04$)**

$$
y_A^* = 1.2 \times 0.04 = 0.048
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the overall driving force and flux**

$$
y_{AG} - y_A^* = 0.15 - 0.048 = 0.102
$$

$$
N_A = K_y (y_{AG} - y_A^*) = 9.677 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.102 = 9.87 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute fractional gas-film resistance**

$$
\dfrac{R_G}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1/k_y}{1/K_y} = \dfrac{833.33}{1033.33} = 0.806 = 80.6\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**: The gas film contributes **80.6%** of the total resistance → **Gas-film controlled** system. The engineer should increase the gas-phase turbulence to enhance $k_y$.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Gas-Film Control | Liquid-Film Control |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility of gas | Low (large $m$) | High (small $m$) |
| Controlling resistance | $1/k_y$ | $m/k_x$ |
| Way to enhance $N_A$ | Increase gas velocity | Increase liquid velocity |
| Typical gases | $\text{O}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{N}_2$ in water | $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{HCl}$, $\text{SO}_3$ in water |
| Fractional $R_G$ | $\to 1$ | $\to 0$ |
| Effect of $T$ rise on $K$ | Marginal | Significant (decreases $m$, may shift regime) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concentration Profile Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["BULK GAS PHASE"] --> B["GAS FILM Region yAG to yAi"]
    B --> C["INTERFACE Two-Film Boundary"]
    C --> D["LIQUID FILM Region xAi to xAL"]
    D --> E["BULK LIQUID PHASE"]

    F["Turbulent Mixing Uniform yAG"] --> A
    G["Turbulent Mixing Uniform xAL"] --> E
    H["Equilibrium yAi equals m times xAi"] --> C

    B -. "Resistance 1 over ky" .- J["GAS FILM RESISTANCE"]
    D -. "Resistance m over kx" .- K["LIQUID FILM RESISTANCE"]

    J --> L["Total Resistance 1 over Ky Equals Sum"]
    K --> L

    L --> M["Driving Force yAG minus yAstar"]
    M --> N["Flux NA Equals Ky times Driving Force"]
```

### 4.2 Resistance Network Analogy (Electrical Equivalent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Driving Force yAG minus yAstar"] --> R1["Resistor 1 over ky Gas Film"]
    R1 --> J1["Node Junction at Interface"]
    J1 --> R2["Resistor m over kx Liquid Film"]
    R2 --> B["Ground yAstar"]

    J1 --> C["Interfacial Composition yAi, xAi"]
    C --> D["In Equilibrium yAi equals m times xAi"]
```

### 4.3 Mass Transfer Direction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Solute A in Bulk Gas yAG"] --> Q1["Diffuse Through Gas Film ky"]
    Q1 --> R1["Reach Interface yAi, xAi, No Resistance"]
    R1 --> S1["Diffuse Through Liquid Film kx"]
    S1 --> T1["Dissolve in Bulk Liquid xAL"]

    U1["Equal Flux Through Both Films Steady State"] -.-> Q1
    U1 -.-> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams**: 
> - The first diagram shows the **physical sequence** of two films in series with the interface as the meeting point.
> - The second is the **electrical analog** where the two resistors ($1/k_y$ and $m/k_x$) add up in series.
> - The third emphasizes the **steady-state** condition: the flux through the gas film equals the flux through the liquid film (no buildup at the interface).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

State **Whitman's two-film theory** for gas-liquid mass transfer. List any **four** assumptions of the theory.

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
Whitman's two-film theory postulates that during gas-liquid contact, a **stagnant film** exists on each side of the interface. Mass transfer occurs by **steady-state molecular diffusion** through these films, while the bulk phases are well-mixed.

**Assumptions** (any four):
1. Two stagnant films — one in gas, one in liquid — exist adjacent to the interface.
2. Transfer is steady-state (no accumulation at the interface).
3. Transfer across each film is by **molecular diffusion** only.
4. The interface is in **thermodynamic equilibrium**, offering **zero resistance**.
5. The bulk phases are perfectly mixed (uniform composition).
6. The system is isothermal and isobaric.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

Distinguish between **individual mass transfer coefficient** and **overall mass transfer coefficient**. How are they related under two-film theory?

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Individual coefficient** ($k_y$ or $k_x$): Based on the driving force across a **single film** (e.g., $N_A = k_y(y_{AG} - y_{Ai})$).
- **Overall coefficient** ($K_y$ or $K_x$): Based on the driving force between the **bulk phases**, using an equilibrium equivalent composition (e.g., $N_A = K_y(y_{AG} - y_A^*)$).

**Relation (gas basis):**
$$
\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}
$$

This is the **additive resistance law** — the total resistance equals the sum of the two individual film resistances.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing $y_A^*$ and $y_{Ai}$**: $y_A^*$ is the gas composition **in equilibrium with bulk liquid** $x_{AL}$, NOT with the interface. Marks deducted for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by slope $m$** in the resistance term $m/k_x$ when working on gas basis. Symmetric error in the liquid basis ($1/(m \cdot k_y)$).
> 3. **Skipping units** in $K_y$ and $N_A$ — KTU strictly awards 1 mark for units in numerical problems.
> 4. **Misnaming resistances**: The gas-film resistance is $1/k_y$, the liquid-film resistance **on gas basis** is $m/k_x$. Students often write $1/k_x$ alone.
> 5. **Not specifying the controlling film** at the end of a numerical problem — KTU examiners look for the engineering interpretation, not just the calculation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A(a)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

Derive the expression for the **overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient $K_y$** in terms of the individual film coefficients $k_y$ and $k_x$ and the slope of the equilibrium line $m$, starting from the two-film theory. **State the additive resistance law** clearly. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution**:
- Write the two film flux equations (1 mark): $N_A = k_y(y_{AG} - y_{Ai})$ and $N_A = k_x(x_{Ai} - x_{AL})$.
- Use the equilibrium relation at interface (1 mark): $y_{Ai} = m \, x_{Ai}$.
- Express the bulk-equivalent gas composition (1 mark): $y_A^* = m \, x_{AL}$.
- Combine to eliminate $y_{Ai}$ and $x_{Ai}$ (2 marks): derive $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$.
- State the additive resistance law (1 mark) and physical meaning (1 mark): "Total resistance is the sum of individual film resistances; the two films act in series, analogous to electrical resistors in series."

**Q.A(b)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

In an absorption column, a gas mixture containing **10 mol%** of $\text{NH}_3$ is contacted with water. The inlet liquid contains **0.005 mol fraction** of $\text{NH}_3$. The individual gas-film coefficient is $k_y = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ and the liquid-film coefficient is $k_x = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$. The equilibrium relation is $y_A^* = 0.8 \, x_A$. Calculate:
1. The overall coefficient $K_y$. **\[3 Marks\]**
2. The molar flux $N_A$ at the top of the column. **\[2 Marks\]**
3. The percentage of total resistance in the gas film. **\[2 Marks\]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Compute $K_y$**:
$$
\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}} + \dfrac{0.8}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 333.33 + 533.33 = 866.67
$$
$$
K_y = 1.154 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $y_A^*$ and $N_A$**:
$$
y_A^* = 0.8 \times 0.005 = 0.004
$$
$$
N_A = K_y (y_{AG} - y_A^*) = 1.154 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.10 - 0.004) = 1.154 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.096
$$
$$
N_A = 1.108 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Fractional gas-film resistance**:
$$
\dfrac{R_G}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1/k_y}{1/K_y} = \dfrac{333.33}{866.67} \times 100\% = 38.46\%
$$

**Engineering Interpretation**: Since $R_L = 61.54\% > R_G = 38.46\%$, the **liquid film is the controlling resistance** for $\text{NH}_3$ absorption (highly soluble gas). To increase $N_A$, the engineer should increase the liquid flow rate to enhance $k_x$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B(a)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

Starting from the two-film theory, derive the relationship:
$$
\dfrac{1}{K_x} = \dfrac{1}{k_x} + \dfrac{1}{m \, k_y}
$$

Explain physically what each term represents. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution**:
- Write the two film flux equations and the equilibrium relation (1 mark).
- Eliminate $x_{Ai}$ using $x_{Ai} = y_{Ai} / m$ (1 mark).
- Use the bulk-equivalent liquid composition $x_A^* = y_{AG} / m$ (1 mark).
- Combine the equations and rearrange to get $N_A = K_x(x_A^* - x_{AL})$ (2 marks).
- State the additive resistance law on a liquid basis (1 mark).
- Physical interpretation: $1/k_x$ = liquid-film resistance; $1/(m k_y)$ = gas-film resistance expressed on a liquid-concentration basis (1 mark).

**Q.B(b)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

A wetted-wall column is used to absorb $\text{SO}_2$ from a gas mixture. At a particular point, the bulk gas contains $y_{AG} = 0.20$ and the bulk liquid contains $x_{AL} = 0.0025$. The individual film coefficients are $k_y = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ and $k_x = 9.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$, with equilibrium slope $m = 30$. Calculate:
1. The overall coefficient $K_y$. **\[2 Marks\]**
2. The interfacial compositions $y_{Ai}$ and $x_{Ai}$. **\[3 Marks\]**
3. The molar flux $N_A$. **\[2 Marks\]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Compute $K_y$**:
$$
\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} + \dfrac{30}{9.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 666.67 + 3333.33 = 4000
$$
$$
K_y = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $y_A^*$**:
$$
y_A^* = m \, x_{AL} = 30 \times 0.0025 = 0.075
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $N_A$**:
$$
N_A = K_y (y_{AG} - y_A^*) = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.20 - 0.075) = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.125
$$
$$
N_A = 3.125 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Find interfacial compositions** (interface is at the "node" between two film resistances):
The flux through the gas film gives the interfacial drop:
$$
y_{AG} - y_{Ai} = \dfrac{N_A}{k_y} = \dfrac{3.125 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02083
$$
$$
y_{Ai} = 0.20 - 0.02083 = 0.1792
$$
$$
x_{Ai} = \dfrac{y_{Ai}}{m} = \dfrac{0.1792}{30} = 5.972 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation**: With $m = 30$ (low solubility), the liquid-film resistance $m/k_x = 3333.3$ is much larger than the gas-film resistance $1/k_y = 666.7$, confirming **liquid-film control** for $\text{SO}_2$ in water.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Two-Film Theory**

- ✅ **Theory proposed by**: Whitman & Lewis (1924).
- ✅ **Core idea**: Two stagnant films at the interface; bulk phases well-mixed; steady-state molecular diffusion through films.
- ✅ **No interfacial resistance**; interface is at **thermodynamic equilibrium** ($y_{Ai} = m \, x_{Ai}$).
- ✅ **Two individual film flux equations**:
  - Gas film: $N_A = k_y (y_{AG} - y_{Ai})$
  - Liquid film: $N_A = k_x (x_{Ai} - x_{AL})$
- ✅ **Two overall flux equations**:
  - Gas basis: $N_A = K_y (y_{AG} - y_A^*)$
  - Liquid basis: $N_A = K_x (x_A^* - x_{AL})$
- ✅ **Additive resistance law** (the heart of the theory):
  - $\dfrac{1}{K_y} = \dfrac{1}{k_y} + \dfrac{m}{k_x}$
  - $\dfrac{1}{K_x} = \dfrac{1}{k_x} + \dfrac{1}{m \, k_y}$
- ✅ **Conversion relation**: $K_y = m \, K_x$.
- ✅ **Fractional resistance**: $\dfrac{R_G}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1/k_y}{1/K_y} = \dfrac{1/k_y}{1/k_y + m/k_x}$.
- ✅ **Controlling film identification**:
  - Gas-film control: $\text{O}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2$ in water (low $m$, large $1/k_y$).
  - Liquid-film control: $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{HCl}$, $\text{SO}_3$ in water (high $m$, large $m/k_x$).
- ✅ **Typical film thickness**: $\delta \approx 10$ to $100 \ \mu\text{m}$ (effective).
- ✅ **Real-world use**: Design of absorption/stripping columns, identification of controlling film, basis for $K_G a$ correlations (Onda, etc.).
- ✅ **Limitation**: Assumes dilute solution and linear equilibrium. For concentrated systems, use the **film theory for equimolar counter-diffusion** or **Stefan diffusion** corrections.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local and Overall k-Type Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1 > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Local and Overall k-Type Coefficients # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **gas–liquid mass transfer** operations (absorption, stripping, humidification), the **local mass transfer coefficient** (*k*-type) is defined at any point in the two-phase contacting equipment as the proportionality constant between the **local molar flux** of the diffusing species *A* and the **local concentration driving force** measured across a *single* phase (i.e., between the bulk of that phase and the interface). The **overall mass transfer coefficient** (*K*-type) is similarly defined, but the driving force is taken between the **bulk of one phase and the bulk of the other phase in equilibrium with it**. Hence, *K*-coefficients *encompass* the resistance of *both* films in a single mathematical expression. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Exam Focus Point** > The most frequently tested idea is the **interconversion between local (*k*) and overall (*K*) coefficients** and the criterion under which a *local coefficient becomes numerically equal* to its corresponding overall coefficient. Memorising the resistance-additivity form $\frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{k} + \frac{\text{equilibrium factor}}{k}$ is mandatory for the **14-mark derivation question**. ### The Eight Standard Forms | Family | Concentration basis | Mole-fraction / Ratio basis | |---|---|---| | Gas film (local) | $k_G$ | $k_Y$ | | Liquid film (local) | $k_L$ | $k_X$ | | Overall on gas | $K_G$ | $K_Y$ | | Overall on liquid | $K_L$ | $K_X$ | The flux of species *A* can therefore be written in **eight equivalent ways**: $$N_A = k_G(p-p_i) = k_L(C_i-C) = K_G(p-p^*) = K_L(C^*-C)$$ $$N_A = k_Y(Y-Y_i) = k_X(X_i-X) = K_Y(Y-Y^*) = K_X(X^*-X)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Notation rule:** *Lowercase* $k$ ⇒ **local** (single-film); *Uppercase* $K$ ⇒ **overall** (both films). Subscript $G$ or $L$ ⇒ **driving force in partial pressure or concentration units**. Subscript $Y$ or $X$ ⇒ **driving force in mole-ratio units**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Filter Water Purifier" Imagine water flowing through a purifier that has **two filters in series** — a coarse pre-filter (sand) and a fine polishing filter (activated carbon). The drop in dirt concentration across each filter is *local*; the total drop from inlet to outlet involves *both* filters. - The **local coefficient** $k$ is like the *filter's own efficiency* — it depends only on the dirt load it is *currently* seeing. - The **overall coefficient** $K$ describes the *combined efficiency* of the **two filters together**, expressed in terms of the inlet-to-outlet drop. - When the filters are very different in quality (one very good, one very poor), the **overall behaviour is dominated by the poorer filter** (the controlling resistance). In mass transfer, the **gas film** and **liquid film** are the two "filters". An **equilibrium correction factor** $m$ (slope of the equilibrium line) acts like a "gear ratio" that converts the liquid-side concentration drop into an equivalent gas-side partial pressure drop (or vice versa), so the two resistances can be added on a common basis. > [!TIP] > **Physical constants used in this module:** Universal gas constant $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$, total pressure typically **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**, and the dilute-solution approximation $C_{BM} \approx C_T$ (total molar concentration). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — What "Local" vs. "Overall" Really Means In an actual packed absorption column, the gas and liquid compositions *change continuously* from the top to the bottom of the column. Therefore, at any height $z$: 1. The bulk gas has partial pressure $p(z)$ and bulk liquid has concentration $C(z)$. 2. At the gas–liquid interface, the two phases are in **local equilibrium**: $p_i$ and $C_i$ are related by $p_i = f(C_i)$ (Henry's law or Raoult's law). 3. The flux at that location is $N_A(z) = k_G(z)\,[p(z)-p_i(z)] = k_L(z)\,[C_i(z)-C(z)]$. These *local* $k_G$ and $k_L$ are **pointwise** coefficients. If the operating line and equilibrium line are both **straight** and the individual-phase coefficients are **constant**, then $k_G$ and $k_L$ are constant *throughout the column*, and the **overall coefficients** $K_G$ and $K_L$ (computed from them) are *also* constant. The "overall" concept is therefore a **mathematical convenience** — it lets us write the rate equation using *measurable bulk driving forces* $(p - p^*)$ or $(C^*-C)$ without ever needing to know the unknown interface compositions $p_i$ and $C_i$. --- ## 2.2 The Equilibrium Slope $m$ — The Key to Interconversion For a linear equilibrium relation in the operating range: $$y = m\,x \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad p^* = m\,C^* \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad Y^* = m'\,X^*$$ where: $$m = \frac{dy^*}{dx} \bigg|_{\text{operating pt.}} \qquad \text{and} \qquad m' = \frac{m\,Y}{X}\bigg|_{\text{operating pt.}}$$ For **dilute solutions** $Y \approx y$, $X \approx x$, and $m' \approx m$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight** > The factor $m$ (or $m'$) appearing in the denominator of $k_L$ (or $k_X$) when adding resistances is *not* a physical property of the liquid — it is the **local slope of the equilibrium curve**. It tells us "how much partial pressure change is *equivalent* to a unit change in liquid concentration". A *steep* equilibrium line ($m$ large) means a small change in $C$ produces a *large* change in $p^*$, so the gas-film resistance is amplified when expressed on a liquid basis. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $N_A = k_G(p-p_i)$ | Flux through gas film alone | $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ | | 2 | $N_A = k_L(C_i-C)$ | Flux through liquid film alone | $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ | | 3 | $N_A = K_G(p-p^*)$ | Overall flux on gas basis | $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ | | 4 | $N_A = K_L(C^*-C)$ | Overall flux on liquid basis | $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ | | 5 | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Resistance additivity, gas basis | $\text{m}^2\text{·s·kmol}^{-1}\text{·Pa}$ | | 6 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G} + \dfrac{1}{k_L}$ | Resistance additivity, liquid basis | $\text{m·s}^{-1}$ | | 7 | $\dfrac{1}{K_Y} = \dfrac{1}{k_Y} + \dfrac{m'}{k_X}$ | Resistance additivity, ratio basis (gas) | $\text{ratio unit·m}^{2}\text{·s·kmol}^{-1}$ | | 8 | $\dfrac{1}{K_X} = \dfrac{1}{m'\,k_Y} + \dfrac{1}{k_X}$ | Resistance additivity, ratio basis (liquid) | $\text{ratio unit·m}^{2}\text{·s·kmol}^{-1}$ | | 9 | $k_Y = k_G\,P$ | Conversion in dilute gas | $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ | | 10 | $k_X = k_L\,C_{M,L}$ | Conversion in dilute liquid | $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ | | 11 | $K_Y = K_G\,P$ | Conversion (dilute gas) | $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ | | 12 | $K_X = K_L\,\rho_{s,M}$ | Conversion (dilute liquid, mass basis) | $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$ | | 13 | $K_G\,m = K_L$ | Reciprocal relation | — | | 14 | $K_Y\,m' = K_X$ | Reciprocal relation | — | | 15 | $K_G \cdot p_{BM} = k_G \cdot p_{i,BM}$ | Flux-equality at interface | — | | 16 | $K_L \cdot C_{BM} = k_L \cdot C_{i,BM}$ | Flux-equality at interface | — | > [!NOTE] > All *local* coefficients ($k$) become numerically *equal* to their *overall* counterparts ($K$) **only** when one of the two films is the **controlling resistance** (e.g., for a *highly soluble* gas $k_L$ is enormous, making $1/k_L \to 0$, so $K_G \approx k_G$ and $K_L \approx m\,k_G$). For *poorly soluble* gases the liquid film controls and $K_L \approx k_L$, $K_G \approx k_L/m$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Designing an ammonia absorber** (highly soluble gas → gas-film controls): We use $K_G \approx k_G$ and pilot-plant data on $k_G$ directly. Avoiding the liquid-film term saves a difficult experimental measurement. - **Designing a CO₂ stripper** (poorly soluble gas → liquid-film controls): We use $K_L \approx k_L$ and design using liquid-phase properties only. - **Process intensification**: When $K_G$ is found to be too small, the engineer can compute *which* film dominates using the $1/k$ ratio. If liquid-film dominates, increasing gas flow rate (which only raises $k_G$) is a waste — the fix is to increase liquid flow, raise temperature (changes $m$), or use a more efficient packing. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Two-Film Model to Overall Coefficient $K_G$ **Starting point** (Whitman's two-film theory, 1923): At steady state, the flux through the gas film equals the flux through the liquid film (no accumulation at the interface). $$N_A = k_G\,(p - p_i) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$N_A = k_L\,(C_i - C) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 1 — Express liquid-side driving force on a gas-equivalent basis using the equilibrium slope.** For a linear equilibrium $p^* = m\,C^*$, at the interface $p_i = m\,C_i$ and at the bulk liquid $p^* = m\,C$. Hence: $$C_i = \frac{p_i}{m}, \qquad C = \frac{p^*}{m}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into equation (ii).** $$N_A = k_L\left(\frac{p_i}{m} - \frac{p^*}{m}\right) = \frac{k_L}{m}\,(p_i - p^*)$$ **Step 3 — Recognise that $(p_i - p^*)$ is the driving force still "hidden" across the gas film that we have not yet eliminated.** Rearrange equation (i) for $p_i$: $$p - p_i = \frac{N_A}{k_G} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad p_i = p - \frac{N_A}{k_G}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $p_i$ into the Step 2 expression and collect $N_A$ terms.** $$N_A = \frac{k_L}{m}\left[\left(p - \frac{N_A}{k_G}\right) - p^*\right]$$ **Step 5 — Expand and isolate $N_A$ on the left.** $$N_A = \frac{k_L}{m}\,(p - p^*) - \frac{k_L}{m\,k_G}\,N_A$$ $$N_A + \frac{k_L}{m\,k_G}\,N_A = \frac{k_L}{m}\,(p - p^*)$$ $$N_A \left(1 + \frac{k_L}{m\,k_G}\right) = \frac{k_L}{m}\,(p - p^*)$$ **Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $m\,k_G$ to obtain a clean expression.** $$N_A \left(\frac{m\,k_G + k_L}{m\,k_G}\right) = \frac{k_L}{m}\,(p - p^*)$$ $$N_A = \frac{k_L}{m}\,(p - p^*) \cdot \frac{m\,k_G}{m\,k_G + k_L} = \frac{k_L\,k_G}{m\,k_G + k_L}\,(p - p^*)$$ **Step 7 — Identify the overall coefficient $K_G$.** $$N_A = \underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}}\right)}_{K_G}\,(p - p^*)$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}\;}$$ > **Reading the result:** the **resistances** $1/k_G$ and $m/k_L$ add in *series*, exactly like electrical resistances. The factor $m$ appears on the liquid-side term because we are adding resistances on a **common (gas-side) basis**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Companion Relation $1/K_L$ Starting from the same $N_A$ expression, we now write it on a *liquid-concentration* basis. Because $K_G (p-p^*) = K_L (C^*-C)$ and $p^*=mC^*$, $p=mC$: $$K_L = \frac{N_A}{C^*-C} = \frac{K_G(p-p^*)}{C^*-C} = \frac{K_G\,m\,(C^*-C)}{C^*-C} = m\,K_G$$ Therefore, $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{m\,K_G}$. Substituting the expression for $K_G$ from §3.1: $$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{m}\left(\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}\right) = \frac{1}{m\,k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L}$$ $$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{m\,k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L}\;}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Ratio-Based Forms $K_Y$ and $K_X$ For concentrated solutions, mole fractions must be replaced by **mole ratios** $Y = y/(1-y)$ and $X = x/(1-x)$ to keep the molar flow rates $G'$ and $L'$ (kmol inert/s) constant. Define the equilibrium relation in ratio form: $$Y^* = m'\,X^*, \qquad m' = m\,\frac{Y}{X}\bigg|_{\text{operating point}}$$ A differential flux balance on a differential height $dZ$ of a packed column gives the **HTU-NTU** form: $$dY = \frac{K_Y\,a\,(Y-Y^*)}{G'}\,dZ$$ with $K_Y$ having units of $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$. By identical algebraic manipulation as in §3.1 (replacing $k_G \to k_Y$, $k_L \to k_X$, $m \to m'$): $$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{K_Y} = \frac{1}{k_Y} + \frac{m'}{k_X}\;}, \qquad \boxed{\;\frac{1}{K_X} = \frac{1}{m'\,k_Y} + \frac{1}{k_X}\;}$$ For *dilute* systems $m' \approx m$ and the expressions collapse to the $K_G$/$K_L$ forms. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Interconversion Between Concentration-Based and Ratio-Based Local Coefficients In the gas phase, $Y = y/(1-y)$. For a *dilute* solute, $1-y \approx 1$ and the inert gas molar flow $G' \approx G$. Then: $$dY \approx \frac{dy}{(1-y)^2} \approx dy, \qquad \text{and} \qquad N_A = k_G(p-p_i) = k_G\,P\,(y-y_i) = (k_G P)\,(Y-Y_i)$$ So **the conversion factor is the total pressure $P$**: $$\boxed{\;k_Y = k_G\,P\;}$$ A parallel argument in the liquid phase (dilute solute, $X \approx x$, total liquid concentration $C_{M,L}$ constant): $$N_A = k_L(C_i - C) = k_L\,C_{M,L}\,(x_i - x) = (k_L\,C_{M,L})\,(X_i - X)$$ $$\boxed{\;k_X = k_L\,C_{M,L}\;}$$ > The same relations hold between overall coefficients: $K_Y = K_G P$ and $K_X = K_L \rho_{s,M}$ (where $\rho_{s,M}$ is the molar density of the solute-free liquid, in $\text{kmol·m}^{-3}$). --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Criterion for $k = K$ The local coefficient equals the overall coefficient numerically only when one film is *infinitely fast* (its resistance is zero). From the resistance-additivity formula: $$K_G = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}}$$ - If $k_L \to \infty$ (highly soluble gas, **gas-film controls**): $\dfrac{m}{k_L} \to 0$, hence $K_G \to k_G$. - If $k_G \to \infty$ (poorly soluble gas, **liquid-film controls**): $\dfrac{1}{k_G} \to 0$, hence $K_G \to \dfrac{k_L}{m}$ and $K_L = mK_G \to k_L$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often write "$K_G = k_G$" universally. This is **wrong** for gases of intermediate solubility (e.g., SO₂, Cl₂ in water). Equality holds *only* at the limits above. In general, $K_G$ and $k_G$ are different quantities with different meanings. --- ## 3.6 Worked Analytical Matrix — Typical Numerical Conditions | System | Solute | $m = dy^*/dx$ | Controlling film | Approximation valid | |---|---|---|---|---| | NH₃ – H₂O | Ammonia | $\approx 0.95$ (very large) | Gas film | $K_G \approx k_G$, $K_L \approx m\,k_G$ | | SO₂ – H₂O | Sulfur dioxide | $\approx 30$ at 25 °C | Gas film (mostly) | $K_G \approx 0.7\,k_G$ (correction needed) | | CO₂ – H₂O | Carbon dioxide | $\approx 1400$ at 25 °C | Liquid film | $K_L \approx k_L$, $K_G \approx k_L/m$ | | O₂ – H₂O | Oxygen | $\approx 4 \times 10^{4}$ | Liquid film | $K_L \approx k_L$ | (The Henry's-law constant $m$ values above assume a linear $y^* = m x$ form. *Verify* against your textbook's tabulated $H$ or $m$ before using in exams.) # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Two-Film Concentration Profile & Local/Overall Driving Forces The figure below shows the concentration profile of solute *A* across the two stagnant films, identifying *which* distance corresponds to the *local* vs. *overall* driving force on the gas and liquid sides. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Bulk Gas
p (or Y)"] --> B["Gas-Film
Boundary
p_i (or Y_i)"] B --> C["Interface
Equilibrium:
p_i = m C_i"] C --> D["Liquid-Film
Boundary
C_i (or X_i)"] D --> E["Bulk Liquid
C (or X)"] F["p* in equilibrium
with bulk liquid C"] -.-> A E -.-> F G1["Local gas driving force
p - p_i = Δp_local"] --- A G2["Local liquid driving force
C_i - C = ΔC_local"] --- D G3["Overall gas driving force
p - p* = Δp_overall"] --- F G4["Overall liquid driving force
C* - C = ΔC_overall"] --- E style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style E fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style B fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style C fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 style F fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Horizontal distance from a node's position to the next is *proportional* to the magnitude of that driving force. The local gas-film driving force $(p-p_i)$ is a *subset* of the overall driving force $(p-p^*)$, which spans the entire diagram. The same logic holds on the liquid side: $(C_i-C) \subset (C^*-C)$. --- ## 4.2 Resistance-Analogy Block Diagram (Electrical Network Equivalent) The two-film model has a direct analogy to two electrical resistances in *series* on either a gas-side or a liquid-side basis. Below is the equivalent circuit for the gas-side overall resistance. ```mermaid flowchart LR IN["Driving Force
(p - p*)"] --> R1["1 divided by k_G
Gas-film resistance"] R1 --> R2["m divided by k_L
Liquid-film resistance
expressed on gas basis"] R2 --> OUT["N_A
molar flux"] style IN fill:#0d6efd,stroke:#032468,color:#ffffff style R1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style R2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style OUT fill:#198754,stroke:#0a3622,color:#ffffff ``` > **Interpretation:** The two resistances are in *series* because the same flux $N_A$ must pass through *both*. The voltage drop $(p-p^*)$ is shared between them in proportion to their magnitudes. The reciprocal of the sum gives the overall conductance $K_G$. --- ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Coefficient Should I Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Pick a mass-transfer problem"] --> B{"Is the solute
concentrated or dilute?"} B -- "Dilute" --> C["Use k_G, k_L, K_G, K_L"] B -- "Concentrated" --> D["Use k_Y, k_X, K_Y, K_X"] C --> E{"Is the gas highly
soluble (m small)?"} D --> F{"Is the gas highly
soluble (m small)?"} E -- "Yes" --> G["Gas film controls
K_G ≈ k_G"] E -- "No" --> H["Use full formula:
1 over K_G = 1 over k_G + m over k_L"] F -- "Yes" --> I["Gas film controls
K_Y ≈ k_Y"] F -- "No" --> J["Use full formula:
1 over K_Y = 1 over k_Y + m-prime over k_X"] style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style G fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 style H fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style I fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 style J fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between local mass transfer coefficient and overall mass transfer coefficient. Under what condition are they numerically equal?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Local mass transfer coefficient** $k$ is defined at a point in the equipment and relates the molar flux to the *concentration driving force across a single phase* (one film). Example: $N_A = k_G(p - p_i)$ — driving force spans only the gas film. - **Overall mass transfer coefficient** $K$ relates the flux to the driving force measured *between the bulk of one phase and the bulk of the other phase in equilibrium with it*. Example: $N_A = K_G(p - p^*)$ — driving force spans both films. - They are **numerically equal** only when one film offers *negligible resistance*, i.e., $K_G = k_G$ if the gas film controls ($1/k_L \to 0$, meaning a *highly soluble* gas), and $K_L = k_L$ if the liquid film controls ($1/k_G \to 0$, meaning a *poorly soluble* gas). **(3 marks)** ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Write the resistance-additivity relations for $K_G$ and $K_L$ in terms of $k_G$, $k_L$ and the equilibrium slope $m$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} \qquad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{m\,k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L} \qquad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ with the reciprocal relation $K_L = m\,K_G$. The factor $m$ appears on the *opposite* phase from the basis chosen because it converts one phase's concentration drop into an equivalent drop on the other phase's basis via the linearised equilibrium line $y^* = m x$. **(1 mark for explanation)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **one full question of 14 marks** per module, with internal choice. Two alternatives are given below; only one needs to be attempted. --- ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]** **(a)** Starting from the two-film model, derive the resistance-additivity formula $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A solute $A$ is being absorbed from a gas mixture into water at 30 °C and 1 atm total pressure. The local individual-phase coefficients at a particular point in the column are $k_G = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$ and $k_L = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$. The equilibrium relation in the operating range is linear: $p^* = 5000\,C$ (with $p^*$ in Pa and $C$ in $\text{kmol·m}^{-3}$). Compute: (i) The overall coefficients $K_G$ and $K_L$ at this point. **(2 marks)** (ii) The percentage of the total resistance offered by the gas film. **(1 mark)** (iii) The flux $N_A$ when the local bulk driving forces are $p - p^* = 2000\,\text{Pa}$ and $C^* - C = 0.0004\,\text{kmol·m}^{-3}$. Verify that both definitions give the same $N_A$. **(4 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** *Assumptions (2 marks):* 1. Steady-state operation (no solute accumulation at the interface). 2. The two-film model is valid — mass transfer is by molecular diffusion through two stagnant films adjacent to the interface. 3. The equilibrium relation is linear over the operating range: $p^* = m C^*$ (slope $m$ is constant). 4. Local coefficients $k_G$ and $k_L$ are constant at the point of interest. *Derivation steps (5 marks):* The full derivation is given in §3.1. Key intermediate steps to be written out: - $N_A = k_G (p - p_i)$ ...(i) - $N_A = k_L (C_i - C)$ ...(ii) - Use $p_i = m C_i$ and $p^* = m C$, so $C_i - C = (p_i - p^*)/m$. - $N_A = (k_L/m)(p_i - p^*)$ ...(iii) - From (i): $p_i = p - N_A/k_G$. Substitute into (iii): $$N_A = \frac{k_L}{m}\left(p - \frac{N_A}{k_G} - p^*\right) = \frac{k_L}{m}(p - p^*) - \frac{k_L}{m\,k_G}\,N_A$$ - Collect $N_A$: $$N_A\left(1 + \frac{k_L}{m\,k_G}\right) = \frac{k_L}{m}(p - p^*)$$ $$N_A\,\frac{m\,k_G + k_L}{m\,k_G} = \frac{k_L}{m}(p - p^*)$$ $$N_A = \frac{k_L\,k_G}{m\,k_G + k_L}(p - p^*) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}}}_{K_G}(p - p^*)$$ $$\therefore \frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} \qquad \blacksquare$$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** Given: $m = 5000$ Pa·m³·kmol⁻¹, $k_G = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$ kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹, $k_L = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹. **(i) Compute $K_G$ and $K_L$ (2 marks):** $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-8}} + \frac{5000}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_G} = 6.667 \times 10^{7} + 1.667 \times 10^{8} = 2.333 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Pa·m}^{2}\text{·s·kmol}^{-1}$$ $$K_G = 4.286 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$$ $$K_L = m\,K_G = 5000 \times 4.286 \times 10^{-9} = 2.143 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$$ **(ii) Percentage resistance by gas film (1 mark):** $$\%\text{Gas} = \frac{1/k_G}{1/K_G} \times 100 = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{7}}{2.333 \times 10^{8}} \times 100 = 28.6\%$$ **Liquid film** = 100 − 28.6 = **71.4 %** (controlling). **(iii) Flux verification (4 marks):** Method 1 — using $K_G$ and the gas-side overall driving force: $$N_A = K_G(p - p^*) = 4.286 \times 10^{-9} \times 2000 = 8.57 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ Method 2 — using $K_L$ and the liquid-side overall driving force: $$N_A = K_L(C^* - C) = 2.143 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 8.57 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ ✓ **Both methods give the same $N_A$**, confirming the reciprocity $K_L = m K_G$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Unit mismatch in $m$:** Students frequently forget to keep $m$ in *consistent* units with $k_G$ and $k_L$. If $k_G$ is per Pa and $k_L$ is per kmol·m⁻³, then $m$ must be in Pa·m³·kmol⁻¹. Using $m$ in atm-units or in mole-fraction units is the most common 1–2 mark loss. > 2. **Reciprocal-relation sign error:** $K_L = m K_G$, **not** $K_L = K_G/m$. (The reciprocal is $K_G = K_L/m$.) Many students write the wrong form. > 3. **"Local = overall" trap:** Do not write $K_G = k_G$ unless the problem explicitly states gas-film control. Use the full resistance-additivity form always, then identify the controlling resistance in the answer's last line. > 4. **Forgetting the equilibrium slope:** $1/K_G$ uses $m/k_L$, while $1/K_L$ uses $1/(m k_G)$. Mixing these up is a 3-mark deduction. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Define the four local mass transfer coefficients $k_G$, $k_L$, $k_Y$, $k_X$ and the four overall coefficients $K_G$, $K_L$, $K_Y$, $K_X$. For dilute systems, derive the relations $k_Y = k_G P$ and $k_X = k_L C_{M,L}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For an SO₂–air–water absorption system at 1 atm and 30 °C, the local film coefficients are $k_G = 1.2 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$ and $k_L = 8.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$. The linearised equilibrium slope is $m = 30$ (in mole-fraction units, i.e., $y^* = m x$). Determine the overall coefficients in *all four* forms, comment on the controlling resistance, and compute the molar flux if $y - y^* = 0.02$ and $x^* - x = 6.67 \times 10^{-4}$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation (7 marks):** *Definitions (3 marks — ½ mark each + 1 mark for the table-style summary):* | Coefficient | Defining Equation | Phase | Driving-force basis | |---|---|---|---| | $k_G$ | $N_A = k_G(p - p_i)$ | Gas film | Partial pressure (Pa or atm) | | $k_L$ | $N_A = k_L(C_i - C)$ | Liquid film | Concentration (kmol·m⁻³) | | $k_Y$ | $N_A = k_Y(Y - Y_i)$ | Gas film | Mole ratio | | $k_X$ | $N_A = k_X(X_i - X)$ | Liquid film | Mole ratio | | $K_G$ | $N_A = K_G(p - p^*)$ | Overall | Gas-side bulk ↔ equilibrium | | $K_L$ | $N_A = K_L(C^* - C)$ | Overall | Liquid-side equilibrium ↔ bulk | | $K_Y$ | $N_A = K_Y(Y - Y^*)$ | Overall | Gas mole ratio | | $K_X$ | $N_A = K_X(X^* - X)$ | Overall | Liquid mole ratio | *Derivation of $k_Y = k_G P$ (2 marks):* For a *dilute* gas, $y \ll 1$, so $Y = y/(1-y) \approx y$. The flux equation is: $$N_A = k_G(p - p_i) = k_G\,P\,(y - y_i) = (k_G P)\,(Y - Y_i)$$ Comparing with $N_A = k_Y(Y - Y_i)$, we get $k_Y = k_G P$. **∎** *Derivation of $k_X = k_L C_{M,L}$ (2 marks):* For a *dilute* liquid, $x \ll 1$, $X \approx x$, and the total molar concentration $C_{M,L}$ (kmol·m⁻³) is approximately constant. $$N_A = k_L(C_i - C) = k_L\,C_{M,L}\,(x_i - x) = (k_L C_{M,L})\,(X_i - X)$$ Comparing, $k_X = k_L C_{M,L}$. **∎** **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** Given (in mole-fraction basis): $m = 30$ dimensionless. Note: $k_G$ has been given per Pa, so we should convert $m$ to consistent units. However, the *ratio* $m/k_L$ in the resistance-additivity formula needs the *slope of $p^*$ vs $C$*, not $y^*$ vs $x$. For an ideal gas $p^* = y^* P_{\text{tot}}$ and $C = x\,C_{M,L}$ (dilute), so: $$m_{\text{pressure}} = \frac{p^*}{C} = \frac{y^* P}{x\,C_{M,L}} = \frac{m_{\text{mole frac}} \cdot P}{C_{M,L}}$$ For the present calculation we use the **mole-fraction form** directly: *Step 1 — Compute overall coefficients on the gas mole-fraction basis $K_y$ and liquid mole-fraction basis $K_x$ (2 marks):* $$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}, \quad \text{with} \quad k_y = k_G P = 1.2 \times 10^{-8} \times 101325 = 1.216 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ $$k_x = k_L\,C_{M,L} \approx k_L \times 55.5\,\text{kmol·m}^{-3} = 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 55.5 = 4.44 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{1.216 \times 10^{-3}} + \frac{30}{4.44 \times 10^{-3}} = 822.4 + 6756.8 = 7579.2$$ $$K_y = 1.319 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ $$K_x = m\,K_y = 30 \times 1.319 \times 10^{-4} = 3.958 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ *Step 2 — Convert back to $K_G$ and $K_L$ (1 mark):* $$K_G = \frac{K_y}{P} = \frac{1.319 \times 10^{-4}}{101325} = 1.302 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$$ $$K_L = \frac{K_x}{C_{M,L}} = \frac{3.958 \times 10^{-3}}{55.5} = 7.13 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$$ *Step 3 — Comment on controlling resistance (2 marks):* $$\%\text{Gas-film} = \frac{1/k_y}{1/K_y} \times 100 = \frac{822.4}{7579.2} \times 100 = 10.85\%$$ $$\%\text{Liquid-film} = 89.15\%$$ **Liquid film is the controlling resistance** for SO₂–water at this temperature. *(Note: SO₂ is intermediate-solubility; at higher T, gas-film control strengthens.)* *Step 4 — Molar flux (2 marks):* Using mole-fraction overall driving forces: $$N_A = K_y\,(y - y^*) = 1.319 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02 = 2.639 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ $$N_A = K_x\,(x^* - x) = 3.958 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.67 \times 10^{-4} = 2.640 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ ✓ **Both match** (small rounding). Final answer: $N_A \approx 2.64 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfalls — Question B** > 1. **Unit-system inconsistency:** When $k_G$ is given in $\text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$ but the equilibrium slope $m$ is given in mole-fraction units, you *must* convert $m$ to Pa·m³·kmol⁻¹ (or convert $k_G$ to mole-fraction basis via $k_y = k_G P$). Mixing systems is the #1 source of 2–3 mark losses. > 2. **Neglect of $C_{M,L}$:** Students frequently set $k_x = k_L$, ignoring the conversion factor $C_{M,L}$. For water at 30 °C, $C_{M,L} = 55.5\,\text{kmol·m}^{-3}$ — a large factor that *cannot* be omitted. > 3. **Skipping the verification step:** Always compute $N_A$ using *two* different forms of the overall coefficient and confirm they match. This shows the examiner that you understand the *reciprocity relation* $K_x = m K_y$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ *Local* $k$ = single-film resistance; *Overall* $K$ = combined two-film resistance expressed on a *single* basis. - ✅ The two-film model assumes **steady state, stagnant films, and linear equilibrium** ($p^* = m C^*$ or $Y^* = m' X^*$). - ✅ Resistance-additivity formulas (the *core* of this topic): $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}, \qquad \frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{m\,k_G} + \frac{1}{k_L}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_Y} = \frac{1}{k_Y} + \frac{m'}{k_X}, \qquad \frac{1}{K_X} = \frac{1}{m'\,k_Y} + \frac{1}{k_X}$$ - ✅ Reciprocity relations: $K_L = m K_G$ and $K_X = m' K_Y$ — always verify by computing $N_A$ both ways. - ✅ Conversion to ratio basis (dilute systems): $k_Y = k_G P$, $k_X = k_L C_{M,L}$, $K_Y = K_G P$, $K_X = K_L \rho_{s,M}$. - ✅ **Controlling-resistance limits** (this is the most-tested 2-mark idea): - *Highly soluble gas* (small $m$): gas film controls → $K_G \approx k_G$, $K_L \approx m k_G$. - *Poorly soluble gas* (large $m$): liquid film controls → $K_L \approx k_L$, $K_G \approx k_L / m$. - ✅ $k = K$ **only at the limits** above — never in the general case. - ✅ Equilibrium slope $m$ in the *resistance-additivity formula must be in the same units as the basis chosen*: Pa·m³·kmol⁻¹ for $K_G$ form; mole-fraction ratio (dimensionless) for $K_y$ form. - ✅ Always end a numerical problem with a **reciprocity check**: $K_L \stackrel{?}{=} m K_G$. - ✅ Watch out for the **controlling-film trap**: 28.6 % in the worked example means **71.4 %** is the controlling resistance — don't mis-identify. - ✅ Conceptual difference: $k_Y$ and $k_X$ are used in *concentrated* systems (with constant inert flow $G'$, $L'$); $k_G$ and $k_L$ are used in *dilute* systems (with constant total flow).

topics.title: Problem Solving in Molecular Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Problem Solving in Molecular Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Molecular Diffusion** is the transport mechanism by which individual molecules spontaneously move from a region of higher chemical potential (or concentration) to a region of lower chemical potential (or concentration) under the influence of a concentration gradient, in the absence of any bulk convective motion. In the context of Mass Transfer Operations, it represents the *steady-state, one-dimensional* mass-transfer mechanism governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**.

Mathematically, Fick's First Law for a binary system A–B is expressed as:

$$J_A^{*} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

where $J_A^{*}$ is the **molar diffusive flux** of A relative to the molar average velocity (kmol/m²·s), $D_{AB}$ is the **gas-phase diffusivity** (m²/s), $C_A$ is the molar concentration of A (kmol/m³), and $z$ is the direction of diffusion (m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCHT403 – Module 1) lays strong emphasis on solving numerical problems involving three canonical cases — *equimolar counter-diffusion*, *diffusion of A through stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)*, and *diffusion in liquids*. Mastery of these three cases accounts for **~70% of marks** in Part B questions on Module 1.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Perfume-Scented Room"

Imagine standing in a closed room and someone sprays perfume at one corner. Within seconds, the entire room begins to smell of the perfume, even though the air is *completely still*. This is molecular diffusion in action. There is no fan, no wind — yet the perfume molecules have spread uniformly. Why? Because each individual molecule executes a *random walk* due to its thermal kinetic energy. Over time, statistically more molecules move from the high-concentration corner toward the low-concentration region than the reverse, producing a net flux down the concentration gradient.

The same physical law governs:
- A sugar cube dissolving in a glass of still water
- Oxygen diffusing through stagnant nitrogen in a lung alveolus
- Ammonia being absorbed from an ammonia–air mixture into water in an absorption tower
- Moisture migrating through a polymer packaging film

The perfume room is *gaseous diffusion*, the sugar–water case is *liquid diffusion*, and the polymer packaging film is *solid-state diffusion* — the three classical regimes of molecular diffusion.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (Gases) | Typical Value (Liquids) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_{AB}$ | Binary diffusivity | **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s** | **$10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s** |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K) = 8314 J/(kmol·K)** | — |
| $P$ | Total pressure | 1 atm = **101325 Pa** | — |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | 298 K (25 °C) | 298 K |
| $Sc$ | Schmidt number ($\mu / \rho D_{AB}$) | **0.5 – 3** | **100 – 10000** |
| $P_r$ | Prandtl number analogy | 0.7–1.0 | 1–10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Do *not* confuse the **absolute molar flux** $N_A$ (which includes bulk-flow contribution) with the **diffusive flux** $J_A^{*}$ (which is purely molecular). KTU problems specifically test whether you correctly use $N_A = J_A^{*} + x_A N_{\text{total}}$ in the boundary conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Problem Types

Every molecular diffusion problem in Module 1 reduces to one of three sub-cases, depending on the behaviour of the second component (B):

### Case 1: Equimolar Counter-diffusion ($N_A = -N_B$)
- **Physical scenario:** Two opposing fluxes are exactly equal and opposite.
- **Examples:** Distillation of a binary mixture at constant molar overflow; isomerisation reactions.
- **Bulk-flow contribution:** **Zero**, because $N_{\text{total}} = N_A + N_B = 0$.
- **Governing equation:** Direct application of Fick's law with bulk velocity zero.

### Case 2: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B ($N_B = 0$) — *Stefan Diffusion*
- **Physical scenario:** Component B is non-diffusing, e.g., absorption of ammonia from air into water (B = air is essentially stagnant).
- **Bulk-flow contribution:** **Non-zero**, $N_{\text{total}} = N_A$.
- **Governing equation:** Fick's law with bulk-flow correction; introduces the **log-mean partial pressure of B** ($p_{BM}$).

### Case 3: Liquid-Phase Diffusion
- **Physical scenario:** Solute A diffusing through solvent B (often stagnant).
- **Governing equation:** Same form as Stefan diffusion but with **molar concentrations** and **log-mean mole fraction of B** ($x_{BM}$).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Cheat Sheet:** Memorise every equation in this table. KTU valuation expects zero tolerance for incorrect formula selection or missing $p_{BM}$ / $x_{BM}$ terms.

### A. GAS-PHASE DIFFUSION

| # | Case | Governing Equation | Boundary Inputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| G1 | Fick's First Law (general) | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \dfrac{dy_A}{dz} + y_A N_{\text{total}}$ | — |
| G2 | Equimolar counter-diffusion ($N_B = -N_A$) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT\,z}(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$ | $y_{A1}, y_{A2}$ at two planes |
| G3 | Stefan diffusion ($N_B = 0$) — log form | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)$ | $y_{A1}, y_{A2}$ |
| G4 | Stefan diffusion — $p_{BM}$ form | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT\,z} \cdot \dfrac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$ | $p_{A1}, p_{A2}$ |
| G5 | Log-mean of B (definition) | $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ | $p_{B1}, p_{B2}$ |

### B. LIQUID-PHASE DIFFUSION

| # | Case | Governing Equation | Boundary Inputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | Equimolar counter-diffusion | $N_A = \dfrac{\mathcal{D}_{AB}}{z}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | $C_{A1}, C_{A2}$ |
| L2 | Stagnant B — mole-fraction form | $N_A = \dfrac{\mathcal{D}_{AB}\,C}{z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)$ | $x_{A1}, x_{A2}$, total $C$ |
| L3 | Stagnant B — $x_{BM}$ form | $N_A = \dfrac{\mathcal{D}_{AB}\,C}{z} \cdot \dfrac{x_{A1} - x_{A2}}{x_{BM}}$ | same as L2 |
| L4 | Log-mean of B | $x_{BM} = \dfrac{x_{B2} - x_{B1}}{\ln(x_{B2}/x_{B1})}$ | $x_{B1}, x_{B2}$ |
| L5 | Mass-flux form | $N_A = \dfrac{\mathcal{D}_{AB}\,\rho}{M\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - \omega_{A2}}{1 - \omega_{A1}}\right)$ | $\omega_{A1}, \omega_{A2}$ |

### C. SOLID-PHASE DIFFUSION (Porous Solids)

| # | Case | Governing Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Steady-state through stagnant solid | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}^{\,\text{eff}}}{z}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | $D^{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{AB}$ |
| S2 | Knudsen regime (small pores) | $D_K = 97\,r_p \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{M_A}}$ | $r_p$ in metres, $T$ in K |

### D. DIMENSIONLESS GROUPS

| # | Group | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | Schmidt number | $Sc = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho\,D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity |
| D2 | Schmidt number (alternative) | $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | Kinematic viscosity / mass diffusivity |

> **Note on $\vert$ symbol usage:** For absolute values inside any table cell (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe (`|`) delimiter.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mastery of these equations is the cornerstone of designing **gas-absorption columns** (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ scrubbing with MEA), **distillation columns** (VLE at the interface), **membrane separation units**, **drying operations**, and **humidification–dehumidification systems**. The $p_{BM}$ factor elegantly captures the fact that as A diffuses toward the interface, B must physically move away — a "convective drag" that always *enhances* the net flux of A beyond what pure molecular diffusion would predict. Without the $p_{BM}$ correction, designs would be off by 10–20% — a significant margin in industrial columns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equimolar Counter-diffusion (Gases)

### Step 1 — Start from the general binary flux equation

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A N_{\text{total}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the equimolar constraint

Equimolar counter-diffusion means exactly equal and opposite molar fluxes:

$$N_A = -N_B \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_{\text{total}} = N_A + N_B = 0$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $N_{\text{total}} = 0$

The bulk-flow term vanishes entirely:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

### Step 4 — Convert concentration gradient to mole-fraction gradient

For an ideal gas at constant $P$ and $T$:

$$C_A = \frac{P}{RT}\,y_A \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; dC_A = \frac{P}{RT}\,dy_A$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and separate variables

$$N_A\,dz = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{P}{RT}\,dy_A$$

### Step 6 — Integrate across the diffusion path

Integrate from $z = z_1$ (where $y_A = y_{A1}$) to $z = z_2$ (where $y_A = y_{A2}$):

$$N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -\frac{D_{AB}P}{RT} \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} dy_A$$

$$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = -\frac{D_{AB}P}{RT}\,(y_{A2} - y_{A1})$$

### Step 7 — Final Result

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,(z_2 - z_1)}\,(y_{A1} - y_{A2})}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)

### Step 1 — Start from the general binary flux equation

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A N_{\text{total}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the stagnant-B constraint

If B is stagnant, $N_B = 0$:

$$N_{\text{total}} = N_A + N_B = N_A$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $N_{\text{total}} = N_A$

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A N_A$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange

$$N_A(1 - y_A) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

### Step 5 — Use ideal-gas relation $dC_A = (P/RT)\,dy_A$

$$N_A(1 - y_A)\,dz = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{P}{RT}\,dy_A$$

### Step 6 — Substitute $1 - y_A = y_B$, so $dy_A = -dy_B$

$$N_A\,y_B\,dz = D_{AB} \cdot \frac{P}{RT}\,dy_B$$

### Step 7 — Separate and integrate

$$N_A \int_{0}^{z} dz = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT} \int_{y_{B1}}^{y_{B2}} \frac{dy_B}{y_B}$$

$$N_A\,z = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{y_{B2}}{y_{B1}}\right)$$

### Step 8 — Final Result (log form)

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)}$$

### Step 9 — Convert to $p_{BM}$ form (KTU favourite form)

Recall $y_B = p_B / P$. Then:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z} \cdot \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{p_{BM}}$$

where

$$\boxed{p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}}$$

since $p_{B2} - p_{B1} = (P - p_{A2}) - (P - p_{A1}) = p_{A1} - p_{A2}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Equimolar Counter-diffusion

> **Problem:** A binary gas mixture of H₂ (A) and CO₂ (B) undergoes equimolar counter-diffusion at 1 atm total pressure and 25 °C through a tube of length 0.005 m. At the left end ($z_1 = 0$), $y_{A1} = 0.50$; at the right end ($z_2 = 0.005$ m), $y_{A2} = 0.20$. The diffusivity $D_{AB} = 6.15 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux of H₂ and verify the flux of CO₂.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

| Step | Quantity | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T$ | $25 + 273.15 = 298.15$ K |
| 2 | $P$ | $1\text{ atm} = 101325$ Pa |
| 3 | $D_{AB}$ | $6.15 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s |
| 4 | $z$ | $z_2 - z_1 = 0.005$ m |

Apply the equimolar formula (G2 from §2.2):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z}(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$$

Substitute numerically:

$$N_A = \frac{(6.15 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8314)(298.15)(0.005)}(0.50 - 0.20)$$

Compute numerator and denominator separately:

$$\text{Numerator} = 6.15 \times 10^{-5} \times 101325 = 6.2315$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 8314 \times 298.15 \times 0.005 = 12391.34$$

$$\frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z} = \frac{6.2315}{12391.34} = 5.029 \times 10^{-4}\,\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 5.029 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.30 = 1.509 \times 10^{-4}\;\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}}$$

By equimolarity, $N_B = -N_A = -1.509 \times 10^{-4}$ kmol/(m²·s).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Stefan Diffusion (Ammonia through stagnant Air)

> **Problem:** Ammonia (A) is being absorbed from an ammonia–air mixture at 1 atm and 25 °C using water in an absorption tower. At a particular point in the tower, the bulk gas contains 30 mol% NH₃ and diffuses through a 0.5 mm thick *stagnant* air film to the water surface. The ammonia is essentially completely absorbed at the water surface, so the partial pressure of NH₃ there is zero. The diffusivity of NH₃ in air is $D_{AB} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the steady-state flux of NH₃.

**Given Data:**

- $T = 298.15$ K, $P = 101325$ Pa
- $y_{A1} = 0.30 \;\Rightarrow\; p_{A1} = 0.30 \text{ atm}$
- $y_{A2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; p_{A2} = 0$
- $z = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $D_{AB} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Step 1 — Compute partial pressures of B (air) at both planes**

$$p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 1.0 - 0.30 = 0.70 \text{ atm}$$

$$p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 1.0 - 0 = 1.0 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $p_{BM}$**

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})} = \frac{1.0 - 0.70}{\ln(1.0/0.70)} = \frac{0.30}{0.3567} = 0.8411 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Stefan diffusion formula (G4)**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$

Compute the common prefactor:

$$\frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z} = \frac{(2.3 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8314)(298.15)(0.5 \times 10^{-3})}$$

Numerator: $2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times 101325 = 2.3305$

Denominator: $8314 \times 298.15 \times 0.0005 = 1239.14$

$$\frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z} = \frac{2.3305}{1239.14} = 1.881 \times 10^{-3}\;\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the driving-force correction**

$$\frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}} = \frac{0.30 - 0}{0.8411} = 0.3567$$

**Step 5 — Final Flux**

$$N_A = 1.881 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3567 = 6.71 \times 10^{-4}\;\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}$$

$$\boxed{N_A \approx 6.71 \times 10^{-4}\;\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification using the log-form (G3):** $\ln[(1-0)/(1-0.30)] = \ln(1/0.70) = 0.3567$. Same driving factor, confirming our $p_{BM}$ computation is correct.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Liquid-Phase Diffusion (Stagnant B)

> **Problem:** A dilute solution of ethanol (A) in water (B) flows as a film over a solid surface. Ethanol diffuses from the bulk (where $x_{A1} = 0.08$) through a 2 mm thick stagnant liquid film to the surface where $x_{A2} = 0.02$. The molar density of the solution is $C = 45$ kmol/m³. The diffusivity of ethanol in water is $\mathcal{D}_{AB} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Calculate the steady-state flux assuming B is stagnant.

**Step 1 — Compute $x_B$ values**

$$x_{B1} = 1 - x_{A1} = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92$$

$$x_{B2} = 1 - x_{A2} = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the log-mean $x_{BM}$**

$$x_{BM} = \frac{x_{B2} - x_{B1}}{\ln(x_{B2}/x_{B1})} = \frac{0.98 - 0.92}{\ln(0.98/0.92)} = \frac{0.06}{\ln(1.0652)} = \frac{0.06}{0.06314} = 0.9502$$

**Step 3 — Apply the liquid-phase stagnant-B formula (L3)**

$$N_A = \frac{\mathcal{D}_{AB}\,C}{z} \cdot \frac{x_{A1} - x_{A2}}{x_{BM}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify**

$$N_A = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-9})(45)}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times \frac{0.08 - 0.02}{0.9502}$$

$$N_A = 2.7 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{0.06}{0.9502}$$

$$N_A = 2.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.06314$$

$$\boxed{N_A \approx 1.705 \times 10^{-6}\;\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Problem Solver

Use this decision matrix in the first 30 seconds of any exam problem:

| **Clue in the Problem Statement** | **System Identified** | **Equation to Apply** | **Critical Step** |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Equimolar counter-diffusion" | Gas, $N_B = -N_A$ | G2 | Just use $\Delta y_A$, no $p_{BM}$ |
| "A through stagnant B" + "absorption" | Gas, $N_B = 0$ | G4 | Compute $p_{BM}$ first |
| "Distillation at constant molar overflow" | Gas, equimolar | G2 | $N_A = -N_B$ implicit |
| "A diffuses through stagnant liquid B" | Liquid, $N_B = 0$ | L2 or L3 | Compute $x_{BM}$ |
| "Molar concentration given in kmol/m³" | Liquid | L1 (equimolar) or L3 (stagnant) | Check if $C$ is constant |
| "Mass fractions given" | Liquid or gas | L5 or mass-flux form | Use $\omega$ form |
| "Membrane / polymer film" | Solid | S1 | Use effective diffusivity |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Molecular Diffusion Problem-Solving Decision Tree

The following flowchart provides a visual algorithm for *identifying the correct governing equation* in any KTU-style molecular diffusion problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[START IDENTIFY SYSTEM] --> B1{PHASE PRESENT}
    B1 -->|GAS PHASE| C1{STAGNANT B PRESENT}
    B1 -->|LIQUID PHASE| D1{STAGNANT B PRESENT}
    B1 -->|SOLID PHASE| E1[APPLY EFFECTIVE DIFFUSIVITY WITH POROSITY AND TORTUOSITY]
    C1 -->|NO EQUIMOLAR CASE| F1[USE EQUIMOLAR FORMULA N A EQUALS DAB P OVER RT Z TIMES DELTA Y A]
    C1 -->|YES STAGNANT B| G1[USE STEFAN DIFFUSION FORMULA WITH PBM CORRECTION]
    D1 -->|NO EQUIMOLAR| H1[USE SIMPLE LIQUID DIFFUSION N A EQUALS DAB OVER Z TIMES DELTA C A]
    D1 -->|YES STAGNANT B| I1[USE LIQUID FORMULA WITH XBM LOG MEAN OF SOLVENT]
    F1 --> J1[CALCULATE N A IN KMOL PER M SQUARE S]
    G1 --> J1
    H1 --> J1
    I1 --> J1
    E1 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[SANITY CHECK UNITS AND BOUNDARY CONDITIONS]
    K1 --> L1[FINAL ANSWER N A REPORTED TO THREE SIGNIFICANT FIGURES]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Concentration Profile Across a Stagnant Film

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BulkGas[BULK GAS REGION Y A1 HIGH CONCENTRATION] -->|DIFFUSION THROUGH STAGNANT B| Film[STAGNANT GAS FILM THICKNESS Z]
    Film --> Interface[GAS LIQUID INTERFACE Y A2 LOW OR ZERO]
    Interface --> Liquid[BULK LIQUID REGION ABSORPTION COMPLETE]
    Film -.-> PBMnote[PBM LOG MEAN PARTIAL PRESSURE OF B APPLIED HERE]
    PBMnote -.-> Film
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Notes

- **Node IDs** chosen as `A1, B1, C1, ..., L1` (alphanumeric, prefixed with letters, *never* using reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`).
- **Labels** use **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** (e.g., `STAGNANT B PRESENT` instead of `**Stagnant B**`) — this avoids Mermaid parser conflicts with markdown formatting tags.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Diffusion Cell

The classical *Stefan tube* (a vertical tube with liquid A evaporating at the bottom and a stream of gas B flowing across the top) is best represented as a functional flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LiquidPool[A RESERVOIR OF PURE LIQUID A AT BOTTOM] --> Evap[EVAPORATION SURFACE Y A EQUALS Y ASAT]
    Evap --> DiffZone[DIFFUSION ZONE OF LENGTH Z THROUGH STAGNANT B]
    DiffZone --> TopGas[STREAM OF PURE B AT TOP Y A EQUALS Y A INFINITY]
    DiffZone -.-> Meas[MEASURE N A AT STEADY STATE USE STEFAN EQUATION]
    Meas --> CalcN[CALCULATED FLUX N A EQUALS DAB C TOTAL OVER Z TIMES LN OF RATIO OF Y B VALUES]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define Fick's First Law of Diffusion. State its mathematical form and explain the significance of each term.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that *the molar diffusive flux of a component is proportional to the concentration gradient and occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration*. Mathematically, for component A in a binary mixture A–B:

$$J_A^{*} = -D_{AB}\,\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

- $J_A^{*}$ = molar diffusive flux of A (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ = binary diffusivity of A in B (m²/s)
- $C_A$ = molar concentration of A (kmol/m³)
- $z$ = direction of diffusion (m)
- The **negative sign** indicates flux is opposite to the positive concentration gradient.

---

### Q2. What is meant by equimolar counter-diffusion? Give one industrial example.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Equimolar counter-diffusion is a steady-state mass-transfer process in which two species (A and B) diffuse through a stagnant film in *equal and opposite molar directions*, such that $N_A = -N_B$. As a result, the total molar flux $N_{\text{total}} = N_A + N_B = 0$, eliminating any bulk-flow contribution.

**Industrial example:** Distillation of a binary mixture operating under the *constant molar overflow* assumption — the more volatile component A vaporises while the less volatile component B condenses, both at equal molar rates.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Module 1 Full-Marks Problem
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**(a) Derive the steady-state equation for the molar flux of A when A diffuses through a stagnant gas B (Stefan Diffusion). Clearly state the assumptions used.** **(7 marks)**

**Solution Outline:**

Assumptions:
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion along the $z$-axis.
2. Ideal-gas behaviour at constant $T$ and $P$.
3. Gas B is stagnant ($N_B = 0$), so $N_{\text{total}} = N_A$.
4. $D_{AB}$ is independent of concentration (valid for binary ideal gases at moderate pressures).

Starting from the general flux equation:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A N_{\text{total}}$$

With $N_{\text{total}} = N_A$:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A N_A$$

$$N_A(1 - y_A) = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Using $C_A = (P/RT)\,y_A$, i.e., $dC_A = (P/RT)\,dy_A$, and $1 - y_A = y_B$:

$$N_A\,y_B\,dz = D_{AB}\,\frac{P}{RT}\,dy_B$$

Integrating from $z = 0$ to $z = z$, and from $y_{B1}$ to $y_{B2}$:

$$N_A \cdot z = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{y_{B2}}{y_{B1}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}}$$

where $p_{BM} = (p_{B2} - p_{B1})/\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})$. **(7 marks — full derivation expected.)**

---

**(b) Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S, A) is absorbed from a H₂S–air mixture in a packed absorption tower operating at 1 atm and 30 °C. At a particular location, the bulk gas contains 15 mol% H₂S. The H₂S diffuses through a 0.8 mm thick stagnant air film to the water surface, where it is essentially completely absorbed. The diffusivity of H₂S in air at 30 °C is $D_{AB} = 1.69 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the steady-state flux of H₂S.** **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the case.** Since B (air) is stagnant and A (H₂S) is completely absorbed at $z_2$, this is **Stefan diffusion**.

**Step 2 — Partial pressures.**

$$p_{A1} = 0.15 \text{ atm},\quad p_{A2} = 0$$

$$p_{B1} = 0.85 \text{ atm},\quad p_{B2} = 1.0 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3 — $p_{BM}$:**

$$p_{BM} = \frac{1.0 - 0.85}{\ln(1.0/0.85)} = \frac{0.15}{0.16252} = 0.9229 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Stefan equation.**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z}\cdot\frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$

With $T = 303.15$ K:

$$N_A = \frac{(1.69 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8314)(303.15)(0.8 \times 10^{-3})}\cdot\frac{0.15}{0.9229}$$

Numerator of prefactor: $1.69 \times 10^{-5} \times 101325 = 1.7124$

Denominator: $8314 \times 303.15 \times 0.0008 = 2016.0$

Prefactor = $1.7124 / 2016.0 = 8.494 \times 10^{-4}$

Driving factor: $0.15 / 0.9229 = 0.1625$

$$\boxed{N_A = 8.494 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1625 = 1.380 \times 10^{-4}\;\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}}$$

---

### QUESTION B — Module 1 Full-Marks Problem (Alternative)
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**(a) Discuss the case of equimolar counter-diffusion. Derive the steady-state molar flux equation and identify two industrial examples where this assumption is valid.** **(7 marks)**

**Solution Outline:**

Equimolar counter-diffusion occurs when $N_A = -N_B$, giving $N_{\text{total}} = 0$. The general flux equation reduces to:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Substituting $C_A = (P/RT)\,y_A$ and integrating:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT\,z}\,(y_{A1} - y_{A2}) \quad\quad \text{(Eq. 1)}$$

This is independent of total pressure and is a *linear* function of the mole-fraction difference — a key reason why equimolar cases are simple to design.

**Two industrial examples:**

1. **Distillation columns** under the constant-molar-overflow assumption.
2. **Equimolar chemical reactions** such as the water-gas-shift reaction $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ where the products and reactants balance mole-for-mole.

**(7 marks)** — full marks require the derivation (4 marks) plus both examples (1.5 marks each).

---

**(b) A dilute solution of benzoic acid (A) in water (B) flows as a film over a solid surface. Benzoic acid diffuses from the bulk (where $C_{A1} = 0.025$ kmol/m³) through a 1.5 mm thick stagnant liquid film to the surface where $C_{A2} = 0.005$ kmol/m³. The total molar concentration of the solution is $C = 55.5$ kmol/m³. The diffusivity of benzoic acid in water is $\mathcal{D}_{AB} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Calculate the steady-state molar flux of benzoic acid assuming B is stagnant.** **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert concentrations to mole fractions.**

$$x_{A1} = \frac{C_{A1}}{C} = \frac{0.025}{55.5} = 4.5045 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$x_{A2} = \frac{C_{A2}}{C} = \frac{0.005}{55.5} = 9.009 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$x_{B1} = 1 - x_{A1} \approx 0.99955, \quad x_{B2} = 1 - x_{A2} \approx 0.99991$$

**Step 2 — $x_{BM}$:**

$$x_{BM} = \frac{x_{B2} - x_{B1}}{\ln(x_{B2}/x_{B1})} = \frac{0.99991 - 0.99955}{\ln(0.99991/0.99955)}$$

$$x_{BM} = \frac{0.00036}{\ln(1.00036)} \approx \frac{0.00036}{0.00036} \approx 1.000$$

(Because the solution is highly dilute, $x_{BM} \approx 1$.)

**Step 3 — Apply the stagnant-B liquid formula (L3).**

$$N_A = \frac{\mathcal{D}_{AB}\,C}{z}\cdot\frac{x_{A1} - x_{A2}}{x_{BM}}$$

$$N_A = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-9})(55.5)}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot \frac{4.5045 \times 10^{-4} - 9.009 \times 10^{-5}}{1.000}$$

$$N_A = 3.7 \times 10^{-5} \times (3.6036 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\boxed{N_A \approx 1.333 \times 10^{-8}\;\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $p_{BM}$ / $x_{BM}$ correction.** Many students apply $N_A = (D_{AB}P/RTz) \cdot \Delta p_A$ directly without the log-mean correction — this gives answers **off by 10–25%**, leading to **loss of 3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in $R$.** Use $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K) **only when $P$ is in Pa**. Mixing $P$ in atm with $R$ in SI units yields wrong answers.
> 3. **Confusing $J_A^{*}$ (diffusive flux) with $N_A$ (absolute flux).** KTU expects the **absolute flux** $N_A$ in all problems.
> 4. **Ignoring the dilution assumption.** In highly dilute liquid solutions, $x_{BM} \approx 1$, which simplifies the algebra — but you must *justify* this assumption by computing $x_{BM}$ explicitly to earn full marks.
> 5. **Sign convention errors.** Always quote $|N_A|$ as a positive magnitude unless the question explicitly asks for the signed flux.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision)**

- **Fick's First Law** is the foundation — $J_A^{*} = -D_{AB}\,dC_A/dz$.
- **Three canonical cases** of Module 1: *Equimolar counter-diffusion*, *Stefan Diffusion (A through stagnant B)*, and *Liquid-phase diffusion*.
- **Equimolar flux:** $N_A = (D_{AB}P/RTz)\,\Delta y_A$ — simple, no correction factor.
- **Stefan flux (gas):** $N_A = (D_{AB}P/RTz) \cdot (p_{A1} - p_{A2})/p_{BM}$, where $p_{BM} = (p_{B2} - p_{B1})/\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})$.
- **Stefan flux (liquid):** $N_A = (\mathcal{D}_{AB}C/z) \cdot (x_{A1} - x_{A2})/x_{BM}$, where $x_{BM} = (x_{B2} - x_{B1})/\ln(x_{B2}/x_{B1})$.
- **Log-mean correction** is a *bulk-flow correction* that arises because the diffusing species must physically displace the stagnant species.
- **Total molar concentration** $C$ for liquids = (density)/(mean molar mass) — for dilute aqueous solutions, $C \approx 55.5$ kmol/m³.
- **Units check:** Always confirm $D_{AB}$ in m²/s, $P$ in Pa, $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K), $T$ in K, $z$ in m → final flux in kmol/(m²·s).
- **Schmidt number** $Sc = \mu/(\rho D_{AB})$ — useful for analogy with heat transfer ($Pr$) in dimensionless correlations.
- **Knudsen diffusion** dominates when pore diameter $d_p$ is comparable to mean free path — relevant for *porous catalyst pellets*.
- **Steady-state assumption** $dN_A/dz = 0$ implies $N_A = $ constant along the diffusion path — this is what allows integration.
- **Identify the case first**, then write the equation, then plug in numbers — this sequence prevents **>80% of the silly errors** KTU examiners penalise.

> **Key Takeaway:** Every molecular diffusion problem is a *boundary-value problem*. Identify (1) the phase, (2) whether B is stagnant, (3) whether bulk flow is significant. From these three binary decisions, the governing equation is uniquely determined. Memorise the formula sheet in §2.2 and the decision matrix in §3.6 — and you will comfortably score full marks on every Part B problem in Module 1.
