# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q1. Define a wetted-wall column. Mention two advantages of using it for mass transfer studies.**

**Model Answer:**
A wetted-wall column is a vertical cylindrical contactor in which a liquid is distributed at the top to form a thin film flowing down the inner wall under gravity, while a gas stream passes through the core, contacting the liquid across a precisely known interfacial area $a = \pi d_i Z$.

Advantages:
1. **Geometrically known interfacial area** — enables direct calculation of mass transfer coefficients without empirical correlation of $a$.
2. **Low liquid hold-up** — safe and economical for studying toxic or expensive solutes such as $\text{SO}_2$, $\text{Cl}_2$, or $\text{H}_2\text{S}$.
3. *(Bonus)* Easy to reach steady state; provides reliable data for design correlations in industrial absorbers.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q2. What is the significance of $H_{OG}$ in the design of a wetted-wall column?**

**Model Answer:**
$H_{OG}$ stands for **Height of an Overall Gas-Phase Transfer Unit**, defined as $H_{OG} = G / (K_y a)$. It represents the height of column required to accomplish one transfer unit under the prevailing driving force. A smaller $H_{OG}$ indicates a more efficient contactor. The total column height is given by $Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}$, where $N_{OG}$ is the number of transfer units. For a wetted-wall column, $H_{OG}$ directly incorporates the geometric interfacial area $a$, making it a useful parameter for scaling laboratory data to industrial absorbers.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **wetted-wall column** for the determination of gas-side mass transfer coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wetted-wall column of 30 mm inner diameter and 1.0 m test length is used to absorb **acetone vapour from air into water**. The gas contains 4 mol% acetone at a solute-free flow rate of $1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ kmol/s; the outlet gas contains 0.6 mol% acetone. Pure water flows at $6.0 \times 10^{-4}$ kmol/s. The equilibrium relation is $Y^* = 1.20\,X$. Calculate: **(i)** $N_{OG}$, **(ii)** $H_{OG}$, and **(iii)** the overall gas-side coefficient $K_y$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

1. **Construction:** A wetted-wall column consists of (i) a vertical glass or metal tube of known inner diameter $d_i$ and test length $Z$, (ii) a liquid distributor at the top (overflow weir) that spreads the liquid as a uniform thin film on the inside wall, (iii) gas inlet and outlet ports for co-current or counter-current flow, and (iv) a liquid collection sump at the bottom. A differential manometer or rotameter measures the gas flow rate.
2. **Working:** A metered liquid stream enters the top distributor and forms a **laminar falling film** on the inner wall under gravity. The gas enters either at the bottom (counter-current) or top (co-current) and contacts the film. Solute transfers from gas to liquid (absorption) or vice versa (desorption/stripping). Since the interfacial area is the geometric wall area $a = \pi d_i Z$, measuring the inlet and outlet compositions of both phases directly gives the molar flux $N_A = G(Y_1 - Y_2) / a$, which is then related to $k_y$ via $N_A = k_y (Y - Y^*)_{mean}$.
3. **Typical use:** Determination of $k_y$ for **gas-phase-controlled** systems such as absorption of $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{SO}_2$, or water vapour.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert mole fractions to mole ratios.**

$$
Y_1 = \frac{0.04}{0.96} = 0.04167 \quad ; \quad Y_2 = \frac{0.006}{0.994} = 0.00604
$$

**Step 2 — Overall material balance to find $X_1$ (with $X_2 = 0$).**

$$
X_1 = \frac{G(Y_1 - Y_2)}{L} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.04167 - 0.00604)}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
X_1 = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.03563}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.126 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium values at the two ends.**

$$
Y_1^* = 1.20 \times 7.126 \times 10^{-3} = 8.551 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
Y_2^* = 1.20 \times 0 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Driving forces at the two ends.**

$$
(Y_1 - Y_1^*) = 0.04167 - 0.00855 = 0.03312
$$

$$
(Y_2 - Y_2^*) = 0.00604 - 0 = 0.00604
$$

**Step 5 — Log-mean driving force.**

$$
(Y - Y^*)_{lm} = \frac{0.03312 - 0.00604}{\ln(0.03312 / 0.00604)} = \frac{0.02708}{\ln(5.484)} = \frac{0.02708}{1.7024} = 0.01591
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $N_{OG}$ (Colburn equation).**

$$
N_{OG} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{(Y - Y^*)_{lm}} = \frac{0.03563}{0.01591} = 2.240
$$

**Step 7 — Compute interfacial area and $H_{OG}$.**

$$
a = \pi \times 0.030 \times 1.0 = 0.09425 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
H_{OG} = \frac{Z}{N_{OG}} = \frac{1.0}{2.240} = 0.4464 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute $K_y$.**

$$
K_y = \frac{G}{H_{OG} \cdot a} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{0.4464 \times 0.09425} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{0.04208}
$$

$$
K_y = 2.852 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- $N_{OG} = 2.24$
- $H_{OG} = 0.446$ m
- $K_y = 2.85 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/(m²·s)

---

### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **height of a transfer unit ($H_{OG}$)** and the **number of transfer units ($N_{OG}$)** for a wetted-wall column, starting from a differential mass balance. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **two-film theory** of mass transfer. How are the individual and overall mass transfer coefficients related? Discuss the criteria under which a wetted-wall column is preferred for measuring $k_y$ over $k_x$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:* (i) steady state, (ii) isothermal, (iii) plug flow of gas, (iv) constant molar flow rates (dilute system), (v) equilibrium at interface $y_i = m x_i$, (vi) negligible liquid-side resistance (gas-film-controlled), (vii) straight operating and equilibrium lines.

*Differential material balance over a slice $dZ$:*

$$
G \, dY = N_A \, a \, dZ
$$

*Substitute $N_A = K_y (Y - Y^*)$ (overall gas-side):*

$$
G \, dY = K_y (Y - Y^*) a \, dZ
$$

*Integrate from $Z=0$ to $Z$ and $Y_1$ to $Y_2$ (note $Y_1 > Y_2$ for absorption, so flip limits):*

$$
Z = \int_0^Z dZ = \frac{G}{K_y a} \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*}
$$

*Identify HTU and NTU:*

$$
Z = \underbrace{\frac{G}{K_y a}}_{H_{OG}} \cdot \underbrace{\int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*}}_{N_{OG}}
$$

*For linear equilibrium and operating lines (Colburn):*

$$
N_{OG} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{(Y - Y^*)_{lm}}
$$

*Final compact form:*

$$
Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}
$$

**Part (b) — Two-Film Theory and Preference for $k_y$ (7 marks):**

The **Whitman two-film theory (1923)** postulates that whenever two phases contact, a **stagnant film** exists on each side of the interface. Mass transfer is by steady molecular diffusion through these films, with **equilibrium at the interface** and **bulk turbulence** keeping the bulk phases uniform.

The flux across each film is:

$$
N_A = k_y(y - y_i) = k_x(x_i - x)
$$

Combining using $y_i = m x_i$ gives the **overall relation**:

$$
N_A = K_y(y - y^*) = K_x(x^* - x)
$$

with the resistance-in-series form:

$$
\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} \quad ; \quad \frac{1}{K_x} = \frac{1}{k_x} + \frac{1}{m k_y}
$$

**Why wetted-wall column is preferred for $k_y$:**
1. **Short liquid contact time** (residence time of falling film $\approx 1$–$5$ s) — the liquid film does not have time to develop a full concentration gradient, so liquid-side resistance is small but not fully negligible.
2. The **interfacial area is precisely known** (cylindrical geometry) — no uncertainty in $a$ when back-calculating $k_y$.
3. **Highly gas-phase-controlled** systems (large $m$, e.g., absorption of sparingly soluble gases such as $\text{O}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$, or $\text{H}_2$ in water) show $K_y \approx k_y$ because the term $m/k_x$ becomes negligible.
4. For liquid-phase-controlled systems (e.g., $\text{NH}_3$ in water to a limited extent), wetted-wall data would over-estimate $k_x$ due to incomplete liquid-film development; a **laminar jet absorber** is preferred for $k_x$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students use $G$ in kg/s instead of kmol/s when applying $G\, dY = N_A a\, dZ$. Always convert gas flow rate to **molar (solute-free) basis in kmol/s** before the derivation.
> 2. **Mole fraction vs mole ratio confusion:** In dilute systems, the substitutions $y \approx Y$ and $x \approx X$ are valid **only when $Y, X \ll 1$**. For acetone (4 mol% inlet), $Y = 0.042$ — dilute enough. But for $Y > 0.1$, the full mole ratio must be used in $(1-Y)$ corrections.
> 3. **Forgetting the $m$ in $1/K_y$:** A common mistake is writing $1/K_y = 1/k_y + 1/k_x$ — this is dimensionally wrong because $k_y$ is in gas-phase units and $k_x$ is in liquid-phase units. The slope $m$ is required to convert between them.
> 4. **Log-mean driving force signs:** When $Y_2^* = 0$ (pure solvent inlet), some students take $\ln(Y_1 - Y_1^*)$ as the denominator — the correct log-mean is between $(Y_1 - Y_1^*)$ and $(Y_2 - Y_2^*)$, not individual log values.
> 5. **Interfacial area:** A wetted-wall column has $a = \pi d_i Z$, not $\pi d_i^2 Z / 4$ (which would be volume). Conflating area with volume costs **2–3 marks** per KTU paper.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Wetted-wall column = vertical tube with thin falling liquid film + counter/co-current gas; interfacial area is geometric: $a = \pi d_i Z$.
- **Use:** Primarily for experimental determination of **gas-side mass transfer coefficient $k_y$** in gas-phase-controlled absorption.
- **Working equation:** $G\, dY = N_A\, a\, dZ$ — a **differential molar balance on the gas phase**.
- **HTU–NTU form:** $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ where $H_{OG} = G/(K_y a)$ and $N_{OG} = \int dY/(Y - Y^*)$.
- **Colburn equation** (linear operating and equilibrium lines): $N_{OG} = (Y_1 - Y_2)/(\Delta Y)_{lm}$ with $(\Delta Y)_{lm}$ as the log-mean driving force.
- **Resistances in series:** $1/K_y = 1/k_y + m/k_x$ — always include the slope $m$.
- **Assumptions checklist (write these in every KTU answer):** steady state, isothermal, plug flow, dilute system, constant $G$ and $L$, no interfacial resistance.
- **Limitation:** Not ideal for highly soluble gases where liquid-side resistance dominates; use a **laminar jet** or **packed column** instead.
- **Typical KTU gases studied:** $\text{NH}_3$–air–water, $\text{SO}_2$–air–water, $\text{CO}_2$–air–water, acetone–air–water.
- **Numerical recipe (memorize this flow):**
  1. Convert inlet/outlet $y$ to $Y$.
  2. Material balance to get $X_1$ (with $X_2 = 0$ for pure solvent).
  3. Find $Y_1^* = mX_1$ and $Y_2^* = mX_2$.
  4. Compute $\Delta Y_1$ and $\Delta Y_2$.
  5. Compute $(\Delta Y)_{lm}$.
  6. $N_{OG} = \Delta Y_{overall} / (\Delta Y)_{lm}$.
  7. $H_{OG} = Z / N_{OG}$.
  8. $K_y = G / (H_{OG} a)$.
- **Most-asked KTU question pattern:** "Calculate $N_{OG}$, $H_{OG}$ and $K_y$ for the absorption of [gas] from air into [liquid] in a wetted-wall column of [dimensions]." — Practice at least three numericals with different equilibrium slopes.
- **Mnemonic for the driving force direction:** "**Y goes down, X goes up**" — gas loses solute (absorption), liquid gains solute.
- **Geometric area mantra:** Wetted-wall = "**wall is the area**" — never confuse with cross-sectional area $\pi d_i^2/4$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Venturi Scrubbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Venturi Scrubbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Venturi Scrubber** is a high-energy wet gas-cleaning device that uses the **Venturi effect** to atomize a scrubbing liquid into fine droplets within a converging–throat–diverging geometry. The intense turbulence and high relative velocity between the dust-laden gas and the liquid droplets promote collection of particulate matter (and certain soluble gases) primarily by **inertial impaction and diffusion**. It is classified as a high-efficiency, once-through, wet-collection piece of equipment and is the most widely used industrial wet scrubber for fine particulates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Wet Scrubbing):** Venturi scrubbers are a *high-pressure-drop, high-efficiency* class of scrubbers. The governing design parameter is the **throat velocity (typically 60–180 m/s)**, and the controlling equation in design practice is the **cut-diameter (d₅₀) correlation** originally proposed by Johnstone and modified by Calvert.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are standing on a footbridge over a fast-flowing river, hosing the water below. As you squeeze the nozzle, the water stream accelerates and *pulls in* surrounding air — a Venturi creates the same effect on a much larger scale. The gas stream squeezes through a narrow throat, accelerates violently, and tears the injected liquid into a fine mist. Imagine throwing dust into that mist: the dust particles are too "heavy" to follow the air streamlines around the droplets, so they slam into the droplets and get trapped. The "squeezed" throat is where the cleaning magic happens — and the narrowness controls the cleaning power.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- Liquid (water) density, **ρ_L ≈ 1000 kg/m³**
- Gas density, **ρ_G ≈ 1.2 kg/m³** (air at STP)
- Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C, **η_L ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- Throat velocity, **v_G ≈ 60–180 m/s**
- Typical Liquid-to-Gas ratio, **L/G ≈ 0.4–1.3 L/m³**
- Operating pressure drop, **ΔP ≈ 1.25–25 kPa (5–100 in. of water column)**

> [!NOTE]
> A Venturi scrubber is the **"workhorse" of wet scrubbing** — it handles fine particles (< 1 µm), corrosive gases, and high-temperature streams where fabric filters would fail.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle – Structured Logic Flow

The collection process in a Venturi scrubber proceeds through the following sequential steps:

1. **Pre-conditioned Gas Entry** – Flue/process gas (carrying particulates) enters the **converging section**, where its cross-sectional area reduces and its velocity rises (continuity: A·v = constant).
2. **Liquid Injection** – Scrubbing liquid (water, alkaline solution, or slurry) is injected at or just upstream of the **throat** either by pressure nozzles or by the suction effect of the gas itself.
3. **Throat Atomization (the Heart of the Scrubber)** – At throat velocities of 60–180 m/s, the high-velocity gas **shears the liquid into fine droplets** (10–100 µm). The pressure drop that drives the gas also creates this mist.
4. **Collection by Inertial Impaction** – Particle-laden gas cannot follow the curved streamlines around the droplets. Particles with sufficient inertia impact the droplets and are captured.
5. **Diverging Section – Kinetic Energy Recovery** – The gas decelerates in the expanding section, the droplets coalesce, and the mist–dust agglomerates fall out of the gas stream.
6. **Droplet–Gas Separation** – A downstream **cyclone separator, mist eliminator, or impingement tray** separates the slurry from the cleaned gas, which exits the stack.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Why a converging section?** To convert pressure into kinetic energy (Bernoulli's principle), preparing the gas for droplet atomization.
- **Why atomize the liquid?** A larger liquid surface area (millions of tiny droplets) provides many more impaction targets for dust particles.
- **Why a diverging section?** Recovers some static pressure (subsonic diffuser action) and allows droplet coalescence.
- **Why a separate mist eliminator?** Droplets carrying dust must be removed before the gas exits, otherwise the *scrubber becomes a creator* of aerosol pollution.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **exam-critical formula bank**. Memorize the cut-diameter and pressure-drop equations — they account for ~70% of any numerical question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Throat continuity | $v_{throat} = \dfrac{Q_G}{A_{throat}}$ | m/s; $Q_G$ = gas volumetric flow (m³/s) |
| 2 | Throat gas density | $\rho_G = \dfrac{P\,M}{R\,T}$ | kg/m³; ideal gas law |
| 3 | Total pressure drop (Hesketh) | $\Delta P = \Delta P_G + \Delta P_L$ | Pa |
| 4 | Gas pressure drop | $\Delta P_G = \rho_G \, v_{throat}^{2} / 2$ | Pa (velocity head) |
| 5 | Liquid pressure drop | $\Delta P_L = \rho_L \, v_{throat}^{2} \left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right)$ | Pa; $L/G$ in m³/m³ |
| 6 | **Cut diameter, d₅₀ (Calvert)** | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9\,\eta_L \, d_d}{\rho_p \, v_{throat} \, C_c}}$ | m |
| 7 | Empirical d₅₀ (Calvert) | $d_{50} = 9.6 \left(\dfrac{\eta_L \, d_d}{\rho_p \, v_{throat}^{2} \, C_c}\right)^{0.5}$ | µm, when $v_{throat}$ in cm/s and η in poise |
| 8 | Slip correction (Cunningham) | $C_c = 1 + \dfrac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left(A_1 + A_2\,e^{-A_3\,d_p/2\lambda}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Single-droplet efficiency (impaction) | $\eta_{imp} = \left(\dfrac{S_t}{S_t + 0.7}\right)^{1.5}$ | $S_t$ = Stokes number |
| 10 | Stokes number | $S_t = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^{2} \, v_{rel}}{9\,\eta_L \, d_d}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Overall efficiency (Calvert) | $\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-K\,d_p^{2}\right)$ | K is a function of scrubber geometry |
| 12 | Penetration (clean gas leakage) | $Pt = 1 - \eta$ | fraction of particles escaping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in KTU problems:** $d_d$ is the **droplet diameter** (m), $d_p$ is the **particle diameter** (m), $\eta_L$ is the **dynamic viscosity of the scrubbing liquid** (Pa·s), $\rho_p$ is the **particle density** (kg/m³), and $v_{throat}$ is the **gas velocity in the throat** (m/s).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plants:** Removal of fly ash and SO₂ (using limestone slurry) from coal-combustion flue gas.
- **Steel industry:** Control of iron-oxide fume, tar, and acid mist from coke ovens and pickling lines.
- **Chemical industry:** Mist elimination in sulfuric acid plants, fertilizer granulators.
- **Incinerators:** Handling of sticky, hygroscopic, or combustible dusts that would blind a fabric filter.
- **Acid-gas absorption:** HCl, HF, NH₃, and SO₂ scrubbing with appropriate chemistry.

> [!TIP]
> Venturi scrubbers are the **only practical control device for sticky, high-temperature, or chemically reactive aerosols** — fabric filters plug, electrostatic precipitators are temperature-limited, cyclones are too inefficient for fines. This is why KTU emphasizes them.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cut Diameter (d₅₀) Correlation

The cut diameter $d_{50}$ is the *particle diameter for which the collection efficiency of the scrubber is exactly 50%*. It is the single most important design parameter of a Venturi scrubber.

**Starting physical picture:** A particle moving with gas at velocity $v_{throat}$ in the throat encounters a stationary (or slow-moving) liquid droplet of diameter $d_d$. The particle is captured if its inertia carries it across the gas streamline into the droplet. The *dimensionless number* governing this is the **Stokes number**.

#### Step 1 — Define the Stokes Number

The Stokes number is the ratio of the particle's *stop distance* to the droplet radius:

$$S_t = \frac{\rho_p \, d_p^{2} \, v_{rel}}{9 \, \eta_L \, d_d}$$

where $v_{rel}$ is the relative velocity between the particle and the droplet (≈ gas velocity for small droplets in high-velocity gas).

#### Step 2 — Use an Inertial-Impaction Correlation

Empirically (Langmuir–Blodgett form), the single-droplet collection efficiency is:

$$\eta_{imp} = \left(\frac{S_t}{S_t + 0.7}\right)^{1.5}$$

The factor **0.7** reflects the *finite range* of streamlines that can be intercepted by the droplet (it is not exactly 0.5 as the simple Stokes model predicts).

#### Step 3 — Apply Slip Correction for Fine Particles

For sub-micron particles, the air is no longer a continuous fluid — the Cunningham slip correction $C_c$ must be added to account for the non-continuum regime. Replacing $9\,\eta_L$ with $9\,\eta_L / C_c$ in the Stokes number:

$$S_t = \frac{\rho_p \, d_p^{2} \, v_{throat} \, C_c}{9 \, \eta_L \, d_d}$$

#### Step 4 — Set the Condition for d₅₀

At the cut diameter, the overall penetration $Pt = 0.5$, which corresponds to the **single-droplet efficiency being 0.5**. From the impaction correlation, $\eta_{imp} = 0.5$ when $S_t \approx 0.7$ (working backwards through the Langmuir–Blodgett form for the *threshold* impaction). This is the *critical* Stokes number, denoted $S_{t,crit}$.

Setting $S_{t,crit} = 0.7$ in the corrected Stokes number:

$$\frac{\rho_p \, d_{50}^{2} \, v_{throat} \, C_c}{9 \, \eta_L \, d_d} = 0.7$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for d₅₀

Rearranging for $d_{50}^{2}$:

$$d_{50}^{2} = \frac{0.7 \times 9 \, \eta_L \, d_d}{\rho_p \, v_{throat} \, C_c} = \frac{6.3 \, \eta_L \, d_d}{\rho_p \, v_{throat} \, C_c}$$

This is sometimes written (using the rounded 9 constant) as:

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9\,\eta_L \, d_d}{\rho_p \, v_{throat} \, C_c}}$$

which is the **Johnstone form** of the cut-diameter equation.

#### Step 6 — Convert to Calvert's Empirical Form (µm / cm·s)

If we express $\eta_L$ in **poise** (g/cm·s), $d_d$ in cm, $\rho_p$ in g/cm³, and $v_{throat}$ in cm/s, dimensional analysis yields Calvert's widely used practical equation:

$$d_{50} = 9.6 \left(\frac{\eta_L \, d_d}{\rho_p \, v_{throat}^{2} \, C_c}\right)^{0.5} \quad [\mu m]$$

The factor **9.6** lumps the conversion constants and the empirical tuning of the 0.7 critical Stokes number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important consequence:** $d_{50} \propto v_{throat}^{-1}$ (in the Johnstone form) or $v_{throat}^{-1}$ inside the square root (in the Calvert form). **Increasing throat velocity dramatically reduces the cut diameter** — meaning the scrubber captures smaller particles. This is why higher ΔP ⇒ higher efficiency.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Pressure-Drop Equation (Hesketh / Calvert)

The total pressure drop in a Venturi scrubber has two components: the kinetic-energy head of the gas and the additional drop due to the liquid being accelerated and atomized.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli across the throat**

For a gas flowing through the converging section from area $A_1$ to throat area $A_{th}$, the pressure drop from the gas alone is the dynamic head:

$$\Delta P_G = \frac{1}{2} \rho_G \, v_{throat}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Add the Liquid Drag Term**

The liquid droplets are accelerated by the gas; this momentum transfer creates an additional pressure drop. The classical empirical relation (Calvert) is:

$$\Delta P_L = \rho_L \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) v_{throat}^{2}$$

where $L/G$ is in m³ liquid per m³ gas.

**Step 3 — Sum the Two Contributions**

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{total} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_G \, v_{throat}^{2} + \rho_L \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) v_{throat}^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of the Design Variable**

Because the gas term is typically small (since $\rho_G \ll \rho_L$ and the ½ factor), for engineering design the equation is often written as:

$$\Delta P_{total} \approx \rho_L \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) v_{throat}^{2} \quad \text{(dominant term)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** In numerical problems, examiners often specify "neglect gas pressure drop" or "gas contributes only 10%". Always read the wording carefully and present the **general form first**, then **apply the simplification** explicitly.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (Pedagogical Walk-Through)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A venturi scrubber handles 10 m³/s of flue gas at 200 °C and 1 atm. The throat area is 0.1 m². The L/G ratio is 1.0 L/m³. Calculate: (a) the throat velocity, (b) the throat gas density, and (c) the total pressure drop.

**Given:**
- $Q_G = 10$ m³/s
- $T = 200 + 273 = 473$ K
- $P = 1.013 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
- $A_{throat} = 0.1$ m²
- $L/G = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/m³
- M (air) ≈ 29 g/mol = 0.029 kg/mol

**Step (a) — Throat velocity**

$$v_{throat} = \frac{Q_G}{A_{throat}} = \frac{10}{0.1} = 100 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (b) — Throat gas density**

$$\rho_G = \frac{P M}{R T} = \frac{(1.013 \times 10^{5})(0.029)}{(8.314)(473)} = 0.747 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step (c) — Total pressure drop**

$$\Delta P_G = \frac{1}{2}(0.747)(100)^2 = 3735 \text{ Pa} \approx 3.74 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\Delta P_L = (1000)(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(100)^2 = 10{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 10.0 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\Delta P_{total} = 3.74 + 10.0 = 13.74 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The liquid contribution (~73%) dominates the total ΔP. This is the typical engineering reality in industrial venturi scrubbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block-Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** of a Venturi scrubber, mapping the gas–liquid flow path, atomization zone, collection zone, and separation hardware.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[POLLUTED GAS INLET] --> B[CONVERGING SECTION ACCELERATION]
    B --> C[VENTURI THROAT HIGH VELOCITY 60 TO 180 M PER S]
    I[SCRUBBING LIQUID TANK] --> J[LIQUID PUMP]
    J --> K[ATOMIZATION NOZZLE]
    K --> C
    C --> D[IMPACTION AND DIFFUSION COLLECTION ZONE]
    D --> E[DIVERGING SECTION DECELERATION AND DROPLET COALESCENCE]
    E --> F[INERTIAL MIST ELIMINATOR OR CYCLONE SEPARATOR]
    F --> G[CLEAN GAS OUTLET TO STACK]
    F --> H[CONTAMINATED SLURRY SUMP]
    H --> L[SLUDGE TREATMENT AND WATER RECYCLE UNIT]
    L --> I
```

### 4.2 Annotated Operational Flow Description

| Block Label | Engineering Function | Dominant Physics |
|-------------|----------------------|------------------|
| POLLUTED GAS INLET | Flue/process gas entry | Continuous-phase inlet |
| CONVERGING SECTION | Area contraction, gas acceleration | Bernoulli + continuity |
| VENTURI THROAT | Atomization, high-shear droplet generation | Turbulent shear, surface tension |
| ATOMIZATION NOZZLE | Liquid injection and primary break-up | Pressure-atomization, two-phase flow |
| COLLECTION ZONE | Particle–droplet interaction | Inertial impaction, Brownian diffusion, interception |
| DIVERGING SECTION | Velocity recovery, droplet growth | Subsonic diffuser, coalescence |
| MIST ELIMINATOR | Final droplet removal | Inertial separation, gravity |
| SLURRY SUMP | Waste liquid collection | Gravity settling, neutralization |
| RECYCLE UNIT | Make-up water, reagent addition | Process integration |

### 4.3 Construction Variants (Schematic Description)

There are three principal Venturi scrubber geometries that KTU 2024 students should be able to recognize:

1. **Round-Venturi (Cylindrical Throat)** – Most common; throat is a circular cross-section; gas enters axially.
2. **Rectangular / Slot-Venturi** – Used for very large gas flows; throat is a long narrow slot; multiple liquid lances inject along the slot length.
3. **Annular-Orifice / Packed Throat** – Throat is filled with packing or has an adjustable annular gap; this **adjustable-throat** design lets the operator trade ΔP for efficiency on-line (a KTU-favorite concept question).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why adjustable throat?** In real plants, the inlet dust loading varies with process conditions. By closing/opening the throat, the operator keeps the **throat velocity (and hence d₅₀) constant** as gas flow varies, ensuring design efficiency is maintained.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

> Define a *Venturi scrubber*. State the function of its three main sections.

**Model Answer:**

A Venturi scrubber is a wet gas-cleaning device consisting of three sections: (i) a **converging section** that accelerates the gas, (ii) a **throat** where liquid is atomized and particle–droplet interaction occurs, and (iii) a **diverging section** that recovers pressure and allows droplet coalescence. The function is to remove particulate matter and/or soluble gases from a gas stream by inertial impaction onto a high-density field of fine liquid droplets, using the kinetic energy of the gas itself as the atomization power source.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three sections with their functions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

> What is meant by *cut diameter (d₅₀)* of a Venturi scrubber? How is it affected by throat velocity?

**Model Answer:**

The **cut diameter (d₅₀)** is the aerodynamic diameter of a particle that is collected with **50 % efficiency** by the scrubber; i.e., the particle size at which half of the incoming particles penetrate through the scrubber uncollected. It is the standard design metric for particulate scrubbers.

From the Calvert / Johnstone equation, $d_{50} \propto v_{throat}^{-1}$ (inside the square root in Calvert's form). Hence, **increasing throat velocity reduces the cut diameter** — meaning the scrubber captures smaller particles with higher efficiency, but at the cost of higher pressure drop (which scales as $v_{throat}^{2}$).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the velocity relationship with the correct proportionality.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Convention (2024 Scheme):** Answer **ONE** full question. Each full question carries 14 marks, split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style, Module 2]**

**Q.A (a)** Derive the cut-diameter ($d_{50}$) equation for a Venturi scrubber starting from the Stokes number, clearly stating the assumptions involved. State how the cut diameter varies with throat velocity and throat gas density. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** A venturi scrubber is to treat 8 m³/s of flue gas (at 150 °C, 1 atm) containing dust of density 2200 kg/m³. The throat area is 0.08 m² and the throat velocity is to be 120 m/s. The mean droplet diameter in the throat is 80 µm and the Cunningham slip correction is 1.2. The dynamic viscosity of water at operating temperature is $0.9 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate the **cut diameter** of the scrubber. State whether particles of $2.0\,\mu m$ diameter will be collected with high efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution – Q.A(a):**

1. The particle is collected when, moving on a gas streamline, its inertia is large enough to cross the streamline and impact a droplet of diameter $d_d$.
2. The dimensionless parameter controlling this is the **Stokes number**: $S_t = \rho_p d_p^{2} v_{rel} \, / \, (9 \eta_L d_d)$.
3. For sub-micron particles, apply the **Cunningham slip correction** $C_c$ to correct for non-continuum drag: $S_t = \rho_p d_p^{2} v_{throat} C_c / (9 \eta_L d_d)$.
4. The single-droplet impaction efficiency is empirically: $\eta_{imp} = [S_t / (S_t + 0.7)]^{1.5}$.
5. The *cut diameter* is defined as the particle size at which $\eta = 50\%$, corresponding to the *critical Stokes number* $S_{t,crit} = 0.7$.
6. Setting $S_t = 0.7$ and rearranging:

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \, \eta_L \, d_d}{\rho_p \, v_{throat} \, C_c}}$$

7. **Assumptions:** spherical particles; droplets remain spherical; $v_{rel} \approx v_{throat}$; uniform droplet size; diffusion neglected; impaction is the sole collection mechanism.
8. **Variation:** $d_{50} \propto 1/v_{throat}$ — increasing throat velocity reduces $d_{50}$ (improved fine-particle capture). $d_{50}$ is independent of gas density (the gas appears only through $v_{throat}$, not $\rho_G$).

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation, 2 for assumptions, 1 for variations.)*

---

**Model Solution – Q.A(b):**

**Given data:**
- $\eta_L = 0.9 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $d_d = 80 \,\mu m = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\rho_p = 2200$ kg/m³
- $v_{throat} = 120$ m/s
- $C_c = 1.2$

**Step 1 — Substitute into Johnstone's cut-diameter equation:**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times 0.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}{2200 \times 120 \times 1.2}}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$9 \times 0.9 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} = 6.48 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$2200 \times 120 \times 1.2 = 3.168 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio inside the square root:**

$$\frac{6.48 \times 10^{-7}}{3.168 \times 10^{5}} = 2.046 \times 10^{-12}$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root:**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{2.046 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.43 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1.43 \,\mu m$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

The given particle diameter is $d_p = 2.0 \,\mu m$, which is **larger** than the cut diameter $1.43 \,\mu m$. Because the collection efficiency rises very steeply with $d_p$ (quadratic in $d_p$ for fine particles), a 2.0 µm particle will be collected with **high efficiency (> 90 %)**.

*(7 marks: 3 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 2 for interpretation and decision.)*

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style, Module 2]**

**Q.B (a)** With the help of a labelled schematic, explain the **construction and working of a Venturi scrubber**. Discuss the role of the throat in the collection process. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** A venturi scrubber operates with a throat velocity of 90 m/s and a liquid-to-gas ratio of 1.2 L/m³. The gas density is 1.1 kg/m³ and the liquid (water) density is 1000 kg/m³. Compute: (i) the gas-component pressure drop, (ii) the liquid-component pressure drop, and (iii) the total pressure drop. Comment on the relative magnitudes. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution – Q.B(a):**

**Construction (Labelled Schematic — described in text):**

A Venturi scrubber consists of three main parts aligned along the gas flow direction:

1. **Converging (Entry) Section** – A truncated cone, often with a 25°–45° included half-angle, where the gas enters from a duct and is accelerated.
2. **Throat** – A short, parallel-sided cylindrical (or rectangular) section, typically 0.3–1.5 m long, where the maximum velocity occurs. Liquid is injected here.
3. **Diverging (Exit) Section** – A reverse truncated cone with a 4°–7° half-angle (kept small to avoid flow separation), where the gas decelerates.

**Working:**

- Dust-laden gas enters the converging section and accelerates.
- Scrubbing liquid is injected into the throat either through spray nozzles (pressure-atomization) or by the suction effect of the high-velocity gas (self-priming).
- In the throat, the gas shears the liquid into a dense cloud of fine droplets (10–100 µm).
- Particles carried in the gas impact the droplets by inertia; soluble gases also undergo mass transfer (absorption) into the droplet.
- The agglomerated droplets (droplet + captured dust) are carried into the diverging section where they coalesce.
- A downstream mist eliminator (chevron, cyclonic, or wire-mesh) separates the slurry.
- Clean gas exits to the stack; slurry drains to treatment.

**Role of the Throat:**

The throat is the **collection heart** of the scrubber. Three functions are performed here:
1. **Atomization** – Shear at the gas–liquid interface breaks the liquid into droplets.
2. **High relative velocity** – The large slip between the very fast gas and the much slower droplets gives particles enough inertia to impact.
3. **Residence time** – A short but well-defined gas–droplet contact time (~10–50 ms) determines collection probability.

*(7 marks: 3 for schematic description, 2 for working explanation, 2 for throat role.)*

---

**Model Solution – Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $v_{throat} = 90$ m/s
- $L/G = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/m³
- $\rho_G = 1.1$ kg/m³
- $\rho_L = 1000$ kg/m³

**Step (i) — Gas-component pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_G = \frac{1}{2} \rho_G \, v_{throat}^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(1.1)(90)^{2}$$

$$\Delta P_G = \frac{1}{2}(1.1)(8100) = 4455 \text{ Pa} \approx 4.46 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step (ii) — Liquid-component pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_L = \rho_L \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) v_{throat}^{2} = (1000)(1.2 \times 10^{-3})(90)^{2}$$

$$\Delta P_L = (1000)(1.2 \times 10^{-3})(8100) = 9720 \text{ Pa} = 9.72 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step (iii) — Total pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_G + \Delta P_L = 4.46 + 9.72 = 14.18 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step (iv) — Comment on relative magnitudes:**

The liquid component (9.72 kPa) is **roughly 2.2 × the gas component** (4.46 kPa) and accounts for **~68.5 %** of the total pressure drop. This is consistent with the engineering reality that the kinetic-energy term in the gas is small ($\rho_G \ll \rho_L$), and the dominant energy sink in a Venturi scrubber is the **momentum transfer to the dispersed liquid phase**. Designers therefore often ignore the gas term in preliminary sizing without significant error.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for the comment.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Venturi-Scrubber Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in the Calvert formula.** The *empirical* Calvert equation requires **$v_{throat}$ in cm/s** and **$\eta_L$ in poise** (1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s) to yield $d_{50}$ in **µm**. Using SI units in the empirical equation is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the Cunningham slip correction** for $d_p < 1\,\mu m$. Drop the term, and you will overestimate $d_{50}$ by 20–50 %.
> 3. **Using $Q_G$ in place of $v_{throat}$ in the d₅₀ equation.** $v_{throat} = Q_G / A_{throat}$ — examiners expect this explicit conversion.
> 4. **Ignoring the gas-component ΔP.** Even though liquid dominates, the gas term still appears in the *general* expression. Examiners will deduct marks if the candidate does not even write it.
> 5. **Confusing throat velocity with gas inlet velocity.** The throat velocity is *higher* than the inlet velocity by the area ratio — common-sense-check your answer.
> 6. **Confusing $d_{50}$ (cut diameter) with $d_d$ (droplet diameter).** These are *not* the same. $d_d$ is a property of the spray; $d_{50}$ is a property of the scrubber for a given particle.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A high-energy wet scrubber using converging–throat–diverging geometry to atomize liquid and capture particulates by impaction.
- **Three sections:** Converger (accelerator) → Throat (atomizer + collector) → Diverger (recoverer).
- **Throat velocity range:** 60–180 m/s (typical design: 90–120 m/s).
- **L/G range:** 0.4–1.3 L/m³.
- **Pressure drop range:** 1.25–25 kPa; liquid component dominates (~70–80 %).
- **Cut diameter (Johnstone):** $d_{50} = \sqrt{9 \eta_L d_d / (\rho_p v_{throat} C_c)}$.
- **Cut diameter (Calvert empirical, µm):** $d_{50} = 9.6 \sqrt{\eta_L d_d / (\rho_p v_{throat}^{2} C_c)}$ — use cm/s, poise, cm.
- **Efficiency scaling:** $d_{50} \propto v_{throat}^{-1}$; $\Delta P \propto v_{throat}^{2}$; $\eta_{collection}$ rises sharply with $d_p^{2}$.
- **Slip correction $C_c$** is **mandatory** for $d_p < 1 \,\mu m$.
- **Total pressure drop** = $\frac{1}{2}\rho_G v_{throat}^{2} + \rho_L (L/G) v_{throat}^{2}$.
- **Collection mechanism:** Inertial impaction (dominant), Brownian diffusion (for < 0.1 µm), interception, electrostatic attraction (if charged).
- **Adjustable-throat** design allows in-situ trimming of $d_{50}$ to match inlet conditions.
- **Applications:** Fly ash, SO₂ + fly ash, acid mists, tar, coke-oven gas, fertilizer dust.
- **Advantage:** Handles hot, corrosive, sticky, or combustible dusts — applications where fabric filters and ESPs fail.
- **Disadvantage:** High pressure drop ⇒ high operating cost; wastewater generation; corrosion; visible wet plume.
- **Comparison pointer:** ESPs have lower ΔP but cannot handle high-T or sticky dusts; baghouses have comparable efficiency but are temperature- and moisture-limited.
- **Exam mantra:** "If the question asks for d₅₀, use **Calvert** (µm). If the question asks for the *design basis*, use **Johnstone** (m). Always state the form."
- **Past-paper hotspot:** Pressure-drop calculation with specified L/G and v_throat has appeared in nearly every KTU ESE since 2019.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packed Columns: Packing Materials and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Packed Columns: Packing Materials and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **packed column** (or packed tower) is a vertical pressure vessel employed for continuous **gas–liquid mass transfer operations** such as absorption, stripping, humidification, and direct-contact heat exchange, in which the contact between the gas and liquid phases is achieved by passing the two streams **counter-currently** (most commonly) through a bed of solid, inert packing material. Unlike a tray column, which provides **stage-wise (discrete) contact**, a packed column provides **continuous differential contact**, making it essentially a **plug-flow contactor** with no physical trays.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A packed column is preferred when:
> - The system is **corrosive** (ceramic/plastic packing can be used),
> - The liquid has a tendency to **foam**,
> - **Low pressure drop** is required (e.g., vacuum distillation),
> - The flow rates are relatively **small**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pouring water through a tall cylindrical jar filled with **marbles or pebbles**. As water (liquid) trickles downward through the tiny gaps, imagine simultaneously blowing air from the bottom upward through the same gaps. The water spreads over the surface of every pebble as a **thin film**, and the air bubbles up around those films. Two things happen at every pebble surface:

1. **Mass transfer**: Soluble components in the air dissolve into the water film (like oxygen dissolving into pond water on stones).
2. **Large surface area for exchange**: Each pebble acts like a tiny exchange site.

This is exactly what a packed column does — only the "pebbles" are engineered, shaped objects (packings) chosen to **maximize surface area per unit volume** while allowing both phases to flow easily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** Three competing demands dictate packing design:
> 1. **High surface area** (a, m²/m³) → for mass transfer
> 2. **High void fraction** (ε, m³ voids/m³ bed) → for low pressure drop & high capacity
> 3. **Good liquid spreading** → to keep the packing **wet** at all times

## 1.3 Classification of Packing Materials

Packing materials are broadly classified into two families:

| Type | Geometry | Typical Size | Cost | Efficiency |
|------|----------|--------------|------|------------|
| **Random (Dumped) Packings** | Small, discrete units dumped into the column | 6 mm – 75 mm | Low | Moderate–High |
| **Structured (Regular) Packings** | Corrugated sheets/modules stacked inside the column | Modules of 100–300 mm height | High | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two types?** Random packings are cheap and easy to install (good for general duties), while structured packings dominate **high-vacuum distillation** and **large-diameter columns** (>1 m) because they offer much lower pressure drop and higher capacity.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Random Packings (Detailed)

### 2.1.1 First-Generation Packings

**a) Raschig Rings**
- Hollow cylinders, usually with **H = D** (height equals diameter).
- First introduced in 1907; most widely studied.
- Manufactured in **ceramic, metal, carbon, or plastic**.
- **Disadvantage**: Poor liquid distribution; high pressure drop compared to modern designs.

**b) Lessing Rings**
- Modification of Raschig ring with an **internal partition/web** dividing the ring into two halves.
- Increases mechanical strength but offers little advantage in mass transfer; rarely used today.

### 2.1.2 Second-Generation Packings

**c) Pall Rings**
- Raschig ring with **rectangular windows punched through the cylindrical wall**, and the tongues bent inward.
- Open walls → much **higher void fraction (~0.94–0.96)** than Raschig rings.
- Available in metal and plastic; widely used in absorption/stripping.
- Excellent liquid spreading due to the inward-bent tongues.

**d) Berl Saddles**
- **Saddle-shaped** (hyperbolic paraboloid) ceramic pieces.
- **First truly "shaped" packing** — never roll into a flat orientation, ensuring randomness in the bed.
- Better liquid distribution than Raschig rings but **brittle and heavy**.

### 2.1.3 Third-Generation Packings

**e) Intalox Saddles (or "Berl改良" saddles)**
- Improved Berl saddles with curved internal webs — patented by U.S. Stoneware (later Norton).
- Available in **ceramic, metal, plastic**.
- Higher mechanical strength and capacity than Berl saddles.

**f) Tellerettes (Rosettes)**
- Plastic, spiral-spring-like pieces with multiple holes.
- Very low pressure drop, but poor liquid distribution.

**g) Maspac, Hiflow, Cascade Mini-Rings, NORPAC, Top-Pak, IMTP**
- Modern high-performance packings — variants of Pall rings/saddles with further optimization.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU):** Packing nominal size should be **≤ 1/8 of the column diameter** to avoid poor liquid distribution and channeling along the wall.

## 2.2 Key Geometric Characteristics of Packing

### 2.2.1 Specific Surface Area ($a$ or $a_p$)

$$a = \frac{\text{Total geometric surface area of one piece}}{\text{Volume of one piece}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{m}^3}\right]$$

- For Raschig rings (H = D), thin-walled:
$$a \approx \frac{4}{D} \quad \text{(per piece, but for bed — multiplied by packing density)}$$

- **Smaller packings → higher surface area** but **higher pressure drop** and **greater flooding risk**.

### 2.2.2 Void Fraction ($\varepsilon$)

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Volume of voids in bed}}{\text{Total bed volume}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^3}\right]$$

- Higher $\varepsilon$ → lower pressure drop, higher capacity, but **lower surface area**.
- Typical values: Raschig rings = **0.55–0.75**, Pall rings = **0.90–0.96**, structured = **0.90–0.97**.

### 2.2.3 Equivalent Packing Diameter ($D_p$)

$$D_p = \frac{6(1 - \varepsilon)}{a}$$

This is the diameter of a sphere having the **same ratio of surface area to volume of voids** as the packing.

### 2.2.4 Packing Factor ($F_p$)

An **empirical** number (dimensionless) determined experimentally — used in the **Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC)** by Eckert and Leva:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{Z} = f\left(F_p, G, L, \rho_G, \rho_L, \mu\right)$$

| Packing | $F_p$ (1/in or 1/ft) — indicative |
|---------|-----------------------------------|
| 25 mm Raschig rings (ceramic) | 179 |
| 25 mm Pall rings (metal) | 48 |
| 50 mm Pall rings (metal) | 20 |
| 50 mm Intalox saddles (ceramic) | 60 |
| Mellapak 250Y (structured) | 9 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lower $F_p$ = better packing** (lower pressure drop, higher capacity).

### 2.2.5 Critical Properties for Selection

| Property | Symbol | Engineering Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------------------|
| **Nominal size** | $D_p$ | Outer dimension; affects capacity vs efficiency |
| **Specific surface area** | $a$ | Determines HETP / HTU |
| **Void fraction** | $\varepsilon$ | Controls pressure drop & flooding |
| **Bulk density** | $\rho_b$ | Mechanical load on column support |
| **Material of construction** | — | Corrosion resistance, temperature limit |
| **Packing factor** | $F_p$ | Empirical performance indicator |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU university examinations. Master them.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $a = \dfrac{A_s}{V_p}$ | Specific surface area | m²/m³ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_v}{V_b}$ | Void fraction | m³/m³ |
| 3 | $D_p = \dfrac{6(1-\varepsilon)}{a}$ | Equivalent spherical diameter | m |
| 4 | $\rho_b = \rho_s(1-\varepsilon)$ | Bulk density of bed | kg/m³ |
| 5 | $a_v = (1-\varepsilon)\,a$ | Volumetric surface (per unit bed vol.) | m²/m³ |
| 6 | Raschig ring (H=D, thin wall): $a \approx \dfrac{4}{D}$ | Quick estimate | m²/m³ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{Z} \propto F_p \cdot \rho_G^{0.5}\,G^{2.1}$ | Pressure drop trend | Pa/m |
| 8 | HETP = $\dfrac{Z}{N_{og}}$ | Height equivalent to a theoretical plate | m |

> **Memory tip:** "**SAVA**" — **S**urface area, **A** (a), **V**oid fraction (**ε**), **A** (bed density relation).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Petrochemical industry**: CO₂ and H₂S absorption using MEA/MDEA in packed columns.
- **Air separation**: Structured packings (Mellapak, Sulzer) used in cryogenic distillation of air.
- **Wastewater treatment**: Stripping of ammonia/VOCs from water using Pall rings.
- **Pharmaceutical**: Solvent recovery by absorption in packed towers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Specific Surface Area of a Raschig Ring (Thin-Walled)

We want to derive the **geometric surface area per unit volume** of a Raschig ring with:
- Outer diameter = $D_o$
- Inner diameter = $D_i$
- Height = $H$
- Wall thickness = $t$ (so $D_i = D_o - 2t$)

### Step 1: External Geometric Surface Area of One Ring

The ring has **four surfaces**:
- Outer cylindrical wall: $\pi D_o H$
- Inner cylindrical wall: $\pi D_i H$
- Two annular end rings (top + bottom): $2 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$

Adding these:

$$A_{total} = \pi D_o H + \pi D_i H + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$$

### Step 2: Volume of One Ring

Subtract the inner hollow cylinder from the outer solid cylinder:

$$V_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D_o^2 \cdot H - \dfrac{\pi}{4}D_i^2 \cdot H = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)H$$

### Step 3: Specific Surface Area Definition

$$a = \dfrac{A_{total}}{V_p}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{\pi D_o H + \pi D_i H + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)H} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4\left[(D_o + D_i)H + \dfrac{1}{2}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)\right]}{(D_o^2 - D_i^2)H}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Thin-Wall Approximation

For ceramic or thin-metal rings where $t \ll D_o$:
- $D_i \approx D_o = D$
- $(D_o^2 - D_i^2) = (D_o - D_i)(D_o + D_i) = (2t)(2D) = 4tD$
- $(D_o + D_i) \approx 2D$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
a &\approx \frac{4\left[2D \cdot H + \dfrac{1}{2}(4tD)\right]}{4tD \cdot H} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{8DH + 4tD}{4tDH} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4(2H + t)}{4tH} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2H + t}{tH}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Final Simplified Form (Standard Raschig Ring, H = D)

For the **standard Raschig ring** where height equals outer diameter ($H = D_o = D$):

$$a \approx \frac{2D + t}{tD} \;\;\xrightarrow{t \ll D}\;\; a \approx \frac{2}{t}$$

This is the classical textbook result. It tells us that the surface area is **inversely proportional to wall thickness** — which is why thin ceramic walls are used to maximize area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Calculation:** For a 25 mm Raschig ring with $t = 2$ mm:
> $$a \approx \frac{2(0.025) + 0.002}{(0.002)(0.025)} = \frac{0.052}{0.00005} = 1040 \;\frac{m^2}{m^3 \text{ of packing}}$$
> After accounting for void fraction, **bed-level** specific surface = $(1-\varepsilon) \cdot a$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Packing Diameter ($D_p$)

The equivalent diameter of a non-spherical packing is **defined** as the diameter of a sphere having the same ratio of **wetted surface to free cross-section** for flow as the packing.

For a packed bed, the **wetted surface per unit volume of bed** is $a(1-\varepsilon)$, and the **free volume for flow per unit volume of bed** is $\varepsilon$. The hydraulic diameter analogy gives:

$$D_p = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Free volume available for flow}}{\text{Wetted surface}} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon}{a(1-\varepsilon)}$$

However, the **mass-transfer convention** commonly used in Treybal and McCabe–Smith–Harriott defines it based on the volume of solids (used in pressure-drop correlations such as the Ergun equation):

$$\boxed{\;D_p = \frac{6(1-\varepsilon)}{a}\;}$$

> **Why 6 instead of 4?** Because for a *sphere*, $a = 6/D$ and $\varepsilon = 1$, giving $D_p = D$ — the formula self-consistently recovers the sphere case.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Common Packings

The matrix below summarizes typical ranges (representative values; actual depends on size and material):

| Packing Type | Size (mm) | $a$ (m²/m³) | $\varepsilon$ | $F_p$ (1/m) | HETP (m) |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|----------------|-------------|----------|
| **Raschig Rings (ceramic)** | 25 | 190 | 0.68 | 580 | 0.50–0.60 |
| **Raschig Rings (ceramic)** | 50 | 95 | 0.74 | 200 | 0.80–1.10 |
| **Berl Saddles (ceramic)** | 25 | 250 | 0.69 | 720 | 0.40–0.55 |
| **Pall Rings (metal)** | 25 | 205 | 0.94 | 157 | 0.40–0.50 |
| **Pall Rings (metal)** | 50 | 115 | 0.96 | 66 | 0.65–0.85 |
| **Intalox Saddles (ceramic)** | 25 | 255 | 0.77 | 525 | 0.40–0.50 |
| **Intalox Saddles (metal)** | 50 | 105 | 0.97 | 33 | 0.60–0.80 |
| **Mellapak 250Y (structured)** | — | 250 | 0.97 | 30 | 0.30–0.45 |
| **Sulzer BX (structured)** | — | 500 | 0.90 | 16 | 0.20–0.30 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Comparing 25 mm Pall rings with 25 mm Raschig rings — Pall rings have **~30% more void fraction** at the **same surface area**, which dramatically reduces pressure drop and flooding tendency.

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Sample)

> **Q:** A packed column uses ceramic Raschig rings of nominal size 25 mm with bulk density 720 kg/m³ and material density 2300 kg/m³. Calculate the void fraction.

**Solution:**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_s} = 1 - \dfrac{720}{2300}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= 1 - 0.313 \\[4pt]
\varepsilon &= 0.687
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the typical range (0.65–0.75) for ceramic Raschig rings.

    A2 -->|"Liquid trickles down as thin film"| M0["Film Contact with Rising Gas"]

> [!NOTE]
