# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Random Packings and Structured Packings. **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Random Packings | Structured Packings |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| Geometry | Small discrete pieces | Corrugated sheets/modules |
| Installation | Dumped randomly | Precisely stacked |
| Surface area | Moderate | Very high |
| Pressure drop | Higher | Very low |
| Cost | Low | High (5–10× random) |
| Typical use | General absorption | Vacuum distillation, cryogenic air |
| Examples | Raschig rings, Pall rings | Mellapak, Flexipac |

Random packings offer cost-effective general-purpose contact, whereas structured packings dominate high-efficiency, low-pressure-drop applications.

---

### Q2. What is the significance of the Packing Factor $F_p$? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

The **packing factor $F_p$** is an empirical dimensionless number used to characterize the **pressure-drop performance** of a packing in the Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC).

**Significance:**
- It **combines the effects** of surface area, void fraction, and shape irregularities into a single number.
- **Lower $F_p$** indicates **better packing** (lower pressure drop, higher flooding capacity).
- Typical values: Raschig rings ($F_p \approx 500$–$1000$) vs Mellapak 250Y ($F_p \approx 30$).

$F_p$ is determined experimentally (cannot be calculated from geometry alone) and is **the single most important number** used in column sizing via the GPDC chart.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Part B carries **14 marks** with sub-parts usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### **(a) Classify packing materials used in packed columns. Describe the constructional features and limitations of Raschig rings and Pall rings.** (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Packings:**

```
Packings
   ├── Random (Dumped)        ├── Structured (Regular)
   │     • Raschig Rings            • Corrugated sheets
   │     • Lessing Rings            • Knitted mesh
   │     • Pall Rings               • Gauze types
   │     • Berl Saddles
   │     • Intalox Saddles
   │     • IMTP, Tellerettes
```

**Raschig Rings — Constructional Features:**
1. Hollow right cylinders with **height equal to outer diameter** ($H = D_o$).
2. Manufactured by **extrusion** (ceramic) or **stamping/welding** (metal).
3. No internal partitions — simply a thick-walled cylinder.

**Limitations:**
- Poor liquid distribution → wall flow and channeling.
- Higher pressure drop (low void fraction ~0.55–0.75).
- Lower capacity and efficiency vs Pall rings.
- Brittle in ceramic form — fragile during handling.

**Pall Rings — Constructional Features:**
1. Modified Raschig ring: cylindrical with **rectangular windows** punched through the wall.
2. The "tongues" formed are **bent inward** (not outward) into the cylinder.
3. Available in metal, polypropylene, PVDF, stainless steel.

**Advantages over Raschig Rings:**
- **Void fraction ~0.94–0.96** (much higher than Raschig).
- Inward-bent tongues enhance liquid spreading.
- 30–40% lower pressure drop at same surface area.
- Higher flooding capacity.
- Better mechanical strength; widely preferred in modern design.

#### **(b) Define specific surface area and void fraction of a packing. A 25 mm ceramic Raschig ring has a bulk density of 720 kg/m³ and the material density is 2300 kg/m³. Calculate the void fraction. What does this tell you about its suitability compared to a 25 mm metal Pall ring with the same surface area?** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**
- **Specific surface area** $a$: Geometric surface area per unit volume of packing piece [$m^2/m^3$].
- **Void fraction** $\varepsilon$: Volume of voids in the bed per unit volume of packed bed [dimensionless, $m^3/m^3$].

**Calculation:**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_s}$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{720}{2300}$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 - 0.3130 = \boxed{0.687}$$

**Suitability comparison:**

| Parameter | Raschig Ring | Pall Ring |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| Void fraction | 0.687 | ~0.94 |
| Surface area | Same | Same |
| Pressure drop | **High** | **Low** (~30–40% less) |
| Flooding limit | Lower | Higher |
| Liquid distribution | Poor | Better |

**Conclusion:** The Pall ring, with **much higher void fraction at equal surface area**, allows higher gas throughput with lower pressure drop — making it the **economically superior choice** in most applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **(a) Explain the desirable characteristics of an ideal packing material. Discuss any four (4) in detail with their significance.** (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

An **ideal packing material** should provide maximum contact area between gas and liquid with minimum resistance to flow. The desirable characteristics are:

**1. High Specific Surface Area ($a$):**
- Larger surface area → more area for mass transfer per unit volume.
- Reduces column height (lower HETP).
- Trade-off: too high $a$ reduces void fraction.

**2. High Void Fraction ($\varepsilon$):**
- Reduces pressure drop across the bed.
- Increases flooding velocity → higher gas throughput.
- Prevents premature flooding in high-vacuum applications.

**3. Good Liquid Distribution & Wetting:**
- Packing must promote uniform **film flow** over all surfaces.
- Poor wetting → dry patches → loss of effective area.
- Saddles and Pall rings with internal tongues encourage spreading.

**4. Mechanical Strength:**
- Must withstand its own weight (especially for tall beds).
- Ceramic packings resist corrosion but are brittle.
- Metal packings resist thermal shock and impact.

**5. Chemical Inertness:**
- Should not react with process fluids.
- Material options: ceramic (acid), polypropylene (alkali), stainless steel (most organics).

**6. Low Cost and Availability:**
- Random packings are cheaper than structured.
- Should be easily replaceable during maintenance.

**7. Low Weight:**
- Reduces load on the packing support plate.

#### **(b) Compare Raschig rings, Berl saddles, and Pall rings in terms of surface area, void fraction, pressure drop, and efficiency. When would you prefer structured packing over random packing?** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Comparative Table:**

| Property | Raschig Ring (25 mm) | Berl Saddle (25 mm) | Pall Ring (25 mm) |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Surface area ($m^2/m^3$) | 190 | 250 | 205 |
| Void fraction | 0.68 | 0.69 | 0.94 |
| Pressure drop | **High** | Moderate | **Low** |
| Liquid distribution | Poor | Good | Better |
| Mechanical strength | Brittle | Brittle | Strong |
| Efficiency (HETP) | 0.55 m | 0.45 m | 0.45 m |

**Analysis:**
- **Raschig rings** are the historical reference but are now largely obsolete.
- **Berl saddles** offered better liquid distribution but are also being phased out by **Intalox saddles** (superior strength).
- **Pall rings** combine high surface area with very high void fraction — the best balance.

**When to prefer Structured Packings:**

Choose **structured packings** (Mellapak, Flexipac) when:
1. **Large column diameter** (>1.5 m) — random packings cause severe wall-channeling.
2. **Low pressure drop is critical** — vacuum distillation, cryogenic separation.
3. **Very high gas rates** — structured packings have higher flooding capacity.
4. **Fouling is minimal** — structured packings have tighter channels that clog easily with solids.
5. **High mass-transfer efficiency** required in small height.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing bed surface area with piece surface area** — $a$ is per piece; multiply by $(1-\varepsilon)$ to get bed-level surface area. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting units of $F_p$** — In SI, $F_p$ is in 1/m; many students quote it as dimensionless. **Always state units**.
> 3. **Mixing up HETP and HTU** — HETP is for staged design (number of stages), HTU is for rate-based design. They are **NOT** interchangeable without conversion factors.
> 4. **Not drawing a labeled diagram** — In 14-mark questions, a **neat, labeled sketch** of a packed column (with distributor, support, bed) easily fetches 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping numerical units in calculations** — KTU scripts lose 1–2 marks/unit-step if final answers are presented without units.
> 6. **Stating that "higher $a$ is always better"** — Wrong. Higher $a$ usually means **lower $\varepsilon$**, which means **higher pressure drop**. Always discuss the trade-off.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Packed column** = continuous contact gas–liquid column, alternative to tray column.
- **Two main classes**: *Random (dumped)* and *Structured (regular)* packings.
- **Random packings evolution**: Raschig → Berl → Pall → Intalox → IMTP/Maspac (each generation improved void fraction and reduced pressure drop).
- **Key geometric parameters**: Specific surface area ($a$, m²/m³), void fraction ($\varepsilon$), equivalent diameter ($D_p = 6(1-\varepsilon)/a$).
- **Packing factor $F_p$** is the *empirical* single-number indicator of pressure-drop performance (lower = better).
- **Typical values to memorize**:
  - Raschig rings: $a \approx 190$ m²/m³, $\varepsilon \approx 0.68$, $F_p \approx 580$ m⁻¹ (25 mm ceramic).
  - Pall rings: $a \approx 205$ m²/m³, $\varepsilon \approx 0.94$, $F_p \approx 157$ m⁻¹ (25 mm metal).
  - Mellapak 250Y: $a \approx 250$ m²/m³, $\varepsilon \approx 0.97$, $F_p \approx 30$ m⁻¹.
- **Selection rules**: packing size ≤ 1/8 of column diameter; corrosive → ceramic; alkali → polypropylene; vacuum → structured.
- **Raschig ring surface area formula** (thin wall, $H = D$): $a \approx 2/t$ — *smaller wall thickness → higher area*.
- **Bulk density–void relation**: $\rho_b = \rho_s (1 - \varepsilon)$.
- **HETP = Z / N** — directly used in design once experimental HETP value is known for the chosen packing.
- **Trade-off rule**: high $a$ → better mass transfer but higher pressure drop; high $\varepsilon$ → lower pressure drop but lower $a$. Engineering design balances both.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Column Performance: Flooding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Column Performance: Flooding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flooding** is the operating condition in a gas–liquid mass transfer contactor (tray column or packed column) at which the **counter-current flow of the two phases can no longer be sustained**. The continuous (vapour/gas) phase reaches a critical superficial velocity, entrains the dispersed/descending liquid phase in the upward direction, and a sharp discontinuity occurs in the column's pressure drop vs. gas velocity curve.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, flooding is treated as the **upper hydrodynamic limit** of column operation, and the **flooding velocity ($v_f$)** is the design anchor for the entire mass transfer equipment. Every commercial design (packed or tray) sizes the column such that the *operating* velocity is a fixed fraction of $v_f$.

The KTU syllabus specifically maps the term to:

- **Tray column**: the gas velocity at which liquid backs up in the downcomers, or excessive liquid entrainment up the column occurs.
- **Packed column**: the gas velocity at which the entire free volume of the packing is filled with liquid, and the column operates as a liquid-filled pipe with no gas–liquid interface area available for mass transfer.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Crowded Staircase:**
> Imagine a staircase in a busy mall where people (gas/vapour) move **up** and shoppers with bags (liquid) move **down**. As more people walk up, the shoppers get pushed *back upward* and the staircase gets jammed. The exact moment when shoppers start rising *with* the crowd is the **flooding point**. Before this, traffic is smooth. After this, the staircase is unusable. The same phenomenon occurs in a distillation or absorption column.

## 1.3 Loading vs. Flooding — Critical Distinction

| Phenomenon | What Happens | Operating Zone |
|---|---|---|
| **Loading** | Liquid starts to accumulate at hold-up points; pressure drop rises non-linearly with gas velocity. | Onset of hydrodynamic instability |
| **Flooding** | Liquid is violently entrained upwards; pressure drop skyrockets; mass transfer collapses. | **Upper operating limit (DESIGN CEILING)** |

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Flooding velocity ($v_f$)** in **m/s** — superficial gas velocity at the flooding condition.
- **Operating velocity ($v_{op}$)** — typically **50 % to 80 %** of $v_f$ for tray columns and **50 % to 75 %** for packed columns.
- **Capacity parameter ($C_{SB}$ or $C_G$)** — empirical constant capturing packing/tray geometry effects, units **m/s**.
- **Flow parameter ($F_{LV}$)** — dimensionless ratio $F_{LV} = \dfrac{L}{G}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_V}{\rho_L}}$, characterising the L/V ratio effect on flooding.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Flooding — A Stepwise Logic

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently award 3–4 marks for a *clear staged explanation* of the flooding mechanism. Use the following five-step narrative in your answers.

1. **Step 1 — Initial Trickling Regime:** At low gas velocity, liquid flows freely down the packing or across the tray. The pressure drop follows the dry-packing (or dry-tray) relationship raised to the power ~2.
2. **Step 2 — Hold-up Build-up:** As gas velocity increases, the **liquid hold-up** (volume fraction of liquid retained in the column) increases. Liquid spreads over more of the packing surface, restricting the cross-section available for gas.
3. **Step 3 — Loading Point:** The pressure drop curve bends upward; liquid begins to accumulate at packing intersections or weir outlets. This is the **loading point**.
4. **Step 4 — Onset of Entrainment:** A small fraction of liquid is now being dragged upward by the gas. The **effective cross-section for gas** shrinks further, accelerating the pressure drop.
5. **Step 5 — Flooding Point:** A critical gas velocity $v_f$ is reached where the drag force on the descending liquid balances (and then exceeds) the gravitational + surface-tension retention force. Liquid is entrained en masse; the column can no longer function.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Flooding (KTU High-Yield)

The flooding velocity is a strong function of the following engineering parameters. KTU 2024 Scheme examiners expect you to *classify* them and *explain* their directional effect.

### A. Operating / Throughput Variables
1. **Vapour (gas) mass flux ($G$)** — Increasing $G$ reduces the safety margin below $v_f$. The most dominant operational factor.
2. **Liquid mass flux ($L$)** — Higher $L$ lowers $v_f$ because more liquid must flow through the same cross-section against the same drag.

### B. Physical Property Variables
3. **Density difference $(\rho_L - \rho_V)$** — Larger density difference → higher $v_f$. Low-pressure distillation (large $\Delta\rho$) floods at much higher velocity than high-pressure distillation.
4. **Liquid viscosity ($\mu_L$)** — Higher viscosity → lower $v_f$. Viscous systems (e.g., absorption of CO₂ in glycerol) flood early.
5. **Surface tension ($\sigma_L$)** — Lower surface tension promotes entrainment, reducing $v_f$.

### C. System / Service Variables
6. **Operating pressure** — Higher pressure → higher $\rho_V$ → smaller density difference → lower $v_f$.
7. **Foaming tendency** — Foaming liquids behave as pseudo-fluids of higher effective density, drastically reducing $v_f$ (50 % or more in some systems).
8. **Presence of solids / suspended particles** — Promotes premature flooding.

### D. Geometric / Design Variables
9. **Tray spacing** (in tray columns) — Greater spacing → higher $v_f$.
10. **Packing size and type** (in packed columns) — Larger packings → higher $v_f$, but lower mass-transfer area.
11. **Weir height** — Higher weir height → greater liquid hold-up → earlier flooding.
12. **Hole/valve aperture** in sieve/valve trays — Affects the gas distribution and the operating range.

## 2.3 The Souders–Brown Equation — The KTU Workhorse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Souders–Brown equation is the single most-tested correlation on this topic in the KTU ESE. Memorise the form, the units, and the table of $C_{SB}$ values.

$$v_{f} = C_{SB}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}}{\rho_{V}}}$$

where:
- $v_f$ = flooding superficial vapour velocity, **m/s**
- $C_{SB}$ = Souders–Brown capacity parameter, **m/s**
- $\rho_L, \rho_V$ = liquid and vapour densities, **kg/m³**

### Representative $C_{SB}$ values (per KTU board-exam data)

| Type of Service | $C_{SB}$ (m/s) |
|---|---|
| **Foaming systems** (e.g., amines, caustic) | 0.030 – 0.045 |
| **Crude oil / vacuum distillation** | 0.045 – 0.060 |
| **Pressure distillation (non-foaming)** | 0.075 – 0.090 |
| **Atmospheric distillation (non-foaming)** | 0.090 – 0.110 |
| **Absorbers (low pressure, non-foaming)** | 0.110 – 0.140 |
| **Stabilisers / natural gas processing** | 0.100 – 0.120 |

## 2.4 Flooding Correlations (Tray and Packed)

### 2.4.1 Fair's Correlation for Sieve and Valve Trays

$$v_{f}\;\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{V}}{\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}}} \;=\; C_{SB,\,Fair}$$

Read $C_{SB,\,Fair}$ from Fair's chart (provided in the data book) using the **flow parameter** $F_{LV}$ and the **tray spacing**.

$$F_{LV} \;=\; \dfrac{L}{G}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{V}}{\rho_{L}}}$$

### 2.4.2 Sherwood–Lobo–Friend Correlation (Packed Columns)

$$\dfrac{v_{f}^{2}\,a\,\mu_{L}^{0.2}}{g\,\varepsilon^{3}}\cdot\dfrac{\rho_{V}}{\rho_{L}} \;=\; f\!\left(\dfrac{L}{G}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{V}}{\rho_{L}}}\right)$$

where:
- $a$ = specific surface area of packing, **m²/m³**
- $\varepsilon$ = void fraction of packing
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity, **9.81 m/s²**
- $\mu_L$ = liquid viscosity, **cP**

### 2.4.3 Eckert's Generalised Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC)

Used to read the *pressure drop per unit height* of a packed column at a given operating velocity, with the flooding curve marked. Inputs:
- X-axis (flow parameter): $\dfrac{L}{G}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{V}}{\rho_{L}}}$
- Y-axis (capacity parameter): $\dfrac{G^{2}\,C_{f}\,\mu_{L}^{0.1}\,\psi}{\rho_{V}\,(\rho_{L} - \rho_{V})\,g_{c}}$

where $C_f$ is the packing factor, $\psi$ is the liquid density correction (water = 1.0), and $g_c = 1.0$ in SI units.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Equation | Used For | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{f}=C_{SB}\sqrt{(\rho_{L}-\rho_{V})/\rho_{V}}$ | Flooding velocity (general) | $v_f$ in m/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 2 | $F_{LV}=(L/G)\sqrt{\rho_{V}/\rho_{L}}$ | Flow parameter / L–V ratio effect | dimensionless |
| 3 | $v_{op}=\eta \cdot v_{f}$ | Operating velocity; $\eta=0.6$–$0.8$ | m/s |
| 4 | $C_{SG}=v_{op}\sqrt{\rho_{V}/(\rho_{L}-\rho_{V})}$ | Capacity parameter at operating point | m/s |
| 5 | $A_{col}=Q_{V}/v_{op}$ | Required column cross-section | m² |
| 6 | $D_{col}=\sqrt{4A_{col}/\pi}$ | Column diameter | m |
| 7 | Sherwood: $v_f^{2}a\mu_{L}^{0.2}\rho_{V}/(g\varepsilon^{3}\rho_{L})=f(F_{LV})$ | Flooding in packed columns | dimensionless |
| 8 | Fair (tray): read $C_{SB,\,Fair}$ off chart | Flooding in sieve/valve trays | m/s |
| 9 | GPDC capacity param: $G^{2}C_{f}\mu_{L}^{0.1}\psi/[\rho_{V}(\rho_{L}-\rho_{V})g_{c}]$ | Packed-column pressure drop | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to design a column, *always* start by computing $v_f$, then multiply by an operating factor (typically **0.7** unless specified), then determine the diameter. Skipping the $v_f$ computation is a guaranteed 2–3 mark penalty.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flooding analysis is not an academic exercise — it is the basis of:

- **Distillation column design** in oil refineries (atmospheric and vacuum crude units).
- **Absorber design** in ammonia, CO₂-capture, and natural-gas sweetening plants.
- **Stripper design** in wastewater treatment (e.g., steam stripping of VOCs).
- **Scrubber design** in pollution-control equipment for SO₂, HCl, and particulate removal.

A column designed at 95 % of $v_f$ operates close to its hydrodynamic ceiling; any upsets (foaming, surge in feed) will cause flooding. Most KTU board questions test the *safety margin* (0.5–0.8) and the *remedy* for a flooded column (reduce vapour rate, increase tray spacing, lower weir, change packing).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Below, we develop the Souders–Brown form from a **force balance on an entrained liquid droplet** and then connect it to the empirical constants. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly, as KTU valuations penalise skipped steps.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Souders–Brown Form from Force Balance

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a spherical liquid droplet of diameter $d_p$ being dragged upward by rising gas of velocity $v$, while gravity pulls it downward. At the **flooding condition**, the droplet is in *quasi-equilibrium* — any infinitesimal increase in $v$ carries the droplet upward.

Forces on the droplet (forces in SI units; $g$ in m/s², densities in kg/m³, $d_p$ in m, $v$ in m/s):

- **Gravitational force (downward):**

$$F_{g} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,d_{p}^{3}}{6}\,\bigl(\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}\bigr)\,g$$

- **Buoyancy is already included** through the $(\rho_L - \rho_V)$ term.
- **Drag force (upward):**

$$F_{d} \;=\; C_{D}\,\dfrac{\pi\,d_{p}^{2}}{4}\,\dfrac{\rho_{V}\,v^{2}}{2}$$

where $C_D$ is the drag coefficient (dimensionless).

### 3.1.2 Equilibrium at Flooding

Setting $F_d = F_g$ at the flooding velocity $v = v_f$:

$$C_{D}\,\dfrac{\pi\,d_{p}^{2}}{4}\,\dfrac{\rho_{V}\,v_{f}^{2}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,d_{p}^{3}}{6}\,\bigl(\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}\bigr)\,g$$

Cancel $\pi$ from both sides:

$$C_{D}\,\dfrac{d_{p}^{2}}{4}\,\dfrac{\rho_{V}\,v_{f}^{2}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{d_{p}^{3}}{6}\,\bigl(\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}\bigr)\,g$$

Multiply both sides by 12 to clear the denominators:

$$3\,C_{D}\,d_{p}^{2}\,\rho_{V}\,v_{f}^{2} \;=\; 2\,d_{p}^{3}\,\bigl(\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}\bigr)\,g$$

Divide both sides by $3\,C_{D}\,d_{p}^{2}\,\rho_{V}$:

$$v_{f}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,d_{p}\,g\,\bigl(\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}\bigr)}{3\,C_{D}\,\rho_{V}}$$

### 3.1.3 Rearranging into the Souders–Brown Form

Take the square root:

$$v_{f} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,d_{p}\,g}{3\,C_{D}}}\;\cdot\;\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}}{\rho_{V}}}$$

Now identify the **grouped constant** as the Souders–Brown capacity parameter:

$$C_{SB} \;\equiv\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,d_{p}\,g}{3\,C_{D}}}$$

This is a constant for a given *system* (it depends on droplet size $d_p$ and drag coefficient $C_D$, which are properties of the system and packing/tray geometry). Therefore:

$$v_{f} \;=\; C_{SB}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}}{\rho_{V}}}$$

This is the **Souders–Brown equation**, fully derived.

### 3.1.4 How the Factors Manifest Themselves in the Derivation

| Factor | Where it appears | Effect on $v_f$ |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho_L - \rho_V$ | Inside the square root | Larger $\Delta\rho$ → larger $v_f$ (favours low-pressure columns) |
| $\rho_V$ | Inside the square root | Larger $\rho_V$ → smaller $v_f$ (favours low-pressure columns) |
| $d_p$ (packing/pellet size) | Inside $C_{SB}$ | Larger $d_p$ → larger $C_{SB}$ → larger $v_f$ |
| $\mu_L$ | Drag coefficient $C_D$ | Higher $\mu_L$ → higher $C_D$ → lower $C_{SB}$ → lower $v_f$ |
| $L$ (liquid rate) | Affects $d_p$ and $C_D$ via hold-up | Higher $L$ → larger $d_p$, higher hold-up → lower $v_f$ |

## 3.2 Worked Example — Flooding Velocity by Souders–Brown

**Problem.** A tray column operating at **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** is to be sized. The vapour density is $\rho_V = 1.20\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and the liquid density is $\rho_L = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3$. The service is atmospheric crude distillation, for which the recommended Souders–Brown coefficient is $C_{SB} = 0.095\ \text{m/s}$. Find (i) the flooding velocity and (ii) the design column diameter for a vapour volumetric flow of $Q_V = 0.50\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, taking 70 % of flooding as the design operating point.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Flooding Velocity

$$v_{f} \;=\; C_{SB}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}}{\rho_{V}}}$$

Substitute the numbers:

$$v_{f} \;=\; 0.095 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{850 - 1.20}{1.20}}$$

Compute the ratio inside the square root:

$$\dfrac{850 - 1.20}{1.20} \;=\; \dfrac{848.80}{1.20} \;=\; 707.33$$

Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{707.33} \;=\; 26.595$$

Therefore:

$$v_{f} \;=\; 0.095 \times 26.595 \;=\; 2.527\ \text{m/s}$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Operating Velocity

Take 70 % of $v_f$:

$$v_{op} \;=\; 0.70 \times 2.527 \;=\; 1.769\ \text{m/s}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Required Cross-Sectional Area

$$A_{col} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{V}}{v_{op}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.50}{1.769} \;=\; 0.2826\ \text{m}^{2}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Column Diameter

$$D_{col} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{col}}{\pi}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.2826}{\pi}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.3598} \;=\; 0.5998\ \text{m} \;\approx\; 0.60\ \text{m}$$

### 3.2.5 Result Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $v_f$ | **2.527 m/s** |
| $v_{op}$ (70 % of $v_f$) | **1.769 m/s** |
| $A_{col}$ | **0.2826 m²** |
| $D_{col}$ | **≈ 0.60 m** (round up to standard 0.61 m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 0.70?** KTU board problems *frequently* give the operating factor (e.g., "operate at 75 % of flooding"). If not stated, default to **0.6 – 0.8**. Mentioning your assumption in the answer sheet is a hallmark of a top-grade student.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factor × Direction of Effect on $v_f$

| Factor | Increase → $v_f$ Effect | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|
| Vapour velocity $G$ | Decreases safety margin | Throttle reboiler / feed preheater |
| Liquid rate $L$ | Decreases | Reduce reflux / use pump-around |
| $\rho_L - \rho_V$ | Increases | Operate at lower pressure (if feasible) |
| $\mu_L$ (viscosity) | Decreases | Pre-heat the liquid; reduce viscosity |
| Surface tension $\sigma_L$ | Decreases | Add anti-foam; avoid detergents |
| Pressure | Decreases (via $\rho_V\uparrow$) | Use vacuum for viscous/heat-sensitive systems |
| Foaming | Strongly decreases | Use anti-foam; change packing |
| Tray spacing | Increases | Use wider tray spacing (1.0 m instead of 0.6 m) |
| Packing size | Increases | Use larger packings (e.g., 50 mm Pall) |
| Weir height | Decreases | Lower the weir height |
| Holes/valve aperture | Aperture size: too small = flooding | Use proper aperture design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are rendered directly inside the KTU study portal. They depict the **mechanism of flooding** in a packed column and the **factor-to-effect map**. Node IDs are alphanumeric-only and labels contain no markdown formatting, in compliance with the rendering safeguards.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Flooding Mechanism in a Packed Column

```mermaid
graph TD
    id1[Gas Stream Enters Bottom] --> id2[Liquid Distributed at Top]
    id2 --> id3[Counter-current Contact in Packing]
    id1 --> id3
    id3 --> id4[Liquid Holdup Increases With Gas Velocity]
    id4 --> id5[Loading Point Reached]
    id5 --> id6[Liquid Entrainment Begins Upward]
    id6 --> id7[Flooding Point VF]
    id7 --> id8[Column Pressure Drop Skyrockets]
    id7 --> id9[Liquid Cannot Descend Further]
    id8 --> id10[Mass Transfer Stops]
    id9 --> id10
    id10 --> id11[Design Action: Reduce Gas Velocity OR Increase Tray Spacing]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Factor-to-Effect Map for Flooding Velocity

```mermaid
graph TD
    idF1[Flooding Velocity VF] --> idF2[Operating Variables]
    idF1 --> idF3[Physical Properties]
    idF1 --> idF4[Geometric Design]
    idF2 --> idF2a[Gas Mass Flux G]
    idF2 --> idF2b[Liquid Mass Flux L]
    idF3 --> idF3a[Density Difference]
    idF3 --> idF3b[Liquid Viscosity]
    idF3 --> idF3c[Surface Tension]
    idF3 --> idF3d[Foaming Tendency]
    idF4 --> idF4a[Tray Spacing]
    idF4 --> idF4b[Packing Size and Type]
    idF4 --> idF4c[Weir Height]
    idF4 --> idF4d[Hole Aperture]
    idF2a --> idR1[Higher G Lowers VF Margin]
    idF2b --> idR2[Higher L Lowers VF]
    idF3a --> idR3[Larger Density Diff Raises VF]
    idF3b --> idR4[Higher Viscosity Lowers VF]
    idF3c --> idR5[Lower Surface Tension Lowers VF]
    idF3d --> idR6[Foaming Strongly Lowers VF]
    idF4a --> idR7[Larger Spacing Raises VF]
    idF4b --> idR8[Larger Packing Raises VF]
    idF4c --> idR9[Higher Weir Lowers VF]
    idF4d --> idR10[Proper Aperture Raises VF]
```

## 4.3 Pressure Drop vs. Gas Velocity — Operating Regimes

```mermaid
graph LR
    idP1[Low Velocity] --> idP2[Trickling Regime]
    idP2 --> idP3[Loading Point]
    idP3 --> idP4[Loading Region]
    idP4 --> idP5[Flooding Point VF]
    idP5 --> idP6[Flooded Region Column Fails]
    idP2 -.Delta P Proportional To V Squared.-> idP3
    idP3 -.Delta P Rises Sharply.-> idP5
    idP5 -.Delta P Approaches Infinity.-> idP6
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the curve (KTU favourites):** A common KTU question presents the *pressure drop vs. gas velocity* plot and asks the student to label the loading and flooding points. The plot has three distinct zones — *trickling* (low $\Delta P$), *loading* (curved transition), and *flooding* (vertical asymptote). Always mark the **operating point** at **50–80 % of $v_f$**, *not* at the flooding point itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define flooding in a mass transfer column. Mention the operating range of a tray column expressed as a fraction of the flooding velocity.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Flooding** is the condition in a gas–liquid contactor at which the upward drag force exerted by the gas on the descending liquid becomes so large that the liquid can no longer flow down — it is entrained upward en masse, and counter-current operation ceases (1 mark).
> Mechanistically, the column experiences a sharp rise in pressure drop, loss of interfacial area, and complete collapse of mass-transfer efficiency (1 mark).
> The **operating velocity** is typically held at **50–80 %** of the flooding velocity ($v_{op} = 0.5$ to $0.8 \times v_f$), with a common design value of **70 %** (1 mark).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the Souders–Brown equation for flooding velocity. List any **four** factors that affect the flooding velocity.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Souders–Brown equation:** $v_{f} = C_{SB}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}}{\rho_{V}}}$ (1 mark).
> **Four factors affecting $v_f$ (1/2 mark each):**
> 1. Vapour (gas) mass flux
> 2. Liquid mass flux
> 3. Density difference $(\rho_L - \rho_V)$
> 4. Surface tension of the liquid
> 5. Liquid viscosity
> 6. Operating pressure
> 7. Tray spacing / packing size
> 8. Foaming tendency

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 of PCCHT403 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Both alternatives are given below; answer **any one**.

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **mechanism of flooding in a packed column**, showing the relationship between pressure drop and gas velocity. Mark the **loading** and **flooding points** clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sieve-tray column is to be designed for separating a mixture at atmospheric pressure. The vapour density is $\rho_V = 1.18\ \text{kg/m}^3$, liquid density is $\rho_L = 980\ \text{kg/m}^3$, and the service coefficient is $C_{SB} = 0.090\ \text{m/s}$. If the vapour volumetric flow rate is $Q_V = 0.85\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and the design factor is 0.75 of flooding, calculate the required column diameter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A (a)**

The packed column is shown schematically in the Mermaid diagram of SECTION 4.1. The pressure drop vs. gas velocity curve has three regimes:

1. **Trickling regime** — At low gas velocity, the pressure drop $\Delta P$ is low and increases approximately as $v^2$ (1 mark).
2. **Loading regime** — As the gas velocity rises, liquid hold-up increases at the points of contact within the packing, and $\Delta P$ rises more steeply. The **loading point** is identified as the point where the curve begins to deviate from the $v^2$ trend (1 mark).
3. **Flooding regime** — Past the flooding velocity $v_f$, the liquid cannot flow downward; the entire void of the packing fills with liquid, and the pressure drop curve becomes asymptotic — $\Delta P \to \infty$ (1 mark).

The **mechanism** is: (a) liquid hold-up rises, (b) effective cross-section for gas decreases, (c) drag force on liquid equals and then exceeds gravity, (d) liquid is entrained upward (2 marks).

**The design operating point is fixed at 50–80 % of $v_f$**, with 70 % being a typical design value (1 mark).

**Sketch:** (1 mark) — Show a packed column with gas entering at the bottom and liquid entering at the top; show the $\Delta P$–$v$ curve with loading and flooding points marked and the operating point at 0.7 $v_f$.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A (b)**

**Step 1 — Flooding velocity using Souders–Brown (2 marks):**

$$v_{f} \;=\; C_{SB}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}}{\rho_{V}}} \;=\; 0.090 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{980 - 1.18}{1.18}}$$

Compute inside the square root:

$$\dfrac{978.82}{1.18} \;=\; 829.51$$

$$\sqrt{829.51} \;=\; 28.802$$

$$v_{f} \;=\; 0.090 \times 28.802 \;=\; 2.592\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Operating velocity (1 mark):**

$$v_{op} \;=\; 0.75 \times v_{f} \;=\; 0.75 \times 2.592 \;=\; 1.944\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional area (2 marks):**

$$A_{col} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{V}}{v_{op}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.85}{1.944} \;=\; 0.4372\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Column diameter (2 marks):**

$$D_{col} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{col}}{\pi}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.4372}{\pi}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.5567} \;=\; 0.7461\ \text{m}$$

**Final answer:** $D_{col} \approx 0.75\ \text{m}$ (round up to next standard size, e.g., **0.80 m**).

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define the terms **loading** and **flooding** as applied to mass transfer columns. Discuss, with reasoning, the **effect of the following on flooding velocity**: (i) liquid flow rate, (ii) gas density, (iii) tray spacing, and (iv) foaming. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A packed absorber is to be designed for removing $\text{CO}_2$ from a gas mixture. The gas density is $\rho_V = 2.50\ \text{kg/m}^3$, the liquid density is $\rho_L = 1100\ \text{kg/m}^3$, and the Sherwood correlation constant (combined factor) gives $C_{SB} = 0.105\ \text{m/s}$. Calculate the flooding velocity. If the operating gas volumetric flow rate is $Q_V = 1.20\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and the design uses 65 % of flooding, determine the **tower cross-sectional area** and **diameter**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B (a)**

- **Loading (1 mark):** The condition in a gas–liquid contactor at which liquid begins to accumulate at hold-up points (e.g., packing intersections or weir outlets) due to the rising drag of the gas, marking the onset of non-linear pressure drop behaviour.
- **Flooding (1 mark):** The condition beyond loading at which liquid can no longer flow downward against the gas drag; the liquid is entrained upward en masse, and counter-current operation is no longer possible.

**Effect of factors (1 mark each):**

- **(i) Liquid flow rate ↑** → $v_f$ ↓ (more liquid must traverse the same cross-section; higher hold-up; higher drag per unit liquid volume).
- **(ii) Gas density ↑** → $v_f$ ↓ (because $\Delta\rho$ decreases in the Souders–Brown numerator while $\rho_V$ in the denominator rises, net: $v_f$ drops sharply).
- **(iii) Tray spacing ↑** → $v_f$ ↑ (more distance for liquid droplets to fall back; less entrainment; greater gas-handling capacity).
- **(iv) Foaming ↑** → $v_f$ ↓ (foam behaves as a low-density pseudo-fluid, increasing effective liquid density and gas pressure drop).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B (b)**

**Step 1 — Flooding velocity (3 marks):**

$$v_{f} \;=\; C_{SB}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{V}}{\rho_{V}}} \;=\; 0.105 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1100 - 2.50}{2.50}}$$

Compute inside the square root:

$$\dfrac{1097.5}{2.50} \;=\; 439.0$$

$$\sqrt{439.0} \;=\; 20.952$$

$$v_{f} \;=\; 0.105 \times 20.952 \;=\; 2.200\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Operating velocity (1 mark):**

$$v_{op} \;=\; 0.65 \times 2.200 \;=\; 1.430\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional area (1.5 marks):**

$$A_{col} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{V}}{v_{op}} \;=\; \dfrac{1.20}{1.430} \;=\; 0.8392\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Column diameter (1.5 marks):**

$$D_{col} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.8392}{\pi}} \;=\; \sqrt{1.0686} \;=\; 1.0338\ \text{m}$$

**Final answer:** $D_{col} \approx 1.03\ \text{m}$, round up to next standard size, e.g., **1.05 m** or **1.10 m**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks in flooding problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $\mu_L$ in cP must be converted when plugging into the Sherwood correlation; $\rho$ in kg/m³ and $Q$ in m³/s are SI-consistent.
> 2. **Using the wrong $C_{SB}$** — Picking a $C_{SB}$ for "absorber" when the service is "crude distillation" (or vice versa) leads to 1.5–2× errors.
> 3. **Not applying the operating factor** — Designing the column at $v_f$ itself (factor = 1.0) is unsafe; KTU expects $v_{op} = \eta v_f$ with $\eta = 0.5$ to $0.8$.
> 4. **Confusing loading and flooding** — These are *distinct* points on the $\Delta P$–$v$ curve, not the same point. Drawing a single mark for both is a half-mark penalty.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch** in 7-mark theory sub-parts — A neat, labelled diagram is worth a full mark and is often the difference between a 6 and a 7.
> 6. **Failing to round up the diameter** — Always round up to the next standard commercial size (e.g., 0.6 m → 0.61 m; 0.75 m → 0.80 m).
> 7. **Not writing the assumption** of the operating factor (η) when not stated — Examiners reward students who declare their assumption explicitly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — Factors Affecting Column Performance: Flooding (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Flooding** is the upper hydrodynamic limit; the column must be designed *below* it.
- ✅ **Operating velocity = 0.5 – 0.8 × flooding velocity** (typical: 0.7).
- ✅ **Souders–Brown equation:** $v_f = C_{SB}\sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ — master this and the $C_{SB}$ table.
- ✅ **Flow parameter:** $F_{LV} = (L/G)\sqrt{\rho_V/\rho_L}$ — the key X-axis variable in Fair and GPDC charts.
- ✅ **Higher $L$** → lower $v_f$ | **Higher $G$** → lower safety margin.
- ✅ **Higher $\rho_L - \rho_V$** → higher $v_f$ (low-pressure distillation floods at higher velocity).
- ✅ **Higher liquid viscosity** → lower $v_f$.
- ✅ **Foaming liquids** flood very early — anti-foam + derated $C_{SB}$.
- ✅ **Greater tray spacing** → higher $v_f$ (wider design = higher capacity).
- ✅ **Larger packing size** → higher $v_f$ but lower mass-transfer area.
- ✅ **Higher weir height** → more liquid hold-up → earlier flooding.
- ✅ **Loading** → bend in $\Delta P$–$v$ curve | **Flooding** → asymptote in $\Delta P$–$v$ curve.
- ✅ For tray columns use **Fair's correlation**; for packed columns use **Sherwood** or **Eckert's GPDC**.
- ✅ In numericals, *always* show: (i) flooding velocity, (ii) operating velocity, (iii) cross-section, (iv) diameter, (v) standard-size round-up.
- ✅ Sketch a $\Delta P$–$v$ curve with **loading**, **flooding**, and **operating points** marked — earns an easy mark in 7-mark theory questions.
- ✅ End your answer with **two remedies for flooding** (e.g., reduce vapour rate, increase tray spacing, lower weir, change to larger packing, add anti-foam, lower operating pressure) — examiners reward forward-looking design comments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priming
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Priming

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mass Transfer Operations**, **Priming** is a pre-operational conditioning procedure that prepares mass transfer equipment (such as packed columns, tray columns, and centrifugal pumps) for stable, design-specification performance. Specifically, it refers to one of two closely related engineering practices:

1. **Priming of Pumps** — The process of completely filling the impeller casing and suction line of a centrifugal pump with the process liquid (or a compatible priming fluid) to displace air, vapor, or gas, thereby establishing a continuous liquid column on the suction side so that the pump can generate the required suction head (NPSH) upon start-up.

2. **Priming of Packed Columns** — The pre-wetting of the dry packing material (Raschig rings, Pall rings, structured packing, etc.) with the liquid phase by circulating liquid through the column for an extended period (typically **24 to 48 hours**) before introducing the gas/vapor phase. This ensures the formation of a stable liquid film on the packing surface, achieving the **wetted surface area** and **effective interfacial area** assumed in the design correlations (e.g., Onda's correlation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Priming is a critical operating concept under **Equipment for Mass Transfer Operations**. Questions typically appear in the form of "Explain priming in packed columns" (3 marks) or "Discuss the procedure and importance of priming" (7–14 marks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy 1 — Priming a Water Pump (Daily Life):**
Think of a bicycle pump. If you try to pump water with air trapped inside the cylinder, the pump will simply compress the air and fail to lift water. You must first pour water into the pump cylinder to fill it completely, expelling the air. This "pre-filling" is exactly what **priming** does for a centrifugal pump in a chemical plant. Without priming, the pump will "air-bind" and fail to deliver liquid.

**Real-World Analogy 2 — Priming a Packed Column (Packing Wetting):**
Imagine a new sponge. When dry, it feels hard and does not absorb much liquid from a single dip. However, if you submerge it in water for some time (pre-soaking = priming), the sponge becomes fully saturated and ready to do its job efficiently. Similarly, dry packing must be soaked with liquid first; only then does it offer the full interfacial area required for mass transfer between gas and liquid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Priming is **NOT** normal operation. It is a *transient* start-up procedure that engineers must perform before the equipment is allowed to operate at its design throughput and composition targets.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSHₐ)** — typically must exceed **NPSHᵣ** by at least **0.5 to 1.0 m** of liquid.
- **Wetting Rate** — typical design value: **0.06 to 0.15 m³/h per m of perimeter** (for random packing).
- **Pre-wetting Duration** — usually **24–48 hours** for industrial columns.
- **Pump Priming Time** — typically **2 to 10 minutes** depending on suction line length and pump size.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown: Priming in Centrifugal Pumps

### Why Priming is Required
A centrifugal pump cannot "suck" a liquid. It can only *push* liquid. The suction lift works on the principle that atmospheric pressure pushes the liquid up into the low-pressure region created at the impeller eye. This requires the suction line to be **completely filled with liquid**.

If air is present:
- Air is compressible; the pump compresses the air pocket instead of lifting liquid.
- The condition is called **"air binding"** or **"vapor lock."**
- The pump motor may overload and trip.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Pump Priming

1. **Close the discharge valve** to prevent back-flow during priming.
2. **Open the priming fluid source** (e.g., priming tank, ejector, or vacuum pump).
3. **Introduce priming liquid** through the priming port at the top of the casing.
4. **Displace all trapped air/vapor** through the air vent valve until a continuous stream of liquid flows.
5. **Close the air vent and priming port.**
6. **Verify liquid level** in the suction line and priming tank.
7. **Start the pump motor** and gradually open the discharge valve.

## 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown: Priming in Packed Columns

### Why Priming is Required for Packing
Mass transfer correlations (e.g., Onda, Billet, Bravo-Fair) assume that the packing surface is **completely wetted** by the liquid. The **effective interfacial area $a_e$** equals the **total surface area $a_t$** of the packing only when the **wetting efficiency $\eta_w$ = 1.0**:

$$\eta_w = \frac{a_e}{a_t}$$

For dry or partially wetted packing, $\eta_w$ is significantly less than unity, leading to:
- Lower mass transfer coefficients ($K_G$ and $K_L$).
- Higher required column height.
- Inaccurate design predictions.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Packed Column Priming

1. **Fill the column shell** with the liquid (typically the solvent) up to the bottom distributor level.
2. **Start the liquid pump** and circulate the liquid at **50% to 100% of the design liquid rate** through the packing and back to the sump.
3. **Maintain circulation for 24–48 hours** to ensure complete wetting, including the crevices of irregular packings.
4. **Verify the liquid distribution** is uniform on the top distributor; check for maldistribution using a visual inspection or pressure-tap survey.
5. **Introduce the gas phase slowly** (ramp-up over 15–30 minutes) to avoid sudden flooding.
6. **Establish design operating conditions** of gas ($G$) and liquid ($L$) flow rates.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|----------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Wetting Efficiency | $\eta_w = a_e / a_t$ | dimensionless | Packed column design |
| 2 | Onda's Wetting Efficiency | $\eta_w = 1 - \exp\!\left(-1.45 \left(\sigma_c/\sigma_L\right)^{0.75} \, \mathrm{Re}_L^{0.1} \, \mathrm{Fr}_L^{-0.05} \, \mathrm{We}_L^{0.2}\right)$ | dimensionless | Random packing |
| 3 | Liquid Reynolds Number | $\mathrm{Re}_L = \rho_L \, u_L \, d_p / \mu_L$ | dimensionless | Packing hydrodynamics |
| 4 | Froude Number | $\mathrm{Fr}_L = u_L^2 / (g \, d_p)$ | dimensionless | Flow stability |
| 5 | Weber Number | $\mathrm{We}_L = \rho_L \, u_L^2 \, d_p / \sigma_L$ | dimensionless | Droplet/bubble formation |
| 6 | Critical Surface Tension | $\sigma_c$ (function of packing material) | N/m or dyne/cm | Wettability criterion |
| 7 | Net Positive Suction Head Available | $\mathrm{NPSH}_a = \dfrac{P_s - P_v}{\rho_L \, g} - h_s$ | m of liquid | Pump suction |
| 8 | Net Positive Suction Head Required | $\mathrm{NPSH}_r$ (from manufacturer curve) | m of liquid | Pump selection |
| 9 | Priming Condition | $\mathrm{NPSH}_a \geq \mathrm{NPSH}_r + 0.5$ | m of liquid | Operational safety |
| 10 | Pump Suction Head (cold) | $h_s = h_{static} - h_{friction} - h_{velocity}$ | m of liquid | Suction line design |
| 11 | Vapor Pressure of Liquid | $P_v = f(T)$ from Antoine or steam tables | Pa or bar | NPSH calculation |
| 12 | Packing Wetting Rate | $L_w = L / (a_t \cdot A_c)$ | m³/(h·m²) | Minimum wetting criterion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Wetting Rate (CWR):** The Onda correlation provides $\eta_w$ for a given set of fluid and packing properties. A common KTU exam trick is to ask students to **verify whether the operating wetting rate exceeds the Critical Wetting Rate (CWR ≈ 0.08 m³/h per m of wetted perimeter)**. If not, the column is said to be **under-primed**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Refineries:** Priming the absorption oil pump before the FCCU starts up.
- **Ammonia Plants:** Pre-wetting the CO₂ absorption packing (MEA solution) before CO₂ introduction.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Pre-conditioning of structured packing in vacuum distillation columns to avoid dry spots that lead to thermal degradation.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Stripping towers (NH₃, H₂S removal) require priming of the packing to prevent channeling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: NPSH Available for a Centrifugal Pump (Suction Side)

The **Bernoulli equation with friction losses** applied between the suction reservoir surface (point 1) and the pump impeller eye (point 2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho_L \, g} + \frac{u_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho_L \, g} + \frac{u_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_{friction}$$

Applying the simplifications:
- $u_1 \approx 0$ (large reservoir surface)
- $z_1 - z_2 = h_s$ (static suction lift, positive if liquid is below pump)
- $P_1 = P_{atm}$ (open reservoir) or system pressure
- $P_2 = P_{impeller\ eye}$
- $h_{friction}$ includes pipe friction, fittings, and entrance losses.

Rearranging for the absolute pressure at the impeller eye:

$$\frac{P_2}{\rho_L \, g} = \frac{P_1}{\rho_L \, g} - h_s - \frac{u_2^2}{2g} - h_{friction}$$

Now, the **NPSH available** is the margin between the absolute pressure at the impeller eye and the vapor pressure of the liquid at the operating temperature, expressed in head units:

$$\mathrm{NPSH}_a = \frac{P_2 - P_v}{\rho_L \, g}$$

Substituting the expression for $P_2 / (\rho_L \, g)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{NPSH}_a &= \frac{P_1 - P_v}{\rho_L \, g} - h_s - \frac{u_2^2}{2g} - h_{friction} \\
&= \left(\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho_L \, g} + \frac{P_{system}}{\rho_L \, g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho_L \, g}\right) - h_s - \frac{u_2^2}{2g} - h_{friction}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation of each term:**

- $\dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho_L \, g}$ → atmospheric pressure head available to push the liquid (≈ **10.33 m of water** at sea level).
- $\dfrac{P_{system}}{\rho_L \, g}$ → additional pressure if the suction tank is pressurized (e.g., nitrogen blanketed tank).
- $\dfrac{P_v}{\rho_L \, g}$ → vapor pressure head "subtracted" because if $P_2$ falls below $P_v$, the liquid will flash to vapor and the pump will cavitate.
- $h_s$ → static suction lift, *subtracted* (or *added* if the liquid is above the pump in a flooded suction).
- $\dfrac{u_2^2}{2g}$ → velocity head at the impeller eye, *subtracted* (kinetic energy demand).
- $h_{friction}$ → friction losses in suction piping and fittings, *subtracted*.

**Priming Implication:** If the suction line contains any air pocket, the effective $P_1$ at the impeller eye drops dramatically (air at low pressure), causing the pump to lose suction. **Priming ensures the suction line is a continuous liquid column**, restoring $P_1$ to the design value and guaranteeing $\mathrm{NPSH}_a \geq \mathrm{NPSH}_r + 0.5$.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Verifying Packing Wetting

**Problem Statement:**
A packed absorption column uses 25 mm ceramic Raschig rings. The liquid superficial velocity is $u_L = 0.002$ m/s, the liquid density $\rho_L = 1000$ kg/m³, viscosity $\mu_L = 0.001$ Pa·s, surface tension $\sigma_L = 0.072$ N/m, and the critical surface tension of the ceramic is $\sigma_c = 0.061$ N/m. The packing nominal size $d_p = 0.025$ m. Determine the **wetting efficiency** using Onda's correlation.

**Step 1: Compute the Liquid Reynolds Number.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Re}_L &= \frac{\rho_L \, u_L \, d_p}{\mu_L} \\
&= \frac{(1000)(0.002)(0.025)}{0.001} \\
&= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

The Reynolds number is low (laminar liquid flow regime, typical of absorption).

**Step 2: Compute the Froude Number.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Fr}_L &= \frac{u_L^2}{g \, d_p} \\
&= \frac{(0.002)^2}{(9.81)(0.025)} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{0.24525} \\
&= 1.63 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Froude number is very small, indicating a stable, non-flooded flow regime.

**Step 3: Compute the Weber Number.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{We}_L &= \frac{\rho_L \, u_L^2 \, d_p}{\sigma_L} \\
&= \frac{(1000)(0.002)^2(0.025)}{0.072} \\
&= \frac{(1000)(4 \times 10^{-6})(0.025)}{0.072} \\
&= \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{0.072} \\
&= 1.389 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Weber number is very small, indicating that surface tension forces dominate over inertial forces at the liquid surface.

**Step 4: Compute the Surface Tension Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_L} &= \frac{0.061}{0.072} \\
&= 0.847
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sigma_c < \sigma_L$, the liquid is **not** expected to perfectly wet the packing; partial wetting is anticipated.

**Step 5: Compute the Wetting Efficiency using Onda's Correlation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_w &= 1 - \exp\!\Big(-1.45 \cdot (0.847)^{0.75} \cdot (50)^{0.1} \cdot (1.63 \times 10^{-5})^{-0.05} \cdot (1.389 \times 10^{-3})^{0.2}\Big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sub-step 5.1: Compute the surface tension factor.**

$$(0.847)^{0.75} = 0.880$$

**Sub-step 5.2: Compute the Reynolds factor.**

$$(50)^{0.1} = 1.585$$

**Sub-step 5.3: Compute the inverse Froude factor (negative exponent).**

$$(1.63 \times 10^{-5})^{-0.05} = (1.63 \times 10^{-5})^{0.05} \cdot (\text{with sign flip})$$

We use the algebraic identity $x^{-0.05} = 1 / x^{0.05}$:

$$(1.63 \times 10^{-5})^{0.05} = \exp\!\big(0.05 \cdot \ln(1.63 \times 10^{-5})\big)$$

$$\ln(1.63 \times 10^{-5}) = -11.02$$

$$(1.63 \times 10^{-5})^{0.05} = \exp(-0.551) = 0.576$$

Therefore:

$$(1.63 \times 10^{-5})^{-0.05} = \frac{1}{0.576} = 1.736$$

**Sub-step 5.4: Compute the Weber factor.**

$$(1.389 \times 10^{-3})^{0.2} = \exp\!\big(0.2 \cdot \ln(1.389 \times 10^{-3})\big)$$

$$\ln(1.389 \times 10^{-3}) = -6.578$$

$$(1.389 \times 10^{-3})^{0.2} = \exp(-1.316) = 0.268$$

**Sub-step 5.5: Multiply all factors inside the exponent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exponent product} &= 1.45 \times 0.880 \times 1.585 \times 1.736 \times 0.268 \\
&= 1.45 \times 0.880 \times 1.585 \times 1.736 \times 0.268
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplication chain:

- $1.45 \times 0.880 = 1.276$
- $1.276 \times 1.585 = 2.022$
- $2.022 \times 1.736 = 3.510$
- $3.510 \times 0.268 = 0.941$

**Sub-step 5.6: Compute the wetting efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_w &= 1 - \exp(-0.941) \\
&= 1 - 0.390 \\
&= 0.610
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result and Engineering Interpretation:**

The wetting efficiency is approximately **0.610 (61%)**. This means only 61% of the packing surface is effective for mass transfer; the rest is dry. This is **inadequate** for a properly primed column.

**Remedy — Priming Action:** To increase $\eta_w$ to a target value of $\eta_w = 0.9$, the column must be primed by:
- Increasing $u_L$ (higher liquid irrigation rate).
- Switching to a more wettable packing material (e.g., metal or plastic with $\sigma_c > \sigma_L$).
- Extending the pre-wetting time to allow complete film formation in the interstices.

**If we re-calculate with $u_L = 0.005$ m/s (after priming):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Re}_L &= \frac{(1000)(0.005)(0.025)}{0.001} = 125 \\
\mathrm{Fr}_L &= \frac{(0.005)^2}{(9.81)(0.025)} = 1.02 \times 10^{-4} \\
\mathrm{We}_L &= \frac{(1000)(0.005)^2(0.025)}{0.072} = 8.68 \times 10^{-3} \\
(\sigma_c/\sigma_L)^{0.75} &= 0.880 \\
\mathrm{Re}_L^{0.1} &= (125)^{0.1} = 1.747 \\
\mathrm{Fr}_L^{-0.05} &= (1.02 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.05} = 1/(1.02 \times 10^{-4})^{0.05}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\ln(1.02 \times 10^{-4}) = -9.19 \Rightarrow (1.02 \times 10^{-4})^{0.05} = \exp(-0.4595) = 0.632$$

$$\mathrm{Fr}_L^{-0.05} = 1/0.632 = 1.582$$

$$\mathrm{We}_L^{0.2} = (8.68 \times 10^{-3})^{0.2} = \exp(0.2 \cdot \ln(8.68 \times 10^{-3}))$$

$$\ln(8.68 \times 10^{-3}) = -4.747 \Rightarrow (8.68 \times 10^{-3})^{0.2} = \exp(-0.949) = 0.387$$

**Exponent product:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.45 \times 0.880 \times 1.747 \times 1.582 \times 0.387 &= 1.276 \times 1.747 \times 1.582 \times 0.387 \\
&= 2.229 \times 1.582 \times 0.387 \\
&= 3.526 \times 0.387 \\
&= 1.365
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\eta_w = 1 - \exp(-1.365) = 1 - 0.255 = 0.745$$

Even at 2.5× the liquid rate, $\eta_w = 0.745$. To reach $\eta_w \geq 0.9$, a more thorough priming (e.g., use of a pre-soak with surfactant, or a wettable packing material) is necessary.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Priming Procedure for a Centrifugal Pump in a Mass Transfer Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pump is OFF and DRY] --> B[Close Discharge Valve]
    B --> C[Open Air Vent Valve on Casing]
    C --> D[Open Priming Port on Pump Casing]
    D --> E[Connect Priming Tank or Vacuum Source]
    E --> F[Inject Priming Liquid into Casing]
    F --> G{Air Fully Displaced?}
    G -->|No| F
    G -->|Yes| H[Liquid Stream Visible at Air Vent]
    H --> I[Close Air Vent Valve]
    I --> J[Close Priming Port]
    J --> K[Check Suction Line is Full of Liquid]
    K --> L{Check NPSH Available vs Required}
    L -->|NPSHa < NPSHr + 0.5| M[STOP - Re-prime and Check Suction Line]
    M --> F
    L -->|NPSHa >= NPSHr + 0.5| N[Start Pump Motor]
    N --> O[Gradually Open Discharge Valve]
    O --> P[Reach Design Flow Rate Q_design]
    P --> Q[END: Pump Primed and Operational]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Priming Procedure for a Packed Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New or Refilled Packed Column] --> B[Inspect Packing for Damage and Foreign Material]
    B --> C[Check Liquid Distributor for Levelness]
    C --> D[Fill Column Sump with Process Solvent]
    D --> E[Start Solvent Circulation Pump]
    E --> F[Circulate Liquid at 50 to 100 Percent of Design Liquid Rate]
    F --> G{Maintain Circulation for 24 to 48 Hours}
    G --> G
    G --> H[Inspect Top Distributor for Uniform Liquid Distribution]
    H --> I{Maldistribution Detected?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Adjust Distributor and Re-level]
    J --> F
    I -->|No| K[Verify Wetting Rate Exceeds Critical Wetting Rate]
    K --> L{Wetting Rate Adequate?}
    L -->|No| M[Increase Liquid Rate and Extend Pre-wet Time]
    M --> F
    L -->|Yes| N[Begin Slow Gas Introduction Over 15 to 30 Minutes]
    N --> O[Monitor Pressure Drop and Avoid Flooding]
    O --> P[Establish Design Gas and Liquid Flow Rates]
    P --> Q[END: Column Primed and Ready for Mass Transfer Operation]
```

## 4.3 Component-Level Architecture: Role of Priming in a Mass Transfer System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUMP[CENTRIFUGAL PUMP SUBSYSTEM]
    A1[Suction Tank] --> A2[Suction Line]
    A2 --> A3[Pump Casing]
    A3 --> A4[Discharge Line]
    A3 -. Priming Fluid .-> A5[Priming Tank]
    A5 -. Air Vent .-> A6[Atmosphere]
    end

    subgraph COLUMN[PACKED COLUMN SUBSYSTEM]
    B1[Liquid Distributor] --> B2[Packing Section]
    B2 --> B3[Support Plate]
    B3 --> B4[Column Sump]
    B4 --> B5[Recycle Pump]
    B5 --> B1
    B6[Gas Inlet] --> B2
    B2 --> B7[Gas Outlet]
    end

    A4 --> B1
    A4 --> B5

    C1[Priming Enables: Continuous Liquid Column] -.Controls.-> A2
    C2[Priming Enables: Full Packing Wetting] -.Controls.-> B2
    C1 --> C2
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define priming. Why is it important in centrifugal pumps? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Priming is the pre-operational procedure of completely filling the suction line and impeller casing of a centrifugal pump with a liquid (called the priming liquid) to displace all air, vapor, and gas pockets. 

It is important because:
1. A centrifugal pump can only push liquid; it cannot create suction on air (air is compressible).
2. Priming ensures a continuous liquid column on the suction side, which allows atmospheric pressure (or system pressure) to push the liquid into the impeller eye.
3. Without priming, the pump undergoes **air binding** or **vapor lock**, leading to loss of suction, motor overload, and complete failure to deliver liquid.
4. Priming guarantees that the **Net Positive Suction Head Available (NPSHₐ) ≥ Net Positive Suction Head Required (NPSHᵣ) + 0.5 m**, avoiding cavitation.

### Question 2: What is meant by priming of a packed column? Mention the typical pre-wetting time. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

Priming of a packed column is the pre-operational procedure of pre-wetting the dry packing material by circulating the process liquid through the column for an extended duration (typically **24 to 48 hours**) before introducing the gas phase.

Its purpose is to ensure the formation of a stable liquid film on the entire packing surface, so that the **wetting efficiency $\eta_w$** approaches unity. This makes the **effective interfacial area $a_e$** equal to the **total packing surface area $a_t$**, as assumed in design correlations such as Onda's correlation. Without priming, $\eta_w$ is low, leading to under-designed columns with poor mass transfer performance.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note for Students:** KTU End-Semester Examinations under the 2024 Scheme offer internal choice between two full 14-mark questions. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternative sets.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure and importance of priming a centrifugal pump used in a mass transfer operation. State the conditions that must be satisfied before starting the pump. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump is to deliver water at 30°C from an open reservoir to a distillation column overhead condenser. The following data are given: static suction lift $h_s = 3.5$ m, suction pipe velocity head $u_2^2 / (2g) = 0.4$ m, friction loss in suction line $h_f = 0.8$ m, atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 10.33$ m of water, vapor pressure of water at 30°C $P_v / (\rho g) = 0.43$ m of water. The manufacturer's NPSH required is $\mathrm{NPSH}_r = 1.8$ m. Determine if the pump is safely primed. If not, suggest two practical remedies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Procedure for Priming a Centrifugal Pump:**
   - Close the discharge valve.
   - Open the air vent valve at the top of the pump casing.
   - Open the priming port.
   - Introduce priming liquid (water or process fluid) from a priming tank or ejector until the entire casing and suction line are full.
   - Continue introducing liquid until a steady stream flows from the air vent (indicating all air is displaced).
   - Close the air vent valve, then close the priming port.
   - Verify the suction line is full by checking the gauge at the suction flange.

2. **Importance in Mass Transfer Operations:**
   - Establishes a continuous liquid column on the suction side.
   - Prevents **cavitation** and **air binding**.
   - Ensures $\mathrm{NPSH}_a \geq \mathrm{NPSH}_r + 0.5$ m.
   - Protects the pump impeller from dry running, which can cause seal damage and overheating.
   - Critical for steady flow rates to the column, which directly affects mass transfer coefficient and product purity.

3. **Conditions Before Starting the Pump:**
   - Suction line and casing completely filled with liquid.
   - Air vent closed; priming port closed.
   - Bearing lubrication confirmed; coupling guard in place.
   - Discharge valve closed; suction valve open.
   - Direction of rotation verified (correct coupling).
   - **NPSH available verified to be ≥ NPSH required + safety margin.**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Apply the NPSH Available Equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{NPSH}_a &= \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho_L \, g} - h_s - \frac{u_2^2}{2g} - h_{friction} \\
&= 10.33 - 0.43 - 3.5 - 0.4 - 0.8 \\
&= 5.20 \text{ m of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with NPSH Required and Safety Margin.**

$$\mathrm{NPSH}_{r} + 0.5 = 1.8 + 0.5 = 2.3 \text{ m}$$

Since $\mathrm{NPSH}_a = 5.20$ m $> 2.3$ m, the pump is **safely primed** and the suction condition is acceptable.

**Step 3: Two Practical Remedies (in case the answer had been unsatisfactory, e.g., if NPSHₐ < 2.3 m):**

1. **Reduce static suction lift** by lowering the pump elevation relative to the reservoir (e.g., flood the suction by placing the pump below the reservoir level).
2. **Reduce friction losses** by enlarging the suction pipe diameter, shortening the suction line, or reducing the number of fittings (elbows, valves).
3. **Cool the liquid** to reduce vapor pressure $P_v$ (increases the numerator $P_{atm} - P_v$).
4. **Pressurize the suction tank** with an inert gas (e.g., nitrogen blanket) to increase $P_1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):** Many students forget the sign convention for $h_s$ and the velocity head term. The suction lift and velocity head are **subtracted**, not added. Marks are deducted when students write:
> $\mathrm{NPSH}_a = P_{atm}/\rho g + h_s + u^2/2g - P_v/\rho g - h_f$ (WRONG)
> Correct form: $\mathrm{NPSH}_a = (P_{atm} - P_v)/\rho g - h_s - u^2/2g - h_f$ (CORRECT)
> Also, the 0.5 m safety margin is **mandatory** for industrial pumps; failing to include it costs 0.5 marks.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the importance of priming a packed absorption column. How does the wetting efficiency depend on the pre-wetting procedure? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A packed column uses 1-inch metal Pall rings ($\sigma_c = 0.075$ N/m, $d_p = 0.025$ m, $a_t = 220$ m²/m³) for absorbing SO₂ from air using water. The liquid superficial velocity is $u_L = 0.0035$ m/s, $\rho_L = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu_L = 0.001$ Pa·s, $\sigma_L = 0.072$ N/m. The critical wetting rate (CWR) is 0.08 m³/h per m of wetted perimeter. Determine:
   (i) The liquid Reynolds, Froude, and Weber numbers.
   (ii) The wetting efficiency using Onda's correlation.
   (iii) Whether the column is adequately primed, and if not, suggest corrective action. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Importance of Priming a Packed Absorption Column:**

1. **Establishes Full Wetting:** Packing materials (especially ceramic and some plastics) have dry surface areas that are not immediately available for mass transfer. Pre-wetting via priming creates a liquid film that exposes the true interfacial area.

2. **Achieves Design $K_G a$:** The overall mass transfer coefficient $K_G a$ depends directly on the effective interfacial area $a_e$. Without priming, $a_e \ll a_t$, and the column will under-perform, often requiring a taller column to meet the design separation.

3. **Prevents Maldistribution:** Pre-wetting allows the operator to identify and correct maldistribution (e.g., tilted distributors, channeling) before gas flow begins. Maldistribution cannot be easily detected once the column is operational.

4. **Stabilizes the Hydrodynamic Regime:** The pre-wet film stabilizes the liquid hold-up and the flooding/entrainment behavior of the column, enabling the design gas and liquid flow rates to be achieved without instabilities.

5. **Reduces Start-up Time to Steady State:** A primed column reaches steady composition and temperature profiles much faster than an unprimed one, reducing the off-spec product time and saving energy.

**Dependence of Wetting Efficiency on Pre-Wetting Procedure:**

- **Duration:** Longer pre-wetting (24–48 hours) increases $\eta_w$ by allowing liquid to penetrate crevices and micro-pores.
- **Liquid Rate During Pre-wet:** Higher irrigation rate during priming pushes the film into more contact points, improving $\eta_w$.
- **Quality of Initial Distribution:** A well-leveled distributor is essential; otherwise, dry zones form and $\eta_w$ remains low.
- **Pre-wet Fluid Chemistry:** Use of a low-surface-tension fluid (e.g., aqueous surfactant solution) for the initial soak can dramatically increase $\eta_w$ on hard-to-wet packings.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Dimensionless Numbers:**

**Reynolds Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Re}_L &= \frac{\rho_L \, u_L \, d_p}{\mu_L} = \frac{(998)(0.0035)(0.025)}{0.001} \\
&= 87.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Froude Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Fr}_L &= \frac{u_L^2}{g \, d_p} = \frac{(0.0035)^2}{(9.81)(0.025)} \\
&= \frac{1.225 \times 10^{-5}}{0.24525} \\
&= 4.995 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Weber Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{We}_L &= \frac{\rho_L \, u_L^2 \, d_p}{\sigma_L} = \frac{(998)(0.0035)^2(0.025)}{0.072} \\
&= \frac{(998)(1.225 \times 10^{-5})(0.025)}{0.072} \\
&= \frac{3.056 \times 10^{-4}}{0.072} \\
&= 4.244 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Wetting Efficiency (Onda's Correlation):**

The Onda correlation is:

$$\eta_w = 1 - \exp\!\left(-1.45 \left(\frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_L}\right)^{0.75} \mathrm{Re}_L^{0.1} \mathrm{Fr}_L^{-0.05} \mathrm{We}_L^{0.2}\right)$$

**Compute each factor:**

- $\left(\dfrac{0.075}{0.072}\right)^{0.75} = (1.0417)^{0.75} = 1.0311$

- $\mathrm{Re}_L^{0.1} = (87.3)^{0.1} = \exp(0.1 \cdot \ln 87.3) = \exp(0.1 \cdot 4.469) = \exp(0.4469) = 1.563$

- $\mathrm{Fr}_L^{-0.05} = (4.995 \times 10^{-5})^{-0.05} = \dfrac{1}{(4.995 \times 10^{-5})^{0.05}}$

  - $\ln(4.995 \times 10^{-5}) = -9.904$
  - $(4.995 \times 10^{-5})^{0.05} = \exp(-0.4952) = 0.6095$
  - $\mathrm{Fr}_L^{-0.05} = 1/0.6095 = 1.641$

- $\mathrm{We}_L^{0.2} = (4.244 \times 10^{-3})^{0.2} = \exp(0.2 \cdot \ln(4.244 \times 10^{-3}))$
  - $\ln(4.244 \times 10^{-3}) = -5.464$
  - $\mathrm{We}_L^{0.2} = \exp(-1.093) = 0.335$

**Exponent product:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.45 \times 1.0311 \times 1.563 \times 1.641 \times 0.335
\end{aligned}
$$

Stepwise multiplication:

- $1.45 \times 1.0311 = 1.495$
- $1.495 \times 1.563 = 2.337$
- $2.337 \times 1.641 = 3.835$
- $3.835 \times 0.335 = 1.285$

**Therefore:**

$$\eta_w = 1 - \exp(-1.285) = 1 - 0.277 = 0.723 \quad (\text{or } 72.3\%)$$

**(iii) Priming Adequacy Check:**

The wetting efficiency is **0.723 (72.3%)**, which is **below the recommended design threshold of 0.85–0.90**. Hence, the column is **not adequately primed**.

**Check against the CWR:** The actual wetting rate must also be compared with the critical wetting rate of 0.08 m³/h per m of wetted perimeter. (Numerical verification can be done using the actual packing geometry; the present low $\eta_w$ already signals under-priming.)

**Corrective Actions:**

1. **Increase the liquid rate** during pre-wetting (e.g., to $u_L = 0.006$ m/s) to push the film into more contact points.
2. **Extend the pre-wetting duration** beyond 48 hours.
3. **Switch the packing material** to one with higher critical surface tension (e.g., oxidized metal or treated plastic with $\sigma_c > \sigma_L$).
4. **Pre-soak the packing with a low-surface-tension aqueous solution** (e.g., 0.1 wt% surfactant) to improve initial wetting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):** Three common mistakes cost students 1–2 marks each:
> 1. Forgetting the negative sign on the Froude exponent ($\mathrm{Fr}_L^{-0.05}$ means **inverse** of $\mathrm{Fr}_L^{0.05}$).
> 2. Using $g = 9.8$ instead of **9.81 m/s²**, causing cumulative rounding error.
> 3. Reporting the final $\eta_w$ as a percentage **or** as a fraction but inconsistently across sub-parts.
> Also, the examiner expects students to **comment on whether $\eta_w$ is acceptable**, not just calculate it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Priming**

- **Definition:** Priming = pre-operational conditioning of mass transfer equipment (pumps, columns) to ensure design performance.

- **Pump Priming:** Filling the suction line and casing with liquid to displace air; **mandatory** for centrifugal pumps (which cannot handle air).

- **Pump Priming Failure Mode:** Air binding / vapor lock → loss of suction → motor trip.

- **Critical Pump Equation:** $\mathrm{NPSH}_a = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho_L g} - h_s - \dfrac{u^2}{2g} - h_f \geq \mathrm{NPSH}_r + 0.5$

- **Packed Column Priming:** Circulating liquid through the packing for **24 to 48 hours** to wet the surface before gas introduction.

- **Wetting Efficiency Equation (Onda):**
  $$\eta_w = 1 - \exp\!\left(-1.45 \left(\frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_L}\right)^{0.75} \mathrm{Re}_L^{0.1} \mathrm{Fr}_L^{-0.05} \mathrm{We}_L^{0.2}\right)$$

- **Design Target:** $\eta_w \geq 0.85$ for most absorption/stripping applications.

- **Critical Wetting Rate (CWR):** ~ **0.08 m³/h per m of wetted perimeter** for random packing; the operating rate must exceed this.

- **Standard Sign Convention (NPSH):** Static suction lift $h_s$ is **subtracted**; velocity head **subtracted**; friction loss **subtracted**.

- **Maldistribution Detection:** Visual inspection of top distributor during priming; pressure-tap survey for confirmation.

- **Atmospheric Pressure Head (Sea Level):** **10.33 m of water**.

- **Vapor Pressure of Water at 30°C:** **0.43 m of water head** (≈ 4.25 kPa).

- **Packing Material Influence on Wetting:**
  - **Ceramic:** $\sigma_c \approx 0.061$ N/m — moderate wettability.
  - **Metal (oxidized):** $\sigma_c \approx 0.075$ N/m — high wettability.
  - **Plastic (PE/PP):** $\sigma_c \approx 0.033$ N/m — low wettability (requires surfactant pre-soak).

- **Common KTU Pitfalls:**
  1. Confusing priming with flooding.
  2. Confusing priming with normal operation.
  3. Forgetting the 0.5 m NPSH safety margin.
  4. Reporting $\eta_w$ as a percentage in one part and a fraction in another.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Coning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Coning** is a specific type of *liquid maldistribution* phenomenon that occurs in **packed columns** (gas-liquid contactors) used in absorption, stripping, and distillation operations. It refers to the formation of a **cone-shaped liquid flow pattern** at the point of liquid introduction onto a packed bed, where the liquid stream fails to spread uniformly across the entire cross-sectional area of the packing.

In coning, the liquid discharged from a distribution point spreads outward in a conical trajectory (analogous to water from a garden hose hitting a surface), creating a **wetted zone** directly beneath the distributor and leaving **dry, unwetted regions** of packing between adjacent distribution points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
> Coning is classified under **operating problems / maldistribution phenomena in packed towers**, directly impacting the **effective interfacial area (a_w)** available for mass transfer. It is a critical consideration in the mechanical design and rating of absorption columns.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine watering a rectangular garden lawn with a watering can that has only **three holes** at the bottom. When you pour water, each stream creates a small circular wet patch on the grass. If the holes are too far apart, the wet patches remain as **isolated circles**, leaving the grass between them completely dry. Now, if you replace those three holes with a **sprinkler head** that throws water in overlapping arcs, the entire lawn gets uniformly wet.

This is exactly what happens inside a packed column:
- The **packing surface** is like the grass (it must be wetted to perform mass transfer).
- The **liquid distributor** is like the watering can / sprinkler.
- **Coning** is the situation where the "wet circles" from individual distribution points **do not overlap**, leaving dry zones of packing that contribute almost nothing to mass transfer.

## 1.3 Physical Significance and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| **Minimum liquid distribution density** (random packings) | **30 to 50 drip points / m²** |
| **Minimum liquid distribution density** (structured packings) | **100 to 200 drip points / m²** |
| **Minimum wetting rate (MWR)** for ceramic packings | **0.5 m³/(m·h)** |
| **Minimum wetting rate (MWR)** for metallic/plastic packings | **2.0 m³/(m·h)** |
| **Cone half-angle (β)** (theoretical, gravity-driven) | **≈ 30° to 45°** from vertical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Coning is *not* caused by the packing itself — it is caused by **inadequate liquid distribution** at the top of the bed. Even the best packing in the world cannot compensate for poor initial distribution.

## 1.4 Classification of Coning

Coning can be sub-classified based on severity and location:

1. **Primary Coning** – Occurs at the top of the bed, immediately below the liquid distributor.
2. **Secondary Coning** – Develops within the bed when local flow instabilities cause liquid streams to re-converge into cone patterns at intermediate heights.
3. **Inverted Coning** – Less common; liquid flows preferentially to the *center* of the column (often due to wall effects or packing geometry).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Coning — Step-by-Step Logic

The formation of a cone is governed by three competing physical forces acting on a liquid stream leaving a distributor orifice:

- **Step 1: Liquid Jet Discharge**
  A liquid stream of volumetric flow rate $q_L$ exits a distributor orifice of diameter $d_o$ with velocity $v_o$. The jet possesses downward momentum and begins to fall under gravity.

- **Step 2: Radial Spreading on Packing Surface**
  On impacting the top layer of packing, the jet's downward momentum is converted into **radial (horizontal) spreading** along the packing surface. The liquid wets a roughly circular area, the radius of which grows with the square root of the distance from the impact point.

- **Step 3: Cone Formation by Wetting-Front Propagation**
  As more liquid accumulates, the wetted front propagates outward in all radial directions. The wetted zone takes the shape of an inverted cone (apex at the distributor point, base on the packing). The half-angle of this cone depends on the **liquid surface tension**, **packing wettability**, and **local flow rate**.

- **Step 4: Failure of Cone Overlap (Coning Defect)**
  If the spacing between adjacent distributor points is greater than the diameter of the wetted cone at the top of the bed, **dry zones** appear between the cones. The packing in these dry zones does not participate in mass transfer, effectively reducing the **wetted surface area**.

- **Step 5: Steady-State Maldistribution**
  Once steady state is reached, the column operates with a non-uniform liquid distribution profile, leading to:
