✅ **Safety hierarchy** — Flash point > TLV > environmental impact > cost (in decreasing priority for safety screening)

✅ **Common mistakes** — Don't confuse K_D with β; always use consistent mass or mole basis; tabulate numerical comparisons

✅ **KTU favorites** — Robbins chart construction, derivation of β, ternary diagram interpretation, numerical comparison of two solvents, calculation of S/F for a specified recovery

✅ **Industrial examples** — TBP for U extraction, sulfolane for aromatics, MIBK for acetone, supercritical CO₂ for caffeine
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Balance in Counter-Current and Co-Current Absorption and Stripping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Balance in Counter-Current and Co-Current Absorption and Stripping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Material Balance in Counter-Current and Co-Current Absorption and Stripping

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

**Absorption** is a mass transfer operation in which one or more soluble components (solutes) of a **gas mixture** are selectively dissolved into a **liquid solvent**. The **gas** from which the solute is removed is called the **gas carrier** or **inert gas**, the **liquid** that absorbs the solute is called the **solvent** or **absorbent**, and the component being transferred is called the **solute** or **absorbate**.

**Stripping** (or **desorption**) is the reverse operation: a dissolved solute is removed from a **liquid phase** by contacting it with a **gas phase** in which the solute concentration is low. The role of the phases is essentially inverted compared to absorption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module 2:**
> The material balance for both **counter-current** and **co-current (parallel)** flow of gas and liquid streams must be derived for both **absorption** and **stripping** operations, together with the corresponding **operating line equations**.

**Counter-current flow**: The gas and liquid streams flow in **opposite directions** inside the tower (packed or tray).

**Co-current (concurrent) flow**: Both gas and liquid streams flow in the **same direction**, usually **downwards** from the top.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tall, narrow vertical **staircase** (the absorber column). People (the solute molecules) start at the bottom crowded onto an escalator (the **rich gas** stream, moving upward). A friendly guide (the **solvent** stream) walks down the staircase from the top, scooping up the people as they pass each other. Because the guide and the people are moving in **opposite directions (counter-current)**, the guide meets the most crowded escalator first, and exits at the bottom having collected the maximum number of people. The escalator exits at the top almost empty.

If instead both the guide and the escalator moved **downwards together (co-current)**, the guide would only meet the crowded escalator briefly at the very top, would scoop up very few people, and would leave the staircase together with a still-crowded escalator. **Counter-current is far more efficient for transfer than co-current.**

For **stripping**, imagine washing sticky candy residue (solute) off a handkerchief (liquid) by holding it under a high-velocity air jet (gas). The air picks up the residue and carries it away — the gas here is doing the *stripping* work.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Mole fraction, $y$**: moles of solute per mole of gas mixture (dimensionless, **mol/mol**).
- **Mole fraction, $x$**: moles of solute per mole of liquid mixture (dimensionless, **mol/mol**).
- **Molar flow rate of gas (inert-free basis), $G$**: **kmol/s** (or **kmol/h**).
- **Molar flow rate of liquid (inert-free basis), $L$**: **kmol/s** (or **kmol/h**).
- **Gas velocity, $G_y$** and **Liquid velocity, $L_x$**: often expressed in **kmol inert/s**.
- **Universal Gas Constant, $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board problems, the **inert (solvent)-free basis** is almost always preferred because the inerts (carrier gas, solvent) pass through the tower unchanged — this simplifies material balances considerably.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Material Balance Setup

Consider a steady-state absorber of cross-section $S$ with the following notation:

- $G$ = molar flow rate of **inert (carrier) gas**, **kmol/s** (constant through the tower).
- $L$ = molar flow rate of **inert (solvent) liquid**, **kmol/s** (constant through the tower).
- $Y$ = moles of solute **per mole of inert gas** (gas concentration on solvent-free basis).
- $X$ = moles of solute **per mole of inert liquid** (liquid concentration on solute-free basis).
- Subscript **1** → **rich end** (concentrated end of gas).
- Subscript **2** → **lean end** (concentrated end of liquid).

### 2.2 Material Balance Across an Elemental Section

For a differential slice of height $dZ$ in the tower, with transfer rate $N_A$ (kmol solute/s):

- Solute entering the slice in the gas = $G(Y+dY)$.
- Solute entering the slice in the liquid = $L(X+dX)$.
- Solute leaving the slice in the gas = $G \cdot Y$.
- Solute leaving the slice in the liquid = $L \cdot X$.

At **steady state**, the total moles of solute entering equals the total moles leaving, plus the net transfer. Integration from the bottom to any height gives the **overall operating line equation**.

### 2.3 Counter-Current Absorption – Operating Line Derivation

For **counter-current** flow, gas enters at the **bottom** with composition $Y_1$ and leaves at the **top** with composition $Y_2$. Liquid enters at the **top** with composition $X_2$ and leaves at the **bottom** with composition $X_1$.

The **overall material balance** for the solute across the entire tower:

$$G \, Y_1 + L \, X_2 = G \, Y_2 + L \, X_1$$

Rearranging:

$$L \, (X_1 - X_2) = G \, (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

For the section of the tower **above** any plane (i.e., from the top down to that plane), assuming $L$ and $G$ are constant:

$$G \, Y + L \, X_2 = G \, Y_2 + L \, X$$

Solving for $Y$:

$$Y = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) X + \left(Y_2 - \frac{L}{G} X_2\right)$$

This is the **operating line equation for counter-current absorption**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The operating line for counter-current absorption is **straight** (because $L$ and $G$ are constants on an inert-free basis) and has a **positive slope** $= L/G$. It always passes through the point $(X_2, Y_2)$ at the top and $(X_1, Y_1)$ at the bottom.

### 2.4 Co-Current (Concurrent) Absorption – Operating Line Derivation

In **co-current flow**, both gas and liquid enter from the **top** and leave from the **bottom**. Subscripts:
- Gas: $Y_1$ (entering top), $Y_2$ (leaving bottom).
- Liquid: $X_1$ (entering top), $X_2$ (leaving bottom).

**Overall material balance**:

$$G \, Y_1 + L \, X_1 = G \, Y_2 + L \, X_2$$

Rearranging:

$$L \, (X_1 - X_2) = G \, (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

For the section of the tower **below** any plane (i.e., from the top down to that plane), the material balance gives:

$$G \, Y + L \, X = G \, Y_1 + L \, X_1$$

Solving for $Y$:

$$Y = Y_1 + \frac{L}{G} (X_1 - X) = -\left(\frac{L}{G}\right) X + \left(Y_1 + \frac{L}{G} X_1\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The operating line for co-current absorption has a **negative slope** $= -L/G$. It connects the two terminal points $(X_1, Y_1)$ and $(X_2, Y_2)$, but unlike the counter-current case, the line lies on **one side only** of the equilibrium curve and driving force diminishes monotonically.

### 2.5 Stripping – Operating Line Derivation

In **stripping**, the solute moves **from liquid to gas**. By convention for stripping towers:

- **Lean liquid** enters at the **top** with composition $X_2$.
- **Rich liquid** enters at the **bottom** with composition $X_1$.
- **Rich gas** leaves at the **top** with composition $Y_1$.
- **Lean gas** enters at the **bottom** with composition $Y_2$.

**Overall material balance**:

$$L \, X_1 + G \, Y_2 = L \, X_2 + G \, Y_1$$

$$L \, (X_1 - X_2) = G \, (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

**Operating line** (counter-current stripping, derived by balancing above any plane):

$$Y = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) X + \left(Y_2 - \frac{L}{G} X_2\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Stripping and counter-current absorption share the **same form** of operating line because both operate counter-currently. The difference lies only in the **direction of mass transfer** (relative position of operating line vs. equilibrium curve).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Operation | Flow Pattern | Operating Line | Slope | Material Balance | Terminal Relation |
|---|-----------|--------------|----------------|-------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Absorption | Counter-current | $Y = \dfrac{L}{G}X + \left(Y_2 - \dfrac{L}{G}X_2\right)$ | $+L/G$ | $L(X_1 - X_2) = G(Y_1 - Y_2)$ | Passes through $(X_2,Y_2)$ & $(X_1,Y_1)$ |
| 2 | Absorption | Co-current | $Y = -\dfrac{L}{G}X + \left(Y_1 + \dfrac{L}{G}X_1\right)$ | $-L/G$ | $L(X_1 - X_2) = G(Y_1 - Y_2)$ | Passes through $(X_1,Y_1)$ & $(X_2,Y_2)$ |
| 3 | Stripping | Counter-current | $Y = \dfrac{L}{G}X + \left(Y_2 - \dfrac{L}{G}X_2\right)$ | $+L/G$ | $L(X_1 - X_2) = G(Y_1 - Y_2)$ | Passes through $(X_2,Y_2)$ & $(X_1,Y_1)$ |
| 4 | Stripping | Co-current | $Y = -\dfrac{L}{G}X + \left(Y_1 + \dfrac{L}{G}X_1\right)$ | $-L/G$ | $L(X_1 - X_2) = G(Y_1 - Y_2)$ | Passes through $(X_1,Y_1)$ & $(X_2,Y_2)$ |

| Concept | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---------|--------|------|--------------|
| Mole ratio (gas) | $Y$ | kmol solute / kmol inert | Solute concentration in gas |
| Mole ratio (liquid) | $X$ | kmol solute / kmol inert | Solute concentration in liquid |
| Liquid flow rate | $L$ | kmol/s (inert) | Constant through tower |
| Gas flow rate | $G$ | kmol/s (inert) | Constant through tower |
| Slope of operating line | $L/G$ | dimensionless | Determines driving force profile |
| Minimum solvent ratio | $(L/G)_{\min}$ | dimensionless | For absorption — operating line tangent to equilibrium |
| Minimum gas ratio | $(G/L)_{\min}$ | dimensionless | For stripping — operating line tangent to equilibrium |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Natural gas sweetening**: counter-current absorption of $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ and $\text{CO}_2$ from methane using **MDEA** (methyldiethanolamine) in tall packed columns.
- **Ammonia synthesis**: stripping of dissolved $\text{NH}_3$ from the absorber liquid by steam in a stripper.
- **$\text{CO}_2$ capture from flue gas**: counter-current absorption using **monoethanolamine (MEA)** solvents.
- **Petroleum refining**: steam stripping of volatile hydrocarbons from crude oil fractions.
- **Pharmaceutical industry**: co-current absorption is occasionally used for **highly exothermic** systems to manage temperature rise by reducing contact time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Counter-Current Absorption Operating Line

Consider a counter-current packed tower of cross-section $S$ and total height $Z$. The gas enters at the bottom with flow rate $G$ (kmol inert/s) and solute mole ratio $Y_1$. The liquid enters at the top with flow rate $L$ (kmol inert/s) and solute mole ratio $X_2$.

### Step 1 — Define the Elemental Slice

Take a horizontal slice of thickness $dZ$ at height $z$ measured from the bottom. Across this slice:

- Gas enters with composition $Y+dY$ and leaves with composition $Y$.
- Liquid enters with composition $X$ and leaves with composition $X+dX$.

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Solute Balance

At steady state, the net solute brought in by the gas equals the net solute taken away by the liquid:

$$G \cdot dY = -L \cdot dX$$

(The negative sign appears because as $Y$ decreases upward, $X$ increases upward; but here we measure $Y$ going up and $X$ going down — careful sign handling.)

Equivalently:

$$G \cdot (dY) = -L \cdot (dX)$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Across the Tower

Integrate from the bottom (where $Y = Y_1$, $X = X_1$) to any height $z$ (where composition is $Y$, $X$):

$$\int_{Y_1}^{Y} G \, dY = -\int_{X_1}^{X} L \, dX$$

Since $G$ and $L$ are constants (inert basis):

$$G (Y - Y_1) = -L (X - X_1) = L (X_1 - X)$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to the Operating Line Form

$$G Y + L X = G Y_1 + L X_1$$

Solving for $Y$:

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} X + \left( Y_1 - \frac{L}{G} X_1 \right)$$

This is the same equation seen earlier. It represents a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $\dfrac{L}{G}$
- **$Y$-intercept** = $Y_1 - \dfrac{L}{G} X_1$
- Passes through $(X_1, Y_1)$ at the bottom and $(X_2, Y_2)$ at the top.

> [!NOTE]
> The signs here assume $Y$ decreases as we move **up** the tower (gas loses solute), while $X$ decreases as we move **down** (liquid picks up solute) — this is the correct physical picture for counter-current **absorption**.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Co-Current Absorption Operating Line

In co-current flow, both phases move from top to bottom.

### Step 1 — Mass Balance Across Elemental Slice

Take $dZ$ at height $z$ from the bottom. In co-current flow, as $z$ decreases (moving upward), both $Y$ and $X$ **decrease** (solute is being absorbed):

$$G \cdot dY = L \cdot dX$$

### Step 2 — Integrate

Integrate from the bottom (where $Y = Y_2$, $X = X_2$) up to the top (where $Y = Y_1$, $X = X_1$):

$$\int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} G \, dY = \int_{X_2}^{X_1} L \, dX$$

$$G (Y_1 - Y_2) = L (X_1 - X_2)$$

### Step 3 — Material Balance Above Any Cross-Section

For the section **above** any cross-section (i.e., from the top down to that cross-section), the solute entering in the gas and the liquid must equal the solute leaving:

$$G Y_1 + L X_1 = G Y + L X$$

Solving for $Y$:

$$Y = -\frac{L}{G} X + \left( Y_1 + \frac{L}{G} X_1 \right)$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Geometric Properties

- **Slope** = $-\dfrac{L}{G}$ (negative because $Y$ decreases as $X$ increases when descending the column).
- **$Y$-intercept** = $Y_1 + \dfrac{L}{G} X_1$
- Passes through $(X_1, Y_1)$ at the top and $(X_2, Y_2)$ at the bottom.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Counter-Current Stripping Operating Line

For stripping, the solute moves **from liquid to gas**.

### Step 1 — Configuration

- Liquid enters at the **top** with $X_2$ (lean) and exits at the **bottom** with $X_1$ (rich).
- Gas enters at the **bottom** with $Y_2$ (lean) and exits at the **top** with $Y_1$ (rich).

### Step 2 — Overall Material Balance

$$L X_2 + G Y_2 = L X_1 + G Y_1$$

Rearranging:

$$L (X_1 - X_2) = G (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

### Step 3 — Material Balance Above Any Cross-Section

For the section from the top of the tower to any height $z$:

- Solute entering in liquid = $L X_2$.
- Solute entering in gas = $G Y$.
- Solute leaving in liquid = $L X$.
- Solute leaving in gas = $G Y_2$.

Balance:

$$L X_2 + G Y = L X + G Y_2$$

Solving for $Y$:

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} X + \left( Y_2 - \frac{L}{G} X_2 \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **algebraic form** of the operating line for counter-current stripping is **identical** to that of counter-current absorption. The difference appears only in how it is **plotted against the equilibrium curve** (stripping operating line lies *above* the equilibrium curve, while absorption operating line lies *below* it).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Counter-Current Absorption | Co-Current Absorption | Counter-Current Stripping |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Driving force at top | $Y_2 - Y_2^* > 0$ | Decreases monotonically | $X_2^* - X_2 > 0$ |
| Driving force at bottom | $Y_1 - Y_1^* > 0$ | Decreases monotonically | $X_1^* - X_1 > 0$ |
| Operating line slope | $+L/G$ | $-L/G$ | $+L/G$ |
| Position vs. equilibrium | Below equilibrium curve | Below equilibrium curve | Above equilibrium curve |
| Limiting condition | $(L/G)_{\min}$ tangent to equilibrium | Always finite transfer | $(G/L)_{\min}$ tangent to equilibrium |
| Efficiency | **Highest** | **Lowest** | **Highest** |
| Typical use | Most industrial absorbers | Highly exothermic systems | Industrial strippers |

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example (Derivation of Minimum Solvent Ratio)

For a counter-current absorber, the minimum solvent flow rate $L_{\min}$ occurs when the operating line is **tangent** to the equilibrium curve at some point $(X^*, Y^*)$.

### Step 1 — Slope Condition

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$$

where $X_1^*$ is the liquid concentration in equilibrium with $Y_1$ at the bottom.

### Step 2 — Solve for $L_{\min}$

$$L_{\min} = G \cdot \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$$

### Step 3 — Actual Operating Solvent Rate

Typically, the actual liquid rate is chosen as:

$$L = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot L_{\min}$$

to provide a reasonable safety margin and an adequate driving force throughout the tower.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU board mistake is forgetting that $(L/G)_{\min}$ depends on $X_1^*$ — the **equilibrium** value of $X$ corresponding to $Y_1$. The equilibrium relationship $Y^* = m X$ (linear) or the full curve $Y^*(X)$ (non-linear) must always be consulted.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Counter-Current Absorption Tower — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GasInA[Rich Gas Inlet: G, Y1] --> AbsorberTowerA[Counter-Current Absorber Tower]
    AbsorberTowerA --> GasOutA[Lean Gas Outlet: G, Y2]
    SolventInA[Lean Solvent Inlet: L, X2] --> AbsorberTowerA
    AbsorberTowerA --> LiquidOutA[Rich Liquid Outlet: L, X1]
    AbsorberTowerA --> MassTransferA[Solute Transfer: Gas to Liquid]
    OpLineA[Operating Line: Slope +L/G] --> AbsorberTowerA
    EqCurveA[Equilibrium Curve Y* vs X] --> AbsorberTowerA
    AbsorberTowerA --> DrivingForceA[Driving Force: Y - Y*]
```

**Block Functionality:**

1. **GasInA / LiquidOutA** are streams converging at the **bottom** of the tower.
2. **SolventInA / GasOutA** are streams converging at the **top** of the tower.
3. **OpLineA** has a positive slope and lies below the **EqCurveA**.
4. **DrivingForceA** decreases monotonically from bottom to top in absorption.

## 4.2 Co-Current Absorption Tower — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GasInB[Rich Gas Inlet: G, Y1] --> AbsorberTowerB[Co-Current Absorber Tower]
    SolventInB[Lean Solvent Inlet: L, X1] --> AbsorberTowerB
    AbsorberTowerB --> GasOutB[Lean Gas Outlet: G, Y2]
    AbsorberTowerB --> LiquidOutB[Rich Liquid Outlet: L, X2]
    AbsorberTowerB --> MassTransferB[Solute Transfer: Gas to Liquid]
    OpLineB[Operating Line: Slope -L/G] --> AbsorberTowerB
    EqCurveB[Equilibrium Curve Y* vs X] --> AbsorberTowerB
    AbsorberTowerB --> DrivingForceB[Driving Force: Shrinking with Height]
```

**Block Functionality:**

1. Both streams enter at the **top** and leave at the **bottom**.
2. The operating line has **negative slope**, crossing the equilibrium region only once.
3. Effective mass transfer only occurs in a small initial zone — the rest of the tower is **under-utilised**.

## 4.3 Counter-Current Stripping Tower — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LiquidInC[Rich Liquid Inlet: L, X1] --> StripperTowerC[Counter-Current Stripper Tower]
    GasInC[Lean Gas Inlet: G, Y2] --> StripperTowerC
    StripperTowerC --> LiquidOutC[Lean Liquid Outlet: L, X2]
    StripperTowerC --> GasOutC[Rich Gas Outlet: G, Y1]
    StripperTowerC --> MassTransferC[Solute Transfer: Liquid to Gas]
    OpLineC[Operating Line: Slope +L/G] --> StripperTowerC
    EqCurveC[Equilibrium Curve Y* vs X] --> StripperTowerC
    StripperTowerC --> DrivingForceC[Driving Force: X - X*]
```

**Block Functionality:**

1. **LiquidInC** enters at the **top** with high solute, leaves at the **bottom** lean.
2. **GasInC** enters at the **bottom** lean, leaves at the **top** enriched.
3. The operating line has positive slope and lies **above** the equilibrium curve.
4. Driving force $(X - X^*)$ decreases from bottom to top.

## 4.4 Comparative Flow Architecture — Master Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Counter-Current Absorption] --> B[Op Line: +L/G, Below Eq Curve]
    C[Co-Current Absorption] --> D[Op Line: -L/G, Below Eq Curve]
    E[Counter-Current Stripping] --> F[Op Line: +L/G, Above Eq Curve]
    G[Co-Current Stripping] --> H[Op Line: -L/G, Above Eq Curve]
    B --> I[Highest Efficiency]
    D --> J[Lowest Efficiency]
    F --> I
    H --> J
    I --> K[Industrial Standard]
    J --> L[Special Cases Only]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**: State the material balance equation for a counter-current absorption tower using inert-free mole ratios. Define each term clearly.

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

For a counter-current absorption tower, the overall material balance on the solute is:

$$L (X_1 - X_2) = G (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

where:
- $L$ = molar flow rate of **inert solvent** (kmol/s).
- $G$ = molar flow rate of **inert carrier gas** (kmol/s).
- $X_1$, $X_2$ = mole ratios of solute in liquid at the **bottom** and **top** (kmol solute/kmol solvent).
- $Y_1$, $Y_2$ = mole ratios of solute in gas at the **bottom** and **top** (kmol solute/kmol gas).

**Significance**: This equation states that the solute picked up by the liquid equals the solute lost by the gas — the foundation for drawing the operating line.

### Question 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**: Compare counter-current and co-current absorption with respect to operating line slope and driving force profile.

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | Counter-Current | Co-Current |
|--------|-----------------|------------|
| Operating line slope | $+L/G$ | $-L/G$ |
| Driving force profile | Positive throughout, decreases monotonically | Decreases monotonically to zero |
| Maximum transfer | Unbounded by equilibrium line at both ends | Capped at one end where line meets equilibrium |
| Industrial preference | **Standard** | Rarely used |

**Key takeaway**: Counter-current operation maintains a **positive driving force** at both ends of the tower, ensuring continuous mass transfer, whereas co-current transfer becomes limited as the operating and equilibrium lines converge.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Counter-Current Absorption Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q3(a)]**:

**(a)** A gas mixture containing **15 mol% $\text{NH}_3$** and **85 mol% air** is to be scrubbed with **pure water** in a counter-current packed tower to recover **90%** of the ammonia. The gas flow rate is **1500 kmol/h**. The equilibrium relation is given by $Y^* = 0.8 X$ (where $Y^*$ is in mol $\text{NH}_3$/mol air and $X$ is in mol $\text{NH}_3$/mol water). Calculate the **minimum solvent flow rate** in kmol/h of water.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Convert mole fractions to mole ratios**:

$$Y_1 = \frac{y_1}{1 - y_1} = \frac{0.15}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{0.15}{0.85} = 0.1765 \;\text{kmol NH}_3 / \text{kmol air}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate outlet gas composition ($Y_2$)** (90% removal means 10% remains):

$$Y_2 = Y_1 \times (1 - 0.90) = 0.1765 \times 0.10 = 0.01765 \;\text{kmol NH}_3 / \text{kmol air}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate inert gas flow rate ($G$)**:

$$G = 1500 \times 0.85 = 1275 \;\text{kmol air/h}$$

**Step 4 — Inlet liquid composition ($X_2$) — pure water**:

$$X_2 = 0 \;\text{kmol NH}_3 / \text{kmol water}$$

**Step 5 — Identify $X_1^*$ (equilibrium value at the bottom)**:

At the bottom, the gas enters with $Y_1 = 0.1765$. The corresponding equilibrium liquid composition:

$$Y_1 = m \cdot X_1^* \implies X_1^* = \frac{Y_1}{m} = \frac{0.1765}{0.8} = 0.2206 \;\text{kmol NH}_3/\text{kmol water}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the minimum solvent formula**:

$$L_{\min} = G \cdot \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2} = 1275 \times \frac{0.1765 - 0.01765}{0.2206 - 0}$$

$$L_{\min} = 1275 \times \frac{0.1588}{0.2206} = 1275 \times 0.7199 = 917.8 \;\text{kmol water/h}$$

**Final Answer**: $L_{\min} \approx \mathbf{917.8 \;\text{kmol water/h}}$.

---

**(b)** For the system described in (a), if the actual solvent rate is **1.5 times** the minimum, determine the actual outlet liquid composition $X_1$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Calculate actual solvent flow rate**:

$$L = 1.5 \times L_{\min} = 1.5 \times 917.8 = 1376.7 \;\text{kmol water/h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the overall material balance**:

$$L (X_1 - X_2) = G (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

Substituting $X_2 = 0$:

$$L \cdot X_1 = G (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

$$X_1 = \frac{G (Y_1 - Y_2)}{L} = \frac{1275 \times 0.1588}{1376.7}$$

$$X_1 = \frac{202.5}{1376.7} = 0.1471 \;\text{kmol NH}_3/\text{kmol water}$$

**Final Answer**: $X_1 \approx \mathbf{0.1471 \;\text{kmol NH}_3/\text{kmol water}}$.

**Verification (check driving force is positive)**:
- At the bottom: $Y_1 - mX_1 = 0.1765 - 0.8 \times 0.1471 = 0.1765 - 0.1177 = 0.0588 > 0$ ✓
- At the top: $Y_2 - mX_2 = 0.01765 - 0 = 0.01765 > 0$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students frequently forget to convert mole fractions to **mole ratios** before applying the operating line equation. Using mole fractions in the material balance is **wrong** because the inert flow rates (G, L) would then be inconsistent. Always use the inert-free mole ratios $X$ and $Y$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — Co-Current vs Counter-Current Comparison

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q3(b)]**:

**(a)** Derive the operating line equation for a **co-current absorption tower** in terms of inert-free mole ratios $X$ and $Y$. State the slope and identify the points through which the line passes.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Define terminal compositions for co-current flow**:
- Gas enters at the top: $Y_1$ (rich), leaves at the bottom: $Y_2$ (lean).
- Liquid enters at the top: $X_1$ (lean), leaves at the bottom: $X_2$ (rich).

**Step 2 — Overall material balance**:

$$G Y_1 + L X_1 = G Y_2 + L X_2$$

$$L (X_1 - X_2) = G (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

**Step 3 — Material balance above any plane** (from the top down to the plane):

Gas entering from top + Liquid entering from top = Gas leaving at plane + Liquid leaving at plane:

$$G Y_1 + L X_1 = G Y + L X$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Y$**:

$$Y = -\frac{L}{G} X + \left( Y_1 + \frac{L}{G} X_1 \right)$$

**Step 5 — Identify geometric properties**:
- **Slope** = $-\dfrac{L}{G}$ (negative).
- **$Y$-intercept** = $Y_1 + \dfrac{L}{G} X_1$.
- The line passes through $(X_1, Y_1)$ at the top and $(X_2, Y_2)$ at the bottom.

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation**:

Because the slope is **negative**, the operating line slopes **downward** from top-left to bottom-right on the $X$–$Y$ diagram. Unlike counter-current absorption where the operating line lies entirely below the equilibrium curve, here the operating line and equilibrium curve intersect at most at one point. The driving force $(Y - Y^*)$ shrinks rapidly as we descend the tower.

**Final Answer (Summary)**: Operating line: $Y = -\dfrac{L}{G} X + \left(Y_1 + \dfrac{L}{G} X_1\right)$, slope $= -L/G$, passes through $(X_1, Y_1)$ and $(X_2, Y_2)$.

---

**(b)** A counter-current stripping tower is to remove **95%** of the **acetone** from an aqueous solution using **air** at **25°C**. The inlet liquid contains **8 wt% acetone** and the inlet gas is **pure air**. Liquid flow rate = **1200 kmol/h** (water basis) and gas flow rate = **800 kmol/h** (air basis). The equilibrium relation is $Y^* = 1.6 X$. Compute:
1. The mole ratios $X_1$, $X_2$, $Y_2$.
2. The outlet gas composition $Y_1$.
3. The minimum gas flow rate $G_{\min}$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Convert wt% to mole ratio** for the inlet liquid:

Molecular weights: acetone = 58 g/mol, water = 18 g/mol.

In 100 kg of solution: 8 kg acetone + 92 kg water.
- Moles acetone = $8000/58 = 137.93$ kmol.
- Moles water = $92000/18 = 5111.11$ kmol.

$$X_1 = \frac{137.93}{5111.11} = 0.0270 \;\text{kmol acetone/kmol water}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $X_2$ (95% removal means 5% remains)**:

$$X_2 = 0.05 \times X_1 = 0.05 \times 0.0270 = 0.00135 \;\text{kmol acetone/kmol water}$$

**Step 3 — Inlet gas is pure air**: $Y_2 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Outlet gas composition using overall material balance**:

$$L (X_1 - X_2) = G (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

$$Y_1 = Y_2 + \frac{L (X_1 - X_2)}{G} = 0 + \frac{1200 \times (0.0270 - 0.00135)}{800}$$

$$Y_1 = \frac{1200 \times 0.02565}{800} = \frac{30.78}{800} = 0.0385 \;\text{kmol acetone/kmol air}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $G_{\min}$**:

For stripping, $G_{\min}$ occurs when the operating line is tangent to the equilibrium curve. For a linear equilibrium curve and lean inlet gas ($Y_2 = 0$):

At the tangent point, the operating line has slope $L/G$ equal to the equilibrium slope $m$:

$$\frac{L}{G_{\min}} = m = 1.6$$

$$G_{\min} = \frac{L}{1.6} = \frac{1200}{1.6} = 750 \;\text{kmol air/h}$$

**Verification**: Since actual $G = 800 > G_{\min} = 750$, the operation is feasible ✓.

**Final Answer**:
- $X_1 = 0.0270$, $X_2 = 0.00135$, $Y_2 = 0$.
- $Y_1 = 0.0385$ kmol acetone/kmol air.
- $G_{\min} = 750$ kmol air/h.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. In **stripping**, students often interchange $X_1$ and $X_2$. Remember: subscript **1** is the **rich** end (inlet of solute-rich stream), subscript **2** is the **lean** end.
> 2. For **stripping with linear equilibrium**, the minimum gas condition often corresponds to operating line slope = equilibrium slope $m$ when $Y_2 = 0$. Always double-check using the tangent-point formula.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2:**

- **Always use inert-free mole ratios** $X$ and $Y$ for material balances — never mole fractions.
- **Counter-current absorption operating line**: $Y = \dfrac{L}{G}X + \left(Y_2 - \dfrac{L}{G}X_2\right)$, slope $= +L/G$, lies **below** equilibrium curve.
- **Co-current absorption operating line**: $Y = -\dfrac{L}{G}X + \left(Y_1 + \dfrac{L}{G}X_1\right)$, slope $= -L/G$, lies **below** equilibrium curve.
- **Counter-current stripping operating line**: same algebraic form as counter-current absorption, but lies **above** equilibrium curve.
- **Overall material balance (universal)**: $L(X_1 - X_2) = G(Y_1 - Y_2)$.
- **Minimum solvent** ($L_{\min}$) for absorption: operating line tangent to equilibrium curve.
- **Minimum gas** ($G_{\min}$) for stripping: operating line tangent to equilibrium curve.
- **Actual operating rate**: typically $L = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) L_{\min}$ or $G = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) G_{\min}$.
- **Subscript convention**: subscript **1** = concentrated (rich) end, subscript **2** = dilute (lean) end.
- **Industrial preference**: counter-current always (highest efficiency), co-current only for highly exothermic systems or instantaneous reactions.
- **Driving force**: $(Y - Y^*)$ for absorption; $(X - X^*)$ for stripping — both must remain positive throughout the tower.
- **Linear equilibrium**: $Y^* = mX$; **non-linear**: must use the curve $Y^*(X)$ graphically or numerically.
- **Common KTU mistake**: forgetting to convert mole fractions to mole ratios; mislabelling $X_1$ and $X_2$; using mole fractions in operating line equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: L/G Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** L/G Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In gas-liquid mass transfer operations (specifically **gas absorption** and **stripping**), the **L/G ratio** is defined as the ratio of the **liquid mass flow rate (L)** to the **inert gas mass flow rate (G)** entering a counter-current contact tower (packed or tray column). It is expressed in units of **kg inert liquid per kg inert gas** (or kmol/kmol when using molar units).

Mathematically:

$$\frac{L}{G} = \frac{\text{Mass flow rate of solute-free liquid}}{\text{Mass flow rate of solute-free gas}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (PCCHT403 - Module 2):** The L/G ratio is the **slope of the operating line** on an X–Y equilibrium diagram. The selection of an appropriate L/G ratio is the **first and most critical design decision** in any absorption/stripping column, as it directly determines the number of theoretical stages, the column diameter, and the solvent circulation cost.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy toll-booth plaza where **cars (gas)** enter from one side and **cleaning crew workers (liquid solvent)** stand in a long row. Each worker absorbs/removes dust from a few passing cars. 

- If you have **too few workers (low L/G)** → cars pass through without being fully cleaned (gas leaves with solute still present).
- If you have **too many workers (high L/G)** → the air gets cleaned perfectly, but you are paying wages to workers who are mostly idle (high operating cost).
- The **minimum L/G ratio** is the absolute bare-minimum number of workers needed to just barely meet the cleaning target — the moment you have any fewer, the target is missed.
- In practice, engineers choose a **practical L/G ratio** that is **1.2 to 2.0 times the minimum**, balancing solvent cost against the column height required.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical KTU Convention |
|---|---|---|
| **L** | Liquid mass flow rate (solute-free) | **kg inert liquid/hr** |
| **G** | Gas mass flow rate (solute-free) | **kg inert gas/hr** |
| **L′** | Liquid molar flow rate (solute-free) | **kmol inert liquid/hr** |
| **G′** | Gas molar flow rate (solute-free) | **kmol inert gas/hr** |
| **X** | Liquid-phase mole ratio (solute/solvent) | kmol solute/kmol solvent |
| **Y** | Gas-phase mole ratio (solute/carrier) | kmol solute/kmol carrier gas |

> [!NOTE]
> Always work in **mole ratios (X, Y)** rather than mole fractions (x, y) when plotting operating lines, because the resulting operating-line equation becomes **strictly linear**, which is the form required for the McCabe–Thiele graphical stage calculation expected in KTU university exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Line — The Geometric Meaning of L/G

For a counter-current **gas absorption** column operating at steady state, a mass balance on the solute around the bottom of the tower gives the **operating line equation**:

$$Y = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) X + \left(Y_1 - \frac{L}{G} X_2\right)$$

Breaking the logic into structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Why is the line straight?** Because we assume the liquid and gas flow rates L and G are **constant** through the column (dilute-solution assumption: the molar flow of solute transferred is small compared to total molar flow). With constant L and G, the mass balance is linear in X and Y.
- **Step 2 — What is the slope?** The slope of the operating line on an X–Y plot is **L/G**. A steeper line = more liquid relative to gas = richer solvent at the bottom.
- **Step 3 — What are the end points?** The operating line passes through **(X₂, Y₁)** — the rich-gas top enters the lean-liquid top — and **(X₁, Y₂)** — the lean-gas bottom leaves the rich-liquid bottom. (Subscript 1 = bottom, subscript 2 = top, by the standard KTU convention in Treybal and Dutta's textbooks.)

### 2.2 Minimum L/G Ratio — The Pinch-Point Condition

If L/G is reduced, the operating line **rotates clockwise about the point (X₂, Y₁)**, becoming **steeper**. As L/G approaches its minimum value, the operating line **touches the equilibrium curve** at exactly one point. This tangential contact is called the **"pinch point."** 

- At the pinch, the **driving force (Y − Y*) → 0** at one point in the column, meaning the number of theoretical stages required → **∞**.
- Hence **(L/G)min** represents the **theoretical lower limit** of solvent rate.

The pinch may occur at:
1. **The top of the column** (most common for gas absorption of **dilute gases** with a **linear equilibrium curve**).
2. **The bottom of the column** (less common, occurs for very steep equilibrium curves like NH₃–H₂O or SO₂–H₂O).
3. **An interior point** (general case, found by trial).

**General formula (valid for ANY pinch location):**

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2^*}{X_1^* - X_2}$$

### 2.3 Special Case — Linear Equilibrium (Henry's Law Region)

If the equilibrium curve is a **straight line** `Y* = mX`, the pinch always occurs at the **top of the column** and the minimum L/G simplifies to:

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - mX_2}{X_1 - X_2} = m \cdot \frac{(Y_1/m) - X_2}{X_1 - X_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the **linear case**, the pinch-point intersection gives: Y₁ = m·X₁_top, where X₁_top is the liquid composition at the top in equilibrium with Y₁. The minimum L/G is just the slope of the chord joining (X₂, Y₁) to the equilibrium point above X₁_top.

### 2.4 Practical (Operating) L/G Ratio

Because operating at (L/G)min would require an infinitely tall column, an **actual operating ratio** is chosen as a multiplier of the minimum:

$$\frac{L}{G}\bigg|_{\text{operating}} = \lambda \cdot \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min}$$

- **λ** is called the **solvent-to-minimum-solvent ratio** (always λ > 1).
- **KTU textbook range:** λ = **1.2 to 2.0** (Treybal recommends 1.4 as a typical starting value).
- **Higher λ** → steeper operating line → fewer stages (shorter column) → **higher solvent cost** (larger pump, larger column diameter because more liquid = more pressure drop).
- **Lower λ** → flatter operating line → more stages (taller column) → **lower solvent cost** but higher capex on column height.

The **optimum λ** is found by **total annualized cost minimization** (sum of fixed cost of column + operating cost of solvent + operating cost of gas blower) — but at the KTU 2024 scheme level, a value in the **1.25 – 1.5** range is the default design assumption unless otherwise specified.

### 2.5 L/G Ratio in Stripping (Inverse Operation)

In **stripping**, the solute is transferred from the liquid to the gas. The operating line slope is still L/G, but the geometric picture is "flipped":

- The operating line lies **below** the equilibrium curve.
- An **L/G max** now exists (because too much liquid relative to gas cannot be stripped sufficiently).
- The **practical L/G** is **0.5 to 0.8 times (L/G)max** for stripping columns.

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\max,\text{strip}} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1 - X_1^*}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y = (L/G)X + (Y_1 - (L/G)X_2)$ | Operating line for absorption | mole ratio |
| 2 | $(L/G)_{\min} = (Y_1 - Y_2^*)/(X_1^* - X_2)$ | General minimum L/G (any pinch location) | kg/kg |
| 3 | $(L/G)_{\min} = (Y_1 - mX_2)/(X_1 - X_2)$ | Minimum L/G for **linear** equilibrium | kg/kg |
| 4 | $L_{\text{operating}} = \lambda \cdot L_{\min}$ | Design liquid rate, λ ∈ [1.2, 2.0] | kg/hr |
| 5 | $G_{\text{required}} = L_{\text{operating}} / (L/G)_{\text{operating}}$ | Gas rate from specified L and ratio | kg/hr |
| 6 | $(L/G)_{\max} = (Y_1 - Y_2)/(X_1 - X_1^*)$ | Maximum L/G for **stripping** | kg/kg |
| 7 | $\text{Col. diameter} \propto \sqrt{L + G}$ | Quick sizing correlation (Ergun/K-factor for pressure drop) | m |
| 8 | $A_{\text{col}} = (G/\rho_G)/u_G$ | Cross-section from superficial gas velocity | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** In mole-fraction coordinates (x, y), the operating line is a **gentle curve** because L and G change with composition (solute transfer changes total molar flow). In mole-ratio coordinates (X, Y), the line is **rigorously straight** under the constant-molar-overflow assumption. **Always use X–Y coordinates** unless explicitly asked to use mole fractions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Operating Line Equation from a Solute Mass Balance

Consider a counter-current absorption column. Let:
- Gas enters the **bottom** with solute mole ratio **Y₁**, leaves the **top** with **Y₂**.
- Liquid enters the **top** with solute mole ratio **X₂**, leaves the **bottom** with **X₁**.

Take a **control volume** from the **bottom** of the tower up to an arbitrary horizontal cross-section at which the gas and liquid compositions are **Y** (gas) and **X** (liquid).

**Solute mass entering the control volume (from the bottom):**
- In the gas stream: $G \cdot Y_1$ (kg or kmol solute/hr)
- In the liquid stream: $L \cdot X$ (kg or kmol solute/hr)

**Solute mass leaving the control volume (at the top of the slice):**
- In the gas stream: $G \cdot Y$
- In the liquid stream: $L \cdot X_2$

**Setting In = Out at steady state with no accumulation:**

$$G \cdot Y_1 + L \cdot X = G \cdot Y + L \cdot X_2$$

Rearranging to solve for Y as a function of X:

$$G \cdot Y_1 - G \cdot Y = L \cdot X_2 - L \cdot X$$

$$G(Y_1 - Y) = L(X_2 - X)$$

$$Y_1 - Y = \frac{L}{G}(X_2 - X)$$

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} X + Y_1 - \frac{L}{G} X_2$$

$$\boxed{Y = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right) X + \left(Y_1 - \frac{L}{G} X_2\right)}$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- The slope of the line is **L/G** → a direct proof of why the operating line slope IS the L/G ratio.
- The y-intercept is $Y_1 - (L/G)X_2$ → if you plug in X = 0, the liquid is pure solvent, and the gas Y intercept equals what the gas would be in equilibrium with pure solvent (but generally > 0 unless L/G is enormous).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — General Minimum L/G Ratio

The minimum L/G ratio corresponds to the operating line **just touching the equilibrium curve** at some pinch point $(X^*, Y^*)$. 

At the pinch:
- The operating line passes through $(X_2, Y_1)$ — this point is **fixed** (the rich gas entering and lean liquid entering are specifications from the problem).
- The operating line also passes through $(X^*, Y^*)$ — a point on the equilibrium curve.
- The two points define a line; its slope is (L/G)min.

Slope of line joining (X₂, Y₁) and (X*, Y*):

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y^*}{X^* - X_2}$$

Because $(X^*, Y^*)$ lies on the equilibrium curve, we can write $Y^* = f(X^*)$, and the search for the **maximum slope** of all such chords gives the true (L/G)min. If the equilibrium curve is monotonic, the maximum-slope chord will touch the equilibrium curve tangentially, which is the graphical condition students will be asked to apply in KTU exams.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Special Case: Minimum L/G for Linear Equilibrium

If $Y^* = mX$ (Henry's Law with slope m), the equilibrium curve is a straight line through the origin. The chord of maximum slope from (X₂, Y₁) touches the equilibrium curve at the point where the chord is parallel to the line. The touch point is at the **top** of the column because that's where the line from (X₂, Y₁) can rise most steeply.

Setting the slope equal to m at the pinch:

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - mX_1}{X_1 - X_2}$$

Solving for X₁ at the pinch (where Y = mX):

$$Y_1 = mX_1 \Rightarrow X_1 = \frac{Y_1}{m}$$

Substituting back:

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - m \cdot (Y_1/m)}{Y_1/m - X_2} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_1}{Y_1/m - X_2} = \frac{0}{(\ldots)}$$

This is a **0/0 form** — meaning the chord degenerates. We need to take the limit. Use the proper formula directly:

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - mX_2}{X_1 - X_2}$$

with $X_1 = Y_1/m$ (the liquid at the top in equilibrium with the rich gas Y₁):

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - mX_2}{Y_1/m - X_2}$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Numerator: how much solute the gas must transfer per kg of gas.
- Denominator: how much solute the liquid picks up per kg of liquid between top and bottom.
- A large denominator (very rich equilibrium, m large) → small (L/G)min → solvent is effective → little solvent needed.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A gas stream of 100 kmol/hr containing 20 mol% acetone in air is to be scrubbed with water in a counter-current absorber to reduce the acetone content to 2 mol%. The acetone-free water flow rate is 150 kmol/hr. The equilibrium relation is Y* = 1.2 X (linear). Calculate:
1. (L/G)min
2. Actual L/G used (λ = 1.5)
3. The actual solvent rate required.

**Step 1 — Convert to solute-free mole ratios.**

For the gas:
$$Y_1 = \frac{y_1}{1-y_1} = \frac{0.20}{0.80} = 0.250 \text{ kmol acetone/kmol air}$$

$$Y_2 = \frac{0.02}{0.98} = 0.0204$$

For the liquid (pure water entering): $X_2 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Find (L/G)min using the linear formula.**

The pinch at the top: $X_1 = Y_1/m = 0.250/1.2 = 0.2083$

$$(L/G)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - mX_2}{X_1 - X_2} = \frac{0.250 - 1.2 \times 0}{0.2083 - 0} = \frac{0.250}{0.2083}$$

$$(L/G)_{\min} = 1.200 \text{ kmol water/kmol air}$$

**Step 3 — Find the actual L/G with λ = 1.5.**

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\text{operating}} = 1.5 \times 1.200 = 1.800$$

**Step 4 — Find the actual water rate.**

The inert gas rate is the acetone-free portion of the inlet gas:

$$G = 100 \times (1 - 0.20) = 80 \text{ kmol air/hr}$$

$$L = (L/G) \times G = 1.800 \times 80 = 144 \text{ kmol water/hr}$$

**Result:** The actual solvent requirement is **144 kmol/hr** of water, slightly less than the given 150 kmol/hr — so the given flow is adequate (λ ≈ 1.56, well within the practical 1.2–2.0 range).

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix for L/G Selection

| Operating Condition | L/G Choice | Effect on Column Height | Effect on Solvent Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| L/G = (L/G)min | ∞ stages → infinite height | Zero | Lowest |
| λ = 1.2 | Very tall column | Highest stages | Low |
| λ = 1.5 (typical) | Moderate height | Moderate stages | Moderate |
| λ = 2.0 | Short column | Few stages | High |
| λ > 2.0 | Very short | Almost no benefit beyond λ = 2 | Very high (waste) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Role of L/G in Absorption Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Gas Stream G, Y1, T, P] --> B[Specify Y2 from product purity]
    C[Inlet Solvent L, X2, T, P] --> D[Specify X2 from solvent purity]
    B --> E[Define Required Separation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Obtain Equilibrium Data Y* = f X]
    F --> G{Is Equilibrium Linear?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Use Henry Law Y* = m X]
    G -- No --> I[Use Tabulated Curve]
    H --> J[Plot Equilibrium Curve]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Plot Fixed Operating Point X2, Y1]
    K --> L[Find L over G minimum via Pinch Construction]
    L --> M[Calculate L over G min]
    M --> N[Choose Lambda 1.2 to 2.0]
    N --> O[Compute Actual L over G]
    O --> P[Compute L actual = L over G times G]
    P --> Q[Step 1: Column Height via NOG or NTS]
    Q --> R[Step 2: Column Diameter via G and rho G]
    R --> S[Final Design Specs]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: L/G Effect on Operating Line Slope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH L/G Slope = 1.80 (lambda = 1.5)"]
        H1[Rich gas point X2, Y1] --> H2[Pinch at bottom region]
        H2 --> H3[Lean gas point X1, Y2]
    end
    subgraph MIN["MINIMUM L/G Slope (Pinch Touches Equilibrium)"]
        M1[Rich gas point X2, Y1] --> M2[Pinch point touches Y* curve]
        M2 --> M3[Lean gas point X1, Y2 infinite stages]
    end
    subgraph LOW["LOW L/G Slope = 0.5 times L/G min (infeasible)"]
        L1[Rich gas point X2, Y1] --> L2[Operating line CROSSES equilibrium]
        L2 --> L3[Negative driving force impossible]
    end
```

### 4.3 Counter-Current Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GasIn["Rich Gas: G kmol/hr, Y1"] -- Bottom --> Tower["Counter-Current Packed Column"]
    LiqIn["Lean Liquid: L kmol/hr, X2"] -- Top --> Tower
    Tower -- Top --> GasOut["Lean Gas: G kmol/hr, Y2"]
    Tower -- Bottom --> LiqOut["Rich Liquid: L kmol/hr, X1"]
    Tower --> MassBal["Steady-State Solute Balance:\nG Y1 + L X2 = G Y2 + L X1"]
    MassBal --> OpLine["Operating Line:\nY = L over G times X + (Y1 minus L over G times X2)"]
```

### 4.4 L/G Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([New Absorption Problem]) --> Q1{Equilibrium curve known?}
    Q1 -- No --> Fail[Obtain VLE data first]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Gas and liquid rates specified?}
    Q2 -- Only gas G --> Solve1[Calculate L over G min from pinch]
    Q2 -- Only liquid L --> Solve2[Calculate L over G min, then find required G]
    Q2 -- Both G and L --> Check[Check L over G greater than L over G min]
    Solve1 --> Choice[Pick lambda in 1.2 to 2.0]
    Solve2 --> Choice
    Check --> Q3{Is L over G adequate?}
    Q3 -- No --> Warn[Increase L or decrease G]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Done[Proceed to stage calculation]
    Choice --> Done
```
