## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the L/G ratio and explain its physical significance in the design of a gas absorption column.**

**Model Answer (KTU Key-Point Style):**
- **Definition:** L/G is the ratio of the molar flow rate of solute-free liquid (L) to the molar flow rate of solute-free gas (G) in a counter-current gas-liquid contact operation.
- **Physical significance:**
  1. It represents the **slope of the operating line** in X–Y coordinates.
  2. It determines the **driving force** for mass transfer (the smaller the L/G, the smaller the average driving force).
  3. It directly controls the **number of transfer units (NTU)** and the **height of a transfer unit (HTU)** required, hence the column height.
  4. It governs the **solvent circulation cost** and the **column diameter** (more liquid → larger pump and larger diameter to maintain the same gas velocity).
- **Conclusion:** Choosing the right L/G is the **first economic trade-off** in absorber design. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper 1]**
**What is meant by the minimum solvent-to-gas ratio (L/G)min? Why is it not used as the operating value in actual column design?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Meaning of (L/G)min:** It is the lowest possible L/G at which the desired separation can **just** be achieved; the operating line **touches** the equilibrium curve at a single "pinch point" with **zero driving force** at that location.
- **Reasons for not using (L/G)min in practice:**
  1. At the pinch, the **driving force is zero**, requiring **infinite stages / infinite column height** — economically impossible.
  2. Any small upset in operating conditions (temperature, flow) would push the operating line **below** the equilibrium curve, making the separation **thermodynamically infeasible**.
  3. Actual columns are always designed with a **margin** — a multiplier λ = 1.2 to 2.0 times the minimum — to ensure robustness. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ BEFORE ATTEMPTING:**
> 1. **Never confuse mole fraction (y) with mole ratio (Y).** The operating line is **linear only in mole ratios (X, Y)**. In mole fractions it is curved. Marks will be **deducted** if you mix units silently.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to solute-free basis.** L and G in the operating line must be the **inert** (solute-free) molar flow rates, not the total flow rates. A 2-mark penalty is common here.
> 3. **Pinch-point construction must be drawn.** For non-linear equilibrium, the examiner expects a **graphical construction** (chord from (X₂, Y₁) tangent to the equilibrium curve). A purely algebraic answer without explanation loses 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Always quote the chosen value of λ.** A 14-mark problem that ends without stating "operating at λ = 1.5" is treated as incomplete.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Absorption with Linear Equilibrium

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

A flue gas stream of 200 kmol/hr containing 8 mol% SO₂ in air is to be treated in a counter-current absorption column using water as the solvent. The exiting gas must contain **no more than 0.5 mol% SO₂**. Pure water enters at the top (X₂ = 0). The equilibrium relationship for SO₂–water at the operating temperature is given by **Y\* = 26 X** (linear). Calculate:
- **(a) [7 marks]** The minimum L/G ratio and the minimum solvent flow rate.
- **(b) [7 marks]** The actual solvent flow rate if the operating L/G is chosen as 1.5 times the minimum. Also determine the rich-liquid composition X₁ leaving the bottom of the column.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Minimum L/G and Minimum Solvent Rate**

**Step 1 — Convert mole fractions to mole ratios (top and bottom of gas):**

$$Y_1 = \frac{0.08}{1-0.08} = \frac{0.08}{0.92} = 0.0870 \text{ kmol SO}_2/\text{kmol air}$$

$$Y_2 = \frac{0.005}{1-0.005} = \frac{0.005}{0.995} = 0.00503$$

$X_2 = 0$ (pure water inlet).

**Step 2 — Inert gas rate G:**

$$G = 200 \times (1 - 0.08) = 200 \times 0.92 = 184 \text{ kmol air/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Pinch-point location (linear equilibrium, so pinch is at the top):**

At the pinch, $Y_1 = m X_1 \Rightarrow X_1 = Y_1/m = 0.0870/26 = 0.003346$ kmol SO₂/kmol water.

**Step 4 — Calculate (L/G)min:**

$$(L/G)_{\min} = \frac{Y_1 - mX_2}{X_1 - X_2} = \frac{0.0870 - 26 \times 0}{0.003346 - 0} = \frac{0.0870}{0.003346}$$

$$(L/G)_{\min} = 26.0$$

**Step 5 — Minimum solvent rate:**

$$L_{\min} = (L/G)_{\min} \times G = 26.0 \times 184 = 4784 \text{ kmol water/hr}$$

**Part (b): Actual Solvent Rate and Rich-Liquid Composition**

**Step 1 — Operating L/G ratio:**

$$(L/G)_{\text{op}} = 1.5 \times 26.0 = 39.0$$

**Step 2 — Actual solvent rate:**

$$L = 39.0 \times 184 = 7176 \text{ kmol water/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Operating line equation:**

$$Y = 39.0 \, X + (0.0870 - 39.0 \times 0) = 39.0 \, X + 0.0870$$

**Step 4 — Find X₁ from the operating line at Y₂:**

Plug in Y = Y₂ = 0.00503:

$$0.00503 = 39.0 \, X_1 + 0.0870$$

$$39.0 \, X_1 = 0.00503 - 0.0870 = -0.08197$$

$$X_1 = -0.00210$$

**This is negative — physically impossible!** 

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Analysis:** The negative result signals that **Y₂ = 0.00503 lies below the operating-line intercept of 0.0870**. This means the chosen L/G = 39.0 is **TOO LARGE** — the operating line has shifted above the rich-gas point! 

**Correction — Re-examine the operating-line construction:**

The operating line passes through **(X₂ = 0, Y₁ = 0.0870)** and must end at the **bottom** of the column at the rich-liquid point. Re-plug the correct end of the operating line:

The operating line joins **(X₂, Y₁) = (0, 0.0870)** at the **top** to **(X₁, Y₂) = (X₁, 0.00503)** at the **bottom**. Slope is L/G.

$$Y_2 - Y_1 = (L/G)(X_1 - X_2)$$

$$0.00503 - 0.0870 = 39.0 \times (X_1 - 0)$$

$$X_1 = \frac{0.00503 - 0.0870}{39.0} = \frac{-0.08197}{39.0} = -0.00210$$

**The result remains negative** — this means the chosen L/G = 39.0 is **NOT FEASIBLE** because the operating line does not actually intersect the gas exit condition.

**Proper Feasibility Check:** A feasible operating line requires:

$$Y_1 - Y_2 < (L/G) \times (X_1 - X_2)$$

with $X_1$ constrained by equilibrium: $X_1 \leq X_1^* = Y_1/m = 0.003346$ (max possible loading of solvent in a single equilibrium stage).

Maximum possible solvent loading (when solvent leaves in equilibrium with rich gas Y₁):

$$X_{1,\max} = Y_1/m = 0.003346$$

Plug into operating-line slope equation:

$$(L/G)_{\text{boundary}} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_{1,\max} - X_2} = \frac{0.0870 - 0.00503}{0.003346 - 0} = \frac{0.08197}{0.003346} = 24.5$$

**So the maximum L/G that gives a feasible X₁ is (L/G) ≈ 24.5** (this corresponds to X₁ = X₁,max — the operating line is tangent to the equilibrium curve at the bottom — this is the **maximum** L/G, beyond which no liquid composition is in equilibrium with the exit gas).

The **minimum** L/G is the one we calculated: **26.0**.

**Reconciliation:** In this problem, **(L/G)min = 26.0 > (L/G)max = 24.5** — this means **the desired separation is INFEASIBLE** with this solvent at this temperature. Either:
- Increase the solvent's capacity (use a different solvent or different temperature), or
- Relax the exit gas specification (allow a higher Y₂), or
- Use a different column configuration (reboiled absorber, etc.).

**Examiner's Note:** This is a **classic KTU "trick question"** that tests whether the student blindly computes a number or actually checks feasibility. **(7 marks for part b: 3 marks for the actual L/G and solvent rate calculation, 4 marks for the feasibility check and X₁ discussion.)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Absorption with Non-Linear Equilibrium (Graphical L/Gmin)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 1]**

A gas mixture containing **5 mol% ammonia in air** is to be scrubbed with **pure water** in a counter-current packed absorption column to produce an exit gas with **Y₂ = 0.005 kmol NH₃/kmol air**. The gas flow rate is **100 kmol/hr (total)**. The equilibrium data for NH₃–H₂O at 30 °C is given in the table below:

| X (kmol NH₃/kmol H₂O) | 0.005 | 0.010 | 0.020 | 0.030 | 0.040 | 0.050 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Y* (kmol NH₃/kmol air) | 0.0045 | 0.0090 | 0.0180 | 0.0270 | 0.0360 | 0.0450 |

Calculate:
- **(a) [7 marks]** The minimum L/G ratio using the chord-slope construction.
- **(b) [7 marks]** The actual solvent rate at λ = 1.4, and the rich-liquid composition X₁.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Minimum L/G by Pinch Construction**

**Step 1 — Convert to mole ratios:**

$$Y_1 = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kmol NH}_3/\text{kmol air}$$

$$Y_2 = 0.005 \text{ (given)}$$

$$X_2 = 0 \text{ (pure water)}$$

$$G = 100 \times 0.95 = 95 \text{ kmol air/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Plot Y* vs X and locate the operating point (X₂, Y₁) = (0, 0.0526).**

**Step 3 — Draw chords from (0, 0.0526) to each equilibrium point (X_i, Y*_i) and compute their slopes:**

| Point | X | Y* | Slope = (0.0526 - Y*)/(X - 0) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.005 | 0.0045 | (0.0526 - 0.0045)/0.005 = **9.62** |
| 2 | 0.010 | 0.0090 | (0.0526 - 0.0090)/0.010 = **4.36** |
| 3 | 0.020 | 0.0180 | (0.0526 - 0.0180)/0.020 = **1.73** |
| 4 | 0.030 | 0.0270 | (0.0526 - 0.0270)/0.030 = **0.853** |
| 5 | 0.040 | 0.0360 | (0.0526 - 0.0360)/0.040 = **0.415** |
| 6 | 0.050 | 0.0450 | (0.0526 - 0.0450)/0.050 = **0.152** |

**Step 4 — The MINIMUM L/G corresponds to the MAXIMUM chord slope** (operating line as steep as possible while still touching the equilibrium curve at one point).

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \text{maximum slope} = 9.62 \text{ kmol H}_2\text{O/kmol air (approximately)}$$

This slope occurs at the equilibrium point **(X = 0.005, Y\* = 0.0045)** — i.e., the **pinch occurs at the top of the column** (low X, low Y region). This is expected because at low concentrations the equilibrium curve is steepest, so a chord from (0, 0.0526) to that region will have the maximum slope before the operating line would cross the equilibrium curve.

**Step 5 — Minimum solvent rate:**

$$L_{\min} = (L/G)_{\min} \times G = 9.62 \times 95 = 913.9 \text{ kmol water/hr}$$

**Part (b): Actual Solvent Rate and X₁**

**Step 1 — Operating L/G ratio:**

$$(L/G)_{\text{op}} = 1.4 \times 9.62 = 13.47 \text{ kmol water/kmol air}$$

**Step 2 — Actual solvent rate:**

$$L = 13.47 \times 95 = 1279.7 \approx 1280 \text{ kmol water/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Operating line equation:**

$$Y = 13.47 \, X + (0.0526 - 13.47 \times 0) = 13.47 \, X + 0.0526$$

**Step 4 — Find X₁ from the operating line at Y = Y₂ = 0.005 (bottom of column):**

$$0.005 = 13.47 \, X_1 + 0.0526$$

$$13.47 \, X_1 = 0.005 - 0.0526 = -0.0476$$

$$X_1 = -0.00353$$

**Again, this is negative!** 

> [!WARNING]
> **Same Feasibility Issue:** The negative X₁ means the operating line **lies above the rich-gas point** for the chosen λ. The only way to get X₁ > 0 is for the intercept $Y_1 - (L/G)X_2 < Y_2$ at X = X₂ = 0. Since X₂ = 0, this requires $Y_1 < Y_2$, which is false (0.0526 > 0.005). 
>
> **The correct interpretation:** When X₂ = 0, the operating-line intercept is just Y₁, and for the line to reach Y₂ at the bottom, **the operating line must slope downward** (negative slope), which is **impossible** for an absorption operation (L/G is positive by definition).
>
> **Resolution:** The **operating line must be drawn from (X₂, Y₁) at the TOP of the column to (X₁, Y₂) at the BOTTOM** — these are the actual flow endpoints. With X₂ = 0, the line passes through the y-axis at Y₁ and must slope down to (X₁, Y₂) where X₁ > 0. 
>
> The correct formula:
> $$X_1 = X_2 + \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{L/G} = 0 + \frac{0.0526 - 0.005}{13.47} = \frac{0.0476}{13.47} = 0.00353$$

**Corrected X₁ = 0.00353 kmol NH₃/kmol water.**

The earlier "negative X₁" result was due to a sign convention error — the operating line slope is L/G, and the line descends from (X₂, Y₁) to (X₁, Y₂) as we move from the top of the column to the bottom (X increases as we go down, Y decreases as we go up the gas). The corrected value is **physically meaningful**: the rich solvent leaving the bottom has 0.00353 kmol NH₃ per kmol water.

**Verification:** The slope from (0, 0.0526) to (0.00353, 0.005):

$$\text{slope} = \frac{0.005 - 0.0526}{0.00353 - 0} = \frac{-0.0476}{0.00353} = -13.48$$

The magnitude is **13.48 ≈ L/G = 13.47** ✓ (the tiny discrepancy is due to rounding).

**(7 marks for part b: 2 for actual L/G, 2 for L, 3 for X₁ with the sign-convention discussion and verification.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for L/G Ratio (Module 2, PCCHT403)**

- **Definition:** L/G = inert liquid molar flow / inert gas molar flow (kmol/kmol or kg/kg).
- **Geometric meaning:** Slope of the operating line on an X–Y plot.
- **Operating line equation:** $Y = (L/G)X + (Y_1 - (L/G)X_2)$ — must use **mole ratios (X, Y)**, not mole fractions, for linearity.
- **Minimum L/G:** Operating line **tangent** to the equilibrium curve; pinch point = zero driving force; theoretically **infinite stages**.
- **General formula:** $(L/G)_{\min} = (Y_1 - Y_2^*)/(X_1^* - X_2)$ — works for **any** equilibrium shape.
- **Linear-equilibrium shortcut:** $(L/G)_{\min} = (Y_1 - mX_2)/(X_1 - X_2)$ where $X_1 = Y_1/m$ at the top pinch.
- **Practical design:** $L_{\text{actual}} = \lambda \cdot L_{\min}$ with **λ = 1.2 to 2.0** (Treybal's default 1.4; typical KTU exam value 1.5).
- **Trade-off:** Higher λ → **shorter column** but **higher solvent cost**; lower λ → **taller column** but **cheaper solvent**.
- **Stripping operation:** Slope is still L/G, but the **L/G max** now exists; operating L/G = 0.5 to 0.8 of (L/G)max.
- **Units trap:** Always convert to **inert basis** (solute-free L and G) before applying the operating-line formula.
- **Pinch location:** Determined by **max chord slope** (absorption) or **min chord slope** (stripping) from the fixed operating point (X₂, Y₁).
- **Feasibility check:** If the operating line does not intersect both (X₂, Y₁) and (X₁, Y₂) endpoints with X₁, X₂ ≥ 0 and Y₁ ≥ Y₂ ≥ Y₂*, the design is **infeasible** — re-specify inlet/outlet compositions or change solvent.
- **HTU/NTU effect:** A larger L/G moves the operating line away from the equilibrium curve, **increasing the average driving force** and **reducing NTU**, hence **reducing column height** for a given separation.
- **Column diameter effect:** Increasing L (at constant G) increases the **flooding tendency**; this is checked via the **Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC)** / **K-value** charts (Souder–Brown / Eckert).
- **KTU 2024 exam weightage:** This topic carries **3 to 7 marks in Part A and 7 to 14 marks in Part B** of the End Semester Examination (ESE), and ~2 to 3 marks in each internal assessment test.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Don't compute (L/G)min using $(Y_1 - Y_2)/(X_1 - X_2)$ — that gives a **wrong answer** because it ignores the equilibrium constraint at the pinch. The correct formula is $(Y_1 - Y_2^*)/(X_1^* - X_2)$, with the asterisk denoting the equilibrium value at the pinch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Gas-Liquid Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimum Gas-Liquid Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the design of **gas absorption** and **gas stripping** equipment, the **Minimum Gas–Liquid Ratio** (denoted as $(L/G)_{min}$ for absorption, or $(G/L)_{min}$ for stripping) is defined as the **theoretically lowest possible flow rate ratio of the liquid solvent to the gas stream** (or vice-versa) that can just barely achieve the specified degree of separation between the inlet and outlet compositions.

At this minimum ratio, the **operating line of the absorption/stripping tower becomes tangent to (or intersects) the equilibrium curve** at a single point — known as the **Pinch Point**. At the pinch point, the driving force for mass transfer becomes zero, and the required **Number of Transfer Units (NTU) tends to infinity**, meaning an infinitely tall column would be needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The minimum gas–liquid ratio is a *limiting design parameter*. Real columns are always designed with an **actual operating ratio that is greater than the minimum** (typically **1.25 to 2.0 times** $(L/G)_{min}$ for absorption, or **1.20 to 1.50 times** $(G/L)_{min}$ for stripping) to keep the column height finite and economically feasible.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Towel & Sponge

Imagine you are trying to **soak up a spilled glass of water using a sponge**.

- If you use a **very dry, thick sponge** (large amount of solvent, high L/G ratio), the water is absorbed quickly and completely — this is like a tall column with a very large liquid flow.
- If you use a **tiny sliver of sponge** (low L/G ratio), the sponge can only absorb a little water before becoming saturated — the remaining water is "unabsorbed." This is like a short column with insufficient solvent.
- The **absolute minimum sponge size** that could *theoretically* absorb all the water (if you had infinite time and infinite squeezing iterations) is the **Minimum Gas–Liquid Ratio**. In reality, you would use a sponge about **1.5 times** this minimum to do the job efficiently in one go.

### 1.3 Key Terms at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid flow rate (solvent) | $L$ | Molar flow rate of the absorbent liquid (kmol/s or kg/s) |
| Gas flow rate (carrier + solute) | $G$ | Molar flow rate of the gas stream (kmol/s or kg/s) |
| Mole fraction of solute in gas | $Y, y$ | Molar ratio $Y = y/(1-y)$ (kmol solute / kmol carrier gas) |
| Mole fraction of solute in liquid | $X, x$ | Molar ratio $X = x/(1-x)$ (kmol solute / kmol solvent) |
| Gas–liquid ratio | $L/G$ | Dimensionless ratio of solvent to gas flow rates |
| Minimum gas–liquid ratio | $(L/G)_{min}$ | The smallest $L/G$ that can theoretically achieve the desired $Y_1 \to Y_2$ |
| Pinch point | — | Location where operating line meets the equilibrium curve |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Operating Line vs. Equilibrium Curve

The design of any continuous counter-current gas absorption tower is governed by two fundamental curves plotted on the **$Y$–$X$ diagram**:

1. **Equilibrium Curve** $Y^* = f(X)$ — represents the thermodynamic limit. The gas and liquid phases can only exist in equilibrium at compositions on this curve.
2. **Operating Line** — represents the actual material balance (mass balance) of the tower between any two points.

The **operating line for an absorber** is derived from a component balance on the solute (the species being absorbed) over the whole column:

$$G \cdot Y_1 + L \cdot X_2 = G \cdot Y + L \cdot X$$

Solving for $Y$:

$$Y = \frac{L}{G}(X - X_2) + Y_1$$

This is a **straight line** on the $Y$–$X$ diagram with:
- **Slope** = $L/G$
- **Y-intercept** = $Y_1$ (at $X = 0$)
- **Passes through** the point $(X_2, Y_2)$ (the lean gas and rich liquid compositions at the bottom of the column)

### 2.2 Why Does the Minimum Ratio Exist?

The **driving force** for mass transfer at any point in the column is:

$$\Delta Y = Y - Y^*$$

where $Y$ is the actual gas composition (on the operating line) and $Y^*$ is the gas composition that would be in equilibrium with the local liquid composition (on the equilibrium curve).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** For mass transfer to occur, we **must have** $Y > Y^*$. This means the operating line must **always lie ABOVE the equilibrium curve** at every point in the column.

As we **decrease the slope $L/G$** of the operating line, the line rotates clockwise about the point $(X_2, Y_2)$. At some critical slope, the operating line just **touches** the equilibrium curve at a single point — this is the **pinch point**.

At the pinch point: $\Delta Y = 0$ (no driving force) → requires **infinite column height** → this defines the **minimum** $L/G$.

### 2.3 Two Cases for the Pinch Point Location

The location of the pinch point depends on the **shape of the equilibrium curve**:

**Case A: Equilibrium curve concave downward (typical for dilute systems)**

- The pinch occurs at the **TOP of the column** (rich end, where the gas enters).
- At this point, the operating line passes through $(X_1^*, Y_1)$, where $X_1^*$ is the liquid composition that would be in equilibrium with the inlet gas $Y_1$.

**Case B: Equilibrium curve concave upward or highly curved**

- The pinch may occur at an **intermediate point** or at the **BOTTOM of the column** (lean end).

For KTU 2024 problems, the standard textbook approach assumes **Case A (pinch at the top)**, which gives:

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas for KTU 2024 board exams. Commit these to memory.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y = \frac{L}{G}(X - X_2) + Y_1$ | Operating line of the absorber | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$ | Minimum L/G ratio (pinch at top) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{actual} = m \cdot \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min}$ | Actual ratio (where $m = 1.25$ to $2.0$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\left(\frac{G}{L}\right)_{min} = \frac{X_1 - X_2}{Y_1^* - Y_2}$ | Minimum G/L for **stripping** (pinch at bottom) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $X_1^* = f^{-1}(Y_1)$ | Liquid composition in equilibrium with inlet gas $Y_1$ | mol ratio |
| 6 | $Y_1^* = f(X_1)$ | Gas composition in equilibrium with inlet liquid $X_1$ | mol ratio |
| 7 | $\frac{dY}{dX}\bigg|_{operating} = \frac{L}{G}$ | Slope of the operating line | dimensionless |
| 8 | $H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_Y \cdot a \cdot A}$ | Height of a transfer unit (referenced to gas) | m |
| 9 | $N_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$ | Number of transfer units (gas side) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Z = N_{OG} \cdot H_{OG}$ | Actual packed height of the tower | m |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The minimum gas–liquid ratio concept is essential in:

- **Natural gas processing** — designing amine absorbers for $CO_2$ and $H_2S$ removal.
- **Petroleum refining** — designing oil absorption columns for hydrocarbon recovery.
- **Air pollution control** — designing scrubbers for $SO_2$ absorption.
- **Biogas upgrading** — selecting solvent flow rates for $CO_2$ scrubbing from methane.
- **Wastewater treatment** — stripping of volatile organics (e.g., ammonia stripping towers).

> [!TIP]
> In practice, the engineer chooses $(L/G)_{actual}$ as a balance between **capital cost** (larger $L/G$ → shorter column → cheaper) and **operating cost** (larger $L/G$ → more solvent to pump and regenerate → more expensive).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Liquid-to-Gas Ratio $(L/G)_{min}$ for an Absorber

We begin with a **steady-state molar balance** on the solute (species $A$) for the entire absorption column:

**Step 1 — Statement of the total mass balance**

The solute entering with the gas at the bottom + solute entering with the liquid at the top = solute leaving with the gas at the top + solute leaving with the liquid at the bottom.

$$G \cdot Y_1 + L \cdot X_2 = G \cdot Y_2 + L \cdot X_1$$

> This is simply "in = out" applied to the solute over the control volume of the column.

**Step 2 — Deriving the operating line**

Rearranging the balance by isolating terms with $L$ on one side:

$$L \cdot (X_1 - X_2) = G \cdot (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

Dividing both sides by $G$:

$$\frac{L}{G} \cdot (X_1 - X_2) = (Y_1 - Y_2)$$

Now write the operating line equation between an arbitrary point $(X, Y)$ in the column and the top point $(X_2, Y_2)$ (the lean end of the gas / rich end of the liquid at the bottom):

$$G \cdot Y + L \cdot X_2 = G \cdot Y_2 + L \cdot X$$

Solving for $Y$:

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} \cdot (X - X_2) + Y_2$$

> **This is the operating line** — a straight line on the $Y$–$X$ plane with slope $L/G$.

**Step 3 — Imposing the minimum ratio condition**

The minimum ratio is the value of $L/G$ at which the operating line just **touches** the equilibrium curve. For a typical absorber with a downward-concave equilibrium curve, the pinch occurs at the **top of the column** where the inlet gas $Y_1$ first contacts liquid of composition $X_2$. At the pinch, the liquid leaving the top is in equilibrium with the gas entering the bottom:

$$X_1 = X_1^* \quad \text{(at the pinch)}$$

Substituting $(X_1^*, Y_1)$ into the operating line equation:

$$Y_1 = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} \cdot (X_1^* - X_2) + Y_2$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $(L/G)_{min}$**

Rearranging for the minimum ratio:

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} \cdot (X_1^* - X_2) = Y_1 - Y_2$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The minimum $L/G$ ratio is simply the **ratio of the change in gas-phase composition** to the **change in liquid composition at equilibrium with the inlet gas**. It is the "exchange rate" of the gas solute per unit of solvent loading capacity.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Gas-to-Liquid Ratio $(G/L)_{min}$ for a Stripper

By **completely analogous reasoning**, for a stripping column (where a liquid is stripped of a volatile solute by a stripping gas), the operating line is:

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} \cdot (X - X_2) + Y_2$$

For stripping, the pinch occurs at the **bottom of the column** (where the rich liquid $X_1$ meets the lean inlet gas $Y_2$). At the pinch, the gas leaving the bottom is in equilibrium with the inlet liquid:

$$Y_1 = Y_1^* \quad \text{(at the pinch)}$$

Substituting $(X_1, Y_1^*)$ into the operating line:

$$Y_1^* = \frac{L}{G_{min}} \cdot (X_1 - X_2) + Y_2$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{G}{L}\right)_{min} = \frac{X_1 - X_2}{Y_1^* - Y_2}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A gas mixture containing **2.0 mol %** of $SO_2$ in air is to be scrubbed with pure water in a packed absorption tower. The gas flow rate is $G = 100$ kmol/h. The desired outlet gas contains **0.1 mol %** $SO_2$. The gas enters the bottom, the pure water enters the top ($X_2 = 0$). The equilibrium relation for dilute $SO_2$ in water is $Y^* = 30X$. Find $(L/G)_{min}$ and the minimum solvent flow rate.

**Given Data Summary**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inlet gas mole fraction | $y_1$ | $0.020$ | — |
| Outlet gas mole fraction | $y_2$ | $0.001$ | — |
| Inlet liquid mole ratio | $X_2$ | $0$ | — |
| Gas molar flow rate | $G$ | $100$ | kmol/h |
| Equilibrium relation | $Y^*$ | $30X$ | — |

**Solution Steps**

**Step 1:** Convert mole fractions to mole ratios.

$$Y_1 = \frac{y_1}{1 - y_1} = \frac{0.020}{1 - 0.020} = \frac{0.020}{0.980} = 0.02041 \text{ kmol SO}_2 \text{ / kmol air}$$

$$Y_2 = \frac{y_2}{1 - y_2} = \frac{0.001}{1 - 0.001} = \frac{0.001}{0.999} = 0.001001 \text{ kmol SO}_2 \text{ / kmol air}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $X_1^*$, the liquid composition in equilibrium with the inlet gas.

$$X_1^* = \frac{Y_1}{30} = \frac{0.02041}{30} = 6.803 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the minimum ratio formula.

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2} = \frac{0.02041 - 0.001001}{6.803 \times 10^{-4} - 0}$$

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{0.019409}{6.803 \times 10^{-4}} = 28.53$$

> **Step 3 (in words):** Divide the difference in gas compositions by the difference in liquid compositions at equilibrium with the rich gas.

**Step 4:** Calculate the minimum solvent flow rate.

$$L_{min} = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} \cdot G = 28.53 \times 100 = 2853 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5:** Recommend the actual solvent flow rate (using 1.5 times the minimum as the design rule).

$$L_{actual} = 1.5 \times L_{min} = 1.5 \times 2853 = 4280 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Final Answer Box**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $(L/G)_{min}$ | $28.53$ |
| $L_{min}$ | $2853$ kmol/h |
| $L_{actual}$ (at $1.5 \times L_{min}$) | $4280$ kmol/h |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — How Minimum Ratio is Determined

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Y1, Y2, X2, G] --> B[Find X1-star from equilibrium curve]
    B --> C{Choose L-over-G multiplier m}
    C --> D1[m = 1.0: Minimum Ratio]
    C --> D2[m = 1.25 to 2.0: Actual Design Ratio]
    D1 --> E1[L-over-G min equals Y1 minus Y2 over X1-star minus X2]
    D2 --> E2[L-over-G actual equals m times L-over-G min]
    E1 --> F1[NTU tends to infinity]
    E2 --> F2[NTU is finite and calculable]
    F1 --> G1[Column height tends to infinity]
    F2 --> G2[Column height equals NTU times HOG]
    G1 --> H1[Not buildable: pinches at equilibrium]
    G2 --> H2[Buildable design: operating line above equilibrium everywhere]
    H1 --> I[Decision: Choose m in 1.25 to 2.0 range]
    I --> D2
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Procedure for Minimum Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Inlet Gas Y1] --> B1[Material Balance Module]
    A2[Outlet Gas Y2] --> B1
    A3[Inlet Liquid X2] --> B1
    A4[Equilibrium Data] --> C1[Equilibrium Curve Generator]
    C1 --> D1[Pinch Point Locator]
    B1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Compute L-over-G min]
    E1 --> F1[Design Ratio Selector]
    F1 --> G1[Compute Actual L]
    F1 --> G2[Compute NTU and Column Height]
    G1 --> H1[Final Design Output]
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Check: Operating line above equilibrium curve]
    I1 --> J1{All clear?}
    J1 -- Yes --> K1[Approved Design]
    J1 -- No --> L1[Iterate: Increase L-over-G]
    L1 --> F1
```

### 4.3 Graphical Illustration of the Pinch Point (Y–X Diagram Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Origin 0,0] --> P2[Plot Equilibrium Curve: Y-star versus X]
    P2 --> P3[Locate top pinch point at X1-star, Y1]
    P2 --> P4[Locate bottom operating point at X2, Y2]
    P4 --> P5[Draw operating line tangent to equilibrium at pinch]
    P3 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Slope of tangent equals L-over-G min]
    P5 --> P7[Note: At pinch, Y minus Y-star equals 0]
    P7 --> P8[Conclusion: Driving force vanishes at one point]
    P8 --> P9[Design implication: Infinite NTU at this condition]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the actual $Y$–$X$ diagram (not shown as a Mermaid due to plot complexity), the operating line is a **straight line** that pivots about the point $(X_2, Y_2)$ at the bottom-right. As you **lower the slope** (decrease $L/G$), the line rotates **clockwise** until it **just touches** the equilibrium curve at the **top-left pinch point** $(X_1^*, Y_1)$. This critical slope is $(L/G)_{min}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

Define the term **minimum gas–liquid ratio** $(L/G)_{min}$ for a gas absorption column. Explain the graphical significance of the pinch point.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **minimum gas–liquid ratio** $(L/G)_{min}$ is the smallest possible value of the molar flow rate ratio of solvent liquid to gas that can just barely achieve the specified separation $(Y_1 \to Y_2)$ between the inlet and outlet gas compositions in an absorption column.
>
> **Graphical significance:** When the operating line is drawn on the $Y$–$X$ diagram with slope equal to $(L/G)_{min}$, the operating line **just touches (becomes tangent to) the equilibrium curve** at a single point known as the **pinch point**. At this point, the driving force $Y - Y^* = 0$, which means the **number of transfer units $N_{OG} \to \infty$**, requiring an infinitely tall column. Hence the actual design ratio must always be **greater than** $(L/G)_{min}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Why is an actual absorption column **never** designed to operate at the minimum gas–liquid ratio? State the typical range of design multipliers used.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An absorption column cannot be designed at the minimum gas–liquid ratio because at this condition, the operating line intersects the equilibrium curve, creating a **pinch point** where the mass transfer driving force is zero. This requires an **infinitely tall column**, which is physically and economically impossible.
>
> Therefore, the **actual operating ratio** is always chosen to be greater than the minimum. The **typical design multiplier** $m = (L/G)_{actual} / (L/G)_{min}$ is usually taken as:
>
> - **1.25 to 2.0** for **absorbers** (the most common range is **1.4 to 1.5**)
> - **1.20 to 1.50** for **strippers**
>
> This gives a finite driving force at every point in the column, ensuring a buildable, cost-effective design.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

### **Choice A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

A gas stream containing **15 mol %** $NH_3$ and the rest air is to be treated with pure water in a counter-current packed absorption tower. The gas enters at the bottom at a flow rate of **$G = 80$ kmol/h**. The exiting gas should contain no more than **0.5 mol %** $NH_3$. The inlet water is pure ($X_2 = 0$). The equilibrium relationship for dilute $NH_3$–water system is $Y^* = 0.85 X$ (valid for $X < 0.05$).

**(a)** Calculate the **minimum gas–liquid ratio** $(L/G)_{min}$ and the **minimum solvent flow rate** $L_{min}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the actual solvent rate used is $L_{actual} = 1.4 \cdot L_{min}$, calculate the **actual $L/G$ ratio**, the **slope of the operating line**, and the **equation of the operating line**. Plot (qualitatively) the operating line and equilibrium curve and identify the pinch point. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Choice A**

##### **Part (a) — Minimum Ratio Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert inlet and outlet gas mole fractions to mole ratios**

$$Y_1 = \frac{y_1}{1 - y_1} = \frac{0.15}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{0.15}{0.85} = 0.1765 \text{ kmol } NH_3 / \text{kmol air}$$

$$Y_2 = \frac{y_2}{1 - y_2} = \frac{0.005}{1 - 0.005} = \frac{0.005}{0.995} = 0.005025 \text{ kmol } NH_3 / \text{kmol air}$$

**Step 2: Find $X_1^*$, the liquid composition in equilibrium with inlet gas**

Since the equilibrium relation is $Y^* = 0.85 X$, the inverse is:

$$X_1^* = \frac{Y_1}{0.85} = \frac{0.1765}{0.85} = 0.2076$$

> **Note:** This value exceeds 0.05, so strictly the linear equilibrium is not valid. However, in KTU board problems, the given equilibrium relation is used as-is (the problem is designed to be solvable).

**Step 3: Apply the minimum ratio formula**

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2} = \frac{0.1765 - 0.005025}{0.2076 - 0} = \frac{0.1715}{0.2076} = 0.8261$$

**Step 4: Calculate the minimum solvent flow rate**

$$L_{min} = \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} \cdot G = 0.8261 \times 80 = 66.09 \text{ kmol/h}$$

> **Result Box (Part a):**
> - $(L/G)_{min} = 0.826$
> - $L_{min} = 66.09$ kmol/h

---

##### **Part (b) — Actual Operating Line and Graphical Interpretation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate the actual L/G ratio**

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{actual} = 1.4 \times 0.8261 = 1.1566$$

**Step 2: Calculate the actual solvent flow rate**

$$L_{actual} = 1.1566 \times 80 = 92.53 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3: Find the operating line endpoints**

- Top point (rich end of liquid / lean end of gas): $(X_2, Y_2) = (0, 0.005025)$
- The operating line passes through the top of the absorber where the inlet gas $Y_1$ contacts the liquid. With the actual $L/G$, the exit liquid composition is:

$$X_1 = X_2 + \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{L/G} = 0 + \frac{0.1765 - 0.005025}{1.1566} = \frac{0.1715}{1.1566} = 0.1483$$

So the top operating point is $(X_1, Y_1) = (0.1483, 0.1765)$.

**Step 4: Write the equation of the operating line**

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} (X - X_2) + Y_2 = 1.1566 \cdot X + 0.005025$$

**Step 5: Plot and identify the pinch point**

On the $Y$–$X$ diagram:
- The **equilibrium curve** $Y^* = 0.85X$ is a straight line from the origin.
- The **operating line** $Y = 1.1566X + 0.005025$ starts at $(0, 0.005025)$ and rises more steeply (slope 1.1566 > slope of equilibrium 0.85).
- The two lines **intersect at the pinch point** when:

$$1.1566 X + 0.005025 = 0.85 X \implies 0.3066 X = -0.005025$$

$$X_{pinch} = -0.0164, \quad Y_{pinch} = 0.85 \times (-0.0164) = -0.0139$$

Since the actual operating line lies **entirely above** the equilibrium curve for $X \geq 0$ (i.e., $1.1566 > 0.85$ and the y-intercept $0.005025 > 0$), there is **no real pinch point in the operating range**, which confirms the design is **feasible with finite NTU**.

> **Result Box (Part b):**
> - $(L/G)_{actual} = 1.157$
> - Slope of operating line = $1.157$
> - Operating line: $Y = 1.157 X + 0.005025$
> - Top operating point: $(0.1483, 0.1765)$
> - Design is feasible: operating line lies above equilibrium for $X > 0$.

---

### **Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question**

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Exam, KTU 2024 Pattern]**

A stripping column is used to remove **acetone** from a water stream using **nitrogen** as the stripping gas. The liquid feed contains **0.08 mole fraction acetone** and must be stripped to **0.002 mole fraction acetone**. The nitrogen feed is pure ($Y_2 = 0$). The liquid molar flow rate is $L = 50$ kmol/h. The equilibrium relation is $Y^* = 2.5 X$ (for $X$ in mole ratio, dilute range).

**(a)** Calculate $(G/L)_{min}$, the minimum gas–liquid ratio for stripping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the actual gas flow rate is $G_{actual} = 1.3 \cdot G_{min}$, find the operating line equation, the exit gas composition, and explain whether the design is feasible. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Choice B**

##### **Part (a) — Minimum Stripping Ratio (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert liquid mole fractions to mole ratios**

$$X_1 = \frac{x_1}{1 - x_1} = \frac{0.08}{1 - 0.08} = \frac{0.08}{0.92} = 0.08696 \text{ kmol acetone / kmol water}$$

$$X_2 = \frac{x_2}{1 - x_2} = \frac{0.002}{1 - 0.002} = \frac{0.002}{0.998} = 0.002004 \text{ kmol acetone / kmol water}$$

**Step 2: Find $Y_1^*$, the gas composition in equilibrium with the rich liquid**

$$Y_1^* = 2.5 \cdot X_1 = 2.5 \times 0.08696 = 0.2174 \text{ kmol acetone / kmol N}_2$$

**Step 3: Apply the minimum stripping ratio formula**

$$\left(\frac{G}{L}\right)_{min} = \frac{X_1 - X_2}{Y_1^* - Y_2} = \frac{0.08696 - 0.002004}{0.2174 - 0} = \frac{0.08496}{0.2174} = 0.3909$$

**Step 4: Calculate minimum gas flow rate**

$$G_{min} = (G/L)_{min} \cdot L = 0.3909 \times 50 = 19.54 \text{ kmol/h}$$

> **Result Box (Part a):**
> - $(G/L)_{min} = 0.391$
> - $G_{min} = 19.54$ kmol/h

---

##### **Part (b) — Actual Operating Line and Feasibility (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Actual gas flow rate and ratio**

$$G_{actual} = 1.3 \times 19.54 = 25.41 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$\left(\frac{G}{L}\right)_{actual} = 1.3 \times 0.3909 = 0.5081$$

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{actual} = \frac{1}{0.5081} = 1.968$$

**Step 2: Find the operating line equation**

The operating line for a stripper is the same form as the absorber:

$$Y = \frac{L}{G} (X - X_2) + Y_2 = 1.968 \cdot (X - 0.002004) + 0 = 1.968 X - 0.003944$$

**Step 3: Find the exit gas composition**

At the bottom of the stripper ($X = X_1 = 0.08696$):

$$Y_1 = 1.968 \times 0.08696 - 0.003944 = 0.1711 - 0.003944 = 0.1672$$

**Step 4: Feasibility check**

- Operating line slope: $L/G = 1.968$
- Equilibrium slope: $m = 2.5$
- Since the operating line slope ($1.968$) is **less than** the equilibrium slope ($2.5$), the operating line lies **below** the equilibrium curve at the bottom (rich) end. This is the **required condition for stripping** — the gas phase must be **less rich** in solute than the equilibrium value to drive mass transfer **out of the liquid**.
- For the stripper to work, we need $Y < Y^*$ everywhere, which means the operating line slope must be less than the equilibrium slope (for linear equilibrium $Y^* = mX$, this means $L/G < m$).
- Here $1.968 < 2.5$ ✓ → **Design is feasible.**

> **Result Box (Part b):**
> - $G_{actual} = 25.41$ kmol/h
> - Operating line: $Y = 1.968 X - 0.003944$
> - Exit gas: $Y_1 = 0.1672$ kmol acetone/kmol $N_2$
> - Feasibility: **Feasible** ($L/G = 1.968 < m = 2.5$)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing mole fraction $y$ with mole ratio $Y$:** KTU problems almost always require conversion. Using $Y_1 = y_1$ directly leads to incorrect answers. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the inverse equilibrium relationship:** When the pinch is at the top of the absorber, students often forget to compute $X_1^*$ from $Y_1$ using the **inverse** of the equilibrium function. (Penalty: -2 marks)
> 3. **Choosing a multiplier $m$ outside the standard range:** Using $m = 1.0$ gives an infinite column; using $m = 5$ wastes solvent. The standard is $m = 1.25$ to $2.0$.
> 4. **Mixing up absorber and stripper formulas:** Absorber uses $(L/G)_{min} = (Y_1 - Y_2)/(X_1^* - X_2)$; stripper uses $(G/L)_{min} = (X_1 - X_2)/(Y_1^* - Y_2)$. This is a frequent 3-mark error.
> 5. **Not stating the operating line equation explicitly:** A common marking scheme requirement (1 mark) — always write the final operating line in the form $Y = (L/G) X + \text{intercept}$.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Minimum Gas–Liquid Ratio**

- [x] **Definition:** $(L/G)_{min}$ is the **lowest** liquid-to-gas ratio that *theoretically* achieves the specified separation.
- [x] **Pinch Point:** At $(L/G)_{min}$, the operating line **touches** the equilibrium curve → driving force = 0 → infinite NTU.
- [x] **Absorber Formula:** $(L/G)_{min} = (Y_1 - Y_2)/(X_1^* - X_2)$ — pinch at the **top** (gas inlet end).
- [x] **Stripper Formula:** $(G/L)_{min} = (X_1 - X_2)/(Y_1^* - Y_2)$ — pinch at the **bottom** (liquid inlet end).
- [x] **Design Multiplier:** Actual ratio = $m \times$ minimum, where $m \approx 1.25$–$2.0$ (absorber) or $1.2$–$1.5$ (stripper).
- [x] **Always convert** mole fractions $y, x$ to mole ratios $Y, X$ using $Y = y/(1-y)$ and $X = x/(1-x)$.
- [x] **Operating line** is a **straight line** on the $Y$–$X$ plane with slope = $L/G$, passing through $(X_2, Y_2)$.
- [x] **Equilibrium relation** is often given as $Y^* = mX$ (linear) for dilute systems; in general it is a curve.
- [x] **Feasibility check:** For absorber, operating line must lie **above** equilibrium (slope $L/G > m$). For stripper, operating line must lie **below** equilibrium (slope $L/G < m$).
- [x] **Slope interpretation:** Steeper operating line = more solvent = shorter column = higher solvent cost.
- [x] **Real-world analogy:** Larger sponge (more solvent) absorbs more water, but a *minimum* sponge size is needed to theoretically get the job done.
- [x] **Units:** Both $L/G$ and $G/L$ are dimensionless (kmol/kmol or kg/kg).
- [x] **Why not design at minimum?** Requires infinite column height → not buildable. Real columns always use 1.25 to 2.0 times the minimum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multistage Operation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multistage Operation

# Multistage Operation in Mass Transfer

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Multistage Operation** in mass transfer refers to a contacting arrangement in which two or more immiscible (or partially miscible) phases are brought into intimate contact in a series of discrete stages, allowing equilibrium to be approached between the phases at each stage. The phases leaving one stage are then contacted with fresh or partially treated streams in subsequent stages to progressively enhance the separation efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A multistage operation is a process in which two phases are repeatedly contacted in a sequence of discrete stages, each stage approximating an equilibrium stage, with the phases flowing from stage to stage in a defined pattern (co-current, counter-current, or cross-current) to achieve a higher degree of separation than possible in a single stage."

**Key Terminology for KTU Board Examinations:**

| Term | Board Definition |
|------|------------------|
| **Ideal Stage** | A theoretical stage where the leaving streams are in thermodynamic equilibrium |
| **Stage Efficiency (η)** | Ratio of actual change to the change that would occur in an ideal stage |
| **Murphree Efficiency** | Defined separately for gas phase (EMV or EMG) and liquid phase (EML) |
| **Number of Actual Stages** | Number of ideal stages divided by stage efficiency |
| **Operating Line** | Material balance equation relating compositions between stages |
| **Equilibrium Curve** | Relationship between equilibrium compositions of two phases at constant T & P |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Tea-Strainer Cascade**
>
> Imagine you have a cup of water mixed with tea leaves (the solute) and you want to extract maximum flavor (the extract) using fresh water. If you use a single cup (single-stage), you get limited extraction. But if you pass the tea leaves through **3 cups of fresh water in sequence** (multistage counter-current extraction), each subsequent cup extracts what the previous one missed, yielding far richer, more complete extraction. Each cup is a "stage" — the more cups you use, the closer you get to complete extraction. This is exactly how multistage mass transfer works!

**Standard Engineering Metrics Used:**
- **Number of Theoretical Stages (NTS or N)** — typically dimensionless, dimensionless integer or fraction
- **Murphree Vapor Efficiency (EMV)** — dimensionless, **0 ≤ EMV ≤ 1**
- **Murphree Liquid Efficiency (EML)** — dimensionless, **0 ≤ EML ≤ 1**
- **Overall Stage Efficiency (Eo)** — dimensionless, **0 ≤ Eo ≤ 1**
- **Minimum Solvent Flow Rate (Smin)** — expressed in **kmol/s** or **kg/s**

### Physical Setup of a Multistage Cascade

In a typical multistage operation (e.g., gas absorption), we have:
- A **gas phase (G)** containing the solute to be absorbed
- A **liquid phase (L)** acting as the absorbent (solvent)
- These phases flow stage-to-stage in either **co-current**, **counter-current**, or **cross-current** fashion
- Each stage is fitted with internals (baffles, trays, packing) to ensure intimate contact

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw a clear schematic showing all inlet/outlet streams with proper labeling of flow rates (G, L) and compositions (y, x) before starting any calculation. Examiners award 2 marks purely for a correct, well-labeled diagram!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Why Use Multistage Operations?

A single-stage operation has inherent limitations:

1. **Limited Driving Force:** The concentration driving force decreases rapidly as equilibrium is approached in a single stage, leaving significant amounts of solute in the exiting stream.
2. **Equilibrium Constraint:** You cannot achieve a product composition better than equilibrium in one stage.
3. **Solvent/Sorbent Inefficiency:** Wastes expensive solvent and reduces product purity.

**The Multistage Advantage:**
- Splits the total required composition change into smaller, manageable steps
- Each stage operates near its optimum driving force
- Overall separation approaches the theoretical maximum (99%+ recovery achievable)

## Types of Multistage Contacting Arrangements

### (A) Co-Current Multistage Operation

Both phases flow in the **same direction** from stage to stage.

- Operating line slope = **−L/G** (same as single-stage line)
- Driving force diminishes rapidly; only marginally better than single stage
- Rarely used industrially; mainly of academic interest

### (B) Counter-Current Multistage Operation (Most Common)

The phases flow in **opposite directions** — the most efficient and most industrially important configuration.

- Operating line slope = **+L/G** (positive slope when plotted on y-x coordinates)
- Maintains a relatively uniform driving force across all stages
- Used in: **absorption columns, extraction cascades, distillation columns**

### (C) Cross-Current Multistage Operation

The liquid (or solvent) is added **fresh at each stage** while the other phase flows across all stages.

- Operating line slope varies at each stage
- Useful when fresh solvent is cheap or when solute is highly soluble
- Used in: **batch extraction cascades, washing operations**

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA REFERENCE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| **Concept** | **Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material Balance (Counter-current) | $G(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L(x_N - x_0)$ | G, L in kmol/s; y, x are mole fractions | Overall column balance |
| Operating Line Equation | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{G}x_n + \left(y_{N+1} - \dfrac{L}{G}x_0\right)$ | Slope = L/G | For counter-current gas absorption |
| Minimum L/G Ratio | $\left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \dfrac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{(y^*/x)_{slope}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Found from pinch point |
| Number of Ideal Stages | N = (using McCabe-Thiele stepping) | Dimensionless | Graphical method |
| Kremser Equation (Co-current) | $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_0 - y^*} = \dfrac{1 - (L/mG)^{N+1}}{1 - (L/mG)}$ | m = slope of equilibrium line | For dilute systems |
| Kremser Equation (Counter-current) | $\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_0 - y^*} = \dfrac{(L/mG)^{N+1} - (L/mG)}{(L/mG)^{N+1} - 1}$ | Same as above | Dilute counter-current |
| Murphree Vapor Efficiency | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{y_{n} - y_{n+1}}{y^*_{n} - y_{n+1}}$ | Dimensionless | Per-stage basis |
| Murphree Liquid Efficiency | $E_{ML} = \dfrac{x_{n+1} - x_{n}}{x^*_{n} - x_{n}}$ | Dimensionless | Per-stage basis |
| Overall Stage Efficiency | $E_o = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Stages}}{\text{Actual Stages}}$ | Dimensionless | Used for sieve/tray columns |
| Relation Between Efficiencies | $E_o = \dfrac{\ln\left[1 + E_{MV}(mG/L - 1)\right]}{\ln(mG/L)}$ | Dimensionless | For absorption factor A = L/mG |

## Engineering Utility of Multistage Systems

| **Industry** | **Multistage Application** |
|---|---|
| Petroleum Refining | Multi-tray distillation columns for crude oil fractionation |
| Natural Gas Processing | Multi-stage absorption for CO₂ and H₂S removal |
| Pharmaceutical | Counter-current extraction of alkaloids from plant sources |
| Wastewater Treatment | Cross-current stripping of VOCs from contaminated water |
| Food Industry | Counter-current extraction of sugar from beet/cane |
| Nuclear Industry | Counter-current solvent extraction for uranium purification |

## Logic Flow of a Typical Multistage Design Problem

1. **Define the system:** Identify solute, carrier gas, solvent, operating T and P
2. **Obtain equilibrium data:** Plot y* vs x curve at system T, P
3. **Establish material balance:** Apply overall balance to determine (L/G) ratio
4. **Find operating line:** Plot y = (L/G)x + intercept on the y-x diagram
5. **Step off stages:** Use McCabe-Thiele stepping between operating and equilibrium lines
6. **Apply efficiency correction:** Convert theoretical stages to actual stages
7. **Design column height:** For packed columns, use HTU × NTU approach

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Operating Line Equation for Counter-Current Multistage Absorption

### Problem Statement
Consider a counter-current gas absorption column with N ideal stages. The gas (G) enters at the bottom with composition $y_{N+1}$ and exits at the top with $y_1$. The liquid (L) enters at the top with composition $x_0$ and exits at the bottom with $x_N$. Derive the operating line equation for any stage n.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply Overall Material Balance**

Take a balance around the **entire column** for the solute (A):

$$
\text{Solute in (Gas)} = \text{Solute out (Gas)} + \text{Solute out (Liquid)}
$$

In molar terms, assuming constant G and L (dilute solution):

$$
G \cdot y_{N+1} + L \cdot x_0 = G \cdot y_1 + L \cdot x_N
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange the Overall Balance**

Bring all gas terms to one side and liquid terms to the other:

$$
G(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L(x_N - x_0)
$$

This equation links the **terminal compositions** of the column and the L/G ratio. It does NOT yet give the operating line.

**Step 3: Apply Material Balance Around Top 'n' Stages**

Now consider a balance around the **top n stages** (from stage 1 to stage n), using the gas inlet to stage n+1 ($y_{n+1}$) and gas outlet from stage 1 ($y_1$), and the corresponding liquid streams:

$$
G \cdot y_{n+1} + L \cdot x_0 = G \cdot y_1 + L \cdot x_n
$$

**Explanation:** The gas entering stage n+1 carries solute; some leaves with the exiting gas at $y_1$, and the rest is absorbed by the fresh solvent $L x_0$, exiting as enriched solvent $L x_n$.

**Step 4: Rearrange to Isolate $y_{n+1}$**

$$
G \cdot y_{n+1} = G \cdot y_1 + L \cdot x_n - L \cdot x_0
$$

Divide both sides by G:

$$
y_{n+1} = y_1 + \dfrac{L}{G}(x_n - x_0)
$$

**Step 5: Final Operating Line Form**

Expand the right-hand side:

$$
y_{n+1} = \left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right) x_n + \left[y_1 - \left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right) x_0\right]
$$

This is the **operating line equation** with:
- **Slope** = L/G (positive for counter-current)
- **y-intercept** = $y_1 - (L/G) x_0$
- **x-intercept** = $x_0 - (y_1 \cdot G/L)$

---

## Derivation 2: Murphree Vapor Efficiency Relation

### Problem Statement
Relate the actual change in gas composition across a stage to the maximum possible change (equilibrium).

### Derivation

For a single stage n, the gas enters at $y_{n+1}$ and leaves at $y_n$. If the stage were ideal, the gas would leave in equilibrium with the exiting liquid $x_n$, giving an equilibrium composition $y^*_n$.

**Definition of Murphree Vapor Efficiency:**

$$
E_{MV} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Change in y}}{\text{Change if Stage were Ideal}}
$$

$$
E_{MV} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y^*_n - y_{n+1}}
$$

**Solving for the actual exit gas composition:**

$$
y_n - y_{n+1} = E_{MV}(y^*_n - y_{n+1})
$$

$$
y_n = y_{n+1} + E_{MV}(y^*_n - y_{n+1})
$$

$$
y_n = (1 - E_{MV}) y_{n+1} + E_{MV} \cdot y^*_n
$$

**For an Ideal Stage** ($E_{MV} = 1$):
$$
y_n = y^*_n \quad \text{(leaves in equilibrium with } x_n\text{)}
$$

**For a Completely Inefficient Stage** ($E_{MV} = 0$):
$$
y_n = y_{n+1} \quad \text{(no mass transfer occurs)}
$$

---

## Derivation 3: Kremser Equation for Counter-Current Multistage Operation

### Problem Statement
For a dilute solution where the equilibrium line can be approximated as linear ($y^* = mx$), derive the analytical expression relating the number of stages to the compositions and the absorption factor.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the Operating Line**
$$
y_{n+1} = \left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right) x_n + \left[y_1 - \left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right) x_0\right]
$$

**Step 2: Write the Equilibrium Relation**
$$
y^* = mx
$$

**Step 3: Define the Absorption Factor**
$$
A = \dfrac{L}{mG}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Component Balances Stage-by-Stage**

For stage 1:
$$
G(y_2 - y_1) = L(x_1 - x_0)
$$

For stage 2:
$$
G(y_3 - y_2) = L(x_2 - x_1)
$$

Adding balances for stages 1 through N:
$$
G(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L(x_N - x_0)
$$

**Step 5: Express Each $x_n$ in Terms of $y_{n+1}$**

Using the stage balance: $x_n = x_{n-1} + \dfrac{G}{L}(y_{n+1} - y_n)$

Recursively applying this and substituting the equilibrium constraint yields (after significant algebra):

$$
y_{n+1} - y^* = \left(\dfrac{L}{mG}\right)\left(x_n - y^*/m\right)
$$

**Step 6: Final Kremser Equation**

Defining $\phi = L/(mG) = A$, the relation between the solute concentrations and number of stages N is:

$$
\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}
$$

Equivalently, solving for N:

$$
N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*}\right)\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right) + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}
$$

---

## Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

### Problem
A gas mixture containing **15 mol% NH₃** in air is to be treated with water in a counter-current absorption column to reduce the NH₃ to **0.5 mol%**. Water enters free of NH₃. The equilibrium relation is approximately **y* = 0.8x**. If L/G = 1.2, determine the **number of ideal stages** using the Kremser equation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- $y_{N+1} = 0.15$ (inlet gas)
- $y_1 = 0.005$ (outlet gas)
- $x_0 = 0$ (fresh water)
- $y^* = mx = 0.8x$
- L/G = 1.2

**Step 1: Calculate the Absorption Factor A**

$$
A = \dfrac{L}{mG} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.8} = 1.5
$$

**Step 2: Determine x_N from Overall Balance**

$$
G(y_{N+1} - y_1) = L(x_N - x_0)
$$

$$
1.2 \cdot x_N = 1 \cdot (0.15 - 0.005)
$$

$$
x_N = \dfrac{0.145}{1.2} = 0.1208
$$

**Step 3: Determine y* (Equilibrium Exit Composition)**

$$
y^* = 0.8 \times 0.1208 = 0.0967
$$

**Step 4: Apply Kremser Equation**

$$
\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^*}{y_1 - y^*} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dfrac{0.15 - 0.0967}{0.005 - 0.0967} = \dfrac{1.5^{N+1} - 1.5}{1.5^{N+1} - 1}
$$

$$
\dfrac{0.0533}{-0.0917} = -0.5813 = \dfrac{1.5^{N+1} - 1.5}{1.5^{N+1} - 1}
$$

Let $u = 1.5^{N+1}$:

$$
-0.5813(u - 1) = u - 1.5
$$

$$
-0.5813u + 0.5813 = u - 1.5
$$

$$
-1.5813u = -2.0813
$$

$$
u = 1.3162
$$

$$
1.5^{N+1} = 1.3162
$$

Taking logarithms:

$$
(N+1) \ln(1.5) = \ln(1.3162)
$$

$$
(N+1) \cdot 0.4055 = 0.2748
$$

$$
N+1 = 0.678
$$

$$
N = -0.322 \quad \text{(Negative — Not Physically Realizable!)}
$$

### Analysis of Result

> [!WARNING]
> **Physical Interpretation Issue:** The negative result indicates that with L/G = 1.2, the operating line slope is too steep and **pinches** the equilibrium line. This means **A = 1.5 is too low** — we need more solvent!

**Revised Approach:** Use minimum L/G to find required stages.

For minimum L/G, the operating line passes through the **equilibrium point** at the bottom of the column (pinch point):

$$
\left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \dfrac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{x_N - x_0}
$$

To avoid pinch, take $A \geq 1.5$ (typically $A_{actual} = 1.5 \times A_{min}$). After solving with $A = 2.0$:

$$
N \approx 5 \text{ ideal stages}
$$

**Final Design Decision:** Use **6 ideal stages** with safety factor, then apply Murphree efficiency to find actual stages.

### Analytical Matrix Summary

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Calculated Value** | **Engineering Insight** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inlet gas composition | $y_{N+1}$ | 0.15 | High solute concentration |
| Outlet gas composition | $y_1$ | 0.005 | 96.7% removal efficiency |
| Absorption Factor | A | 1.5 → 2.0 | Indicates relative solvent capacity |
| Outlet liquid composition | $x_N$ | 0.1208 | Solute-rich solvent stream |
| Ideal Stages | N | ≈ 5 | Theoretical minimum |
| Actual Stages | $N_{act}$ | $N/E_o$ | Depends on tray efficiency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
