$$x_{N,i} = \frac{V}{L} (y_{N+1,i} - y_{1,i})$$

Since $y_{1,i} \ll y_{N+1,i}$ (deeply absorbed), we can approximate $x_{N,i} \approx (V/L) y_{N+1,i}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{N,\text{acetone}} &\approx (50/200) \times 0.040 = 0.0100 \\
x_{N,\text{methanol}} &\approx (50/200) \times 0.025 = 0.00625 \\
x_{N,\text{ethanol}} &\approx (50/200) \times 0.015 = 0.00375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Operating-Line Endpoint Check**

The operating line endpoint at the bottom of the column:

$$y_1^* = m \cdot x_N$$

For acetone: $y_1^* = 1.05 \times 0.0100 = 0.0105$ mol frac.

This is the **gas composition in equilibrium with the rich outlet liquid**. Our actual $y_1$ from Kremser is $9.07 \times 10^{-7}$, which is **way below** $y_1^*$. Consistency check: $y_1 < y_1^*$ ✓ (since the gas leaves well below equilibrium with the bottom liquid — characteristic of a well-designed absorber).

**Step 3: Effect of Increasing Gas Flow by 50%**

If $V \to 1.5 V = 75$ kmol/h, holding $L$ constant:

- **Absorption factors drop by 50%**: $A_i^{\text{new}} = A_i / 1.5$
- $A_1^{\text{new}} = 2.54$, $A_2^{\text{new}} = 3.14$, $A_3^{\text{new}} = 1.90$
- With $N = 8$ fixed, $A^{8}$ drops sharply: $A_1^{8} = 1.05 \times 10^{3}$ (vs. $4.4 \times 10^{4}$ before)
- **Outlet $y_1$ rises by ~40×** (e.g., acetone from $9 \times 10^{-7}$ to $\sim 3.8 \times 10^{-5}$)
- **To restore spec, $N$ must increase significantly** — qualitatively, $N$ might need to rise from 8 to ~12 stages
- The **ethanol** component (smallest $A$) is the **most sensitive** (its $y_1$ rises disproportionately)

**Engineering implication:** The bottleneck component (smallest $A$, here ethanol) **dictates the design**. The KTU microproject should report this explicitly.

**[7 marks: 2.5 (xN calculation) + 2.0 (consistency check) + 2.5 (qualitative 50% V increase analysis)]**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARK-LOSING MISTAKES IN COMPUTATIONAL ABSORPTION (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $m$ to a consistent basis.** $m$ from Raoult's law ($y^* = Kx$) is a **vapour–liquid $K$-value**, but absorption problems often use $m = y^*/x$ in mole-fraction units. **Always check units.** (−1 to −2 marks)
>
> 2. **Using $A < 1$ silently in the standard Kremser equation.** The form $\Phi = (A^{N+1}-A)/(A^{N+1}-1)$ is valid for $A \neq 1$. For $A = 1$, the **L'Hôpital limit** gives $\Phi = N/(N+1)$ — students often misapply the formula. (−2 marks)
>
> 3. **Confusing mole ratios with mole fractions** in the material balance: $Y = y/(1-y)$ vs. $y$. Henry's law in terms of $Y$ uses different constants. (−1 to −3 marks)
>
> 4. **Rounding $N$ DOWN instead of UP** when the calculated theoretical stages are non-integer (e.g., 3.52 → 4 stages, **not** 3). The column must perform at least the calculated amount of work. (−1 mark)
>
> 5. **Not validating the solver output** by back-substitution. A solver may converge to a spurious root if the initial guess is poor. Always check $f(N) < 10^{-6}$ and physically verify $y_1 < y_{N+1}$. (−1 mark, plus "methodology" marks lost)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Print and Pin to Wall**

### Core Equations
- [x] Kremser (Fractional absorption): $\Phi = (A^{N+1}-A)/(A^{N+1}-1)$ for $A \neq 1$
- [x] Kremser limit $A=1$: $\Phi = N/(N+1)$
- [x] Absorption Factor: $A = L/(mV)$
- [x] Stripping Factor: $S = mV/L = 1/A$
- [x] Operating line (constant molar overflow): $y_{n+1} = (L/V) x_n + y_{N+1} - (L/V) x_0$
- [x] Equilibrium (linear/dilute): $y^* = m x$

### Numerical Workflow
- [x] Define residual $f(N) = \Phi(N) - \Phi_{\text{target}}$
- [x] Verify monotonicity (always true for $A > 1$ or $A < 1$)
- [x] Choose solver: Excel Goal Seek (1 var), Excel Solver (multi-var), MATLAB `fsolve`/`fzero`, Python `scipy.optimize.brentq`/`fsolve`
- [x] Provide bracket for bisection-based methods
- [x] Validate by back-substitution and operating-line check

### Multicomponent
- [x] Each component has its own $A_i = L/(m_i V)$
- [x] Operating-line slope $L/V$ is **common** to all components
- [x] Bottleneck component (smallest $A_i$) **dictates** the number of stages
- [x] Nested loop: bisect $N$ → solve component-wise Kremser

### Common Pitfalls
- [x] Units of $m$
- [x] Rounding direction of $N$
- [x] Spurious roots in non-monotonic regions
- [x] Neglecting Murphree efficiency (use $N_{\text{actual}} = N_{\text{theoretical}} / E_{MV}$ or $E_{MP}$)

### Engineering Judgment
- [x] The minimum solvent rate corresponds to the operating line intersecting the equilibrium line (infinite stages). Real design uses $L_{\text{actual}} = 1.5 L_{\min}$.
- [x] For environmental applications, the bottleneck is usually the **trace** (lowest-concentration, smallest-$A$) component.
- [x] Kremser assumes **constant molar overflow** and **linear equilibrium**; for non-ideal cases, switch to a stage-by-stage Newton solver.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Viva Tip:** If an examiner asks "Why did you choose Brent's method over Newton–Raphson?", your answer should be: *"Because Brent's method combines the guaranteed convergence of bisection with the speed of higher-order interpolation, making it robust for nearly-singular residuals — exactly the situation in low-$A$ absorption where the operating and equilibrium lines nearly pinch."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Humidity Chart to Find Properties of Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Use of Humidity Chart to Find Properties of Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Humidity Chart (Psychrometric Chart):** A graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of an air-water vapour system at a constant total pressure (typically **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**), plotted as **Absolute Humidity (kg water vapour/kg dry air)** versus **Dry Bulb Temperature (°C or K)**, with families of curves for Relative Humidity, Wet Bulb Temperature, Enthalpy, Specific Volume, and Humid Heat.

The chart provides a **one-glance visualisation** of how the moisture-carrying capacity of air varies with temperature, allowing direct graphical determination of all psychrometric properties used in drying, humidification, dehumidification, air-conditioning, and cooling tower design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mastery of reading the humidity chart is essential for solving problems involving **air-water contact equipment** (cooling towers, driers, scrubbers) — a compulsory topic in Module 3 of PCCHT403.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Think of the humidity chart as a "moisture capacity map" for air.** Imagine air as a sponge and water vapour as the water it can soak up:

- **Hot air** = a large sponge → it can hold a *lot* of water vapour.
- **Cold air** = a small sponge → it can only hold a little water vapour.
- The **100% saturation line** = the point where the sponge is *completely* full. Any extra water will "drip out" as liquid (i.e., **dew forms**).

If you know **two properties** of an air sample (say, dry-bulb temperature and relative humidity), the chart is like a coordinate map — the intersection of those two values gives you a single point, from which you can read off **every other property** (wet-bulb temperature, dew point, enthalpy, specific volume, humid heat, etc.) just by following the appropriate lines.

> **Everyday Example:** On a humid Kerala monsoon day, the air feels "heavy" because the relative humidity is near 100% — the air-sponge is almost saturated. On a hot, dry summer afternoon in Palakkad, the same air-sponge is mostly empty, which is why sweat evaporates quickly and your skin feels cool.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and reference states are used throughout humidity-chart calculations:
> - **Molar mass of water, M_w = 18.015 kg/kmol**
> - **Molar mass of air (dry), M_air = 28.97 kg/kmol**
> - **Ratio M_w/M_air ≈ 0.622**
> - **Reference temperature T_ref = 0 °C (273.15 K)** for enthalpy calculations
> - **Latent heat of vaporisation at 0 °C, λ_ref = 2501 kJ/kg**
> - **Specific heat of dry air, c_s = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Specific heat of water vapour, c_w = 1.884 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Specific heat of liquid water, c_L = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**
> - **Gas constant for dry air, R_a = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Standard total pressure, P = 101.325 kPa = 1 atm**

These values are **bolded** because they appear as constants in nearly every humidity-chart based numerical problem in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.4 The Six Readable Properties from a Humidity Chart

For a fixed pressure (usually 1 atm), a humidity chart allows graphical determination of the following six fundamental properties:

| Sl. No. | Property | Symbol | Common Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute Humidity (Moisture content) | H or Y | kg water/kg dry air |
| 2 | Relative Humidity | φ (phi) | % (fraction) |
| 3 | Dry Bulb Temperature | T (DBT) | °C |
| 4 | Wet Bulb Temperature | T_wb | °C |
| 5 | Dew Point Temperature | T_dp | °C |
| 6 | Enthalpy of moist air | i or h | kJ/kg dry air |
| 7 | Specific Volume | v | m³/kg dry air |
| 8 | Humid Heat | c_H | kJ/(kg dry air·K) |
| 9 | Adiabatic Saturation Temperature | T_as | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If you know **any two** independent psychrometric properties of an air sample, you can determine the **other seven** either analytically OR graphically from the chart. This is the most-tested KTU exam concept.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Humidity Chart (Structural Logic)

The chart is built using the following layered structure:

**Step 1 — X-axis:** Dry Bulb Temperature, plotted linearly from **0 °C to ~120 °C** (left to right).

**Step 2 — Y-axis:** Absolute Humidity, H, plotted in kg water/kg dry air, from **0 to ~0.6 kg/kg**, **non-linearly** (tilted axis layout) for greater resolution at low humidities.

**Step 3 — 100% Saturation Curve (φ = 1):** The upper boundary representing air saturated with moisture. This is the **most important curve** because it represents the maximum moisture-carrying capacity of air at each temperature.

**Step 4 — Relative Humidity Curves (φ = constant):** A family of curves drawn *below* the 100% saturation line, for φ = 10%, 20%, 30%, …, 90%. Each curve represents the locus of air states that have a given fractional saturation at varying temperatures.

**Step 5 — Wet Bulb Temperature / Adiabatic Saturation Lines:** A family of straight lines (with negative slope) that run obliquely from lower-right to upper-left, terminating at the saturation curve. These lines allow reading of wet-bulb and adiabatic-saturation temperatures.

**Step 6 — Enthalpy Lines (i = constant):** Parallel lines to the wet-bulb lines but displaced slightly. They allow direct reading of moist-air enthalpy.

**Step 7 — Specific Volume Lines (v = constant):** Nearly vertical lines (slightly tilted), giving the volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air plus its associated vapour.

> [!NOTE]
> For air-water systems, the **wet-bulb temperature and adiabatic saturation temperature are numerically identical** at atmospheric pressure — a key KTU 2024 fact.

---

## 2.2 The Six Governing Equations of the Humidity Chart

These equations together define the entire thermodynamic space of the chart:

### **Equation 1 — Absolute Humidity (H)**

$$H = \frac{18}{29} \cdot \frac{P_w}{P - P_w} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P - P_w}$$

where $P_w$ is the partial pressure of water vapour and $P$ is the total system pressure. Note that $0.622$ is the constant $M_w/M_{air}$.

### **Equation 2 — Saturated Humidity (H_s)**

$$H_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_s}{P - P_s}$$

where $P_s$ is the saturation pressure of water at the dry-bulb temperature. This is the humidity corresponding to the saturation curve at any given T.

### **Equation 3 — Relative Humidity (φ)**

$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_s}$$

(φ is dimensionless, expressed as a fraction or percentage)

### **Equation 4 — Dew Point**

The dew point is the temperature at which the actual partial pressure $P_w$ equals the saturation pressure $P_s$ of water. Graphically, this is found by moving **horizontally to the left** from the state point until it strikes the **100% saturation curve**, then dropping down to the temperature axis.

### **Equation 5 — Enthalpy of Moist Air (per kg dry air)**

$$i = c_s (T - T_{ref}) + H \cdot \lambda_{ref} + H \cdot c_w (T - T_{ref})$$

$$\boxed{i = (c_s + H \cdot c_w)(T - T_{ref}) + H \cdot \lambda_{ref}}$$

With $T_{ref} = 0\ °C$, $c_s = 1.005\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $c_w = 1.884\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $\lambda_{ref} = 2501\ \text{kJ/kg}$:

$$i = (1.005 + 1.884\,H)\,T + 2501\,H \quad [\text{kJ/kg dry air}]$$

where T is in °C.

### **Equation 6 — Humid Heat (c_H)**

$$c_H = c_s + H \cdot c_w = 1.005 + 1.884\,H \quad [\text{kJ/(kg dry air·K)}]$$

### **Equation 7 — Specific Volume of Moist Air (per kg dry air)**

$$v = \frac{R_a \, T}{P - P_w} = \frac{0.287\,T_{abs}}{P - P_w} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}]$$

where $T_{abs}$ is in Kelvin.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table as your one-stop reference when solving KTU numericals on humidity charts.**

| Property | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absolute Humidity, H | $H = 0.622 \cdot P_w / (P - P_w)$ | kg water/kg dry air | $P_w$ = partial pressure of water |
| Saturated Humidity, $H_s$ | $H_s = 0.622 \cdot P_s / (P - P_s)$ | kg water/kg dry air | $P_s$ = saturation pressure at T |
| Relative Humidity, φ | $\phi = P_w / P_s$ | fraction (or %) | $0 \le \phi \le 1$ |
| Dew Point, $T_{dp}$ | $T_{dp} = T$ at which $P_s(T_{dp}) = P_w$ | °C | Read from saturation curve |
| Enthalpy, i | $i = (1.005 + 1.884\,H)T + 2501\,H$ | kJ/kg dry air | Reference: 0 °C, 0 °C liquid water |
| Humid Heat, $c_H$ | $c_H = 1.005 + 1.884\,H$ | kJ/(kg dry air·K) | Slope of saturation line in (H,T) plot |
| Specific Volume, v | $v = 0.287\,T_{abs} / (P - P_w)$ | m³/kg dry air | Uses $T_{abs}$ in Kelvin |
| Adiabatic Saturation, $T_{as}$ | From energy balance: $c_H(T_{as} - T) = \lambda_{as}(H_{as} - H)$ | °C | Numerically equal to $T_{wb}$ for air-water |
| Wet Bulb, $T_{wb}$ | Read off wet-bulb line through state point | °C | Equal to $T_{as}$ for air-water at 1 atm |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Drying Operations (Module 3 core):** Determining the moisture content, drying capacity, and final state of air used in dryers.
- **Air-Conditioning and HVAC Systems:** Designing comfort-air specifications.
- **Cooling Towers:** Calculating water-make-up, blow-down, and evaporation losses.
- **Storage of Hygroscopic Materials (grains, chemicals, pharmaceuticals):** Predicting moisture pickup/loss.
- **Evaporative Cooling:** Predicting achievable temperatures under adiabatic saturation.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Humidity Equation (H = 0.622 Pw / (P − Pw))

Let us derive the fundamental humidity equation from first principles of partial pressure and ideal gas law.

**Step 1 — Define partial pressures.**
For moist air as an ideal-gas mixture:

$$P = P_a + P_w$$

where $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air, $P_w$ = partial pressure of water vapour, and $P$ = total pressure.

**Step 2 — Apply ideal gas law to each component.**

$$P_a V = n_a R T \quad \text{and} \quad P_w V = n_w R T$$

**Step 3 — Form the mol ratio (n_w / n_a).**

$$\frac{n_w}{n_a} = \frac{P_w}{P_a} = \frac{P_w}{P - P_w}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass ratio using molar masses.**

$$H = \frac{m_w}{m_a} = \frac{n_w \cdot M_w}{n_a \cdot M_{air}} = \frac{n_w}{n_a} \cdot \frac{M_w}{M_{air}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$H = \frac{P_w}{P - P_w} \cdot \frac{M_w}{M_{air}} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} \cdot \frac{P_w}{P - P_w}$$

$$\boxed{H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P - P_w}}$$

This is the canonical humidity equation used throughout the humidity chart.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Enthalpy of Moist Air

**Step 1 — Definition.** The enthalpy of moist air is referenced per **kg of dry air**, with both dry air and liquid water at $T_{ref} = 0\ °C$ as the datum.

**Step 2 — Three contributions to total enthalpy:**

(i) Sensible heat of dry air: $c_s (T - T_{ref})$
(ii) Sensible heat of water vapour: $H \cdot c_w (T - T_{ref})$
(iii) Latent heat of vaporisation carried by H kg of vapour: $H \cdot \lambda_{ref}$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$i = c_s (T - T_{ref}) + H \cdot c_w (T - T_{ref}) + H \cdot \lambda_{ref}$$

**Step 4 — Group terms.**

$$i = (c_s + H \cdot c_w)(T - T_{ref}) + H \cdot \lambda_{ref}$$

**Step 5 — Insert numerical constants (T_ref = 0 °C, T in °C):**

$$i = (1.005 + 1.884\,H)\,T + 2501\,H \quad [\text{kJ/kg dry air}]$$

This is the equation that defines the **enthalpy lines (i = constant)** drawn on the humidity chart.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Adiabatic Saturation Temperature

**Step 1 — Physical model.** Air at temperature T with humidity H is brought into perfect contact with water in an **adiabatic saturator**. Liquid water evaporates into the air, cooling it, until the air leaves at temperature $T_{as}$ and saturated humidity $H_{as}$.

**Step 2 — Mass balance on water.** Let $L$ kg water/kg dry air be added. Then:

$$H + L = H_{as} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L = H_{as} - H$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance (adiabatic, steady-state).**

$$i_{\text{in (air)}} + L \cdot c_L (T - T_{ref}) = i_{\text{out (air)}}$$

The water entering is at the air temperature T, and leaves at $T_{as}$, so its enthalpy change must be included:

$$c_H(T) \cdot T + H \cdot \lambda_0 + L \cdot c_L (T - T_{as}) = c_H(T_{as}) \cdot T_{as} + H_{as} \cdot \lambda_0$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $L = H_{as} - H$ and simplifying (assuming $c_L \approx c_w$ and the Lewis number ≈ 1 for air-water):**

$$(c_s + H c_w)(T_{as} - T) = \lambda_{as}(H_{as} - H)$$

$$\boxed{c_H (T_{as} - T) = \lambda_{as}(H_{as} - H)}$$

This transcendental equation is solved iteratively (or read directly from the humidity chart) to obtain $T_{as}$. For air-water, $T_{as} = T_{wb}$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Reading Six Properties from the Humidity Chart

> **KTU Numerical Format:** The chart is read graphically; here we show the analytical equivalents used to verify the graphical readings.

**Given:** Air at **T = 40 °C** and **φ = 40%**, total pressure **P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa**.
**Required:** H, $H_s$, $P_w$, $T_{dp}$, $T_{wb}$ (≈ $T_{as}$), i, $c_H$, v.

**Step 1 — Look up saturation pressure at 40 °C from steam tables:**

$$P_s(40\ °C) = 7.375\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate actual partial pressure of water:**

$$P_w = \phi \cdot P_s = 0.40 \times 7.375 = 2.950\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Humidity:**

$$H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P - P_w} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.950}{101.325 - 2.950}$$

$$H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.950}{98.375} = 0.622 \times 0.02998 = 0.01865\ \text{kg water/kg dry air}$$

**Step 4 — Saturated Humidity (at 40 °C):**

$$H_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{7.375}{101.325 - 7.375} = 0.622 \times 0.07848 = 0.04882\ \text{kg/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Dew Point.** $T_{dp}$ is the temperature at which $P_s = P_w = 2.950$ kPa. From steam tables, $P_s = 2.950$ kPa corresponds to $T_{dp} \approx 23.6\ °C$.

**Step 6 — Enthalpy:**

$$i = (1.005 + 1.884 \times 0.01865) \times 40 + 2501 \times 0.01865$$

$$i = (1.005 + 0.03514) \times 40 + 46.65 = 1.0401 \times 40 + 46.65$$

$$i = 41.60 + 46.65 = 88.25\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Step 7 — Humid Heat:**

$$c_H = 1.005 + 1.884 \times 0.01865 = 1.040\ \text{kJ/(kg dry air·K)}$$

**Step 8 — Specific Volume:**

$$v = \frac{0.287 \times (40 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 2.950} = \frac{0.287 \times 313.15}{98.375}$$

$$v = \frac{89.86}{98.375} = 0.9135\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$

---

## 3.5 Verification with Graphical Humidity Chart

> [!NOTE]
> On a standard humidity chart (Carrier-style), a state point at T = 40 °C, φ = 40% is located in the lower-left region of the saturation envelope. Reading off:
> - **H ≈ 0.0187 kg/kg** ✓ (matches Step 3)
> - **T_dp ≈ 23.5 °C** ✓ (matches Step 5)
> - **T_wb ≈ 26.5 °C** ✓ (read off the wet-bulb line through the point)
> - **i ≈ 88 kJ/kg dry air** ✓ (matches Step 6)
> - **v ≈ 0.91 m³/kg dry air** ✓ (matches Step 8)
> - **c_H ≈ 1.04 kJ/(kg·K)** ✓ (matches Step 7)

This confirms that the **chart and the analytical formulas are mutually consistent** — a critical KTU 2024 board-exam expectation.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Humidity Chart

The following diagram maps the **interactions** between the various property curves on a humidity chart. This is the safe Mermaid-compatible approach for representing the complex multi-line chart.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[X-AXIS: DRY BULB TEMPERATURE T IN C] --> F[CHART REFERENCE FRAME]
    B[Y-AXIS: ABSOLUTE HUMIDITY H IN KG PER KG DRY AIR] --> F
    F --> S[SATURATION CURVE PHI EQUALS 100 PERCENT UPPER BOUNDARY]
    F --> R[FAMILY OF PHI EQUALS 10 20 30 ... 90 PERCENT CURVES]
    F --> W[FAMILY OF WET BULB AND ADIABATIC SATURATION LINES NEGATIVE SLOPE]
    F --> E[FAMILY OF ENTHALPY LINES I EQUALS CONSTANT NEARLY PARALLEL TO WB LINES]
    F --> V[SPECIFIC VOLUME LINES V EQUALS CONSTANT NEARLY VERTICAL]
    F --> C[HUMID HEAT SCALE ON ENTHALPY AXIS]
    S --> P[STATE POINT DEFINED BY ANY TWO KNOWN PROPERTIES]
    R --> P
    P --> O1[OUTPUT H ABSOLUTE HUMIDITY]
    P --> O2[OUTPUT PHI RELATIVE HUMIDITY]
    P --> O3[OUTPUT TWB WET BULB TEMPERATURE]
    P --> O4[OUTPUT TDP DEW POINT TEMPERATURE]
    P --> O5[OUTPUT I ENTHALPY]
    P --> O6[OUTPUT V SPECIFIC VOLUME]
    P --> O7[OUTPUT CH HUMID HEAT]
    P --> O8[OUTPUT TAS ADIABATIC SATURATION TEMPERATURE]
```

---

## 4.2 Flowchart for Reading a Property from the Humidity Chart

This diagram shows the **decision tree** a student should follow when solving a humidity-chart problem in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[START: KNOWN PROPERTIES OF AIR] --> Q1{WHAT TWO PROPERTIES ARE KNOWN}
    Q1 -->|T AND PHI| R1[PLOT POINT AT INTERSECTION OF T AND PHI CURVE]
    Q1 -->|T AND TWB| R2[FOLLOW TWB LINE FROM TWB ON SAT CURVE TO TEMPERATURE T]
    Q1 -->|T AND TDP| R3[MOVE HORIZONTALLY FROM TDP ON SAT CURVE TO TEMPERATURE T]
    Q1 -->|I AND PHI| R4[INTERSECTION OF ENTHALPY LINE AND PHI CURVE]
    Q1 -->|I AND TWB| R5[INTERSECTION OF ENTHALPY LINE AND TWB LINE]
    R1 --> S[STATE POINT LOCATED]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
    S --> OUT[READ REMAINING PROPERTIES BY FOLLOWING CORRESPONDING CURVES THROUGH STATE POINT]
    OUT --> OUT1[ABSOLUTE HUMIDITY H FROM Y AXIS]
    OUT --> OUT2[DEW POINT BY HORIZONTAL MOVE TO SAT CURVE]
    OUT --> OUT3[WET BULB ALONG WB LINE TO SAT CURVE]
    OUT --> OUT4[ENTHALPY FROM ENTHALPY SCALE]
    OUT --> OUT5[SPECIFIC VOLUME FROM V SCALE]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Adiabatic Saturator (Source of Wet-Bulb Lines on the Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INLET[INLET AIR T H] -->|TEMP T| MIX[ADIABATIC SATURATOR TOWER]
    INLET -->|HUMIDITY H| MIX
    WATER[WATER IN AT TEMPERATURE T] -->|EVAPORATES| MIX
    MIX -->|WATER LEAVES| OUTW[WATER OUT AT T AS]
    MIX -->|SATURATED AIR LEAVES| OUTA[OUTLET AIR T AS H AS]
    OUTA -.->|TAS| MEAS[MEASURE TEMPERATURE AND HUMIDITY]
    MEAS -->|READ CHART| WB[READ TWB AND H AS ON HUMIDITY CHART]
    WB -->|JOIN TWB LINE| LINE[WET BULB LINE CONNECTS INLET TO SAT POINT]
```

This schematic illustrates the **physical origin** of the wet-bulb / adiabatic saturation lines drawn on the chart. The wet-bulb line on the chart is a straight line connecting the inlet air state to the adiabatic saturation point on the φ = 100% curve.

---

## 4.4 Construction Layers of the Humidity Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[BARE CHART WITH X Y AXES] --> L1[LAYER 1: DRAW 100 PERCENT SATURATION CURVE]
    L1 --> L2[LAYER 2: DRAW RELATIVE HUMIDITY CURVES BELOW SATURATION]
    L2 --> L3[LAYER 3: DRAW WET BULB AND ADIABATIC SATURATION LINES]
    L3 --> L4[LAYER 4: DRAW ENTHALPY LINES]
    L4 --> L5[LAYER 5: DRAW SPECIFIC VOLUME LINES]
    L5 --> L6[LAYER 6: ADD HUMID HEAT SCALE]
    L6 --> L7[FINAL CHART READY FOR READING]
```

This layered architecture makes explicit **the order in which the chart is built** — useful for KTU viva questions on the construction of a humidity chart.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define absolute humidity and relative humidity. How are they related to the partial pressure of water vapour?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Absolute Humidity (H):** The mass of water vapour carried by one kilogram of **dry air**. It is calculated as:

$$H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P - P_w} \quad [\text{kg water/kg dry air}]$$

**Relative Humidity (φ):** The ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour to the saturation pressure of water at the same temperature.

$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_s} \quad \text{(dimensionless, expressed as percentage)}$$

**Relationship:** From the humidity equation, solving for $P_w$:

$$P_w = \frac{H \cdot P}{0.622 + H}$$

Substituting into the relative humidity expression gives the link between H, φ, and the saturation pressure. Thus, both H and φ can be read directly from the humidity chart at a given state point.

---

### **Question 2: Explain the term "wet-bulb temperature" and state its relation to the adiabatic saturation temperature for an air-water system.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Wet-Bulb Temperature ($T_{wb}$):** It is the dynamic equilibrium temperature a water-wetted surface attains when exposed to a moving stream of unsaturated air. It lies between the dry-bulb temperature and the dew point.

**Adiabatic Saturation Temperature ($T_{as}$):** It is the temperature reached by air when it is brought into contact with water in a perfectly insulated (adiabatic) chamber, where the water evaporates until the air becomes saturated.

**Relation:** For an **air-water system at atmospheric pressure**, the wet-bulb temperature and the adiabatic saturation temperature are **numerically equivalent** ($T_{wb} \approx T_{as}$), even though they are conceptually different. This equality arises because the Lewis number for the air-water system is approximately unity. This is why the **same family of lines** on the humidity chart serves as both wet-bulb and adiabatic saturation lines.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common error 1:** Using $P_w$ instead of $P - P_w$ in the denominator of the humidity equation. (Lose 1 mark.)
> - **Common error 2:** Forgetting to convert °C to K in the specific volume formula. (Lose 1 mark.)
> - **Common error 3:** Confusing relative humidity curves with absolute humidity values. (Lose 0.5–1 mark.)
> - **Common error 4:** Reading dew point by going **down** to the temperature axis instead of moving **horizontally to the saturation curve first**. (Lose 1 mark.)
> - **Common error 5:** Mixing up reference enthalpies (use **0 °C** for both air and water for the KTU 2024 scheme). (Lose 1 mark.)
> - **Common error 6:** Not labelling units. Always write "kg water/kg dry air" or "kJ/kg dry air" — *per kg of dry air*, never per kg of moist air. (Lose 0.5 mark.)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A sample of air at 1 atm total pressure has a dry-bulb temperature of **35 °C** and a wet-bulb temperature of **26 °C**. Using the humidity chart (or analytical equations), determine:
1. Absolute humidity, H
2. Relative humidity, φ
3. Dew point, $T_{dp}$
4. Enthalpy of the moist air
5. Humid heat, $c_H$

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an air-water system at 1 atm, the following values are reported:
- T = 60 °C
- φ = 30%

Calculate:
1. Partial pressure of water vapour
2. Absolute humidity
3. Saturated humidity at 60 °C
4. Specific volume per kg of dry air

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

### **Part (a): T = 35 °C, $T_{wb}$ = 26 °C, P = 1 atm**

**Step 1 — Saturation pressure at the wet-bulb temperature (which equals $T_{as}$ for air-water at 1 atm).**

From steam tables, $P_s(26\ °C) = 3.363$ kPa.

Since the air leaves the adiabatic saturator saturated, $H_{as}$ can be calculated:

$$H_{as} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.363}{101.325 - 3.363} = 0.622 \times 0.03431 = 0.02134\ \text{kg/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the adiabatic saturation equation to find H.**

$$c_H (T_{as} - T) = \lambda_{as}(H_{as} - H)$$

where $c_H = 1.005 + 1.884\,H$, $T_{as} = 26\ °C$, $T = 35\ °C$, $\lambda_{as}$ = latent heat at 26 °C ≈ 2438 kJ/kg.

Substituting:

$$(1.005 + 1.884\,H)(26 - 35) = 2438 \times (0.02134 - H)$$

$$-(1.005 + 1.884\,H)(9) = 2438 \times (0.02134 - H)$$

$$-9.045 - 16.956\,H = 52.04 - 2438\,H$$

$$2438\,H - 16.956\,H = 52.04 + 9.045$$

$$2421.04\,H = 61.085 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H = 0.02523\ \text{kg/kg}$$

So, **H ≈ 0.0252 kg water/kg dry air**.

**Step 3 — Relative Humidity.**

$P_w$ from H:

$$P_w = \frac{H \cdot P}{0.622 + H} = \frac{0.02523 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.02523} = \frac{2.557}{0.6472} = 3.950\ \text{kPa}$$

Saturation pressure at 35 °C: $P_s(35\ °C) = 5.628$ kPa.

$$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_s} = \frac{3.950}{5.628} = 0.702 = 70.2\%$$

So, **φ ≈ 70%**.

**Step 4 — Dew Point.** $T_{dp}$ is the temperature at which $P_s = P_w = 3.950$ kPa. From steam tables:

$$T_{dp} \approx 28.7\ °C$$

**Step 5 — Enthalpy of moist air.**

$$i = (1.005 + 1.884 \times 0.02523) \times 35 + 2501 \times 0.02523$$

$$i = (1.005 + 0.04753) \times 35 + 63.10 = 1.0525 \times 35 + 63.10$$

$$i = 36.84 + 63.10 = 99.94\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

So, **i ≈ 100 kJ/kg dry air**.

**Step 6 — Humid Heat.**

$$c_H = 1.005 + 1.884 \times 0.02523 = 1.0525\ \text{kJ/(kg dry air·K)}$$

So, **$c_H ≈ 1.053$ kJ/(kg·K)**.

### **Part (b): T = 60 °C, φ = 30%, P = 1 atm**

**Step 1 — Saturation pressure at 60 °C:** $P_s(60\ °C) = 19.92$ kPa.

**Step 2 — Partial pressure of water vapour:**

$$P_w = \phi \cdot P_s = 0.30 \times 19.92 = 5.976\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Humidity:**

$$H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{5.976}{101.325 - 5.976} = 0.622 \times 0.06268 = 0.03899\ \text{kg/kg}$$

So, **H ≈ 0.0390 kg water/kg dry air**.

**Step 4 — Saturated Humidity at 60 °C:**

$$H_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{19.92}{101.325 - 19.92} = 0.622 \times 0.2448 = 0.1523\ \text{kg/kg}$$

So, **$H_s ≈ 0.152$ kg water/kg dry air**.

**Step 5 — Specific Volume:**

$$v = \frac{0.287 \times T_{abs}}{P - P_w} = \frac{0.287 \times (60 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 5.976}$$

$$v = \frac{0.287 \times 333.15}{95.349} = \frac{95.61}{95.349} = 1.003\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$

So, **v ≈ 1.00 m³/kg dry air**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction of a humidity chart** and list the various properties that can be read off from it.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air enters an adiabatic dryer at **T₁ = 90 °C** with humidity **H₁ = 0.010 kg/kg** and leaves at **T₂ = 50 °C**. Assuming the process is adiabatic and follows a constant wet-bulb temperature line on the humidity chart, determine:
1. Wet-bulb temperature of the air
2. Humidity of the air leaving the dryer
3. Enthalpy of the inlet and outlet air streams
4. Mass of water evaporated per kg of dry air

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

### **Part (a): Construction of the Humidity Chart**

**Construction (description, 4 marks):**
1. **Axes:** X-axis is dry-bulb temperature (linear, 0–120 °C). Y-axis is absolute humidity, H, drawn on a non-linear (tilted) scale for resolution at low humidities.
2. **100% Saturation Curve (φ = 1):** Plotted as the upper boundary, computed from $H_s = 0.622\,P_s/(P - P_s)$ using steam-table values of $P_s(T)$.
3. **Relative Humidity Curves:** A family of curves below the saturation line, for φ = 10, 20, …, 90%, obtained by computing H at constant P_s but with reduced $P_w = \phi \cdot P_s$.
4. **Wet-Bulb / Adiabatic Saturation Lines:** Drawn as straight lines from each saturation point to lower-right, with negative slope determined by the equation $c_H(T_{as} - T) = \lambda_{as}(H_{as} - H)$.
5. **Enthalpy Lines:** Parallel to the wet-bulb lines, displaced so that the values match the analytical formula.
6. **Specific Volume Lines:** Nearly vertical lines, value given by $v = 0.287\,T_{abs}/(P - P_w)$.
7. **Humid Heat Scale:** An auxiliary scale on the upper boundary allowing direct reading of $c_H$.

**Properties readable from the chart (3 marks):**
- Absolute humidity (H)
- Relative humidity (φ)
- Dry-bulb temperature (T)
- Wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$)
- Dew point ($T_{dp}$)
- Enthalpy (i)
- Specific volume (v)
- Humid heat ($c_H$)
- Adiabatic saturation temperature ($T_{as}$)

(Any 6 of the above fetch 3 marks; full list fetches full credit.)

### **Part (b): Adiabatic Dryer — Inlet T₁ = 90 °C, H₁ = 0.010 kg/kg; Outlet T₂ = 50 °C**

**Step 1 — Locate the inlet state point (90 °C, H = 0.010).**

This point lies in the low-humidity, high-temperature region of the chart.

**Step 2 — Follow the constant wet-bulb (constant-enthalpy) line through the inlet point to T₂ = 50 °C.**

The constant-enthalpy line is the wet-bulb line; it represents the path of adiabatic drying. Reading off the chart at the intersection with T = 50 °C:

- $T_{wb} \approx 32\ °C$
- $H_2 \approx 0.030\ \text{kg/kg}$

**Step 3 — Verify analytically using constant-enthalpy condition.**

**Inlet enthalpy:**

$$i_1 = (1.005 + 1.884 \times 0.010) \times 90 + 2501 \times 0.010$$

$$i_1 = 1.0238 \times 90 + 25.01 = 92.14 + 25.01 = 117.15\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Outlet enthalpy (assumed equal to inlet for adiabatic process):**

$$i_2 = i_1 = 117.15\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

Now solve for $H_2$ at $T_2 = 50\ °C$:

$$117.15 = (1.005 + 1.884\,H_2) \times 50 + 2501\,H_2$$

$$117.15 = 50.25 + 94.2\,H_2 + 2501\,H_2$$

$$117.15 - 50.25 = 2595.2\,H_2$$

$$H_2 = \frac{66.90}{2595.2} = 0.02578\ \text{kg/kg}$$

So, **$H_2 \approx 0.0258$ kg water/kg dry air**.

**Step 4 — Mass of water evaporated per kg of dry air:**

$$\Delta H = H_2 - H_1 = 0.0258 - 0.010 = 0.0158\ \text{kg water/kg dry air}$$

So, **0.0158 kg of water is evaporated per kg of dry air** passing through the dryer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Graphical reading (chart method):** Reading off the humidity chart at the intersection of the constant-enthalpy line through (90 °C, 0.010) and the vertical at T = 50 °C, you obtain $H_2 \approx 0.026$ kg/kg — consistent with the analytical result. This is the standard KTU 2024 procedure: **always show both the graphical and analytical methods** to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exams — Module 3**

- **Humidity equation (golden formula):** $H = 0.622 \cdot P_w / (P - P_w)$. **Always per kg of dry air**, not per kg of moist air.
- **Relative humidity:** $\phi = P_w / P_s$. **φ = 100%** lies on the **saturation curve**; **φ = 0%** is on the **horizontal axis**.
- **Dew point** = temperature where **horizontal line from state point** strikes the **saturation curve**. It is the same as the temperature at which $P_s = P_w$.
- **Wet-bulb line** = straight line with negative slope; on the chart, it is the line that passes through the state point and ends on the saturation curve.
- **For air-water systems at 1 atm:** $T_{wb} = T_{as}$ (numerically).
- **Adiabatic drying** is represented on the chart as motion along a **constant wet-bulb (= constant-enthalpy) line**, from inlet to outlet state.
- **Enthalpy reference:** Dry air and liquid water at **0 °C**; total enthalpy = $i = (1.005 + 1.884H)T + 2501H$ (kJ/kg dry air), T in °C.
- **Specific volume formula** requires temperature in **Kelvin**: $v = 0.287\,T_{abs} / (P - P_w)$.
- **Humid heat** is the slope of the saturation line on the (H, T) plot: $c_H = 1.005 + 1.884H$.
- **Two known properties → all other properties determined** (graphically or analytically).
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting to subtract $P_w$ from P in the humidity denominator.
- **Common pitfall:** Reading the dew point by going straight down to the temperature axis (wrong) — must go horizontally to the **saturation curve first**.
- **Common pitfall:** Using kg of moist air as basis — KTU 2024 convention is **per kg of DRY air**.
- **Real-world relevance:** Humidity charts are the backbone of drying calculations, cooling-tower design, HVAC, and any air-water contact process.
- **Mastery tip:** Practise at least 5 problems in the next 48 hours involving both the **chart-reading** method and the **analytical** method. The KTU 2024 scheme allows the choice, but you should be fluent in both.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lewis Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1 > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Lewis Relation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Lewis Relation** (also known as the *overall coefficient relation* or the *resistance-in-series model*) establishes the precise mathematical linkage between the **overall mass transfer coefficient** and the **individual phase mass transfer coefficients** in a gas-liquid contact operation. It is a direct mass-transfer analogue of the well-known heat-transfer equation for composite walls. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > The Lewis Relation is the cornerstone concept for Module 3 because it permits the engineer to compute the *single* overall driving force required for design calculations from *two* experimentally inconvenient individual coefficients. For a solute **A** transferring from gas to liquid across a stationary interface, the Lewis Relation is expressed as: $$ \frac{1}{K_G} \;=\; \frac{1}{k_G} \;+\; \frac{m}{k_L} $$ where the corresponding liquid-side form is: $$ \frac{1}{K_L} \;=\; \frac{1}{k_L} \;+\; \frac{1}{m\,k_G} $$ The quantities $K_G$, $k_G$, $K_L$, $k_L$ and $m$ have the dimensions tabulated in Section 2. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are standing inside a crowded bus trying to pass a parcel from the **front door (gas side)** to the **back door (liquid side)** through a narrow, packed aisle (the gas-liquid interface). * The aisle itself offers a **gas-side resistance** (passage through the gas film). * The seating-cum-crowd at the back offers a **liquid-side resistance** (passage through the liquid film). * The two resistances act **one after another** — the parcel must *first* cross the gas film *and then* cross the liquid film. You can never deliver the parcel faster than the **slowest bottleneck**. The total "impedance" faced by the parcel is simply the **sum of individual impedances**. That is exactly what the Lewis Relation states: the *reciprocal* of the overall coefficient (which is the *total impedance*) equals the *sum* of the reciprocals of the individual coefficients. > [!NOTE] > **Key take-away for students:** > Resistances always **add**; conductances (the $k$ and $K$ values themselves) **do not** add directly. This is why the equation uses $1/K$ on the left. ## 1.3 The Other Lewis Relation (Humidification Context) In the specific context of **humidification operations**, the term "Lewis Relation" also refers to the psychrometric identity: $$ \frac{h_c}{k_y \, C_{s}} \;=\; C_{p,H} $$ where $C_{s}$ is the humid heat and $C_{p,H}$ is the same humid heat. For an air-water vapour system this simplifies to the celebrated statement: $$ \boxed{\;\text{Lewis Number} \;=\; \frac{h_c}{k_y \, C_{s}} \;\approx\; 1\;} $$ This identity allows adiabatic saturation temperature to be approximated by the **wet-bulb temperature**, and is the foundation of cooling-tower design. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physical Assumption — The Two-Film Theory The Lewis Relation is mathematically a direct consequence of the **Whitman Two-Film Theory (1923)**: 1. A thin, stagnant gas film exists next to the interface. 2. A thin, stagnant liquid film exists on the other side. 3. Steady-state one-dimensional molecular diffusion occurs across each film. 4. The interface itself offers **no resistance** — local equilibrium holds at $y = y_i$, $x = x_i$. Because flux must be **identical** through both films at steady state (no accumulation at the interface), we equate the gas-side flux to the liquid-side flux and eliminate the unknown interface composition through the equilibrium relation. ## 2.2 Derivation Logic (Bulleted Walk-Through) * **Step 1 — Gas-film flux.** $N_A = k_G\,(y - y_i)$ — driving force is gas concentration difference. * **Step 2 — Liquid-film flux.** $N_A = k_L\,(x_i - x)$ — driving force is liquid concentration difference. * **Step 3 — Local equilibrium at interface.** $y_i = m\,x_i$ (linear equilibrium; $m$ is the slope of the equilibrium curve). * **Step 4 — Eliminate $y_i$ and $x_i$.** Solve for $x_i = y_i / m$ and substitute. After algebra (full derivation in Section 3): $$ \frac{1}{K_G} \;=\; \frac{1}{k_G} \;+\; \frac{m}{k_L} $$ * **Step 5 — Interpret.** Each reciprocal term is a **resistance**, so the *overall* resistance is the **series sum** of the gas-film resistance $1/k_G$ and the *converted* liquid-film resistance $m/k_L$. * **Step 6 — Limiting cases.** * If $k_L \gg k_G$ (gas controls) → $1/K_G \approx 1/k_G$ (gas-phase resistance dominates). * If $k_G \gg k_L$ (liquid controls) → $1/K_G \approx m/k_L$ (liquid-phase resistance dominates). ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Dominant Resistance | Why | |---|---|---| | Absorption of **SO₂** in water | Liquid film | SO₂ is highly soluble — $m$ small | | Absorption of **CO₂** in water | Liquid film | Low solubility, slow reaction in liquid | | Humidification with insoluble gas | Gas film | Liquid essentially pure water | | Distillation (most systems) | Either (design-dependent) | Check $m \cdot k_G$ vs $k_L$ | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **single most important reference** for any numerical problem on Lewis Relation in the KTU examination. Memorise every cell. | # | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units | Formula / Definition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k_G$ | Individual gas-film coefficient | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ (mol-fraction)⁻¹ | $N_A / (y - y_i)$ | | 2 | $k_L$ | Individual liquid-film coefficient | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ (mol-fraction)⁻¹ | $N_A / (x_i - x)$ | | 3 | $K_G$ | Overall gas-phase coefficient | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ (mol-fraction)⁻¹ | $N_A / (y - y^{*})$ | | 4 | $K_L$ | Overall liquid-phase coefficient | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ (mol-fraction)⁻¹ | $N_A / (x^{*} - x)$ | | 5 | $m$ | Slope of equilibrium curve | dimensionless | $dy/dx$ at operating point | | 6 | $y^{*}$ | Gas composition in equilibrium with bulk liquid | mol/mol | $m \cdot x$ | | 7 | $x^{*}$ | Liquid composition in equilibrium with bulk gas | mol/mol | $y / m$ | | 8 | $K_G\,a$ | Volumetric overall gas coefficient | kmol·m⁻³·s⁻¹ (mol-fraction)⁻¹ | $K_G \times a$ | | 9 | $G_M$ | Molar gas flow rate per unit area | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Standard | | 10 | $L_M$ | Molar liquid flow rate per unit area | kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Standard | > [!NOTE] > **Units consistency rule:** When $m$ is small (highly soluble gas), the $m/k_L$ term becomes negligible and gas-phase resistance controls. Conversely, large $m$ (poorly soluble gas) makes liquid resistance dominant. ## 2.5 Conversion Between $K_G$ and $K_L$ By direct substitution using $y = m\,x^{*}$, one obtains: $$ K_G \;=\; m \cdot K_L $$ This identity is indispensable for switching between the gas- and liquid-phase forms of the operating-line equation in packed-column design. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Lewis Relation We begin with the steady-state one-dimensional diffusion through the two films. **Starting equations** (two-film theory): $$ N_A \;=\; k_G\,(y - y_i) \tag{3.1} $$ $$ N_A \;=\; k_L\,(x_i - x) \tag{3.2} $$ $$ y_i \;=\; m\,x_i \tag{3.3} $$ **Definition of overall flux based on gas phase:** $$ N_A \;=\; K_G\,(y - y^{*}) \tag{3.4} $$ where $y^{*} = m\,x$ (the gas composition that *would* be in equilibrium with the bulk liquid $x$). ### Step A — Expand the overall driving force $$ y - y^{*} \;=\; y - m\,x $$ Rewrite this in terms of the unknown interface composition $y_i$: $$ y - y^{*} \;=\; (y - y_i) + (y_i - m\,x) $$ Substitute $y_i = m\,x_i$: $$ y - y^{*} \;=\; (y - y_i) + m\,(x_i - x) \tag{3.5} $$ ### Step B — Express each bracket using fluxes From (3.1): $(y - y_i) = N_A / k_G$ From (3.2): $(x_i - x) = N_A / k_L$ Plug into (3.5): $$ y - y^{*} \;=\; \frac{N_A}{k_G} \;+\; m \cdot \frac{N_A}{k_L} $$ Factor out $N_A$: $$ y - y^{*} \;=\; N_A\!\left[\frac{1}{k_G} \;+\; \frac{m}{k_L}\right] \tag{3.6} $$ ### Step C — Compare with the overall definition (3.4) From (3.4): $$ \frac{N_A}{y - y^{*}} \;=\; K_G $$ But from (3.6): $$ \frac{N_A}{y - y^{*}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}} $$ Therefore: $$ K_G \;=\; \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}} $$ Taking the reciprocal of both sides: $$ \frac{1}{K_G} \;=\; \frac{1}{k_G} \;+\; \frac{m}{k_L} $$ which is the **gas-side Lewis Relation**. ### Step D — Liquid-side form By an exactly symmetric procedure (starting from $K_L\,(x^{*} - x)$), or by substituting $K_G = m \cdot K_L$ and cross-multiplying: $$ \frac{1}{K_L} \;=\; \frac{1}{k_L} \;+\; \frac{1}{m\,k_G} $$ The proof is complete. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem (14-mark typical):** A packed column absorbs **acetone from air into water** at **293 K and 1 atm**. At the operating point, the equilibrium relation is $y = 1.75\,x$. The individual coefficients are $k_G = 1.20 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ and $k_L = 1.50 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹. Calculate the overall coefficients $K_G$ and $K_L$, identify the controlling resistance, and find the fractional gas-film resistance. ### Solution **Given data** $$ m = 1.75, \qquad k_G = 1.20 \times 10^{-3}, \qquad k_L = 1.50 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 1 — Compute the two resistance terms** Gas-film resistance: $$ \frac{1}{k_G} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.20 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 833.33 \quad \text{s·m}^{2}\text{·kmol}^{-1} $$ Converted liquid-film resistance: $$ \frac{m}{k_L} \;=\; \frac{1.75}{1.50 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 1166.67 \quad \text{s·m}^{2}\text{·kmol}^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Total resistance** $$ \frac{1}{K_G} \;=\; 833.33 + 1166.67 \;=\; 2000.00 \quad \text{s·m}^{2}\text{·kmol}^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Overall gas coefficient** $$ K_G \;=\; \frac{1}{2000.00} \;=\; 5.00 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1} $$ **Step 4 — Overall liquid coefficient** Using $K_L = K_G / m$: $$ K_L \;=\; \frac{5.00 \times 10^{-4}}{1.75} \;=\; 2.857 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1} $$ **Step 5 — Fractional gas-film resistance** $$ f_G \;=\; \frac{1/k_G}{1/K_G} \;=\; \frac{833.33}{2000.00} \;=\; 0.4167 \;\;\text{(or } 41.67\%\text{)} $$ **Step 6 — Controlling resistance** Since $m/k_L > 1/k_G$, the **liquid film controls** (its resistance $\approx 58.3\%$ of the total). For this highly water-soluble solute, this is unusual — the answer reflects the specific operating point chosen. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is **routinely asked** in KTU as a 7-mark sub-question in Part B. Memorise it. | Case | Condition | Controlling Phase | $1/K_G$ simplifies to | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $m \to 0$ (very soluble gas) | Gas | $1/K_G \approx 1/k_G$ | | 2 | $m \to \infty$ (insoluble gas) | Liquid | $1/K_G \approx m/k_L$ | | 3 | $m\,k_G = k_L$ | Both equal | $1/K_G = 2/k_G$ | | 4 | $m\,k_G \gg k_L$ | Gas | $1/K_G \approx 1/k_G$ | | 5 | $m\,k_G \ll k_L$ | Liquid | $1/K_G \approx m/k_L$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Resistance in Series The Mermaid diagram below models the **physical layout** of the two-film system and traces the diffusion path of solute A through successive resistances. Because the interface is at thermodynamic equilibrium, the conceptual diagram emphasises that **interface composition is the bridge** between the two films. ```mermaid flowchart TD BulkGas[BULK GAS PHASE
Composition y] --> GasFilm{GAS FILM
Individual Resistance 1 OVER kG} GasFilm --> Interface[INTERFACE
Composition yi in equilibrium with xi] Interface --> LiqFilm{LIQUID FILM
Individual Resistance 1 OVER kL
But Expressed via m OVER kL in Gas Units} LiqFilm --> BulkLiq[BULK LIQUID PHASE
Composition x] GasFilm -.Flux N.-> N1[Na Equals kG times y minus yi] LiqFilm -.Flux N.-> N2[Na Equals kL times xi minus x] Interface -.Equilibrium.-> Eq[y i equals m times x i] subgraph DRIVING_FORCE[OVERALL DRIVING FORCE in Gas Units] DF[y minus y star where y star equals m times x] end BulkGas --> DRIVING_FORCE BulkLiq --> DRIVING_FORCE classDef bulk fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef film fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef interfaceBlock fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 classDef output fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 class BulkGas,BulkLiq bulk class GasFilm,LiqFilm film class Interface interfaceBlock class N1,N2,Eq,DRIVING_FORCE,DF output ``` ## 4.2 Decision Tree — Identifying Controlling Resistance The Mermaid decision tree below is a **KTU-style algorithmic aid** used to determine which phase controls the mass-transfer rate in any gas-liquid system, given the equilibrium slope $m$ and the two individual coefficients. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start with kG, kL and m]) --> Calc[Compute both resistances:
R_G equals 1 OVER kG
R_L_CONV equals m OVER kL] Calc --> Compare{R_G versus R_L_CONV} Compare -- R_G GREATER THAN R_L_CONV --> GasCtrl[Gas-film resistance dominates
Use K_G APPROX k_G
for design calculations] Compare -- R_L_CONV GREATER THAN R_G --> LiqCtrl[Liquid-film resistance dominates
Use K_G APPROX k_L OVER m] Compare -- R_G EQUAL R_L_CONV --> Both[Both films contribute equally
Use full Lewis Relation] GasCtrl --> End1([End]) LiqCtrl --> End2([End]) Both --> End3([End]) classDef startend fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000 classDef decide fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000 classDef result fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 class Start,End1,End2,End3 startend class Calc calc class Compare decide class GasCtrl,LiqCtrl,Both result ``` ## 4.3 Functional Mapping — Lewis Relation in Design Workflow This block diagram shows where the Lewis Relation **sits** inside the complete design workflow of a packed absorption column, demonstrating its central role. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Equilibrium Data
y versus x] --> B[Determine slope m] C[Individual Coefficients
kG and kL] --> D[Apply Lewis Relation] B --> D D --> E[Overall Coefficient K_G] E --> F[Volumetric Coefficient K_G a] F --> G[Calculate HTU or NTU] G --> H[Compute Packed Height Z] H --> I[Final Column Design] classDef input fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 class A,C input class B,D,E,F,G,H process class I output ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State and explain the Lewis Relation for a gas-liquid system. What does it physically signify?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Lewis Relation is: $$ \frac{1}{K_G} \;=\; \frac{1}{k_G} \;+\; \frac{m}{k_L} $$ where $K_G$ is the overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient, $k_G$ and $k_L$ are the individual gas- and liquid-film coefficients, and $m$ is the slope of the equilibrium curve. **(1 mark)** Physically, it signifies that the **reciprocal of the overall coefficient — the overall mass-transfer resistance — is the sum of the individual film resistances** acting in series. **(1 mark)** The factor $m$ converts the liquid-film resistance into equivalent gas-phase units so that the two resistances may be added directly. **(1 mark)** ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Under what condition does the gas film alone control the mass-transfer rate? Give one industrial example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The gas film controls when $1/k_G \gg m/k_L$, i.e. when $m\,k_G \ll k_L$. **(1 mark)** This occurs for gases of **low solubility** (small $m$) or systems with a very thin or highly turbulent gas film. **(1 mark)** **Industrial example:** absorption of **ammonia in water** (highly soluble, $m$ small) where the gas film offers the dominant resistance — although, more correctly, ammonia is liquid-film controlled in many designs. The **classic correct example** is **absorption of a slightly soluble gas such as CO₂ into water**, where the liquid film actually controls. For *gas-film* control, the textbook example is **absorption of SO₂ in strong alkaline solution**, where the liquid-phase reaction makes $k_L$ effectively infinite. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > (1) Forgetting to **convert the liquid resistance** using $m$ before adding — students often write $1/K_G = 1/k_G + 1/k_L$, which is dimensionally and conceptually wrong. **(–2 marks minimum)** > (2) Mixing up $K_G$ and $K_L$ formulas. The liquid-side form has $1/(m\,k_G)$, **not** $m/k_G$. > (3) Not stating the **units explicitly** — KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguity. > (4) Failing to identify the **controlling resistance** in the conclusion — this is a mandatory discussion point for full marks. ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3, modified]`** **(a)** Derive the Lewis Relation $$ \frac{1}{K_G} \;=\; \frac{1}{k_G} \;+\; \frac{m}{k_L} $$ starting from the two-film theory. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a gas absorption tower, the individual phase coefficients are $k_G = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ and $k_L = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹. The equilibrium relation is $y = 0.8\,x$. (i) Compute $K_G$ and $K_L$. **(3 marks)** (ii) Identify the controlling resistance and justify. **(2 marks)** (iii) If the operating pressure is doubled, what happens qualitatively to the controlling resistance? **(2 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)** *Assumptions (2 marks):* 1. Steady-state operation — no accumulation at interface. 2. Two stagnant films exist on either side of the interface. 3. Interface is at thermodynamic equilibrium. 4. Equilibrium is linear: $y_i = m\,x_i$. 5. One-dimensional diffusion, equimolar counter-diffusion or stagnant-film limit. *Algebraic derivation (5 marks):* The full derivation as given in Section 3.1 above must be reproduced step by step. Award 1 mark each for: stating the two flux equations, defining the overall flux, introducing the equilibrium relation, algebraic elimination of $y_i$ and $x_i$, and arriving at the final relation. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** *Given:* $k_G = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$, $k_L = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$, $m = 0.8$. **(i) Calculation of $K_G$ and $K_L$ (3 marks)** Compute the two resistance terms: $$ \frac{1}{k_G} \;=\; \frac{1}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 333.33 $$ $$ \frac{m}{k_L} \;=\; \frac{0.8}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 800.00 $$ Sum: $$ \frac{1}{K_G} \;=\; 333.33 + 800.00 \;=\; 1133.33 $$ Therefore: $$ K_G \;=\; 8.824 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1} $$ Using $K_L = K_G / m$: $$ K_L \;=\; \frac{8.824 \times 10^{-4}}{0.8} \;=\; 1.103 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kmol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1} $$ **(ii) Controlling resistance (2 marks)** The converted liquid-film resistance (800.0) is **more than twice** the gas-film resistance (333.3). Hence the **liquid film controls** this absorption. Physically, this reflects the very soluble nature of the solute ($m < 1$), making the gas phase essentially saturated quickly and the liquid-phase diffusion the bottleneck. **(iii) Effect of doubling pressure (2 marks)** For ideal-gas behaviour, $k_G \propto P^{-1}$ while $k_L$ is essentially **independent of pressure** (it depends on liquid-phase concentration gradient). Doubling the pressure halves $k_G$, which **doubles the gas-film resistance** to 666.67. The new ratio $1/k_G : m/k_L$ becomes $666.67 : 800.00$, making the **liquid film even more strongly controlling**. In the limit $P \to \infty$, all resistance lies in the liquid film. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3, modified]`** **(a)** Explain the significance of the slope $m$ in the Lewis Relation. How does the value of $m$ influence whether the gas film or the liquid film is rate-controlling? Provide the limiting forms for two extreme cases. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cooling tower handles air-water contact. At the operating conditions, the humid heat $C_s = 1.18$ kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹, the gas-phase mass transfer coefficient $k_y = 0.85$ kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ and the convective heat transfer coefficient $h_c = 1.0$ kW·m⁻²·K⁻¹. (i) Verify the **Lewis Relation** $h_c = k_y \, C_s$ and compute the Lewis Number. **(3 marks)** (ii) Using this relation, justify why the wet-bulb temperature approximates the adiabatic saturation temperature for the air-water system. **(4 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Discussion (7 marks)** *Significance of $m$ (3 marks):* The slope $m$ is the **local slope of the equilibrium curve** $y$ vs. $x$. It is the **conversion factor** that translates the liquid-film resistance into gas-phase concentration units so it can be added to the gas-film resistance. Without $m$, the two resistances would have different units and could not be summed. A small $m$ indicates a highly soluble gas (steep liquid curve, flat gas curve in $y$–$x$ plot); a large $m$ indicates low solubility. *Influence on controlling film (2 marks):* The term $m/k_L$ is large when $m$ is large (insoluble gas) → liquid film controls. The term $m/k_L$ is small when $m$ is small (very soluble gas) → gas film controls. *Limiting forms (2 marks):* Case A — Highly soluble gas ($m \to 0$): $1/K_G \approx 1/k_G$, so $K_G \approx k_G$. Case B — Insoluble gas ($m \to \infty$): $1/K_G \approx m/k_L$, so $K_G \approx k_L/m$. **Part (b) — Numerical + Concept (7 marks)** **(i) Verification (3 marks)** Compute $k_y \, C_s$. First convert units consistently. $k_y = 0.85$ kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹ = 850 mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹. Multiply by humid heat (treated per unit mass; convert molar basis if needed): For demonstration with consistent units: $$ k_y \, C_s \;=\; (0.85) \times (1.18 \times 10^{3}) \;=\; 1003 \;\; \text{J·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-1}\text{·s}^{-1} $$ This is essentially $h_c = 1.0$ kW·m⁻²·K⁻¹ = $1000$ J·m⁻²·K⁻¹·s⁻¹. The two values agree to within **0.3 %**, confirming: $$ h_c \;=\; k_y \, C_s \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Le} \;=\; \frac{h_c}{k_y C_s} \;\approx\; 1.0 $$ **(ii) Justification of wet-bulb = adiabatic saturation (4 marks)** The adiabatic saturation temperature $T_{as}$ is defined by the energy balance on a wetted surface where the latent heat released by condensation of vapour exactly balances the sensible-heat loss of the gas: $$ h_c \, (T_{as} - T) \;=\; k_y \, \lambda_s \, (Y_{as} - Y) $$ where $\lambda_s$ is the latent heat at $T_{as}$. Rearranging: $$ \frac{Y_{as} - Y}{T_{as} - T} \;=\; \frac{h_c}{k_y \, \lambda_s} $$ The wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb}$ satisfies an *identical* governing equation **only if** the Lewis Relation $h_c = k_y \, C_s$ holds. Since for air-water $C_s \approx \lambda_s$ (because humid heat numerically approximates latent heat for dilute vapour), the two equations become mathematically the same, implying $T_{wb} = T_{as}$. > [!NOTE] > This is the *only* thermodynamic reason a wet-bulb thermometer can be used to measure adiabatic saturation temperature — and it is **specifically** because of the Lewis Relation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Night Before the Exam** * **Lewis Relation (gas-side form):** $1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L$ — the **single most important** equation on this topic. * **Lewis Relation (liquid-side form):** $1/K_L = 1/k_L + 1/(m\,k_G)$ — the $m$ moves to the **denominator** here. * **Conversion identity:** $K_G = m \cdot K_L$ — use this to switch between gas- and liquid-based driving forces. * **Physical meaning:** Resistances (not conductances) **add in series**. * **Limiting cases:** * $m \to 0$ (very soluble) → $K_G \approx k_G$ — gas film controls. * $m \to \infty$ (insoluble) → $K_G \approx k_L/m$ — liquid film controls. * **Fractional gas-film resistance:** $f_G = (1/k_G) \cdot K_G = k_G / (k_G + m\,k_L / k_L)$ — a numerical identity worth memorising. * **Units consistency:** All $k$ and $K$ must be in **identical** units (typically mol-fraction basis). Mixing $y$-based $K_G$ with $x$-based $k_L$ without conversion through $m$ will **lose 100 % of the marks**. * **Humidification form:** $h_c = k_y \, C_s$ → Lewis Number ≈ 1, justifying $T_{wb} = T_{as}$ for air-water. * **Common KTU trap:** Do **not** confuse the **Lewis Relation** (resistance-in-series) with the **Lever Rule** (mass balance on a tie-line) — they have similar-sounding names but are completely different. * **Exam-tip:** Always write the **units of the final answer** explicitly — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less numerical answers in 7-mark sub-parts.

topics.title: Water Cooling with Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Water Cooling with Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Cooling with Air (Humidification–Dehumidification Operation):**
> Water cooling with air is a direct-contact mass transfer operation in which warm water is brought into intimate contact with unsaturated air, causing simultaneous **sensible heat transfer** (from water to air due to temperature gradient) and **latent heat transfer** (due to evaporation of a fraction of water into the air stream). The bulk operation is carried out in a device called a **Cooling Tower**, where the process is *predominantly adiabatic* — the system neither gains nor loses heat from the surroundings.

The operation is governed by two simultaneous phenomena:
1. **Heat Transfer** — driven by the temperature difference between bulk water and bulk air.
2. **Mass Transfer** — driven by the difference between the humidity of saturated air at the water surface ($H^*$) and the humidity of the bulk air ($H$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Breeze on a Wet Hand" Model

Imagine washing your hands with warm water on a hot summer afternoon. Even though the air around you is *hotter* than the water, your skin feels *cool*. Why? Because the unsaturated air evaporates a thin film of water from your hand. The energy needed to evaporate that water (the **latent heat of vaporization**) is drawn from your skin, making you feel cooler.

> [!NOTE]
> **A cooling tower replicates this exact phenomenon at industrial scale.** Hot process water (e.g., from a condenser) is sprayed through rising unsaturated air. The water evaporates slightly (~1–3 % of the circulating water), the latent heat consumed cools the rest of the water, and the air leaves warmer and more humid at the top of the tower.

## 1.3 Key Operating Vocabulary

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Inlet (hot) water temperature** | $T_1$ | Warm water entering the top of the tower |
| **Outlet (cold) water temperature** | $T_2$ | Cooled water leaving the bottom |
| **Inlet air enthalpy** | $h_1$ | Enthalpy of unsaturated air entering at bottom |
| **Outlet air enthalpy** | $h_2$ | Enthalpy of moist air leaving at top |
| **Range** | $T_1 - T_2$ | Actual drop in water temperature |
| **Approach** | $T_2 - T_{wb}$ | Difference between cold water and air wet-bulb |
| **L/G ratio** | — | Liquid-to-gas mass flow rate ratio |
| **NTU / HTU** | — | Number / Height of a Transfer Unit |

## 1.4 Real-World Industrial Significance

Cooling towers are the *backbone* of nearly every thermal power plant, petroleum refinery, and HVAC installation. In India, with its tropical climate (typical wet-bulb = **25–28 °C**), they are indispensable. An efficient tower can cool water within **5–7 °C** of the ambient wet-bulb temperature — a hard physical limit imposed by the **second law of thermodynamics**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Driving Forces — Why Two Curves?

For the design of any countercurrent cooling tower, two curves are plotted on an **enthalpy–temperature (h–T)** psychrometric chart:

1. **Equilibrium / Saturation Curve** — gives $h^*$ (saturation enthalpy) as a function of water temperature $T$. The driving force at any point is the **vertical distance** between the operating line and the saturation curve: $(h^* - h)$.
2. **Operating Line (Material + Energy Balance)** — a straight line on the h–T diagram with slope $\frac{L \, c_L}{G}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the operating line straight?**
> Because — assuming water evaporation loss is small enough that $L$ and $G$ are essentially *constant* — an overall energy balance on the tower yields a *linear* relationship between bulk water temperature $T$ and bulk air enthalpy $h$.

## 2.2 The Three Foundation Concepts

### (i) Wet-Bulb Temperature ($T_{wb}$)
The temperature a parcel of air attains when it is adiabatically saturated by evaporation from a large water surface. It is governed by **combined heat and mass transfer** with the wet bulb in the *radiation-shielded, high-velocity limit*:
$$\frac{h_c}{\rho \, k_y \, c_s} = 1$$

### (ii) Adiabatic Saturation Temperature ($T_{as}$)
The temperature attained when a stream of unsaturated air is contacted with water in a perfectly insulated, recirculating system until equilibrium. For an **air–water system**, $T_{wb} \approx T_{as}$, but they are conceptually different.

### (iii) Lewis Relation (the cornerstone of cooling tower theory)
$$\boxed{\;\frac{h_y}{k_y \, c_s} = 1\;}\quad \text{or} \quad \boxed{\;h_y = k_y \, c_s\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** The Lewis relation states that for an air–water system, the **enthalpy driving force** $(h^* - h)$ can directly replace the **humidity driving force** $(H^* - H)$. This is why the Merkel equation is written using enthalpies — saving us from solving simultaneous heat and mass transfer equations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = c_s \, T + H \, \lambda_0$ | Enthalpy of moist air per kg dry air |
| 2 | $c_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \, H$ | Humid heat (kJ kg⁻¹ dry air K⁻¹) |
| 3 | $\frac{h_y}{k_y \, c_s} = 1$ | **Lewis Relation** |
| 4 | $h_{op} = h_1 + \frac{L \, c_L}{G}\,(T - T_2)$ | Operating line (countercurrent) |
| 5 | $\text{Range} = T_1 - T_2$ | Actual water cooling |
| 6 | $\text{Approach} = T_2 - T_{wb}$ | Closeness to thermodynamic limit |
| 7 | $\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{h^* - h} = \frac{K_y \, a \, V}{L}$ | **Merkel Equation** |
| 8 | $Z = \text{HTU} \times \text{NTU}$ | Height of tower |
| 9 | $\text{HTU}_L = \frac{L}{K_y \, c_s \, a}$ | Height of a transfer unit (liquid basis) |
| 10 | $G\,(h_2 - h_1) = L \, c_L\,(T_1 - T_2)$ | Overall energy balance |

> ⚠️ **Notation Warning:** In all Merkel integrals, $h^*$ is the **saturation enthalpy** at water temperature $T$ (read from the saturation curve), and $h$ is the **bulk air enthalpy** (read from the operating line at the same $T$).

## 2.4 Classification of Cooling Towers (High-Yield for KTU)

| Type | Sub-Type | Air Movement | Water Flow Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Draft** | Atmospheric / Hyperbolic | Wind / density difference | Cross or Counter |
| **Mechanical Draft** | Forced Draft | Fan pushes air | Counterflow |
| | Induced Draft | Fan pulls air | Counter or Crossflow |

## 2.5 Utility in Engineering

The Merkel number $\int \frac{dT}{h^* - h}$ is the **NTU** of the cooling tower. A high NTU implies a tall, efficient tower (closer approach). Industry benchmarks: a *good* tower gives NTU ≈ **1.5 – 2.5**, with HTU typically **1 – 3 m**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Operating Line (Energy Balance Across a Differential Element)

Consider a **countercurrent** cooling tower of cross-sectional area $S$ and differential height $dZ$. Let:
- $L$ = mass flow rate of water (kg/s, dry basis equivalent — constant)
- $G$ = mass flow rate of dry air (kg/s, constant)
- $c_L$ = specific heat of liquid water ≈ **4.187 kJ kg⁻¹ K⁻¹**

At any cross-section, water at temperature $T$ flows down, while air at enthalpy $h$ flows up.

### Step 1 — Write the differential energy balance

The water cools by $dT$ (so it loses heat $L \, c_L \, (-dT)$), and the air is heated by $dh$ (gains heat $G \, dh$). By the **conservation of energy**:

$$L \, c_L \, (-dT) = G \, dh$$

### Step 2 — Re-arrange into a differential form

$$G \, dh = -L \, c_L \, dT$$

$$dh = -\frac{L \, c_L}{G}\, dT$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from the bottom of the tower (reference state)

At the bottom of the tower: water leaves at $T = T_2$ (cold water outlet), air enters with $h = h_1$.

$$dh = -\frac{L \, c_L}{G}\, dT$$

Integrating from $(T_2, h_1)$ to $(T, h)$:

$$\int_{h_1}^{h} dh = -\frac{L \, c_L}{G} \int_{T_2}^{T} dT$$

### Step 4 — Perform the integration

$$h - h_1 = -\frac{L \, c_L}{G}\,(T - T_2)$$

$$h = h_1 - \frac{L \, c_L}{G}\,(T - T_2)$$

### Step 5 — Re-express in the conventional KTU form

Since the temperature axis in a cooling tower is *flipped* (water cools as it descends, so the higher $T$ corresponds to a higher position but a *lower* enthalpy for the air *if no evaporation occurred*) — but the operating line is conventionally written **upward-sloping** in the h–T diagram because the bulk air picks up enthalpy as it rises. The final canonical form is:

$$\boxed{\;h_{op} = h_1 + \frac{L \, c_L}{G}\,(T - T_2)\;}$$

**Verification:** At $T = T_1$ (top of tower), $h_{op} = h_1 + \frac{L c_L}{G}(T_1 - T_2) = h_2$ ✓ (matches the overall energy balance).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Merkel Equation (Rate-Based Design Equation)

The Merkel equation is obtained by combining the **operating line** with the **interfacial rate equation** (using Lewis relation).

### Step 1 — Identify the rate of enthalpy transfer

For a differential volume $dV = S \, dZ$, the rate of enthalpy transfer from water to air, using the Lewis relation, is:

$$dQ = h_y \, a \, S \, (h^* - h) \, dZ$$

where $h_y = k_y \, c_s$ is the *enthalpy* mass transfer coefficient and $h^*$ is the saturation enthalpy at the bulk water temperature $T$.

### Step 2 — Express $dQ$ in terms of water-side energy

By the energy balance (Step 1 of derivation 1):

$$dQ = -L \, c_L \, dT$$

### Step 3 — Equate the two expressions

$$-L \, c_L \, dT = h_y \, a \, S \, (h^* - h) \, dZ$$

### Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate

$$dZ = -\frac{L \, c_L}{h_y \, a \, S} \, \frac{dT}{h^* - h}$$

Integrating from $Z = 0$ (bottom, $T = T_2$) to $Z = Z_T$ (top, $T = T_1$):

$$Z_T = -\frac{L \, c_L}{h_y \, a \, S} \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{h^* - h}$$

### Step 5 — Re-arrange into the canonical Merkel form

Since $h^* > h$ throughout, the integral $\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{h^* - h}$ is *positive*. Flipping the integration limits removes the negative sign:

$$\boxed{\;\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{h^* - h} = \frac{h_y \, a \, S \, Z_T}{L \, c_L} = \frac{h_y \, a \, V}{L \, c_L}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Merkel Integral** — it gives the **NTU** of the cooling tower. The right-hand side is the ratio $\frac{K \cdot a \cdot V}{L}$ where $K = h_y / c_L$ is the combined mass-transfer coefficient. KTU examiners expect this equation in its **standard form**:
> $$\frac{K \, a \, V}{L} = \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{h^* - h}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lewis Relation from Analogy (Brief)

For simultaneous heat and mass transfer with the same geometry, the Reynolds analogies give:

$$\text{St}_H \cdot \text{Pr}^{2/3} = \text{St}_M \cdot \text{Sc}^{2/3}$$

For air–water vapor: $\text{Pr} \approx 0.71$ and $\text{Sc} \approx 0.61$, giving a **Lewis number** $\text{Le} = \text{Sc}/\text{Pr} \approx 0.86 \approx 1$. Therefore:

$$\frac{h_c}{\rho \, c_p \, k_y} \approx 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h_c \, (T^* - T) \approx k_y \, c_s \, (T^* - T)$$

Identifying $h = c_s T$ (with reference state chosen appropriately), one obtains:

$$\frac{h_y}{k_y \, c_s} \approx 1$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Countercurrent Cooling Tower

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *interactions* between streams, states, and the equilibrium interface inside a countercurrent induced-draft cooling tower. (Air enters at bottom and water enters at top — they flow in opposite directions.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inletAir["Inlet Air Stream G kg per s"]
    inletAirState["State: T_g1 DBT, H1, h1"]
    fanSection["Induced Draft Fan at Top"]
    towerBody["Counterflow Tower Packing Region"]
    airRise["Air Rises Upwards Through Packing"]
    waterFall["Water Sprays Downwards Through Packing"]
    interfaceZone["Gas Liquid Interface: Film at Saturation h* at T"]
    massTransfer["Mass Transfer: Water Evaporates k_y H* minus H"]
    heatTransfer["Heat Transfer: Sensible plus Latent h_c delta T"]
    outletAir["Outlet Air Stream G kg per s"]
    outletAirState["State: T_g2 DBT, H2, h2 Saturated"]
    driftEliminator["Drift Eliminator Removes Entrained Droplets"]
    inletWater["Inlet Hot Water L kg per s at T1"]
    waterDistributor["Water Distribution System at Top"]
    outletWater["Outlet Cold Water L kg per s at T2"]
    basin["Collection Basin at Bottom"]
    makeup["Makeup Water Added to Basin"]

    inletAir --> inletAirState
    inletAirState --> airRise
    inletWater --> waterDistributor
    waterDistributor --> waterFall
    airRise --> interfaceZone
    waterFall --> interfaceZone
    interfaceZone --> massTransfer
    interfaceZone --> heatTransfer
    interfaceZone --> towerBody
    towerBody --> outletAir
    outletAir --> driftEliminator
    driftEliminator --> outletAirState
    towerBody --> basin
    basin --> outletWater
    basin --> makeup
    fanSection -.pulls.-> outletAirState
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Driving-Force and Resistance Mapping

This block diagram illustrates *why* the Merkel equation works — how the operating line and the saturation curve interact via a *driving force* that diminishes as we approach the bottom of the tower.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    topTower["Top of Tower: T1 high, h2 saturated, small driving force h* minus h"]
    midTower["Middle: T moderate, h intermediate, moderate driving force"]
    botTower["Bottom: T2 low, h1 unsaturated inlet, large driving force h* minus h"]
    opLine["Operating Line: h equals h1 plus L cL over G times T minus T2"]
    satCurve["Saturation Curve h* versus T from psychrometric chart"]
    merkelInt["Merkel Integral: integral dT over h* minus h"]
    ntuOut["Output: NTU value of tower"]
    htuParam["HTU equals L over h_y a c_s"]
    zCalc["Tower Height Z equals HTU times NTU"]

    topTower --> opLine
    midTower --> opLine
    botTower --> opLine
    topTower --> satCurve
    midTower --> satCurve
    botTower --> satCurve
    opLine --> merkelInt
    satCurve --> merkelInt
    merkelInt --> ntuOut
    ntuOut --> zCalc
    htuParam --> zCalc
```

## 4.3 Schematic Comparison — Forced vs Induced Draft

| Feature | Forced Draft | Induced draft |
|---|---|---|
| Fan location | Bottom (pushes air up) | Top (pulls air up) |
| Air exit velocity | Low — prone to recirculation | High — good dispersion |
| Hot humid air plume | Recirculates back into inlet | Dispersed high — minimal recirculation |
| Typical application | Small industrial units | Large power plants |
| KTU exam weightage | Define-only | Working + diagram + advantages |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between wet-bulb temperature and adiabatic saturation temperature. Show that for an air–water system they are approximately equal.**

**Model Answer:**

*Wet-bulb temperature* ($T_{wb}$) is a **dynamic, single-pass** quantity — the steady-state temperature reached by a *small* amount of water evaporating into a *large* stream of unsaturated air under the combined effect of **convective heat transfer (to the bulb)** and **mass transfer (away from the bulb)**.

*Adiabatic saturation temperature* ($T_{as}$) is a **recirculating, equilibrium** quantity — the temperature attained when unsaturated air is continuously contacted with recirculated water in an *adiabatic* chamber until the air becomes *saturated* and the water temperature becomes constant.

The equivalence arises from the **Lewis Relation**:
$$\frac{h_c}{k_y \, c_s} = 1$$

For the air–water system, the Lewis number $\text{Le} = \alpha / D_{AB} \approx 0.87 \approx 1$, so the heat-transfer driving force and the mass-transfer driving force are scaled identically, leading to:

$$T_{wb} \approx T_{as}$$

| Aspect | $T_{wb}$ | $T_{as}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Process | Single-pass, dynamic | Recirculating, equilibrium |
| Reference bulb | Required | Not required |
| Driving force | Heat & mass at surface | Adiabatic saturation |
| Equality | — | $T_{wb} \approx T_{as}$ for air-water only |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the terms "Range" and "Approach" as applied to cooling towers. Why is "Approach" more important than "Range" for evaluating tower performance?**

**Model Answer:**

$$\text{Range} = T_1 - T_2 \qquad\qquad \text{Approach} = T_2 - T_{wb}$$

**Range** is the *actual* temperature drop of water as it passes through the tower (e.g., from **45 °C to 30 °C** ⇒ Range = **15 °C**). It depends on the *process demand* and is set by the upstream heat exchanger.

**Approach** is the *gap* between the cold-water outlet temperature $T_2$ and the *thermodynamic limit* — the ambient wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb}$. It is a measure of how *closely* the tower approaches the ideal limit.

> **Why Approach matters more:** Two towers with identical Range may have vastly different performances. The one with the smaller Approach (say, 4 °C vs 8 °C) extracts more useful cooling. Approach dictates the **size of the tower** and its operating cost. Industrial specifications always quote the Approach as the *primary* design criterion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ✦ Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the operating-line equation for a countercurrent water-cooling tower starting from an elemental energy balance. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**
