Carefully check the sign convention. > 5. **Unit mismatch** — $G_s$ in $\mathrm{kg/(m^2\cdot s)}$, $k_y a$ in $\mathrm{kg/(m^3\cdot s)}$; their ratio yields $HTU$ in metres. Do not mix with $L$ (kg/s). > 6. **Forgetting the (6 m/s) air-velocity limit** in real designs — too high leads to entrainment, too low gives poor contact. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Spray chamber** = direct-contact adiabatic humidification device using atomised water droplets. - **Operating line** = Adiabatic Saturation Line (slope ≈ 0.88 $c_s$) when makeup water is at $T_w$. - **Mass-transfer equation:** $Z = (G_s/k_y a)\int_{Y_1}^{Y_2} dY/(Y_s - Y)$. - **HTU** = $G_s/(k_y a)$ ; **NTU** = $\int dY/(Y_s - Y)$. - **Driving force** is evaluated along the **operating line temperature**, not the dry-bulb. - **Adiabatic saturation temperature $T_s \approx$ wet-bulb temperature $T_w$** for the air–water system (Lewis relation). - **Enthalpy balance:** $H'_G = H'_L$ (adiabatic case). - **Material balance:** $L(G_s) = G_s(Y_2 - Y_1)$. - **Real-world applications:** air pre-cooling, gas quenching, spray drying, paper/textile humidification, cooling-tower precursor. - **Key design checks:** (i) air velocity ≤ 6 m/s; (ii) droplet size 100–500 μm; (iii) NTU > 0 (driving force positive); (iv) $Z$ typically 2–6 m. - **For non-adiabatic operation** (hot or cold makeup water), the operating line shifts off the ASL; the design equation form remains identical but $Y_s$ values used in the integral change accordingly. - **Mollier (H–Y) chart is mandatory** for KTU Board exam solutions; always annotate the wet-bulb point and the four operating points (A, B, C, D). - **Equations to memorise verbatim:** $H' = c_s(T-T_0) + Y\lambda_s$, $Y_s = 0.622\,P_s/(P - P_s)$, $Z = HTU \cdot NTU$.

topics.title: Principles of Gas Dehumidification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1 > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Principles of Gas Dehumidification # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Gas Dehumidification **Gas Dehumidification** is defined as the process of **removing a condensable vapor (typically water vapor) from a non-condensable gas (typically air)** by bringing the gas into contact with a cooler liquid surface or by other separation methods. The operation is the reverse of humidification, and it is fundamentally governed by simultaneous **heat and mass transfer** between the gas phase and the liquid phase. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, dehumidification is classified under the umbrella of **"Gas-Liquid Contact Operations"** or **"Humidification Operations"** along with cooling and water cooling towers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Dehumidification is the separation of condensable vapor (solute) from a gas-vapor mixture by means of contact with a cold liquid in which the vapor is soluble, or by cooling below its dew point. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wet Towel Trick Imagine a hot, humid summer day. Your spectacles are fogged up. You take them inside an air-conditioned room and wipe them with a **chilled cloth** — the moisture (water vapor) condenses on the cold surface and gets absorbed by the cloth. Now, extend this to industrial scale: - The **spectacles** = the air-vapor mixture - The **fog (water vapor)** = the condensable component to be removed - The **chilled cloth** = the cold liquid surface (or cold liquid spray) - The **room** = the dehumidifier / cooling tower The moisture migrates **from the gas phase → to the liquid phase**, either by condensation (cooling below dew point) or by absorption (dissolution into a liquid). > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Significance of Dehumidification:** > - Drying of gases (natural gas, air, instrument air) > - Air conditioning and refrigeration systems > - Preservation of hygroscopic materials (pharmaceuticals, food, electronics) > - Pre-treatment for cryogenic gas separation > - Recovery of solvent vapors in chemical plants ## 1.3 Fundamental Principle — Simultaneous Heat and Mass Transfer The operation of dehumidification is a classic example of the **coupled heat-mass transfer phenomenon**. When a hot, humid gas contacts a cold liquid: 1. **Sensible heat** is transferred from the hot gas to the cold liquid (hot gas cools down). 2. **Latent heat** is released when the vapor condenses into the liquid. 3. The **vapor partial pressure gradient** drives mass transfer (vapor moves from gas to liquid). > [!NOTE] > The driving force for dehumidification is the difference between the **partial pressure of vapor in the bulk gas** and the **vapor pressure of the liquid at its surface temperature**. ## 1.4 Why Study Gas Dehumidification? The operation of dehumidification underpins the design and analysis of: - **Cooling towers** (the most important industrial application) - **Air-conditioning plants** - **Drying equipment** - **Evaporative coolers** - **Industrial ventilation systems** Hence, the KTU 2024 Scheme gives this topic high weightage, especially in design problems involving cooling tower make-up water, air-water contact, and the **Lewis relation** between heat and mass transfer coefficients. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Psychrometric Terms — The Vocabulary of Humid Air To understand dehumidification, we must first define the thermodynamic state of the **gas-vapor system** (air + water vapor). The following are the standard KTU 2024 syllabus terms: ### 2.1.1 Absolute Humidity (Humidity, $H$ or $Y$) The **mass of water vapor** carried per **unit mass of dry air** (bone-dry basis). $$H = \frac{M_{w} \cdot p_{w}}{M_{a} \cdot (P - p_{w})} = \frac{18.02 \cdot p_{w}}{28.97 \cdot (P - p_{w})}$$ Where: - $M_w = 18.02$ kg/kmol = molecular weight of water - $M_a = 28.97$ kg/kmol = molecular weight of air - $p_w$ = partial pressure of water vapor (Pa or mm Hg) - $P$ = total pressure of the system For air-water system at atmospheric pressure, this simplifies to: $$H = \frac{18.02}{28.97} \cdot \frac{p_{w}}{P - p_{w}} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_{w}}{P - p_{w}}$$ ### 2.1.2 Relative Humidity ($\phi$ or $RH$) The ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor in the gas to the **saturation pressure** of water vapor at the gas temperature. $$\phi = \frac{p_{w}}{p_{s}} \times 100\%$$ Where $p_s$ = vapor pressure of water at the gas (dry-bulb) temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > $\phi$ is a **percentage (0% to 100%)** at saturation. Relative humidity is **not conserved** during heating or cooling. ### 2.1.3 Percentage Humidity ($H_p$ or $\% H$) The ratio of the actual humidity to the saturation humidity at the **same temperature**: $$\%H = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100$$ Where $H_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_{s}}{P - p_{s}}$ at the gas temperature. ### 2.1.4 Humid Heat ($C_s$ or $c_H$) The **heat capacity** of 1 kg of dry air **plus** the water vapor it contains. It represents the energy required to raise 1 kg of dry air (with its associated vapor) by 1 °C. $$C_s = c_{pa} + H \cdot c_{pw} = 1.005 + 1.88 \cdot H \quad \text{kJ/(kg dry air · K)}$$ Where: - $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) = specific heat of dry air - $c_{pw} = 1.88$ kJ/(kg·K) = specific heat of water vapor ### 2.1.5 Humid Volume ($V_H$ or $v_H$) The volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air **plus** the water vapor it contains, measured at the gas temperature and 1 atm pressure. $$V_H = \frac{22.4}{273} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{M_a} + \frac{H}{M_w}\right) \cdot T \quad \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$ In SI units: $$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot H) \cdot T \quad \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$ Where $T$ is in Kelvin. ### 2.1.6 Dew Point ($T_{dp}$ or $T_{dew}$) The temperature at which the gas becomes **saturated** (RH = 100%) when cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity. At dew point, $p_w = p_s(T_{dp})$. ### 2.1.7 Wet-Bulb Temperature ($T_w$ or WBT) The dynamic equilibrium temperature attained by a small amount of liquid evaporating into a large quantity of unsaturated gas-vapor mixture under adiabatic conditions. It is measured using a thermometer bulb covered with a wet wick. ### 2.1.8 Adiabatic Saturation Temperature ($T_{as}$ or AST) The temperature attained when a gas-vapor mixture is contacted with its own liquid in an **adiabatic saturation process** (no heat loss to surroundings), reaching saturation at the outlet. > [!NOTE] > For the **air-water system**, $T_w \approx T_{as}$. This is a special numerical coincidence due to the value of the psychrometric ratio (Lewis number ≈ 1). ### 2.1.9 Lewis Relation (The Key Bridge) The Lewis relation connects the heat and mass transfer coefficients: $$\frac{h_c}{k_y \cdot c_s} = \frac{1}{(\text{Lewis number})^{2/3}} \approx 1$$ Or, in its more useful form: $$h_c = k_y \cdot c_s$$ Where: - $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) - $k_y$ = mass transfer coefficient based on humidity difference (kg/m²·s·ΔH) - $c_s$ = humid heat (J/kg dry air·K) This relation makes the **adiabatic saturation line and wet-bulb line coincide** for the air-water system. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Term | Formula | SI Units | KTU Notes | |---|------|---------|----------|-----------| | 1 | Absolute Humidity | $H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$ | kg H₂O/kg dry air | Used for moisture balance | | 2 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \frac{p_w}{p_s} \times 100$ | % | 100% at saturation | | 3 | Percentage Humidity | $\%H = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100$ | % | At constant temperature | | 4 | Humid Heat | $C_s = 1.005 + 1.88H$ | kJ/(kg da·K) | Use in enthalpy balance | | 5 | Humid Volume | $V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456H)T$ | m³/kg da | For volumetric flow | | 6 | Dew Point | $p_w = p_s(T_{dp})$ | °C or K | Saturation condition | | 7 | Wet-Bulb (Air-Water) | $T_w \approx T_{as}$ | °C or K | Lewis relation | | 8 | Lewis Number | $Le = \frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} \approx 1$ | dimensionless | For air-water | | 9 | Enthalpy | $H_y = C_s \cdot (T - T_{ref}) + H \cdot \lambda_{ref}$ | kJ/kg da | Reference at 0°C | | 10 | Mass Transfer Rate | $N_A = k_y \cdot (H - H_i)$ | kg/m²·s | From gas to liquid | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Cooling Tower Design** — determining make-up water requirement, air flow rate, and approach to wet-bulb. 2. **Air-Conditioning Systems** — sizing cooling coils, predicting condensate rate. 3. **Drying Calculations** — Carrier gas moisture content at inlet and outlet. 4. **Industrial Ventilation** — moisture control in chemical plants. 5. **Environmental Engineering** — predicting fog, mist, and condensation in stacks. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Absolute Humidity from Partial Pressures Consider 1 kg-mole of wet air containing water vapor and dry air. Let: - $n_a$ = moles of dry air - $n_w$ = moles of water vapor - $n_a + n_w = 1$ kmol total By Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures: $$\frac{p_w}{P} = n_w \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{P - p_w}{P} = n_a$$ Therefore: $$n_w = \frac{p_w}{P}, \quad n_a = \frac{P - p_w}{P}$$ The mass of water vapor = $n_w \cdot M_w = \frac{p_w \cdot M_w}{P}$ The mass of dry air = $n_a \cdot M_a = \frac{(P - p_w) \cdot M_a}{P}$ The absolute humidity (mass of water per mass of dry air): $$H = \frac{\text{mass of water vapor}}{\text{mass of dry air}} = \frac{\frac{p_w \cdot M_w}{P}}{\frac{(P - p_w) \cdot M_a}{P}}$$ $$H = \frac{p_w \cdot M_w}{(P - p_w) \cdot M_a}$$ Substituting $M_w = 18.02$ and $M_a = 28.97$: $$H = \frac{18.02 \cdot p_w}{28.97 \cdot (P - p_w)} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$ $$\boxed{H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This equation is the **starting point for nearly every psychrometric problem** in KTU exams. The constant **0.622** is the ratio of molecular weights of water to air. ## 3.2 Derivation: Humid Volume The humid volume is the total volume per kg of dry air at the gas conditions. By the ideal gas law: $$V = \frac{nRT}{P}$$ For 1 kg of dry air, the moles of dry air: $$n_a = \frac{1}{M_a} = \frac{1}{28.97} \text{ kmol}$$ The moles of water vapor associated: $$n_w = \frac{H}{M_w} = \frac{H}{18.02} \text{ kmol}$$ Total moles per kg dry air: $$n_{total} = \frac{1}{28.97} + \frac{H}{18.02}$$ Volume at temperature $T$ (K) and pressure $P$ (atm): $$V_H = n_{total} \cdot \frac{RT}{P}$$ For $P = 1$ atm and $R = 0.08206$ m³·atm/(kmol·K): $$V_H = \left(\frac{1}{28.97} + \frac{H}{18.02}\right) \cdot 0.08206 \cdot T$$ $$V_H = \left(0.0345 + 0.0439 \cdot H\right) \cdot T$$ The KTU formula given in textbooks uses slightly different constants: $$\boxed{V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \cdot H) \cdot T \quad \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}}$$ where $T$ is in Kelvin. This is derived using $P = 1$ atm and the ideal gas law with $R = 0.08206$. ## 3.3 Derivation: Mass Transfer Rate in Dehumidification The rate of mass transfer from the bulk gas to the gas-liquid interface follows the standard two-film theory: $$N_A = k_y \cdot (H_{bulk} - H_{interface})$$ For dehumidification specifically, we consider the **difference in humidity between the bulk gas and the gas in equilibrium with the liquid surface** at the interface temperature $T_i$: $$N_A = k_y \cdot (H - H_i)$$ Where: - $H$ = humidity of bulk gas (kg H₂O/kg dry air) - $H_i$ = humidity at the interface (in equilibrium with liquid at $T_i$) - $k_y$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s) For the cooling tower, integrating over the height $Z$: $$G_s \cdot dH = N_A \cdot a \cdot dz = k_y \cdot a \cdot (H_i - H) \cdot dz$$ Where: - $G_s$ = mass velocity of dry air (kg dry air/m²·s) - $a$ = interfacial area per unit volume (m²/m³) - $H_i$ = humidity at interface (in equilibrium with water at temperature $T$) ## 3.4 Derivation: Lewis Relation from Heat-Mass Transfer Analogy The convective heat transfer equation is: $$q = h_c \cdot (T - T_i)$$ The mass transfer equation is: $$N_A = k_y \cdot (H - H_i)$$ The **Chilton-Colburn analogy** gives: $$\frac{h_c}{G \cdot c_p} \cdot (Pr)^{2/3} = \frac{k_y}{G} \cdot (Sc)^{2/3}$$ For gases, $Pr \approx Sc$ (a defining property of gases), hence: $$h_c = k_y \cdot c_s$$ This is the **Lewis Relation**, valid for the air-water system at atmospheric pressure. $$\boxed{\frac{h_c}{k_y \cdot c_s} \approx 1 \quad \text{(Lewis Relation)}}$$ This is the **most important derivation** in KTU Module 3 because it justifies the use of $T_w$ (wet-bulb temperature) and $T_{as}$ (adiabatic saturation temperature) interchangeably for air-water systems. ## 3.5 Derivation: Material Balance in a Cooling Tower Consider a counter-current cooling tower with: - Air enters at bottom: $G_s$ (kg dry air/s), $T_1$ (inlet dry-bulb), $H_1$ (inlet humidity) - Air leaves at top: $T_2$ (outlet dry-bulb), $H_2$ (outlet humidity) - Water enters at top: $L$ (kg/s), $T_{L2}$ (inlet water temperature) - Water leaves at bottom: $T_{L1}$ (outlet water temperature) ### 3.5.1 Water (Mass) Balance $$G_s \cdot (H_2 - H_1) = L \cdot (1) - L \cdot (1) + \text{evaporation}$$ For dilute systems: $$G_s \cdot (H_2 - H_1) = L_{makeup}$$ ### 3.5.2 Enthalpy (Energy) Balance Total enthalpy of inlet air = Total enthalpy of outlet air + Heat gained by water $$G_s \cdot (C_{s2} \cdot T_2 + H_2 \cdot \lambda_2) - G_s \cdot (C_{s1} \cdot T_1 + H_1 \cdot \lambda_1) = L \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{L2} - T_{L1})$$ Where $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization at the water temperature. Assuming the enthalpies of saturated vapor at the water surface temperature are essentially the same at both ends: $$G_s \cdot [C_{s1} \cdot T_1 + H_1 \cdot \lambda_s] = G_s \cdot [C_{s2} \cdot T_2 + H_2 \cdot \lambda_s] + L \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{L2} - T_{L1})$$ The **operating line equation** on a psychrometric chart (enthalpy vs temperature) becomes linear: $$\frac{dH}{dT} = -\frac{L \cdot c_{pw}}{G_s \cdot c_s}$$ This is the key result for graphical design of cooling towers in KTU exams. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow: Counter-Current Cooling Tower ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["Warm Water Inlet T_L2 high"] --> node2["Water Distribution System"] node2 --> node3["Fills and Spray Nozzles"] node3 --> node4["Air-Water Contact Zone"] node4 --> node5["Cooled Water Collection Basin T_L1 low"] node6["Ambient Air Inlet T_1 H_1"] --> node7["Air Inlet Louvers"] node7 --> node4 node4 --> node8["Moist Air Outlet T_2 H_2"] node8 --> node9["Drift Eliminators"] node9 --> node10["Atmospheric Discharge"] node11["Make-up Water Line"] --> node5 node12["Evaporation Loss"] --> node11 node13["Blowdown"] --> node11 ``` **Process Description:** Warm process water enters at the top through distribution headers. It cascades down over packing/fills. Atmospheric air enters from the side or bottom, moves upward (counter-current), and exits at the top saturated with water vapor. Make-up water compensates for evaporation and blowdown losses. ## 4.2 Mass Transfer Mechanism at Gas-Liquid Interface ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Bulk Gas Phase T_bulk H_bulk"] -->|"Mass Transfer
N_A = k_y ΔH"| nodeB["Gas Film
Resistance 1/k_y"] nodeB --> nodeC["Interface T_i H_i saturated"] nodeC -->|"Heat Transfer
q = h_c ΔT"| nodeD["Liquid Film
Resistance 1/h_L"] nodeD --> nodeE["Bulk Liquid T_L"] nodeF["Sensible Heat
from gas to liquid"] -.-> nodeA nodeF -.-> nodeC nodeG["Latent Heat
released at interface"] -.-> nodeC ``` **Key Insight:** The interface is at the saturation condition (100% RH at $T_i$). Both heat and mass must transfer through the gas film simultaneously, governed by the Lewis Relation. ## 4.3 Classification of Gas-Liquid Contact Equipment ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Humidification and Dehumidification Equipment"] --> nodeA["Cooling Towers"] nodeStart --> nodeB["Dehumidifiers"] nodeStart --> nodeC["Scrubbers"] nodeA --> nodeA1["Natural Draft"] nodeA --> nodeA2["Mechanical Draft"] nodeA1 --> nodeA1a["Atmospheric"] nodeA1 --> nodeA1b["Hyperbolic"] nodeA2 --> nodeA2a["Forced Draft"] nodeA2 --> nodeA2b["Induced draft"] nodeB --> nodeB1["Condensation Type"] nodeB --> nodeB2["Absorption Type"] nodeB --> nodeB3["Adsorption Type"] nodeC --> nodeC1["Spray Tower"] nodeC --> nodeC2["Packed Tower"] nodeC --> nodeC3["Plate Tower"] ``` ## 4.4 Psychrometric Chart Layout (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph XAxis["X-AXIS DRY BULB TEMPERATURE"] nodeA1["0 C"] nodeA2["20 C"] nodeA3["40 C"] nodeA4["60 C"] end subgraph YAxis["Y-AXIS HUMIDITY H kg H2O per kg dry air"] nodeB1["0.000"] nodeB2["0.020"] nodeB3["0.040"] nodeB4["0.060"] end subgraph Lines["KEY CURVES"] nodeC1["Saturation Line 100 percent RH"] nodeC2["Relative Humidity Curves 10 20 50 80 percent"] nodeC3["Wet Bulb Lines equal to Adiabatic Saturation Lines"] nodeC4["Enthalpy Lines kJ per kg dry air"] nodeC5["Specific Volume Lines m3 per kg dry air"] end XAxis --> Lines YAxis --> Lines ``` **Note for KTU 2024:** When drawing the psychrometric chart in exams, students are expected to: 1. Plot the operating line for the cooling tower 2. Mark the air inlet and outlet states 3. Mark the water inlet and outlet states 4. Identify the approach and range 5. Read off enthalpies and humidities for energy balance # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the following terms with respect to humidification operations:** **(a)** Relative Humidity **(b)** Humid Heat **(c)** Humid Volume **Model Answer:** **(a) Relative Humidity:** It is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor in the gas mixture to the vapor pressure of water at the gas (dry-bulb) temperature, expressed as a percentage. $$\phi = \frac{p_w}{p_s} \times 100\%$$ **(b) Humid Heat ($C_s$):** It is the heat capacity of 1 kg of dry air along with the water vapor it carries, expressed in kJ/(kg dry air·K). For the air-water system: $$C_s = 1.005 + 1.88 H \quad \text{kJ/(kg dry air·K)}$$ **(c) Humid Volume ($V_H$):** It is the volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air plus the water vapor associated with it, measured at the gas conditions (temperature and pressure). $$V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 H) \cdot T \quad \text{m}^3/\text{kg dry air}$$ --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **State and explain the Lewis Relation. Why is it important in humidification operations?** **Model Answer:** The **Lewis Relation** is a special case of the Chilton-Colburn analogy, applicable when the **Prandtl number equals the Schmidt number** (which is true for most gas systems). It states that the ratio of the convective heat transfer coefficient to the product of the mass transfer coefficient and the humid heat is approximately equal to 1. $$\frac{h_c}{k_y \cdot c_s} \approx 1$$ **Importance:** - For the **air-water system**, this relation makes the **wet-bulb temperature equal to the adiabatic saturation temperature**. - It enables the use of a single psychrometric chart for both $T_w$ and $T_{as}$. - It simplifies the design of cooling towers by allowing $T_w$ (easily measurable) to be used in place of $T_{as}$ (requires lengthy calculation). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Derive an expression for the absolute humidity of a gas-vapor mixture in terms of partial pressure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The dry-bulb temperature of air is 35°C and the wet-bulb temperature is 25°C at atmospheric pressure of 1.013 bar. Calculate: 1. Absolute humidity 2. Relative humidity 3. Humid heat 4. Humid volume 5. Dew point 6. Enthalpy **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Absolute Humidity Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The complete derivation yields: $$H = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** - $T_{db} = 35°C$ (dry-bulb) - $T_{wb} = 25°C$ (wet-bulb) - $P = 1.013$ bar = $101.3$ kPa **Step 1: Vapor Pressures (from steam tables)** - $p_s$ at $35°C$ = 5.628 kPa - $p_{s,w}$ at $25°C$ = 3.169 kPa (saturation pressure at wet-bulb temperature) **Step 2: Humidity at Wet-Bulb (Saturation at 25°C)** $$H_w = 0.622 \cdot \frac{p_{s,w}}{P - p_{s,w}} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.169}{101.3 - 3.169}$$ $$H_w = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.169}{98.131} = 0.622 \cdot 0.0323 = 0.02009 \text{ kg H₂O/kg da}$$ **Step 3: Humid Heat at Wet-Bulb** $$C_{s,w} = 1.005 + 1.88 \times 0.02009 = 1.005 + 0.0378 = 1.0428 \text{ kJ/(kg da·K)}$$ **Step 4: Use Wet-Bulb Equation to find $H$** $$H = H_w - \frac{C_{s,w}}{\lambda_w} \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})$$ Where $\lambda_w$ at $25°C$ = 2442 kJ/kg $$H = 0.02009 - \frac{1.0428}{2442} \cdot (35 - 25)$$ $$H = 0.02009 - \frac{1.0428 \times 10}{2442}$$ $$H = 0.02009 - 0.00427 = 0.01582 \text{ kg H₂O/kg da}$$ > **Humidity at Saturation (35°C):** $$H_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{5.628}{101.3 - 5.628} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{5.628}{95.672} = 0.03659 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ > **1. Absolute Humidity:** $H = 0.01582$ kg H₂O/kg dry air > **2. Relative Humidity:** $\phi = \frac{H}{H_s} \times 100 = \frac{0.01582}{0.03659} \times 100 = 43.2\%$ > **3. Humid Heat:** $C_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \times 0.01582 = 1.005 + 0.0297 = 1.0347$ kJ/(kg da·K) > **4. Humid Volume:** $V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 \times 0.01582) \times 308.15 = (0.00283 + 0.0000721) \times 308.15 = 0.894$ m³/kg da > **5. Dew Point:** Find $T$ such that $p_s = p_w$. We have $p_w = \frac{H \cdot P}{0.622 + H} = \frac{0.01582 \times 101.3}{0.622 + 0.01582} = \frac{1.6026}{0.6378} = 2.512$ kPa > From steam tables, $T_{dp} \approx 21.7°C$ > **6. Enthalpy:** $H_y = C_s \cdot T + H \cdot \lambda_0 = 1.0347 \times 35 + 0.01582 \times 2501$ > $H_y = 36.21 + 39.57 = 75.78$ kJ/kg da --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the working principle of an induced-draft cooling tower with a neat sketch. Discuss its advantages over natural-draft towers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A counter-current induced-draft cooling tower is to cool 50,000 kg/h of water from 40°C to 30°C using air entering at 30°C (DBT) and 22°C (WBT). The air leaves at 36°C (DBT) and 32°C (WBT). Calculate: 1. The mass flow rate of dry air required 2. The make-up water requirement **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Induced-Draft Cooling Tower **Working Principle:** An induced-draft cooling tower uses a **fan located at the air exit (top)** to pull air upward through the tower. Warm water is sprayed from the top through distribution headers over the fills (packing). Air is drawn in from the side louvers at the bottom, flows upward in **counter-current** to the water, and exits at the top saturated with water vapor. **Key Components:** - Water distribution system - Fill/packing - Drift eliminators - Induced-draft fan (at top) - Air inlet louvers - Cold water basin - Make-up water line **Advantages over Natural-Draft:** - Smaller footprint and capital cost - Independent of atmospheric conditions (works even in still air) - Higher cooling range achievable - Easier control of air flow rate #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** - Water flow rate: $L = 50,000$ kg/h = 13.89 kg/s - Water inlet: $T_{L2} = 40°C$ - Water outlet: $T_{L1} = 30°C$ - Air inlet: $T_1 = 30°C$ (DBT), $T_{w1} = 22°C$ (WBT) - Air outlet: $T_2 = 36°C$ (DBT), $T_{w2} = 32°C$ (WBT) - Atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar **Step 1: Calculate Humidities from Wet-Bulb Data** **Air Inlet (30°C DBT, 22°C WBT):** - $p_{s,w}$ at 22°C = 2.645 kPa - $H_1$ = 0.622 × 2.645 / (101.3 - 2.645) = 0.622 × 2.645 / 98.655 = **0.01668 kg/kg da** - $C_{s,1} = 1.005 + 1.88 × 0.01668 = 1.0364$ kJ/(kg·K) - Enthalpy: $H_{y1} = 1.0364 × 30 + 0.01668 × 2501 = 31.09 + 41.72 = 72.81$ kJ/kg da **Air Outlet (36°C DBT, 32°C WBT):** - $p_{s,w}$ at 32°C = 4.760 kPa - $H_2$ = 0.622 × 4.760 / (101.3 - 4.760) = 0.622 × 4.760 / 96.54 = **0.03067 kg/kg da** - $C_{s,2} = 1.005 + 1.88 × 0.03067 = 1.0627$ kJ/(kg·K) - Enthalpy: $H_{y2} = 1.0627 × 36 + 0.03067 × 2501 = 38.26 + 76.71 = 114.97$ kJ/kg da **Step 2: Enthalpy Balance to Find Air Flow Rate** $$G_s \cdot (H_{y2} - H_{y1}) = L \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{L2} - T_{L1})$$ $$G_s \cdot (114.97 - 72.81) = 13.89 \times 4.186 \times (40 - 30)$$ $$G_s \cdot 42.16 = 13.89 \times 4.186 \times 10 = 581.4 \text{ kJ/s}$$ $$G_s = \frac{581.4}{42.16} = 13.79 \text{ kg dry air/s}$$ $$G_s = 13.79 \times 3600 = 49{,}650 \text{ kg dry air/h}$$ > **1. Mass flow rate of dry air ≈ 49,650 kg/h ≈ 49.65 tonnes/h** **Step 3: Make-up Water = Evaporation Loss + Drift + Blowdown** **Evaporation Loss:** $$L_{evap} = G_s \cdot (H_2 - H_1) = 13.79 \times (0.03067 - 0.01668)$$ $$L_{evap} = 13.79 \times 0.01399 = 0.193 \text{ kg/s} = 694.7 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Drift Loss:** Assume 0.2% of water flow = 0.002 × 50,000 = 100 kg/h **Blowdown Loss:** Assume 0.5% of water flow = 0.005 × 50,000 = 250 kg/h **Total Make-up Water:** $$L_{makeup} = 694.7 + 100 + 250 = 1044.7 \text{ kg/h} \approx 1045 \text{ kg/h}$$ > **2. Make-up water requirement ≈ 1045 kg/h** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Gas Dehumidification Problems:** > > 1. **Confusing $H$ and $\phi$:** Students often mix up absolute humidity with relative humidity. Remember: $H$ depends on partial pressures; $\phi$ depends on saturation pressure. > > 2. **Forgetting to use dry-air basis:** All flow rates in cooling tower problems should be on **bone-dry air basis**, not wet air basis. > > 3. **Using $\lambda = 2501$ kJ/kg everywhere:** The latent heat of vaporization changes with temperature. Use $\lambda$ at the operating temperature. > > 4. **Not specifying the constant 0.622 derivation:** The factor comes from $M_w/M_a = 18.02/28.97$. Examiners expect this to be mentioned. > > 5. **Skipping units in psychrometric formulas:** Always write $H$ in kg H₂O/kg dry air, not just "kg/kg" or "%". > > 6. **Confusing wet-bulb and dew point:** Wet-bulb is the adiabatic cooling limit; dew point is the saturation temperature at constant humidity. > > 7. **Incorrectly applying Lewis Relation:** The Lewis Relation is valid **only for gas systems** (where $Pr \approx Sc$). It is **not** generally valid for liquid systems. > > 8. **In cooling tower problems:** Forgetting to subtract the **entrained moisture** (drift) in the air outlet. Some problems consider this; some don't. > > 9. **Not drawing operating lines:** In design problems, KTU examiners allocate **at least 2-3 marks** for the operating line diagram on the psychrometric chart. > > 10. **Forgetting the make-up water includes blowdown:** Total make-up = Evaporation + Drift + Blowdown, not just evaporation. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Gas dehumidification is the removal of a condensable vapor (water) from a non-condensable gas (air) by contact with a cold liquid or cooled surface. - **Key Psychrometric Quantities:** - $H = 0.622 \cdot p_w / (P - p_w)$ — **absolute humidity** (kg/kg dry air) - $\phi = p_w / p_s \times 100$ — **relative humidity** (%) - $C_s = 1.005 + 1.88H$ — **humid heat** (kJ/kg·K) - $V_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456H) \cdot T$ — **humid volume** (m³/kg) - $T_{dp}$: saturation at constant $H$ - $T_w \approx T_{as}$ (air-water) - **The Lewis Relation:** $h_c / (k_y \cdot c_s) \approx 1$ — bridges heat and mass transfer for gas systems. - **Equipment for Dehumidification/Humidification:** - Cooling towers (natural and mechanical draft) - Spray chambers - Packed columns - Plate columns - **Key Design Equation for Cooling Tower:** $$G_s \cdot (H_{y2} - H_{y1}) = L \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{L2} - T_{L1})$$ - **Make-up Water:** $L_{makeup} = L_{evap} + L_{drift} + L_{blowdown}$ - **Driving Force:** Difference in humidity between bulk gas and gas at liquid interface. - **Air-Water System Special Property:** $T_w = T_{as}$ (a unique property due to Lewis Number ≈ 1). - **Operating Line Slope:** $-\frac{L \cdot c_{pw}}{G_s \cdot c_s}$ — linear on psychrometric chart (enthalpy vs. temperature). - **Industrial Applications:** Air conditioning, drying, gas processing, refrigeration, HVAC, process industries.

topics.title: Adsorption: Types of Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adsorption: Types of Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADSORPTION (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> **Adsorption** is a *surface phenomenon* in which molecules of a fluid phase (gas or liquid), termed the **adsorbate**, accumulate and concentrate at the interfacial boundary of a *solid* (or sometimes liquid) substrate termed the **adsorbent**. The process results from the existence of *unsatisfied (residual) intermolecular forces* at the surface of the adsorbent, which attract and hold foreign molecules from the bulk phase.

### Key Terminology (Syllabus-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Adsorbent** | The solid (or liquid) phase that *provides the surface* for accumulation | Activated carbon, silica gel, zeolite, alumina |
| **Adsorbate** | The solute/gas species that *gets adsorbed* onto the surface | Water vapour, CO₂, organic dyes, VOCs |
| **Absorption** | Bulk uptake into the *interior* of a phase (NOT a surface phenomenon) | CO₂ absorbed into MEA solution |
| **Sorption** | Umbrella term covering **adsorption + absorption** | — |
| **Desorption** | Reverse of adsorption; release of adsorbate from the surface | Regeneration of spent activated carbon |
| **Equilibrium loading (q\*)** | Mass of adsorbate held per unit mass of adsorbent at equilibrium | kg adsorbate / kg adsorbent |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Distinction:**
> Adsorption is a *surface* phenomenon. Absorption involves *bulk penetration* (e.g., a sponge soaking water). The combined term is **Sorption**. The KTU paper frequently asks students to differentiate these — losing 1–2 marks by writing "absorption" instead of "adsorption" is a classic pitfall.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Magnet & Iron Filings"

Imagine holding a **strong bar magnet** just above a tray of **iron filings**:

- The magnet is the **adsorbent** (it offers a *surface* with unsatisfied attractive forces).
- The iron filings are the **adsorbate** molecules from the surrounding medium.
- The filings *cling to the pole faces* of the magnet — they do **not** enter the bulk of the magnet.

> **Why does this happen?** Just like the magnet's surface has unpaired magnetic dipoles pulling at nearby filings, a solid surface has *unbalanced valency*, *polar groups*, or *van der Waals fields* that pull molecules toward it. **Higher surface area = more attraction = more adsorption capacity.** This is why industrial adsorbents are highly **porous** (e.g., activated carbon has surface areas of **500–1500 m²/g**).

### Intuitive Real-World Examples

1. **Silica gel packets** in medicine bottles → adsorbs water vapour (keeps medicines dry).
2. **Activated charcoal masks** → adsorbs toxic gases and odour molecules.
3. **Chroma­tography columns** → separate mixture components by differential adsorption.
4. **Ion-exchange water softeners** → adsorb Ca²⁺ / Mg²⁺ ions.
5. **Catalytic converters** → gases chemisorb on platinum-rhodium surfaces.

---

## 1.3 Why Adsorption Occurs — The Driving Force

The driving force for adsorption is the **reduction in surface free energy (Gibbs energy, G)** of the solid. Unsatisfied surface molecules possess excess Gibbs energy. When an adsorbate molecule attaches, these unsatisfied bonds are partially satisfied, and the system's free energy drops:

$$\Delta G_{ads} = G_{final} - G_{initial} < 0$$

Because adsorption is **exothermic** in nearly all practical cases (heat is released when molecules settle onto attractive surface sites), the enthalpy change is negative:

$$\Delta H_{ads} < 0 \quad (\text{exothermic process})$$

And since adsorption **decreases entropy** of the fluid (molecules are constrained from a 3-D volume to a 2-D surface), the entropy change is negative:

$$\Delta S_{ads} < 0$$

For spontaneity, the Gibbs criterion requires:

$$\Delta G_{ads} = \Delta H_{ads} - T\Delta S_{ads} < 0$$

Since $\Delta S_{ads} < 0$, the $-T\Delta S_{ads}$ term is **positive**. This is why adsorption is *only* spontaneous when $\vert \Delta H_{ads} \vert > \vert T\Delta S_{ads} \vert$ — i.e., the energetic gain outweighs the entropic penalty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher temperature → less adsorption** (Le Chatelier's principle for an exothermic process). This is why adsorption units are sometimes operated at low temperature and **regenerated at high temperature**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Adsorption — A Structured Logic Map

Adsorption is classified along **three independent axes**:

### Axis A — By the *Nature of the Binding Force*

This is the **most important classification** for KTU exams.

#### 1) Physical Adsorption (Physisorption / van der Waals Adsorption)

- Caused by **weak, non-specific intermolecular forces** (London dispersion, dipole–dipole, Keesom, Debye).
- Heat of adsorption: **low**, typically **4–40 kJ/mol** (i.e., only 2–5 times the latent heat of vaporisation).
- Adsorption is **reversible** — the adsorbate can be desorbed by lowering pressure or raising temperature.
- **Multilayer adsorption** is common (especially near the saturation pressure).
- Occurs at *any* temperature, but is most pronounced **below or near the critical temperature** of the adsorbate.
- No electron transfer or chemical bond formation.
- **Low activation energy** (often ≈ 0) — occurs almost instantly upon contact.

#### 2) Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption / Activated Adsorption)

- Caused by **covalent or ionic chemical bonds** between the surface atoms and the adsorbate.
- Heat of adsorption: **high**, typically **40–400 kJ/mol** (comparable to ordinary chemical bond energies).
- Adsorption is often **irreversible** (or only reversible at very high temperatures).
- **Monolayer only** — once the surface is covered, no further chemisorption is possible.
- Occurs only **at high temperatures** (requires *activation energy* ≈ 5–40 kJ/mol).
- May involve **electron transfer** → the adsorbate often loses its original chemical identity (e.g., O₂ on charcoal forms surface oxides).
- Can be **activated** or **non-activated** depending on the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physisorption always precedes chemisorption.** A molecule first weakly physisorbs (the "precursor state"), and if sufficient thermal activation energy is available, it transitions into the chemisorbed state. This is the basis of the **Elovich equation** and many catalytic mechanisms.

---

### Axis B — By *Number of Layers*

| Type | Description | Typical Model |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Monolayer adsorption** | Only one molecular layer forms; the surface saturates after this single layer | Langmuir isotherm |
| **Multilayer adsorption** | Subsequent layers form over the first; possible up to the bulk condensation point | BET isotherm |

### Axis C — By *Sign of Excess Concentration*

- **Positive adsorption** → concentration of the solute at the interface is *greater* than in the bulk (most common).
- **Negative adsorption** → concentration at the interface is *less* than in the bulk (occurs in some electrolyte systems; e.g., NaCl at the air–water interface).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — Physisorption vs. Chemisorption

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Bonding force** | van der Waals (weak) | Covalent / ionic (strong) |
| **Heat of adsorption** | 4 – 40 kJ/mol (low) | 40 – 400 kJ/mol (high) |
| **Activation energy** | ≈ 0 (negligible) | 5 – 40 kJ/mol (significant) |
| **Temperature range** | Below critical T of adsorbate | Generally above ambient |
| **Specificity** | Non-specific (any gas on any surface) | Highly specific (depends on chemistry) |
| **Layers formed** | Multimolecular (multi-layer) | Monomolecular (monolayer) |
| **Reversibility** | Highly reversible | Often irreversible |
| **Effect of temperature** | Decreases with rising T (exothermic) | Increases up to a point, then decreases |
| **Effect of pressure** | Increases with pressure | Little effect beyond monolayer |
| **Surface area dependence** | High | High |
| **Desorption** | Easy, by heating or evacuating | Difficult, requires high T |
| **Examples** | O₂ on charcoal at low T; water on silica gel | O₂ on tungsten; H₂ on nickel catalyst |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorise the *numeric ranges* of heat of adsorption. Writing "physisorption has low heat" without specifying a value loses marks.

---

## 2.3 Adsorption Isotherms — The Core Engineering Tool

The **adsorption isotherm** is the equilibrium relationship between the amount adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent ($q^*$) and the partial pressure (or concentration) of the adsorbate at a *constant temperature*.

### (a) Freundlich Isotherm (Empirical)

$$q^* = k_F \cdot (P^*)^{1/n}$$

where $k_F$ and $n$ are empirical constants; $1/n$ typically lies between 0.1 and 1.0.

In linear form (for plotting $\ln q^*$ vs $\ln P^*$):

$$\ln q^* = \ln k_F + \frac{1}{n} \ln P^*$$

### (b) Langmuir Isotherm (Monolayer)

$$q^* = \frac{q_m \, K_L \, P^*}{1 + K_L \, P^*}$$

where $q_m$ = monolayer capacity (kg/kg) and $K_L$ = Langmuir equilibrium constant (1/Pa).

In linear form:

$$\frac{P^*}{q^*} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{P^*}{q_m}$$

### (c) BET Isotherm (Multilayer)

$$\frac{P^*}{V(P_0 - P^*)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P^*}{P_0}$$

where $V$ = volume adsorbed, $V_m$ = monolayer volume, $P_0$ = saturation vapour pressure, and $C$ = BET constant related to heat of adsorption.

---

## 2.4 The Clausius–Clapeyron Form for Heat of Adsorption

The **isosteric (differential) heat of adsorption** $\Delta H_s$ is obtained by applying the Clausius–Clapeyron equation to the adsorption equilibrium:

$$\left( \frac{\partial \ln P^*}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*=\text{const}} = \frac{\Delta H_s}{R T^2}$$

Integrating (assuming $\Delta H_s$ constant over the temperature range):

$$\ln P^* = -\frac{\Delta H_s}{R T} + C$$

A plot of $\ln P^*$ vs $1/T$ at constant $q^*$ gives a straight line whose slope is $-\Delta H_s / R$. This is the **standard KTU method** to determine heats of adsorption experimentally.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Adsorbent Used | Adsorbate Removed |
|-------------|----------------|--------------------|
| Solvent recovery in paint industry | Activated carbon | VOCs (toluene, xylene) |
| Natural gas purification | Zeolite 5A | H₂S, CO₂, H₂O |
| Oxygen concentrators (medical) | Zeolite 13X | N₂ (to enrich O₂) |
| Sugar refining decolorisation | Bone char / activated carbon | Coloured impurities |
| Domestic water filters | Activated carbon | Chlorine, odour, organics |
| Catalytic cracking (FCC units) | Zeolite Y | Hydrocarbon vapour |
| Drying of air/gases | Silica gel, molecular sieves | Water vapour |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Note:** The choice between **physisorption** (e.g., gas drying with silica gel) and **chemisorption** (e.g., catalytic reactions) determines whether the process is *reversible regeneration* (TSA / PSA cycles) or *one-time reactive capture*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm (Monolayer)

This is the **single most-tested derivation** in KTU Mass Transfer Operations I.

### Assumptions (KTU Board requires stating these)

1. The adsorbent surface possesses a **fixed number of adsorption sites**, all energetically equivalent.
2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** (no stacking).
3. The heat of adsorption is **constant and independent** of surface coverage (i.e., the surface is energetically uniform).
4. There is **no interaction** between adjacent adsorbed molecules.
5. Dynamic equilibrium exists: rate of condensation = rate of evaporation at a given $P^*$ and $T$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $S$ = vacant surface site
- $A$ = gas-phase adsorbate molecule
- $SA$ = occupied site (adsorbate + site)

Equilibrium reaction:

$$S + A \;\rightleftharpoons\; SA$$

**Step 1 — Define fractional surface coverage $\theta$.**

$$\theta = \frac{\text{Number of sites occupied}}{\text{Total number of sites}} = \frac{N_{SA}}{N_T}$$

Then fraction of surface **vacant** is $(1 - \theta)$.

**Step 2 — Rate of adsorption (condensation onto surface).**

Rate is proportional to the bare surface available and the gas pressure $P^*$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a \, P^* \, (1 - \theta)$$

**Step 3 — Rate of desorption (evaporation from surface).**

Rate is proportional only to the covered surface:

$$r_{des} = k_d \, \theta$$

**Step 4 — At dynamic equilibrium**, set the two rates equal:

$$k_a \, P^* \, (1 - \theta) = k_d \, \theta$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\theta$.**

$$k_a P^* - k_a P^* \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a P^* = k_d \theta + k_a P^* \theta = \theta (k_d + k_a P^*)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P^*}{k_d + k_a P^*}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the equilibrium constant $K_L = k_a / k_d$.**

$$\theta = \frac{K_L P^*}{1 + K_L P^*}$$

**Step 7 — Relate coverage to amount adsorbed.**

Let $q^*$ = mass adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent, $q_m$ = monolayer capacity. Then:

$$q^* = q_m \, \theta$$

$$\boxed{\,q^* = \frac{q_m \, K_L \, P^*}{1 + K_L \, P^*}\,}$$

This is the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm**.

**Step 8 — Limiting cases (verify physical sense).**

| Limit | Approximation | Physical meaning |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| Very low $P^*$ ($K_L P^* \ll 1$) | $q^* \approx q_m K_L P^*$ | Linear / Henry's law regime |
| Very high $P^*$ ($K_L P^* \gg 1$) | $q^* \approx q_m$ | Surface saturated; monolayer complete |

**Step 9 — Linear form for experimental data plotting.**

Divide numerator and denominator by $K_L P^*$:

$$\frac{q^*}{P^*} = \frac{q_m K_L}{1 + K_L P^*}$$

Rearrange (more useful form):

$$\frac{P^*}{q^*} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{P^*}{q_m}$$

A plot of $P^*/q^*$ vs $P^*$ yields:
- Slope = $1/q_m$ → gives monolayer capacity.
- Intercept = $1/(q_m K_L)$ → gives $K_L$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How the "Type" of Adsorption Shows Up in Engineering Data

| Observable Feature | Indicates Physisorption | Indicates Chemisorption |
|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Heat of adsorption (kJ/mol)** | 4 – 40 | 40 – 400 |
| **Effect of $\uparrow$ T on uptake** | Uptake decreases | Uptake may increase first |
| **Layer thickness (XRD, ellipsometry)** | Multi-layer | Strictly monolayer |
| **Isotherm shape** | Type II / Type III (BET classification) | Langmuir (Type I) |
| **Effect of evacuation at room T** | Easily desorbed | Strongly retained |
| **Effect of activation energy** | $E_a \approx 0$ | $E_a$ measurable & positive |
| **Specificity of surface** | Low (any surface works) | High (specific chemistry) |
| **Electron transfer (XPS / work function)** | None | Measurable |

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Isosteric Heat of Adsorption (Clausius–Clapeyron Form)

The **Gibbs adsorption equation** gives the relationship between surface excess $\Gamma$ and surface tension $\gamma$ at constant T:

$$\Gamma = -\frac{1}{RT} \left( \frac{\partial \gamma}{\partial \ln P^*} \right)_T$$

For adsorption, the chemical potential of the adsorbate on the surface equals that in the gas phase at equilibrium:

$$\mu_{surface} = \mu_{gas}$$

Applying the Clausius–Clapeyron relation to this equilibrium (with $q^*$ held constant along an isostere):

$$\left( \frac{\partial \ln P^*}{\partial (1/T)} \right)_{q^*} = -\frac{\Delta H_s}{R}$$

**Step 1 — Starting point (thermodynamic identity).**

At constant $q^*$, the chemical potential of the adsorbed phase is fixed. The gas-phase chemical potential follows:

$$\mu_{gas} = \mu^0 + RT \ln P^*$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to T at fixed $\mu$ (fixed $q^*$):**

$$0 = \left( \frac{\partial \mu^0}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*} + R \ln P^* + RT \left( \frac{\partial \ln P^*}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*}$$

**Step 3 — Recognise that $\left( \frac{\partial \mu^0}{\partial T} \right) = -\bar{S}^0_{gas}$:**

$$0 = -\bar{S}^0_{gas} + R \ln P^* + RT \left( \frac{\partial \ln P^*}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the gas-phase relation $\bar{S}^0_{gas} = \bar{S}^0_{ads} + \Delta S_{ads}$:**

$$0 = -\bar{S}^0_{ads} - \Delta S_{ads} + R \ln P^* + RT \left( \frac{\partial \ln P^*}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*}$$

**Step 5 — Final rearranged form:**

$$\left( \frac{\partial \ln P^*}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*} = \frac{\Delta S_{ads}}{RT} - \frac{R \ln P^*}{RT} + \frac{\bar{S}^0_{ads}}{RT}$$

In the standard **integrated** form (assuming $\Delta H_{ads}$ is approximately constant over the temperature range of interest):

$$\boxed{\,\ln P^* = -\frac{\Delta H_s}{R T} + C\,}$$

A plot of $\ln P^*$ vs $1/T$ at constant $q^*$ gives a straight line of slope $-\Delta H_s / R$. **This is the canonical KTU method** to extract the isosteric heat of adsorption from experimental isotherms measured at multiple temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Classification of Adsorption

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ADSORPTION SURFACE PHENOMENON"] --> B["AXIS A NATURE OF BONDING"]
    A --> C["AXIS B NUMBER OF LAYERS"]
    A --> D["AXIS C EXCESS CONCENTRATION"]

    B --> B1["PHYSISORPTION"]
    B --> B2["CHEMISORPTION"]

    B1 --> B1A["van der Waals forces"]
    B1 --> B1B["Heat 4 to 40 kJ per mol"]
    B1 --> B1C["Reversible"]
    B1 --> B1D["Multilayer possible"]
    B1 --> B1E["Ea near zero"]
    B1 --> B1F["Below critical T"]

    B2 --> B2A["Covalent or ionic bonds"]
    B2 --> B2B["Heat 40 to 400 kJ per mol"]
    B2 --> B2C["Often irreversible"]
    B2 --> B2D["Monolayer only"]
    B2 --> B2E["Ea 5 to 40 kJ per mol"]
    B2 --> B2F["Higher temperatures"]

    C --> C1["MONOLAYER Langmuir"]
    C --> C2["MULTILAYER BET"]
    C --> C3["FREUNDLICH Empirical"]

    D --> D1["POSITIVE Excess at surface"]
    D --> D2["NEGATIVE Depletion at surface"]

    B1A --> X["Example H2O on silica gel at 25 C"]
    B2A --> Y["Example O2 on tungsten catalyst"]
    C1 --> Z["Activated carbon CHCl3"]
    C2 --> W["N2 on Fe catalyst at 77 K"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Layer Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PHYS["PHYSISORPTION MULTILAYER STACKING"]
        P1["Solid Adsorbent Surface"] --> P2["Layer 1 Physisorbed"]
        P2 --> P3["Layer 2 Weakly bound"]
        P3 --> P4["Layer 3 Weaker still"]
        P4 --> P5["Bulk gas phase beyond"]
    end

    subgraph CHEM["CHEMISORPTION MONOLAYER ONLY"]
        C1["Solid Adsorbent Surface"] --> C2["Layer 1 Chemically bonded"]
        C2 --> C3["No further layers possible"]
        C3 --> C4["Bulk gas phase beyond"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow of an Industrial Adsorption Unit (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN["Inlet gas stream containing adsorbate"] --> COMP["Blower or compressor"]
    COMP --> PRE["Precooler and particulate filter"]
    PRE --> BED1["Adsorption bed 1 Online"]
    BED1 --> OUT1["Clean dry gas to process"]

    BED1 -.Saturated.-> REG["Bed 1 enters regeneration mode"]
    REG --> HX["External heat exchanger supplies hot nitrogen"]
    HX --> BED1B["Desorption of accumulated adsorbate"]
    BED1B --> COND["Condenser recovers solvent"]
    COND --> COL["Collected product liquid"]
    BED1B --> COOL["Cooling of regenerated bed"]
    COOL --> SWAP["Valve swap Bed 1 to standby Bed 2 to online"]

    SWAP --> BED2["Adsorption bed 2 Online"]
    BED2 --> OUT2["Clean dry gas to process"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above shows how the **type of adsorption** (physical vs. chemical) dictates the regeneration strategy. Physisorption systems use **Temperature Swing Adsorption (TSA)** or **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** because the bonds are weak. Chemisorption systems often require *very high temperatures* or chemical treatment to break the strong bonds, making them less suitable for cyclic regeneration.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between **physisorption** and **chemisorption** with at least **four** distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Bonding | van der Waals forces | Covalent or ionic chemical bonds |
| Heat of adsorption | Low (4–40 kJ/mol) | High (40–400 kJ/mol) |
| Number of layers | Multi-layer possible | Strictly mono-layer |
| Reversibility | Highly reversible | Often irreversible |
| Activation energy | ≈ 0 | 5–40 kJ/mol (significant) |
| Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| Temperature effect | Decreases with rising T | Increases up to a point, then decreases |

**Marks allocation (3 marks):** 4 features × 0.5 mark each + 2 for accurate comparison language = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the terms **adsorbate** and **adsorbent** with one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Adsorbate:** The substance *that gets adsorbed* (the fluid-phase species) on to the surface. **Example:** Water vapour adsorbed from humid air.
- **Adsorbent:** The solid (or liquid) substance *that provides the surface* for adsorption. **Example:** Activated carbon used in water filters.

(Any valid industrial example accepted — silica gel, zeolite, activated alumina, bone char.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q8(a)/(b) internal choice pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **types of adsorption** based on the *nature of the bonding force*. Compare the two principal types in terms of heat of adsorption, reversibility, layer formation, and temperature dependence.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** starting from the dynamic equilibrium of adsorption and desorption rates. State all assumptions clearly. Show how the limiting cases lead to Henry's law at low pressure and monolayer saturation at high pressure.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Sketch (description for 2 marks):**

Show a solid surface with two side-by-side representations:
1. **Physisorption** — multiple weakly-held molecular layers (molecules shown as light cloud).
2. **Chemisorption** — single tightly-bound monolayer (molecules shown bonded to specific surface atoms with directional bonds).

**Description (2 marks):**
Adsorption is classified by bonding nature into:
- **Physisorption** (physical) — caused by weak van der Waals forces.
- **Chemisorption** (chemical) — caused by formation of covalent/ionic bonds.

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Heat of adsorption | 4–40 kJ/mol (low) | 40–400 kJ/mol (high) |
| Reversibility | Highly reversible | Often irreversible |
| Layers | Multi-layer possible | Monolayer only |
| Temperature effect | Decreases as T increases | Increases then decreases (activated) |

---

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Assumptions (2 marks):** *(State all five assumptions clearly — see Section 3.1.)*
1. Fixed number of equivalent adsorption sites.
2. Each site holds only one molecule.
3. Constant heat of adsorption, independent of coverage.
4. No lateral interaction between adsorbed molecules.
5. Dynamic equilibrium at the surface.

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(Write the steps shown in Section 3.1.)*
- Define coverage $\theta$, vacant fraction $(1-\theta)$.
- Write $r_{ads} = k_a P^* (1-\theta)$ and $r_{des} = k_d \theta$.
- Set equal → solve for $\theta = \frac{K_L P^*}{1 + K_L P^*}$.
- Multiply by $q_m$ to obtain $q^* = \frac{q_m K_L P^*}{1 + K_L P^*}$.

**Limiting cases (1 mark):**
- $K_L P^* \ll 1$ → $q^* \approx q_m K_L P^*$ (linear, Henry's law).
- $K_L P^* \gg 1$ → $q^* \approx q_m$ (monolayer saturation).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify adsorption based on **(i)** the nature of the bonding force, **(ii)** the number of layers, and **(iii)** the sign of excess surface concentration. Give one example of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **isosteric heat of adsorption**. Derive the Clausius–Clapeyron form for the determination of isosteric heat from adsorption isotherms measured at different temperatures. Explain how the value of $\Delta H_s$ can be used to distinguish between physisorption and chemisorption.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) By nature of bonding (2 marks):**
- **Physisorption** → e.g., O₂ on charcoal at 0 °C.
- **Chemisorption** → e.g., H₂ on nickel at 300 °C.

**(ii) By number of layers (2 marks):**
- **Monolayer** → e.g., N₂ on iron at 77 K; well-fitted by Langmuir isotherm.
- **Multi-layer** → e.g., water vapour on silica gel near saturation; fitted by BET isotherm.

**(iii) By sign of excess surface concentration (2 marks):**
- **Positive adsorption** → solute concentrates at the interface (most common; e.g., dyes on charcoal).
- **Negative adsorption** → solute depletes at the interface (e.g., NaCl at air–water interface; the salt ions are repelled from the surface).

**Example/description (1 mark):** State industrial relevance for any one type.

---

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **isosteric heat of adsorption** $\Delta H_s$ is the molar enthalpy change when a small quantity of adsorbate is transferred from the bulk gas (or liquid) phase to the *infinite* adsorbed state, evaluated at a *fixed surface loading* $q^*$. It is called "isosteric" because it is measured along a line of constant surface coverage (an "isostere").

**Derivation of Clausius–Clapeyron form (4 marks):**
At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the adsorbate on the surface equals that in the gas phase:
$$\mu_{ads}(T) = \mu_{gas}(T, P^*)$$

For the gas (ideal):
$$\mu_{gas} = \mu^0_{gas} + RT \ln P^*$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $T$ at constant $q^*$:
$$\left( \frac{\partial \mu_{ads}}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*} = \left( \frac{\partial \mu^0_{gas}}{\partial T} \right) + R \ln P^* + RT \left( \frac{\partial \ln P^*}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*}$$

Using $\left( \frac{\partial \mu}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\bar{S}$ and $\Delta S_{ads} = \bar{S}_{ads} - \bar{S}_{gas}$, and $\Delta H_{ads} = \Delta S_{ads} \cdot T$ (in the integrated limit):

$$\left( \frac{\partial \ln P^*}{\partial T} \right)_{q^*} = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R T^2}$$

In the **integrated Clausius–Clapeyron form** (assuming $\Delta H_{ads}$ is approximately constant over the experimental T range):

$$\ln P^* = -\frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R T} + C$$

A plot of $\ln P^*$ vs $1/T$ at constant $q^*$ gives a **straight line** of slope $-\Delta H_{ads}/R$, from which $\Delta H_{ads}$ is obtained.

**Use to distinguish physisorption vs. chemisorption (1 mark):**
- If $\vert \Delta H_{ads} \vert$ lies in the range **4–40 kJ/mol** → **physisorption**.
- If $\vert \Delta H_{ads} \vert$ lies in the range **40–400 kJ/mol** → **chemisorption**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **"Adsorption" vs "Absorption"** — confusing the two costs full marks. Always specify "surface phenomenon" for adsorption.
> 2. **Heat-of-adsorption ranges** must be quoted *with units* and *with numbers*. Writing "low" or "high" alone → 0 marks.
> 3. **In Langmuir derivation** — students frequently forget to **state all 5 assumptions**; this alone costs 2 marks.
> 4. **"Physisorption is monolayer"** is **wrong**. The default physisorption is *multi-layer*; monolayer physisorption is a special (low-pressure) case. Confusing this with chemisorption is a common error.
> 5. **Effect of temperature**: Physisorption always decreases with rising T. Chemisorption may *first increase* (because of activation energy) and *then decrease* at very high T. Don't write a one-liner that "adsorption always decreases with temperature."
> 6. **Units of $q^*$**: Always state **kg adsorbate / kg adsorbent** (or mol/g) to avoid losing ½ mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Adsorption = surface phenomenon; Absorption = bulk phenomenon.** Combined → **sorption**.
- **Two principal types** by bonding:
  - **Physisorption** → van der Waals, **4–40 kJ/mol**, **multilayer**, **reversible**, **$E_a \approx 0$**.
  - **Chemisorption** → covalent/ionic, **40–400 kJ/mol**, **monolayer**, **often irreversible**, **$E_a = 5$–$40$ kJ/mol**.
- **Physisorption always precedes chemisorption** (precursor-state mechanism).
- **Three classification axes:** (i) nature of bonding, (ii) number of layers, (iii) sign of surface excess.
- **Driving force** = reduction in surface Gibbs energy; **exothermic**; entropy decreases.
- **Freundlich isotherm** $q^* = k_F (P^*)^{1/n}$ → empirical; for heterogeneous surfaces.
- **Langmuir isotherm** $q^* = \dfrac{q_m K_L P^*}{1 + K_L P^*}$ → monolayer, uniform surface, dynamic equilibrium.
- **BET isotherm** → multilayer adsorption, used for surface-area measurement.
- **Isosteric heat of adsorption** is obtained from the slope of $\ln P^*$ vs $1/T$ at constant $q^*$ using the **Clausius–Clapeyron form** $\ln P^* = -\Delta H_s / (R T) + C$.
- **Henry's law limit** of Langmuir at very low $P^*$: $q^* \approx q_m K_L P^*$.
- **Industrial adsorbents:** activated carbon, silica gel, zeolites, activated alumina.
- **Industrial applications:** drying, decolorisation, VOC recovery, gas separation (PSA/TSA), catalysis.
- **Memorise** the numeric ranges of heat of adsorption — *the single most-tested fact* in this module.
- **Memorise** the five assumptions of the Langmuir derivation in correct order.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Adsorbents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of Adsorbents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **adsorbent** is a solid, microporous or mesoporous material possessing a high internal surface area onto which molecules of a fluid (gas or liquid) can selectively adhere via surface forces, forming a film at the solid–fluid interface. This phenomenon is termed **adsorption**, and the substance that gets adsorbed onto the surface is the **adsorbate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCHT403 – Module 3):**
> "Adsorbents are highly porous solids characterized by their specific surface area, pore structure, pore volume, particle size distribution, bulk density, and surface chemistry. The performance of any adsorption separation is governed by these intrinsic physical and chemical properties of the chosen solid."

### 1.2 Classification by Pore Size (IUPAC)

| Pore Type | Pore Width (d) | Typical Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Micropores** | d < 2 nm | Activated Carbon, Molecular Sieves (Zeolite 3A, 4A, 5A) |
| **Mesopores** | 2 nm ≤ d ≤ 50 nm | Silica Gel, Alumina, Activated Alumina |
| **Macropores** | d > 50 nm | Calcined Bauxite, Porous Ceramics |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sponge made of carbon** — now shrink that sponge so that it fits on your fingertip, yet contains kilometers of internal tunnels and microscopic walls. That is essentially an adsorbent!

- Each wall of the tunnel can "grab" passing molecules.
- The **more walls** (higher surface area), the **more molecules** it can grab.
- A **cricket ball** (large molecule) cannot enter a **mouse hole** (micropore), but a **tennis ball** (medium molecule) can enter a **dog door** (mesopore). This is the principle of **molecular sieving**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:**
> Industrial adsorbents like **Activated Carbon**, **Silica Gel**, and **Zeolites** can have surface areas exceeding **1000 m²/g** — meaning a single gram has the surface area of nearly **half a football field**! This is why they are the workhorses of drying, purification, and separation industries.

### 1.4 Common Industrial Adsorbents at a Glance

| Adsorbent | Typical Surface Area (m²/g) | Primary Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Activated Carbon** | 500 – 1500 | Decolorization, water treatment, gas masks |
| **Silica Gel** | 600 – 800 | Drying of gases and solvents |
| **Activated Alumina** | 200 – 400 | Drying of gases, defluoridation of water |
| **Zeolite 5A** | 500 – 800 | Separation of n-paraffins from iso-paraffins |
| **Molecular Sieve 13X** | 700 – 1000 | CO₂ and H₂S removal from natural gas |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The performance of an adsorbent in an industrial column is dictated by **six fundamental properties**. Let us break down each one with the operational logic behind it.

### 2.1 Property 1 – Specific Surface Area (BET Surface Area)

- **Definition:** Total internal + external surface area per unit mass of the solid, measured in **m²/g**.
- **Why it matters:** More surface area = more active sites = higher adsorption capacity.
- **How it is measured:** Determined experimentally using the **BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) equation** from N₂ adsorption at **77 K**.

### 2.2 Property 2 – Pore Structure (Size, Volume & Distribution)

- **Pore Volume (V_p):** Total volume of pores per unit mass, expressed in **cm³/g**.
- **Pore Size Distribution:** The fraction of pores falling within micropore, mesopore, and macropore ranges.
- **Why it matters:** Determines which molecules (based on kinetic diameter) can physically access the internal surface. Critical in molecular sieving applications.

### 2.3 Property 3 – Particle Size, Shape, and Size Distribution

- **Particle Size:** Diameter (or equivalent diameter) of the adsorbent pellet/bead, typically in **mm** (0.5 – 5 mm).
- **Shape:** Spherical, cylindrical (extrudates), granules, or pellets.
- **Why it matters:** Smaller particles → higher external surface area and lower diffusion resistance, but cause high pressure drop in packed beds. Trade-off dictates industrial selection.

### 2.4 Property 4 – Density (Three Types)

- **True Density (ρ_t):** Mass per unit volume of the solid material itself (excluding pores). Measured by **helium pycnometry**.
- **Particle Density (ρ_p):** Mass per unit volume of a single particle (including its internal pores).
- **Bulk Density (ρ_b):** Mass per unit volume of the packed bed (including inter-particle voids).

The relationship: **ρ_b < ρ_p < ρ_t**

### 2.5 Property 5 – Hardness / Attrition Resistance

- **Definition:** Mechanical strength of the adsorbent pellet, measured as the percentage of fines generated after a standard abrasion test.
- **Why it matters:** A weak adsorbent will powderize during cyclic operation, causing channeling, high pressure drop, and loss of active material. Required for fixed bed and fluidized bed operations.

### 2.6 Property 6 – Regenerability and Thermal Stability

- **Definition:** Ability to desorb the adsorbed species (usually by heating or pressure swing) and restore the original capacity over many cycles.
- **Why it matters:** Adsorbents are expensive. Economic operation demands that the adsorbent be reused for **hundreds to thousands** of cycles without significant capacity loss.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the high-yield equations you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| Property | Formula / Relationship | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BET Surface Area** | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_o - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{(C-1)}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_o}$ | m²/g | $V_m$ = monolayer volume, $C$ = BET constant |
| **BET Cross-sectional Area of N₂** | $A_m = 16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ | m²/molecule | Standard value at 77 K |
| **Pore Volume from Adsorption** | $V_p = \dfrac{W_{sat}}{\rho_{liq}}$ | cm³/g | $W_{sat}$ = mass adsorbed at saturation |
| **Mean Pore Radius** | $\bar{r} = \dfrac{2 V_p}{S_{BET}}$ | nm (or Å) | Assumes cylindrical pores |
| **Void Fraction of Packed Bed** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_p}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 0.35 – 0.5 |
| **Bed Porosity from Densities** | $\varepsilon_p = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_t}$ | dimensionless | Internal particle porosity |
| **Total Porosity** | $\varepsilon_T = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | dimensionless | Sum of bed + particle porosity |
| **Particle Porosity (alternative)** | $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{V_p}{V_p + 1/\rho_t}$ | dimensionless | $V_p$ in cm³/g |
| **Knudsen Diffusivity** | $D_K = 97 \cdot r_p \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{M}}$ | m²/s | $r_p$ in meters, T in K, M in kg/kmol |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** for medical oxygen generation, **Zeolite 5A** is selected because its pore opening of **0.5 nm** excludes nitrogen (kinetic diameter 0.364 nm is admitted; O₂ 0.346 nm adsorbs faster, but more importantly, N₂ fits while larger species are excluded in some designs — actual mechanism is equilibrium-based).
- In **natural gas dehydration**, **Silica Gel** and **Molecular Sieve 3A** are selected based on pore size: 3A (3 Å pore) adsorbs water but excludes methane (4 Å), preventing hydrocarbon loss.
- In **air separation**, **activated carbon** with very high surface area is the only economic choice for front-end purification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the BET Equation (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller)

The BET equation extends the **Langmuir theory** (which assumes a single molecular layer) to **multilayer physical adsorption**.

**Step 1 – Starting Assumption (Langmuir Basis):**
Langmuir assumed:
- The solid surface has a fixed number of adsorption sites.
- Each site can hold only one molecule (monolayer).
- No interaction between adsorbed molecules.

The Langmuir isotherm:
$$\theta = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}$$

where $\theta$ is the fraction of surface covered and $K$ is an equilibrium constant.

**Step 2 – Extension to Multilayers:**
BET proposed that:
- The first layer forms with heat of adsorption $Q_1$ (high energy, chemisorption-like).
- The second and subsequent layers form with heat of condensation $Q_L$ (low energy, multilayer).
- Each layer above the first behaves like condensation of liquid adsorbate.

**Step 3 – Summation of Infinite Series:**
The total volume adsorbed $V$ at relative pressure $P/P_o$ is derived by summing the volumes in all layers. The grand derivation yields:

$$\dfrac{P}{V(P_o - P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{(C - 1)}{V_m C} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_o}$$

**Step 4 – Identifying the Linear Form:**
This is a straight-line equation of the form:

$$Y = mX + c$$

where:

- $Y = \dfrac{P}{V(P_o - P)}$
- $X = \dfrac{P}{P_o}$
- Slope $m = \dfrac{C - 1}{V_m C}$
- Intercept $c = \dfrac{1}{V_m C}$

**Step 5 – Solving for Monolayer Volume:**

$$V_m = \dfrac{1}{m + c}$$

**Step 6 – Converting Monolayer Volume to Surface Area:**

$$S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot A_m}{M_{N_2} \cdot 22414}$$

where:
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules/mol
- $A_m$ = cross-sectional area of N₂ = $16.2 \times 10^{-20}$ m²/molecule
- $M_{N_2}$ = molar mass of N₂ = 28 g/mol
- $22414$ = molar volume (cm³/mol) at STP

**Simplified numerical form (when V_m is in cm³/g at STP):**

$$S_{BET} = 4.35 \cdot V_m \quad \text{[m}^2\text{/g]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant 4.35 comes from: $\dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 16.2 \times 10^{-20}}{22400} = 4.35$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Property Affects Adsorption Performance

| Property | Effect on Adsorption Capacity | Effect on Pressure Drop | Effect on Regeneration | Trade-off Decision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **↑ Surface Area** | ↑↑ Strongly Increases | Negligible | Slightly Harder | Choose max area within budget |
| **↑ Micropore Volume** | ↑↑ Increases (small molecules) | Negligible | May trap molecules | Critical for size-selective separation |
| **↓ Particle Size** | ↑ Increases (shorter diffusion path) | ↑↑ Strongly Increases | Faster (shorter path) | Optimized at 1–3 mm |
| **↑ Bulk Density** | ↑ More adsorbent per bed | ↓ Decreases | — | Maximize for fixed-volume beds |
| **↑ Hardness** | No effect | Prevents fines | Prevents loss | Mandatory for fluidized beds |
| **↑ Pore Radius** | ↓ May decrease surface area | Negligible | Easier (less capillary) | Use for viscous/large molecules |

### 3.3 Worked Example — BET Surface Area Calculation

**Problem:**
A sample of silica gel adsorbs the following volumes of N₂ at 77 K:

| $P/P_o$ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V$ (cm³/g STP) | 45 | 60 | 80 | 95 |

Determine the BET monolayer volume $V_m$ and the specific surface area $S_{BET}$.

**Step 1 – Compute the BET variables:**

For each $P/P_o$, compute $\dfrac{P}{P_o}$ and $\dfrac{P/P_o}{V(1 - P/P_o)}$.

| $P/P_o$ | $V$ | $1 - P/P_o$ | $\dfrac{P/P_o}{V(1 - P/P_o)}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0.05 | 45 | 0.95 | $\dfrac{0.05}{45 \times 0.95} = 0.001169$ |
| 0.10 | 60 | 0.90 | $\dfrac{0.10}{60 \times 0.90} = 0.001852$ |
| 0.20 | 80 | 0.80 | $\dfrac{0.20}{80 \times 0.80} = 0.003125$ |
| 0.30 | 95 | 0.70 | $\dfrac{0.30}{95 \times 0.70} = 0.004511$ |

**Step 2 – Linear Regression (Y vs X where X = P/P_o):**

Using the form $Y = mX + c$, applying least-squares:

- Sum of X: $\Sigma X = 0.65$
- Sum of Y: $\Sigma Y = 0.010657$
- Mean X̄ = 0.1625, Mean Ȳ = 0.002664
- $\Sigma(X - \bar{X})(Y - \bar{Y}) = 0.001011$
- $\Sigma(X - \bar{X})^2 = 0.03188$

$$m = \dfrac{0.001011}{0.03188} = 0.03172$$

$$c = \bar{Y} - m\bar{X} = 0.002664 - 0.03172 \times 0.1625 = 0.002664 - 0.005155 = -0.002491$$

(Use absolute value for computation of $V_m$.)

**Step 3 – Compute $V_m$:**

$$V_m = \dfrac{1}{m + c} = \dfrac{1}{0.03172 + 0.002491} = \dfrac{1}{0.034211} = 29.23 \quad \text{cm}^3\text{/g STP}$$

**Step 4 – Compute $S_{BET}$:**

$$S_{BET} = 4.35 \times V_m = 4.35 \times 29.23 = 127.1 \quad \text{m}^2\text{/g}$$

**Conclusion:** The silica gel has a BET specific surface area of approximately **127 m²/g**, which is on the lower end — typical of macroporous silica rather than highly activated silica gel.

### 3.4 Derivation of Mean Pore Radius

For a cylindrical pore model, the total pore surface area $S_p$ and pore volume $V_p$ are related to the mean pore radius $\bar{r}$ by:

$$S_p = \dfrac{2 V_p}{\bar{r}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\bar{r} = \dfrac{2 V_p}{S_p} = \dfrac{2 V_p}{S_{BET}}$$

This is the basis of the **Kelvin equation** for pore size analysis and is directly used to characterize adsorbents from N₂ desorption isotherms.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Adsorbent Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Adsorbent Properties] --> B[Physical Properties]
    A --> C[Chemical Properties]
    A --> D[Mechanical Properties]
    A --> E[Operational Properties]

    B --> B1[Specific Surface Area]
    B --> B2[Pore Size Distribution]
    B --> B3[Pore Volume]
    B --> B4[Particle Size and Shape]
    B --> B5[True Particle and Bulk Density]

    C --> C1[Surface Acidity Basicity]
    C --> C2[Active Site Density]
    C --> C3[Hydrophobicity Hydrophilicity]
    C --> C4[Surface Functional Groups]

    D --> D1[Hardness]
    D --> D2[Attrition Resistance]
    D --> D3[Crush Strength]

    E --> E1[Adsorption Capacity]
    E --> E2[Selectivity]
    E --> E3[Regenerability]
    E --> E4[Thermal Stability]
    E --> E5[Cyclic Life]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Selection Logic of Industrial Adsorbents

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Start Application Selection] --> Q1{What is the separation goal?}

    Q1 -->|Drying of gas or liquid| R1[Use Silica Gel or Molecular Sieve 3A]
    Q1 -->|Removal of organics from water| R2[Use Activated Carbon]
    Q1 -->|Oxygen Nitrogen separation| R3[Use Zeolite 5A]
    Q1 -->|Hydrogen Sulfide removal| R4[Use Zeolite 13X or Activated Carbon]
    Q1 -->|Sweetening of natural gas| R5[Use Molecular Sieve 4A]

    R1 --> C1[Criteria: Pore size 3 Angstrom high surface area]
    R2 --> C2[Criteria: High surface area hydrophobic surface]
    R3 --> C3[Criteria: 5 Angstrom pore precise molecular discrimination]
    R4 --> C4[Criteria: Polar surface high capacity for polar molecules]
    R5 --> C5[Criteria: Selective for water and H2S exclusion of hydrocarbons]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three-Density Concept of a Packed Adsorbent Bed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Bed[Industrial Packed Adsorption Bed]
        direction TB
        S1[Solid Material Only] -->|rho_t True Density| P1[Skeletal Volume of Solid]
        P1 -->|rho_p Particle Density| P2[Single Particle Volume including Pores]
        P2 -->|rho_b Bulk Density| P3[Total Bed Volume including Interparticle Voids]
    end

    Bed --> Note[Key Relation: rho_b less than rho_p less than rho_t]
    Bed --> App[Engineering Use: Determines bed capacity and breakthrough time]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — IUPAC Classification of Adsorption Isotherms (for Adsorbent Characterization)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I[Isotherm Type Determines Adsorbent Behavior] --> T1[Type I Microporous Langmuir]
    I --> T2[Type II Nonporous or Macroporous Multilayer]
    I --> T3[Type III Weak Adsorbate Adsorbent Interaction]
    I --> T4[Type IV Mesoporous with Capillary Condensation]
    I --> T5[Type V Weak Interaction with Mesopores]
    I --> T6[Type VI Stepwise Multilayer on Uniform Surface]

    T1 --> Ex1[Example Activated Carbon Molecular Sieves]
    T2 --> Ex2[Example Nonporous Silica]
    T3 --> Ex3[Example Water on Graphite]
    T4 --> Ex4[Example Mesoporous Silica Alumina]
    T5 --> Ex5[Example Water on Activated Carbon]
```
