- **Dalton's Law** of partial pressures: $P_i = y_i P$ (vapor side).
- **Antoine Equation** is the standard way to obtain $P_i^{sat}$ — never compute it from scratch in exams.
- **Cross-check** all VLE results by verifying $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum y_i = 1$.
- The **equilibrium curve** is the *upper boundary* of the McCabe–Thiele stepping region.
- When $\alpha \to 1$ (azeotrope), no amount of stages can produce a pure product by simple distillation.
- A $\alpha > 1$ means component $i$ is more volatile and concentrates in the vapor; for distillation design, choose $i$ as the light key.
- **Murphree efficiency** $E_{MV} = (y_{n} - y_{n+1}) / (y^{*} - y_{n+1})$ relates *real* stages to *theoretical* stages using equilibrium.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bound and Unbound Moisture Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bound and Unbound Moisture Content

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of drying operations and psychrometric analysis, **moisture content** in a solid is classified into several distinct thermodynamic categories based on the energy required for its removal and the physical state in which it exists within the material matrix.

**Bound Moisture (Bound Water):**
Bound moisture is the moisture held within a solid material that exerts a **vapor pressure less than that of pure water at the same temperature**. This occurs because the water is physically retained within the cell walls, capillaries, or is chemically/physically bound to the solid surface (adsorption). Bound moisture cannot be removed easily and requires energy input exceeding the normal latent heat of vaporization of water at that temperature.

**Unbound Moisture (Free Moisture / Free Water):**
Unbound moisture is the moisture held within a solid that exerts a **vapor pressure equal to that of pure water at the same temperature**. This represents the moisture in excess of the equilibrium moisture content (EMC) for the prevailing drying air conditions. Unbound moisture behaves like pure liquid water and can be removed by simple evaporation under given air conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bound and unbound moisture are critical parameters in drying calculations because:
> 1. Only **unbound (free) moisture** can be removed in a given drying operation.
> 2. **Bound moisture** requires specific energy input and cannot be removed by ordinary air-drying.
> 3. The classification governs dryer design, residence time, and energy calculations.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **kitchen sponge soaked with water**:

- **Unbound Moisture (Free Water)**: The water that drips out of the sponge when you lift it up and squeeze gently. This water is held loosely in the large pores by surface tension and behaves like free liquid water. You can remove it by simple mechanical means or by exposing it to dry air.

- **Bound Moisture (Bound Water)**: The water that remains trapped in the tiny micropores and chemically adheres to the cellulose fibers of the sponge, even after you squeeze hard. This water is "stuck" to the solid structure. To remove it, you must either apply heat beyond the normal boiling energy or use very dry air (low humidity).

**Analogy to a Wet Mobile Phone:**
If you drop your phone in water and then dry it in sunlight, the visible water droplets evaporate easily (unbound). But the moisture trapped inside the screen and internal circuits (bound) requires silica gel packets or a vacuum dryer for complete removal.

### 1.3 Standard Terminology & Units

| Term | Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Moisture Content (Dry Basis) | $X$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| Moisture Content (Wet Basis) | $X_w$ | kg water / kg wet solid |
| Equilibrium Moisture Content | $X^*$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| Critical Moisture Content | $X_c$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| Relative Humidity | $RH$ or $\phi$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversions:** 
> $$X = \frac{X_w}{1 - X_w} \quad \text{and} \quad X_w = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$
> KTU examinations typically expect the **dry basis** representation for drying problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Framework of Moisture in Solids

The total moisture in a wet solid can be systematically classified into four mutually exclusive categories:

#### Step 1: Total Moisture Content
This is the **complete amount of water** present in the wet solid, including both removable and non-removable portions. It is the starting point of any drying calculation.

$$X_t = X_b + X_u$$

Where:
- $X_t$ = Total moisture content (dry basis)
- $X_b$ = Bound moisture content
- $X_u$ = Unbound moisture content

#### Step 2: Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)
This is the moisture content of a solid when it is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the surrounding air at given temperature and humidity. It is a **material property** that depends on:
- Type of solid (hygroscopic vs non-hygroscopic)
- Relative humidity of the surrounding air
- Temperature of the system

At equilibrium, the solid neither gains nor loses moisture to the air.

#### Step 3: Bound vs Unbound Moisture (Critical Distinction)

The classification of moisture as bound or unbound depends on its **vapor pressure behavior**:

- **Bound moisture** = portion of total moisture that exerts vapor pressure **LESS THAN** the vapor pressure of pure water at that temperature.
- **Unbound moisture** = portion of total moisture that exerts vapor pressure **EQUAL TO** the vapor pressure of pure water at that temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the distinction matters:** For **non-hygroscopic** materials (sand, ceramics), all moisture behaves as unbound. For **hygroscopic** materials (wood, textiles, food, paper), some moisture is always bound and cannot be removed under given air conditions.

#### Step 4: Free vs Equilibrium Moisture

For a specific drying operation under given air conditions:

$$\text{Total Moisture} = \text{Free Moisture} + \text{Equilibrium Moisture}$$

$$X_t = X_f + X^*$$

Where:
- $X_f$ = Free moisture = moisture removable under given drying conditions
- $X^*$ = Equilibrium moisture = moisture that will remain in the solid even after infinite drying time

#### Step 5: The Master Relationship

The **single most important relationship** in drying operations:

$$\text{Total Moisture} = \text{Bound Moisture} + \text{Unbound Moisture}$$
$$\text{Total Moisture} = \text{Free Moisture} + \text{Equilibrium Moisture}$$

Therefore:
- **Unbound Moisture = Free Moisture** (under specific drying air conditions)
- **Bound Moisture** includes **Equilibrium Moisture** plus any bound moisture that requires conditions beyond the prevailing air to remove

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relationship | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dry basis moisture content | $X = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$ | Standard form for all drying calcs |
| Wet basis conversion | $X = \dfrac{X_w}{1 - X_w}$ | Converts wet to dry basis |
| Total moisture breakdown | $X_t = X_b + X_u$ | Classifies by vapor pressure |
| Free moisture definition | $X_f = X_t - X^*$ | Moisture removable in operation |
| Equilibrium condition | $X \to X^* \text{ as } t \to \infty$ | Long-time drying limit |
| Critical moisture | $X_c$ | Onset of falling-rate period |
| Vapor pressure ratio | $\dfrac{p_w}{p_w^{sat}} < 1 \text{ for bound}$ | Distinguishes bound from unbound |
| Hygroscopic material criterion | $X^* > 0$ at finite RH | Indicates hygroscopic nature |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bound/unbound moisture classification is essential in:

1. **Pharmaceutical Drying**: Removal of bound water from drug crystals without degrading heat-sensitive active ingredients.
2. **Food Processing**: Predicting the shelf life of dried foods (residual bound moisture determines microbial growth).
3. **Ceramics & Refractories**: Ensuring complete moisture removal before high-temperature firing to prevent cracking.
4. **Paper & Textile Industry**: Controlling final moisture content for product quality (paper requires 4-8% moisture for proper printing).
5. **Agricultural Drying**: Grain drying calculations where bound moisture affects storage stability.

### 2.4 Sorption Isotherm (Conceptual Foundation)

A **sorption isotherm** is a plot of equilibrium moisture content $X^*$ versus relative humidity $\phi$ at constant temperature. It is the graphical fingerprint of how a material holds moisture.

- For **non-hygroscopic materials**: $X^* \approx 0$ for all $\phi < 1$
- For **hygroscopic materials**: $X^*$ increases as $\phi$ increases, following characteristic shapes (Type I, II, III, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The intersection of the sorption isotherm with a vertical line at the operating relative humidity of the drying air gives the **equilibrium moisture content** for that operation. The bound moisture is read from the curve at $\phi = 1$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Wet and Dry Basis Moisture Content

**Starting Point:**
Let $m_{total}$ be the total mass of the wet solid, and $m_w$ be the mass of water present.

**Step 1: Definition of Wet Basis**
$$X_w = \frac{m_w}{m_{total}}$$

This is a direct statement of definition — wet basis moisture is the ratio of water mass to total mass.

**Step 2: Express Total Mass in Terms of Components**
$$m_{total} = m_w + m_s$$

Where $m_s$ is the mass of bone-dry solid (moisture-free solid).

**Step 3: Substitute into Definition**
$$X_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}$$

**Step 4: Divide Numerator and Denominator by $m_s$**
$$X_w = \frac{m_w / m_s}{(m_w / m_s) + 1}$$

**Step 5: Recognize the Dry Basis Definition**
$$X = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$

**Step 6: Final Substitution**
$$X_w = \frac{X}{X + 1}$$

**Step 7: Solve for X (Inversion)**
Multiply both sides by $(X + 1)$:
$$X_w(X + 1) = X$$
$$X_w \cdot X + X_w = X$$
$$X_w = X - X_w \cdot X$$
$$X_w = X(1 - X_w)$$
$$X = \frac{X_w}{1 - X_w}$$

This completes the bidirectional conversion formula used universally in KTU drying problems.

### 3.2 Analytical Problem: Determining Bound, Unbound, Free, and Equilibrium Moisture

**Problem Statement (Model KTU-style):**
A wet solid is dried in an air stream at temperature $T = 60°C$ and relative humidity $\phi = 0.20$. The initial moisture content (dry basis) is $X_1 = 0.45$ kg water/kg dry solid. The sorption isotherm data for the solid is given as:

| $\phi$ (RH) | $X^*$ (kg/kg dry) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0.10 | 0.005 |
| 0.20 | 0.013 |
| 0.40 | 0.027 |
| 0.60 | 0.040 |
| 0.80 | 0.060 |
| 1.00 | 0.090 |

Determine: (a) Bound moisture content, (b) Unbound moisture content, (c) Free moisture content, (d) Moisture removed at equilibrium.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Bound Moisture**
Bound moisture is the moisture that exerts a vapor pressure less than pure water. From the sorption isotherm, at $\phi = 1.0$ (i.e., pure water vapor saturation condition), $X^* = 0.090$ kg/kg.

$$X_b = 0.090 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** The bound moisture is read from the isotherm at the *highest* possible relative humidity ($\phi = 1$). This represents the maximum amount of water that the solid can retain in a bound state.

**Step 2: Identify the Unbound Moisture**
By definition, unbound moisture is the total moisture minus bound moisture:

$$X_u = X_1 - X_b = 0.45 - 0.090 = 0.360 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Equilibrium Moisture Content**
For the operating air conditions, $\phi = 0.20$. Reading from the isotherm:

$$X^* = 0.013 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 4: Identify the Free Moisture Content**
Free moisture is the moisture removable under the given air conditions:

$$X_f = X_1 - X^* = 0.45 - 0.013 = 0.437 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 5: Verify the Relationships**

$$\text{Total Moisture} = 0.450 \text{ kg/kg}$$
$$\text{Bound} + \text{Unbound} = 0.090 + 0.360 = 0.450 \text{ kg/kg} \checkmark$$
$$\text{Free} + \text{Equilibrium} = 0.437 + 0.013 = 0.450 \text{ kg/kg} \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Final Drying Outcome**
At infinite drying time, the solid will retain $X^* = 0.013$ kg/kg (the equilibrium moisture). The moisture that can be removed is $X_f = 0.437$ kg/kg.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Complete Moisture Classification

| Category | Definition | Value (kg/kg) | Can it be removed? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total Moisture ($X_t$) | All water in the solid | **0.450** | N/A |
| Bound Moisture ($X_b$) | Holds water tighter than pure water | **0.090** | No, under given conditions |
| Unbound Moisture ($X_u$) | Behaves like free water | **0.360** | Yes, in given operation |
| Free Moisture ($X_f$) | Removable under given air | **0.437** | Yes, by drying |
| Equilibrium Moisture ($X^*$) | Retained at infinite time | **0.013** | No, thermodynamically bound |
| Bound & Equilibrium Overlap | Portion that is both bound and EMC | **0.013** | No |

### 3.4 Mathematical Relation: Vapor Pressure and Moisture Type

The fundamental thermodynamic relationship is given by the **Kelvin equation** and sorption theory:

$$\ln\left(\frac{p_w}{p_w^{sat}}\right) = -\frac{2 \sigma V_m}{rRT} \cdot \cos\theta$$

Where:
- $p_w$ = vapor pressure over the moisture in the solid
- $p_w^{sat}$ = saturation vapor pressure of pure water at temperature $T$
- $\sigma$ = surface tension of water
- $V_m$ = molar volume of water
- $r$ = radius of the capillary/pore
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $R$ = universal gas constant

**For Unbound Moisture:**
$$\frac{p_w}{p_w^{sat}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r \to \infty \text{ (large pores or surface water)}$$

**For Bound Moisture:**
$$\frac{p_w}{p_w^{sat}} < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r \text{ is small (microcapillaries)}$$

This is the *microscopic origin* of why bound moisture is harder to remove — it sits in pores so small that the curvature of the water meniscus suppresses its vapor pressure, requiring more energy for evaporation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Moisture Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Total Moisture in Solid X_t"]:::main
    nodeB["Bound Moisture X_b"]:::bound
    nodeC["Unbound Moisture X_u"]:::unbound
    nodeD["Equilibrium Moisture X*"]:::eq
    nodeE["Free Moisture X_f"]:::free
    nodeF["Moisture Removable in Operation"]:::rem
    nodeG["Moisture Retained at Infinity"]:::ret
    nodeH["Read from Isotherm at phi = 1"]:::note
    nodeI["Read from Isotherm at Operating phi"]:::note
    nodeJ["Cannot be removed by air drying"]:::warn
    nodeK["Behaves like pure liquid water"]:::ok

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeB -.-> nodeH
    nodeD -.-> nodeI
    nodeB -.-> nodeJ
    nodeC -.-> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG

    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2540,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef bound fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7b1a14,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef unbound fill:#27ae60,stroke:#145a32,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef eq fill:#f39c12,stroke:#7a5212,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef free fill:#2980b9,stroke:#154360,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rem fill:#16a085,stroke:#0a4a3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ret fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#3d1a4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef note fill:#ecf0f1,stroke:#7f8c8d,color:#2c3e50,stroke-width:1px
    classDef warn fill:#e67e22,stroke:#7a3d0a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ok fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#1a6f3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Drying Timeline and Moisture Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Initial Wet Solid X_1 = 0.45"]:::start
    nodeB["Constant Rate Period X > X_c"]:::crp
    nodeC["Critical Point X = X_c"]:::crit
    nodeD["Falling Rate Period 1 X_c > X > X*"]:::frp1
    nodeE["Falling Rate Period 2 X approaches X*"]:::frp2
    nodeF["Equilibrium State X = X* = 0.013"]:::final
    nodeG["Air Stream at T, phi"]:::air
    nodeH["Water Vapor Removed"]:::vapor
    nodeI["Bound Moisture Unremovable"]:::bound

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeG -.supplies heat.-> nodeB
    nodeB -.evaporates.-> nodeH
    nodeD -.releases bound.-> nodeH
    nodeF -.retains.-> nodeI

    classDef start fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#0a4a3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef crp fill:#3498db,stroke:#154360,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef crit fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#7b1a14,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef frp1 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#7a5212,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef frp2 fill:#e67e22,stroke:#7a3d0a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef final fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef air fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#3d1a4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vapor fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0a2540,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bound fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7b1a14,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Architecture: Bound vs Unbound Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MacroPores["MACRO PORES (Large Pores)"]
        nodeA1["Unbound Water Film"]:::unbound
        nodeA2["Surface Water"]:::unbound
    end

    subgraph MesoPores["MESO PORES (Capillaries)"]
        nodeB1["Capillary Water with Meniscus"]:::meso
        nodeB2["Partially Bound Water"]:::meso
    end

    subgraph MicroPores["MICRO PORES (Cell Walls, Fibers)"]
        nodeC1["Adsorbed Monolayer"]:::bound
        nodeC2["Bound Water in Cell"]:::bound
        nodeC3["Chemically Bound Water"]:::bound
    end

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2
    nodeC1 --> nodeC3

    classDef unbound fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#1a6f3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef meso fill:#f1c40f,stroke:#7a6a0a,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bound fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#7b1a14,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sorption Isotherm Plot (Schematic Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["phi = 0.0"]:::axis
    nodeB["phi = 0.2"]:::axis
    nodeC["phi = 0.4"]:::axis
    nodeD["phi = 0.6"]:::axis
    nodeE["phi = 0.8"]:::axis
    nodeF["phi = 1.0"]:::axis
    nodeG["X* = 0"]:::pt
    nodeH["X* = 0.013"]:::pt
    nodeI["X* = 0.027"]:::pt
    nodeJ["X* = 0.040"]:::pt
    nodeK["X* = 0.060"]:::pt
    nodeL["X* = 0.090 - BOUND MOISTURE"]:::bound

    nodeA --- nodeG
    nodeB --- nodeH
    nodeC --- nodeI
    nodeD --- nodeJ
    nodeE --- nodeK
    nodeF --- nodeL

    classDef axis fill:#34495e,stroke:#1a242f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pt fill:#3498db,stroke:#154360,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bound fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7b1a14,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the following terms: (a) Bound moisture, (b) Unbound moisture, (c) Equilibrium moisture content. How are they interrelated in a drying operation?**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Bound Moisture:** Bound moisture is the moisture in a solid that exerts a vapor pressure **less than** that of pure water at the same temperature. It is held tightly within the material's micro-capillaries or adsorbed on internal surfaces. Bound moisture cannot be removed under ordinary drying air conditions and requires additional energy beyond the normal latent heat of vaporization.

**(b) Unbound Moisture:** Unbound moisture is the moisture in a solid that exerts a vapor pressure **equal to** that of pure water at the same temperature. It is held loosely in the large pores or on the surface and behaves as free liquid water. Unbound moisture can be removed by simple evaporation under given drying conditions.

**(c) Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$):** The moisture content of a solid when it is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the surrounding air at a given temperature and relative humidity. It represents the lowest moisture content achievable under those specific air conditions.

**Interrelation:** 
$$X_{total} = X_{bound} + X_{unbound} = X_{free} + X^*$$
The unbound moisture equals the free moisture removable under specific air conditions. The bound moisture includes the equilibrium moisture as a subset.

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**A wet solid has moisture content of 40% (wet basis). Calculate the moisture content on dry basis and determine which of the following materials is more likely to have higher bound moisture: (i) sand, (ii) wood. Justify.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Convert wet basis to dry basis using $X = X_w / (1 - X_w)$:**
$$X = \frac{0.40}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.667 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2: Material with higher bound moisture:**
**Wood (ii)** will have higher bound moisture than sand (i).

**Justification:** Wood is a **hygroscopic material** containing cellulose fibers with micro-capillaries and hydroxyl groups that adsorb water molecules. It retains moisture even at low relative humidity. Sand is **non-hygroscopic** — it has no internal structure to retain water, and all moisture behaves as unbound water that evaporates completely when exposed to dry air.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) A granular hygroscopic material is dried from an initial moisture content of 0.45 kg water/kg dry solid to a final moisture content of 0.08 kg water/kg dry solid. The sorption isotherm data at the operating air temperature is given below:**

| Relative Humidity $\phi$ | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.50 | 0.70 | 1.00 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equilibrium Moisture $X^*$ (kg/kg) | 0.012 | 0.020 | 0.028 | 0.045 | 0.065 | 0.105 |

**The drying air is supplied at $\phi = 0.30$. Determine:**
1. The bound moisture content
2. The unbound moisture content initially and finally
3. The free moisture content initially and finally
4. The total moisture removed per kg of dry solid
5. Comment on the nature of the material

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Bound Moisture Content**
Bound moisture is read from the isotherm at $\phi = 1.0$ (saturation):
$$X_b = 0.105 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2: Initial Unbound Moisture**
$$X_{u,1} = X_1 - X_b = 0.450 - 0.105 = 0.345 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 3: Final Unbound Moisture**
$$X_{u,2} = X_2 - X_b = 0.080 - 0.105 = -0.025 \text{ kg/kg}$$

Since the final moisture (0.080) is **less than** the bound moisture (0.105), the unbound moisture is **negative** — meaning all remaining moisture in the final product is bound. This makes physical sense: as drying proceeds, the free moisture is exhausted, and only bound moisture remains.
$$X_{u,2} = 0 \text{ kg/kg (practically no unbound moisture in final product)}$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium Moisture at Operating Condition**
For $\phi = 0.30$ from the isotherm:
$$X^* = 0.028 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 5: Initial Free Moisture**
$$X_{f,1} = X_1 - X^* = 0.450 - 0.028 = 0.422 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 6: Final Free Moisture**
$$X_{f,2} = X_2 - X^* = 0.080 - 0.028 = 0.052 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 7: Total Moisture Removed per kg Dry Solid**
$$\Delta X = X_1 - X_2 = 0.450 - 0.080 = 0.370 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 8: Comment on Material Nature**
The material is **hygroscopic** because:
- It has a non-zero equilibrium moisture content at finite relative humidity ($X^* = 0.028$ at $\phi = 0.30$)
- It retains bound moisture of 0.105 kg/kg at $\phi = 1.0$
- The final product (0.080 kg/kg) is below the bound moisture, indicating that all free moisture has been removed and only bound water remains in the final state.

---

**(b) Explain the significance of sorption isotherms in drying operations. With a neat sketch, describe the classification of moisture in a solid on the basis of (i) total moisture content, (ii) bound and unbound moisture, (iii) free and equilibrium moisture.**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of Sorption Isotherms:**

1. **Prediction of Final Moisture:** The sorption isotherm allows prediction of the **lowest moisture content** achievable in a given drying operation by reading $X^*$ at the operating $\phi$.

2. **Determination of Bound Moisture:** The bound moisture is read at $\phi = 1.0$ from the isotherm.

3. **Material Characterization:** The shape of the isotherm (Type I, II, etc.) indicates the **mechanism of moisture binding** (adsorption, capillary condensation, etc.) and helps classify the material.

4. **Energy Requirements:** Knowing how much moisture is free vs. bound allows estimation of the energy required for drying.

5. **Process Control:** Isotherms are used to set humidity controls in industrial dryers for consistent product quality.

**Sketch and Classification (Schematic Description):**

```
    X*  |
        |\           ___________ Bound + Free Region
        | \         /
        |  \       /  ← Sorption isotherm curve
        |   \     /
        |    \   /
        |     \_/  ____________ Equilibrium Region (Bound only)
        |     
        |________________________ φ (Relative Humidity)
        0   0.3   0.5   0.7   1.0
```

The classification follows the relationships:
- **Total moisture** ($X_t$) = Bound + Unbound = Free + Equilibrium
- At any operating $\phi$, the portion **above the isotherm curve** is **free (unbound) moisture**
- The portion **on or below the isotherm curve** is **equilibrium (bound) moisture**

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a) A batch of wet fabric is dried in a tray dryer. The initial moisture content is 1.20 kg water/kg dry fabric and the final moisture content is 0.15 kg water/kg dry fabric. The equilibrium moisture content at the operating air conditions is 0.08 kg/kg. The bound moisture content of the fabric is 0.25 kg/kg. Calculate: (i) the moisture removed per kg of dry fabric, (ii) the free moisture content initially and finally, (iii) the unbound moisture content initially and finally.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $X_1 = 1.20$ kg/kg (initial)
- $X_2 = 0.15$ kg/kg (final)
- $X^* = 0.08$ kg/kg (equilibrium)
- $X_b = 0.25$ kg/kg (bound)

**(i) Moisture Removed per kg of Dry Fabric:**
$$\Delta X = X_1 - X_2 = 1.20 - 0.15 = 1.05 \text{ kg water/kg dry fabric}$$

**(ii) Free Moisture Content:**

Initial:
$$X_{f,1} = X_1 - X^* = 1.20 - 0.08 = 1.12 \text{ kg/kg}$$

Final:
$$X_{f,2} = X_2 - X^* = 0.15 - 0.08 = 0.07 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**(iii) Unbound Moisture Content:**

Initial:
$$X_{u,1} = X_1 - X_b = 1.20 - 0.25 = 0.95 \text{ kg/kg}$$

Final:
$$X_{u,2} = X_2 - X_b = 0.15 - 0.25 = -0.10 \text{ kg/kg}$$

Since $X_2 < X_b$, the final unbound moisture is **zero** (all unbound moisture has been removed). The product retains 0.15 kg/kg, of which 0.08 kg/kg is equilibrium moisture and the remaining 0.07 kg/kg is bound moisture that exists between $X^*$ and $X_b$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Once drying removes all unbound moisture, the operation enters the **falling rate period** dominated by bound moisture removal, which is much slower.

---

**(b) Differentiate between hygroscopic and non-hygroscopic materials. Explain with examples how the bound moisture content differs in each case and discuss the practical implications for industrial drying.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hygroscopic vs Non-Hygroscopic Materials:**

| Property | Hygroscopic | Non-Hygroscopic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Materials that retain moisture even at low RH | Materials that release all moisture to dry air |
| Bound moisture | Non-zero at all RH | Zero (essentially) |
| Equilibrium moisture | Significant at finite RH | Approaches zero at RH < 1 |
| Examples | Wood, paper, textiles, food, leather | Sand, ceramics, glass, metals, salt crystals |
| Behavior | Hold water in capillaries/cells | Surface moisture only |
| Drying endpoint | Limited by EMC | Moisture can be reduced to ~0 |

**Bound Moisture Differences:**

- **Hygroscopic materials** have substantial bound moisture because their internal structure (cellulose fibers, protein matrices) provides numerous adsorption sites and micro-capillaries. For example, wood can have bound moisture up to 0.20-0.30 kg/kg.

- **Non-hygroscopic materials** have negligible bound moisture. Sand, for instance, has all its moisture as unbound surface water that evaporates completely in dry air.

**Practical Implications for Industrial Drying:**

1. **Energy Calculation:** For hygroscopic materials, dryer design must account for the energy required to remove bound water (often with hot, very dry air).

2. **Residence Time:** Hygroscopic materials require **longer drying times** in the falling rate period, increasing equipment size.

3. **Product Quality:** Residual bound moisture affects product quality — paper needs 4-8% moisture for printability; food needs specific moisture for shelf life.

4. **Air Conditions:** For hygroscopic materials, using **drier air** (lower $\phi$) reduces the equilibrium moisture content and allows lower final product moisture.

5. **Energy Efficiency:** Stopping drying at equilibrium saves energy since further removal of bound water is extremely energy-intensive and slow.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing bound/unbound with free/equilibrium:** These are TWO different classifications. Bound/unbound relates to vapor pressure behavior; free/equilibrium relates to the specific drying air conditions.
> 2. **Forgetting to use dry basis:** KTU problems expect dry basis moisture content. Always convert wet basis to dry basis first.
> 3. **Negative unbound moisture:** If $X < X_b$, the unbound moisture is **zero**, not negative. Always state this clearly.
> 4. **Reading sorption isotherm incorrectly:** Bound moisture is at $\phi = 1.0$, not at the operating $\phi$. Operating $\phi$ gives the equilibrium moisture.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** Always express moisture content in kg water/kg dry solid consistently.
> 6. **Missing the conceptual link:** Bound moisture in a hygroscopic material is a **material property**; it doesn't change with air conditions. Only free/equilibrium depends on air conditions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Bound Moisture** = moisture with vapor pressure **less than** pure water at same T → cannot be removed by ordinary drying
- **Unbound Moisture** = moisture with vapor pressure **equal to** pure water at same T → removable by drying
- **Equilibrium Moisture ($X^*$)** = moisture retained at infinite time under given air conditions
- **Free Moisture** = moisture removable under given drying air conditions = $X_t - X^*$
- **Master Equation:** $X_t = X_b + X_u = X_f + X^*$
- **Bound moisture is a material property** (read at $\phi = 1$ from isotherm)
- **Equilibrium moisture is an operating condition property** (read at operating $\phi$)
- **Hygroscopic materials** have significant bound moisture; **non-hygroscopic** have negligible bound moisture
- **Conversion formulas:** $X = X_w / (1 - X_w)$ and $X_w = X / (1 + X)$
- **Critical moisture $X_c$** marks the start of the falling rate period (different from bound moisture — do not confuse)
- **Sorption isotherm** is the curve of $X^*$ vs $\phi$ at constant T
- At $\phi = 1.0$ on isotherm → $X^* = X_b$ (bound moisture equals EMC at saturation)
- **Practical drying limit:** cannot reduce moisture below $X^*$ for given air conditions
- **Dry basis** is the standard for all KTU drying calculations
- **Vapor pressure ratio** $p_w/p_w^{sat} = 1$ → unbound; $< 1$ → bound
- **Examples — Hygroscopic:** wood, paper, textile, food, leather, soap
- **Examples — Non-hygroscopic:** sand, ceramics, glass, metal turnings, inorganic crystals

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batch Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Batch Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Batch Drying** is a discontinuous drying operation in which a **fixed quantity of wet solid material** is charged into a drying chamber, exposed to a drying medium (typically hot air or flue gas) for a defined residence time, and subsequently discharged after the desired final moisture content is attained. The entire process is transient, and operating conditions (temperature, humidity, airflow) are typically varied with time to achieve the target moisture level.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, batch drying is classified under **intermittent operation** in contact-type dryers (tray dryers, agitated pan dryers, batch fluidized bed dryers, batch rotary dryers, and vacuum shelf dryers).

### 1.2 Key Terminology Used in Batch Drying

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Moisture Content (Dry basis, X)** | $X = \dfrac{\text{Mass of moisture}}{\text{Mass of dry solid}}$ (kg water/kg dry solid) |
| **Moisture Content (Wet basis, w)** | $w = \dfrac{\text{Mass of moisture}}{\text{Total mass of wet solid}}$ (kg water/kg wet solid) |
| **Equilibrium Moisture Content (X\*)** | Minimum moisture content a material attains when exposed to air of given T and RH for infinite time |
| **Free Moisture Content (X − X\*)** | Moisture removable under given drying conditions |
| **Bound Moisture** | Moisture held tightly by the solid; not removable at given T & RH |
| **Unbound Moisture** | Moisture that behaves like pure water (removable easily) |
| **Critical Moisture Content (X\_c)** | Moisture content at which the constant rate drying period ends and the falling rate period begins |
| **Drying Rate (N)** | $N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A} \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ (kg water / m²·h) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Drying Laundry on a Clothesline":**
> Think of a wet shirt hung on a line on a sunny day. Initially, water evaporates from the surface at a constant rate (you see water droplets evaporating quickly — this is the **constant rate period**). As the surface becomes less wet, water from inside the cotton fibers must travel to the surface to evaporate — this happens more slowly, so drying slows down (this is the **falling rate period**). If you take the shirt inside before it is bone-dry, it still contains some moisture that cannot be removed unless you expose it to even drier air. That residual is the **equilibrium moisture content**.

In a **batch tray dryer**, exactly this happens inside a heated chamber with circulating hot air — the engineer must predict *how long* the batch must stay in the dryer.

### 1.4 Why Batch Drying is Used Industrially

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Significance:**
> - Used for **small production volumes**, **multi-product plants**, and **heat-sensitive materials** (pharmaceuticals, food, dyes, pigments).
> - Allows **flexible recipe control** (different drying curves per product).
> - Easier **quality validation** of individual batches (cGMP compliance in pharma).
> - Capital cost lower than continuous dryers for low-tonnage products.

Standard metrics emphasized in KTU exams:
- **Drying rate N = (m\_evaporated) / (A × t)** — units **kg water/m²·h**
- **Residence time t\_b** — units **hours (h)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Batch Drying — A Two-Stage Phenomenon

Batch drying of a wet solid under constant air conditions is divided into two distinct regimes:

#### Stage A — Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During the **CRDP**, the solid surface is saturated with free moisture. Water evaporates as if from a free liquid surface. The rate of evaporation is governed entirely by **external mass and heat transfer** between the air and the wet surface, *not* by the solid's internal structure.

- Surface temperature ≈ wet-bulb temperature of inlet air.
- Drying rate $N_c$ is essentially **constant** with time.
- Ends at the **critical moisture content (X\_c)**.

#### Stage B — Falling Rate Drying Period (FRDP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During the **FRDP**, the surface partially dries out. "Dry patches" appear and grow. Water must now travel from the interior of the solid to the surface by **diffusion, capillarity, or gravity**. The rate is governed by **internal mass transfer** within the solid.

- Surface temperature rises above wet-bulb temperature.
- Drying rate declines continuously with moisture content.
- This is typically the **rate-controlling step** for total batch time.

### 2.2 The Drying Rate Curve — Core Diagnostic Plot

A plot of **drying rate N vs moisture content X (or vs time t)** is the central diagnostic for batch dryer design. From it we extract:
- Constant drying rate $N_c$
- Critical moisture content $X_c$
- Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are direct KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield items.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A} \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Definition of drying rate (kg/m²·h) |
| 2 | $t_c = \dfrac{L_s \,(X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c}$ | Time for constant rate period (h) |
| 3 | $t_f = \dfrac{L_s}{A \, k_s} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | Falling rate period (linear internal diffusion assumed) |
| 4 | $t_f = \dfrac{L_s \,(X_c - X_2)}{A \, N_c}$ | Falling rate period (linear falling rate assumption) |
| 5 | $t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f$ | Total batch drying time |
| 6 | $N_c = \dfrac{h_t\,(T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_s}$ | Constant rate by heat-transfer analogy |
| 7 | $N_c = k_y \,(Y_w - Y_g)$ | Constant rate by mass-transfer analogy |
| 8 | $X = \dfrac{w}{1-w}$ | Dry basis from wet basis |
| 9 | $w = \dfrac{X}{1+X}$ | Wet basis from dry basis |
| 10 | $\text{Free moisture} = X - X^*$ | Removable at given T, RH |
| 11 | $L_s = \dfrac{\text{Total charge}}{1 + X_1}$ | Mass of dry solid per batch |
| 12 | $\lambda_s = 2500.8 - 2.38\,T_s$ (kJ/kg) | Latent heat at surface T (°C) |

**Where:**
- $L_s$ = mass of **bone-dry solid** (kg)
- $A$ = exposed drying area (m²)
- $k_s$ = slope of falling rate (linear assumption) (1/h)
- $k_y$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·h·ΔY)
- $h_t$ = heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $Y_w$, $Y_g$ = humidity ratio at wet surface and in bulk gas (kg water/kg dry air)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Batch drying calculations directly feed into:
- **Dryer sizing** (number of trays, dryer footprint).
- **Energy balance** — steam requirement for air heating.
- **Throughput estimation** (kg dry product/day → number of batches).
- **Process control** — endpoint detection in PLC systems using NIR moisture sensors.
- **Cost estimation** in pharmaceutical, food, and ceramics industries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Constant-Rate Drying Time $t_c$

**Starting point — Drying rate definition:**

$$N = -\frac{L_s}{A} \frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute N = N\_c (constant during CRDP):**

$$N_c = -\frac{L_s}{A} \frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$-N_c \, dt = \frac{L_s}{A} \, dX$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $t = 0, X = X_1$ to $t = t_c, X = X_c$:**

$$-N_c \int_0^{t_c} dt = \frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate both integrals:**

$$-N_c \, t_c = \frac{L_s}{A} (X_c - X_1)$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange (note the negative sign, since $X_1 > X_c$):**

$$\boxed{\,t_c = \frac{L_s \,(X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The negative sign flips $X_c - X_1$ to $X_1 - X_c$ because moisture is being *removed* (X decreases with time).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Falling-Rate Drying Time $t_f$ (Linear-Fall Assumption)

**Assumption:** In the falling rate region, the drying rate $N$ decreases *linearly* with moisture content $X$:

$$N = mX + b \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{N}{N_c} = \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

**Step 1 — Start from the basic differential equation:**

$$N = -\frac{L_s}{A} \frac{dX}{dt} = N_c \, \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$dt = -\frac{L_s}{A \, N_c} \, \frac{(X_c - X^*) \, dX}{X - X^*}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $X_c$ to $X_2$:**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s \,(X_c - X^*)}{A \, N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithmic integral:**

$$\int \frac{dX}{X - X^*} = \ln(X - X^*)$$

**Step 5 — Apply limits:**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s \,(X_c - X^*)}{A \, N_c} \left[ \ln(X - X^*) \right]_{X_2}^{X_c}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s \,(X_c - X^*)}{A \, N_c} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Define a convenient slope parameter $k_s$:**

$$k_s = \frac{N_c}{X_c - X^*} \quad \left( \text{units: } \text{kg/m}^2 \text{ per unit X per h} \right)$$

**Step 8 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,t_f = \frac{L_s}{A \, k_s} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\,}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The slope $k_s$ collapses the constants; this is the form most often used in KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Constant Drying Rate from Heat Transfer (Heat-Transfer Analogy)

**Heat balance on the wet surface during CRDP:**

- Heat transferred from gas to surface: $q = h_t \, A \, (T_g - T_s)$ [W]
- This heat is consumed entirely in evaporation: $q = \dot{m}_{evap} \, \lambda_s = (N_c \, A) \, \lambda_s$ [W]

**Equating the two expressions:**

$$h_t \, A \, (T_g - T_s) = N_c \, A \, \lambda_s$$

**Solving for $N_c$:**

$$\boxed{\,N_c = \frac{h_t \, (T_g - T_s)}{\lambda_s}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This derivation justifies why $N_c$ is independent of the solid's nature — it depends only on air-side heat transfer conditions.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A batch tray dryer holds **150 kg** of a wet granular solid initially containing **40% moisture (wet basis)**. The critical and equilibrium moisture contents (dry basis) are **X\_c = 0.20** and **X\* = 0.05**. The final desired moisture is **X\_2 = 0.08**. The constant drying rate in the dryer under the operating conditions is **N\_c = 1.2 kg/m²·h**, and the slope of the falling-rate line is **k\_s = 4.0 (1/h)**. The total exposed drying area is **A = 8.0 m²**. Calculate the **total batch drying time**.

**Step 1 — Convert initial moisture to dry basis:**

$$X_1 = \frac{w_1}{1 - w_1} = \frac{0.40}{1 - 0.40} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.6667 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the mass of dry solid $L_s$:**

$$L_s = \frac{150}{1 + X_1} = \frac{150}{1 + 0.6667} = \frac{150}{1.6667} = 90.0 \text{ kg dry solid}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate time for constant-rate period $t_c$:**

$$t_c = \frac{L_s \,(X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c} = \frac{90.0 \times (0.6667 - 0.20)}{8.0 \times 1.2}$$

$$t_c = \frac{90.0 \times 0.4667}{9.6} = \frac{42.0}{9.6} = 4.375 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate time for falling-rate period $t_f$:**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s}{A \, k_s} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right) = \frac{90.0}{8.0 \times 4.0} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.20 - 0.05}{0.08 - 0.05}\right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{90.0}{32.0} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.15}{0.03}\right) = 2.8125 \times \ln(5.0) = 2.8125 \times 1.6094$$

$$t_f = 4.527 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Total batch drying time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f = 4.375 + 4.527 = 8.902 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{total}} \approx 8.90 \text{ hours}\,}$$

> **Note:** This ~9-hour cycle (plus 1 h for charging/discharging) determines the number of batches per 24 h.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Batch Drying System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Wet Solid Feed] --> B1[Charging Stage]
    B1 --> C1[Batch Tray Dryer Chamber]
    C2[Air Heater / Blower] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Constant Rate Period]
    D1 --> E1[Falling Rate Period]
    E1 --> F1[Equilibrium Reached at X star]
    F1 --> G1[Discharge of Dried Solid]
    C1 --> H1[Moisture-Laden Exhaust Air]
    H1 --> I1[Optional Cyclone / Scrubber]
    I1 --> J1[Atmospheric Venting]
    C2 --> K1[Steam Supply or Electric Heater]
```

### 4.2 Drying Rate Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Moisture Content X axis] --> B2[X1 initial]
    B2 --> C2[Xc critical]
    C2 --> D2[X2 final]
    D2 --> E2[X star equilibrium]
    C2 --> F2[Constant Rate Nc horizontal line]
    C2 --> G2[Falling Rate declining line]
    F2 --> H2[Drying Rate N vertical axis]
    G2 --> H2
    E2 --> I2[Bound Moisture Region shaded]
    E2 --> J2[Unbound Moisture Region shaded]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Stages in a Batch Drying Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Loading of Wet Material] --> S2[Stage 2: Pre-Heating of Bed]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Constant Rate Period Nc constant]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Critical Point reached at Xc]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Falling Rate Period N decreases]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Endpoint at X2 desired]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Discharge of Dried Product]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8: Cleaning and Re-Loading]
    S8 --> S1
```

### 4.4 Mass & Energy Interaction Map (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MA1[Hot Air Stream] --> MEA1[Convective Heat Transfer ht]
    MEA1 --> MS1[Wet Solid Surface at Tw]
    MS1 --> MMA1[Mass Transfer ky and Yw minus Y]
    MMA1 --> MEV1[Water Evaporation Lambda s]
    MEV1 --> MH1[Moisture Laden Air]
    MH1 --> MEXT1[Exhaust Stream]
    MS1 --> MIC1[Internal Capillary Diffusion]
    MIC1 --> MS1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
> **Q1. Define constant rate drying period and falling rate drying period. How are they identified experimentally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **constant rate drying period (CRDP)** is the initial stage of batch drying during which the solid surface is fully saturated with free moisture and water evaporates at a rate determined solely by external (air-side) heat and mass transfer. It appears as a **horizontal straight line** on the plot of drying rate $N$ vs moisture content $X$.
- The **falling rate drying period (FRDP)** follows the CRDP, beginning at the **critical moisture content $X_c$**, when the surface partially dries and water must diffuse from inside the solid to the surface. It is identified by a **continuously declining curve** on the $N$–$X$ plot.
- The two regions are identified by **experimentally** measuring weight loss vs time under constant air conditions, differentiating the data to obtain $N$, and plotting $N$ vs $X$ or $X$ vs $t$.

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q2. What is meant by equilibrium moisture content? Why is it important in batch drying?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **equilibrium moisture content $X^\*$** is the moisture content a material attains when exposed to air of given temperature and relative humidity for an *infinite* time, at which point the solid and air are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** and net drying stops.
- It depends on the **hygroscopic nature of the material**, air temperature, and relative humidity.
- It is the **lower theoretical limit** of batch drying — a material can never be dried below $X^\*$ using that particular air condition.
- It defines the **free moisture** ($X - X^\*$) that is *actually removable* and hence the **target drying endpoint** for the process.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided — Attempt Either A or B**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** A wet solid containing **30% moisture (wet basis)** is dried in a **batch tray dryer**. The mass of dry solid per batch is **80 kg**. The critical and equilibrium moisture contents (dry basis) are **0.25 and 0.04** respectively. The constant rate of drying is **2.0 kg/m²·h** and the drying area is **4 m²**. Determine the time required for the **constant rate period** if the initial moisture is to be reduced to the critical value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the final desired moisture is **X\_2 = 0.08 (dry basis)** and the falling rate is linear with slope **k\_s = 5.0 (1/h)**, calculate the **total batch drying time** and the **total moisture removed per batch**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Convert initial wet-basis moisture to dry basis:

$$X_1 = \frac{0.30}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \text{ kg/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the constant rate period formula:

$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c} = \frac{80 \times (0.4286 - 0.25)}{4 \times 2.0}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$t_c = \frac{80 \times 0.1786}{8.0} = \frac{14.286}{8.0} = 1.786 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 1.79 \text{ hours}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Falling-rate time:

$$t_f = \frac{L_s}{A \, k_s} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right) = \frac{80}{4 \times 5.0} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.25 - 0.04}{0.08 - 0.04}\right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{80}{20} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.21}{0.04}\right) = 4.0 \times \ln(5.25) = 4.0 \times 1.6582$$

$$t_f = 6.633 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2:** Total batch time:

$$t_{\text{total}} = 1.786 + 6.633 = 8.42 \text{ h}$$

**Step 3:** Total moisture removed per batch:

$$m_{\text{water}} = L_s (X_1 - X_2) = 80 \times (0.4286 - 0.08) = 80 \times 0.3486$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{water}} = 27.89 \text{ kg water per batch}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

**(a)** Derive, starting from the basic definition of drying rate, the expression for the **time required in the constant rate drying period**. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **batch dryer** handles **120 kg of wet solid** at **50% moisture (wet basis)**. The exposed area is **6 m²**, $N_c$ = **1.5 kg/m²·h**, $X_c$ = **0.30**, $X^\*$ = **0.06**, and the **falling rate is linear**. The desired final moisture is **X\_2 = 0.10**. Calculate: (i) time for constant rate period, (ii) time for falling rate period, and (iii) total batch time. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Assumptions:**
- Drying conditions (air T, velocity, humidity) are held constant throughout the batch.
- Heat and mass transfer coefficients are constant.
- Dry solid mass $L_s$ is constant (no solid loss).
- The exposed drying area $A$ does not change.
- In the CRDP, the surface is fully wetted and drying rate $N_c$ is constant.

**Derivation:**

$$N = -\frac{L_s}{A} \frac{dX}{dt} = N_c \quad \text{(during CRDP)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{L_s}{A} dX = -N_c \, dt$$

Integrating from $X_1$ to $X_c$ (corresponding to $t = 0$ to $t = t_c$):

$$\frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX = -N_c \int_0^{t_c} dt$$

$$\frac{L_s}{A} (X_c - X_1) = -N_c \, t_c$$

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Dry-basis initial moisture:**

$$X_1 = \frac{0.50}{1 - 0.50} = 1.0 \text{ kg/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of dry solid:**

$$L_s = \frac{120}{1 + 1.0} = 60 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Constant rate period time:**

$$t_c = \frac{60 \times (1.0 - 0.30)}{6 \times 1.5} = \frac{60 \times 0.70}{9.0} = \frac{42.0}{9.0}$$

$$t_c = 4.667 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Slope of falling rate line:**

$$k_s = \frac{N_c}{X_c - X^*} = \frac{1.5}{0.30 - 0.06} = \frac{1.5}{0.24} = 6.25 \text{ (1/h)}$$

**Step 5 — Falling rate period time:**

$$t_f = \frac{60}{6 \times 6.25} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.30 - 0.06}{0.10 - 0.06}\right) = \frac{60}{37.5} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.24}{0.04}\right)$$

$$t_f = 1.6 \times \ln(6.0) = 1.6 \times 1.7918 = 2.867 \text{ h}$$

**Step 6 — Total batch time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = 4.667 + 2.867 = 7.534 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{total}} \approx 7.53 \text{ hours}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Batch Drying Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks typical):** Mixing dry-basis and wet-basis moisture. Always convert to **dry basis** (X, kg water/kg dry solid) before plugging into the formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting $X^\*$ in falling-rate formula (–2 marks):** Students often write $\ln(X_c / X_2)$ instead of $\ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$. The correct form uses **free moisture**, not absolute moisture.
> 3. **Wrong $L_s$ calculation (–2 marks):** $L_s$ is the mass of **dry solid**, not the total wet charge. Use $L_s = M_{\text{charge}} / (1 + X_1)$ where $X_1$ is on a **dry basis**.
> 4. **Time units mismatch (–1 mark):** If $N_c$ is in kg/m²·h, then $t_c$ automatically comes out in **hours**, not seconds.
> 5. **Assuming constant rate period is dominant:** In **most real batch drying problems**, the falling rate period is the **larger fraction** of total time. Mark the $t_f$ calculation explicitly.
> 6. **Neglecting charging/discharging time** in industrial estimates — the **9-hour drying time** plus 1–2 h of auxiliary operations gives the actual batch cycle.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Batch Drying**
>
> - [x] Batch drying = **transient, fixed-charge** operation; key design output is the **total batch time**.
> - [x] Two periods: **Constant Rate (CRDP)** — surface-controlled; **Falling Rate (FRDP)** — internal-diffusion-controlled.
> - [x] **Critical moisture content $X_c$** is the **boundary** between the two periods (depends on solid thickness, drying rate, and internal structure).
> - [x] **Equilibrium moisture content $X^\*$** is the **theoretical lower limit** of drying; it is the moisture retained in equilibrium with the air.
> - [x] Always work in **dry basis (X)**: $X = w/(1-w)$ and $L_s = M_{\text{charge}}/(1+X_1)$.
> - [x] **CRDP time formula:** $t_c = L_s (X_1 - X_c) / (A \, N_c)$.
> - [x] **FRDP time formula (linear fall):** $t_f = (L_s / A k_s) \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
> - [x] **Total batch time:** $t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f$.
> - [x] **Heat-transfer analogy:** $N_c = h_t (T_g - T_s) / \lambda_s$ — useful when $N_c$ is not given directly.
> - [x] **Mass-transfer analogy:** $N_c = k_y (Y_w - Y_g)$ — equivalent formulation using humidity differences.
> - [x] **Constant-rate period is often short; the falling-rate period dominates total drying time** in most real materials.
> - [x] Industrial design must add **charging + discharging time** (typically 1–2 h) to theoretical drying time to obtain the **batch cycle time**.
> - [x] Common dryers using this principle: **tray dryers, agitated pan dryers, batch fluidized bed dryers, vacuum shelf dryers, batch rotary dryers**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate of Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rate of Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Drying

**Drying** is a unit operation in which the moisture (primarily water) present in a wet solid is removed by thermal means, generally through the application of hot gases (usually air) in contact with the solid, by transferring heat to the solid and mass (moisture) from the solid to the gas phase. Mass transfer in drying is governed simultaneously by **heat transfer** (to evaporate liquid) and **mass transfer** (of vapour from the surface into the bulk gas).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCHT403, Module 4):**
> *Drying is the removal of relatively small amounts of water (or other liquid) from a material by thermal means. The process is characterised by simultaneous heat and mass transfer, where the rate of drying is defined as the mass of moisture removed per unit time per unit area of the drying surface.*

The **rate of drying**, $N$ (kg moisture removed / m²·h), is the central performance parameter that characterises the speed at which a solid loses moisture. It is the cornerstone for **equipment sizing**, **energy balance**, and **drying time calculation** in tray dryers, rotary dryers, fluidised bed dryers, and spray dryers.

## 1.2 Types of Moisture in a Solid

A wet solid contains moisture in two fundamentally different physical states, and the **rate of drying** is determined by which type is being removed at a given instant.

| Moisture Type | Physical State | Bound vs Free |
|---|---|---|
| **Free moisture (X′)** | Liquid held in voids and capillaries; exerts its full vapour pressure | The moisture content in excess of the equilibrium value |
| **Equilibrium moisture (X\*)** | Moisture held in equilibrium with the drying gas; cannot be removed by that gas | Inherent to the solid-gas system at given T, RH |
| **Unbound moisture** | Liquid that exerts a vapour pressure equal to that of pure liquid at the same temperature | Behaves like free moisture |
| **Bound moisture** | Liquid held by physical/chemical forces; exerts a vapour pressure < pure liquid | Cannot be removed at the given T, RH |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick conceptual link:** Free moisture = (Total moisture – Equilibrium moisture). Bound moisture = (Total moisture – Unbound moisture at that RH).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Wet Sponge Story

Imagine you have a **sponge soaked in water** sitting on a table in your room. Initially, water is pooled on top and in the big pores. This is the **free moisture**. The sponge dries quickly — the water simply evaporates from the surface. Now picture the sponge getting drier. Eventually, the large pores are empty, but the **small pores still cling to water** because of capillary suction — this is the **bound moisture**. From this point onward, the sponge dries *much* more slowly because water must travel from inside to the surface before it can evaporate.

This two-stage behaviour — **fast drying → slow drying** — is exactly the shape of the drying curve we will study. The first part (easy water) corresponds to the **Constant Rate Period**, and the second part (trapped water) corresponds to the **Falling Rate Period**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the rate changes:** In the constant rate period, the solid surface remains *wet* — drying is surface-evaporation controlled. In the falling rate period, the surface becomes *dry* — drying is solid-side diffusion controlled. The transition happens at the **critical moisture content (X_c)**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Drying Calculations

The following terms and units are **standard** and appear in virtually every KTU numerical on this topic:

- **Moisture content (X)**: kg moisture / kg dry solid (dry basis, dry-basis is preferred in engineering).
- **Wet basis moisture content (w)**: kg moisture / kg wet solid.
- **Equilibrium moisture content (X\*)**: kg moisture / kg dry solid, in equilibrium with the drying air.
- **Critical moisture content (X_c)**: value of X at which the constant rate period ends and the falling rate period begins.
- **Free moisture content (X_f = X − X\*)**: the moisture that *can* be removed at a given air condition.
- **Drying rate (N)**: kg H₂O removed / (m²·h) — formally the **mass flux of evaporation**.
- **Drying surface area (A)**: m², the exposed wet area of the solid.
- **Mass of bone-dry solid (L_s)**: kg of absolutely dry solid (the invariant reference).

> [!TIP]
> **Always use dry-basis moisture content (X) in KTU derivations and problems.** The dry-basis value is **independent of the moisture still present**, making it the proper basis for mass and rate balances. Convert using $X = \dfrac{w}{1-w}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Drying Rate Curve — The Heart of the Topic

The **drying rate curve** is the plot of the **instantaneous rate of drying (N)** on the y-axis versus the **moisture content (X)** on the x-axis. Every other drying concept hangs on the shape of this curve.

### 2.1.1 Region A → B: Initial Adjustment (Transient)

When wet solid is first placed in hot, dry air, the surface temperature rises (or the water warms to the wet-bulb temperature) and the drying rate climbs to its steady value. This brief **transient zone** is usually short and is often neglected in KTU problems.

### 2.1.2 Region B → C: Constant Rate Period (CRP)

The surface of the solid remains completely **saturated with free moisture**. Drying is controlled by the rate at which vapour can be carried away from the *wet* surface — essentially a **gas-phase mass/heat transfer problem**. All the heat supplied goes to evaporate water, and the surface temperature remains at the **wet-bulb temperature (T_w)** of the air.

**Why is the rate constant?**

- The surface is wet → behaves like a free water surface.
- Vapour pressure at the surface = saturation pressure at T_w.
- Heat transfer to the surface equals the latent heat needed to evaporate water at the rate observed.
- Mass transfer driving force (humidity difference) is constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The constant rate period is governed by **external conditions** — the air velocity, humidity, and temperature — *not* by the type of solid. Any two solids with the same exposed wet area and the same air conditions dry at the same rate in the CRP.

The rate of drying in the CRP is given by:

$$N_c = \dfrac{h_t \left( T_g - T_w \right)}{\lambda_s}$$

where $\lambda_s$ is the latent heat of vaporisation at T_w (kJ/kg). Alternatively, from the mass-transfer perspective:

$$N_c = k_y \, M_B \, \left( H_w - H_g \right)$$

### 2.1.3 Region C → D: First Falling Rate Period (FFRP)

At the **critical moisture content (X_c)**, the surface first begins to dry out. Dry patches appear, and the effective wetted area shrinks. The rate of drying **decreases linearly** with X. The plane of evaporation recedes *into* the solid.

### 2.1.4 Region D → E: Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP)

Below a second transition point, moisture transport is controlled by **internal diffusion** of liquid/vapour through the solid matrix. The rate falls more steeply, often linearly again with X (when plotted vs free moisture, the curve continues a piecewise-linear shape in the simplest KTU model).

## 2.2 The Batch Drying Material Balance

For a batch of dry solid of mass $L_s$ (kg bone-dry solid) being dried from $X_1$ to $X_2$ (kg/kg dry solid), the **instantaneous rate of moisture loss** is:

$$- \dfrac{L_s}{1} \, \dfrac{dX}{dt} = N \cdot A$$

where $A$ is the drying surface area (m²). Therefore:

$$\dfrac{dX}{dt} = - \dfrac{N A}{L_s}$$

This is the **fundamental differential equation of batch drying** and is the starting point of every KTU drying-time derivation.

## 2.3 Drying Time in the Constant Rate Period

Since $N = N_c = $ constant in CRP, integrating from $t = 0$ (where $X = X_1$) to $t = t_1$ (where $X = X_c$):

$$\int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX = - \int_{0}^{t_1} \dfrac{N_c A}{L_s} \, dt$$

$$t_1 = \dfrac{L_s}{A \, N_c} \left( X_1 - X_c \right)$$

## 2.4 Drying Time in the Falling Rate Period

Since $N$ varies with $X$ in FRP, we cannot pull it out of the integral. The general expression is:

$$t_2 = \dfrac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \dfrac{dX}{N}$$

For the **linear falling rate model** (the KTU-favoured simplification), $N$ varies linearly with $X$ from $(X_c, N_c)$ to $(X^*, 0)$:

$$N = N_c \, \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

Substituting:

$$t_2 = \dfrac{L_s}{A} \, \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{L_s}{A} \, \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{N_c} \ln \left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total drying time = t_1 + t_2.** If the entire drying happens in the CRP (i.e., $X_2 \geq X_c$), only the first formula is used.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dry-basis moisture content | $X = \dfrac{w}{1-w}$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| 2 | Mass of dry solid | $L_s = M_{wet} (1 - w)$ | kg bone-dry solid |
| 3 | Differential rate equation | $-\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \dfrac{N A}{L_s}$ | X in kg/kg, t in h, N in kg/m²·h |
| 4 | Constant rate period rate (heat) | $N_c = \dfrac{h_t (T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_s}$ | $h_t$ in kW/m²·K, $\lambda_s$ in kJ/kg |
| 5 | Constant rate period rate (mass) | $N_c = k_y M_B (H_w - H_g)$ | k_y in kg/m²·s·ΔH |
| 6 | CRP drying time | $t_1 = \dfrac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c A}$ | hours |
| 7 | FRP drying time (linear) | $t_2 = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c A} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | hours |
| 8 | Total drying time | $t_{total} = t_1 + t_2$ | hours |
| 9 | Linear FRP rate model | $N = N_c \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$ | Valid for $X^* \leq X \leq X_c$ |
| 10 | Log-mean $\Delta T$ (for air coolers) | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | for cross-flow corrections |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL ALERT:** In Equation 7, the argument of $\ln$ is a **ratio of free moistures**, *not* a ratio of X values. Many KTU candidates lose 2–3 marks by writing $\ln(X_c / X_2)$ — this is dimensionally inconsistent and **wrong**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The drying rate curve and the formulas above are used to:

- **Size industrial dryers** — tray, rotary, fluidised bed, spray, conveyor.
- **Estimate residence time** for continuous dryers.
- **Determine the optimum exit moisture** to minimise energy and floor area.
- **Diagnose over-drying / under-drying** in production lines.
- **Compare solid materials** for their drying behaviour (foods, pharmaceuticals, ceramics, polymers, paper, textiles).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Differential Drying Equation

### Statement

For a batch of wet solid of dry mass $L_s$ (kg) being dried over an exposed area $A$ (m²), show that the rate of moisture removal can be expressed as $-\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \dfrac{N A}{L_s}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the instantaneous moisture inventory.**
At any time $t$, the mass of water in the solid is $L_s \cdot X$ (kg water), where $L_s$ is constant because the dry solid mass is invariant.

**Step 2 — Write the rate of moisture loss.**
The total mass of water removed per unit time is the drying rate $N$ (kg/m²·h) acting on the area $A$ (m²):

$$\dfrac{d(L_s X)}{dt} = - N \cdot A$$

The negative sign indicates that moisture content is *decreasing* with time.

**Step 3 — Pull out the constant.**
Since $L_s$ is constant with respect to time, $\dfrac{d(L_s X)}{dt} = L_s \dfrac{dX}{dt}$:

$$L_s \dfrac{dX}{dt} = - N \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the standard form.**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dX}{dt} = - \dfrac{N A}{L_s}}$$

This is the **master equation** of batch drying. Every drying-time formula derives from it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Constant Rate Period Drying Time

### Statement

If the drying proceeds entirely at a constant rate $N_c$ from moisture $X_1$ to $X_c$, derive the time required $t_1$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the master equation in the CRP.**
In the CRP, $N = N_c$ = constant. Hence:

$$\dfrac{dX}{dt} = - \dfrac{N_c A}{L_s}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables.**

$$dX = - \dfrac{N_c A}{L_s} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply limits.**
At $t = 0$, $X = X_1$. At $t = t_1$, $X = X_c$:

$$\int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX = - \dfrac{N_c A}{L_s} \int_{0}^{t_1} dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$X_c - X_1 = - \dfrac{N_c A}{L_s} \cdot t_1$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t_1$.**

$$\boxed{t_1 = \dfrac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c A}}$$

The expression is **positive** because in the CRP we always have $X_1 > X_c$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Falling Rate Period Drying Time

### Statement

In the falling rate period, the drying rate is assumed to vary linearly with moisture content, passing through the points $(X_c, N_c)$ and $(X^*, 0)$. Derive the FRP drying time $t_2$ for a target moisture $X_2 < X_c$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Establish the linear rate law.**
The line joining $(X_c, N_c)$ and $(X^*, 0)$ has slope $\dfrac{N_c}{X_c - X^*}$:

$$N - 0 = \dfrac{N_c}{X_c - X^*} (X - X^*)$$

$$N = N_c \, \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the master equation.**

$$- \dfrac{dX}{dt} = \dfrac{A}{L_s} \cdot N_c \, \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables.**

$$- \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*} = \dfrac{A N_c}{L_s (X_c - X^*)} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Apply limits.** At $t = 0$, $X = X_c$. At $t = t_2$, $X = X_2$:

$$- \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*} = \dfrac{A N_c}{L_s (X_c - X^*)} \int_{0}^{t_2} dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the left integral.**
Recall $\int \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*} = \ln(X - X^*)$:

$$- \big[\ln(X_2 - X^*) - \ln(X_c - X^*)\big] = \dfrac{A N_c \, t_2}{L_s (X_c - X^*)}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the left side.**

$$\ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right) = \dfrac{A N_c \, t_2}{L_s (X_c - X^*)}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $t_2$.**

$$\boxed{t_2 = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A N_c} \, \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table — CRP vs FRP

| Property | Constant Rate Period | Falling Rate Period |
|---|---|---|
| Surface condition | Completely wet | Partially or fully dry |
| Controlling resistance | External gas film | Internal solid-side diffusion |
| Surface temperature | Wet-bulb of air, $T_w$ | Rises above $T_w$ toward $T_g$ |
| Heat-transfer equation | $q = h_t (T_g - T_w)$ | Falls with rate of evaporation |
| Mass-transfer equation | $N = k_y (H_w - H_g)$ | Falls with drying rate |
| Rate vs X | Independent of X | Decreases with X (often linearly) |
| Time equation | $t_1 = L_s(X_1 - X_c)/(N_c A)$ | $t_2 = (L_s/N_c A)(X_c - X^*)\ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$ |
| Affected by air velocity? | Strongly yes | Weakly / not at all |
| Affected by solid thickness? | No | Strongly yes |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Outline (KTU Pattern)

A solid weighing **200 kg wet** at initial moisture **40 % (wet basis)** is dried to a final moisture of **5 % (wet basis)** in a tray dryer. The critical moisture content is **20 % (dry basis)** and the equilibrium moisture content is **2 % (dry basis)**. The constant drying rate is **$0.30 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/m²·s** and the drying area is **1.5 m²**. Calculate the **total drying time** using the linear FRP model.

**Solution skeleton (typical KTU 14-mark layout):**

1. Convert wet-basis to dry-basis: $X_1 = 0.40/0.60 = 0.667$, $X_2 = 0.05/0.95 = 0.0526$, $X_c = 0.20/0.80 = 0.25$, $X^* = 0.02/0.98 = 0.0204$ kg/kg dry solid.
2. Dry solid mass: $L_s = 200 (1 - 0.40) = 120$ kg.
3. Check $X_2 < X_c$ → use both periods.
4. CRP time: $t_1 = 120 (0.667 - 0.25) / (0.30 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.5 \cdot 3600) = $ (students must convert $N_c$ to kg/m²·h by multiplying by 3600) → final $t_1$ in hours.
5. FRP time: $t_2 = 120 (0.25 - 0.0204) / (0.30 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.5 \cdot 3600) \cdot \ln[(0.25 - 0.0204)/(0.0526 - 0.0204)]$ → final $t_2$ in hours.
6. Total: $t_{total} = t_1 + t_2$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Drying Rate Curve

The drying rate curve is best represented as a multi-stage functional map. The mermaid block below captures the **state transitions, controlling mechanisms, and the surface / internal conditions** that govern each period.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WET SOLID PLACED IN DRYER"] --> B["INITIAL ADJUSTMENT: SURFACE WARMS TO T_W"]
    B --> C{"IS SURFACE STILL WET?"}
    C -- YES --> D["CONSTANT RATE PERIOD: EXTERNAL GAS FILM CONTROLS"]
    D --> E["X DECREASES DOWN TO X_C AT RATE N_C"]
    E --> F{"HAS X REACHED X_C?"}
    C -- NO DIRECT DRYING --> G["FALLING RATE PERIOD: INTERNAL DIFFUSION CONTROLS"]
    F -- YES --> G
    G --> H["FIRST FALLING RATE: SURFACE DRYING PROGRESSES"]
    H --> I["SECOND FALLING RATE: PURE INTERNAL DIFFUSION"]
    I --> J["X APPROACHES X* AS RATE TENDS TO ZERO"]
    J --> K["EQUILIBRIUM ACHIEVED: NO FURTHER DRYING"]
    D --> L["HEAT TRANSFER: Q = H_T TIMES T_G MINUS T_W"]
    D --> M["MASS TRANSFER: N_C = K_Y TIMES H_W MINUS H_G"]
    G --> N["HEAT AND MASS FLUXES COUPLE TO INTERNAL MOISTURE MOVEMENT"]
    K --> O["DRYING OPERATION COMPLETE"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Mapping of the Batch-Drying Time Calculation

The figure below maps the **two-period calculation** as a sequential process-control block diagram. Each block corresponds to a single equation from Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["INPUT DATA: L_S, X_1, X_2, X_C, X_STAR, N_C, A"] --> P2["CONVERT ALL MOISTURES TO DRY BASIS"]
    P2 --> P3{"X_2 GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO X_C?"}
    P3 -- YES --> P4["ENTIRE DRYING IN CRP: T = L_S X_1 MINUS X_2 DIVIDED BY N_C A"]
    P3 -- NO --> P5["CRP BLOCK: T_1 = L_S X_1 MINUS X_C DIVIDED BY N_C A"]
    P5 --> P6["FRP BLOCK: T_2 = L_S X_C MINUS X_STAR DIVIDED BY N_C A TIMES LN OF RATIO"]
    P6 --> P7["TOTAL TIME: T_TOTAL = T_1 PLUS T_2"]
    P4 --> P8["OUTPUT: DRYING TIME IN HOURS"]
    P7 --> P8
    P2 --> P9["UNITS CHECK: N_C IN KG PER M SQUARE PER H"]
    P9 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of the Two Simultaneous Transfer Processes in the CRP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["HOT DRY AIR: T_G, H_G"] -- CONVECTION --> B["GAS FILM AT SOLID SURFACE"]
    B -- HEAT TRANSFER H_T --> C["WET SOLID SURFACE AT T_W"]
    C -- EVAPORATION LATENT HEAT LAMBDA --> D["WATER VAPOUR ENTERING GAS FILM"]
    D -- MASS TRANSFER K_Y --> B
    B -- CARRY AWAY --> E["EXIT AIR: T_G EXIT, H_G EXIT"]
    C -- CAPILLARY FEED --> F["INTERIOR FREE MOISTURE"]
    F -- CONTINUOUS SUPPLY --> C
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation aid:** The two-way arrows in the block diagram emphasise that **heat flows in** (gas → surface) and **mass flows out** (surface → gas). In the CRP the surface is replenished by capillary flow, so the two streams are *coupled but steady*. In the FRP the capillary flow becomes the bottleneck, and the rate falls.

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Mechanism of Moisture Movement in Solids

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["SOURCE: INTERIOR MOISTURE"] --> M2{"MOISTURE TYPE?"}
    M2 -- "FREE IN VOIDS" --> M3["CAPILLARY FLOW: DRIVEN BY SURFACE TENSION GRADIENTS"]
    M2 -- "BOUND IN PORES" --> M4["LIQUID DIFFUSION THROUGH PORES"]
    M2 -- "ADSORBED" --> M5["SURFACE DIFFUSION ALONG PORE WALLS"]
    M3 --> M6["WET SURFACE: EVAPORATES AT T_W"]
    M4 --> M6
    M5 --> M6
    M6 --> M7["VAPOUR DIFFUSION THROUGH GAS FILM"]
    M7 --> M8["BULK GAS STREAM CARRYING VAPOUR AWAY"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above blocks correspond to the four classical **mechanisms of moisture movement** in solids: (1) capillary flow, (2) liquid diffusion, (3) surface diffusion, (4) vapour diffusion. In **any** falling rate period, *one or more* of the first three is rate-controlling.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by the constant rate period in drying? Why is the rate constant in this period?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **constant rate period** of drying is the initial phase of a batch-drying operation during which the rate of moisture removal remains essentially **constant with time (and with moisture content)**. It occurs as long as the surface of the solid remains completely covered with free moisture. The rate is constant because:

1. The wet surface behaves like a free water surface, exerting its saturation vapour pressure at the **wet-bulb temperature** $T_w$ of the air. (**1 mark**)
2. The driving force for mass transfer, $H_w - H_g$, and the driving force for heat transfer, $T_g - T_w$, are both steady, and the surface is continuously replenished by capillary flow from the interior. (**1 mark**)
3. Therefore the heat transferred to the surface equals the latent heat needed to evaporate the water removed, with no surface-temperature change, and the rate stays at $N_c = h_t (T_g - T_w)/\lambda_s$. (**1 mark**)

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between the critical moisture content and the equilibrium moisture content.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Critical Moisture ($X_c$) | Equilibrium Moisture ($X^*$) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical meaning | Moisture content at which the constant rate period ends and the falling rate period begins. | The lowest moisture content attainable when the solid is in equilibrium with the drying air. |
| Value depends on | Solid type, thickness, drying rate, surface conditions. | The **temperature and relative humidity** of the drying air and the solid. |
| Role in drying | Marks the **transition** from surface-evaporation to internal-diffusion control. | Marks the **lower limit** of drying — drying stops here. |
| Units | kg water / kg dry solid | kg water / kg dry solid |

(**2 marks** for the distinctions + **1 mark** for the tabulated format.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse $X_c$ with $X^*$. $X_c$ is a *transition point inside* the drying process; $X^*$ is the *theoretical limit* of drying. They are **not** the same.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question B1 (Option A) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the typical **drying rate curve** and clearly identify all the regions. State the controlling mechanism in each region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet solid weighing **400 kg wet** (initial moisture **50 % wet basis**) is dried in a tray dryer with a constant drying rate of **$0.25 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/m²·s** to a final moisture of **10 % wet basis**. The critical moisture content is **$X_c = 0.30$ kg/kg dry solid**, the equilibrium moisture content is **$X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg dry solid**, and the drying area is **$A = 2.0$ m²**. Calculate the **total drying time** assuming a linear falling rate model. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B1(a) — 7 marks

The drying rate curve plots the **instantaneous drying rate $N$ (kg/m²·h)** on the y-axis against the **moisture content $X$ (kg/kg dry solid)** on the x-axis. It is divided into four regions:

- **Region A–B — Initial adjustment (transient):** The wet solid's surface temperature rises to the wet-bulb temperature $T_w$, and the rate builds up to its steady value. **(1 mark)**
- **Region B–C — Constant Rate Period (CRP):** The surface is fully wet. Drying is controlled by the **external gas-film resistance** to heat and mass transfer. $N = N_c$ = constant. Surface stays at $T_w$. **(2 marks)**
- **Region C–D — First Falling Rate Period (FFRP):** Dry patches appear on the surface. The effective wetted area shrinks, and the plane of evaporation recedes into the solid. The rate decreases **linearly with X** (in the simplified KTU model). Controlling mechanism: combined external + internal. **(2 marks)**
- **Region D–E — Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP):** Surface is essentially dry. Drying is controlled by **internal moisture diffusion** through the solid. The rate falls more steeply and asymptotically approaches zero as $X \to X^*$. **(1 mark)**
