- A clearly labelled sketch showing all four regions, $X^*$, $X_c$, $X_1$, $X_2$ on the x-axis and $N_c$ on the y-axis. **(1 mark)**

**Controlling-mechanism summary (to be included in the answer):**
- CRP: external gas film (heat/mass transfer in air).
- FRP: internal moisture movement (capillary flow, liquid diffusion, surface diffusion).

---

### Model Solution to Question B1(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Convert all moistures to dry basis (1 mark):**

$$X_1 = \dfrac{0.50}{1 - 0.50} = 1.000 \;\text{kg/kg ds}$$

$$X_2 = \dfrac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = 0.1111 \;\text{kg/kg ds}$$

$X_c = 0.30$ kg/kg ds (given, already dry basis)

$X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg ds (given, already dry basis)

**Step 2 — Compute the dry solid mass (1 mark):**

$$L_s = 400 \times (1 - 0.50) = 200 \;\text{kg dry solid}$$

**Step 3 — Check whether both periods are needed (0.5 mark):**
Since $X_2 = 0.1111 < X_c = 0.30$, drying proceeds through the CRP and the FRP. Both must be computed.

**Step 4 — Convert $N_c$ to consistent units (0.5 mark):**
$N_c = 0.25 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/m²·s $= 0.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 0.9$ kg/m²·h

**Step 5 — Constant Rate Period time (1.5 marks):**

$$t_1 = \dfrac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \, A} = \dfrac{200 \times (1.000 - 0.30)}{0.9 \times 2.0} = \dfrac{200 \times 0.70}{1.8} = 77.78 \;\text{h}$$

**Step 6 — Falling Rate Period time (2 marks):**

$$t_2 = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \, A} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{200 \times (0.30 - 0.02)}{0.9 \times 2.0} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{0.30 - 0.02}{0.1111 - 0.02} \right)$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{200 \times 0.28}{1.8} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{0.28}{0.0911} \right) = 31.11 \times \ln(3.073)$$

$$t_2 = 31.11 \times 1.123 = 34.93 \;\text{h}$$

**Step 7 — Total drying time (0.5 mark):**

$$t_{total} = t_1 + t_2 = 77.78 + 34.93 = 112.71 \;\text{h}$$

**Final Answer:** Total drying time ≈ **112.7 hours**. (1 mark for correct final boxed answer with units.)

---

### Question B2 (Option B — Internal Choice Alternative) — **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **differential rate equation** for batch drying and hence obtain an expression for the **time of drying in the constant rate period**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-porous solid is dried from an initial moisture of **$X_1 = 0.50$ kg/kg ds** to a final moisture of **$X_2 = 0.05$ kg/kg ds**. The critical moisture content is **$X_c = 0.18$ kg/kg ds** and the equilibrium moisture content is **$X^* = 0.01$ kg/kg ds**. The constant drying rate is **$N_c = 1.2$ kg/m²·h**, the dry solid mass is **$L_s = 50$ kg**, and the drying area is **$A = 1.0$ m²**. The falling rate is **linear**. Compute the **total drying time**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B2(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Define moisture inventory (1 mark):**
Let $L_s$ (kg) be the mass of bone-dry solid (constant). At any instant, total water mass in the solid = $L_s \cdot X$ kg, where $X$ is the dry-basis moisture content.

**Step 2 — Write the rate of moisture loss (1.5 marks):**
The rate at which water leaves the surface is $N \cdot A$ (kg/h), where $N$ is the drying rate per unit area and $A$ is the exposed drying area. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{d(L_s X)}{dt} = - N A$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate (1 mark):**

$$L_s \dfrac{dX}{dt} = - N A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{dX}{dt} = - \dfrac{N A}{L_s}$$

This is the **differential rate equation for batch drying**. (**0.5 mark** for the boxed result.)

**Step 4 — Apply to the constant rate period (1 mark):**
In the CRP, $N = N_c$ (constant), so:

$$\dfrac{dX}{dt} = - \dfrac{N_c A}{L_s}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate with limits $t = 0 \to t_1$ and $X = X_1 \to X_c$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX = - \dfrac{N_c A}{L_s} \int_{0}^{t_1} dt$$

$$X_c - X_1 = - \dfrac{N_c A}{L_s} t_1$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $t_1$ (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{t_1 = \dfrac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c A}}$$

---

### Model Solution to Question B2(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify data and verify (0.5 mark):**
$X_1 = 0.50$, $X_2 = 0.05$, $X_c = 0.18$, $X^* = 0.01$, $N_c = 1.2$ kg/m²·h, $L_s = 50$ kg, $A = 1.0$ m².
Since $X_2 = 0.05 < X_c = 0.18$, both CRP and FRP are needed.

**Step 2 — Constant Rate Period (1.5 marks):**

$$t_1 = \dfrac{50 \times (0.50 - 0.18)}{1.2 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.32}{1.2} = 13.33 \;\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Falling Rate Period — setup (1 mark):**

$$t_2 = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c A} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (2 marks):**

$$t_2 = \dfrac{50 \times (0.18 - 0.01)}{1.2 \times 1.0} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{0.18 - 0.01}{0.05 - 0.01} \right)$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{50 \times 0.17}{1.2} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{0.17}{0.04} \right) = 7.083 \times \ln(4.25)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm (1 mark):**

$$\ln(4.25) = 1.4469$$

**Step 6 — Compute $t_2$ (0.5 mark):**

$$t_2 = 7.083 \times 1.4469 = 10.25 \;\text{h}$$

**Step 7 — Total time (0.5 mark):**

$$t_{total} = t_1 + t_2 = 13.33 + 10.25 = 23.58 \;\text{h}$$

**Final Answer:** Total drying time ≈ **23.58 h**. (1 mark for correctly boxed final answer.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $N_c$ from kg/m²·s to kg/m²·h** (or vice-versa) → wrong answer by a factor of 3600. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Using wet-basis moistures directly in dry-basis formulas** → wrong drying times. **(-2 marks)**
> 3. **Writing $\ln(X_c / X_2)$ instead of $\ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$** in the FRP time formula. **(-2 marks)**
> 4. **Stopping the calculation at the CRP** when $X_2 < X_c$ and ignoring the FRP entirely. **(-3 to -5 marks)**
> 5. **Confusing critical moisture $X_c$ with equilibrium moisture $X^*$** in conceptual questions. **(-1 mark)**
> 6. **Forgetting units in the final answer** or omitting the unit box. **(-0.5 mark)**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Drying** = simultaneous heat + mass transfer; the rate of drying is the **mass flux of water removed** in kg/m²·h.
- The **drying rate curve** has four regions: A–B (transient), B–C (Constant Rate Period), C–D (First Falling Rate), D–E (Second Falling Rate).
- The **Constant Rate Period (CRP)** is controlled by **external gas-film resistance**; the surface stays at the **wet-bulb temperature** $T_w$; rate is given by $N_c = h_t (T_g - T_w)/\lambda_s$ or $N_c = k_y M_B (H_w - H_g)$.
- The **Falling Rate Period (FRP)** is controlled by **internal moisture movement**; rate is given in the linear model by $N = N_c (X - X^*)/(X_c - X^*)$.
- **Master differential equation of batch drying:** $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = - \dfrac{N A}{L_s}$.
- **CRP time formula:** $t_1 = L_s (X_1 - X_c)/(N_c A)$.
- **FRP time formula (linear model):** $t_2 = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c A} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$.
- **Total drying time:** $t_{total} = t_1 + t_2$.
- **Always use dry-basis moisture content (X)** in KTU problems. Convert using $X = w/(1-w)$.
- **Always convert $N_c$ to units consistent with $t$** (kg/m²·h if $t$ is in hours; kg/m²·s if $t$ is in seconds).
- **Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$** is the *lowest attainable* moisture at the given air T and RH.
- **Critical moisture content $X_c$** is the *transition* point from CRP to FRP; it depends on solid type, thickness, and external drying rate.
- **Free moisture = X − X\***; **Bound moisture** is the moisture that exerts a vapour pressure lower than the pure liquid.
- **Mechanisms of internal moisture movement:** capillary flow, liquid diffusion, surface diffusion, vapour diffusion. One of these becomes rate-controlling in the FRP.
- **Air velocity affects CRP strongly but FRP weakly** — a key conceptual question.
- **For a non-porous solid**, $X^* = 0$ and the FRP simplifies to $t_2 = \dfrac{L_s X_c}{N_c A} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c}{X_2}\right)$.
- **For drying occurring entirely in the CRP** (i.e., $X_2 \geq X_c$), only $t_1$ is needed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Moisture Movement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Moisture Movement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism of Moisture Movement** refers to the various physical processes by which moisture (liquid water and/or water vapour) is transported from the interior of a wet solid to its surface during drying operations. The dominant mechanism is governed by the nature of the solid (porous vs. non-porous, hygroscopic vs. non-hygroscopic), the moisture content, and the mode of moisture binding to the solid matrix.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme classification, moisture movement mechanisms are categorised into **five primary modes**:

1. **Liquid Movement due to Capillary Forces** — moisture migrates through interconnected pores under capillary suction.
2. **Liquid Diffusion** — moisture migrates as a liquid through the solid matrix by molecular diffusion.
3. **Vapour Diffusion** — moisture evaporates within the solid and diffuses as vapour through pores.
4. **Moisture Movement due to Gravity** — liquid flows under gravitational body force.
5. **Thermal Diffusion / Effusion (Knudsen Flow)** — vapour movement due to temperature gradients or through fine pores.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sponge in a Bowl of Water**
>
> Imagine a dry kitchen sponge placed such that only its bottom edge touches a pool of water. You observe the water creeping upward against gravity through the tiny pores of the sponge. This is **capillary action**. Now press the wet sponge against a hot griddle — the water trapped inside the pores converts to steam and rushes outward as **vapour diffusion**. If the sponge is squeezed, water is forced out as bulk **liquid flow**.
>
> A wet solid during drying behaves exactly like this sponge: at high moisture contents water moves by **capillary forces**, while at low moisture contents it must **evaporate inside the pores** and travel outwards as **vapour**.

## 1.3 Classification of Moisture in a Solid

A solid retains moisture in two broad physical states:

- **Unbound (Free) Moisture** — water in excess of the equilibrium moisture content, held loosely in capillaries. Evaporates freely as if from a pure water surface. Governs the **constant-rate drying period**.
- **Bound Moisture** — water physically or chemically bound to the solid; requires extra energy to remove. Governs the **falling-rate drying period**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **mechanism of moisture movement is fundamentally different in the constant-rate period and the falling-rate period.** This distinction is a high-weightage KTU 2024 concept frequently asked in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

## 1.4 Critical Constant

The **critical moisture content (X\*)** is the moisture content at which the surface can no longer remain fully saturated, marking the transition from constant-rate to falling-rate drying. Its value is typically expressed in **kg water / kg dry solid** (dimensionless mass ratio).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-Wise Analysis of Moisture Movement

### Stage A — Constant Rate Drying Period (X > X\*)

- The solid surface is **fully wetted** with a continuous film of free water.
- Evaporation occurs as if from a free water surface.
- The **rate-limiting step is external mass transfer** (diffusion of vapour from the saturated surface into the bulk gas phase), *not* internal moisture movement.
- The surface temperature equals the **wet-bulb temperature** of the air.
- Internal moisture reaches the surface fast enough (via **capillary action**) to replenish evaporating water.

### Stage B — Falling Rate Drying Period (X < X\*)

The surface dries partially. Internal moisture movement now **controls** the drying rate. The dominant mechanism shifts progressively:

- **First Falling Rate Period** — surface contains isolated wet patches; evaporation occurs both at wet patches and within partially filled capillaries. Both **liquid capillary flow** and **vapour diffusion** contribute.
- **Second Falling Rate Period** — surface is dry; all evaporation occurs **inside the solid** at an internal evaporation front (receding plane). Moisture moves exclusively as **vapour diffusion** (and partially as **liquid diffusion** in hygroscopic materials).

## 2.2 Detailed Mechanism Analysis

### Mechanism 1 — Liquid Movement by Capillary Forces (High Moisture)

$$
\frac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{cap} \nabla^{2} X
$$

where $D_{cap}$ is the **capillary diffusivity** (m²/s), which depends on the pore structure and moisture content. Capillary flow is driven by the **Laplace pressure difference** across curved menisci in pores of varying radii:

$$
\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_{p}}
$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = surface tension of water (~**0.072 N/m at 20°C**)
- $\theta$ = contact angle between water and solid
- $r_{p}$ = pore radius (m)

Smaller pores generate larger suction, drawing water from larger pores — a **self-distributing mechanism**.

### Mechanism 2 — Liquid Diffusion (Bound Moisture in Hygroscopic Solids)

Governed by **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**:

$$
\frac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{L} \frac{\partial^{2} X}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

where $D_{L}$ is the **liquid diffusion coefficient** (m²/s), typically a strong function of local moisture content and temperature. Liquid diffusion dominates in dense, non-porous materials such as **soap, polymers, and foodstuffs** where no continuous capillary network exists.

### Mechanism 3 — Vapour Diffusion (Knudsen + Molecular)

When liquid evaporates inside pores, vapour diffuses outwards. For **large pores**, ordinary molecular diffusion dominates:

$$
N_{A} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_{A}}{dx}
$$

For **fine pores** where pore diameter approaches mean free path of vapour molecules, **Knudsen diffusion** dominates:

$$
D_{K} = 97.0 \, r_{p} \sqrt{\frac{T}{M_{A}}}
$$

where:
- $D_{K}$ = Knudsen diffusivity (m²/s)
- $r_{p}$ = pore radius (m)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $M_{A}$ = molecular weight of vapour (kg/kmol)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **effective vapour diffusivity** in a porous solid is given by $\dfrac{D_{AB} \, D_{K}}{D_{AB} + D_{K}}$, a harmonic mean — a standard KTU derivation question.

### Mechanism 4 — Gravity Drainage

In thick beds of granular solids, liquid may flow downward under gravity. Significant only when pore size is large and drying rate is low. Often neglected in tray-drying analysis.

### Mechanism 5 — Thermal Diffusion (Soret Effect)

Vapour migrates from a hot region to a cold region within the solid. Generally a **minor contributor** compared to capillary and vapour diffusion in standard drying operations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Mechanism** | **Governing Equation** | **Key Parameter** | **Dominant In** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capillary Flow | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_{p}}$ | Pore radius $r_{p}$ | Constant rate & 1st falling rate |
| Liquid Diffusion (Fick's Law) | $\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{L} \dfrac{\partial^{2} X}{\partial x^{2}}$ | $D_{L}$ (m²/s) | Hygroscopic, non-porous solids |
| Vapour Molecular Diffusion | $N_{A} = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_{A}}{dx}$ | $D_{AB}$ (m²/s) | Large pores (2nd falling rate) |
| Knudsen Diffusion | $D_{K} = 97 \, r_{p} \sqrt{T/M_{A}}$ | $D_{K}$ (m²/s) | Fine pores, low X |
| Combined Vapour Diffusion | $D_{eff} = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, D_{K}}{D_{AB} + D_{K}}$ | $D_{eff}$ | Porous solids (general) |
| Capillary Diffusion | $\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{cap} \nabla^{2} X$ | $D_{cap}$ (m²/s) | Porous, capillary active solids |
| Critical Point Identification | $X = X^{*}$ | $X^{*}$ (kg/kg dry) | Transition criterion |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Drying**: Granule beds and coated tablets — capillary and liquid diffusion dictate residence time in fluidized bed dryers.
- **Food Processing**: Milk powder, fruit drying — bound moisture removal via falling-rate analysis sets dryer dimensions.
- **Ceramics & Paper**: Porous solids where capillary forces dominate the early stage — wet-bulb temperature assumption holds.
- **Polymer Processing**: Non-porous hygroscopic films — only liquid diffusion applies, leading to long drying cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Vapour Diffusivity in a Porous Solid

### Problem Statement
Derive the expression for the effective diffusion coefficient of a vapour diffusing through a fine capillary pore when **both molecular and Knudsen diffusion** operate simultaneously.

### Derivation

Consider a single cylindrical capillary of radius $r_{p}$ and length $L$. Vapour flux $N_{A}$ occurs through it. The two diffusion resistances are in **series** (one is bulk gas-phase resistance, the other is wall-collision resistance).

**Step 1:** Express the molar flux by molecular diffusion:

$$
N_{A,mol} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_{A}}{dx}
$$

**Step 2:** Express the molar flux by Knudsen diffusion (molecule-wall collisions dominate):

$$
N_{A,K} = -D_{K} \frac{dC_{A}}{dx}
$$

**Step 3:** The net flux must equal the **smaller** of the two. Treating them as **independent parallel paths** but combining via the **harmonic mean** (because their contributions are additive at each cross-section but their effective driving forces are equal):

$$
\frac{1}{D_{eff}} = \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_{K}}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$
D_{eff} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_{K}}{D_{AB} + D_{K}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the Knudsen diffusion expression $D_{K} = 97 \, r_{p} \sqrt{T / M_{A}}$:

$$
D_{eff} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot \left(97 \, r_{p} \sqrt{T / M_{A}}\right)}{D_{AB} + 97 \, r_{p} \sqrt{T / M_{A}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting cases:**
> - When $D_{K} \gg D_{AB}$ (large pores) → $D_{eff} \to D_{AB}$ (molecular diffusion dominates).
> - When $D_{AB} \gg D_{K}$ (fine pores) → $D_{eff} \to D_{K}$ (Knudsen diffusion dominates).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capillary Pressure Derivation (Young–Laplace)

### Problem Statement
Derive the capillary pressure difference across a curved meniscus in a cylindrical pore.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a spherical meniscus of radius $R$ inside a cylindrical pore of radius $r_{p}$. Geometrically:

$$
R = \frac{r_{p}}{\cos\theta}
$$

where $\theta$ is the contact angle.

**Step 2:** Surface tension $\sigma$ acts along the perimeter of the meniscus. The total upward force on the liquid column:

$$
F_{up} = (2\pi r_{p}) \, \sigma \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3:** The capillary suction pressure acts over the cross-sectional area $A = \pi r_{p}^{2}$:

$$
F_{down} = \Delta P_{cap} \cdot \pi r_{p}^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Equating $F_{up} = F_{down}$:

$$
\Delta P_{cap} \cdot \pi r_{p}^{2} = 2\pi r_{p} \, \sigma \cos\theta
$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $\Delta P_{cap}$:

$$
\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_{p}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows that **smaller pores generate larger capillary suction**, explaining why water preferentially resides in fine pores and why moisture redistributes from coarse to fine pores during drying.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Liquid Diffusion Solution (Semi-Infinite Solid, KTU Favourite)

### Problem Statement
For a slab of thickness $2L$ with initial uniform moisture $X_{0}$ and surface suddenly brought to $X_{s}$, derive the average moisture content at time $t$ assuming liquid diffusion control.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Fick's second law in 1D:

$$
\frac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_{L} \frac{\partial^{2} X}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

**Step 2:** Initial and boundary conditions:

$$
X(x, 0) = X_{0}, \quad X(L, t) = X_{s}, \quad \frac{\partial X}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Introduce dimensionless variables:

$$
\theta = \frac{X - X_{s}}{X_{0} - X_{s}}, \quad \zeta = \frac{x}{L}, \quad Fo = \frac{D_{L} t}{L^{2}}
$$

**Step 4:** The transformed PDE:

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial Fo} = \frac{\partial^{2} \theta}{\partial \zeta^{2}}
$$

**Step 5:** Separating variables, the series solution is:

$$
\theta(\zeta, Fo) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} \sin\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi}{2}\zeta\right] \exp\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}}{4} Fo\right]
$$

**Step 6:** The average moisture content across the slab is:

$$
\bar{\theta}(Fo) = \frac{\bar{X} - X_{s}}{X_{0} - X_{s}} = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^{2}} \exp\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}}{4} Fo\right]
$$

**Step 7:** For long times ($Fo > 0.1$), only the first term matters:

$$
\frac{\bar{X} - X_{s}}{X_{0} - X_{s}} \approx \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^{2}}{4} Fo\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Fourier number** $Fo = D_{L} t / L^{2}$ is a dimensionless time widely used in KTU numerical problems to estimate drying time in the falling-rate period.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Constant Rate Period** | **Falling Rate Period** |
|---|---|---|
| Surface Condition | Fully wet | Partially or fully dry |
| Evaporation Location | At surface | At surface → recedes inside |
| Controlling Resistance | External gas-phase mass transfer | Internal moisture movement |
| Surface Temperature | Wet-bulb temperature | Approaches dry-bulb temperature |
| Dominant Mechanism | Capillary flow | Liquid diffusion + Vapour diffusion (Knudsen) |
| Governing Equation | $N = k_{y}(Y_{s} - Y)$ | $\partial X/\partial t = D_{L} \partial^{2} X / \partial x^{2}$ |
| Rate Behaviour | Independent of X | Function of X (often linear with X − X\*) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Moisture Movement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WET SOLID WITH MOISTURE X"] --> B{"MOISTURE CONTENT CHECK"}
    B -- "X GREATER THAN X STAR" --> C["CONSTANT RATE PERIOD"]
    B -- "X LESS THAN X STAR" --> D["FALLING RATE PERIOD"]
    
    C --> C1["SURFACE FULLY WET"]
    C1 --> C2["EVAPORATION AT SURFACE"]
    C2 --> C3["CAPILLARY FLOW REPLENISHES SURFACE"]
    C3 --> C4["EXTERNAL MASS TRANSFER CONTROLS"]
    
    D --> D1{"INTERNAL MOISTURE STATE"}
    D1 -- "BOUND IN PORES" --> D2["LIQUID DIFFUSION FICK LAW"]
    D1 -- "EVAPORATED INSIDE" --> D3["VAPOUR DIFFUSION"]
    D1 -- "FINE PORES" --> D4["KNUDSEN DIFFUSION"]
    D1 -- "PORE NETWORK" --> D5["CAPILLARY DIFFUSION"]
    
    D2 --> D6["INTERNAL RESISTANCE CONTROLS"]
    D3 --> D6
    D4 --> D6
    D5 --> D6
    D6 --> D7["DRY SOLID WITH X EQUAL TO X STAR"]
```

## 4.2 Receding Evaporation Front Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["DRY OUTER LAYER"] --> P2["RECEDING EVAPORATION FRONT"]
    P2 --> P3["WET INNER CORE"]
    P3 --> P4["UNBOUND MOISTURE REGION"]
    
    P1 -- "VAPOUR DIFFUSION OUT" --> OUT["EXIT TO GAS PHASE"]
    P2 -- "PHASE CHANGE LIQUID TO VAPOUR" --> P1
    P3 -- "LIQUID FLOW TOWARD FRONT" --> P2
    P4 -- "CAPILLARY SUPPLY" --> P3
```

## 4.3 Drying Period Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["START DRYING AT X ZERO"] --> A1["CONSTANT RATE PERIOD"]
    A1 --> A2["REACH CRITICAL X STAR"]
    A2 --> B1["FIRST FALLING RATE"]
    B1 --> B2["SECOND FALLING RATE"]
    B2 --> C1["APPROACH EQUILIBRIUM X STAR"]
    C1 --> D1["DRYING STOPS AT X EQUAL TO X STAR"]
    
    A1 -- "SURFACE WET" --> A1
    B1 -- "SURFACE PARTIALLY DRY" --> B1
    B2 -- "SURFACE COMPLETELY DRY" --> B2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**"Differentiate between bound and unbound moisture in a solid."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Unbound Moisture (1 Mark):** Moisture in a solid in excess of the equilibrium moisture content. It exerts a vapour pressure equal to that of pure water at the same temperature. It is held loosely in capillaries and evaporates readily.
- **Bound Moisture (1 Mark):** Moisture retained by the solid when its vapour pressure is less than that of pure water at the same temperature. It is held by physical adsorption in capillaries or by chemical bonds; requires extra energy for removal.
- **Distinction (1 Mark):** Unbound moisture evaporates at the wet-bulb temperature in constant-rate drying; bound moisture removal controls the falling-rate period and depends on solid–liquid interaction.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is Knudsen diffusion? Under what conditions does it dominate?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Knudsen diffusion is the transport of gas/vapour molecules through fine pores where the mean free path of the molecules is comparable to or greater than the pore diameter; molecule–wall collisions dominate over molecule–molecule collisions.
- **Governing Equation (1 Mark):** $D_{K} = 97 \, r_{p} \sqrt{T / M_{A}}$
- **Dominance Condition (1 Mark):** When $r_{p} \le \lambda$ (pore radius ≤ mean free path, typically < 100 nm for gases at atmospheric conditions), or in **low moisture content** regions of porous solids in the second falling-rate period.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often confuse **capillary action** (bulk liquid flow in interconnected pores) with **liquid diffusion** (molecular-level movement in non-porous matrices). Clearly distinguish them in your answer.
> - In derivation questions, marks are awarded for **writing the differential equation WITH its initial and boundary conditions**. Skipping IC/BC loses 2–3 marks.
> - For "mechanism" questions, **mention the critical moisture content** and explicitly state how mechanism changes at $X = X^{*}$. This is the most-skipped high-weightage point.
> - Do not write vague statements like "water moves due to heat". Always specify **capillary pressure**, **concentration gradient**, or **vapour pressure gradient** as the driving force.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Module 4 Question]**

**(a) Explain in detail the various mechanisms by which moisture moves through a solid during drying. State clearly how the dominant mechanism changes at the critical moisture content. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):** Moisture movement inside a wet solid during drying is a transport process driven by gradients in moisture content, capillary pressure, vapour pressure, temperature, and gravity. The dominant mechanism depends on the type of solid and the moisture content range.

**2. Five Mechanisms (4 Marks — 0.8 each):**

- **(i) Capillary Movement:** Free water in interconnected pores is drawn toward the drying surface by capillary suction. The driving force is the **Laplace pressure** $\Delta P_{cap} = 2\sigma \cos\theta / r_{p}$. Smaller pores exert stronger suction. Dominant in the **constant rate period** and early falling rate.
- **(ii) Liquid Diffusion:** Bound water moves through the solid matrix by molecular diffusion governed by **Fick's law** $\partial X / \partial t = D_{L} \partial^{2} X / \partial x^{2}$. Dominant in **hygroscopic, non-porous solids** (soap, polymers, food).
- **(iii) Vapour Diffusion:** Liquid water evaporates inside pores and the vapour diffuses outwards via ordinary **molecular diffusion** $N_{A} = -D_{AB} \, dC_{A}/dx$. Dominant in the **second falling rate period** when the surface is dry.
- **(iv) Knudsen Diffusion:** When pores are very fine ($r_{p} < \lambda$), vapour molecules collide more with pore walls than with each other, governed by $D_{K} = 97 \, r_{p}\sqrt{T/M_{A}}$. Dominant in low-moisture regions of microporous solids (catalysts, ceramics).
- **(v) Gravity Drainage:** Liquid may flow downward under gravity in thick, coarse beds. Usually **minor** in tray and rotary drying.

**3. Change at Critical Moisture Content (2 Marks):**
- For $X > X^{*}$: surface is saturated; evaporation occurs only at the surface. **Capillary movement** supplies water fast enough; rate is governed by **external mass transfer**.
- For $X < X^{*}$: surface becomes unsaturated. The evaporation front recedes inside. Mechanisms shift progressively from **capillary + liquid diffusion** to **vapour diffusion / Knudsen diffusion**. **Internal resistance** controls the rate.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the effective diffusion coefficient when both molecular and Knudsen diffusion occur simultaneously in a porous solid. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Consider a cylindrical pore of radius $r_{p}$. Vapour A diffuses through stagnant air B.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Molecular flux by bulk diffusion: $N_{A,mol} = -D_{AB} \, dC_{A}/dx$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Knudsen flux by wall-collision: $N_{A,K} = -D_{K} \, dC_{A}/dx$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Since both mechanisms act in **parallel** along the same concentration gradient, total flux is:

$$
N_{A} = -(D_{AB} + D_{K}) \frac{dC_{A}}{dx}
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Defining the effective diffusivity $D_{eff}$ such that $N_{A} = -D_{eff} \, dC_{A}/dx$:

$$
D_{eff} = D_{AB} + D_{K}
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Substituting $D_{K} = 97 \, r_{p} \sqrt{T/M_{A}}$:

$$
D_{eff} = D_{AB} + 97 \, r_{p} \sqrt{\frac{T}{M_{A}}}
$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** **Discussion of limiting cases:**
- $D_{K} \gg D_{AB}$ (large pores) → $D_{eff} \approx D_{AB}$
- $D_{AB} \gg D_{K}$ (fine pores) → $D_{eff} \approx D_{K}$
- **In practice**, when pore structure adds series resistance to the parallel bulk paths (e.g., tortuous pores with constrictions), the harmonic mean form $D_{eff} = D_{AB} D_{K} / (D_{AB} + D_{K})$ is also used — explain both forms in the answer for full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> The **parallel-sum** form is the standard answer for diffusion **through a single straight pore**. The **harmonic-mean** form applies when both bulk and Knudsen resistances act **in series** along the diffusion path (more realistic for tortuous porous solids). KTU examiners accept both with proper justification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, describe the mechanism of moisture movement during the falling rate drying period. Distinguish clearly between the first and second falling rate periods. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Sketch (2 Marks):** Draw a porous solid showing:
- Dry outer layer (no moisture)
- Receding evaporation plane (vapour–liquid interface)
- Wet inner core (liquid water in capillaries)

**2. First Falling Rate Period (2.5 Marks):**
- Surface is partially dry — wet patches exist intermittently.
- Evaporation occurs partly at the surface and partly at the receding plane.
- **Two mechanisms operate simultaneously:** liquid diffusion through the partially dry surface layer and capillary flow from the wet core.
- The wetted surface area shrinks progressively. Rate decreases roughly linearly with $(X - X^{*})$.

**3. Second Falling Rate Period (2.5 Marks):**
- Surface is **completely dry** throughout this period.
- All evaporation occurs at an **internal evaporation front** that recedes into the solid.
- Moisture transport is now exclusively as **vapour diffusion** (molecular + Knudsen) through the dry outer layer.
- Rate is controlled by **internal mass transfer resistance** of the dry shell.

**Distinction Table (already covered in Section 3.4 above).**

---

**(b) A wet solid is dried from an initial moisture content of 0.40 kg water/kg dry solid to a final value of 0.05 kg/kg under constant drying conditions. The critical moisture content is 0.18 kg/kg and the equilibrium moisture content is 0.02 kg/kg. The constant rate is $N_{c} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s. Calculate (i) the time for the constant rate period and (ii) the time for the falling rate period, assuming a linear falling-rate relation. The drying surface area is 0.5 m² and the dry solid mass is 5 kg. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$X_{1} = 0.40$, $X_{2} = 0.05$, $X^{*} = 0.18$, $X^{*} = 0.02$, $N_{c} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s, $A = 0.5$ m², $M_{s} = 5$ kg.

**Part (i) — Constant Rate Time (3 Marks):**

Rate of moisture removal in constant rate period = $N_{c} \cdot A$

$$
t_{c} = \frac{M_{s} (X_{1} - X^{*})}{N_{c} \cdot A}
$$

$$
t_{c} = \frac{5 \times (0.40 - 0.18)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5}
$$

$$
t_{c} = \frac{5 \times 0.22}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.10}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
t_{c} = 1.467 \times 10^{4} \text{ s} \approx 14667 \text{ s} \approx 4.07 \text{ hours}
$$

**Part (ii) — Falling Rate Time (4 Marks):**

For linear falling rate behaviour:

$$
N = N_{c} \left(\frac{X - X^{*}}{X^{*} - X^{*}}\right)
$$

Time for falling rate period:

$$
t_{f} = \frac{M_{s}(X^{*} - X_{2})}{N_{c} \cdot A} \ln\left(\frac{X^{*} - X^{*}}{X_{2} - X^{*}}\right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
t_{f} = \frac{5 \times (0.18 - 0.05)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5} \ln\left(\frac{0.18 - 0.02}{0.05 - 0.02}\right)
$$

$$
t_{f} = \frac{5 \times 0.13}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}} \ln\left(\frac{0.16}{0.03}\right)
$$

$$
t_{f} = \frac{0.65}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}} \times \ln(5.333)
$$

$$
t_{f} = 8666.67 \times 1.674
$$

$$
t_{f} \approx 14510 \text{ s} \approx 4.03 \text{ hours}
$$

**Total Drying Time:**

$$
t_{total} = t_{c} + t_{f} \approx 14667 + 14510 \approx 29177 \text{ s} \approx 8.1 \text{ hours}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanism of Moisture Movement**

- ☐ Moisture movement inside a solid occurs by **five mechanisms**: capillary flow, liquid diffusion, vapour diffusion, Knudsen diffusion, and gravity drainage.
- ☐ In the **constant rate period**, the surface is wet; **capillary action** supplies water; **external mass transfer** controls the rate.
- ☐ In the **falling rate period**, the surface dries; **internal moisture movement** (liquid + vapour diffusion) controls the rate.
- ☐ The **first falling rate** has both surface and internal evaporation; the **second falling rate** has evaporation only at an internal receding front.
- ☐ **Capillary pressure** $\Delta P_{cap} = 2\sigma\cos\theta / r_{p}$ — smaller pores exert larger suction.
- ☐ **Fick's Law** for liquid diffusion: $\partial X / \partial t = D_{L} \, \partial^{2} X / \partial x^{2}$.
- ☐ **Knudsen diffusivity** $D_{K} = 97 \, r_{p}\sqrt{T/M_{A}}$ dominates in **fine pores** (mean free path > pore radius).
- ☐ **Critical moisture content $X^{*}$** is the transition point between constant and falling rate periods.
- ☐ **Equilibrium moisture content $X^{*}$** is the lower limit of drying under given air conditions.
- ☐ The **Fourier number** $Fo = D_{L} t / L^{2}$ governs dimensionless drying time in the falling rate.
- ☐ For non-porous hygroscopic solids, **only liquid diffusion** applies; no capillary mechanism.
- ☐ For porous, non-hygroscopic solids (sand, ceramics), **capillary + vapour diffusion** dominate.
- ☐ The **harmonic mean** effective diffusivity form $D_{eff} = D_{AB} D_{K}/(D_{AB} + D_{K})$ applies when bulk and Knudsen resistances are in **series**; the **sum form** applies when they act in **parallel**.
- ☐ **Gravity drainage** is significant only in thick, coarse granular beds; often neglected in standard KTU problems.
- ☐ Surface temperature equals **wet-bulb temperature** during constant rate and approaches **dry-bulb temperature** during the second falling rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Continuous Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Continuous Drying

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Continuous Drying** is an industrial drying operation in which the wet solid material is continuously fed into the dryer at a steady rate and the dried product is continuously discharged, while a continuous stream of drying gas (usually hot air or flue gas) is brought into contact with the solid. The system operates at **steady state**, meaning the inlet and outlet conditions (temperatures, humidities, moisture contents) remain constant with respect to time at any fixed location inside the equipment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, continuous drying falls under the broader classification of **drying as a simultaneous heat and mass transfer operation** and is treated as an extension of the batch drying analysis (Module 3) to a steady-state, plug-flow configuration. The moisture carried by the solid and the heat supplied by the gas interact in a continuous transfer zone (or a series of equilibrium stages for tunnel/belt dryers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**: Continuous drying operations, material and energy balance in continuous dryers, the theory of through-circulation drying, hold-up in rotary dryers, and the design equations of common continuous dryers (rotary, tunnel, belt, spray, fluidized, drum, pneumatic) form the core of this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **laundromat's industrial drying line** versus the small **household dryer** you have at home.

- In the **household (batch) dryer**, you put wet clothes in, close the door, run a timer, and after a fixed time (say 60 minutes) you open the door to take out dry clothes. Time is the controlling variable. This is **batch drying**.
- In an **industrial laundromat's continuous drying line**, wet clothes are hung on a moving conveyor at one end. The conveyor slowly moves them through a long tunnel of hot air, and they come out fully dry at the other end. The clothes that enter at time 9:00 AM will exit at 9:30 AM, but the *process* keeps running, and another batch enters at 9:00:01 AM. The dryer never stops, and the conditions at every point of the tunnel remain constant. This is **continuous drying**.

The same logic applies to drying fertilizer granules, milk powder, wheat, ceramics, or pigments in a chemical plant. The key signature is **steady state + plug flow + continuous feed/discharge**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Defining Characteristics of Continuous Drying**
> 1. **Steady-state operation** – conditions at any point in the dryer do not change with time.
> 2. **Continuous feed and discharge** – material enters and leaves at a constant mass flow rate.
> 3. **Spatial, not temporal, control** – residence time is a function of position/length rather than elapsed clock time.

## 1.3 Why Continuous Drying? — Engineering Justification

| Criterion | Batch Drying | Continuous Drying |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput | Low | **High** (mass flow rate scale-up) |
| Labour | High (loading/unloading) | **Low** (automated) |
| Heat utilisation | Poor (start-up losses) | **Excellent** (steady-state, no cyclic heating) |
| Product uniformity | Variable (top–bottom differences) | **Uniform** (every element sees same time–temperature history) |
| Floor area | Large per unit mass | **Compact** for same capacity |
| Capital cost | Lower (small scale) | **Higher** (large initial investment) |

For large-tonnage operations (e.g., cement clinker cooling, fertilizer granulation, food powders), **continuous drying is the only economically viable option**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU convention)**
> - **Moisture content (dry basis)**: $X$ = kg water / kg dry solid (dimensionless, can exceed 1)
> - **Moisture content (wet basis)**: $X_w$ = kg water / kg wet solid (always between 0 and 1)
> - **Free moisture content**: $X^*$ = $X - X^*$ where $X^*$ is the equilibrium moisture at the operating gas humidity
> - **Residence time $\theta$** in seconds or minutes
> - **Hold-up $H$** = mass of material inside the dryer at any instant (kg)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Continuous Dryers

Continuous dryers are classified based on the **mode of contact** between the gas and the solid.

1. **Direct / Convective Dryers** (gas contacts solid directly)
   - **Through-circulation dryers** – gas passes *through* a packed/perforated bed of particles (belt, conveyor, screen-conveyor dryers).
   - **Fluidized-bed dryers** – gas velocity exceeds the incipient fluidization velocity; particles are suspended.
   - **Pneumatic / Flash dryers** – gas transports particles in dilute phase.
   - **Spray dryers** – liquid feed is atomized into a hot gas stream.
   - **Rotary dryers** – tumbling action in a rotating inclined cylinder.
   - **Tunnel / Truck dryers** – product on trays moves through a tunnel (intermediate between batch and continuous).

2. **Indirect / Conductive Dryers** (heat transferred through a wall)
   - **Drum dryers** – rotating heated drum picks up a thin liquid film.
   - **Screw-conveyor dryers** – heated screw shaft.
   - **Steam-tube rotary dryers** – internal steam tubes inside a rotating shell.

3. **Radiant / Dielectric Dryers** (less common in MTO-1 scope).

## 2.2 Material Balance in a Continuous Dryer

At steady state, the moisture entering with the wet feed must equal the moisture leaving with the dry product plus the moisture picked up by the drying gas.

**Notation (KTU convention):**
- $F_s$ = mass flow rate of *dry* solid (kg dry solid / s)
- $X_1, X_2$ = moisture contents (dry basis) of feed and product
- $G$ = mass flow rate of *dry* gas (kg dry gas / s)
- $H_1, H_2$ = absolute humidity of inlet and exit gas (kg water / kg dry gas)
- $W$ = evaporation rate (kg water / s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Material Balance Identity**
> The *dry solid flow rate $F_s$* is the true invariant in a dryer (assuming no loss of solid). Moisture comes and goes; the bone-dry solid backbone does not.

### Water (moisture) balance on the solid stream:
$$ W = F_s (X_1 - X_2) $$

### Water balance on the gas stream:
$$ W = G (H_2 - H_1) $$

Equating both:
$$ F_s (X_1 - X_2) = G (H_2 - H_1) $$

## 2.3 Energy Balance in a Continuous Dryer

The heat supplied by the heater $Q$ is consumed in:
1. Heating the wet solid from inlet $T_{s1}$ to outlet $T_{s2}$.
2. Evaporating the moisture at the product temperature.
3. Heating the evaporated moisture (as vapour) from the surface temperature to the exit gas temperature.
4. Sensible heat of the gas.
5. Heat losses to the surroundings $Q_L$.

The simplified **KTU-standard** steady-state energy balance (referenced to an adiabatic saturation temperature or a chosen datum) is:

$$ Q = F_s \left[ (c_{ps} + c_{pw} X_1)(T_{s2} - T_{s1}) + (X_1 - X_2)\lambda_{s2} \right] + Q_L $$

For a *thorough* balance on the gas side:

$$ Q = G (1 + H_1) c_{H1} (T_{g1} - T_{g0}) $$

where $c_{H1}$ is the humid heat of the inlet gas and $T_{g0}$ is the ambient gas temperature entering the heater.

## 2.4 Through-Circulation (Through-Drying) Theory — The Core Analytical Tool

In through-circulation drying, the gas flows *perpendicularly* through a thin, well-mixed bed of particles, so the **external resistance to mass transfer is negligible**. The drying rate is governed almost entirely by the **internal (diffusion) resistance** inside the porous particle.

### The Continuous Through-Drying Equation

For a plug-flow continuous dryer, the local drying rate at any point depends on the free moisture content of the solid at that point. Integrating over the residence time gives the design equation:

$$ \theta = \frac{\rho_s L}{G'} \int_{X_2^*}^{X_1^*} \frac{dX^*}{N_A(X^*)} $$

where:
- $\theta$ = residence time (s)
- $\rho_s$ = bulk density of dry solid in the bed (kg/m³)
- $L$ = thickness of the bed (m)
- $G'$ = superficial mass velocity of gas (kg dry gas / s·m²)
- $N_A(X^*)$ = local drying flux (kg water / s·m²) as a function of free moisture
- $X^*, X_1^*, X_2^*$ = local, inlet, outlet free moisture (dry basis)

For the **constant-rate (free water) period**, $N_A$ is independent of $X^*$:

$$ N_A = \frac{h_{fg}\,(T_g - T_s)}{\lambda_s} \cdot \text{(mass-transfer factor)} \quad \approx \frac{h(T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_s} $$

For the **falling-rate period**, $N_A$ decreases with $X^*$. A common empirical form (linear) is:

$$ N_A = N_{A,cr} \left( \frac{X^* - X_{eq}^*}{X_{cr}^* - X_{eq}^*} \right) $$

This is the basis of the **graphical design method** in McCabe–Smith–Harriott and Treybal.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Through-Circulation" is Exam Favourite**: It reduces a 2-D batch drying problem to a 1-D steady-state integral that has a clean closed-form in each drying period. KTU frequently asks for the **residence time and the bed area** for a given throughput.

## 2.5 Hold-up in Rotary and Belt Dryers

### Rotary Dryer Hold-up
The mass of solid held inside a rotating cylinder at any instant:

$$ H = \frac{G_s \cdot \theta}{60} \quad \text{(kg)} $$

where $G_s$ is the feed rate in kg/h and $\theta$ is the residence time in minutes.

The residence time in a rotary dryer can be estimated by the **Friedman–Marshall equation**:

$$ \theta = \frac{0.23\, L}{S\, D^{0.5}\, N^{0.9}} \cdot \frac{1}{\tan \alpha} $$

where:
- $L$ = length of cylinder (m)
- $D$ = diameter (m)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- $S$ = slope (m/m)
- $\alpha$ = angle of repose of the solid (°)

### Belt Dryer Hold-up
For a perforated belt moving at linear speed $U$ carrying a bed of height $L$ and bulk density $\rho_b$:

$$ H = \rho_b \, L \, W \, L_{belt} \quad ; \quad \theta = \frac{L_{belt}}{U} $$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to keep tables safe in markdown. The following table collects every formula a student is likely to need in a Module 4 KTU examination.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wet basis $\rightarrow$ Dry basis | $X = \dfrac{X_w}{1 - X_w}$ | kg/kg | Always evaluate before use |
| 2 | Dry basis $\rightarrow$ Wet basis | $X_w = \dfrac{X}{1 + X}$ | kg/kg | For reporting in problem statement |
| 3 | Evaporation rate | $W = F_s(X_1 - X_2)$ | kg/s | Mass balance on water |
| 4 | Gas-side water balance | $W = G(H_2 - H_1)$ | kg/s | Use dry-gas flow $G$ |
| 5 | Humid heat of gas | $c_H = c_{pg} + H \cdot c_{pv}$ | kJ/(kg·K) | $c_{pv} \approx 1.88$ kJ/(kg·K) |
| 6 | Energy balance (gas side) | $Q = G(1+H_1)c_{H1}(T_{g1}-T_{g0})$ | kW | Heater duty |
| 7 | Energy balance (solid side) | $Q = F_s[(c_{ps}+c_{pw}X_1)(T_{s2}-T_{s1})+(X_1-X_2)\lambda_{s2}]+Q_L$ | kW | Used when $Q$ is not given |
| 8 | Constant-rate drying flux | $N_A = \dfrac{h(T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/(m²·s) | $T_w$ = wet-bulb temperature |
| 9 | Linear falling-rate flux | $N_A = N_{A,cr}\!\left(\dfrac{X^*-X_{eq}^*}{X_{cr}^*-X_{eq}^*}\right)$ | kg/(m²·s) | Valid for $X^* < X_{cr}^*$ |
| 10 | Through-drying residence time | $\theta = \dfrac{\rho_s L}{G'}\displaystyle\int_{X_2^*}^{X_1^*}\dfrac{dX^*}{N_A(X^*)}$ | s | The master design equation |
| 11 | Closed-form for constant-rate period | $\theta_{cr} = \dfrac{\rho_s L (X_1^* - X_{cr}^*)}{G' N_A}$ | s | Integrate above with const $N_A$ |
| 12 | Closed-form for falling-rate period | $\theta_f = \dfrac{\rho_s L (X_{cr}^*-X_{eq}^*)}{G' N_{A,cr}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_{cr}^*-X_{eq}^*}{X_2^*-X_{eq}^*}\right)$ | s | Requires $X_2^* > X_{eq}^*$ |
| 13 | Rotary dryer hold-up | $H = G_s \theta / 60$ | kg | $G_s$ in kg/h, $\theta$ in min |
| 14 | Friedman–Marshall residence time | $\theta = \dfrac{0.23 L}{S D^{0.5} N^{0.9} \tan \alpha}$ | min | Empirical, rotary only |
| 15 | Through-circulation bed area | $A = \dfrac{F_s(X_1-X_2)}{N_{A,\text{avg}}}$ | m² | Use the average flux for design |
| 16 | Belt dryer residence time | $\theta = L_{belt}/U$ | s | $U$ = belt linear speed (m/s) |
| 17 | Evaporative capacity | $E_c = W / A$ | kg/(m²·h) | Benchmark for dryer sizing |
| 18 | Spray dryer evaporation (Bond's) | $D_{50} \propto (F^{0.5}/(\Delta T N_v)^{0.6})$ | μm | For atomizer selection |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fertilizer industry** – NPK granules dried in **rotary co-current dryers** to avoid overheating of the heat-sensitive urea.
- **Food industry** – Milk, coffee, and detergent powders produced in **spray dryers** with strict control of particle size and residual moisture.
- **Cement industry** – Clinker cooling in **rotary coolers** and preheater tower dryers handling > 5000 TPD.
- **Pharmaceutical industry** – **fluidized-bed dryers** for granules because of uniform product temperature and short residence time.
- **Ceramics & pigments** – **belt / tunnel dryers** for shaped articles requiring gentle handling and uniform drying.
- **Mineral industry** – **pneumatic (flash) dryers** for filter cakes and sludges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connecting to the KTU Course Outcome (CO) Map**: This topic directly addresses **CO4 — "Apply the principles of mass transfer to design drying and crystallization equipment"** (2024 Scheme).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Master Material Balance on a Generic Continuous Dryer

**Statement**: In a continuous dryer operating at steady state, prove that the evaporation rate equals $W = F_s (X_1 - X_2) = G (H_2 - H_1)$.

**Step 1 — Define control volume.**
Draw an imaginary boundary around the entire dryer. Inside, gas and solid coexist and exchange heat and mass. Steady state implies zero accumulation of mass or energy inside the boundary.

**Step 2 — Water carried by the solid stream at the inlet.**
Solid enters carrying $F_s$ kg/s of dry bone-dry solid plus $F_s X_1$ kg/s of water.

**Step 3 — Water carried by the solid stream at the outlet.**
Solid leaves with $F_s$ kg/s of dry solid plus $F_s X_2$ kg/s of water.

**Step 4 — Net water lost by the solid per second.**
$$ \text{Water leaving with product} = F_s X_2 $$
$$ \text{Water entering with feed} = F_s X_1 $$

Since the *dry* solid flow is invariant:

$$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net water transferred solid $\rightarrow$ gas} &= F_s X_1 - F_s X_2 \\ &= F_s (X_1 - X_2) \end{aligned} $$

**Step 5 — Water carried by the gas stream at the inlet and outlet.**
Gas enters with $G$ kg/s of dry gas plus $G H_1$ kg/s of water vapour.
Gas exits with $G$ kg/s of dry gas plus $G H_2$ kg/s of water vapour.

$$ \text{Net water gained by the gas per second} = G (H_2 - H_1) $$

**Step 6 — Apply conservation of water (steady state $\Rightarrow$ net accumulation = 0).**

$$ \begin{aligned} \text{Water gained by gas} &= \text{Water lost by solid} \\ G (H_2 - H_1) &= F_s (X_1 - X_2) \end{aligned} $$

**Step 7 — Define evaporation rate $W$.**
By definition, the evaporation rate is the rate at which liquid water is converted to vapour and removed from the solid:

$$ \boxed{\, W = F_s (X_1 - X_2) = G (H_2 - H_1) \,} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Through-Circulation Drying Equation

**Statement**: For a continuous through-circulation dryer with a bed of thickness $L$ and bulk dry density $\rho_s$, derive the residence time required to dry the solid from a free moisture $X_1^*$ to $X_2^*$.

**Step 1 — Local flux formulation.**
At any plane $z$ along the dryer, the local drying flux (kg water leaving per m² of bed cross-section per second) is $N_A(z)$. By the **definition of a flux per unit bed area**:

$$ N_A = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{water removed from layer}}}{dA_{\text{bed}}} $$

**Step 2 — Material balance on a differential slice of the bed.**
Consider a thin slice of the bed of thickness $dz$ and unit cross-sectional area $A=1$ m². The mass of *dry* solid in this slice is $\rho_s \, dz$ (kg dry solid / m² of bed). The local moisture content of the solid at $z$ is $X^*(z)$.

The rate at which the slice loses moisture (in the *frame of the moving solid*, i.e., for a particle traversing the slice) equals the flux $N_A(X^*)$.

In terms of the time $\theta$ that a particle has spent in the dryer (the residence time), the local moisture balance is:

$$ \rho_s \, dz \cdot \frac{dX^*}{d\theta} = -N_A(X^*) $$

**Step 3 — Convert the spatial variable $z$ to residence time $\theta$.**
The plug-flow assumption implies each particle moves through the dryer at a uniform linear speed, so the time $\theta$ spent between inlet and a point at axial position $z$ is monotonic. Therefore $dz$ and $d\theta$ are linearly related, and $dz$ cancels in the integral form. We integrate directly in the $(X^*, \theta)$ plane.

**Step 4 — Separation of variables.**

$$ \begin{aligned} d\theta &= -\frac{\rho_s \, dz}{N_A(X^*)} \\[4pt] \theta &= \int_0^{L} \frac{\rho_s \, dz}{N_A(X^*)} = \rho_s \int_0^L \frac{dz}{N_A(X^*)} \end{aligned} $$

But $X^*$ varies with $z$, so we convert the integration variable:

$$ \theta = \rho_s \int_{X_1^*}^{X_2^*} \frac{1}{N_A(X^*)} \cdot \frac{dz}{dX^*} \, dX^* $$

For the through-circulation configuration with superficial gas mass velocity $G'$ and assuming the gas picks up moisture at the same rate the solid loses it, a *macroscopic* differential balance over a length $dz$ of the bed gives:

$$ G' \, dH = -\rho_s \, dX^* \quad (\text{on a per m}^2 \text{basis, gas picks up what solid loses}) $$

Hence $\dfrac{dz}{dX^*} = -\dfrac{\rho_s}{G'}$ (using the relation between gas humidity and solid moisture change).

**Step 5 — Final integral.**

$$ \begin{aligned} \theta &= \rho_s \int_{X_1^*}^{X_2^*} \frac{1}{N_A(X^*)} \cdot \left(-\frac{\rho_s}{G'}\right) dX^* \\[4pt] &= \frac{\rho_s^2}{G'} \int_{X_2^*}^{X_1^*} \frac{dX^*}{N_A(X^*)} \end{aligned} $$

For the special (and KTU-favourite) case where the bed thickness $L$ is the controlling variable, this expression is more commonly written in the **layered form**:

$$ \boxed{\, \theta = \frac{\rho_s L}{G'} \int_{X_2^*}^{X_1^*} \frac{dX^*}{N_A(X^*)} \,} $$

**Step 6 — Closed-form for the constant-rate period.**
In the constant-rate period, $N_A = N_{A,cr}$ = constant. The integral collapses to a rectangle:

$$ \theta_{cr} = \frac{\rho_s L (X_1^* - X_{cr}^*)}{G' N_{A,cr}} $$

**Step 7 — Closed-form for the linear falling-rate period.**
For the linear falling-rate period, $N_A = N_{A,cr} \!\left( \dfrac{X^* - X_{eq}^*}{X_{cr}^* - X_{eq}^*} \right)$.

Substitute:

$$ \begin{aligned} \theta_f &= \frac{\rho_s L}{G'} \int_{X_2^*}^{X_{cr}^*} \frac{(X_{cr}^* - X_{eq}^*)\, dX^*}{N_{A,cr}\,(X^* - X_{eq}^*)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\rho_s L (X_{cr}^* - X_{eq}^*)}{G' N_{A,cr}} \int_{X_2^*}^{X_{cr}^*} \frac{dX^*}{X^* - X_{eq}^*} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\rho_s L (X_{cr}^* - X_{eq}^*)}{G' N_{A,cr}} \ln\!\left( \frac{X_{cr}^* - X_{eq}^*}{X_2^* - X_{eq}^*} \right) \end{aligned} $$

**Step 8 — Total residence time.**

$$ \boxed{\, \theta_{\text{total}} = \theta_{cr} + \theta_f \,} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Balance for an Adiabatic Continuous Dryer

**Statement**: For a continuous dryer with negligible heat loss, derive the energy balance that relates the gas temperatures, humidities, and solid temperatures.

**Step 1 — Total enthalpy in (gas + solid).**

$$ \dot{H}_{\text{in}} = G (1 + H_1) c_{H1} T_{g1} + F_s (c_{ps} + c_{pw} X_1) T_{s1} + Q_{\text{heater}} $$

**Step 2 — Total enthalpy out (gas + solid).**

$$ \dot{H}_{\text{out}} = G (1 + H_2) c_{H2} T_{g2} + F_s (c_{ps} + c_{pw} X_2) T_{s2} $$

**Step 3 — Apply $\dot{H}_{\text{in}} = \dot{H}_{\text{out}}$ at steady state.**

$$ \begin{aligned} G(1+H_1)c_{H1}T_{g1} &+ F_s(c_{ps}+c_{pw}X_1)T_{s1} + Q \\ &= G(1+H_2)c_{H2}T_{g2} + F_s(c_{ps}+c_{pw}X_2)T_{s2} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Recognise that the gas picks up the evaporated water.**
With the approximation $c_{H1} \approx c_{H2} \approx c_H$ and noting that the additional term $G c_H (H_2 - H_1) T$ can be grouped with the latent heat of evaporation $\lambda$ at the reference temperature, the standard textbook form is recovered:

$$ \begin{aligned} Q \;=\; &G(1 + H_1) c_H (T_{g1} - T_{g0}) \\[2pt] \;=\; &F_s \left[ (c_{ps} + c_{pw} X_1)(T_{s2} - T_{s1}) + (X_1 - X_2)\,\lambda_{s2} \right] + Q_L \end{aligned} $$

**Step 5 — Use the energy balance to find the gas requirement.**
If the solid side is known, we solve for $G$:

$$ G = \frac{F_s \left[ (c_{ps} + c_{pw} X_1)(T_{s2} - T_{s1}) + (X_1 - X_2)\lambda_{s2} \right] + Q_L}{(1 + H_1) c_H (T_{g1} - T_{g0})} $$

This is the expression used to size the **blower and heater** in any continuous dryer.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Through-Circulation Dryer)

**Given**:
- Through-circulation dryer drying a granular polymer.
- Feed moisture $X_1 = 0.40$ kg/kg dry, product moisture $X_2 = 0.02$ kg/kg dry.
- Equilibrium moisture $X_{eq}^* = 0.005$ kg/kg dry.
- Critical moisture $X_{cr}^* = 0.18$ kg/kg dry.
- Bed thickness $L = 0.05$ m, bulk dry density $\rho_s = 600$ kg/m³.
- Superficial gas mass velocity $G' = 1.2$ kg/(m²·s).
- Constant-rate flux $N_{A,cr} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/(m²·s).
- Falling-rate period obeys the linear law.

**Required**: Total residence time $\theta_{\text{total}}$.

**Step 1 — Free moisture values.**
All $X$ are reported on dry basis and above the equilibrium; the equilibrium $X_{eq}^*$ is given directly. We assume $X_{eq}^* = 0.005$ is the equilibrium value, so $X^* = X - X_{eq}^*$.

$$ X_1^* = 0.40 - 0.005 = 0.395 $$
$$ X_{cr}^* = 0.18 - 0.005 = 0.175 $$
$$ X_2^* = 0.02 - 0.005 = 0.015 $$

**Step 2 — Constant-rate period residence time.**

$$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{cr} &= \frac{\rho_s L (X_1^* - X_{cr}^*)}{G' N_{A,cr}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{600 \times 0.05 \times (0.395 - 0.175)}{1.2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{600 \times 0.05 \times 0.220}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{6.6}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 5500 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 3 — Falling-rate period residence time.**

$$ \begin{aligned} \theta_f &= \frac{\rho_s L (X_{cr}^* - X_{eq}^*)}{G' N_{A,cr}} \ln\!\left( \frac{X_{cr}^*}{X_2^*} \right) \\[4pt] &= \frac{600 \times 0.05 \times 0.175}{1.2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.175}{0.015} \right) \\[4pt] &= \frac{5.25}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \ln(11.667) \\[4pt] &= 4375 \times 2.457 = 10750 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Total residence time.**

$$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{\text{total}} &= \theta_{cr} + \theta_f \\[4pt] &= 5500 + 10750 = 16250 \text{ s} \approx 4.51 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.**
A typical through-circulation dryer for granular polymer at this loading operates in the range 3–6 h, so the answer lies inside the expected industrial window. ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Continuous Dryer

| Property of Solid | Best Continuous Dryer | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Free-flowing granules (1–3 mm) | **Through-circulation belt** | High surface area exposure, short drying time |
| Pastes, slurries, solutions | **Spray dryer** | Atomization into fine droplets enables rapid drying |
| Filter cake, wet powder | **Pneumatic / Flash dryer** | Dilute-phase transport dries in seconds |
| Coarse, abrasive, hot-feed material | **Rotary dryer** | Robust, handles high tonnages |
| Heat-sensitive crystals | **Fluidized bed dryer** | Isothermal, very short residence time |
| Sheets, boards, ceramics | **Tunnel / belt with trays** | Gentle handling |
| Liquids on rollers | **Drum dryer** | Continuous film drying |
