Without evaporation, there is **no cooling effect** in an adiabatic tower. --- # 📘 Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Part A questions demand **precise definitions, key equations, and 2–3 line explanations**. No lengthy derivations. ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State and explain the material balance equation for an adiabatic gas–liquid contactor operating counter-currently. Define each term.** **Model Answer:** For a counter-current adiabatic gas–liquid contactor at steady state, the material balance on water is: $$ G(Y_2 - Y_1) = L(X_1 - X_2) $$ where $G$ = mass flow rate of dry gas (kg/s), $L$ = mass flow rate of dry liquid (kg/s), $Y_1, Y_2$ = inlet and outlet humidities of gas (kg water/kg dry gas), and $X_1, X_2$ = outlet and inlet moisture content of liquid (kg water/kg dry liquid). The equation states that the **water lost by the liquid equals the water gained by the gas**, and it represents a straight line of slope $-L/G$ on the $Y$–$X$ diagram. **(3 marks: 1.5 for equation, 1 for definition of terms, 0.5 for interpretation.)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Differentiate between adiabatic saturation temperature and wet-bulb temperature. Why are they approximately equal for the air–water system?** **Model Answer:** The **adiabatic saturation temperature ($T_{as}$)** is the temperature attained when a stream of hot, dry gas is adiabatically humidified to saturation by contact with a recirculating liquid, reaching a steady-state where the material and enthalpy balances are simultaneously satisfied. The **wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$)** is the temperature a small amount of liquid attains when exposed to a large, unsaturated gas stream under conditions where the mass-transfer and heat-transfer coefficients are interrelated by the Lewis relation, $h/(k_Y \cdot C_{s,b}) \approx 1$. For the **air–water system**, the psychrometric ratio $h/(k_Y \cdot C_s)$ is very close to unity, making $T_{wb} \approx T_{as}$. For other systems (e.g., air–toluene), they differ noticeably. **(3 marks: 1 each for definition, 1 for explanation of equality.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU Part B questions carry **internal choice**: Answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — Option A **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 4 Modified]** A counter-current **induced-draft cooling tower** is to cool water from $T_{L1} = 42\,°C$ to $T_{L2} = 28\,°C$. The atmospheric air enters at $T_{G1} = 32\,°C$ with relative humidity $\phi_1 = 0.30$, and leaves at $T_{G2} = 36\,°C$. **Liquid flow rate:** $L = 5 \text{ kg/s}$ **Dry-gas flow rate:** $G = 2 \text{ kg/s}$ Take $C_{pg} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_{pv} = 1.88$ kJ/(kg·K), $C_{pL} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), and $\lambda_0$ at 32 °C = 2420 kJ/kg. Vapour pressure of water at 32 °C $= 4.76$ kPa; total pressure = 101.3 kPa. #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Determine the humidity of inlet and outlet air, the enthalpy of the inlet and outlet gas streams, and verify the enthalpy balance. **Step 1 — Humidity of inlet air $Y_1$:** Humidity at partial pressure ratio: $$ Y_1 = \frac{M_v}{M_a} \cdot \frac{p_v}{P - p_v} = \frac{18}{29} \cdot \frac{\phi_1 \cdot p_{vs}(32\,°C)}{P - \phi_1 \cdot p_{vs}(32\,°C)} $$ $$ Y_1 = \frac{18}{29} \cdot \frac{0.30 \times 4.76}{101.3 - 0.30 \times 4.76} = 0.6207 \cdot \frac{1.428}{99.872} = 0.6207 \times 0.01430 = 0.00888 \text{ kg/kg} $$ **Step 2 — Humid heat of inlet air:** $$ C_{s1} = 1.005 + 0.00888 \times 1.88 = 1.005 + 0.0167 = 1.0217 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} $$ **Step 3 — Enthalpy of inlet air:** $$ I_{G1} = 1.0217 \times 32 + 0.00888 \times 2420 = 32.69 + 21.49 = 54.18 \text{ kJ/kg dry air} $$ **Step 4 — Humidity of outlet air $Y_2$ (from material balance):** $$ G(Y_2 - Y_1) = L(X_1 - X_2) $$ For pure water (no solute), $X_1 \approx 0$ (dry gas basis is irrelevant for liquid water; using total water for $L$, we have water loss): $$ \dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = G(Y_2 - Y_1) \quad \text{and} \quad L_{\text{loss}} = G(Y_2 - Y_1) $$ From the **energy balance**, we can solve for $Y_2$: $$ G \cdot I_{G1} + L \cdot I_{L1} = G \cdot I_{G2} + L \cdot I_{L2} $$ Reference $T_0 = 0$ °C: $I_{L1} = C_{pL} T_{L1} = 4.18 \times 42 = 175.56$ kJ/kg, $I_{L2} = 4.18 \times 28 = 117.04$ kJ/kg. $$ I_{G2} = \frac{G \cdot I_{G1} + L \cdot I_{L1} - L \cdot I_{L2}}{G} = I_{G1} + \frac{L}{G} (I_{L1} - I_{L2}) $$ $$ I_{G2} = 54.18 + \frac{5}{2} (175.56 - 117.04) = 54.18 + 2.5 \times 58.52 = 54.18 + 146.30 = 200.48 \text{ kJ/kg dry air} $$ **Step 5 — Solve $Y_2$ from outlet enthalpy equation:** At $T_{G2} = 36$ °C, assume $C_{s2} \approx 1.05$ (initial guess): $$ I_{G2} = C_{s2}(36) + Y_2 \cdot 2420 $$ $$ 200.48 = 1.05 \times 36 + 2420 \cdot Y_2 = 37.80 + 2420 \cdot Y_2 $$ $$ Y_2 = \frac{200.48 - 37.80}{2420} = \frac{162.68}{2420} = 0.0672 \text{ kg/kg} $$ **Verify $C_{s2}$:** $C_{s2} = 1.005 + 0.0672 \times 1.88 = 1.005 + 0.1263 = 1.1313$. Iterate: $$ I_{G2} = 1.1313 \times 36 + 2420 \times 0.0672 = 40.73 + 162.62 = 203.35 $$ Close enough. Final $Y_2 \approx 0.067$ kg/kg. **Step 6 — Enthalpy balance check:** $$ \text{LHS: } G \cdot I_{G1} + L \cdot I_{L1} = 2 \times 54.18 + 5 \times 175.56 = 108.36 + 877.80 = 986.16 \text{ kW} $$ $$ \text{RHS: } G \cdot I_{G2} + L \cdot I_{L2} = 2 \times 200.48 + 5 \times 117.04 = 400.96 + 585.20 = 986.16 \text{ kW} $$ ✅ **Balance closes perfectly.** #### (b) **[7 Marks]** Calculate the operating-line slope on the $I$–$Y$ chart, the evaporative make-up water rate (kg/s and kg/h), and the latent-heat fraction of the total heat rejected. **Step 1 — Operating-line slope:** $$ \text{slope} = \frac{I_{G2} - I_{G1}}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \frac{200.48 - 54.18}{0.067 - 0.00888} = \frac{146.30}{0.0581} = 2518 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 2 — Compare with theoretical $L \cdot C_{pL} / G$:** $$ \frac{L \cdot C_{pL}}{G} = \frac{5 \times 4.18}{2} = 10.45 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ The large discrepancy again reflects the curvature of the true operating line on the I–Y chart (because $C_s$ changes substantially with $Y$). **Step 3 — Evaporative make-up water:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = G(Y_2 - Y_1) = 2 \times (0.067 - 0.00888) = 2 \times 0.0581 = 0.1162 \text{ kg/s} = 418.4 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 4 — Latent-heat fraction:** Latent heat carried by water vapour: $$ \dot{Q}_{\text{latent}} = G \cdot Y_2 \cdot \lambda_0 - G \cdot Y_1 \cdot \lambda_0 = G \lambda_0 (Y_2 - Y_1) = 2 \times 2420 \times 0.0581 = 281.2 \text{ kW} $$ Total heat rejected by liquid (sensible cooling duty): $$ \dot{Q}_{\text{total}} = L \cdot C_{pL} (T_{L1} - T_{L2}) = 5 \times 4.18 \times (42 - 28) = 5 \times 4.18 \times 14 = 292.6 \text{ kW} $$ Latent-heat fraction: $$ f_{\text{latent}} = \frac{281.2}{292.6} = 0.961 = 96.1\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **~96% of the cooling in a counter-current cooling tower is by evaporation**, only ~4% by sensible heat transfer. This is a classic KTU viva question and a key industrial takeaway. --- ### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Option B (Internal Choice Alternative) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4]** A **forced-draft cooling tower** handles hot water at $T_{L1} = 40\,°C$ and discharges it at $T_{L2} = 30\,°C$. Atmospheric air enters at $T_{G1} = 28\,°C$ with wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb} = 22\,°C$, and leaves at $T_{G2} = 34\,°C$. Atmospheric pressure = 101.3 kPa. #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Using the psychrometric chart, find the inlet humidity $Y_1$ and the inlet gas enthalpy $I_{G1}$. Then estimate the outlet humidity $Y_2$ if the operating line is assumed to pass through the gas outlet state on the $I$–$Y$ chart. **Solution:** **Step 1 — At inlet ($T_{db} = 28\,°C$, $T_{wb} = 22\,°C$):** The wet-bulb temperature of 22 °C acts as the adiabatic saturation temperature for air–water. Reading the **psychrometric chart** at the intersection of dry-bulb 28 °C and wet-bulb 22 °C: $$ Y_1 \approx 0.0140 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad I_{G1} \approx 65.0 \text{ kJ/kg dry air} $$ **Step 2 — At outlet ($T_{db} = 34\,°C$):** We need to find $Y_2$ on the I–Y chart such that the operating line from $(Y_1, I_{G1})$ reaches the dry-bulb = 34 °C saturation line at the correct enthalpy. The slope of operating line: $$ \text{slope} = \frac{L \cdot C_{pL}}{G} = \frac{L}{G} \times 4.18 $$ We need the value of $L/G$ to find the slope. Assume $L/G = 1.0$ (typical for cooling tower design): $$ \text{slope} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg per unit Y} $$ Then on the chart, draw a line from $(Y_1 = 0.014, I_{G1} = 65)$ with slope 4.18 until it meets $T_{db} = 34\,°C$: $$ \Delta I_G = 4.18 \times \Delta Y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta Y = \frac{\Delta I_G}{4.18} $$ By iteration on the chart, $Y_2 \approx 0.0225$ kg/kg, and $I_{G2} \approx 75$ kJ/kg dry air. **Step 3 — Material-balance closure:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = G(Y_2 - Y_1) = G \times 0.0085 $$ If $G = 1$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = 0.0085$ kg/s = 30.6 kg/h. #### (b) **[7 Marks]** Discuss the role of the operating line slope $L \cdot C_{pL} / G$ in cooling tower design. What happens if (i) the slope is too steep, (ii) the slope is too shallow, and (iii) why is the operating line actually curved in practice? **Model Answer:** (i) **Slope too steep (large $L/G$):** Insufficient gas flow to absorb the water evaporated. The exit gas becomes **saturated** or **supersaturated** prematurely, producing fog and reducing the effective mass-transfer driving force $(Y_i - Y)$. The tower becomes **gas-limited** (short of air). Practical remedy: Increase $G$ (larger fan) or reduce $L$ (use parallel cells). (ii) **Slope too shallow (small $L/G$):** Excessive gas flow. The exit gas is **far from saturation**, wasting fan power. The tower is **liquid-limited** and the approach to the wet-bulb temperature cannot be tight. Practical remedy: Reduce $G$ (smaller fan) to optimise the approach. (iii) **Why the line is curved in practice:** The humid heat $C_s = C_{pg} + Y C_{pv}$ **increases with humidity** $Y$. The slope $L \cdot C_{pL} / G$ is strictly only valid when the **gas sensible-heat pickup** is small compared with **latent-heat pickup**. In real cooling towers, the latent component is 70–95% of the total heat duty, so the operating line **curves upward** on the I–Y chart. For accurate Merkel-equation integration, point-by-point construction is used. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not** confuse **$X$ (liquid moisture content)** with **$Y$ (gas humidity)** — they have different reference bases (kg water/kg dry liquid vs. kg water/kg dry gas). Many students interchange them and lose **2–3 marks per sub-part**. > 2. **Forgetting to convert** $C_s$ in the I–Y equation — the constant 1.005 and 1.88 are in **kJ**, not kcal. KTU papers expect SI units throughout. > 3. **Reporting humidity as a percentage** (e.g., $Y = 1.4\%$) is acceptable; but the **algebraic form** $Y = 0.014$ kg/kg is mandatory for any calculation step. > 4. **Sign errors in the enthalpy balance** — the equation $G \cdot I_{G1} + L \cdot I_{L1} = G \cdot I_{G2} + L \cdot I_{L2}$ holds **only** for adiabatic operation with steady state. If there is an external heat duty $\dot{Q}$, modify as: $G I_{G1} + L I_{L1} + \dot{Q} = G I_{G2} + L I_{L2}$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨ > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-revision checklist — print this and review before the exam:** - ✅ **Two key balances** govern humidification: (1) material balance on water, (2) total enthalpy balance. They must be applied **simultaneously** for any gas–liquid contact problem. - ✅ **Material balance on water:** $G(Y_2 - Y_1) = L(X_1 - X_2)$ — straight line of slope $-L/G$ on $Y$–$X$ chart. - ✅ **Enthalpy balance (adiabatic):** $G I_{G1} + L I_{L1} = G I_{G2} + L I_{L2}$. - ✅ **Gas enthalpy formula:** $I_G = C_s (T - T_0) + Y \lambda_0$, where $C_s = 1.005 + 1.88 Y$ for the air–water system (in **kJ/(kg dry air·K)**). - ✅ **Operating line on I–Y chart:** $I_{G2} - I_{G1} = (L C_{pL} / G) (Y_2 - Y_1)$ — a straight line only if $C_s$ is treated as constant. - ✅ **Evaporative loss / make-up water:** $\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = G(Y_2 - Y_1)$ (kg/s); convert to **kg/h** for plant reports. - ✅ **In a counter-current cooling tower, ~70–95% of cooling is by evaporation** (latent heat); only the remainder is sensible heat transfer. - ✅ **Adiabatic saturation temperature ($T_{as}$)** and **wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$)** coincide for the air–water system because the psychrometric ratio $h/(k_Y C_s) \approx 1$ (Lewis relation). - ✅ **Operating line is curved in practice** because $C_s$ increases with $Y$ — for precise Merkel-equation work, integrate point-by-point. - ✅ **All streams referenced on a dry basis** ($G$ = dry gas, $L$ = dry liquid) — eliminates confusion when water is transferred. - ✅ **Sign convention:** Heat leaving the liquid = positive duty; mass gained by gas = positive $Y_2 - Y_1$. - ✅ **For non-adiabatic operation** (cooling coils, dehumidifiers), add an external duty term $\dot{Q}$ to the enthalpy balance. > 🎯 **KTU 2024 Module 4 Mastery:** If you can (1) draw the I–Y operating line from given end-points, (2) verify the enthalpy balance numerically, and (3) compute the make-up water and latent-heat fraction — you have mastered this topic. ---

topics.title: Classification of Industrial Dryers for Batch and Continuous Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Industrial Dryers for Batch and Continuous Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying** is a **mass transfer operation** in which a volatile liquid (usually water) is removed from a solid material by **thermal evaporation** into a gaseous phase, accompanied by simultaneous **heat transfer** (conduction, convection, or radiation) and **mass transfer** (liquid diffusion within the solid and vapor diffusion in the gas phase).

In the language of KTU Mass Transfer Operations 1, drying is classified as a **simultaneous heat and mass transfer (SHMT)** process in which the **driving force** is the difference between the **vapor pressure of moisture at the surface of the solid** and the **partial pressure of moisture in the bulk gas stream**.

### 1.2 Industrial Dryer — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> An **Industrial Dryer** is an engineered equipment / vessel that supplies **heat** to a wet solid, removes the **generated vapor**, and delivers a product with a specified **final moisture content**, while meeting quality, throughput, and energy constraints.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are drying a wet shirt on a clothesline on a sunny afternoon:

- **Sunlight** = source of heat
- **Wind** = carrier gas (air) sweeping the vapor away
- **Moisture inside fibers** = must diffuse outward (internal mass transfer)
- **Evaporation at surface** = moisture leaves the surface (external mass transfer)
- **Heat must reach the wet zone** = conduction through the fabric (heat transfer)

A **batch dryer** is like drying the shirt once and removing it when dry. A **continuous dryer** is like a conveyor passing the shirt through a heated tunnel — the shirt enters wet and exits dry at the other end, with no stoppage.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Drying

- **Moisture content (X)** = kg moisture per kg bone-dry solid — expressed as **dry basis** in engineering analysis.
- **Critical moisture content (X_c)** = the moisture level below which the drying rate becomes **internally controlled**.
- **Equilibrium moisture content (X\*)** = minimum moisture achievable under given air conditions.
- **Free moisture** = $X - X^*$
- **Bound moisture** = moisture held tightly within the solid (X > X_c region).

### 1.5 Why Classification Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No single dryer fits all products.** The correct dryer selection depends on:
> 1. **Physical form of feed** (powder, granules, slurry, sheet, paste)
> 2. **Heat sensitivity** of the material
> 3. **Production scale** (kg/h vs tonnes/h)
> 4. **Desired final moisture content**
> 5. **Capital and operating cost**
> 6. **Continuous vs batch** nature of upstream/downstream operations
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Classification Criteria

Industrial dryers are classified along several engineering axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following hierarchy:

| **Criterion** | **Categories** | **Engineering Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Operation** | Batch / Continuous (Semi-batch) | Determines production logic |
| **Heat Transfer Mechanism** | Direct (convection), Indirect (conduction), Radiation, Dielectric | Sets energy efficiency & product quality |
| **Physical State of Feed** | Liquid, Slurry, Paste, Free-flowing solid, Sheet/Board | Dictates contact geometry |
| **Operating Pressure** | Atmospheric / Vacuum | Vacuum for heat-sensitive products |
| **Gas–Solid Contact Pattern** | Co-current, Counter-current, Cross-flow | Sets driving force profile |
| **Solid Movement** | Stationary, Agitated, Rotating, Fluidized, Sprayed | Affects heat & mass transfer area |

### 2.2 Operating Mode — The Master Distinction

**A. Batch Dryers**
- Feed is loaded, dried for a fixed cycle, and discharged.
- Suitable for: small tonnage, multi-product plants, pharma & specialty chemicals.
- Lower capital cost, higher labor cost per kg.
- Typical units: **Tray (Cabinet) Dryer, Vacuum Shelf Dryer, Freeze Dryer, Agitated Pan Dryer, Rotary Batch (Cone) Dryer, Bin Dryer**.

**B. Continuous Dryers**
- Wet feed enters and dry product exits simultaneously at steady rates.
- Suitable for: large tonnage, single-product dedicated lines.
- Higher capital cost, lower labor cost per kg, consistent product quality.
- Typical units: **Rotary Dryer, Drum Dryer, Spray Dryer, Fluidized Bed Dryer, Pneumatic (Flash) Dryer, Belt / Band Dryer, Tunnel Dryer, Spouted Bed Dryer, Vibrated Fluidized Bed Dryer**.

### 2.3 Heat Transfer Mechanism (Direct vs Indirect)

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct (Adiabatic) Dryer** — Hot gas contacts the wet solid directly. The gas acts as both the **heat carrier** and the **moisture carrier**. Example: Rotary hot-air dryer, Spray dryer, Fluidized bed dryer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Indirect (Non-adiabatic) Dryer** — Heat is supplied through a metal wall (conduction). The gas only carries away the vapor. Example: Drum dryer, Vacuum shelf dryer, Steam-tube rotary dryer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Radiation Dryer** — Infrared energy is used. Example: IR tunnel for paint/coat drying.
> **Dielectric Dryer** — Microwave or RF heating. Example: Microwave vacuum dryer.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture content (dry basis) | $X = \dfrac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{dry solid}}}$ | $\text{kg water} \cdot (\text{kg dry solid})^{-1}$ | Measure of wetness |
| Bone-dry mass | $m_s = m_{\text{total}} \cdot (1 + X)^{-1}$ | kg | Solid mass reference |
| Drying rate | $N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | Rate of moisture removal |
| Heat required (sensible + latent) | $Q = \dot{m}_s \left[ c_{ps}(T_2 - T_1) + \Delta H_v (X_1 - X_2) \right]$ | kW | Total heat duty |
| Evaporation rate | $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_s (X_1 - X_2)$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | Water removed per second |
| Air humidity ratio (psychrometric) | $H = 0.622 \dfrac{p_w}{P - p_w}$ | $\text{kg water} \cdot (\text{kg dry air})^{-1}$ | Moisture in air |
| Adiabatic saturation temperature | $H_s - H_1 = \dfrac{c_s}{y_s}(T_1 - T_{as})$ | dimensionless ratio | Psychrometric relation |
| Log-mean driving force (humidity) | $\Delta H_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta H_1 - \Delta H_2}{\ln(\Delta H_1 / \Delta H_2)}$ | — | For LMTD-type design |
| Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_a}$ | — | Energy benchmark |
| Volumetric heat-transfer coefficient | $U_v = \dfrac{Q}{V \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$ | $\text{kW} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ | For scale-up |
| NTU-type design equation (direct dryer) | $\dfrac{dH}{dY} = \dfrac{c_s}{y_s}$ | — | Coupled heat–mass balance |

> All symbols: $L_s$ = mass of dry solid (kg), $A$ = drying area (m²), $c_s$ = humid heat (kJ/kg dry air·K), $y_s$ = slope of saturation curve, $P$ = total pressure (Pa), $p_w$ = partial pressure of water vapor (Pa), $\Delta H_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry** → Freeze dryers & vacuum tray dryers (heat-sensitive APIs).
- **Dairy industry** → Spray dryers for milk powder; Drum dryers for milk food.
- **Cement / minerals** → Rotary dryers for ores, sand, slag.
- **Chemical industry** → Fluidized bed dryers for polymers, fertilizers.
- **Food industry** → Belt / tunnel dryers for noodles, fruits, fish.
- **Pulp & paper** → Cylinder / Yankee dryers (conduction-based, specialized).

### 2.6 Comparative Logic: Why We Need Both Batch and Continuous

| **Aspect** | **Batch** | **Continuous** |
|---|---|---|
| Flexibility | High (multi-product) | Low (dedicated) |
| Throughput | Low to medium | High |
| Labour cost/kg | High | Low |
| Product uniformity | Variable | Excellent |
| Heat-sensitive handling | Easier (vacuum) | Requires precise control |
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Instrumentation need | Simple | Advanced DCS / PLC |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Drying Rate from a Differential Mass Balance

Consider a thin layer of wet solid of bone-dry mass $L_s$ (kg) and surface area $A$ (m²). Let the moisture content at time $t$ be $X$ (kg water/kg dry solid).

**Step 1: Total mass balance on the system at time $t$**

$$m_{\text{total}} = L_s (1 + X)$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to time**

$$\frac{d m_{\text{total}}}{dt} = L_s \frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 3: The total mass loss rate is governed by moisture evaporation**

$$\frac{d m_{\text{total}}}{dt} = -\dot{m}_w = -N \cdot A$$

**Step 4: Equate both expressions for the rate of mass change**

$$L_s \frac{dX}{dt} = -N \cdot A$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to obtain the canonical drying rate definition**

$$N = -\frac{L_s}{A}\frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Interpretation:** The drying rate $N$ is positive when the moisture content $X$ is decreasing with time. This is the **universal differential equation** that links operating data ($X$ vs $t$) to the fundamental rate $N$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coupled Heat–Mass Balance for an Adiabatic Direct Dryer

For a counter-current direct dryer, consider a differential slice $dY$ (kg dry solid/kg dry gas) traversed.

**Step 1: Differential moisture balance on the gas**

The gas picks up $dH$ (kg water/kg dry air) as it passes through $dY$:

$$dH = -c_s \, dY$$

**Step 2: Differential heat balance (adiabatic — no wall losses)**

The enthalpy lost by the gas equals the heat used to evaporate water plus the sensible heat change of the solid:

$$c_s dY = y_s dH$$

**Step 3: Combine both to get the fundamental operating line**

$$\frac{dH}{dY} = \frac{c_s}{y_s}$$

where:
- $c_s$ = humid heat of air = $1.005 + 1.88 H$ (kJ/kg dry air·K)
- $y_s$ = slope of the adiabatic saturation curve at the operating condition

**Step 4: Integrate from inlet $(H_1, Y_1)$ to outlet $(H_2, Y_2)$**

$$Y_2 - Y_1 = \int_{H_1}^{H_2} \frac{y_s}{c_s} \, dH$$

**Step 5: For dilute operation $c_s \approx$ constant, and $y_s$ is taken at the mean driving point, giving**

$$Y_2 - Y_1 \approx \frac{y_{s,\text{mean}}}{c_s}(H_2 - H_1)$$

This equation is the **working tool** for the design of tray, rotary, and spray dryers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Heat Duty of a Dryer

A dryer must supply: (a) latent heat for evaporation, and (b) sensible heat to raise the solid and its remaining moisture to the outlet temperature.

**Step 1: Total water to evaporate**

$$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_s (X_1 - X_2)$$

**Step 2: Latent heat duty**

$$Q_{\text{lat}} = \dot{m}_w \, \Delta H_v$$

**Step 3: Sensible heat to dry solid**

$$Q_{\text{sen,s}} = \dot{m}_s \, c_{ps} (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})$$

**Step 4: Sensible heat to unevaporated water**

$$Q_{\text{sen,w}} = \dot{m}_s X_2 \, c_{pw} (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})$$

**Step 5: Total duty**

$$Q = \dot{m}_s \left[ (c_{ps} + c_{pw} X_2)(T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}) + \Delta H_v (X_1 - X_2) \right]$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Selection Matrix — Which Dryer When?

| **Feed / Product Form** | **Production Scale** | **Heat-Sensitive?** | **Recommended Dryer Type** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free-flowing granules | Large | No | Rotary / Fluidized bed |
| Fine powder (slurry) | Large | Moderate | Spray dryer |
| Paste / slurry | Medium | No | Drum dryer |
| Sheets / board | Large | No | Cylinder / Belt dryer |
| Crystals (heat-sensitive API) | Small | Yes | Vacuum shelf / Freeze |
| Bulk food (e.g., noodles) | Medium | Yes | Belt / Tunnel |
| Wet cake (filter cake) | Small–Medium | Sometimes | Tray / Vacuum / Agitated pan |
| Waxy polymer chips | Large | No | Pneumatic (Flash) dryer |
| Coarse mineral ore | Very large | No | Rotary counter-current |
| Pharmaceutical vials | Small | Yes | Freeze dryer (Lyophilizer) |
| Continuous milk powder | Very large | Moderate | Spray dryer (cocurrent) |

### 3.5 Batch vs Continuous — Engineering Trade-off Curve

A useful rule of thumb from KTU reference data:

- **< 100 kg/h** → Batch justified (capital amortization dominates).
- **100 – 1000 kg/h** → Pilot judgment zone; either may be optimum.
- **> 1000 kg/h** → Continuous strongly preferred (labor & energy dominate).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[INDUSTRIAL DRYERS] --> B[Batch Dryers]
    A --> C[Continuous Dryers]
    A --> D[Selection Criteria]

    B --> B1[Tray Cabinet Dryer]
    B --> B2[Vacuum Shelf Dryer]
    B --> B3[Freeze Lyophilizer]
    B --> B4[Agitated Pan Dryer]
    B --> B5[Cone Screw Dryer]

    C --> C1[Rotary Dryer]
    C --> C2[Drum Dryer]
    C --> C3[Spray Dryer]
    C --> C4[Fluidized Bed Dryer]
    C --> C5[Pneumatic Flash Dryer]
    C --> C6[Belt Tunnel Dryer]
    C --> C7[Spouted Bed Dryer]

    D --> D1[Heat Transfer Mode]
    D --> D2[Feed Physical State]
    D --> D3[Production Scale]
    D --> D4[Heat Sensitivity]

    D1 --> D1a[Direct Convection]
    D1 --> D1b[Indirect Conduction]
    D1 --> D1c[Radiation]
    D1 --> D1d[Dielectric MW RF]

    D2 --> D2a[Liquid Slurry]
    D2 --> D2b[Paste]
    D2 --> D2c[Granular Powder]
    D2 --> D2d[Sheet Board]

    C1 --> M1[Co-current]
    C1 --> M2[Counter-current]
    C2 --> M3[Single Drum]
    C2 --> M4[Double Drum]
    C3 --> M5[Co-current]
    C3 --> M6[Counter-current]
    C3 --> M7[Mixed Flow]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Industrial Drying System

```mermaid
graph LR
    WET[Wet Solid Feed] --> PS[Pre-treatment / Size Reduction]
    PS --> HXIN[Heater Air Heater / Steam Coil]
    HXIN --> GIN[Hot Gas Inlet]
    GIN --> DV[Drying Vessel Chamber]
    DV --> SEP[Gas Solid Separator Cyclone / Bag Filter]
    DV --> VAP[Moist Vapor Stream]
    SEP --> EXH[Exhaust to Atmosphere / Scrubber]
    DV --> COOL[Product Cooler]
    COOL --> PK[Packaging / Storage]
    DV --> REC[Recycle of Fines Optional]
    REC --> DV
    DV --> CTRL[DCS PLC Temperature Humidity Control Loop]
    CTRL --> HXIN
    CTRL --> DV
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Direct vs Indirect Drying

```mermaid
graph TD
    DIR[Direct Convective Dryer] --> DIR1[Hot Gas Contacts Solid Directly]
    DIR1 --> DIR2[Gas = Heat Carrier AND Moisture Carrier]
    DIR2 --> DIR3[Examples: Rotary, Spray, FBD, Pneumatic]

    IND[Indirect Conductive Dryer] --> IND1[Heat via Metal Wall]
    IND1 --> IND2[Gas Sweeps Vapor Only]
    IND2 --> IND3[Examples: Drum, Vacuum Shelf, Steam Tube Rotary]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading aid for the Mermaid diagrams:** The above block diagrams map the **functional flow** of a drying train, not the physical equipment. In the KTU exam, you may be asked to draw a similar block schematic for any specific dryer — practice labeling **inlet streams, outlet streams, and recycle loops** as shown above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 pattern]**
Differentiate between **batch** and **continuous** dryers. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Batch Dryer** | **Continuous Dryer** |
|---|---|---|
| Mode of operation | Feed loaded, dried for a cycle, then discharged | Feed enters and dry product exits simultaneously at steady rates |
| Production | Small tonnage, multi-product | Large tonnage, single-product |
| Labour | Higher per kg | Lower per kg |
| Capital | Lower | Higher |
| **Examples** | Tray dryer, Vacuum shelf dryer, Freeze dryer | Rotary dryer, Spray dryer, Fluidized bed dryer, Drum dryer |

*(1 mark for each correct row + 1 mark for examples ⇒ 3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 pattern]**
Classify industrial dryers based on the **mode of heat transfer** to the wet solid. Give an example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial dryers can be classified by heat transfer mechanism as:

1. **Direct (convective) dryers** — Hot gas contacts the solid. The gas is the heat carrier and vapor carrier. *(Example: Spray dryer, Rotary dryer, Fluidized bed dryer)* — **1 mark**.
2. **Indirect (conductive) dryers** — Heat supplied through metal wall; gas only removes vapor. *(Example: Drum dryer, Vacuum shelf dryer, Steam-tube rotary dryer)* — **1 mark**.
3. **Radiation dryers** — Heat by infrared/IR. *(Example: IR tunnel for paint drying)* — **0.5 mark**.
4. **Dielectric dryers** — Heat by microwave / RF. *(Example: Microwave vacuum dryer)* — **0.5 mark**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU-style 2024 ESE pattern]**

**(a)** Classify the various industrial dryers used in chemical process industries. Discuss the **tray dryer** in detail with neat sketch, working principle, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a schematic, explain the working principle, design features, and industrial applications of a **rotary dryer**. State clearly the difference between **co-current** and **counter-current** rotary dryers. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Classification of Industrial Dryers (3 marks):**

> Dryers can be classified based on the following criteria:
> - **Mode of operation** — Batch vs Continuous.
> - **Heat transfer mechanism** — Direct (convective), Indirect (conductive), Radiation, Dielectric.
> - **Feed physical form** — Liquid, slurry, paste, granular, sheet.
> - **Operating pressure** — Atmospheric vs Vacuum.

**2. Tray Dryer — Detailed Discussion (4 marks):**

- **Construction** *(1 mark)*: An insulated rectangular cabinet with internal **trays** stacked on a rack. Trays are typically 0.6 m × 0.6 m to 1 m × 1 m, with perforated or solid bottoms. Air flows over the trays, sometimes with internal fans.
- **Working principle** *(1 mark)*: Wet solid is spread in a thin layer (~10–100 mm) on trays. Steam-heated air or electric heaters supply hot air. Moisture evaporates into the air stream and is exhausted through a vent. The cycle runs for 4–48 hours depending on product.
- **Operating parameters** *(0.5 mark)*: Air velocity 1–10 m/s, temperature 50–150 °C, batch size 50–2000 kg.
- **Applications** *(0.5 mark)*: Pharmaceuticals, dyes, pigments, food powders, catalysts, small-scale specialty chemicals.
- **Advantages**: Simple, low capital, multi-product; **Disadvantages**: Labour-intensive, non-uniform drying, long cycle.

**Block sketch (text-based, KTU board pattern):**

```
+-----------------------------+
|     Exhaust damper (top)    |
+-----------------------------+
|  Tray 4  (top, with sample) |
|  Tray 3                     |
|  Tray 2                     |
|  Tray 1  (bottom)           |
+-----------------------------+
| Steam coils  +  Fan inlet   |
+-----------------------------+
```

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Rotary Dryer — Construction and Working (4 marks):**

- A **slightly inclined rotating cylindrical shell** (diameter 0.5–4 m, length 4–40 m, slope 1:20).
- Lifting **flights (shelves)** welded on the inside wall lift and cascade the solid through the gas stream.
- Rotation speed: 4–8 rpm; residence time: 5–90 min.
- **Working**: Wet solid enters at one end; hot gas flows either co- or counter-current. Solid is repeatedly lifted and showered through the gas, exposing fresh surface for evaporation.

**2. Co-current vs Counter-current (2 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Co-current** | **Counter-current** |
|---|---|---|
| Solid–gas flow | Same direction | Opposite direction |
| Evaporation | Fast, near inlet | Slow, gradual |
| Max solid temperature | Limited to gas inlet — lower (good for heat-sensitive) | Approaches gas inlet at hot end — higher |
| Thermal efficiency | Lower (cold exit gas) | Higher |
| Typical use | Heat-sensitive materials | Non-sensitive, high throughput |

**3. Applications (1 mark):** Sand, ores, fertilizers, slags, mineral concentrates, polymers.

**Schematic (text-based):**

```
Gas + Wet solid  ===>     [===ROTATING CYLINDER===]    Dry solid + Exhaust
        (Hot gas & wet feed)    with internal flights    (cool solid exits)
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative — KTU Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **spray dryer** with a neat sketch. Discuss its principle, atomization methods, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **fluidized bed dryer** in detail. Compare its **advantages and disadvantages** with those of a **tray dryer** and explain why FBD is preferred for granular, free-flowing products. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Working Principle (2 marks):**
- The feed (solution, slurry, or paste) is **atomized** into a fine spray of droplets (10–500 µm) using an atomizer.
- Hot air enters at the top (or bottom, depending on design) and contacts the droplets in a large **drying chamber**.
- Moisture evaporates in milliseconds; dry powder falls to the bottom for collection, while exhaust gas passes through a **cyclone and bag filter** for fines recovery.

**2. Atomization Methods (2 marks):**

| **Method** | **Mechanism** | **Droplet size** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure nozzle | Hydraulic pressure | Fine | Large scale, low viscosity |
| Rotary disk | Centrifugal force | Uniform | Abrasive, pasty feeds |
| Two-fluid nozzle | Compressed air + liquid | Very fine | Low capacity, lab scale |
| Ultrasonic | High-freq vibration | Very uniform | Pharma specialty |

**3. Components (1.5 marks):** Feed tank → pump → atomizer → drying chamber → cyclone separator → bag filter → exhaust fan → product collection hopper.

**4. Applications (1.5 marks):** Milk powder, coffee, detergents, dyes, ceramics, enzymes, instant foods, antibiotics (heat-sensitive due to short residence time).

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Fluidized Bed Dryer — Construction (2 marks):**
- A vertical column with a **perforated distributor plate** at the bottom.
- Hot air is forced upwards through the distributor at a velocity high enough to **fluidize** the particulate bed (typically $U_m < U < U_t$).
- Particles behave like a boiling liquid, providing excellent gas–solid contact and high heat and mass transfer rates.

**2. Working Principle (1.5 marks):**
- Wet granules are charged onto the distributor.
- Hot air fluidizes the bed; moisture evaporates rapidly.
- Dry product is discharged through an overflow or discharge valve.
- Typical residence time: 5–60 min; air temperature 60–180 °C.

**3. Tray Dryer vs FBD — Comparison (2.5 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Tray Dryer** | **Fluidized Bed Dryer** |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer | Poor (still bed) | Excellent (fluidized) |
| Drying time | 4–48 h | 5–60 min |
| Uniformity | Uneven (corners/edges) | Very uniform |
| Particle damage | Minimal | Possible attrition |
| Continuous operation | Difficult | Easy |
| Capital cost | Low | Higher |
| Application | Pastes, fragile crystals | Free-flowing granules |
| Scale-up | Linear | Requires careful hydrodynamics |

**4. Why FBD is preferred for granular, free-flowing products (1 mark):**
- High surface area exposure due to fluidization → 10–30× higher $h$ (heat transfer coefficient).
- Short residence time → less thermal damage.
- Continuous operation → lower labour cost per kg.
- Excellent temperature uniformity → consistent product moisture.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sketch omission** — A dry, unlabeled text diagram is worth **0 marks** in part (a). Always label **feed inlet, gas inlet, gas outlet, product outlet, internal components (flights, trays, distributor, atomizer)**.
> 2. **Conflating heat transfer mechanisms** — Writing "spray dryer uses conduction" costs you. Spray dryer is **direct convection** with the gas. Drum dryer is **conduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention operating parameters** — Stating only the principle without air velocity, temperature, or residence time ranges loses 1–2 marks in 7-mark answers.
> 4. **Confusing co-current and counter-current** — Always state the **direction of solid** and **direction of gas**; the comparison table is what KTU expects.
> 5. **Numerical units mismatch** — Use SI consistently: kg/s, kW, m², m/s, °C (or K). Mixing units loses credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Drying** = simultaneous heat and mass transfer; driven by vapor pressure difference.
- Industrial dryers are classified by: **mode (batch/continuous)**, **heat-transfer mode (direct/indirect/radiation/dielectric)**, **feed form**, **pressure**, and **gas–solid contact pattern**.
- **Batch dryers**: Tray, Vacuum Shelf, Freeze, Agitated Pan, Cone Screw — chosen for **small tonnage, heat-sensitive, multi-product** jobs.
- **Continuous dryers**: Rotary, Drum, Spray, FBD, Pneumatic Flash, Belt/Tunnel, Spouted Bed — chosen for **large tonnage, single-product** lines.
- **Direct (convective)** dryers dominate by count and capacity. **Indirect (conductive)** dryers are essential for closed systems and heat-sensitive powders.
- Key formulas to memorize:
  - $X = m_w / m_s$ (dry basis)
  - $N = -L_s/A \cdot dX/dt$
  - $Q = \dot{m}_s [c_{ps}(T_2 - T_1) + \Delta H_v (X_1 - X_2)]$
  - $H = 0.622 \, p_w / (P - p_w)$ (humidity ratio)
  - $dH/dY = c_s / y_s$ (adiabatic operating line)
- **Selection rule of thumb**: < 100 kg/h → batch; 100–1000 kg/h → judgment zone; > 1000 kg/h → continuous.
- **Spray dryer residence time is < 5 seconds** — this is the key reason it handles heat-sensitive products like milk and antibiotics.
- **FBD gives 10–30× higher heat transfer coefficient** than tray dryers — reason for its dominance in granular drying.
- **Always draw a labeled block diagram** in the KTU exam — it is worth at least 1–2 marks by itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crystallization: Principles of Crystallization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crystallization: Principles of Crystallization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Crystallization**
> Crystallization is a **mass transfer–driven separation process** in which solid crystals are formed from a homogeneous solution (or melt) by changing the solution's thermodynamic state (typically through cooling, evaporation, or addition of an anti-solvent) so that the concentration of the solute exceeds its equilibrium solubility. The excess solute precipitates as an ordered, geometrically defined solid phase, leaving behind a depleted liquid phase known as the **mother liquor**.

In a chemical engineering context, crystallization is classified as a **solid–liquid separation** operation and is governed by both **thermodynamic equilibrium** (the solubility curve) and **kinetic transport phenomena** (nucleation and growth rates).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Making Rock Candy"

Imagine dissolving a huge amount of sugar in hot water until no more can dissolve — you now have a **saturated solution**. If you let this hot syrup cool slowly (or hang a string in it and wait for water to evaporate), tiny sugar crystals begin to form and grow on the string, eventually producing **rock candy**.

What just happened in the syrup?
1. At high temperature, the water could "hide" more sugar than at room temperature — this is the **solubility limit**.
2. As the syrup cooled, the solubility dropped, and the sugar solution became **supersaturated** (holding more dissolved sugar than it normally should).
3. This unstable syrup **released** the extra sugar by forming ordered solid crystals — a process called **crystallization**.

The sugar-and-water system is the prototype for industrial crystallization of salts, sugars, pharmaceuticals, and fertilizers.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Saturated Solution** | A solution that contains the maximum possible dissolved solute at a given temperature; it is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the solid phase. |
| **Supersaturated Solution** | A solution that contains *more* dissolved solute than its equilibrium solubility — a **metastable** state. |
| **Mother Liquor** | The residual liquid remaining after crystals have been formed and removed. |
| **Magma / Slurry** | The mixture of crystals and mother liquor inside the crystallizer. |
| **Nucleation** | The birth of a *new*, infinitesimally small crystal nucleus. |
| **Crystal Growth** | The increase in size of an existing crystal by addition of solute molecules to its surface. |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Tip**
> Examiners love to ask: *"Define a saturated, supersaturated, and metastable solution."* Memorize the hierarchy: **Unsaturated < Saturated (stable) < Metastable (labile) < Unstable zone.**

### 1.4 Industrial Importance

Crystallization is one of the most widely used separation/purification techniques because it:
- Produces solids in a desirable, uniform, free-flowing, and pure crystal form.
- Operates at **relatively low temperatures**, making it suitable for heat-sensitive products (e.g., pharmaceuticals, food).
- Has **lower energy requirements** compared to distillation for many inorganic salt–water systems.
- Allows separation of mixtures based on differences in solubility.

Common industrial products: **sugar, salt (NaCl), ammonium sulfate, urea, citric acid, potassium nitrate, sodium carbonate, aspirin, paracetamol.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Driving Force — Supersaturation

Crystallization **cannot occur** in a saturated solution. The actual driver is **supersaturation**, which is the deviation of the actual solution concentration $c$ from the equilibrium solubility $c^*$.

The two common measures of supersaturation are:

1. **Supersaturation ratio (S):**
$$S = \dfrac{c}{c^*}$$
Where $c$ is the actual solution concentration and $c^*$ is the saturation concentration at the same temperature.

2. **Absolute supersaturation (Δc):**
$$\Delta c = c - c^*$$

3. **Relative supersaturation (σ):**
$$\sigma = \dfrac{\Delta c}{c^*} = S - 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Concept**
> Supersaturation is the **thermodynamic driving force** for *both* nucleation and crystal growth. Without supersaturation, neither will occur. The rate of nucleation scales roughly as $\Delta c^n$ where $n$ is a large integer, and growth rate scales as $\Delta c^g$ where $g$ is typically close to 1.

### 2.2 How Supersaturation is Generated Industrially

| Method | Principle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Cooling** | Reduce solubility by lowering T | Crystallization of $\text{KNO}_3$, sugar |
| **Evaporation** | Remove solvent to concentrate solute | Production of common salt (NaCl) from seawater |
| **Salting-out (Anti-solvent addition)** | Add a miscible liquid in which solute is insoluble | Precipitation of proteins using acetone/ethanol |
| **Reaction (Precipitation)** | Chemical reaction creates a sparingly soluble product | Production of $\text{BaSO}_4$, $\text{CaCO}_3$ |
| **pH change** | Alters ionization and effective solubility | Amino acid / pharmaceutical crystallization |

### 2.3 Mier's Supersaturation Theory — The Three Zones

This is a **board-favorite diagram** and a compulsory concept for KTU examinations.

Professor J. W. Mier (1902) proposed that, in addition to the normal **solubility curve**, there exists a second curve called the **supersolubility curve** (or metastable-limit curve). The supersolubility curve lies *above* the solubility curve and represents the concentration above which **spontaneous nucleation** occurs.

This divides the concentration–temperature space into three regions:

- **Stable zone (unsaturated):** Below the solubility curve. Crystals will *dissolve*; crystallization is impossible.
- **Metastable zone (between the two curves):** Crystals *will grow* if added as seed, but spontaneous nucleation is negligible. This is the **operating zone** of an industrial crystallizer.
- **Labile / Unstable zone (above the supersolubility curve):** Spontaneous nucleation occurs vigorously.

> [!NOTE] **The Width of the Metastable Zone**
> The metastable zone width (MSZW) is a kinetic property, NOT a thermodynamic one. It depends on:
> - Cooling/evaporation rate
> - Presence of impurities
> - Agitation intensity
> - Vessel surface properties
> A wider MSZW is *favorable* for producing large, uniform crystals.

### 2.4 Nucleation — The Birth of Crystals

Nucleation is the formation of a new crystal phase from a supersaturated solution. It is classified into:

**A) Primary Nucleation** (no crystals present initially)
- **Homogeneous Primary Nucleation:** Spontaneous aggregation of solute molecules in the bulk of a highly supersaturated solution. Requires overcoming a critical energy barrier.
- **Heterogeneous Primary Nucleation:** Nucleation induced by foreign surfaces (dust, container walls, impurities) at *lower* supersaturation than homogeneous.

**B) Secondary Nucleation** (crystals of the same solute already present)
- The dominant mechanism in industrial crystallizers.
- Caused by:
  - **Contact (collision) nucleation** — crystals striking impellers, walls, or each other.
  - **Fluid shear nucleation** — high-velocity fluid shears off tiny fragments.
  - **Attrition nucleation** — breakage of parent crystals.

**C) Primary vs. Secondary Nucleation — Comparison Table**

| Aspect | Primary | Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| Crystals initially present | No | Yes |
| Supersaturation required | Very high | Low (within metastable zone) |
| Industrial dominance | Rarely dominant | **Dominant** in seeded/circulating crystallizers |
| Mechanism | Bulk molecular clustering | Contact, shear, attrition |

### 2.5 Crystal Growth — The Increase in Size

After a stable nucleus forms, it grows by addition of solute molecules from the solution onto its surface. The two-step mechanism is:

1. **Bulk diffusion** of solute through the boundary layer surrounding the crystal (convective/diffusive transport).
2. **Surface integration** (or surface reaction) — the solute molecule is incorporated into the crystal lattice.

**McCabe's ΔL Law** (a key KTU concept):
$$\dfrac{dL}{dt} = G = \text{constant (independent of } L\text{)}$$

This states that the linear growth rate of *all* crystals in a well-mixed crystallizer is the same, **regardless of their size**. Therefore, in a given residence time, every crystal increases in size by the same amount ΔL.

> [!NOTE] **Why ΔL Law Matters**
> It allows prediction of the **Crystal Size Distribution (CSD)** from the residence time distribution of the crystallizer. It is the foundation of the **Mixed Suspension Mixed Product Removal (MSMPR)** crystallizer model.

### 2.6 The Overall Population Balance (Qualitative)

The general population balance for crystal number density $n(L,t)$ is:

$$\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial (G \cdot n)}{\partial L} + \dfrac{n}{\tau} = B - D$$

Where:
- $G$ = linear growth rate (using ΔL law, $G$ is constant)
- $\tau$ = mean residence time
- $B$ = birth rate (nucleation)
- $D$ = death rate (attrition, breakage, usually negligible)

For a **steady-state MSMPR crystallizer** (no birth/death, $\partial n/\partial t = 0$, $B = D = 0$):

$$\dfrac{dn}{dL} = -\dfrac{n}{G\tau}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Always double-check the units in yield problems — the most common mistake on KTU papers!**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Supersaturation ratio | $S = c / c^*$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Absolute supersaturation | $\Delta c = c - c^*$ | $\text{kg solute / kg solvent}$ or $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 3 | Relative supersaturation | $\sigma = (c - c^*)/c^*$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Nucleation rate (empirical) | $B^0 = k_N \, \Delta c^n$ | $n$ is large (often 3–8) |
| 5 | Growth rate (empirical) | $G = k_g \, \Delta c^g$ | $g \approx 1$ to 2 |
| 6 | ΔL law | $dL/dt = G$ | m/s (constant w.r.t. L) |
| 7 | MSMPR CSD | $n(L) = n^0 \, \exp(-L / G\tau)$ | Number density $\text{m}^{-4}$ |
| 8 | Dominant crystal size | $L_d = 3G\tau$ | m |
| 9 | Yield (anhydrous salt) | $Y = w \cdot R \cdot (c_1 - c_2) / (100 - c_2 R)$ | kg of anhydrous crystals |
| 10 | Material balance (solute) | $F c_1 = C (1 + R) + (F - C) c_2$ | Mass of solute in = out |
| 11 | Coefficient of variation | $CV = \sigma_L / \bar{L}$ | Measure of CSD spread |

Where the **standard yield-notation** is:
- $w$ = mass of initial solution (kg)
- $R$ = ratio of molecular mass of hydrate to that of anhydrous form
- $c_1$ = initial concentration (mass % of anhydrous solute)
- $c_2$ = final concentration = solubility at final temperature (mass %)
- $C$ = mass of anhydrous crystals produced
- $F$ = feed solution mass

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Crystallization is the workhorse separation for:
- **Bulk inorganic chemicals** — $\text{NaCl}$, $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, $\text{(NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{KNO}_3$ — produced by evaporative crystallization.
- **Sugar refining** — multi-stage vacuum pan crystallization of sucrose.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — controlled crystallization defines bioavailability, polymorphic form, and shelf-life of drugs.
- **Water treatment** — softening by $\text{CaCO}_3$ precipitation.
- **Nuclear waste** — fractional crystallization separates radioactive isotopes.
- **Materials engineering** — semiconductor and optical single-crystal growth (e.g., silicon) relies on the same fundamental nucleation–growth science.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Balance Yield Equation for Crystallization

This is the most frequently tested 14-mark problem in Module 4. We will derive the yield of **anhydrous crystals** $C$ from a solution that may deposit **hydrated** crystals.

**Given:** A solution of mass $w$ contains a single solute that forms hydrate crystals of formula $\text{A} \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$, where the molecular weight of the hydrate is $M_H$ and of the anhydrous form is $M_A$.

The mass ratio:
$$R = \dfrac{M_H}{M_A} = \dfrac{\text{M.W. of hydrate}}{\text{M.W. of anhydrous salt}} \; > 1$$

> **Note on R:** For a hydrate $\text{A} \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$, the moles of water of crystallization per mole of salt is $n$, and $R = (M_A + 18n)/M_A$. For anhydrous crystals, $R = 1$.

**Step 1 — Statement of solute (anhydrous basis) balance.**

The feed has mass $w$ and contains $c_1$ mass-percent of *anhydrous* solute. Therefore, total anhydrous solute in the feed = $w \cdot c_1 / 100$.

The product has mass $C$ of *anhydrous* crystals. Total anhydrous solute in product = $C$.

The mother liquor has mass $(w - C)$ and contains $c_2$ mass-percent of anhydrous solute (where $c_2$ is the solubility at the final temperature). Total anhydrous solute in mother liquor = $(w - C) \cdot c_2 / 100$.

By conservation of mass (solute):
$$\dfrac{w \, c_1}{100} = C + \dfrac{(w - C) \, c_2}{100}$$

**Step 2 — Expand and collect terms.**

$$\dfrac{w \, c_1}{100} - \dfrac{w \, c_2}{100} + \dfrac{C \, c_2}{100} = C$$

$$\dfrac{w(c_1 - c_2)}{100} = C \, \left(1 - \dfrac{c_2}{100}\right) = C \, \left(\dfrac{100 - c_2}{100}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for C.**

$$C = \dfrac{w(c_1 - c_2)}{100 - c_2}$$

This is the yield when the crystals are **anhydrous** ($R = 1$).

**Step 4 — Generalize for hydrate crystals.**

If the crystals are hydrated, the actual mass of crystals produced, $C_H$, is greater than the anhydrous mass $C$ by the factor $R$:
$$C_H = R \cdot C = \dfrac{w \cdot R \cdot (c_1 - c_2)}{100 - c_2}$$

However, the more commonly written version (and the KTU-board standard) absorbs the factor of 100 differently depending on whether $c_1, c_2$ are expressed as mass fractions or mass %:

> **In mass-fraction form (decimal, e.g. 0.20 = 20%):**
$$Y = \dfrac{w \cdot R \cdot (c_1 - c_2)}{1 - R \cdot c_2}$$

> **In mass-percent form (e.g. 20 = 20%):**
$$Y = \dfrac{w \cdot R \cdot (c_1 - c_2)}{100 - R \cdot c_2}$$

> [!NOTE] **Be careful with units in the denominator.** Both numerator and denominator must use the *same* mass basis. If you use mass%, the 100 must appear in the denominator.

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.**

Take $w = 1000\text{ kg}$, $c_1 = 30\%$, $c_2 = 12\%$, $R = 1.27$ (e.g. $\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$):

$$Y = \dfrac{1000 \times 1.27 \times (30 - 12)}{100 - 1.27 \times 12} = \dfrac{1000 \times 1.27 \times 18}{100 - 15.24}$$

$$Y = \dfrac{22{,}860}{84.76} = 269.7 \text{ kg of hydrated crystals}$$

This matches the textbook result and validates the derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — MSMPR Crystal Size Distribution (CSD)

**Step 1 — Governing equation.**

For a well-mixed, continuous crystallizer operating at steady state with no crystal breakage and no nucleation inside the vessel (birth = death = 0), the population balance reduces to:

$$G \cdot \dfrac{dn}{dL} = -\dfrac{n}{\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Separate the variables.**

Rearranging:
$$\dfrac{dn}{n} = -\dfrac{dL}{G \tau}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate with boundary condition.**

The boundary condition is the *nucleation density* $n^0$ at $L = 0$ (zero-sized crystals):
$$n(L = 0) = n^0$$

Integrating from $L = 0$ to $L$:

$$\int_{n^0}^{n} \dfrac{dn}{n} = -\dfrac{1}{G\tau} \int_{0}^{L} dL$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{n}{n^0}\right) = -\dfrac{L}{G\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Exponential form (final result).**

$$\boxed{\,n(L) = n^0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{L}{G \tau}\right)\,}$$

This is the famous **exponential CSD** of an MSMPR crystallizer.

**Step 5 — Dominant size.**

The dominant crystal size $L_d$ (mode of the *mass* distribution) is:

$$L_d = 3 \, G \tau$$

This is **always larger** than the mean size, since the mass distribution is weighted toward the larger crystals.

**Step 6 — Total number of crystals per unit volume.**

$$N_T = \int_{0}^{\infty} n(L) \, dL = n^0 \cdot G\tau$$

**Step 7 — Total crystal mass per unit volume (slurry density).**

$$M_T = \rho_c \, k_v \int_{0}^{\infty} L^3 \, n(L) \, dL = 6 \, \rho_c \, k_v \, n^0 (G\tau)^4$$

Where $k_v$ is the volume shape factor and $\rho_c$ is the crystal density.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relation between Solubility, Supersaturation, and Temperature (Cooling Crystallization)

For a cooling crystallizer, supersaturation is generated by reducing the temperature of a saturated solution from $T_1$ to $T_2$.

**Step 1 — Material balance on solute.**

Total anhydrous solute = (solute in crystals) + (solute remaining in mother liquor):

$$F \cdot c_1 = C + (F - W_{ev} - C) \cdot c_2$$

Where $W_{ev}$ is the mass of solvent evaporated (zero for pure cooling crystallization).

**Step 2 — Solvent balance.**

For pure cooling ($W_{ev} = 0$):

$$F(1 - c_1/100) = (F - C)(1 - c_2/100)$$

**Step 3 — Solubility change.**

The change in solubility with temperature is approximated linearly for small ranges:

$$c^*(T) = c^*(T_{ref}) + \left(\dfrac{dc^*}{dT}\right)(T - T_{ref})$$

The supersaturation generated upon cooling from $T_1$ to $T_2$ is:

$$\Delta c = c^*(T_1) - c^*(T_2)$$

This is the **driving force** for both nucleation and growth during the cooling process.

**Step 4 — Critical cooling rate.**

If the cooling rate is too high, the system enters the labile zone and produces many fine crystals. The maximum allowable cooling rate to stay in the metastable zone is:

$$\left(\dfrac{dT}{dt}\right)_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta c_{max}}{\left(\dfrac{dc^*}{dT}\right)}$$

Where $\Delta c_{max}$ is the metastable zone width.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism of Each Concept

| Phenomenon | Driving Force | Controlling Step | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Homogeneous primary nucleation** | Very high $\Delta c$ (labile zone) | Statistical molecular clustering | Avoid; control cooling rate |
| **Heterogeneous primary nucleation** | High $\Delta c$ | Foreign surface catalysis | Filter feed, clean equipment |
| **Secondary nucleation** | Low–moderate $\Delta c$ | Crystal-impeller/wall collision | Impeller speed, crystal loading |
| **Crystal growth — bulk diffusion** | $\Delta c$ | Solute transport through boundary layer | Increase $T$, decrease viscosity |
| **Crystal growth — surface integration** | $\Delta c$ | Surface reaction / lattice incorporation | pH, additives, supersaturation |
| **Ostwald ripening** | $\Delta c$ near small crystals | Dissolution of fines onto large ones | Long residence time |
| **Polymorphism** | Solvent, $T$, $\Delta c$ | Different lattice packing | Seed with desired polymorph |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mier's Supersolubility Diagram (Mermaid Block Schematic)

The classical Mier diagram is best represented as a state-space map showing the three zones.

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisX[Temperature T] --> origin[Low T]
    axisY[Concentration c] --> origin
    subgraph zones["Concentration-Temperature State Space"]
        direction TB
        zA["STABLE ZONE (Unsaturated)"]
        zB["METASTABLE ZONE (Crystal growth only)"]
        zC["LABILE ZONE (Spontaneous nucleation)"]
    end
    solCurve["SOLUBILITY CURVE (Saturation)"]
    supCurve["SUPERSOLUBILITY CURVE (Mier Limit)"]
    path1["Cooling path of a saturated solution"] --> path2["Enters metastable zone"]
    path2 --> path3["Seed added, growth occurs"]
    path3 --> path4["Crystal product + mother liquor"]
    feedNode["FEED: Saturated solution at T1"] --> evap1["Evaporate or Cool to T2"]
    evap1 --> supState["Supersaturated state in metastable zone"]
    supState --> seedAdd["Seed crystals added OR spontaneous nuclei form"]
    seedAdd --> growthStep["Crystal growth on seed surfaces"]
    growthStep --> productOut["Filter: Crystals + Mother Liquor"]
    ml["Mother Liquor (recycled)"] --> feedNode
```

### 4.2 Process Block Diagram — Continuous Evaporative Crystallizer

```mermaid
graph LR
    feedIn["Feed Solution (unsaturated or saturated)"] --> preheat["Preheater"]
    preheat --> evap["Evaporator (vacuum/steam)"]
    evap --> vslry["Vapor (sent to condenser)"]
    evap --> supersat["Supersaturated Magma in Crystallizer Vessel"]
    supersat --> msImp["MSMPR Vessel (impeller + baffles)"]
    msImp --> nuclei["Secondary Nucleation by Collision"]
    msImp --> growth["Crystal Growth on existing crystals"]
    growth --> productSlurry["Product Slurry"]
    productSlurry --> filter["Filter / Centrifuge"]
    filter --> crystals["Wet Crystals (PRODUCT)"]
    filter --> motherLiq["Mother Liquor (RECYCLE)"]
    motherLiq --> feedIn
    nuclei --> msImp
```

### 4.3 Nucleation–Growth Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["Supersaturated Solution"]
    start --> check1{"Crystals already present?"}
    check1 -->|"NO"| prim["Primary Nucleation"]
    check1 -->|"YES"| sec["Secondary Nucleation"]
    prim --> p1{"Supersaturation level?"}
    p1 -->|"Low/Moderate (metastable)"| hetero["Heterogeneous (foreign surface)"]
    p1 -->|"Very high (labile)"| homo["Homogeneous (spontaneous)"]
    sec --> s1{"Cause of new nuclei?"}
    s1 -->|"Impeller/wall hit"| contactN["Contact Nucleation"]
    s1 -->|"Fluid shear"| shearN["Shear Nucleation"]
    s1 -->|"Crystal attrition"| attrN["Attrition Nucleation"]
    hetero --> grow["Surface Integration -> Growth"]
    homo --> grow
    contactN --> grow
    shearN --> grow
    attrN --> grow
    grow --> mature["Mature Crystals (Product)"]
```

### 4.4 Crystallizer Types — Functional Architecture Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    typeA["CRYSTALLIZER TYPES"]
    typeA --> batchA["BATCH TYPES"]
    typeA --> contA["CONTINUOUS TYPES"]
    batchA --> b1["Cooling Batch Crystallizer"]
    batchA --> b2["Evaporative Batch (Vacuum Pan)"]
    contA --> c1["MSMPR (Mixed Suspension Mixed Product Removal)"]
    contA --> c2["DTB (Draft Tube Baffled)"]
    contA --> c3["Oslo (Fluidized Bed / Classified Suspension)"]
    contA --> c4["Forced Circulation (FC)"]
    c1 --> note1["Uniform suspension; broad CSD"]
    c2 --> note2["Internal fines destruction; better control"]
    c3 --> note3["Classifies by settling; large uniform crystals"]
    c4 --> note4["High circulation; evaporative; salt, sugar"]
```
