<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 4]**
*Define supersaturation. Explain Mier's supersaturation theory with a neat sketch.*

**Model Answer:**

**Supersaturation** is the state of a solution in which the concentration of dissolved solute exceeds its equilibrium solubility (saturation concentration) at the given temperature. It is the *driving force* for crystallization.

Mathematically:
$$S = c / c^* > 1 \quad \text{or} \quad \Delta c = c - c^* > 0$$

**Mier's theory** (1902) postulates that, in addition to the **solubility curve**, a second curve called the **supersolubility curve** exists, lying *above* the solubility curve. The two curves divide the concentration-temperature plane into three zones:

1. **Stable (unsaturated) zone** — below the solubility curve. Crystallization impossible; crystals will dissolve.
2. **Metastable zone** — between the two curves. Crystal *growth* occurs if seeds are present, but spontaneous *nucleation* does not. This is the desired operating region of an industrial crystallizer.
3. **Labile (unstable) zone** — above the supersolubility curve. Spontaneous primary nucleation occurs vigorously, producing many fine crystals.

> **Sketch (text representation):**
> Concentration (y-axis) vs Temperature (x-axis).
> - Lower curve: Solubility (smooth, increasing with T for most salts).
> - Upper curve: Supersolubility (parallel offset above solubility).
> - Region between them: metastable (working zone).
> - Above upper curve: labile.
> - Below lower curve: stable/unsaturated.

**(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for Mier's theory with sketch)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Exam]**
*Differentiate between primary nucleation and secondary nucleation.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Nucleation | Secondary Nucleation |
|---|---|---|
| **Presence of parent crystals** | No crystals of the solute are present in the solution | Crystals of the same solute are present |
| **Supersaturation required** | Very high (must enter the labile zone) | Low to moderate (occurs within the metastable zone) |
| **Mechanism** | Homogeneous: spontaneous molecular clustering in bulk. Heterogeneous: induced by foreign surfaces/dust | Contact (crystal–impeller/wall collision), fluid shear, attrition |
| **Industrial relevance** | Rarely dominant; problematic (causes fines) | **Dominant** in seeded/continuous industrial crystallizers |
| **Controllability** | Difficult to control | Easily controlled by impeller speed, crystal loading, supersaturation |

**(Mark Distribution: 3 marks for tabulated comparison covering mechanism, supersaturation, industrial relevance)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each. Both Question A and Question B are full alternative choices — students answer **either** A **or** B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define the **ΔL Law** of crystal growth. State its significance in the design of an MSMPR crystallizer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A feed solution of **1000 kg** containing **30 mass %** of anhydrous $\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$ is cooled from 60 °C to 20 °C. The solubility of $\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$ at 20 °C is **42 mass %** (anhydrous basis, in the pentahydrate form, $\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$). The molecular weight of the anhydrous salt is **158 g/mol** and of the hydrate is **248 g/mol**. Calculate the yield of **hydrated** crystals produced. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — McCabe's ΔL Law:**

**Statement:** The ΔL Law, proposed by McCabe, states that the linear growth rate of a crystal face is **independent of the size of the crystal**, provided all crystals in the crystallizer are subjected to identical hydrodynamic and supersaturation conditions. Mathematically:
$$\dfrac{dL}{dt} = G = \text{constant (independent of } L\text{)}$$

**Significance in MSMPR design:**
1. It implies that in a given residence time $\tau$, **every crystal grows by the same absolute length** $\Delta L = G \tau$, regardless of its initial size.
2. It allows the population balance to be solved analytically, leading to the **exponential crystal size distribution** $n(L) = n^0 \exp(-L / G\tau)$.
3. It enables prediction of the **dominant crystal size** $L_d = 3G\tau$ directly from operating conditions.
4. It forms the basis for **scale-up and CSD control** in industrial MSMPR crystallizers used for ammonium sulfate, potassium chloride, etc.

**(Marks: 2 for statement, 3 for significance, 2 for the resulting exponential CSD formula)**

---

**Part (b) — Yield Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $w = 1000 \text{ kg}$ (initial solution)
- $c_1 = 30 \text{ mass \%}$ (initial concentration, anhydrous basis)
- $c_2 = 42 \text{ mass \%}$ (final solubility at 20 °C, anhydrous basis)
- $M_A = 158 \text{ g/mol}$, $M_H = 248 \text{ g/mol}$
- Crystals are pentahydrate, so $R = M_H / M_A$

**Step 1 — Compute R.**
$$R = \dfrac{M_H}{M_A} = \dfrac{248}{158} = 1.5696$$

**Step 2 — Apply yield formula (mass-% basis).**
$$Y = \dfrac{w \cdot R \cdot (c_1 - c_2)}{100 - R \cdot c_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numbers.**

Numerator:
$$1000 \times 1.5696 \times (30 - 42) = 1000 \times 1.5696 \times (-12) = -18{,}835.4$$

Denominator:
$$100 - 1.5696 \times 42 = 100 - 65.92 = 34.08$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Y.**
$$Y = \dfrac{-18{,}835.4}{34.08} = -552.6 \text{ kg}$$

> [!WARNING] **Sign Convention Alert — Common Mistake on KTU Papers!**
> A *negative* yield means the solution at 20 °C is **not supersaturated**; in fact, the solubility (42%) is *higher* than the feed concentration (30%). Cooling actually makes the solution *more undersaturated*, so no crystals form. The student must check whether the solubility decreases or increases on cooling, and the answer may be zero (no crystallization).
>
> **Physical interpretation:** For $\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3$, the solubility *increases* dramatically with temperature; in this problem the feed at 60 °C is *below* the saturation value at 20 °C, so cooling alone does not produce crystals. **Industrial solution:** Evaporate solvent, or seed with a different (correct) supersaturation scenario.

**(Marks: 2 for R, 3 for formula, 2 for conclusion with physical interpretation)**

> **Alternative numerical Q (for practice):** If a *reversed* problem gives $c_1 = 50\%$ (at 60 °C) and $c_2 = 42\%$ (at 20 °C), the yield becomes:
> $$Y = \dfrac{1000 \times 1.5696 \times (50 - 42)}{100 - 1.5696 \times 42} = \dfrac{12{,}556.8}{34.08} = 368.5 \text{ kg of hydrated crystals}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the **solubility-supersolubility diagram** and explain the concept of the **metastable zone**. How is its width controlled in practice? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An MSMPR crystallizer is operating at steady state with a mean residence time of **$\tau = 2$ hours** and a linear growth rate of **$G = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ m/s**. Calculate:
   (i) the **dominant crystal size** $L_d$,
   (ii) the **number density** $n^0$ at $L = 0$ if the slurry contains $N_T = 10^{12}$ crystals per m³, and
   (iii) the **mean crystal size** $\bar{L}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Solubility-Supersolubility Diagram:**

**Sketch description:**

- X-axis: Temperature $T$ (increasing rightward).
- Y-axis: Solute concentration $c$ (increasing upward).
- **Lower curve (Solubility curve):** Smooth, monotonically increasing for most salts. Above this curve the solution is saturated; below it, unsaturated.
- **Upper curve (Supersolubility curve of Mier):** Lies *above* the solubility curve. Above this curve spontaneous nucleation occurs.
- **Stable zone:** Below the solubility curve.
- **Metastable zone:** The shaded band between the two curves. Crystals grow if seeds are added, but spontaneous nucleation is *negligible*. This is the **operating region** of a well-controlled crystallizer.
- **Labile zone:** Above the supersolubility curve.

**Metastable zone width (MSZW):** The horizontal (or vertical) distance between the two curves at any temperature. It is **not a thermodynamic property**; it is a kinetic one and depends on:
- Rate of supersaturation generation (cooling/evaporation rate).
- Agitation intensity.
- Presence of impurities or seed crystals.
- Vessel surface characteristics.
- History of the solution.

**Control of MSZW in practice:**
- **Reduce cooling rate** to widen MSZW and grow larger crystals.
- **Add seed crystals** to consume supersaturation and prevent spontaneous nucleation.
- **Operate at lower supersaturation** to stay deep in the metastable zone.
- **Use additives** that inhibit nucleation (e.g., surfactants).
- **Adequate but not excessive agitation** to homogenize supersaturation without generating excessive contact nuclei.

**(Marks: 2 for sketch description, 2 for metastable zone definition, 3 for control parameters)**

---

**Part (b) — MSMPR Calculations:**

**Given:**
- $\tau = 2 \text{ hours} = 2 \times 3600 = 7200 \text{ s}$
- $G = 5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}$
- $N_T = 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-3}$

**Step 1 — Dominant crystal size $L_d$.**
$$L_d = 3 \, G \, \tau = 3 \times (5 \times 10^{-8}) \times 7200$$
$$L_d = 3 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-4} = 1.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{L_d = 1.08 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Nucleation density $n^0$ at $L = 0$.**

From the MSMPR relation:
$$N_T = \int_{0}^{\infty} n(L) \, dL = n^0 \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{L}{G\tau}\right) dL = n^0 \cdot G\tau$$

Therefore:
$$n^0 = \dfrac{N_T}{G \, \tau} = \dfrac{10^{12}}{(5 \times 10^{-8}) \times 7200}$$

$$n^0 = \dfrac{10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.778 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{n^0 \approx 2.78 \times 10^{15} \text{ crystals per m}^4}$$

**Step 3 — Mean crystal size $\bar{L}$.**

For the exponential distribution, the *number-weighted* mean size is:
$$\bar{L} = G \, \tau = (5 \times 10^{-8}) \times 7200 = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{\bar{L} = 0.36 \text{ mm}}$$

**Note for the student:** The *dominant* size $L_d = 3G\tau = 1.08$ mm is **3 times** the number-mean size $\bar{L} = 0.36$ mm. This is a hallmark of the exponential CSD and is a frequently-asked conceptual question.

**(Marks: 2 for $L_d$, 3 for $n^0$ with full integration, 2 for $\bar{L}$ with comparison)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Lose Marks on Crystallization Questions**
>
> 1. **Sign of yield (Section 5.2 Q-A(b)):** A negative yield is *not* an error; it means supersaturation is not generated. Many students incorrectly write a negative mass as "no yield" without comment. **You must state explicitly** that the feed is *undersaturated* at the final temperature and explain why (solubility increases on cooling, etc.). Expect a deduction of 1–2 marks for missing the physical interpretation.
>
> 2. **Units in yield formula:** Mixing mass-% and mass-fraction conventions is the #1 careless error. If you use $c_1 = 30$, the denominator must contain 100. If you use $c_1 = 0.30$, the denominator must contain 1.
>
> 3. **R factor confusion:** $R$ is the ratio of **molecular weights** (hydrate/anhydrous), NOT the moles of water of crystallization. For $\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $n = 5$ (moles of water) and $R = (158 + 90)/158 = 1.5696$. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Population balance derivation:** Examiners deduct 2 marks for missing the steady-state assumption, 2 more for not stating the boundary condition $n(L=0) = n^0$, and 1 more for not justifying why $B = D = 0$ in a well-mixed MSMPR.
>
> 5. **CSD units:** $n(L)$ has units of $\text{m}^{-4}$ (number per m³ per m of size). Many students incorrectly write $\text{m}^{-3}$. Examiner will deduct 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Mier's diagram:** A sketch *without labels* for the three zones costs 2 marks. A sketch *without* units on axes costs 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Confusing nucleation vs. growth:** Nucleation creates *new* crystals (more particles, smaller mean size); growth enlarges *existing* crystals (same number, larger mean size). Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principles of Crystallization (Module 4)**
>
> - [x] **Crystallization** is a solid–liquid separation driven by **supersaturation**.
> - [x] The **driving force** is $\Delta c = c - c^* > 0$, or the **supersaturation ratio** $S = c/c^* > 1$.
> - [x] Supersaturation is generated by **cooling, evaporation, anti-solvent addition, or chemical reaction**.
> - [x] **Mier's supersolubility curve** divides the state space into **stable, metastable, and labile** zones.
> - [x] Industrial crystallizers operate in the **metastable zone** with controlled seeding.
> - [x] **Primary nucleation** requires very high supersaturation; **secondary nucleation** is dominant in industry.
> - [x] **McCabe's ΔL Law:** $dL/dt = G = $ constant, independent of crystal size.
> - [x] **MSMPR CSD:** $n(L) = n^0 \exp(-L / G\tau)$ — exponential distribution.
> - [x] **Dominant size** $L_d = 3G\tau$ is **3×** the number-mean size $\bar{L} = G\tau$.
> - [x] **Yield formula (mass-%):** $Y = w R (c_1 - c_2) / (100 - R c_2)$.
> - [x] **R = M_H / M_A** (hydrate to anhydrous molecular weight ratio); $R = 1$ for anhydrous crystals.
> - [x] **Always check the sign of $c_1 - c_2$** — a negative result means no crystallization occurs.
> - [x] **Watch the convention** of $c_1, c_2$: mass-percent (denominator = 100) vs mass-fraction (denominator = 1).
> - [x] **Rate processes:** Nucleation rate $B^0 \propto \Delta c^n$ (large $n$); Growth rate $G \propto \Delta c^g$ ($g \approx 1$).
> - [x] **MSZW (metastable zone width)** is a kinetic property — controllable by cooling rate, agitation, seeding, additives.
> - [x] **Crystallizer types:** Batch cooling, Vacuum pan, MSMPR, DTB, Oslo, Forced circulation.
> - [x] **Industrial applications:** Sugar refining, common salt, ammonium sulfate, pharmaceuticals, fertilizers, water softening.
> - [x] **CSD quality metric:** Coefficient of variation $CV = \sigma_L / \bar{L}$; lower $CV$ means more uniform crystals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Purity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Purity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Purity in Mass Transfer Operations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mass Transfer Operations – 1 (Humidification & Dehumidification, Module 4)**, the term **"Purity"** refers to the *absence of non-volatile dissolved solutes* (impurities) in the liquid phase that is brought into contact with a gas stream during gas–vapor mass transfer operations. 

Mathematically, purity is defined by the mole fraction of the liquid solvent (usually water) in the liquid phase:

$$
x_w = 1 - \sum_{i} x_i \quad \text{(where } x_i \text{ = mole fraction of impurity } i\text{)}
$$

A liquid is termed **"pure"** when $x_w \rightarrow 1.0$, meaning it is essentially free from dissolved non-volatile solids. Conversely, an **"impure"** liquid (e.g., brine, caustic solution, glycol solution) carries solutes that depress its vapor pressure, elevate its boiling point, and shift its psychrometric properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Purity is a critical assumption behind several psychrometric equations. Students must recognize *when* the assumption $x_w = 1$ holds (pure water system) and *when* it fails (industrial solutions such as cooling tower circulating water).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sweet vs. Salted Tea" Intuition

Imagine two identical cups of tea, one pure (just water + tea leaves) and one with added **salt** (an impurity). When you blow hot air across both:

- The **pure tea** evaporates readily. Air picks up moisture until it reaches a specific wet-bulb temperature. This wet-bulb temperature matches what you'd predict from adiabatic saturation theory.

- The **salted tea** is reluctant to give up its moisture. The dissolved salt holds water molecules back, lowering the effective vapor pressure at the surface. The wet-bulb temperature now drifts *below* the adiabatic saturation temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** This is exactly why industrial cooling towers using hard water (containing $Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$, $Cl^-$ ions) behave differently from theoretical predictions. The circulating water is *impure*, and engineers must correct the psychrometric calculations for purity effects.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics for Purity

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value (Pure) | Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mole fraction of solvent | $x_w$ | **1.0000** | 0.95 – 0.9999 |
| Mass fraction of water | $\omega_w$ | **1.0000** | 0.90 – 0.9999 |
| Total Dissolved Solids | TDS | **0 ppm** | 200 – 3000 ppm |
| Vapor pressure ratio | $p_w / p_w^{\circ}$ | **1.000** | 0.85 – 0.99 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Purity Matters in Mass Transfer

In humidification operations, the mass transfer driving force between the bulk gas and the liquid interface is governed by the vapor pressure of water at the interface. For a **pure liquid**, this vapor pressure is read directly from steam tables as $p_w^{\circ}$ at the interface temperature $T_i$. However, when non-volatile solutes are dissolved, **Raoult's Law** dictates that the actual vapor pressure is *reduced*:

$$
p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)
$$

This simple linear relationship has profound implications for every psychrometric calculation downstream.

## 2.2 Structured Logic Steps — Effect of Purity

**Step 1: Identify the system purity**
- If $x_w = 1$ → Pure system → Wet-bulb temperature = Adiabatic saturation temperature
- If $x_w < 1$ → Impure system → Discrepancy arises

**Step 2: Quantify the vapor pressure depression**
- Calculate $p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)$
- The driving force for evaporation $\Delta p = p_w - p_{w,\text{bulk}}$ is reduced

**Step 3: Recalculate psychrometric ratios**
- The **humid heat** $C_s$ depends on gas composition, not on liquid purity (unchanged)
- The **humidity** at saturation, $\mathcal{H}_s$, depends on the saturation pressure *at the interface*

**Step 4: Apply corrections to wet-bulb / adiabatic saturation equations**

For a pure system, the well-known relation between wet-bulb and adiabatic saturation temperature is:

$$
\frac{\mathcal{H}_s - \mathcal{H}_1}{T_s - T_1} = \frac{h_y / M_B \cdot k_H}{k_y \cdot C_s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **pure** liquid, the wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ and the adiabatic saturation temperature $T_{as}$ are **numerically identical** because the Lewis relation holds exactly. For an **impure** liquid, they **diverge** — a frequent KTU viva question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Description | Units | Applies To |
|---|---|---|---|
| $p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}$ | Raoult's Law (vapor pressure reduction) | Pa or atm | Impure liquid |
| $\mathcal{H} = \dfrac{M_A \cdot p_A}{M_B \cdot (P - p_A)}$ | Absolute humidity (purity of gas matters) | kg vapor / kg dry air | Any gas–vapor mix |
| $\mathcal{H}_s = \dfrac{M_A \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)}{M_B \cdot (P - p_w^{\circ}(T_i))}$ | Saturation humidity at interface | kg/kg | Pure system |
| $C_s = C_p + \mathcal{H} \cdot C_{pA}$ | Humid heat of gas–vapor mixture | kJ/(kg·K) | Independent of liquid purity |
| $\dfrac{\mathcal{H}_s - \mathcal{H}}{T_i - T} = \dfrac{h_y}{k_y \cdot C_s}$ | Operating line of adiabatic saturator | kg/(kg·K) | Pure system |
| $T_w = T_{as}$ (only if $x_w = 1$) | Equivalence condition | °C or K | Pure system only |
| $\dfrac{h}{k_y \cdot C_s} = c_s$ | Lewis relation (psychrometric ratio) | dimensionless | Air–water at low $\mathcal{H}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The equivalence $T_w = T_{as}$ is a property of **air–pure water** systems only. For **air–impure water** (or air–organic liquid) systems, students must keep these temperatures distinct in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cooling Tower Design:** Circulating water accumulates dissolved solids ($CaCO_3$, silica). Purity drops over cycles of concentration, requiring *blowdown* to maintain purity.
- **Air Conditioning:** Pure water systems are the standard; theoretical psychrometric charts assume pure water.
- **Industrial Drying:** Solvent purity affects drying kinetics for paints, pharmaceuticals, and polymers.
- **Evaporators & Crystallizers:** Purity of feed liquor governs boiling point elevation (BPE), directly tied to mass transfer rates.
- **Dehumidification:** Purity of absorbent (e.g., LiBr, glycol) determines dehumidification capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Vapor Pressure of an Impure Liquid (Raoult's Law Foundation)

Starting from the thermodynamic definition of chemical potential equality at the liquid–vapor interface:

$$
\mu_w^{\text{liq}}(T, P, x_w) = \mu_w^{\text{vap}}(T, P)
$$

For an ideal solution, the liquid chemical potential is:

$$
\mu_w^{\text{liq}} = \mu_w^{\circ,\text{liq}} + RT \ln x_w
$$

The vapor phase (assumed ideal gas) gives:

$$
\mu_w^{\text{vap}} = \mu_w^{\circ,\text{vap}}(T) + RT \ln \dfrac{p_w}{P}
$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$
\mu_w^{\circ,\text{liq}} + RT \ln x_w = \mu_w^{\circ,\text{vap}}(T) + RT \ln \dfrac{p_w}{P}
$$

Recognizing that at equilibrium for a **pure** liquid ($x_w = 1$), $p_w = p_w^{\circ}$, we can rearrange to isolate the impurity effect:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT \ln x_w &= RT \ln \dfrac{p_w}{P} - RT \ln \dfrac{p_w^{\circ}}{P} \\
RT \ln x_w &= RT \ln \dfrac{p_w}{p_w^{\circ}} \\
\ln \dfrac{p_w}{p_w^{\circ}} &= \ln x_w \\
\therefore \quad p_w &= x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Raoult's Law** — the foundational equation linking liquid purity $x_w$ to the actual vapor pressure driving mass transfer.

## 3.2 Derivation — Effect of Purity on Humidity at Saturation

The absolute humidity at saturation for a **pure** system at interface temperature $T_i$ is:

$$
\mathcal{H}_s = \dfrac{M_A \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)}{M_B \cdot \bigl(P - p_w^{\circ}(T_i)\bigr)}
$$

When the liquid is impure, the saturation pressure must be replaced by the *actual* vapor pressure from Raoult's Law:

$$
\mathcal{H}_s^{\text{impure}} = \dfrac{M_A \cdot x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)}{M_B \cdot \bigl(P - x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)\bigr)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the *non-linear* coupling: $x_w$ appears in both numerator and denominator. As $x_w$ decreases, both terms shrink, but the overall humidity drops significantly.

## 3.3 Derivation — Modified Adiabatic Saturation Equation for Impure Liquids

For an adiabatic saturator operating with an impure liquid, the steady-state energy balance gives:

$$
G_s \cdot C_s \cdot (T_s - T_1) = L_s \cdot C_{pL} \cdot (T_s - T_2)
$$

Combined with the mass balance for water:

$$
G_s \cdot (\mathcal{H}_s^{\text{impure}} - \mathcal{H}_1) = L_s \cdot (x_{w,2} - x_{w,1})
$$

Where $x_{w,1}, x_{w,2}$ are the water mass fractions in the liquid entering and leaving. Eliminating $L_s / G_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\mathcal{H}_s^{\text{impure}} - \mathcal{H}_1}{T_s - T_1} &= \dfrac{C_{pL}}{C_s} \cdot \dfrac{(x_{w,2} - x_{w,1})}{(T_s - T_2)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $C_s$ itself contains $\mathcal{H}$, this becomes a transcendental equation solvable iteratively for the impure case.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pure vs. Impure System Comparison

| Property | Pure System ($x_w = 1$) | Impure System ($x_w < 1$) |
|---|---|---|
| Vapor pressure at interface | $p_w^{\circ}(T_i)$ | $x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)$ — **lower** |
| Saturation humidity $\mathcal{H}_s$ | Standard psychrometric value | **Reduced** |
| Wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ | = $T_{as}$ | **<** $T_{as}$ |
| Adiabatic saturation temp $T_{as}$ | Read from charts | Read from charts (gas-side) |
| Driving force for evaporation | Maximum | **Reduced** |
| Mass transfer rate | Maximum | **Reduced** |
| Lewis relation applicability | Exact | Approximate / requires correction |
| Boiling point | Normal | **Elevated** (BPE) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define purity in the context of humidification operations. Why is purity important in mass transfer calculations?

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Purity in humidification operations refers to the absence of non-volatile dissolved solutes in the liquid phase being contacted with the gas stream. Mathematically, it is quantified by the mole fraction of the solvent $x_w$ in the liquid, where $x_w = 1$ represents a pure liquid and $x_w < 1$ represents an impure system.

Purity is important because it directly governs the actual vapor pressure at the gas–liquid interface through Raoult's Law: $p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)$. Impurities reduce the vapor pressure, which in turn reduces the mass transfer driving force $(\Delta p = p_w - p_{\text{bulk}})$, the saturation humidity $\mathcal{H}_s$, and alters the relationship between wet-bulb and adiabatic saturation temperatures. Industrial equipment such as cooling towers must therefore account for purity loss due to dissolved solids to avoid performance deviations from design.

### Question 2: State Raoult's Law and explain its significance for impure liquids in mass transfer operations.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Raoult's Law states that the partial vapor pressure of a solvent above an ideal solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid:

$$
p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T)
$$

Where $p_w$ is the actual vapor pressure of water over the solution, $x_w$ is the mole fraction of water in the liquid, and $p_w^{\circ}(T)$ is the vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature.

**Significance in mass transfer operations:**
1. It quantifies how dissolved impurities depress vapor pressure, reducing the driving force for evaporation.
2. It explains why wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ differs from adiabatic saturation temperature $T_{as}$ in non-ideal systems.
3. It underlies the boiling point elevation (BPE) observed in evaporators and crystallizers handling impure feeds.
4. It allows engineers to predict mass transfer rates in cooling towers where circulating water purity drops over time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between vapor pressure and liquid purity starting from thermodynamic principles. Show clearly how Raoult's Law emerges from equating chemical potentials. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A cooling tower uses circulating water that has accumulated dissolved solids equivalent to a mole fraction $x_w = 0.98$. The dry-bulb temperature of incoming air is $35°\text{C}$ and its wet-bulb temperature is $26°\text{C}$. The total pressure is $101.325 \text{ kPa}$. Compare the saturation humidity $\mathcal{H}_s$ for the impure system against the pure water case at the wet-bulb temperature. Use $M_A = 18 \text{ kg/kmol}$ and $M_B = 29 \text{ kg/kmol}$. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting with equality of chemical potentials at the interface:

$$
\mu_w^{\text{liq}} = \mu_w^{\text{vap}}
$$

For an ideal solution (liquid):

$$
\mu_w^{\text{liq}} = \mu_w^{\circ,\text{liq}} + RT \ln x_w
$$

For an ideal gas (vapor):

$$
\mu_w^{\text{vap}} = \mu_w^{\circ,\text{vap}} + RT \ln \dfrac{p_w}{P}
$$

Equating:

$$
\mu_w^{\circ,\text{liq}} + RT \ln x_w = \mu_w^{\circ,\text{vap}} + RT \ln \dfrac{p_w}{P}
$$

Since $\mu_w^{\circ,\text{liq}} = \mu_w^{\circ,\text{vap}} + RT \ln \dfrac{p_w^{\circ}}{P}$ for pure liquid equilibrium:

$$
RT \ln x_w = RT \ln \dfrac{p_w}{p_w^{\circ}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T)
$$

This is **Raoult's Law**, demonstrating that impurities reduce the vapor pressure proportionally to their mole fraction.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** At $T_w = 26°\text{C}$ (wet-bulb temperature for pure system):

From steam tables, $p_w^{\circ}(26°\text{C}) = 3.363 \text{ kPa}$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\mathcal{H}_s$ for the **pure** system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{H}_s^{\text{pure}} &= \dfrac{M_A \cdot p_w^{\circ}}{M_B \cdot (P - p_w^{\circ})} \\
&= \dfrac{18 \times 3.363}{29 \times (101.325 - 3.363)} \\
&= \dfrac{60.534}{29 \times 97.962} \\
&= \dfrac{60.534}{2840.90} \\
&= 0.02131 \text{ kg vapor / kg dry air}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Raoult's correction for impure system, $x_w = 0.98$:

$$
p_w = 0.98 \times 3.363 = 3.2957 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{H}_s^{\text{impure}} &= \dfrac{18 \times 3.2957}{29 \times (101.325 - 3.2957)} \\
&= \dfrac{59.323}{29 \times 98.029} \\
&= \dfrac{59.323}{2842.85} \\
&= 0.02087 \text{ kg vapor / kg dry air}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the percentage reduction:

$$
\Delta \mathcal{H}_s = \dfrac{0.02131 - 0.02087}{0.02131} \times 100\% = 2.07\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Even a 2% mole fraction of dissolved solids reduces saturation humidity by approximately 2.07%, demonstrating the significant impact of liquid purity on mass transfer performance in cooling towers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the effect of liquid purity on the relationship between wet-bulb temperature and adiabatic saturation temperature. Under what condition does $T_w = T_{as}$ hold exactly? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A brine solution with $x_w = 0.95$ is contacted adiabatically with air at $T_1 = 40°\text{C}$ and $\mathcal{H}_1 = 0.010 \text{ kg/kg}$. The total pressure is $101.325 \text{ kPa}$. Determine the saturation humidity $\mathcal{H}_s$ at the operating interface temperature $T_i = 30°\text{C}$. Compare with the pure water case. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Discussion (7 marks):**

For a **pure liquid** ($x_w = 1$), the wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ and the adiabatic saturation temperature $T_{as}$ are **identical**. This equivalence arises from the Lewis relation, which states that for air–water systems at low humidity:

$$
\dfrac{h_y}{k_y \cdot C_s} \approx c_s \approx 1.0
$$

This relation holds exactly when:
1. The liquid is pure water ($x_w = 1$)
2. The gas phase obeys ideal gas behavior
3. Humidity is low enough that $C_s \approx C_{p,\text{air}}$
4. The interface is at thermodynamic equilibrium

When the liquid is **impure** ($x_w < 1$), Raoult's Law reduces the vapor pressure at the interface, making $T_w < T_{as}$. The wet-bulb reflects the *combined* effect of heat and mass transfer resistances at the interface, while the adiabatic saturation temperature reflects only the energy balance. Impurities break this neat equivalence.

**Practical implication:** In cooling towers with hard water, instruments reading the wet-bulb temperature underestimate the true adiabatic saturation temperature, leading to incorrect cooling range predictions.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Find $p_w^{\circ}$ at $T_i = 30°\text{C}$ from steam tables:

$$
p_w^{\circ}(30°\text{C}) = 4.246 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Raoult's correction:

$$
p_w = 0.95 \times 4.246 = 4.034 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\mathcal{H}_s$ for the **impure** system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{H}_s^{\text{impure}} &= \dfrac{18 \times 4.034}{29 \times (101.325 - 4.034)} \\
&= \dfrac{72.612}{29 \times 97.291} \\
&= \dfrac{72.612}{2821.44} \\
&= 0.02574 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\mathcal{H}_s$ for the **pure** system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{H}_s^{\text{pure}} &= \dfrac{18 \times 4.246}{29 \times (101.325 - 4.246)} \\
&= \dfrac{76.428}{29 \times 97.079} \\
&= \dfrac{76.428}{2815.29} \\
&= 0.02715 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Percentage reduction:

$$
\Delta \mathcal{H}_s = \dfrac{0.02715 - 0.02574}{0.02715} \times 100\% = 5.19\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A 5% impurity mole fraction causes a 5.19% reduction in saturation humidity, confirming that even moderate impurity levels meaningfully impact mass transfer driving forces.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply Raoult's correction** — many students use $p_w^{\circ}$ directly even when $x_w < 1$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Conflating $T_w$ and $T_{as}$** — these are equal ONLY for pure water systems; mixing them in impure systems costs full sub-part marks.
> 3. **Unit inconsistencies** — using $p_w$ in atm without converting $P$ similarly. Always use SI units (Pa or kPa) consistently.
> 4. **Ignoring the non-linear denominator** — $x_w$ appears in BOTH numerator and denominator of $\mathcal{H}_s$, not just one.
> 5. **Forgetting molecular weights** — $M_A = 18$ (water) and $M_B = 29$ (air) are mandatory in every humidity problem.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** — always mention "ideal solution, ideal gas, low humidity" at the start of derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Purity** in humidification refers to absence of non-volatile solutes, quantified by $x_w$.
- **Raoult's Law:** $p_w = x_w \cdot p_w^{\circ}(T_i)$ — the foundational equation.
- **Pure liquid condition:** $x_w = 1$ gives $p_w = p_w^{\circ}$, full driving force.
- **Wet-bulb equals adiabatic saturation temperature ONLY for pure systems.**
- **Impure systems:** $T_w < T_{as}$, reduced $\mathcal{H}_s$, reduced mass transfer rate.
- **Humid heat $C_s$** is independent of liquid purity (depends on gas composition only).
- **Molecular weights:** always $M_A = 18 \text{ kg/kmol}$ for water, $M_B = 29 \text{ kg/kmol}$ for air.
- **Cooling towers** require blowdown to maintain liquid purity and prevent scale formation.
- **Industrial implications:** purity affects cooling range, approach, and tower effectiveness.
- **Assumptions to state:** ideal solution, ideal gas, low humidity (Lewis relation valid).
- **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** is a direct consequence of impurity-induced vapor pressure reduction.
- **Psychrometric charts** assume pure water — corrections needed for industrial solutions.
- **KTU favorite question pattern:** derive Raoult's Law from chemical potential OR compare $\mathcal{H}_s$ for pure vs. impure cases numerically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Mass Transfer Operations, **Yield** is a fundamental performance metric that quantifies the **efficiency of a separation process** by expressing the actual quantity of desired product (or recovered solute) obtained as a fraction of the maximum theoretically obtainable quantity from the feed. It is a dimensionless quantity (often expressed as a percentage) and is one of the most critical parameters evaluated in KTU board examinations for assessing process efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Yield is the ratio of the mass of the desired product (crystals, extract, distillate, or dry product) actually recovered from a separation operation to the mass of that component theoretically available in the feed, expressed either as a fraction or as a percentage.

Mathematically, the generalized yield is expressed as:

$$\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Actual quantity of desired product recovered}}{\text{Theoretical maximum quantity of desired product in feed}} \times 100\%$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Analogy: Sugarcane Juice Extraction**

Imagine you have **10 kg of sugarcane** that theoretically contains **1 kg of sugar** (sucrose). You press the cane and extract the juice, then boil it down to crystallize the sugar. After this entire process, suppose you recover **0.75 kg of pure sugar crystals**.

- The **theoretical yield** = 1 kg (what was there in the cane)
- The **actual yield** = 0.75 kg (what you got)
- The **process yield** = (0.75 / 1) × 100% = **75%**

The missing 25% represents sugar lost in:
- The bagasse (pressed cane residue) → represents **mother liquor losses**
- Spillage during handling → represents **mechanical/handling losses**
- Unconverted dissolved sugar in the liquor → represents **equilibrium limitations**

This intuitive understanding directly mirrors the real engineering challenge in crystallization, extraction, and distillation, where **equilibrium constraints, kinetics, and mechanical handling** dictate how much of the available product we can actually recover.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Yield is *not* the same as **Selectivity** or **Separation Factor**. Selectivity tells you *how pure* your product is, whereas Yield tells you *how much* of the available component you actually captured.

## 1.3 Types of Yield Encountered in Module 4

In Module 4 of PCCHT403, the topic of **Yield** typically applies to three primary separation operations:

| Operation | Definition of Yield | Recovery Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Crystallization Yield** | Mass of crystals obtained per mass of solute in original feed | Solute in feed |
| **Extraction Yield** | Mass of solute transferred to extract phase | Solute in feed |
| **Distillation Yield (Recovery)** | Moles of light key in distillate | Light key in feed |

The standard SI unit of yield is **dimensionless (kg/kg or kmol/kmol)**, though it is conventionally reported as a **percentage (%)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant Used in Yield Calculations:**
> - Molecular weights of solute, solvent, and antisolvent (in **g/mol** or **kg/kmol**)
> - Solubility limits (in **g/100 g solvent** or **kg/kg solvent**)
> - Densities of solutions (in **kg/m³**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Yield Calculations

The concept of yield is rooted in three fundamental engineering realities:

1. **Thermodynamic Equilibrium Limitations:** No real separation process achieves 100% recovery. Equilibrium constraints (vapor-liquid, liquid-liquid, solid-liquid) always leave some target component behind in the residual phase.
2. **Kinetic Limitations:** Mass transfer is governed by finite driving forces and resistance. Insufficient contact time, poor mixing, or low interfacial area reduces recovery.
3. **Mechanical & Handling Losses:** Spillage, entrainment, product adhering to equipment walls, and filter cake retention all reduce the net yield below 100%.

## 2.2 Yield in Crystallization (Most Frequently Tested in KTU)

In a **cooling crystallization** or **evaporative crystallization** process, the feed solution contains the solute dissolved in a solvent. As the solution is cooled or solvent is evaporated, the solubility drops and excess solute crystallizes out. The yield is determined by a **material balance** between solute crystallized and solute originally in solution.

### Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the inlet stream composition:**
The feed contains mass $F$ of solution with solute mass fraction $x_F$ (kg solute/kg solution).

**Step 2 — Identify the outlet streams:**
After crystallization, we get two streams:
- **Crystal product** of mass $C$ (pure or near-pure crystals)
- **Mother liquor (filtrate)** of mass $M$ with solute mass fraction $x_M$

**Step 3 — Apply total material balance:**
$$F = C + M$$

**Step 4 — Apply solute material balance:**
$$F \cdot x_F = C + M \cdot x_M$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the crystal mass $C$:**
$$C = F \cdot \frac{x_F - x_M}{1 - x_M}$$

**Step 6 — Express yield:**
The **theoretical yield** assumes complete recovery of all crystallizable solute, i.e., the mass of solute above the equilibrium solubility at the final temperature. The **actual yield** is the mass of crystals obtained. The **percentage yield** is:

$$\eta_{\text{crystal}} = \frac{C}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100\% = \frac{x_F - x_M}{x_F(1 - x_M)} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Crystallization Yield (used for KTU 14-mark problems):** If the final temperature solubility is $x_M^*$ (mass fraction of solute in mother liquor at equilibrium), the **maximum possible crystal mass** is:
> $$C_{\text{max}} = F \cdot \frac{x_F - x_M^*}{1 - x_M^*}$$
> Students often confuse $x_M$ (actual mother liquor concentration) with $x_M^*$ (equilibrium saturation concentration). The difference is the source of "lost yield."

## 2.3 Yield in Liquid-Liquid Extraction

In a single-stage extraction, the feed (solvent $A$ + solute $C$) is contacted with an immiscible extracting solvent $B$. The yield of solute recovered in the extract phase is:

$$\eta_{\text{extraction}} = \frac{\text{Mass of solute in extract phase}}{\text{Mass of solute in feed}} \times 100\%$$

For a single-stage extraction with distribution coefficient $K = \frac{y}{x}$ (where $y$ is equilibrium concentration in extract and $x$ in raffinate), masses $L$ (feed) and $B$ (solvent):

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + K \cdot (B/L)}$$

$$\eta_{\text{extraction}} = \left(1 - \frac{x_R}{x_F}\right) \times 100\% = \frac{K \cdot (B/L)}{1 + K \cdot (B/L)} \times 100\%$$

## 2.4 Yield (Recovery) in Distillation

In distillation, the **recovery** of the light key component in the distillate is:

$$\text{Recovery} = \frac{D \cdot y_D}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100\%$$

where $D$ is the distillate flow rate, $y_D$ is the mole fraction of light key in distillate, $F$ is the feed flow rate, and $x_F$ is the mole fraction of light key in feed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The terms **Recovery**, **Yield**, and **Fraction Recovered** are used interchangeably in KTU examinations for distillation columns.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Formula | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Yield** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual product}}{\text{Theoretical product}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | **Crystallization Crystal Mass** | $C = F \cdot \dfrac{x_F - x_M}{1 - x_M}$ | $F$ = feed mass (kg), $x_F$, $x_M$ = mass fractions |
| 3 | **Crystallization % Yield** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{x_F - x_M}{x_F(1 - x_M)} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 4 | **Theoretical Max Crystal Mass** | $C_{\text{max}} = F \cdot \dfrac{x_F - x_M^*}{1 - x_M^*}$ | $x_M^*$ = saturation solubility at final T |
| 5 | **Extraction Yield (Single Stage)** | $\eta_e = \dfrac{K \cdot (B/L)}{1 + K \cdot (B/L)} \times 100$ | $K$ = distribution coefficient, $B/L$ = solvent ratio |
| 6 | **Raffinate Concentration** | $x_R = \dfrac{x_F}{1 + K \cdot (B/L)}$ | Mass or mole fraction |
| 7 | **Distillation Recovery** | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{D \cdot y_D}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100$ | $D$, $F$ = flow rates (kmol/h), $x$, $y$ = mole fractions |
| 8 | **Solvent-Free Crystal Yield Basis** | $\eta = \dfrac{C}{F \cdot x_F - M \cdot x_M^*} \times 100$ | Accounts for liquor retention |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Yield is the **economic driver** of every separation process. A low yield means higher raw material costs, larger recycle streams, and increased downstream waste treatment — directly impacting plant profitability and sustainability metrics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Crystallization Yield from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A saturated solution containing **1000 kg** of a salt solution at **80°C** is cooled to **20°C** to crystallize the salt. The feed solution contains **30% by mass** of the salt ($x_F = 0.30$). The solubility of the salt at 20°C is **20 g per 100 g of water** (i.e., $x_M^* = 20/120 = 0.1667$ mass fraction). The actual mother liquor after filtration was found to have a concentration of $x_M = 0.18$. Calculate (a) the theoretical yield, (b) the actual yield, and (c) the percentage loss.

### Part (a) — Theoretical Maximum Crystal Mass

**Step 1:** Identify the mass of solute in the feed.
$$\text{Mass of solute} = F \cdot x_F = 1000 \times 0.30 = 300 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the mass of solvent (water) in the feed.
$$\text{Mass of solvent} = F \cdot (1 - x_F) = 1000 \times 0.70 = 700 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** At the final temperature (20°C), the solubility is $x_M^* = 0.1667$. This means that for every 1 kg of mother liquor, 0.1667 kg is dissolved salt and 0.8333 kg is water.

**Step 4:** Calculate the mass of mother liquor that must exist at equilibrium to retain all the dissolved salt.
$$\text{Mass of mother liquor at equilibrium} = \frac{\text{Mass of solvent}}{1 - x_M^*} = \frac{700}{1 - 0.1667} = \frac{700}{0.8333}$$

$$\text{Mass of mother liquor at equilibrium} = 840.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the mass of salt retained in the mother liquor.
$$\text{Mass of salt in equilibrium mother liquor} = 840.0 \times 0.1667 = 140.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6:** The theoretical maximum crystal mass is the difference between the initial solute and the solute retained in the mother liquor.
$$C_{\text{max}} = 300 - 140.0 = 160.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7:** Verification using the direct formula.
$$C_{\text{max}} = F \cdot \frac{x_F - x_M^*}{1 - x_M^*} = 1000 \times \frac{0.30 - 0.1667}{1 - 0.1667}$$

$$C_{\text{max}} = 1000 \times \frac{0.1333}{0.8333} = 1000 \times 0.1600 = 160.0 \text{ kg} \checkmark$$

### Part (b) — Actual Crystal Mass (using $x_M = 0.18$)

**Step 1:** Apply the actual mother liquor concentration.
$$C_{\text{actual}} = F \cdot \frac{x_F - x_M}{1 - x_M} = 1000 \times \frac{0.30 - 0.18}{1 - 0.18}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the numerator and denominator.
$$C_{\text{actual}} = 1000 \times \frac{0.12}{0.82}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the final value.
$$C_{\text{actual}} = 1000 \times 0.1463 = 146.3 \text{ kg}$$

### Part (c) — Percentage Yield and Loss

**Step 1:** Compute the percentage yield.
$$\eta_c = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100\% = \frac{146.3}{300} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_c = 48.77\%$$

**Step 2:** Compute the percentage loss (due to actual mother liquor being above saturation, indicating wet cake or incomplete washing).
$$\text{Loss} = 100\% - 48.77\% = 51.23\%$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio of actual to theoretical yield.
$$\text{Performance Ratio} = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{max}}} \times 100\% = \frac{146.3}{160.0} \times 100\% = 91.44\%$$

This 91.44% represents the **efficiency** of the operation relative to the thermodynamic maximum.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Single-Stage Extraction Yield

**Problem Setup:** An aqueous feed solution of **100 kg** containing **20 kg of acetic acid (solute)** is extracted once with **100 kg of pure benzene** (extractant). The distribution coefficient $K$ (acid concentration in benzene / acid concentration in water) at the operating temperature is **0.865**. Calculate the yield of acetic acid recovery.

**Step 1:** Express the equilibrium distribution.
$$K = \frac{y}{x} = 0.865$$
where $y$ = kg acetic acid per kg benzene in extract, $x$ = kg acetic acid per kg water in raffinate.

**Step 2:** Apply the solute material balance.
$$L \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_R + B \cdot y_E$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $y_E = K \cdot x_R$ into the balance.
$$L \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_R + B \cdot K \cdot x_R = x_R(L + B \cdot K)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the raffinate concentration.
$$x_R = \frac{L \cdot x_F}{L + B \cdot K} = \frac{100 \times 0.20}{100 + 100 \times 0.865}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the denominator.
$$x_R = \frac{20}{100 + 86.5} = \frac{20}{186.5} = 0.1072 \text{ kg acid/kg water}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the mass of acetic acid in the extract.
$$\text{Acid in extract} = B \cdot y_E = B \cdot K \cdot x_R = 100 \times 0.865 \times 0.1072 = 9.27 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate the extraction yield.
$$\eta_e = \frac{9.27}{20} \times 100\% = 46.35\%$$

**Step 8:** Verify using the direct yield formula.
$$\eta_e = \frac{K \cdot (B/L)}{1 + K \cdot (B/L)} \times 100 = \frac{0.865 \times 1}{1 + 0.865 \times 1} \times 100$$

$$\eta_e = \frac{0.865}{1.865} \times 100 = 46.38\% \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The small discrepancy is due to rounding. In KTU board exams, either approach (component balance or direct formula) is acceptable, but the **direct formula** is preferred for time efficiency.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Distillation Recovery

**Problem Setup:** A distillation column separates a binary mixture. The feed is **100 kmol/h** with $x_F = 0.50$ (light key). The distillate is **45 kmol/h** with $y_D = 0.95$. Calculate the recovery.

**Step 1:** Apply the recovery definition.
$$\text{Recovery} = \frac{D \cdot y_D}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100 = \frac{45 \times 0.95}{100 \times 0.50} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Simplify.
$$\text{Recovery} = \frac{42.75}{50} \times 100 = 85.5\%$$

**Step 3:** Material balance check for bottoms.
$$B = F - D = 100 - 45 = 55 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$x_B = \frac{F \cdot x_F - D \cdot y_D}{B} = \frac{50 - 42.75}{55} = 0.1318$$

This 85.5% recovery means **14.5% of the light key is lost in the bottoms** — a critical design consideration in KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Yield in a Crystallization Operation

The following block-level functional architecture illustrates how the **yield concept** maps onto a typical cooling crystallization process, showing the flow of material, the points of loss, and the corresponding yield-impacting factors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Solution F kg] --> B[Heater or Cooler Unit]
    B --> C[Supersaturated Solution at T2]
    C --> D[Crystallizer Vessel]
    D --> E[Crystal Slurry Formation]
    E --> F[Filter or Centrifuge Unit]
    F --> G[Crystal Product C kg]
    F --> H[Mother Liquor M kg xM]
    H --> I[Recycle or Waste Stream]
    G --> J[Yield Calculation Node]
    A --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Actual Yield percent]
    J --> L[Theoretical Yield percent]
    J --> M[Loss Percentage]
    K --> N[Performance Efficiency Report]
    L --> N
    M --> N

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style G fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    style H fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    style J fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    style N fill:#e2d5f0,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- The **Feed (A)** enters with known mass $F$ and solute concentration $x_F$.
- After **crystallization (D–E)** and **separation (F)**, the system bifurcates into:
  - **Crystal Product (G)** → the desired output, contributing to actual yield.
  - **Mother Liquor (H)** → the residual stream carrying dissolved solute, representing the inherent loss.
- The **Yield Calculation Node (J)** receives the inlet and outlet information to compute actual, theoretical, and loss percentages.
- The **Performance Efficiency Report (N)** consolidates these metrics for the plant engineer.

## 4.2 Yield Impact Factor Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Total Process Yield] --> T[Thermodynamic Loss]
    R --> K[Kinetic Loss]
    R --> M[Mechanical Loss]
    T --> T1[Equilibrium Solubility Limit]
    T --> T2[Azeotrope or Eutectic Pinch]
    K --> K1[Insufficient Contact Time]
    K --> K2[Low Mass Transfer Coefficient]
    K --> K3[Inadequate Interfacial Area]
    M --> M1[Filter Cake Retention]
    M --> M2[Entrainment in Vapor Liquid]
    M --> M3[Handling and Spillage]

    style R fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style T fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    style K fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    style M fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation of the Loss Tree:**
This diagram highlights the **three root causes of yield reduction** in any mass transfer operation. KTU board problems often ask students to identify which factor dominates in a given scenario.

## 4.3 Multi-Stage Countercurrent Extraction Yield Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Single Stage Extraction] --> Y1[Yield 46.4 percent]
    S2[Two Stage Cross Current] --> Y2[Yield 70.2 percent]
    S3[Three Stage Countercurrent] --> Y3[Yield 85.1 percent]
    S4[Infinite Stages Kremser] --> Y4[Yield 95.0 percent max]

    style S1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    style S2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    style S3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    style S4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:** This comparative schematic demonstrates how **increasing the number of equilibrium stages** dramatically improves yield — a concept directly tested in KTU 14-mark problems involving the **Kremser equation**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define "Yield" in the context of a crystallization operation. How is it differentiated from "Recovery" in distillation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Yield in crystallization** is defined as the ratio of the actual mass of crystals obtained to the mass of solute originally present in the feed solution, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta_c = \frac{\text{Actual mass of crystals recovered}}{\text{Mass of solute in the feed}} \times 100\%$$

**Recovery in distillation** is the fraction of the light-key component in the feed that appears in the distillate product:

$$\text{Recovery} = \frac{D \cdot y_D}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100\%$$

**Key Difference:** Yield is calculated on the basis of the **total solute in the feed** (mass balance basis), whereas recovery in distillation is calculated on the basis of the **flow rate and composition** of input and output streams. In practice, both terms convey the same idea — efficiency of capturing the target component — but are conventionally used in different unit operations.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**A solution of 1000 kg containing 25% by mass of Na₂SO₄ is cooled from 80°C to 30°C. The solubility at 30°C is 30.5 g per 100 g of water. Calculate the theoretical yield of crystals (anhydrous Na₂SO₄, MW = 142).**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Mass of Na₂SO₄ in feed.
$$\text{Solute} = 1000 \times 0.25 = 250 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Mass of water in feed.
$$\text{Water} = 1000 - 250 = 750 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Solubility at 30°C in mass fraction.
$$x_M^* = \frac{30.5}{100 + 30.5} = \frac{30.5}{130.5} = 0.2337$$

**Step 4:** Apply the theoretical yield formula.
$$C_{\text{max}} = 1000 \times \frac{0.25 - 0.2337}{1 - 0.2337} = 1000 \times \frac{0.0163}{0.7663} = 21.27 \text{ kg}$$

**Theoretical Yield = 21.27 kg of Na₂SO₄ crystals**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Alert:** Students frequently forget to convert the solubility (g/100 g water) into a mass fraction (kg solute/kg solution) before substituting into the formula. Always perform this conversion explicitly to avoid losing 1 mark on setup.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 4 Choice Question]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the yield of crystals obtained in a cooling crystallization process. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A saturated solution of KNO₃ containing 1500 kg total mass at 60°C (solubility = 110 g/100 g water) is cooled to 20°C (solubility = 32 g/100 g water). If the actual mother liquor analysis shows 35% by mass KNO₃, calculate:
- (i) The theoretical yield of crystals
- (ii) The actual yield obtained
- (iii) The percentage loss compared to theoretical maximum
- (iv) The performance ratio of the actual process. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Derivation:**

Consider a feed solution of mass $F$ kg containing solute with mass fraction $x_F$. After cooling and crystallization:

- Crystal product: mass $C$ kg (pure solute, assuming anhydrous crystals)
- Mother liquor: mass $M$ kg with mass fraction $x_M$

**Assumption:** The crystals formed are pure (anhydrous) and contain no adhering mother liquor.

**Total material balance:**
$$F = C + M$$

**Solute material balance:**
$$F \cdot x_F = C + M \cdot x_M$$

**Eliminating $M = F - C$:**
$$F \cdot x_F = C + (F - C) \cdot x_M$$

$$F \cdot x_F = C + F \cdot x_M - C \cdot x_M$$

$$C(1 - x_M) = F(x_F - x_M)$$

$$\boxed{C = F \cdot \frac{x_F - x_M}{1 - x_M}}$$

**Percentage yield:**
$$\eta_c = \frac{C}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100\% = \frac{x_F - x_M}{x_F(1 - x_M)} \times 100\%$$

For **theoretical maximum**, $x_M$ is replaced by $x_M^*$ (saturation solubility at the final temperature):
$$C_{\text{max}} = F \cdot \frac{x_F - x_M^*}{1 - x_M^*}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $F = 1500$ kg, solubility at 60°C = 110 g/100 g water, at 20°C = 32 g/100 g water.

**Step 1:** Convert 60°C solubility to mass fraction (this is $x_F$, since feed is saturated).
$$x_F = \frac{110}{100 + 110} = \frac{110}{210} = 0.5238$$

**Step 2:** Convert 20°C solubility to mass fraction.
$$x_M^* = \frac{32}{100 + 32} = \frac{32}{132} = 0.2424$$

**Step 3:** Use the actual mother liquor concentration $x_M = 0.35$.

**(i) Theoretical yield:**
$$C_{\text{max}} = 1500 \times \frac{0.5238 - 0.2424}{1 - 0.2424} = 1500 \times \frac{0.2814}{0.7576}$$

$$C_{\text{max}} = 1500 \times 0.3714 = 557.1 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Actual yield:**
$$C_{\text{actual}} = 1500 \times \frac{0.5238 - 0.35}{1 - 0.35} = 1500 \times \frac{0.1738}{0.65}$$

$$C_{\text{actual}} = 1500 \times 0.2674 = 401.1 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Percentage loss compared to theoretical max:**
$$\text{Loss} = \frac{C_{\text{max}} - C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{max}}} \times 100 = \frac{557.1 - 401.1}{557.1} \times 100$$

$$\text{Loss} = \frac{156.0}{557.1} \times 100 = 28.0\%$$

**(iv) Performance ratio:**
$$\text{Performance Ratio} = \frac{401.1}{557.1} \times 100 = 72.0\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Alert (14-mark questions):**
> 1. **Do not skip the derivation** — Part (a) of any yield question expects 4–5 lines of algebraic manipulation. Vague "by material balance" answers lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Unit conversion of solubility** is a notorious mark-killer. Always show the conversion $x = \frac{S}{100 + S}$ where $S$ is solubility in g/100 g water.
> 3. **Distinguish between $x_M$ and $x_M^*$** in the solution. Using the wrong variable is a common 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Round-off to 1 decimal** is the KTU standard. Over-precise values (4 decimal places) are penalized as "presentation errors."

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 4 Choice Question — Alternative]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of yield in single-stage liquid-liquid extraction. Derive the expression for percentage extraction yield in terms of the distribution coefficient $K$ and solvent-to-feed ratio $(B/L)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aqueous solution of 200 kg containing 40 kg of benzoic acid is to be extracted with toluene. The distribution coefficient $K$ (acid in toluene / acid in water) is **12.0**. If **150 kg of toluene** is used:
- (i) Calculate the yield in a **single-stage** extraction.
- (ii) Calculate the yield if the **same total toluene (150 kg)** is split into **two equal cross-current stages** (75 kg each).
- (iii) Compare the two yields and comment on the engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Setup:**
- Feed: mass $L$ kg, solute concentration $x_F$ (kg solute/kg feed solvent)
- Solvent: mass $B$ kg, initially solute-free
- After mixing and settling: raffinate (mass $L$ kg, $x_R$) and extract (mass $B$ kg, $y_E$)

**Equilibrium relation:**
$$K = \frac{y_E}{x_R}$$

**Solute balance:**
$$L \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_R + B \cdot y_E = L \cdot x_R + B \cdot K \cdot x_R$$

**Solving for $x_R$:**
$$x_R = \frac{L \cdot x_F}{L + B \cdot K} = \frac{x_F}{1 + K \cdot (B/L)}$$

**Mass of solute in extract:**
$$\text{Extracted solute} = B \cdot y_E = B \cdot K \cdot x_R = \frac{B \cdot K \cdot L \cdot x_F}{L + B \cdot K}$$

**Percentage extraction yield:**
$$\boxed{\eta_e = \frac{B \cdot K}{L + B \cdot K} \times 100 = \frac{K \cdot (B/L)}{1 + K \cdot (B/L)} \times 100\%}$$

**Sketch** (described verbally): A mixer-settler with the feed entering from the top, solvent from the side, mixed in an agitator, and the two phases separated by gravity in a settler — raffinate exiting from the bottom, extract from the top.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $L = 200$ kg, $x_F = 40/200 = 0.20$, $K = 12.0$, $B_{\text{total}} = 150$ kg.

**(i) Single-stage yield (B = 150 kg):**
$$\eta_{\text{single}} = \frac{K \cdot (B/L)}{1 + K \cdot (B/L)} \times 100 = \frac{12.0 \times (150/200)}{1 + 12.0 \times (150/200)} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{\text{single}} = \frac{12.0 \times 0.75}{1 + 9.0} \times 100 = \frac{9.0}{10.0} \times 100 = 90.0\%$$

**(ii) Two-stage cross-current yield (B = 75 kg per stage):**

For each stage with $B_i = 75$ kg, the fraction of solute remaining after one stage is:
$$f_{\text{remaining}} = 1 - \eta_{\text{single, B=75}} = \frac{1}{1 + K \cdot (B_i/L)}$$

$$f_{\text{remaining}} = \frac{1}{1 + 12.0 \times (75/200)} = \frac{1}{1 + 4.5} = \frac{1}{5.5} = 0.1818$$

After two stages, the cumulative fraction remaining:
$$f_{\text{remaining, total}} = (0.1818)^2 = 0.03306$$

Two-stage yield:
$$\eta_{\text{two-stage}} = (1 - 0.03306) \times 100 = 96.69\%$$

**(iii) Comparison and Engineering Significance:**

| Configuration | Yield | Solvent Used |
|---|---|---|
| Single stage (B = 150 kg) | 90.00% | 150 kg |
| Two cross-current stages (B = 75 + 75 kg) | 96.69% | 150 kg (same total) |

**Comment:** Splitting the same total solvent into multiple stages yields a **6.69% improvement** in recovery with **no additional solvent cost**. This demonstrates the principle that **staging is more effective than increasing solvent quantity** — a fundamental design insight emphasized in KTU's mass transfer curriculum. In industrial practice, multi-stage countercurrent extractors (e.g., Podbielniak, Scheibel columns) exploit this principle to achieve >99% yield with minimal solvent inventory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Alert (Choice Question B):**
> 1. **Stage-wise material balance is mandatory** for multi-stage problems. Skipping intermediate steps for the two-stage calculation loses 3 marks.
> 2. **Show the cross-current formula explicitly** as $f_{\text{remaining}}^n$ for $n$ equal stages — do not just write the final number.
> 3. **Engineering comment in (iii)** carries **2 marks** separately. A one-line "two stages are better" is insufficient; you must quantify and explain.
> 4. **Do not confuse cross-current with countercurrent** — countercurrent (Kremser equation) would give an even higher yield, but the question specifies cross-current.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Yield (Module 4, PCCHT403)**

- **Definition:** Yield = (Actual product recovered) / (Theoretical product in feed) × 100%
- **Crystallization Master Formula:** $C = F \cdot (x_F - x_M) / (1 - x_M)$ — memorize this exactly.
- **Solubility Conversion:** Always convert $S$ (g/100 g water) to $x = S / (100 + S)$ (kg/kg solution) before substitution.
- **$x_M$ vs. $x_M^*$:** Actual mother liquor concentration vs. equilibrium saturation concentration. The difference equals "lost yield."
- **Extraction Yield:** $\eta_e = K \cdot (B/L) / [1 + K \cdot (B/L)] \times 100$ — valid for single-stage with $K$ = distribution coefficient.
- **Distillation Recovery:** Recovery = $D \cdot y_D / (F \cdot x_F) \times 100$ — light key basis.
- **Staging Effect:** Multi-stage extraction (cross-current or countercurrent) always gives higher yield than single-stage for the same total solvent.
- **Assumption for Crystal Purity:** Standard derivation assumes **anhydrous, pure crystals** with no occluded mother liquor. For hydrated crystals, multiply by the hydrate factor (e.g., 1.143 for Na₂SO₄·10H₂O).
- **Material Balance is King:** Every yield problem starts with a solute material balance — set up total + component balances and solve simultaneously.
- **Final Answer Format:** Always present the answer in **kg** (or kmol) and the **percentage** separately, with **1 decimal place** precision as per KTU valuation norms.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not confuse **Yield** with **Selectivity** or **Purity**. Yield = how much; Purity = how clean.
- **Bonus Industrial Insight:** In commercial crystallizers (e.g., Oslo crystallizer), yield is often boosted by **evaporative crystallization** (solvent removal) in addition to cooling, achieving yields >95% even for high-solubility salts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Super-Saturation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Super-Saturation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Super-Saturation** is a thermodynamically non-equilibrium, meta-stable state of a solution in which the concentration of the dissolved solute **exceeds** its normal equilibrium saturation concentration (solubility) at a given temperature. In a super-saturated solution, the chemical potential of the solute is higher than that of the pure solid phase, making the system inherently unstable and prone to spontaneous precipitation or crystallization once activated.

Mathematically, the **Degree of Super-Saturation** is defined as:

$$\Delta C = C - C^*$$

$$\text{Super-saturation Ratio: } \quad S = \frac{C}{C^*}$$

$$\text{Absolute Super-saturation: } \quad \sigma = S - 1 = \frac{C - C^*}{C^*} = \frac{\Delta C}{C^*}$$

Where:
- $C$ = actual concentration of solute in solution (kg solute / kg solvent or kg/m³)
- $C^*$ = equilibrium saturation concentration at the operating temperature (kg solute / kg solvent or kg/m³)
- $S$ = super-saturation ratio (dimensionless)
- $\sigma$ = relative super-saturation (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Super-saturation is the **fundamental driving force** for ALL crystallization operations. Without super-saturation, no crystal nucleation or growth can occur. Every crystallizer design question in KTU exams traces back to establishing and controlling super-saturation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded bus (the solution) during rush hour. The bus has a legal maximum capacity of 40 passengers (this is the **saturation concentration**, $C^*$). If 45 people somehow squeeze in by stuffing themselves into impossible positions, the bus is **super-saturated** ($C > C^*$). The system is unstable — one person shouting "the conductor is coming!" is enough to trigger a chain reaction where multiple people bail out (this is **nucleation** and **crystal growth**), until the passenger count returns to 40.

In chemical terms:
- The "squeeze" of extra solute into solution is the creation of super-saturation.
- The "conductor announcement" represents an external trigger (seed crystal, mechanical shock, dust particle).
- The "people bailing out" represents solute molecules precipitating as solid crystals.

> [!NOTE]
> A super-saturated solution is like a ball resting at the top of a small hill — it has **higher Gibbs free energy** than the equilibrium state. Any tiny disturbance causes it to roll down to the stable, lower-energy state (saturated solution + crystals).

## 1.3 Why Super-Saturation Matters in KTU

Super-saturation directly controls:
1. **Nucleation rate** (how fast new crystals form)
2. **Crystal growth rate** (how fast existing crystals enlarge)
3. **Crystal size distribution** (CSD) — narrow or broad?
4. **Crystal habit** (shape: needles, plates, prisms)
5. **Yield of crystallization process**

## 1.4 The Solubility Curve vs. Super-Saturation Curve

It is critical to distinguish these two curves for KTU problems:

| Curve Type | What it Represents | Location |
|------------|--------------------|----------|
| **Solubility Curve** | True thermodynamic equilibrium between solid and dissolved solute | Lower boundary |
| **Super-solubility Curve** | Limit of meta-stable super-saturation before spontaneous nucleation | Upper boundary |
| **Meta-stable Region** | Zone between solubility and super-solubility — crystals grow but no new nuclei form | Middle zone |
| **Labile / Unstable Region** | Above super-solubility curve — spontaneous nucleation occurs | Upper zone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** Many students confuse *solubility* with *super-saturation*. Solubility is a thermodynamic property (fixed at given T,P). Super-saturation is a **kinetic/operational state** that engineers CREATE and CONTROL.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Super-Saturation

### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Basis

For a solid-liquid equilibrium, the saturation concentration $C^*$ at temperature $T$ follows the **van't Hoff relation** for dilute solutions:

$$C^* = C_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right)$$

Where:
- $C_0$ = solubility at reference temperature $T_0$
- $\Delta H_{sol}$ = enthalpy of solution (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> For most inorganic salts, solubility **increases with temperature** (endothermic dissolution, $\Delta H_{sol} > 0$). Therefore, **cooling a saturated solution is the most common method to generate super-saturation** in KTU problems.

### 2.1.2 Mier's Super-Saturation Theory (1927)

Mier proposed that for any solution, there exist **two distinct curves**:
1. **Solubility curve (lower)** — thermodynamic equilibrium
2. **Super-solubility curve (upper)** — the boundary above which spontaneous nucleation occurs

Between these two curves lies the **meta-stable zone width ($\Delta C_{max}$)** — the heart of industrial crystallization control.

$$\Delta C_{max} = C_{ss} - C^*$$

Where $C_{ss}$ = concentration at the super-solubility boundary.

## 2.2 Five Methods of Creating Super-Saturation (KTU High-Yield Topic)

This is one of the **most frequently asked questions** in KTU Mass Transfer Operations exams.

| # | Method | How it Works | Industrial Example |
|---|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Cooling** | Lower $T$ → lower $C^*$ → solution becomes super-saturated | Batch crystallizers for KNO₃, sugar |
| 2 | **Evaporation** | Remove solvent → increase $C$ above $C^*$ | Salt production from sea water |
| 3 | **Salting-out** | Add a third component (anti-solvent) to reduce solubility | Precipitation of proteins using acetone |
| 4 | **Reaction** | Chemical reaction produces a product whose concentration exceeds its solubility | Precipitation of BaSO₄ from BaCl₂ + H₂SO₄ |
| 5 | **pH change** | Shift pH to reduce solubility of an ionic species | Amino acid, drug crystallization |

## 2.3 Super-Saturation and Crystal Formation

### 2.3.1 Primary Nucleation
- **Homogeneous:** Spontaneous in bulk super-saturated solution, no foreign particles
- **Heterogeneous:** Induced by foreign surfaces (dust, container walls)

$$\text{Primary nucleation rate: } \quad B_p = A \exp\left(-\frac{16\pi \gamma^3 v^2}{3k^3 T^3 (\ln S)^2}\right)$$

Where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential frequency factor
- $\gamma$ = interfacial tension (J/m²)
- $v$ = molecular volume of solute (m³/mol)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $S$ = super-saturation ratio
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **extreme sensitivity of nucleation rate to $S$**. A small change in super-saturation ratio causes an enormous change in nucleation rate. This is why KTU problems emphasize **careful control of $S$**.

### 2.3.2 Secondary Nucleation
- Induced by **existing crystals** in the super-saturated solution
- Dominant mechanism in industrial crystallizers (MSMPR — Mixed Suspension Mixed Product Removal)

### 2.3.3 Crystal Growth Rate

For diffusion-controlled growth:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{k_g A}{L} (C - C^*) = \frac{k_g A}{L} \Delta C$$

Where:
- $k_g$ = growth rate constant (m/s)
- $A$ = surface area of crystal (m²)
- $L$ = characteristic crystal size (m)

> [!NOTE]
> The growth rate is **directly proportional to $\Delta C$** (degree of super-saturation). At low $S$, growth dominates → large crystals. At high $S$, nucleation dominates → many small crystals.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $S = C / C^*$ | Super-saturation ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta C = C - C^*$ | Absolute super-saturation | kg/m³ |
| 3 | $\sigma = (C - C^*)/C^* = S - 1$ | Relative super-saturation | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\Delta C_{max} = C_{ss} - C^*$ | Meta-stable zone width | kg/m³ |
| 5 | $G = k_g \Delta C^n$ | Crystal growth rate law | m/s |
| 6 | $B_p = A \exp(-B / \ln^2 S)$ | Primary nucleation rate | nuclei/(m³·s) |
| 7 | $B_s = k_N M_T^j N^p \Delta C^n$ | Secondary nucleation rate | nuclei/(m³·s) |
| 8 | $Y = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in} - C^*(T_{out})}$ | Crystallization yield | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_{actual}$ | Approach to saturation (cooling) | K or °C |
| 10 | $C^* = f(T)$ from solubility data | Equilibrium concentration | kg/kg solvent |

> [!CAUTION]
> For KTU 14-mark problems, students often confuse $S$, $\Delta C$, and $\sigma$. Memorize: $S$ is a **ratio**, $\Delta C$ is an **absolute difference**, $\sigma$ is a **fractional difference**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Super-saturation control is critical in:
- **Pharmaceutical industry** — controlling crystal habit affects drug dissolution rate
- **Sugar refining** — sucrose crystallization
- **Fertilizer production** — urea, ammonium nitrate
- **Water treatment** — softening via CaCO₃ precipitation
- **Semiconductor manufacturing** — high-purity silicon crystallization
- **Protein and biomolecule purification** — gentle crystallization of delicate molecules
