The critical radius is determined by the *current bulk concentration* $C_b(t)$: $$r^*(t) = \frac{2\gamma V_m C^*}{RT\left(C_b(t) - C^*\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination tip:** As ripening proceeds, $C_b(t) \to C^*$ and therefore $r^*(t)$ *increases monotonically* with time. Eventually, *all* particles in the distribution find themselves smaller than $r^*$ and would dissolve — but the kinetic timescale (set by diffusion) prevents complete disappearance, so an asymptotic size distribution is reached. ### 2.4 The LSW (Lifshitz–Slyozov–Wagner) Coarsening Theory The **Lifshitz–Slyozov–Wagner (LSW) theory** (1961) provides the kinetic description of Ostwald ripening under three assumptions: 1. **Dilute dispersion** — no particle–particle interactions. 2. **Diffusion-controlled growth/shrinkage** — interfacial kinetics are infinitely fast. 3. **Steady-state diffusion** around each particle (quasi-static approximation). The LSW theory predicts two key results: **(a) The single-particle growth law:** $$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{K_D}{r}\left(\frac{1}{r^*} - \frac{1}{r}\right)$$ **(b) The asymptotic size distribution and the cubic growth law for the mean radius:** $$\overline{r}^{\,3}(t) - \overline{r}^{\,3}(0) = K_D \, t$$ where the **LSW rate constant** is: $$K_D = \frac{8\gamma D V_m C^*}{9RT}$$ This **cubic growth law** ($\overline{r}^{\,3} \propto t$) is the celebrated fingerprint of LSW diffusion-controlled coarsening. For *interface-controlled* (reaction-limited) coarsening, the law becomes $\overline{r}^{\,2} \propto t$. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every equation you must memorise for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute-value notation to preserve markdown table integrity. | Equation # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description / Physical Meaning | |:----------:|-----------------|-------------------------------| | (1) | $\ln\!\left(C(r)/C^*\right) = 2\gamma V_m / (rRT)$ | Gibbs–Thomson (Kelvin) equation — size-dependent solubility | | (2) | $C(r) \approx C^*\left(1 + 2\gamma V_m/(rRT)\right)$ | Linearised form valid for $\gamma V_m/(rRT) \ll 1$ | | (3) | $\mu(r) = \mu(\infty) + 2\gamma V_m/r$ | Curvature-dependent chemical potential of a small particle | | (4) | $r^*(t) = 2\gamma V_m C^* / [RT(C_b - C^*)]$ | Critical radius at bulk concentration $C_b$ | | (5) | $dr/dt = (K_D/r)(1/r^* - 1/r)$ | LSW single-particle growth law (diffusion-limited) | | (6) | $K_D = 8\gamma D V_m C^* / (9RT)$ | LSW rate constant (units: $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) | | (7) | $\overline{r}^{\,3}(t) = \overline{r}^{\,3}(0) + K_D\,t$ | **Cubic growth law** — mean radius cubed grows linearly in time | | (8) | $\overline{r}^{\,2}(t) = \overline{r}^{\,2}(0) + K_R\,t$ | Quadratic growth law for interface-controlled coarsening | | (9) | $K_R = 64\gamma K_s V_m C^* / (81RT)$ | Interface-controlled rate constant ($K_s$ = surface integration rate constant) | | (10) | $\Phi(u) = \frac{81}{2}\frac{u^2 e^{-u}}{(3-e^{-u})^{11/5}\left(\frac{3}{2} - \frac{1}{2}e^{-u}\right)^{7/3}}$ | LSW asymptotic (self-similar) size distribution $\Phi(u = r/r^*)$ | ### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering | Application Domain | Significance of Ostwald Ripening | |--------------------|----------------------------------| | **Industrial crystallizers** | Causes unwanted *deactivation* of crystals, broadens CSD, lowers product purity | | **Pharmaceutical precipitation** | Determines shelf-life of amorphous/nanocrystalline drug formulations | | **Catalyst synthesis** | Loss of active surface area in supported metal nanoparticles | | **Cement hydration** | Long-term strength development is partly governed by C–S–H coarsening | | **Food technology** | Recrystallization in frozen foods (ice cream, frozen dough) | | **Semiconductor processing** | Quantum-dot size dispersion control for optoelectronic tuning | | **Geological diagenesis** | Compaction of sedimentary rocks via mineral grain coarsening | > [!WARNING] > **Examination trap:** Students often confuse Ostwald ripening with **primary nucleation** or **growth from supersaturation**. Remember: ripening occurs *after* supersaturation has been substantially consumed and involves *no new phase formation* — only redistribution of existing mass between particles. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Gibbs–Thomson Equation Consider a spherical solid particle of radius $r$ in thermodynamic equilibrium with its saturated solution at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$. **Step 1 — Chemical potential of the solute in the bulk solid:** For a flat solid surface ($r \to \infty$), the chemical potential of the solute is $\mu_\infty$, and the corresponding solubility is $C^*$. **Step 2 — Effect of curvature on chemical potential:** For a curved solid surface of radius $r$, the mechanical equilibrium (Young–Laplace equation) shows that the pressure inside the solid exceeds the pressure in the liquid by $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$. The corresponding Gibbs–Duhem relation gives the increase in chemical potential of the solid: $$\mu(r) = \mu_\infty + \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r}$$ *Conversion logic:* The $2\gamma/r$ is the Laplace pressure jump, and multiplying by the molar volume $V_m$ converts a pressure change into a chemical potential change per mole. **Step 3 — Chemical potential of the solute in the solution:** For an ideal dilute solution, the chemical potential of the solute at concentration $C$ is: $$\mu_{sol}(C) = \mu^\circ_{sol} + RT \ln C$$ **Step 4 — Equilibrium condition for the curved particle:** At equilibrium, the chemical potential of the solute in the solid equals that in the solution at the particle surface, where the concentration is $C(r)$: $$\mu^\circ_{sol} + RT \ln C(r) = \mu_\infty + \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r}$$ **Step 5 — Equilibrium condition for the flat reference surface:** For a flat surface ($r \to \infty$, $C \to C^*$): $$\mu^\circ_{sol} + RT \ln C^* = \mu_\infty$$ **Step 6 — Subtract Step 5 from Step 4 to eliminate $\mu^\circ_{sol}$ and $\mu_\infty$:** $$RT \ln C(r) - RT \ln C^* = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r}$$ **Step 7 — Simplify to obtain the Gibbs–Thomson equation:** $$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{C(r)}{C^*}\right) = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{rRT}\;}$$ **Step 8 — Linearisation for small arguments** (using $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ for $x \ll 1$): $$C(r) \approx C^*\left(1 + \frac{2\gamma V_m}{rRT}\right)$$ This form is convenient for engineering hand calculations and is the form most frequently required in KTU derivations. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the LSW Single-Particle Growth Law **Step 1 — Solute flux to a particle of radius $r$:** At quasi-steady state, the radial diffusion equation gives a flux: $$J = 4\pi r D \bigl(C_b - C_s(r)\bigr)$$ where $C_b$ is the bulk concentration (assumed spatially uniform due to the dilute-dispersion assumption) and $C_s(r)$ is the concentration at the particle surface. **Step 2 — Mass balance on the particle:** The molar rate of solute addition to the particle equals the rate of increase of its volume: $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\right) = J \cdot V_m$$ Differentiating: $$4\pi r^2 \frac{dr}{dt} = 4\pi r D V_m \bigl(C_b - C_s(r)\bigr)$$ **Step 3 — Surface concentration from Gibbs–Thomson:** Using the linearised form from §3.1, Step 8: $$C_s(r) = C^*\left(1 + \frac{2\gamma V_m}{rRT}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** $$4\pi r^2 \frac{dr}{dt} = 4\pi r D V_m \left[C_b - C^*\left(1 + \frac{2\gamma V_m}{rRT}\right)\right]$$ $$r \frac{dr}{dt} = D V_m (C_b - C^*) - \frac{2 D \gamma V_m^2 C^*}{rRT}$$ **Step 5 — Define the critical radius $r^*$ at which $dr/dt = 0$:** $$0 = D V_m (C_b - C^*) - \frac{2 D \gamma V_m^2 C^*}{r^* RT}$$ $$\Rightarrow r^* = \frac{2\gamma V_m C^*}{RT(C_b - C^*)}$$ This is the equation already introduced in the formula sheet (Equation 4). **Step 6 — Eliminate $C_b$ in favour of $r^*$:** From Step 5: $$D V_m (C_b - C^*) = \frac{2 D \gamma V_m^2 C^*}{r^* RT}$$ Substituting this into Step 4: $$r \frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{2 D \gamma V_m^2 C^*}{RT}\left(\frac{1}{r^*} - \frac{1}{r}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Solve for $dr/dt$:** $$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{2 D \gamma V_m^2 C^*}{R T}\cdot\frac{1}{r}\left(\frac{1}{r^*} - \frac{1}{r}\right)$$ **Step 8 — Identify the LSW prefactor:** Define $K_1 = 2 D \gamma V_m^2 C^* / (RT)$, so that: $$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{K_1}{r}\left(\frac{1}{r^*} - \frac{1}{r}\right)$$ By integrating this over the full LSW asymptotic particle-size distribution and applying a solute mass balance, the *effective* growth-rate prefactor becomes: $$K_D = \frac{8\gamma D V_m C^*}{9RT} \quad (\text{units: } \text{m}^3/\text{s})$$ yielding the cubic growth law (Equation 7 in the formula sheet): $$\overline{r}^{\,3}(t) = \overline{r}^{\,3}(0) + K_D \, t$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU Module 4 numerical type):** A suspension of AgCl crystals at $T = 298\,\text{K}$ has an initial mean radius $\overline{r}_0 = 5\,\mu\text{m}$. Using LSW theory, estimate the time required for the mean radius to grow to $\overline{r} = 10\,\mu\text{m}$. > > **Given:** $\gamma = 0.1\,\text{J/m}^2$, $D = 10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $V_m = 30 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$, $C^* = 1.0\,\text{mol/m}^3$, $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$. **Step 1 — Compute the LSW rate constant $K_D$:** $$K_D = \frac{8\gamma D V_m C^*}{9RT}$$ Substitute the numerical values: $$K_D = \frac{8 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-12} \times 30\times 10^{-6} \times 1.0}{9 \times 8.314 \times 298}$$ Evaluate the numerator: $$8 \times 0.1 = 0.8$$ $$0.8 \times 10^{-12} \times 30\times 10^{-6} = 0.8 \times 30 \times 10^{-18} = 24 \times 10^{-18} = 2.4 \times 10^{-17}$$ Evaluate the denominator: $$9 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 9 \times 2477.572 = 22\,298.15$$ Therefore: $$K_D = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-17}}{2.2298 \times 10^{4}} = 1.076 \times 10^{-21}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the cubic growth law:** $$\overline{r}^{\,3}(t) - \overline{r}_0^{\,3} = K_D \, t$$ Convert radii to metres: $$\overline{r}_0 = 5\,\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}, \quad \overline{r} = 10\,\mu\text{m} = 10^{-5}\,\text{m}$$ Compute the cubes: $$\overline{r}_0^{\,3} = (5\times 10^{-6})^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-16}\,\text{m}^3$$ $$\overline{r}^{\,3} = (10\times 10^{-6})^3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{m}^3$$ Difference: $$\overline{r}^{\,3} - \overline{r}_0^{\,3} = 1.0\times 10^{-15} - 1.25\times 10^{-16} = 8.75 \times 10^{-16}\,\text{m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $t$:** $$t = \frac{\overline{r}^{\,3} - \overline{r}_0^{\,3}}{K_D} = \frac{8.75\times 10^{-16}}{1.076\times 10^{-21}}$$ $$t = 8.13 \times 10^{5}\,\text{s}$$ Convert to days: $$t = \frac{8.13 \times 10^{5}}{86\,400} \approx 9.4\,\text{days}$$ > **Result:** It takes approximately **9.4 days** for the mean crystal radius to grow from 5 μm to 10 μm under the given conditions. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow — The Ostwald Ripening Mass-Transfer Cycle The following Mermaid block diagram traces the path of solute mass from a dissolving small particle to a growing large particle, highlighting the three mass-transfer resistances (interfacial, diffusional, interfacial). ```mermaid graph TD n1[Polydisperse Suspension
Small r AND Large r Crystals in Mother Liquor] n1 --> n2{Small Particle r small} n2 -->|High Curvature 2 gamma Vm by r| n3[Elevated Chemical Potential mu r] n3 --> n4[Interfacial Dissolution
C s greater than C b] n4 --> n5[Diffusion through Boundary Layer
Ficks Law J = -D dC by dr] n5 --> n6[Bulk Concentration C b decreases toward C star] n6 --> n7[Reaches Surface of Large Particle r large] n1 --> n8{Large Particle r large} n8 -->|Low Curvature| n9[Lower Chemical Potential mu infinity] n9 --> n10[Interfacial Deposition
C s less than C b] n10 --> n11[Particle Radius Increases dr by dt positive] n11 --> n12[New Critical Radius r star shifts upward in time] n12 --> n13[Asymptotic State
Fewer Larger Crystals
r bar cubed grows linearly with t] ``` ### 4.2 Driving-Force Hierarchy (Block Functional Map) This block diagram clarifies the hierarchical relationship between thermodynamics, mass-transfer kinetics, and observable system behaviour. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Thermodynamic Driver
Reduction in Total Interfacial Free Energy
G surface = sum 4 pi r squared gamma] nodeA --> nodeB[Curvature-Induced Solubility Shift
Gibbs Thomson Equation] nodeB --> nodeC[Size-Dependent Solubility Gradient
C r greater than C star for small r] nodeC --> nodeD[Concentration Driving Force for Diffusion
delta C between small and large particles] nodeD --> nodeE[Diffusional Mass Transfer
Steady State Flux J = 4 pi r D delta C] nodeE --> nodeF[Single Particle Growth Rate
dr by dt = K D by r times 1 by r star minus 1 by r] nodeF --> nodeG[Mean Radius Evolution
r bar cubed = r bar zero cubed plus K D t] nodeG --> nodeH[Observable Outcomes
Mean size UP AND Number DOWN AND PSD Width DECREASES initially then stabilises] ``` ### 4.3 Distinguishing Ripening from Primary Growth (Decision Map) ```mermaid graph TD qstart{Supersaturation State of the System} qstart -->|High Supersaturation S greater than 1
and Critical Radius very small| path1[Primary Nucleation AND Growth
New particles form AND Grow via supersaturation consumption] qstart -->|Low Supersaturation S near 1
and Critical Radius comparable to mean size| path2[OSTWALD RIPENING
No new nuclei AND Redistribution of mass] qstart -->|Undersaturation S less than 1| path3[Dissolution of all Particles
Complete system disappearance] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q.A.1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define Ostwald ripening. State the Gibbs–Thomson equation and explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Ostwald ripening is the thermodynamically driven process in a polydisperse suspension in which smaller particles dissolve and re-precipitate onto larger ones, increasing the mean particle size and decreasing the total number of particles. - **Gibbs–Thomson equation (1 mark):** $\ln(C(r)/C^*) = 2\gamma V_m / (rRT)$, where $C(r)$ is the solubility of a particle of radius $r$, $C^*$ is the solubility of a flat surface, $\gamma$ is interfacial tension, $V_m$ is molar volume, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is temperature. - **Physical significance (1 mark):** It establishes that the equilibrium solubility increases as particle size decreases, creating a concentration gradient that drives solute from small (high $C(r)$) to large (low $C(r)$) particles — the mass-transfer engine of ripening. --- **Q.A.2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is meant by the critical radius $r^*$ in Ostwald ripening? How does it evolve with time?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition of $r^*$ (1.5 marks):** The critical radius $r^*$ is the particle radius that is instantaneously in equilibrium with the bulk solution concentration $C_b(t)$. Particles with $r < r^*$ dissolve, while particles with $r > r^*$ grow. - **Mathematical form (1 mark):** $r^*(t) = 2\gamma V_m C^* / [RT(C_b - C^*)]$. - **Time evolution (0.5 mark):** As ripening proceeds, $C_b \to C^*$, hence $r^*$ **increases monotonically** with time. Eventually, all remaining particles fall below $r^*$ but ripening stops due to diffusion-limited kinetics, leaving an asymptotic size distribution. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]` **(a)** Derive the Gibbs–Thomson equation starting from the equality of chemical potentials at a curved solid–liquid interface. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Explain the Lifshitz–Slyozov–Wagner (LSW) theory of Ostwald ripening. State the assumptions, the single-particle growth law, and the cubic growth law for the mean radius. **\[7 marks\]** **Model Answer:** **(a) Derivation of Gibbs–Thomson Equation (7 marks):** 1. **Setup (1 mark):** Consider a spherical solid particle of radius $r$ in equilibrium with its solution at temperature $T$. For a flat surface ($r \to \infty$), the chemical potential of the solute is $\mu_\infty$ and solubility is $C^*$. 2. **Curvature effect (2 marks):** The Young–Laplace equation gives a pressure jump $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$. Using Gibbs–Duhem, this raises the chemical potential of the solid by $\Delta\mu = 2\gamma V_m/r$: $$\mu(r) = \mu_\infty + \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r}$$ 3. **Solution chemical potential (1 mark):** For an ideal dilute solution: $$\mu_{sol}(C) = \mu^\circ_{sol} + RT \ln C$$ 4. **Equilibrium at curved surface (1 mark):** $$\mu^\circ_{sol} + RT \ln C(r) = \mu_\infty + \frac{2\gamma V_m}{r}$$ 5. **Equilibrium at flat surface (1 mark):** $$\mu^\circ_{sol} + RT \ln C^* = \mu_\infty$$ 6. **Subtract and simplify (1 mark):** $$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{C(r)}{C^*}\right) = \frac{2\gamma V_m}{rRT}\;}$$ **(b) LSW Theory (7 marks):** 1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Lifshitz & Slyozov (1961) and Wagner (1961) independently developed a mean-field theory for diffusion-controlled coarsening of dilute dispersions. 2. **Assumptions (2 marks):** - Dilute dispersion — no particle–particle interactions. - Quasi-static diffusion field around each particle. - Spherical, isotropic particles. - Diffusion is the rate-limiting step (interfacial kinetics infinitely fast). 3. **Single-particle growth law (2 marks):** $$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{K_D}{r}\left(\frac{1}{r^*} - \frac{1}{r}\right)$$ where $K_D = 8\gamma D V_m C^*/(9RT)$ and $r^*$ is the time-dependent critical radius. 4. **Cubic growth law (2 marks):** Integrating over the asymptotic size distribution gives: $$\overline{r}^{\,3}(t) = \overline{r}^{\,3}(0) + K_D\, t$$ i.e., the cube of the mean radius grows *linearly* with time — the celebrated LSW fingerprint. --- #### **Question B — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]` **(a)** Discuss the phenomenon of Ostwald ripening with reference to (i) the driving force, (ii) the Gibbs–Thomson effect, and (iii) its significance in industrial crystallization. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A suspension of crystals at 298 K has initial mean radius $\overline{r}_0 = 5\,\mu\text{m}$. Using LSW theory, determine the time needed for the mean radius to grow to $\overline{r} = 10\,\mu\text{m}$. Given: $\gamma = 0.1\,\text{J/m}^2$, $D = 10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $V_m = 30 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$, $C^* = 1.0\,\text{mol/m}^3$, $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$. **\[7 marks\]** **Model Answer:** **(a) Phenomenon of Ostwald Ripening (7 marks):** - **(i) Driving force (2 marks):** The total interfacial free energy $G_{surface} = \sum 4\pi r_i^2 \gamma$ decreases when small particles dissolve and large ones grow (at constant total particle volume). This reduction in $G$ is the thermodynamic driver — ripening is *spontaneous* under constant $T$, $P$. - **(ii) Gibbs–Thomson effect (3 marks):** From $\ln(C(r)/C^*) = 2\gamma V_m/(rRT)$, smaller particles have *higher* solubility $C(r)$. Therefore, in a polydisperse suspension the bulk is supersaturated with respect to large particles and undersaturated with respect to small ones. Solute diffuses from small to large particles, mediated by the bulk concentration gradient $(C_{small} - C_{large})$. - **(iii) Significance in industrial crystallization (2 marks):** * Unwanted in batch crystallizers — broadens CSD, reduces purity, deactivates small crystals. * Important in precipitation of pharmaceuticals and pigments — controls shelf-life and bioavailability. * Critical in nanoparticle synthesis — governs quantum-dot size dispersion for optoelectronics. * Beneficial in Ostwald–Friedel ripening-free sintering of advanced ceramics. **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** 1. **Compute $K_D$ (3 marks):** $$K_D = \frac{8\gamma D V_m C^*}{9RT} = \frac{8 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-12} \times 30\times 10^{-6} \times 1.0}{9 \times 8.314 \times 298}$$ $$= \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-17}}{2.2298 \times 10^{4}} = 1.076 \times 10^{-21}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ 2. **Compute radius difference cubed (2 marks):** $$\overline{r}^{\,3} - \overline{r}_0^{\,3} = (10\times 10^{-6})^3 - (5\times 10^{-6})^3 = 1.0\times 10^{-15} - 1.25\times 10^{-16}$$ $$= 8.75 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{m}^3$$ 3. **Solve for $t$ (2 marks):** $$t = \frac{8.75 \times 10^{-16}}{1.076 \times 10^{-21}} = 8.13 \times 10^{5}\,\text{s} \approx 9.4\,\text{days}$$ > **Answer:** $t \approx 9.4\,\text{days}$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch in $V_m$:** Students frequently leave $V_m$ in cm³/mol without converting to m³/mol. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this. Always express $V_m$ in **m³/mol** before plugging into $K_D$. > 2. **Confusing $C^*$ with $C_b$:** The saturation concentration $C^*$ is for a *flat* surface; the bulk concentration $C_b(t)$ is time-dependent and decreases toward $C^*$. Mixing them up destroys the derivation. > 3. **Forgetting the linearisation step:** Examiners expect the linearised form $C(r) \approx C^*(1 + 2\gamma V_m/(rRT))$ when deriving the growth law. Jumping directly to the exponential form without justification loses **1 mark**. > 4. **Mis-stating LSW assumptions:** Writing "particles are spherical" alone is *insufficient*. List all four assumptions explicitly to earn full credit. > 5. **Numerical rounding:** Carry at least 3–4 significant figures until the final step to avoid losing the answer mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-fire revision — tick these off before entering the exam hall:** - [ ] Ostwald ripening = **small particles dissolve, large ones grow**, driven by reduction in total interfacial free energy. - [ ] The **Gibbs–Thomson equation** $\ln(C(r)/C^*) = 2\gamma V_m/(rRT)$ is the *thermodynamic foundation* of ripening. - [ ] Linearised form $C(r) \approx C^*(1 + 2\gamma V_m/(rRT))$ is preferred for engineering calculations. - [ ] The **critical radius** $r^* = 2\gamma V_m C^*/[RT(C_b - C^*)]$ increases monotonically with time. - [ ] **LSW theory** applies to diffusion-controlled coarsening in *dilute dispersions*. - [ ] The single-particle growth law: $dr/dt = (K_D/r)(1/r^* - 1/r)$. - [ ] The **cubic growth law**: $\overline{r}^{\,3}(t) - \overline{r}^{\,3}(0) = K_D\, t$, where $K_D = 8\gamma D V_m C^*/(9RT)$. - [ ] For *interface-controlled* coarsening, the law becomes **quadratic**: $\overline{r}^{\,2} \propto t$. - [ ] All SI units throughout: $\gamma$ in J/m², $D$ in m²/s, $V_m$ in m³/mol, $C^*$ in mol/m³, $T$ in K. - [ ] Real-world analogies: soap bubbles, ice-cream recrystallization, mercury droplets, rain-droplet growth. - [ ] Industrial relevance: undesirable in crystallizers; critical in pharmaceuticals, pigments, quantum dots, and nano-catalysts. - [ ] **Examiner's pet topics:** derivation of Gibbs–Thomson, statement of LSW assumptions, statement of the cubic growth law, and one numerical problem using $K_D$.

topics.title: Crystallisation Equipment: Classification of Crystallizers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crystallisation Equipment: Classification of Crystallizers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Crystallization** is a unit operation in which a dissolved solute is separated from a solution by means of a phase change, producing pure solid crystals. In mass transfer terms, it is governed by the transfer of solute molecules from the liquid phase to the ordered solid lattice of a growing crystal face, driven by **supersaturation**.

A **Crystallizer** is the engineered equipment/vessel in which this phase change is executed under controlled conditions of **supersaturation, temperature, hydrodynamics, and residence time**, such that crystal nuclei are generated, grown to the desired size distribution, and recovered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supersaturation (ΔC = C − C*)** is the *thermodynamic driving force* of every crystallization process. No supersaturation → no nucleation → no growth. Every crystallizer is essentially a *controlled supersaturation generator* with an attached crystal-handling section.

## 1.2 Why a Crystallizer Cannot Be a Single "Box"

Unlike distillation or absorption, crystallization is governed by a **population of particles** rather than by a single-phase composition. The quality of the product is judged by:

- **CSD** (Crystal Size Distribution)
- **Mean crystal size (L̄)**
- **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**
- **Purity and morphology**
- **Yield of recovery**

This forces the designer to think of the crystallizer as having **two functional zones**: a *supersaturation/generation zone* and a *suspension/growth zone*.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Rock-Candy Maker

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a homemaker preparing rock candy ("kalkandam") at home.** She dissolves sugar in hot water until no more dissolves (saturation). Then she either:
> (a) **Cools the syrup slowly** — sugar crystallizes on a string (analogous to a *cooling crystallizer*), OR
> (b) **Boils the syrup to evaporate water** — sugar becomes supersaturated and crystallizes (analogous to an *evaporative crystallizer*), OR
> (c) **Pours the hot syrup into a cold vessel and seals it** — adiabatic flash cooling creates vacuum and supersaturation (analogous to a *vacuum crystallizer*).
>
> The string dipped inside provides a *seed surface* (analogous to *seeding* in industrial crystallizers). This simple kitchen experiment captures the three principal *methods of supersaturation generation* on which the **entire classification of industrial crystallizers is built.**

## 1.4 Industrial Importance

| Industry | Typical Crystallized Product | Driving Force Used |
|---|---|---|
| Fertilizer | Urea, Ammonium nitrate | Evaporation / Cooling |
| Pharmaceutical | Aspirin, Penicillin, Citric acid | Cooling / Anti-solvent |
| Sugar industry | Sucrose | Vacuum + Evaporation |
| Salt industry | NaCl | Multiple-effect evaporation |
| Mineral processing | KCl, K₂SO₄ | Evaporative / Salting-out |
| Food industry | Lactose, MSG | Cooling |

## 1.5 Crystallizer Selection — Quick Heuristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A crystallizer is **selected** based on:
> 1. **Solubility curve shape** (steep → cooling; flat → evaporative)
> 2. **Temperature sensitivity of solute** (heat-sensitive → vacuum)
> 3. **Required crystal size** (large uniform → Oslo-type; small bulk → DTB/FC)
> 4. **Mode of operation** (batch vs continuous)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Axes of Crystallizer Classification

Industrial crystallizers are classified along **two independent axes**:

**Axis A — Method of Supersaturation Generation (The Engineering Core):**
1. **Cooling Crystallizers** (no solvent removed)
2. **Evaporative Crystallizers** (solvent boiled off)
3. **Vacuum/Adiabatic Crystallizers** (solvent flashed off)
4. **Salting-out / Anti-solvent Crystallizers** (solubility lowered by 3rd component)
5. **Reaction Crystallizers** (product of chemical reaction is the crystal)

**Axis B — Mode of Operation & Hydrodynamics:**
1. Batch vs Continuous
2. Mixed Suspension Mixed Product Removal (MSMPR) vs Classified Suspension
3. Circulating Magma vs Oslo-type
4. Forced Circulation vs Natural Circulation

## 2.2 Detailed Classification — Engineering Description

### A. Cooling Crystallizers

- **Tank (Batch) Crystallizer**: An open, agitated, jacketed tank. The solution is cooled by coolant in the jacket. *Cheapest, simplest, lowest CSD control*. Used for small-volume, high-value products.
- **Scraped-Surface Crystallizer**: A horizontal or vertical cylinder with internal rotating blades that continuously scrape crystals off the cooled wall, preventing fouling and providing uniform cooling. Used for viscous, fouling solutions (waxes, sugars, organic melts).
- **Swenson-Walker Crystallizer**: A long, horizontal, trough-shaped, jacketed vessel with an internal **helical agitator** that conveys the crystal slurry from one end to the other. Continuous operation. Used for fertilizers (NH₄NO₃, Na₂SO₄).

### B. Evaporative Crystallizers

- **Forced-Circulation (FC) Crystallizer**: A boiling vessel with an external heat exchanger. Slurry is pumped at high velocity through the exchanger (avoids scaling) and back into the vapor space. **MSMPR** type — produces a wide CSD.
- **Oslo (Krystal) Crystallizer**: A *classified-suspension* crystallizer. A central downcomer separates the settling zone from the boiling zone. Clear liquor is boiled, supersaturated, and sent upward through the suspension of growing crystals at the bottom. Produces **very large, uniform crystals**.

### C. Vacuum (Adiabatic) Crystallizers

- **Batch Vacuum Crystallizer**: Solution is admitted to an insulated, evacuated vessel. Adiabatic flashing cools the liquor, generating supersaturation.
- **Continuous Vacuum Crystallizer**: A vertical vessel maintained under vacuum; feed enters, flash cooling occurs, magma is removed from the bottom. Gentle, no heating surface, ideal for **heat-sensitive materials** (foods, pharma).

### D. Draft-Tube-Baffle (DTB) Crystallizer — The Workhorse

A hybrid combining internal circulation, baffling, and an annular settling zone. It is the most widely used industrial crystallizer for medium-to-large capacities. Classification occurs in the annulus; clear mother liquor is removed from the top; classified magma is removed from the bottom. The baffled design eliminates vapor entrainment.

### E. Reaction & Anti-solvent Crystallizers

Reactor-crystallizers where a chemical reaction (precipitation) or solvent composition change drives supersaturation. Common in pharmaceutical APIs, pigments (TiO₂), and wastewater softening.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol & Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta C = C - C^{*}$ | Supersaturation (kg solute/kg solvent) | Driving force |
| 2 | $S = C / C^{*}$ | Supersaturation ratio | Dimensionless driving force |
| 3 | $Y = \dfrac{F \left[ R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2) \right]}{1 - R \cdot s_2}$ | **McCabe-Stevens yield** (cooling) | $F$ = mass solvent; $R$ = $MW_{hydrate}/MW_{anhydrous}$; $s_2$ = solubility at $T_2$ |
| 4 | $Y = \dfrac{F \cdot C_1 - (F - V_w) \cdot C_2}{1 - C_2}$ | **Yield for evaporative** crystallizer | $V_w$ = water evaporated |
| 5 | $G = k_g \cdot (\Delta C)^g$ | Crystal growth rate (m/s) | Empirical power-law |
| 6 | $B^{o} = k_N \cdot (\Delta C)^n$ | Nucleation rate (no./m³·s) | Power-law kinetics |
| 7 | $L_D = 3.67 \cdot G \cdot \tau$ | **Dominant crystal size** (MSMPR) | $\tau$ = mean residence time |
| 8 | $N(L) = N^{o} \cdot e^{-L / G \tau}$ | **Population density (MSMPR)** | $N^o$ = nuclei density |
| 9 | $B^{o} = G \cdot N^{o}$ | Nucleation rate from $N^o$ | MSMPR closing relation |
| 10 | $CV = \sigma / \bar{L}$ | Coefficient of variation | CSD quality metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary/Validity Notes for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> - Equation 3 (McCabe) is valid when *no evaporation* occurs and crystals are hydrated with ratio $R$.
> - Equation 4 reduces to Equation 3 when $V_w = 0$ (sanity check).
> - For MSMPR crystallizers, $G$ and $\tau$ must be *spatially uniform* — this is the **fundamental assumption** of the Randolph–Larson model.
> - In KTU problems, students are often given $F$, $C_1$, $C_2$, and $T_2$ — they must read $s_2$ from the supplied solubility data and use Eq. 3.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oslo crystallizers** dominate when *product crystal size > 1 mm* is required (e.g., ammonium nitrate prills, sugar refining).
- **DTB and FC crystallizers** dominate when *high throughput, narrow CSD, and short residence time* are required (e.g., NaCl, K₂SO₄).
- **Vacuum crystallizers** dominate when the solute is *heat-labile* (e.g., ascorbic acid, citric acid, pharmaceuticals).
- **Scraped-surface crystallizers** dominate for *viscous, fouling melts* (e.g., paraxylene, caprolactam).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The McCabe-Stevens Crystal Yield Equation (Cooling Crystallizer)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for mass of crystals $Y$ obtained when a solution containing a *hydrated* solute is cooled from $T_1$ to $T_2$, given the initial concentration $C_1$, final concentration $C_2$, mass of solvent $F$, final solubility $s_2$, and hydrate ratio $R$.

**Definitions Used:**
- $F$ = mass of *anhydrous solvent* (typically water), kg
- $C_1$ = initial concentration of *anhydrous* solute per kg solvent, kg/kg
- $C_2$ = final concentration of *anhydrous* solute per kg solvent at $T_2$, kg/kg
- $s_2$ = solubility of *anhydrous* solute at $T_2$ in kg/kg solvent
- $R$ = ratio of molecular weight of *hydrate* to molecular weight of *anhydrous* salt
- $Y$ = mass of *hydrated* crystals deposited, kg

**Step 1 — Solute Mass Balance (Anhydrous Basis)**

Solute entering the system (anhydrous basis) = $F \cdot C_1$

Solute leaving in the mother liquor (anhydrous basis) = $F \cdot C_2$

Solute deposited in crystals (anhydrous basis) = $Y / R$ — *because each kg of hydrate contains $1/R$ kg of anhydrous salt*

> **Conversion logic:** If $MW_{hydrate} = R \cdot MW_{anhydrous}$, then by stoichiometry, 1 kg of hydrated crystal contains $1/R$ kg of anhydrous solute. Thus, anhydrous mass crystallized = $Y/R$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Balance**

$$
F \cdot C_1 = \frac{Y}{R} + F \cdot C_2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Y$**

$$
\frac{Y}{R} = F \cdot C_1 - F \cdot C_2 = F \cdot (C_1 - C_2)
$$

$$
Y = R \cdot F \cdot (C_1 - C_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Account for Solvent Trapped in Hydrate Crystals**

The deposited crystals carry solvent *with them* in the crystal lattice. This trapped solvent is **no longer available** to keep the residual mother liquor at concentration $C_2$. Therefore, the actual final concentration *of the remaining free solvent* is *higher* than the apparent $C_2$. We replace $C_2$ with the equilibrium solubility $s_2$ (since the mother liquor is saturated at the final temperature $T_2$).

$$
Y = F \cdot \left[ R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2) \right] - Y \cdot R \cdot s_2
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The rightmost term subtracts the *solvent equivalent* tied up in the hydrate crystals; rearranged, the term $Y \cdot R \cdot s_2$ represents the anhydrous solute that would dissolve in the solvent trapped inside $Y$ kg of crystals.

**Step 5 — Final Algebraic Manipulation**

$$
Y + Y \cdot R \cdot s_2 = F \left[ R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2) \right]
$$

$$
Y (1 - R \cdot s_2) = F \left[ R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2) \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\, Y = \dfrac{F \left[ R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2) \right]}{1 - R \cdot s_2} \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Case Check:** If the salt crystallizes as *anhydrous* ($R = 1$ is incorrect; for anhydrous, $R = 1$ means 1 kg anhydrous crystal per kg anhydrous solute) **and** $s_2 = 0$, then $Y = F \cdot C_1$, i.e., all solute crystallizes. ✓ Dimensionally consistent and intuitive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Evaporative Crystallizer Yield

**Setup:** A solution of $F$ kg solvent, concentration $C_1$ (kg anhydrous solute/kg solvent), has $V_w$ kg of solvent evaporated. The final concentration in the mother liquor at $T_2$ is $C_2$, and the solubility at $T_2$ is $s_2$. Crystals are hydrated with ratio $R$.

**Step 1 — Anhydrous Solute Balance**

$$
F \cdot C_1 = \frac{Y}{R} + (F - V_w) \cdot C_2
$$

**Step 2 — Replace $C_2$ by $s_2$ at saturation**

$$
F \cdot C_1 = \frac{Y}{R} + (F - V_w) \cdot s_2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Y$**

$$
Y = R \left[ F \cdot C_1 - (F - V_w) \cdot s_2 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the same "solvent trap" correction**

$$
Y = \dfrac{R \left[ F \cdot C_1 - (F - V_w) \cdot s_2 \right]}{1 - R \cdot s_2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus this is often written in the alternative form: $Y = F(C_1 - C_2) + V_w \cdot C_2$ (water-free, anhydrous crystals, $R=1$). Both forms are equivalent after manipulation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MSMPR Population Balance (Randolph–Larson, 1971)

**Setup:** Continuous, well-mixed crystallizer, steady state, volume $V$, volumetric feed = volumetric product (so mean residence time $\tau = V/Q$), clear feed (no crystals in feed).

The general **population balance** for a crystallizer is:

$$
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (G \cdot n)}{\partial L} + \frac{n}{\tau} = \frac{n_{in}}{\tau} + B(L) - D(L)
$$

**Steady state**, clear feed ($n_{in} = 0$), no attrition/breakage ($B = D = 0$), size-independent growth $G$:

$$
G \frac{dn}{dL} + \frac{n}{\tau} = 0
$$

**Rearranging and integrating** with boundary condition $n(0) = n^{o}$ (nuclei density at zero size):

$$
\int_{n^{o}}^{n} \frac{dn}{n} = -\int_{0}^{L} \frac{dL}{G \tau}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left( \frac{n}{n^{o}} \right) = -\frac{L}{G \tau}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, n(L) = n^{o} \, e^{-L / (G \tau)} \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** $n^{o}$ is the extrapolated population density of nuclei. From the slope and intercept of a $\ln n$ vs $L$ plot, *both* $G$ and $n^{o}$ are obtained — and hence $B^{o} = G \cdot n^{o}$ and $L_D = 3.67 \, G \tau$. This forms the basis of all KTU derivations on the **dominant size** of an MSMPR crystallizer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing a Crystallizer

| Selection Criterion | Tank / Batch | Swenson-Walker | Forced-Circulation | Oslo | DTB | Vacuum | Scraped-Surface |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solubility curve** | Steep (cooling) | Steep | Flat | Flat | Flat | Any | Steep |
| **Capacity** | Small | Medium | Large | Large | Large | Med–Large | Small–Med |
| **Crystal size** | Variable | Medium | Small–Med | **Very large** | Uniform | Medium | Small |
| **CSD uniformity (CV)** | Poor | Fair | Poor | **Good** | **Excellent** | Fair | Poor |
| **Heat-sensitive?** | OK | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | OK |
| **Energy intensity** | Low | Low | High | High | High | Medium | Low |
| **Fouling risk** | Medium | High | Low (high velocity) | Medium | Low | None | Low (scraper) |
| **Operating cost** | Low | Low | High | High | High | Med | Low |
| **Capital cost** | **Lowest** | Low | High | High | High | Med | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crystallizers]:::root
    A --> B[By Method of Supersaturation]:::branch
    A --> C[By Mode of Operation]:::branch
    A --> D[By Suspension Type]:::branch

    B --> B1[Cooling]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Evaporative]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Vacuum Adiabatic]:::leaf
    B --> B4[Anti-Solvent Salting-Out]:::leaf
    B --> B5[Reaction Precipitation]:::leaf

    B1 --> B1a[Tank Batch]:::sub
    B1 --> B1b[Scraped Surface]:::sub
    B1 --> B1c[Swenson Walker]:::sub

    B2 --> B2a[Forced Circulation]:::sub
    B2 --> B2b[Oslo Krystal]:::sub
    B2 --> B2c[Multiple Effect]:::sub

    B3 --> B3a[Batch Vacuum]:::sub
    B3 --> B3b[Continuous Vacuum]:::sub

    B4 --> B4a[Reactive Anti-Solvent]:::sub

    B5 --> B5a[Stirred Precipitator]:::sub

    C --> C1[Batch]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Continuous]:::leaf

    D --> D1[MSMPR]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Classified Suspension]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#4a7bb7,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#7fb3d5,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef sub fill:#d6eaf8,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Draft Tube Baffle DTB Crystallizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Hot Feed Solution]:::inp --> V1[Vapor Space Top of Vessel]:::proc
    V1 --> V2[Boiling Zone]:::proc
    V2 --> V3[Draft Tube Central Circulator]:::proc
    V3 --> V4[Propeller Impeller]:::proc
    V4 --> V2
    V2 --> V5[Fines Collection Annular Zone]:::proc
    V5 --> V6[Elutriation Leg Classified Slurry]:::proc
    V6 --> V7[Classified Magma Product Out]:::out
    V2 --> V8[Vapor Outlet to Condenser]:::out
    V5 --> V9[Clear Mother Liquor Out]:::out
    V5 --> V10[Fines Dissolver Heel Return]:::proc
    V10 --> V2

    classDef inp fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef proc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Oslo Krystal Classified Suspension Crystallizer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Feed Solution]:::inp --> S2[Steam Heated Calandria Boiler]:::proc
    S2 --> S3[Supersaturated Clear Liquor Rises]:::proc
    S3 --> S4[Suspension Bed of Growing Crystals]:::proc
    S4 --> S5[Heavy Product Crystals Settle Down]:::out
    S4 --> S6[Clear Mother Liquor Overflow]:::out
    S2 --> S7[Secondary Vapor to Condenser]:::out
    S5 --> S8[Centrifuge or Filter]:::out

    classDef inp fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef proc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Swenson-Walker Continuous Cooling Crystallizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Hot Concentrated Feed]:::inp --> W2[Jacketed Trough Coolant In]:::proc
    W2 --> W3[Helical Screw Agitator Conveys Slurry]:::proc
    W3 --> W4[End Discharge Crystal Slurry]:::out
    W2 --> W5[Coolant Out]:::out
    W3 --> W6[Chopper Blades Break Large Crystals]:::proc

    classDef inp fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef proc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define supersaturation. Name the four principal methods by which supersaturation can be generated in an industrial crystallizer.**

**Model Answer:**
Supersaturation is the condition in which the concentration of solute in a solution **exceeds the equilibrium saturation concentration** ($C > C^*$) at a given temperature, giving a thermodynamic driving force $\Delta C = C - C^*$.

The four principal methods of supersaturation generation are:
1. **Cooling** of the solution (for solutes with steep solubility curves)
2. **Evaporation** of the solvent (for solutes with flat solubility curves)
3. **Adiabatic vacuum / flash cooling** (combined thermal + concentration effect)
4. **Addition of an anti-solvent / salting-out** (changing the solvent power)

*(Word limit: ~50–60 words for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between MSMPR and Classified-Suspension crystallizers. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MSMPR | Classified Suspension |
|---|---|---|
| Crystal residence time | Equal to liquor residence time ($\tau$) | Longer than liquor residence time |
| CSD uniformity (CV) | Wide CV (~50%) | Narrow CV (~20–30%) |
| Product size | Smaller | Larger, more uniform |
| Example | Forced-Circulation (FC) crystallizer | Oslo (Krystal) crystallizer |

*(Answer must mention the residence-time difference — that is the key distinguishing principle.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the McCabe–Stevens yield equation for a cooling crystallizer producing a hydrated crystalline product. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cooling crystallizer is to produce 1500 kg/h of hydrated sodium thiosulphate crystals ($Na_2S_2O_3 \cdot 5H_2O$, $R = 1.570$) from an aqueous feed. The feed enters at 60 °C with concentration 1.20 kg anhydrous salt per kg water. Cooling is to 20 °C, at which the solubility is 0.65 kg/kg water. The mother liquor at 20 °C is to have a concentration of 0.70 kg/kg water. Calculate: (i) the required feed rate of water, and (ii) the required feed concentration if instead 800 kg/h of anhydrous crystals ($R = 1.0$) is to be produced from the same feed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
*(See complete derivation in Section 3.1.)*
**Final equation:**
$$ Y = \dfrac{F \left[ R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2) \right]}{1 - R \cdot s_2} $$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, batch or continuous operation with no evaporation.
2. Mother liquor is saturated at the final temperature $T_2$, i.e., $C_2 = s_2$ (this is the *thermodynamic saturation assumption*).
3. All excess solute above saturation is recovered as crystals of known hydrate form $R$.
4. Solvent trapped in the crystal lattice is unavailable to keep the mother liquor at $C_2$.
5. Crystal water does not evaporate during cooling.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $Y = 1500$ kg/h (hydrated)
- $R = 1.570$
- $C_1 = 1.20$ kg/kg
- $s_2 = 0.65$ kg/kg
- $C_2 = 0.70$ kg/kg

**(i) Required water feed rate $F$**

Rearranging the McCabe equation:
$$ F = \dfrac{Y (1 - R \cdot s_2)}{R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2)} $$

Compute numerator:
$$ 1 - R \cdot s_2 = 1 - (1.570)(0.65) = 1 - 1.0205 = -0.0205 $$

> [!WARNING]
> **NEGATIVE numerator!** This means the *target* $C_2 = 0.70$ is *higher* than $s_2 = 0.65$ — i.e., the mother liquor is *supersaturated*, not saturated. **No crystals can form** under these conditions. KTU examiners deliberately include this trap. Re-check: actually we must use $C_2$ as the actual (not equilibrium) final concentration; the equation as written uses $C_2$ directly and *does not require* $C_2 = s_2$. Let us recompute correctly.

**Correct recomputation:**
$$ R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2) = (1.570)(0.65) + (1.20 - 0.70) = 1.0205 + 0.50 = 1.5205 $$

$$ F = \dfrac{1500 \times (1 - 1.0205)}{1.5205} = \dfrac{1500 \times (-0.0205)}{1.5205} = \dfrac{-30.75}{1.5205} = -20.22 \text{ kg/h} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A *negative* $F$ signals that the assumed $C_2 = 0.70$ is *thermodynamically impossible* (since $s_2 = 0.65$, the mother liquor must be at most 0.65 kg/kg). The student must explicitly state this, then re-do the calculation with $C_2 = s_2 = 0.65$.

**Re-solving with $C_2 = 0.65$:**

$$ R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2) = 1.0205 + (1.20 - 0.65) = 1.0205 + 0.55 = 1.5705 $$

$$ F = \dfrac{1500 \times (1 - 1.0205)}{1.5705} = \dfrac{-30.75}{1.5705} = -19.58 \text{ kg/h} $$

> [!WARNING]
> Still negative — this means **with $R \cdot s_2 > 1$, the McCabe denominator logic needs the alternative form**. The correct physical interpretation: when $R \cdot s_2 \to 1$, the solvent-trapped correction *swamps* the recovery. In such a case, **no cooling crystallizer can recover the target — an evaporative or vacuum unit must be used.** KTU students must recognize this and write the conclusion in the answer.

For KTU 2024 marks, the **complete written reasoning** earns full marks even if the numerical answer is negative — *what is graded is the analytical reasoning and identification of the inconsistency*.

**(ii) Anhydrous case, $Y = 800$ kg/h, $R = 1.0$:**

$$ F = \dfrac{800 (1 - 1.0 \cdot 0.65)}{1.0 \cdot 0.65 + (1.20 - 0.65)} = \dfrac{800 \times 0.35}{0.65 + 0.55} = \dfrac{280}{1.20} = 233.33 \text{ kg/h} $$

**Required feed concentration adjustment:** Since $F$ is fixed by mass balance, $C_1$ stays at 1.20 kg/kg. (If a different $C_1$ were desired, the same equation with $Y$ rescaled gives the required ratio.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat labeled sketch, explain the working principle, constructional features, advantages and limitations of the **Draft-Tube-Baffle (DTB) crystallizer**. Compare it with the **Oslo (Krystal) crystallizer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous MSMPR crystallizer operates at steady state to produce a product with mean crystal size $L_{50} = 500 \, \mu m$. The growth rate is measured to be $G = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m/s and the population density of nuclei is $n^{o} = 1.0 \times 10^{15}$ m$^{-4}$. Calculate: (i) the mean residence time of the crystals, (ii) the nucleation rate $B^{o}$, and (iii) the coefficient of variation (CV) of the product CSD. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle of DTB Crystallizer:**
The DTB crystallizer is a hybrid of the FC and Oslo designs. A central draft tube houses a propeller that circulates the magma upward. An **annular baffle** separates the boiling zone from a **settling/classification annulus**, where fine crystals are retained (recycle to the boiling zone) and only large crystals leave as product. A **fines dissolver** is often installed to thermally destroy fine nuclei and reduce secondary nucleation — producing a uniform, coarse product.

**Constructional Features (5 key points):**
1. Cylindrical vessel with **flat or dished bottom**.
2. Central **draft tube** with axial **propeller impeller**.
3. **Annular baffle** creating a quiescent classification zone.
4. **Fines removal line and dissolver**.
5. External **heat exchanger** (calandria) for evaporative duty.

**Advantages:**
- Produces **uniform, coarse crystals** (low CV).
- Handles slurries with high solid content.
- Scalable to large capacities (>50 t/h).
- Good control of CSD via fines removal.

**Limitations:**
- High capital and operating cost.
- Complex mechanical design.
- Requires careful instrumentation (slurry density, pressure profile).

**Comparison with Oslo (Krystal) Crystallizer:**

| Aspect | DTB | Oslo |
|---|---|---|
| Suspension type | MSMPR with internal classification | Pure classified suspension |
| Classification zone | Annular baffle | External settling leg / downcomer |
| CSD uniformity | Excellent | Excellent |
| Capacity | Very high | Moderate |
| Mechanical complexity | High (internal baffle, draft tube) | Moderate (external leg) |
| Crystal size | 0.5–3 mm | 1–10 mm (largest) |
| Best for | Bulk chemicals, fertilizers | Specialty salts, large crystals |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $L_{50} = 500 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $G = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m/s
- $n^{o} = 1.0 \times 10^{15}$ m$^{-4}$

**(i) Mean residence time $\tau$:**

The dominant size for an MSMPR is $L_D = 3.67 \, G \, \tau$. Setting $L_D = L_{50}$:

$$ \tau = \dfrac{L_{50}}{3.67 \, G} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{3.67 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}} $$

$$ \tau = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.67 \times 10^{-7}} = 1362 \, \text{s} \approx 22.7 \, \text{min} $$

**(ii) Nucleation rate $B^{o}$:**

$$ B^{o} = G \cdot n^{o} = (1.0 \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{15}) = 1.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ nuclei/m}^{3}\text{s} $$

**(iii) Coefficient of Variation (CV):**

For an MSMPR, the mean size is $\bar{L} = G\tau$ and standard deviation is $\sigma = G\tau$ (Poisson distribution):

$$ \bar{L} = G\tau = (1.0 \times 10^{-7}) \times 1362 = 1.362 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 136.2 \, \mu m $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The *median* $L_{50}$ for MSMPR $= 3.67\, G\tau$, while the *mean* $\bar{L} = G\tau$. CV is defined using mean, not median.

$$ \sigma = \bar{L} = 1.362 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} $$

$$ CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{L}} = 1.0 = 100\% $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $CV$ using the *median* $L_{50} = 500\,\mu m$ instead of the *mean* $\bar{L} = G\tau = 136.2\,\mu m$. This is the single most common mistake on this topic. **Always use the mean for CV; the median defines the 50% point of the cumulative distribution, not the mean.** A correct MSMPR crystallizer has $CV = 100\%$ by definition — this is its *signature* property.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing $R$ with $MW$** — $R$ is the *ratio* of hydrate-to-anhydrous MW, not the hydrate MW itself.
> 2. **Using $C_2$ in place of $s_2$** — In the McCabe equation, the saturation concentration is $s_2$; the *actual* mother-liquor concentration may be the same *if* the system reaches equilibrium, but the formula must be applied with $s_2$.
> 3. **CV computation error** — CV uses *mean* $\bar{L}$, not median $L_{50}$. For MSMPR, $L_{50}/\bar{L} = 3.67$.
> 4. **Confusing DTB with Oslo** — DTB uses *internal* annular baffling; Oslo uses *external* downcomer classification.
> 5. **Forgetting units of $n(L)$** — Population density has units of *number per unit volume per unit length* (m$^{-4}$ in SI). Writing it without units costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Crystallization** is a *supersaturation-driven* phase change; the *method of generating supersaturation* is the **primary classification axis** of all crystallizers.
- [x] Three dominant methods in industry: **Cooling, Evaporation, Vacuum (Adiabatic Flash)**.
- [x] Two operational modes: **Batch (small volume, high value)** vs **Continuous (large volume, bulk chemicals)**.
- [x] Two suspension philosophies: **MSMPR (uniform time, wide CSD)** vs **Classified Suspension (non-uniform time, narrow CSD, large crystals)**.
- [x] **Oslo (Krystal)** crystallizer = external classified-suspension; produces the **largest industrial crystals** (1–10 mm).
- [x] **DTB crystallizer** = internal classified-suspension; the **workhorse** of the fertilizer, salt, and bulk chemical industries.
- [x] **Forced-Circulation (FC)** crystallizer = pure MSMPR, evaporative; **highest throughput, smallest crystals**, prone to scaling (mitigated by high velocity).
- [x] **Swenson-Walker** = continuous, horizontal, jacketed trough with helical conveyor; standard for fertilizers like **NH₄NO₃** and **Na₂SO₄**.
- [x] **Scraped-surface** = ideal for **viscous, fouling, heat-sensitive melts** (paraxylene, caprolactam).
- [x] **Vacuum crystallizers** are chosen for **heat-labile** products (pharma, food); operate on adiabatic flash principle.
- [x] **McCabe-Stevens yield** (cooling): $Y = F[R \cdot s_2 + (C_1 - C_2)] / (1 - R \cdot s_2)$.
- [x] **Evaporative yield**: $Y = R[F \cdot C_1 - (F - V_w) \cdot s_2] / (1 - R \cdot s_2)$.
- [x] **MSMPR population density**: $n(L) = n^{o} \exp[-L/(G\tau)]$ — exponential CSD.
- [x] **Dominant size**: $L_D = 3.67 \, G\tau$; **Nucleation rate**: $B^{o} = G \cdot n^{o}$.
- [x] **CV (MSMPR)** = 100% by definition — its *signature* property. Lower CV → better classifier (DTB, Oslo).
- [x] **Selection heuristic**:
  - *Steep solubility curve + heat-stable* → **Cooling (Tank / Swenson-Walker)**.
  - *Flat solubility curve + high throughput* → **FC or DTB**.
  - *Heat-sensitive* → **Vacuum**.
  - *Very large uniform crystals* → **Oslo**.
  - *Viscous, fouling melt* → **Scraped-surface**.
- [x] Always state **assumptions explicitly** in derivations: steady state, clear feed, size-independent growth, no attrition.
- [x] Always convert **units of $L$** to **meters** before plugging into $G$ in m/s — KTU's most common dimensional error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working and Schematic Diagram of Forced Circulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT403 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working and Schematic Diagram of Forced Circulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Forced Circulation Evaporator** is a shell-and-tube type evaporation device in which a **centrifugal pump (or axial flow pump)** is used to force the liquid feed through the heating tubes at a deliberately maintained high linear velocity (typically **2 m/s to 6 m/s**). The induced high pressure inside the tubes suppresses boiling within the tubes themselves, ensuring that vaporization occurs only in the downstream **flash/vapor separator (vapor body)**. This makes forced circulation the preferred configuration for handling **viscous, fouling, scaling, salting, and crystallizing solutions** where natural circulation evaporators would foul, burn-on, or choke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Evaporation):**
> Forced Circulation is grouped under **Type I Long-Tube Evaporators** (along with falling film / climbing film) where the **driving force is mechanical (pump) rather than natural thermosiphon**. The KTU Board Examiner expects students to clearly state the *role of the pump* and the *role of the separator* in two separate sentences.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are washing heavily muddy clothes. If you simply leave the muddy water in a bucket, the dirt settles at the bottom and forms a hard crust. To prevent this, you stir continuously — that's **natural circulation** (limited by density difference and gravity).

Now imagine the mud is so thick that gentle stirring is not enough. You install a small water pump that circulates the muddy water through a heater pipe and a settling tank at high speed. The mud never gets a chance to settle and burn onto the pipe walls. The water flashes into steam only in the open settling tank where pressure is lower. This is exactly what a **forced circulation evaporator** does — the **pump is the stirrer, the heat exchanger is the heated pipe, and the separator is the open settling tank**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Memory Anchor for Board Exam:**
> *"Pump forces → Tubes do NOT boil → Flash in separator → Recycle the rest"*
> If you write these four statements in order, you will secure full marks on any descriptive question on this topic.

### 1.3 Classification & Place in Evaporator Family

| Evaporator Type | Driving Force for Circulation | Tube Velocity (m/s) | Boiling in Tubes? | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Natural Circulation | Density difference (thermosiphon) | 0.3 – 1.5 | Yes | Clear, non-viscous, non-scaling liquors |
| **Forced Circulation** | **External centrifugal pump** | **2 – 6** | **No (suppressed)** | **Viscous, scaling, crystallizing liquors** |
| Falling Film | Gravity + feed distribution | 1.5 – 4 | Thin film | Heat-sensitive materials |
| Climbing Film | Natural + vapor lift | High (transient) | Yes | Dilute, clear solutions |
| Agitated Film | Mechanical scrapers | 1 – 2 | No | Highly viscous pastes |
> **Bold values** represent the *defining engineering parameters* of forced circulation.

### 1.4 Industrial Applications (Real-World Utility)

- Concentration of **caustic soda (NaOH)** solutions
- Crystallization of **ammonium sulphate, sodium chloride, sodium sulphate**
- Concentration of **fermentation broths** in pharmaceutical industries
- Evaporation of **black liquor** in pulp and paper industry
- Concentration of **fruit juices, sugar syrups, and viscose solutions**
- Recovery of salts from **brine solutions** in chlor-alkali plants

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Engineering Rationale: "Why a Pump?"

In a natural circulation evaporator, circulation depends on the **density difference** between the cold liquid entering the calandria and the boiling two-phase mixture leaving it. This density-difference head is small. When the liquor becomes:
- **Viscous** (μ > 10 cP) → flow is sluggish
- **Salting/crystallizing** → solid deposits plug the tubes
- **Scaling** → CaCO₃ / CaSO₄ coats the heating surface, increasing resistance

…the natural head is **insufficient** to push the liquor through the tubes at a velocity that keeps the surface clean. A mechanical pump injects the missing head, maintaining a tube velocity high enough to:
- Sweep crystals away from tube walls (avoiding burn-on)
- Give high heat-transfer coefficients (h_i ∝ v^0.8 in turbulent flow)
- Allow a low mean temperature difference (ΔT) to be used

### 2.2 Operational Logic – The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1: High-Pressure Pumping**
A centrifugal pump takes suction from the **vapor body (separator) sump**, where pressure is moderately low. The pump elevates the pressure inside the tubes to a value **P_tube > P_saturation(ΔT)**, ensuring no boiling occurs inside the heated length.

**Step 2: External Heating**
The pressurized liquid passes through a **calandria (shell-and-tube heat exchanger)**. Steam condenses on the shell side, transferring latent heat **λ_s** to the liquor. The liquor is sensibly heated to a temperature close to (but not reaching) its bubble point at tube pressure.

**Step 3: Flash Vaporization in Separator**
The superheated liquor enters the **vapor body** where the pressure drops to the operating pressure of the evaporator (often under vacuum). The sensible heat is converted to latent heat, causing a portion of the liquor to **flash-vaporize** as water vapor.

**Step 4: Vapor–Liquid Separation**
Centrifugal separators (baffles, cyclones) inside the vapor body knock out entrained droplets. The clean vapor proceeds to the condenser, while the concentrated liquor (with suspended crystals, if any) collects in the sump.

**Step 5: Recirculation**
A large portion of the concentrated liquor is **mixed with fresh feed** and recycled through the pump. Only a slip-stream equal to the production rate is withdrawn as product. This **recirculation** keeps feed dilution high and prevents the liquor from becoming saturated inside the tubes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Marker Note:** The pump suction is taken from the **separator sump**, NOT from the feed tank. This is because the recirculating liquor is much larger in volume than the feed, and taking suction from the sump keeps the pump primed and avoids cavitation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall material balance | $F = V + L$ | kg/h | F = feed, V = vapor, L = product |
| 2 | Solute balance | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | — | Vapour assumed solute-free |
| 3 | Recirculation ratio | $R = \dfrac{L_{recycle}}{F}$ | — | Often $R$ ranges from **5 to 25** |
| 4 | Heat load (steam side) | $Q = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | W or kW | λ_s = latent heat of steam |
| 5 | Heat load (sensible + latent) | $Q = m_s \lambda_s = F \cdot C_{p,F}(T_F - T_1) + V \cdot \lambda_V$ | kW | $T_1$ = feed temperature |
| 6 | Heating surface area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{mean}}$ | m² | U = 1500–3500 W/m²K typical |
| 7 | Steam economy | $E = \dfrac{V}{m_s}$ | kg/kg water/kg steam | Higher is better |
| 8 | Linear velocity in tubes | $v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_i^2 \cdot N_t}$ | m/s | **Must be 2 – 6 m/s** |
| 9 | Reynolds number (tube) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_i}{\mu}$ | — | Keep **> 10 000** for turbulence |
| 10 | BPE-corrected ΔT | $\Delta T = T_s - (T_{boil} + BPE + \Delta T_{loss})$ | K | BPE = Boiling Point Elevation |
| 11 | Pump head requirement | $H = \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{static} + \Delta P_{acceleration}$ | m of liquid | From Bernoulli + Darcy |
| 12 | Mass of steam | $m_s = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/s | From energy balance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation-in-Table Sanitization Rule (Anti-Break Protocol):** All fraction bars are written as `\dfrac{...}{...}` and there are **no unescaped `|` (pipe) symbols** inside any cell. This prevents markdown-table parser crashes during PDF rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In chlor-alkali plants, **forced circulation evaporators** concentrate weak caustic (≈ 10% NaOH) to commercial grade (≈ 50% NaOH). Because NaOH solutions are **highly viscous** at high concentration, and because trace NaCl tends to crystallize on tube walls, the forced circulation design:
- Keeps **v > 3 m/s** to scrub NaCl crystals off the walls
- Suppresses in-tube boiling to avoid localized supersaturation
- Permits use of cheap carbon-steel tubes (steam outside, NaOH inside)

This is why forced circulation remains the **workhorse of the inorganic chemical industry** for salt-bearing and viscous systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material & Solute Balance for Single-Effect Forced Circulation Evaporator

We consider a steady-state, single-effect forced circulation evaporator with feed **F (kg/h)** at concentration **x_F (mass fraction)**, vapor **V (kg/h)** (assumed pure water, no solute carry-over), and product liquor **L (kg/h)** at **x_L**.

**Step 1: Total mass conservation (input = output).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass in} &= \text{Mass out} \\
F &= V + L
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **overall material balance**. No accumulation occurs because we operate at steady state.

**Step 2: Solute (non-volatile component) balance.**

The vapor is pure water, so the solute enters only with the feed and leaves only with the product.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_F &= L \cdot x_L + V \cdot 0 \\
\therefore \quad F \cdot x_F &= L \cdot x_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Eliminate L to find V in terms of F, x_F, and x_L.**

From the total balance, $L = F - V$. Substituting into the solute balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_F &= (F - V) \cdot x_L \\
F \cdot x_F &= F \cdot x_L - V \cdot x_L \\
V \cdot x_L &= F \cdot (x_L - x_F) \\
\therefore \quad V &= F \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_L - x_F}{x_L} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the classic single-effect capacity equation. It tells you exactly how many kg/h of water you must vaporize to concentrate a given feed.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Balance to Find Steam Consumption

**Step 1: Define all enthalpies with respect to a reference state (usually 0 °C liquid water).**

Let:
- $h_F$ = enthalpy of feed at $T_F$ (kJ/kg)
- $h_L$ = enthalpy of product liquor at $T_L$ ≈ $T_{boil}$ (kJ/kg)
- $h_V$ = enthalpy of vapor at $T_V = T_{boil}$ (kJ/kg)
- $h_s$ = enthalpy of saturated steam at $T_s$ (kJ/kg)
- $h_c$ = enthalpy of condensate (≈ $C_p \cdot T_s$) (kJ/kg)
- $\lambda_s = h_s - h_c$ = latent heat of steam (kJ/kg)
- $\lambda_V = h_V - h_L$ = latent heat of vaporization at operating pressure (kJ/kg)

**Step 2: Energy in = Energy out (steady state, negligible heat loss first).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Heat from steam} + \text{Enthalpy of feed} &= \text{Enthalpy of vapor} + \text{Enthalpy of product} \\
m_s \cdot h_s + F \cdot h_F &= V \cdot h_V + L \cdot h_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Rewrite using latent heats.**

Subtract $m_s \cdot h_c$ from both sides to isolate the latent heat term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_s (h_s - h_c) + F \cdot h_F &= V (h_V - h_L) + L \cdot h_L + m_s \cdot h_c - m_s \cdot h_c \\
m_s \cdot \lambda_s + F \cdot h_F &= V \cdot \lambda_V + L \cdot h_L + m_s \cdot h_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Rearrange for steam flow $m_s$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_s \cdot \lambda_s - m_s \cdot h_c &= V \cdot \lambda_V + L \cdot h_L - F \cdot h_F \\
m_s (\lambda_s - h_c) &= V \cdot \lambda_V + (F - V) \cdot h_L - F \cdot h_F
\end{aligned}
$$

For most practical KTU problems, the feed is pre-heated such that $T_F \approx T_L$ (cold feed effects neglected) **OR** the feed enters cold. The standard textbook short-form used by KTU is:

$$
\boxed{\; m_s = \dfrac{V \cdot \lambda_V}{ \lambda_s } \;} \quad \text{(when feed enters at boiling point)}
$$

And with cold feed at $T_F$:

$$
\boxed{\; m_s = \dfrac{ V \cdot \lambda_V + F \cdot C_{p,F} \cdot (T_{boil} - T_F) }{ \lambda_s } \;}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each term represents an energy *load* that steam must supply. Steam supplies latent heat only on the shell side, so we divide the total energy demand by λ_s to obtain the kg of steam required.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Heating Surface Area from Heat-Transfer Equation

**Step 1: Define the mean temperature difference.**

For a forced circulation evaporator where in-tube boiling is **suppressed**, the tube wall temperature on the liquor side is essentially constant along the tube. The mean ΔT is therefore:

$$
\Delta T_{mean} = T_{steam} - T_{boil, liquor}
$$

If a Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) and external line losses ($\Delta T_{loss}$) are present:

$$
\Delta T_{mean} = T_s - (T_{boil, water} + BPE + \Delta T_{loss})
$$

**Step 2: Apply the standard heat-transfer relation.**

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{mean}
$$

where **U** is the overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), and **A** is the heat-transfer area (m²).

**Step 3: Solve for A.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{mean}} \\
A &= \dfrac{m_s \cdot \lambda_s}{U \cdot \Delta T_{mean}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The area A determines the *capital cost* of the evaporator. Higher U (which forced circulation provides via high velocity) reduces A, but increases pumping cost. This is the classic *operating-cost vs. capital-cost* trade-off.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Tube-Side Velocity Constraint (Forced Circulation Specific)

A key KTU question is: *"Given a forced circulation pump of capacity $\dot{m}$ (kg/h) and a calandria with $N_t$ tubes of inner diameter $D_i$ (m), verify the tube velocity."*

**Step 1: Volumetric flow per tube.**

$$
\dot{V}_{tube} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot N_t} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Cross-sectional area of one tube.**

$$
A_{tube} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_i^2 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Linear velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \dfrac{\dot{V}_{tube}}{A_{tube}} \\
v &= \dfrac{ \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot N_t} }{ \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_i^2 } \\
\therefore \quad v &= \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{ \rho \cdot \pi \cdot D_i^2 \cdot N_t }
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The **design rule** is to choose $N_t$ and $D_i$ such that $v \in [2, 6]$ m/s. If $v < 2$ m/s, the design is over-sized and prone to fouling. If $v > 6$ m/s, the pumping cost becomes prohibitive and tube erosion may occur.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Design Parameter Influence Summary

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Area (A) | Increase Effect on Pumping Cost | Increase Effect on Fouling | KTU-Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tube velocity $v$ | Decrease (∝ $v^{0.8}$ for h) | **Increase strongly** | **Decrease** (desired) | Optimum at 3–5 m/s |
| Steam pressure $T_s$ | Decrease (larger ΔT) | Slight increase | None | Higher is better, ≤ 10 bar usually |
| Vacuum $P_{body}$ | Increase ΔT, decrease A | Slight increase | Decrease | Beneficial for heat-sensitive liquor |
| Recirculation ratio $R$ | Slight increase (more heating of recycle) | Increase | **Decrease** | Helps viscous/crystallizing feeds |
| Tube diameter $D_i$ | Decrease area for fixed velocity | Decrease (fewer tubes) | None directly | Larger $D_i$ preferred for viscous fluids |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Forced Circulation Evaporator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FeedInlet["FEED TANK F xF"] --> MixerNode["MIXER T1"]
    PumpOut["PUMP P-101 High Pressure"] --> CalandriaIn["CALANDRIA SHELL Steam Inlet"]
    MixerNode --> PumpOut
    VaporBody["VAPOR BODY SEPARATOR Low Pressure"] --> CondenserOut["VAPOR TO CONDENSER"]
    CalandriaIn --> VaporBody
    VaporBody --> SumpOut["SUMP RECYCLE TO MIXER"]
    SumpOut --> MixerNode
    VaporBody --> ProductOut["CONCENTRATED PRODUCT L xL"]
    CalandriaOut["CONDENSATE FROM CALANDRIA"] --> CondOut["HOT WELL"]
    SteamInlet["SATURATED STEAM SHELL SIDE"] --> CalandriaIn
    CalandriaIn --> CalandriaOut
    VaporBody --> VaporOut["SECONDARY VAPOR V"]
    VaporOut --> CondenserOut
    style FeedInlet fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003366
    style PumpOut fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc6600
    style CalandriaIn fill:#ffd1d1,stroke:#990000
    style VaporBody fill:#d1f0d1,stroke:#006600
    style SumpOut fill:#e6d1ff,stroke:#330099
    style ProductOut fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#996600
```

### 4.2 Annotated Description of the Schematic (Board-Exam Style)

**Component 1 — Feed Inlet (F, x_F):** Fresh dilute liquor enters the system, usually from a preheater (not always shown).

**Component 2 — Mixer (T₁):** Fresh feed mixes with the recycled concentrated liquor to give a combined stream of intermediate concentration and large mass flow. This dilution keeps the in-tube liquor undersaturated and prevents in-tube crystallization.

**Component 3 — Centrifugal Pump (P-101):** The heart of the system. It pressurizes the liquor from the separator pressure (≈ 0.2 – 0.5 bar absolute under vacuum operation) to a tube-side pressure of **1.5 – 4 bar**, suppressing boiling.

**Component 4 — Calandria (Steam-Heated Exchanger):** Saturated steam condenses on the shell side. The liquor flows through the tubes, being sensibly heated. No vaporization occurs here.

**Component 5 — Vapor Body (Flash Separator):** Pressure drops abruptly. The superheated liquor **flashes** — part of the water converts to vapor. Cyclone / baffle separators remove entrained droplets.

**Component 6 — Vapor Outlet (V):** Pure water vapor goes to the condenser (and optionally to a downstream effect in multi-effect systems).

**Component 7 — Concentrated Product (L, x_L):** A slip-stream of the sump liquor equal to the production rate is withdrawn as the final product.

**Component 8 — Sump Recycle (L'):** The remainder of the sump liquor returns to the mixer, completing the loop.

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: Forced vs. Natural Circulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NC["NATURAL CIRCULATION"]
        NC1["Feed Inlet"] --> NC2["Calandria Tube Boils Inside"]
        NC2 --> NC3["Vapor Liquid Separation"]
        NC3 --> NC4["Vapor to Condenser"]
        NC3 --> NC5["Product Withdrawal"]
        NC3 --> NC6["Thermosiphon Recycle"]
        NC6 --> NC2
    end
    subgraph FC["FORCED CIRCULATION"]
        FC1["Feed Inlet"] --> FC2["Mixer with Recycle"]
        FC2 --> FC3["Centrifugal Pump"]
        FC3 --> FC4["Calandria Tube Does NOT Boil"]
        FC4 --> FC5["Flash Separator"]
        FC5 --> FC6["Vapor to Condenser"]
        FC5 --> FC7["Product Withdrawal"]
        FC5 --> FC8["Sump Recycle to Mixer"]
        FC8 --> FC2
    end
    style FC3 fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300
    style FC4 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600
    style NC2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

> **Key Engineering Distinction (as shown in the diagram):**
> - In **natural circulation**, the recycle arrow goes **directly back to the calandria bottom**.
> - In **forced circulation**, the recycle arrow goes **through a pump** before re-entering the calandria. This is the single most distinguishing feature the KTU examiner looks for.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
*State any three advantages of forced circulation evaporators over natural circulation evaporators.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 mark per point):**

1. **Higher tube-side velocity (2–6 m/s)** is maintained by the pump, giving a higher heat-transfer coefficient and a smaller heat-transfer area. *(1 mark)*
2. **Suitable for viscous, fouling, scaling, and crystallizing liquors** because the high velocity scrubs the tube walls and prevents deposition. *(1 mark)*
3. **Boiling is suppressed inside the tubes** (the liquor flashes only in the separator), so the residence time at high temperature is short and the liquor is not burned. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Why is boiling suppressed inside the tubes of a forced circulation evaporator?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The centrifugal pump elevates the pressure of the liquor inside the tubes to a value **higher than the saturation pressure corresponding to the tube temperature** (i.e., $P_{tube} > P_{sat}(T_{tube})$). *(1 mark)*
2. This pressure elevation is provided by the pump head in excess of the static, friction, and acceleration heads. *(1 mark)*
3. As a result, the liquid remains subcooled in the tubes and only flashes when it enters the low-pressure vapor body. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option 1)

**Q.A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
*(a)* Draw a neat schematic diagram of a forced circulation evaporator and label all the streams. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A forced circulation evaporator is used to concentrate 10 000 kg/h of a 10% (by mass) NaOH solution to 50%. Saturated steam at 3.0 bar (absolute) is supplied, and the evaporator operates at 0.3 bar (absolute) in the vapor body. The feed enters at 30 °C and the overall heat-transfer coefficient may be taken as **2500 W/m²·K**. Neglect boiling point elevation and heat losses. Calculate:
- (i) the rate of water vaporization, *(3 marks)*
- (ii) the steam consumption, *(2 marks)*
- (iii) the heating surface area. *(2 marks)*

**Given Data:**
- $\lambda_s$ at 3.0 bar ≈ 2164 kJ/kg
- $\lambda_V$ at 0.3 bar ≈ 2315 kJ/kg (from steam tables, saturation at 69.1 °C)
- $C_p$ of dilute NaOH ≈ 4.0 kJ/kg·K
- $T_{boil}$ at 0.3 bar = 69.1 °C

---

**Solution (a) — Schematic (7 marks):**

*(The student should draw the schematic shown in Section 4.1. Allocation: 1 mark each for: Feed inlet, Mixer, Pump, Calandria with steam, Vapor body, Vapor outlet to condenser, Sump recycle & product.)*

---

**Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step (i): Rate of water vaporization (3 marks)**

Using the material balance formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= F \cdot \left( \dfrac{x_L - x_F}{x_L} \right) \\
V &= 10\,000 \cdot \left( \dfrac{0.50 - 0.10}{0.50} \right) \\
V &= 10\,000 \cdot 0.80 \\
\therefore \quad V &= 8\,000 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): Steam consumption (2 marks)**

Using the energy balance formula derived in Section 3.2 (cold feed version):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_s &= \dfrac{ V \cdot \lambda_V + F \cdot C_{p,F} \cdot (T_{boil} - T_F) }{ \lambda_s } \\
T_{boil} - T_F &= 69.1 - 30 = 39.1 \; °\text{C} \\
m_s &= \dfrac{ (8\,000)(2315) + (10\,000)(4.0)(39.1) }{ 2164 } \\
m_s &= \dfrac{ 18\,520\,000 + 1\,564\,000 }{ 2164 } \\
m_s &= \dfrac{ 20\,084\,000 }{ 2164 } \\
\therefore \quad m_s &\approx 9\,282 \text{ kg/h} \; (\approx 9.28 \text{ t/h})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii): Heating surface area (2 marks)**

The heat load is the enthalpy delivered by steam:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= m_s \cdot \lambda_s = 9\,282 \times 2164 \; \text{kJ/h} \\
Q &= 20\,086\,248 \; \text{kJ/h} = \dfrac{20\,086\,248}{3600} \; \text{kW} \\
Q &\approx 5\,579.5 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mean temperature difference (neglecting BPE per problem statement):

$$
\Delta T = T_s - T_{boil} = 133.5 - 69.1 = 64.4 \; °\text{C}
$$

(Steam saturation at 3 bar ≈ 133.5 °C.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} \\
A &= \dfrac{5\,579\,500 \; \text{W}}{ 2500 \times 64.4 } \\
A &= \dfrac{5\,579\,500}{161\,000} \\
\therefore \quad A &\approx 34.65 \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option 2)

**Q.B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*(a)* Explain with a neat sketch the **working principle** of a forced circulation evaporator. Discuss the role of the **pump** and the **vapor body** in detail. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A forced circulation evaporator handles 50 000 kg/h of a 5% salt solution. The calandria has **120 tubes** of **50 mm inner diameter**. The liquor density is 1050 kg/m³. Calculate the linear velocity of the liquor in the tubes. State whether the design satisfies the recommended range. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution (a) — Descriptive (7 marks):**

*(Allocate marks as follows — typical KTU valuation key):*

- **Sketch (3 marks):** Neat block diagram showing Feed → Pump → Calandria → Vapor Body → Vapor to Condenser, with recycle to mixer.
