# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**State the fundamental differential equation governing the rate of moisture removal in a batch drying operation and identify each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The governing equation is:

$$L_s \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} = -A \cdot N_A(X)$$

where:
- $L_s$ = mass of bone-dry solid (kg), invariant during drying,
- $X$ = free moisture content on dry basis (kg water / kg dry solid),
- $t$ = time (hr),
- $A$ = exposed drying surface area (m²),
- $N_A(X)$ = local drying flux (kg / m²·hr).

The negative sign indicates that moisture decreases with time. **(3 marks — 1 for equation, 2 for terms)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three numerical methods that can be implemented in a spreadsheet to evaluate the falling-rate drying time when the drying flux $N_A$ is given as a tabulated function of $X$.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Trapezoidal Rule** — Uses the average of integrand values at two adjacent $X$ points. Excel formula: `=SUMPRODUCT((dX),(f_i+f_i+1)/2)`.
2. **Simpson's 1/3 Rule** — Fits parabolic segments; requires an even number of intervals. Higher order accuracy (O($h^4$)) than trapezoidal.
3. **Euler's Method (ODE form)** — Rewrites $-dX/dt = N_A(X) \cdot A / L_s$ and steps forward in time using $X_{n+1} = X_n + h \cdot f(X_n)$. Useful when $N_A(X)$ has no closed antiderivative.

**(3 marks — 1 mark each)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### **Question A — Drying Time Using Numerical Integration [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question aligned with PCCHT403 Module 4 outcomes]**

A pharmaceutical company dries a wet cake in a **batch tray dryer**. The following data are available:

- Total initial wet mass = **350 kg**
- Initial moisture content = **45 % (wet basis)**
- Critical moisture content = **22 % (wet basis)**
- Equilibrium moisture content = **3 % (wet basis)**
- Final moisture content = **8 % (wet basis)**
- Drying surface area = **12 m²**
- Constant drying flux = **2.0 kg / m²·hr**
- The drying rate in the falling rate period is **non-linear**, with the following empirical table:

| $X$ (dry basis) | 0.282 | 0.260 | 0.235 | 0.205 | 0.175 | 0.143 | 0.108 | 0.083 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N_A$ (kg/m²·hr) | 2.00 | 1.65 | 1.30 | 0.96 | 0.68 | 0.44 | 0.25 | 0.12 |

**(a)** Compute the **time for the constant rate period** and the **time for the falling rate period using the trapezoidal rule**. Show every conversion step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prepare the **Excel worksheet layout** (column-by-column) that you would use to compute the falling rate time. Write down the exact Excel cell formula to evaluate the trapezoidal sum and compute the total drying time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A**

##### **Part (a) — Step-by-Step Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Convert wet-basis moistures to dry-basis:**

$$X_1 = \frac{0.45}{0.55} = 0.8182\ \text{kg/kg}$$

$$X_c = \frac{0.22}{0.78} = 0.2821\ \text{kg/kg}$$

$$X^* = \frac{0.03}{0.97} = 0.0309\ \text{kg/kg}$$

$$X_2 = \frac{0.08}{0.92} = 0.0870\ \text{kg/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute dry solid mass:**

$$L_s = 350 \times (1 - 0.45) = 350 \times 0.55 = 192.5\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Constant rate period time:**

$$t_c = \frac{L_s}{A \cdot N_A}(X_1 - X_c) = \frac{192.5}{12 \times 2.0}(0.8182 - 0.2821)$$

$$t_c = 8.021 \times 0.5361 = 4.300\ \text{hr}$$

**Step 4 — Falling rate period via trapezoidal rule:**

The integral is:

$$t_f = -\frac{L_s}{A}\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{N_A(X)} = \frac{192.5}{12}\int_{X_2}^{X_c}\frac{dX}{N_A(X)}$$

Compute the integrand $g(X) = 1/N_A(X)$ at each tabulated $X$:

| $X$ | 0.282 | 0.260 | 0.235 | 0.205 | 0.175 | 0.143 | 0.108 | 0.083 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $1/N_A$ | 0.5000 | 0.6061 | 0.7692 | 1.0417 | 1.4706 | 2.2727 | 4.0000 | 8.3333 |

Apply the trapezoidal rule over the 7 intervals:

$$\text{Interval 1: } \Delta X = 0.022,\ (0.5000 + 0.6061)/2 = 0.5530 \Rightarrow 0.022 \times 0.5530 = 0.01217$$

$$\text{Interval 2: } \Delta X = 0.025,\ (0.6061 + 0.7692)/2 = 0.6877 \Rightarrow 0.025 \times 0.6877 = 0.01719$$

$$\text{Interval 3: } \Delta X = 0.030,\ (0.7692 + 1.0417)/2 = 0.9055 \Rightarrow 0.030 \times 0.9055 = 0.02716$$

$$\text{Interval 4: } \Delta X = 0.030,\ (1.0417 + 1.4706)/2 = 1.2562 \Rightarrow 0.030 \times 1.2562 = 0.03769$$

$$\text{Interval 5: } \Delta X = 0.032,\ (1.4706 + 2.2727)/2 = 1.8717 \Rightarrow 0.032 \times 1.8717 = 0.05989$$

$$\text{Interval 6: } \Delta X = 0.035,\ (2.2727 + 4.0000)/2 = 3.1364 \Rightarrow 0.035 \times 3.1364 = 0.10977$$

$$\text{Interval 7: } \Delta X = 0.025,\ (4.0000 + 8.3333)/2 = 6.1667 \Rightarrow 0.025 \times 6.1667 = 0.15417$$

**Sum of trapezoidal contributions:**

$$\sum = 0.01217 + 0.01719 + 0.02716 + 0.03769 + 0.05989 + 0.10977 + 0.15417 = 0.41804$$

**Falling rate time:**

$$t_f = \frac{192.5}{12} \times 0.41804 = 16.042 \times 0.41804 = 6.706\ \text{hr}$$

**Total drying time:**

$$\boxed{\,t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 4.300 + 6.706 = 11.006\ \text{hr}\,}$$

---

##### **Part (b) — Excel Worksheet Layout [7 marks]**

**Recommended Excel layout:**

| Column A | Column B | Column C | Column D | Column E |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $X_i$ (dry basis) | $X_{i+1}$ | $1/N_A(i)$ | $1/N_A(i+1)$ | Contribution $\Delta X \cdot (g_i + g_{i+1})/2$ |
| 0.282 | 0.260 | 0.5000 | 0.6061 | =ABS(B2-A2)*(C2+D2)/2 |
| 0.260 | 0.235 | 0.6061 | 0.7692 | =ABS(B3-A3)*(C3+D3)/2 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 0.108 | 0.083 | 4.0000 | 8.3333 | =ABS(B8-A8)*(C8+D8)/2 |

**Cell formulas:**

- **Total trapezoidal sum (cell F1):** `=SUM(E2:E8)` → gives 0.41804
- **Falling rate time (cell F2):** `=192.5/12*F1` → gives 6.706 hr
- **Constant rate time (cell F3):** `=192.5/(12*2)*(0.8182-0.2821)` → gives 4.300 hr
- **Total drying time (cell F4):** `=F2+F3` → gives **11.006 hr**

**Discussion points to be included in the microproject report:**
- Justification for choosing trapezoidal rule over Simpson's 1/3 (8 points = 7 intervals → odd number; need even intervals for Simpson's).
- Source and accuracy of $N_A$ vs. $X$ data.
- Effect of halving $\Delta X$ on convergence (refinement study).

---

### **Question B — Crystallization Yield Using Iterative Solver [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question aligned with PCCHT403 Module 4 outcomes]**

A continuous **evaporative crystallizer** is fed with **$F = 8000\ \text{kg/hr}$** of an aqueous **KNO$_3$** solution at **60 °C** with concentration **$C_F = 0.45$ (mass fraction)**. The crystallizer operates at **30 °C** where the solubility of KNO$_3$ is **$S = 45.3\ \text{g per 100 g water}$**. The crystal yield required is **$Y_{target} = 2400\ \text{kg/hr}$**.

**(a)** Derive the working equation for yield $Y$ in terms of $F$, $C_F$, $V$ (water evaporated), and $y_W$. Then, using algebraic manipulation, solve for the required evaporation rate $V$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Excel Solver / Goal Seek procedure** step-by-step that you would use if the algebraic rearrangement were not straightforward (e.g., if $S$ depends non-linearly on $T$). Include a sketch of the worksheet cell layout and the convergence-trace table. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Write the three governing balances:**

Total balance: $F = L + V$
Solvent balance: $F(1 - C_F) = W + V$ → with $W \approx L$ for clear mother liquor
Solute balance: $F \cdot C_F = W \cdot y_W + Y$

**Step 2 — Mother liquor composition at saturation ($T_2 = 30\ ^\circ$C):**

$$y_W = \frac{S}{100 + S} = \frac{45.3}{145.3} = 0.3118$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $W = F(1 - C_F) - V$ into the solute balance:**

$$Y = F \cdot C_F - (F(1 - C_F) - V) \cdot y_W$$

**Step 4 — Plug numbers and rearrange algebraically:**

$$2400 = 8000 \times 0.45 - (8000 \times 0.55 - V) \times 0.3118$$

$$2400 = 3600 - (4400 - V) \times 0.3118$$

$$3600 - 2400 = (4400 - V) \times 0.3118$$

$$1200 = 0.3118(4400 - V)$$

$$4400 - V = \frac{1200}{0.3118} = 3848.62$$

$$V = 4400 - 3848.62 = 551.38\ \text{kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\,V \approx 551.4\ \text{kg/hr water must be evaporated to obtain the target yield of 2400 kg/hr.\,}$$

---

##### **Part (b) — Solver / Goal Seek Procedure [7 marks]**

**When the equation is implicit (e.g., $S$ depends non-linearly on $T$), the algebraic route fails. The iterative procedure is:**

**Step 1 — Set up the worksheet:**

| Cell | Variable | Value / Formula |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | $F$ | 8000 |
| B2 | $C_F$ | 0.45 |
| B3 | $T_2$ (°C) | 30 |
| B4 | $S(T_2)$ | (lookup or formula) = 45.3 |
| B5 | $y_W$ | =B4/(100+B4) |
| B6 | $V$ (initial guess) | 500 |
| B7 | $Y_{calc}$ | =B1*B2 - (B1*(1-B2) - B6)*B5 |
| B8 | $Y_{target}$ | 2400 |
| B9 | Error | =B7 - B8 |

**Step 2 — Excel Solver invocation:**

`Data tab → Solver → Set Objective: B9 → Value of: 0 → By Changing Variable Cells: B6 → Solving Method: GRG Nonlinear → Solve`

**Step 3 — Convergence trace table:**

| Iteration | $V$ (kg/hr) | $Y_{calc}$ (kg/hr) | Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500.0 | 2361.8 | −38.2 |
| 2 | 540.0 | 2399.0 | −1.0 |
| 3 | 551.4 | 2400.0 | 0.0 ✓ |

**Step 4 — Sensitivity extension (optional microproject):** Build a 2-D table varying $T_2$ from 20 °C to 50 °C and recording the resulting $V$ and $Y$. Plot $V$ vs. $T_2$ to discuss energy economy.

**Step 5 — Discussion paragraph:** Comment on the physical meaning — at lower final temperatures, less evaporation is needed (cooling-dominated), while at higher temperatures more evaporation is needed (evaporation-dominated). The optimum operating point balances energy cost vs. crystal recovery.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on this Type of Question**
>
> 1. **Wet-basis vs. Dry-basis confusion (–2 marks typical):** Many students apply $X_1 = 0.40$ directly without converting $w_1 = 0.40$ to $X_1 = 0.6667$. Always check the basis before plugging values.
>
> 2. **Sign error in the drying-time integral (–2 marks typical):** The integral $-dX/dt$ must yield positive time. Students often write $+dX$ on both sides, producing negative drying times.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by $L_s/A$ factor in trapezoidal rule (–3 marks):** The numerical sum gives $\int dX/N_A(X)$ but the actual time is $(L_s/A) \times \text{sum}$. This is the single most common error in KTU valuation.
>
> 4. **Crystallization: setting $W = F$ instead of $W = F(1-C_F) - V$ (–2 marks):** The mother liquor flow is **not** the entire feed; solvent (water) leaves partly as vapour.
>
> 5. **Excel answer without engineering interpretation (–2 marks):** KTU 2024 Scheme rewards Process / Engineering discussion. A numerical answer alone, without a paragraph explaining "why the drying time is longer for non-linear FRP" or "what happens if $V$ is increased", loses the application-level marks.
>
> 6. **Unit mismatches (–1 mark each occurrence):** Mixing seconds and hours, m² and cm², kg and g without conversion is a recurring penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 Microproject — Last-Minute Revision Bullets**
>
> - **Drying time always requires three numbers:** $L_s$ (dry solid mass), $A$ (area), and $N_A$ (flux). Never substitute $m_{total}$ for $L_s$.
> - **Conversion formulas:** $X = w/(1-w)$ and $w = X/(1+X)$ — memorize both directions.
> - **Constant-rate formula:** $t_c = (L_s/AN_A)(X_1 - X_c)$.
> - **Linear FRP formula:** $t_f = [L_s(X_c - X^*)/(AN_A)] \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
> - **Non-linear FRP:** Use trapezoidal rule — Excel formula `=SUMPRODUCT(dX, (g_i + g_{i+1})/2)`.
> - **Crystallization yield equation:** $Y = F C_F - (F(1-C_F) - V) \cdot [S/(100+S)]$.
> - **Saturation constraint:** Mother liquor exits at the solubility concentration $S(T_2)/(100+S(T_2))$.
> - **Solver / Goal Seek:** Use for implicit equations where algebraic rearrangement is cumbersome.
> - **Trapezoidal accuracy** improves as $O(h^2)$; refine $\Delta X$ to converge the answer.
> - **Engineering edge cases:**
>   - If $X_2 > X_c$, drying is entirely in CRP (no FRP contribution).
>   - If $V = 0$, crystallizer is purely cooling-type — the equation still holds with $V = 0$.
>   - If $S = 0$ at $T_2$, complete crystallization occurs (extreme case).
> - **Always include units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct for missing units.
> - **Plotting a drying-rate curve ($N_A$ vs. $X$) is a common Part B sub-question** — practise it in Excel.
> - **Sensitivity analysis** is a hallmark of a strong microproject: vary one input, plot the output.

---
